Why is Doug Ford planning to send cheques to millionaires?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-is-doug-ford-planning-send-cheques/docview/3118364406/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+Doug+Ford+planning+to+send+cheques+to+millionaires%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tony Staffieri, CEO of Rogers, gets $200. RBC boss Dave McKay gets $200. Hockey star Mitch Marner gets $200.
That's the latest imaginative idea from our Ontario government.
According to news reports, the province will soon send a cheque for $200 to every Ontarian. Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy will make it official on October 30, the Star's Robert Benzie recently reported.
This may be good politics - free money is free money - but it is terrible policy.
It's also somewhat surprising.
In normal times, Conservatives pride themselves on being careful stewards of public dollars. They don't mind being called cheap, or stingy; they call it being thrifty and prudent.
But these are not normal times, it seems. Premier Doug Ford's September announcement that he will build a traffic tunnel under Highway 401 signalled a new era in Ontario public finance: the era where we have infinite money to spend on things nobody asked for.
The cost of the tunnel is both unknown and, to the premier, irrelevant: "We're getting that tunnel built, I guarantee it," he says. If it is ever dug, the final price tag of this elongated hole in the ground will be too high to count.
In contrast, the cost of sending $200 to every Ontarian is easily estimated: It's about $3.2 billion. But here's a question: Where are they getting the money?
In the short term, it will be borrowed: the province has a budget deficit right now. But in the long term, that $3.2 billion can only come out of Ontarians' pockets - or the services they depend on.
In recent decades, right-wing pundits have been successful at separating public dollars, in the public mind, from the things those dollars pay for. They've created the impression that tax cuts, and cash giveaways, have no consequences.
This is not true. It has never been true.
For starters, the latest free-money plan will send public dollars to millions of Ontarians who clearly do not need them. Earlier this month, Statistics Canada reported that the richest 20 per cent of Canadians own two-thirds of all the wealth in the country. Meanwhile, income inequality has hit record levels.
If Queen's Park wants to give away money because "people are hurting" - a frequent Ford comment - then that assistance should go to people who are actually hurting.
With $3.2 billion, the province could triple the Ontario Child Benefit for a year and still have half a billion left over. That would give low- and middle-income families some breathing space.
With $3.2 billion, the province could double payments to Ontario Works recipients, whose incomes have been frozen since 2018. That would support Ontarians mired in deep poverty.
Alternatively, the province could boost critical programs that are currently starved for funds. Spending $3.2 billion could double, for a year, the funding for social services that support children at risk or with special needs. Programs like child protection, children's treatment, and the Complex Special Needs program (to name a few) need all the help they can get.
The list of good places to spend $3.2 billion is a long one. Think hospitals. Home care. Housing. Municipalities. Crumbing schools with ever-larger class sizes.
With that kind of money, we could even get serious about tackling the climate emergency that threatens us all. At the moment, Ontario's "Climate Change and Resiliency" program has a budget of $15 million - less than one two-hundredth of the cost of sending those $200 cheques to every Ontarian. And every dollar spent on climate action now will save money down the road.
The province, of course, says it is already making big investments in public services and infrastructure. But accounting for inflation and population growth, spending on Ontario's public services is, in almost every area, stagnant or falling. And our support for Ontarians in need is going backwards.
That's where Queen's Park should be putting our money.
We don't need to mail cheques to millionaires.
CAPTION: Doug Ford is reportedlyplanning to send cheques for $200 to everyone in Ontario, including millionaires.
CREDIT: Randy Robinson, Contributor

Subject: Public services; Climate change; Budget deficits; Wealth

Business indexing term: Subject: Budget deficits Wealth

Why the FBI want this Canadian Olympian + the fate of Toronto's cube homes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-fbi-want-this-canadian-olympian-fate-torontos/docview/3117980571/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+the+FBI+want+this+Canadian+Olympian+%2B+the+fate+of+Toronto%27s+cube+homes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, October 18 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a Canadian Olympian turned alleged drug lord, the fate of Toronto's iconic Cube House and the nation's shifting views on immigration.
DON'T MISSWhy the FBI is after this Canadian former Olympic snowboarder
Two decades ago, Ryan James Wedding represented Canada in the 2002 Winter Olympics. Today, the decorated snowboarder is wanted by the FBI for allegedly orchestrating a massive international cocaine ring and ordering multiple killings across Ontario, including the slaying of a Caledon couple in what police say was a case of mistaken identity. Calvi Leon has the details of a case seemingly straight out of a crime drama.
Wanted: Wedding, 43, and Andrew Clark, 34, are facing a bevy of charges after allegedly directing the killings in retaliation over a stolen drug shipment. Clark is currently in custody.Mistaken identity: Police say the two allegedly ordered gunmen to storm the home of an "innocent family" last year, leaving Jagtar Singh Sidhu, 57, and Harbhajan Kaur Sidhu, 55, dead and their 28-year-old daughter with life-changing injuries.Demolition looms for Toronto's whimsical cube homes
One of the city's most iconic architectural experiments could soon face the wrecking ball. The land owner of Toronto's Cube House has applied for a demolition permit after "exploring multiple paths" and determining that it "could not be safely preserved," Mahdis Habibinia reports. Instead, they're partnering with artist Benjamin Von Wong to repurpose its materials into an art installation. Take a look at the future of the shapely dwellings.
Missed potential: Built in 1996 by Ottawa architect Ben Kutner and his partner Jeff Brown, the geometric structure was intended to be the first of a larger community of cuboid buildings and whimsical homes. But that dream never took shape.Now what? Landowner Block Developments is now working with the city to bring a potential grocery store and "various housing options" to the corktown neighbourhood.Canadians' views on immigration are changing
For the first time in a quarter-century, a clear majority of Canadians now believe the country has accepted too many immigrants. A long-term tracking poll found 58 per cent of Canadians say there's too much immigration - a major shift after years of pro-immigration consensus, Nicholas Keung reports. The results accompany "changes in how Canadians view immigrants themselves and how they are fitting in," the study reads. Check out what it found.
Word from Marc Miller: In an exclusive conversation with the Star, Canada's Immigration Minister addressed Canadians' shifting sentiment on the subject and the recent reforms his government made. Read what he had to say.Context: Canada's overall population has soared in recent years, largely due to a surge of temporary residents including international students, foreign workers and asylum seekers after the 2020 pandemic.WHAT ELSE

Document 3 of 500

How is Ford's blatant attempt to buy votes not bribery?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-is-fords-blatant-attempt-buy-votes-not/docview/3117980516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+is+Ford%27s+blatant+attempt+to+buy+votes+not+bribery%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 16
As a long-time criminal lawyer, I wonder how such a blatant attempt to buy votes is not bribery. Were Premier Doug Ford to send me, only, a cheque, followed shortly thereafter with an early election call, I'd certainly feel as if he were trying to bribe me. Is the difference to be found in the sheer magnitude of Ford's attempt to influence all Ontario voters? Does that not make it worse? If Ford does this, I hope that people who can afford to will direct the bribe money to some of the causes such as housing, education and poverty reduction, to name just three, that Ford underfunds, because he can. Alternatively, donate it to a group or party to defeat him. Please, don't allow Ford to buy you off for $200.
John Liss, Toronto
I would rather Premier Doug Ford and his pals put all the $200 cheques into one pot and use the funds to provide health care for many of the 16 million Ontarians who have no primary care. However, our Premier seems focused on liquor and beer, cutting down trees, bulldozing the Greenbelt, building parking lots, spas and tunnels, and cancelling bikes lanes while at the same time increasing speed limits on main highways. Now he is sending out bribes to voters. Why do we keep voting in this man who is squandering taxpayers' money instead of using it for the common good of all people in the province? Maybe we voters are the short-sighted ones.
Sharon Phelps, Lindsay
Yesterday I was told I needed a CT scan and the soonest I can get one is January 2025. Wouldn't it be better to invest in our health system instead of sending us all a cheque just before election time?
Madeleine Nevins, Markham
I am dismayed at the prospect of the Ontario government spending an estimated $3.2 billion of taxpayer money by sending out $200 cheques to Ontarians as part of its bid to win re-election in 2025. At least when he gave out $20 bills to constituents as a city councillor in 2013, it was from his own pocket. Now he is reaching into all of ours to try and win our votes. Shame. There are so many ways that this funding could be better spent. How about giving access to the 2.5 million Ontarians without a primary care provider, reducing hallway medicine and the embarrassingly long wait times for medical procedures and access to long-term care facilities, building more affordable housing, increasing disability support funding, improving the education system, enabling faster access to better mental health and addiction treatment, and so on. Imagine what an infusion of an additional $3.2 billion in one year could do to give people access to much needed and continuously underfunded government services and programs. Instead, this province will go further into deficit and programs and services will go further into decline. I can't and won't vote for that. What I can and do vote for are governments that actually invest in the health and well-being of this province and the people who call it home.
Isabella Tatar, Toronto
Why Trudeau must stay on as Liberal leader
Voters hoping to avoid another Liberal-NDP partnership after the next general election should encourage Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to stay on as Liberal leader. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is sufficiently unpopular that he may not win a majority without Trudeau's continued help.
Patrick Cowan, North York
More reasons to support science centres
We need more science centres, Oct. 15
Here are two more points to add to Joanne O'Meara's excellent article. Science centres provide much-needed support for school teachers in the challenging task of teaching science and related STEM subjects through field trips, curriculum materials, webinars and other resources for teachers. In astronomy, my subject, few teachers have any background in astronomy, or astronomy teaching. Science centres help. Science centres also provide a base for non-profit community organizations which voluntarily provide public education and outreach. The Ontario Science Centre, until it closed, for instance, was the home of the Toronto branch of the Royal Astronomical Society of Canada (founded 1868) which provides dozens of free presentations and star parties across the GTA. And let's not forget that it was a previous Ontario Conservative government whose "common sense revolution" led to the 1995 demise of Toronto's McLaughlin Planetarium, which educated and inspired hundreds of thousands of visitors of all ages!
John Percy, vice-chair (1992-96) of the Board of Trustees of the Ontario Science Centre, Etobicoke
Replace entire Metrolinx board for a fresh start
Metrolinx shakeup sees departure of senior leaders, head of light-rail transit, Oct. 16
At last, someone is being held accountable for the many fiascos Metrolinx has inflicted on Ontarians. One hopes it is just a start. After all, the rot starts at the top. When the entire Metrolinx board has been sacked and when the CEO has been shown the door (with cause and without severance) then we'll have the fresh start that is desperately needed.
John Gavin, Toronto
If this is a war on cars, the cars are winning
We should only be putting bike lanes in places that make sense, Oct. 13
The Ontario Minister of Transportation wants to restrict and remove bike lanes in Toronto. He says we need to consult the data. The data, however, is pretty clear. Looking at Bathurst Street for example, from the lake to Highway 401 there are 14 major, two-way streets. Of these, only three have bike lanes. The data speaks volumes - if this is a war on cars, the cars are winning handily. To reduce these three bike lanes to a mere two would only serve to make cyclists more unsafe and the city less livable.
Bruce Stratton, Toronto
It's time to truly separate church and state
Ford minister slams trustees' $145,000 trip to Italy to buy artwork for new school, Oct. 16
Brantford Catholic School board trustees swanned off to Italy this summer on an art buying trip. Ontario's Education Minister Jill Dunlop is enraged. My thoughts are along the same line. I will go farther. It's time to have only two school boards in Ontario, the English School Board and the French School Board. Religious education can be given by the various churches. Then tax dollars will not be going to buy life size statues of St. Pio. Then four different busses will not be going down the same country road. Then church and state will really be separate.
Sheila Craig, Barrie
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford attends the US-Canada Summit in Toronto on Tuesday, June 11, 2024. F

Document 4 of 500

'A complete no-brainer': How Toronto's parking lots could drive a green-power revolution

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/complete-no-brainer-how-torontos-parking-lots/docview/3117980266/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+complete+no-brainer%27%3A+How+Toronto%27s+parking+lots+could+drive+a+green-power+revolution&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When most people look at a parking lot, all they see is a place to ditch their car for a few hours.
But these expanses of asphalt scattered across the city could hold the key to reducing carbon emissions, improving air quality, preventing blackouts and avoiding unnecessary spending on new gas plants that contribute to climate change.
If they were covered in solar panels, the surface parking lots of Toronto could produce enough electricity to meet one-third of the entire city's electricity needs, according to a new report commissioned by the Ontario Clean Air Alliance (OCAA).
That's enough energy to shut down the Port Lands gas plant - the number one source of greenhouse gasses in the city - and support decarbonization through electrification of transport and home heating, says OCAA chair Jack Gibbons.
"It's one of the simplest, most pragmatic, lowest cost options to phase out the dirty Port Lands gas plant. It's just a complete no-brainer," he said.
While the city has put parking lot solar panels in its TransformTO climate action plan to become carbon neutral by 2040, there are no current bylaws on the books to incentivize or require their installation. In fact, there are only two examples of solar panels on surface lots anywhere in the city - at Centennial College and York University - according to Toronto Hydro.
France requires all larger parking lots to erect solar panels on metal frames over the spots. The panels shade the parked cars in the summer and keep the snow off in the winter and can be used to power electric vehicle (EV) chargers on site or feed electricity into the grid.
The reduced reliance on gas plants would improve local air quality, while the shade provided by the panels would reduce the urban heat island effect and passively cool the surrounding areas, the report states.
Not only would this be cheaper than constructing new gas plants and power lines, but because parking lot solar would be located in the city, if it were paired with batteries it could be used to prevent blackouts when a storm knocks out a transformer station, as one did this summer.
Asked about parking lot solar, Isha Chaudhuri, a spokesperson for Energy Minister Stephen Lecce, said the province would be unveiling a plan to "enable more generation and conservation using renewable energy and storage" in the coming weeks.
"Our government is focused on removing regulatory barriers and making it easier for families, businesses and municipalities to produce energy for their own use," she said.
The French law requires parking lots above 1,500 square metres, or about 80 spots, to cover at least half their area with solar panels. If this were applied in Toronto, more than 7,000 lots would be affected, and the resulting generation would be more than 2.5 times more electricity than the Port Lands plant produces in a year.
It would also reduce the city's emissions by 1.9 megatonnes per year, or the equivalent of taking more than half a million cars off the road, the report found.
If the surface covered was bumped up to 70 per cent, that would produce nearly four times more energy than the Port Lands plant - 34 per cent of all of Toronto's electricity consumption in 2023.
Last summer, the Star published estimates of the enormous potential of solar if it was put on some of the city's biggest and most iconic buildings.
Gibbons says the only reason there hasn't already been movement on this is because the provincial government is beholden to the interests of large energy companies.
"The government of Ontario's not responding to this because they want to meet our future electricity needs with a combination of polluting fossil gas and high cost nuclear power," he said.
"Doug Ford's electricity policies benefit the largest energy corporations, like Enbridge Gas, TC Energy and Bruce Power. But they're not in the best interests of consumers, public health or our climate."
The OCAA says the province is barrelling ahead with billion dollar electricity decisions that will have impacts for generations without considering many cleaner and cheaper alternatives popular around the world, like offshore wind, which has been under a moratorium for over a decade.
Solar panels are sometimes referred to as nature's peaker plant because they produce electricity on the hottest summer days when it's needed most. This is typically when the gas plants are fired up to provide power as people crank up their air conditioning units.
Only unlike peaker plants, which charge a premium for being on standby, solar provides the cheapest electricity in history, according to the International Energy Agency.
And solar also does so without the health problems created by releasing toxic gasses in the middle of the city, as the Port Lands plant currently does.
The OCAA is calling on the city, the province, Toronto Hydro and the Independent Electricity System Operator (IESO) to create a plan to facilitate more parking lot solar. The first hurdle would be allowing private parking lot owners to sell electricity into the grid as gas plants and nuclear reactors do (and as the province used to allow small solar installations to do).
Currently, solar panels are only permitted to be net metered, which means they can only generate the same amount of electricity as they use, otherwise the rest is given away for free.
"There's just absolutely no reason why they shouldn't pay a fair market price that would incentivize parking lot owners to install solar panels," Gibbons said.
"It will be a lower cost source of new electricity for the grid. And that means that it'll be a bargain for consumers, and lead to lower electricity bills.
"This is win-win for everyone except for the fossil fuel industry and the nuclear industry."
CAPTION: If more parking lots, like those at Centennial College, were covered with solar panels pictured here, a report from the Ontario Clean Air Alliance (OCAA) suggests they could generate enough electricity to meet one-third of Toronto's total needs.

Subject: Air pollution; Outdoor air quality; Electricity; Energy industry; Climate change

Document 5 of 500

'A David vs. Goliath fight': Court allows appeal from youth in landmark climate case against province

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/david-vs-goliath-fight/docview/3117870337/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27A+David+vs.+Goliath+fight%27%3A+Court+allows+appeal+from+youth+in+landmark+climate+case+against+province&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a landmark case, Ontario's top court has ruled that an appeal from seven young Ontarians suing the provincial government over its new greenhouse gas emissions targets can proceed.
However, the Court of Appeal declined to rule on the case's substance, extending a legal battle in Canada's courts over their role in overseeing government climate action.
In a decision released Thursday, three judges - Lois B. Roberts, Steve A. Coroza and Sally A. Gomery - sided with the appellants in Mathur v. Ontario that a Superior Court of Justice judge "erred" in deciding the young people's case was a "positive rights case" in an April 2023 decision.
"This is not a positive rights case. The application does not seek to impose on Ontario any new positive obligations to combat climate change," judges Roberts, Coroza and Gomery wrote. In legal studies and political theory, positive rights refers to rights that give people something, such as a right to have an education or a right to housing.
But the judges fell short of determining the merits of the case's arguments, writing the appeals court was "not well placed" to make such a ruling. Instead, they called for a new hearing, which could be overseen by the original judge or a different one.
Thursday's ruling gave the seven young appellants - lead applicant Sophia Mathur, Madison Dyck, Shelby Gagnon, Beze Gray, Zoë Keary-Matzner, Alex Neufeldt and Shaelyn Wabegijig - a small victory in their lawsuit against the provincial government that was first filed in 2019.
At issue - which lawyers for the seven young people argued before the Court of Appeal earlier this year - was Ontario's weakened 2030 emissions targets passed by Premier Doug Ford's then-newly elected Progressive Conservative government in 2018 that reduced goals from 37 per cent lower emissions below 2005 levels to 30 per cent. According to the appellants claim, this reduction violates the charter rights of current youth and future generations, specifically sections 7 and 15 that provide the right to life, liberty and security of the person and equal protection under the law respectively.
"It's an important day for climate justice and an important day for government accountability," Nader Hasan, lead counsel to the appellants, told the Star shortly after the decision was released.
Hasan says the young Ontarians at the centre of the case are feeling encouraged by Thursday's decision. "We're feeling very optimistic, because, despite dismissing the application, the trial judge agreed with us entirely on the facts and the evidence," he said.
In her 2023 ruling, Superior Court of Justice Judge Marie-Andrée Vermette rejected the Ontario government's arguments that its actions would have no effect on climate change and wrote that the province's emissions "contribute to climate change and the increased risks that it creates."
Still, Vermette ruled against the young peoples' challenge because the "disproportionate impact" caused by climate change was not caused by provincial laws.
Even if the new hearing comes before Vermette again, Hasan said the appellants' legal team was confident the case would be handled "appropriately and effectively."
"We are in the middle of a climate crisis. It's getting worse day by day, so this is a matter of urgency, and we'd be happy to be in front of any judge in the Superior Court, just as long as this matter proceeds with all haste."
In a statement sent to the Star, Jack Fazzari, press secretary for Ontario's attorney general, said that the province is a leader in tackling climate change and that it's on track to meet emission reduction targets. "No decision from the Court of Appeal was made regarding the constitutionality of Ontario's climate change plan or target. The matter was sent back to the superior court for a new hearing," he added.
Mathur v. Ontario is the first case of its kind to be tried in Canada, and Thursday's ruling could shape decisions in other cases facing courts in Canada and internationally asking judiciaries to more actively hold governments accountable for their climate policies. .
In the U.S., judges have ruled in favour of young people from Montana and Hawaii challenging governments for their alleged climate in action. And in 2019, the top court in the Netherlands ruled the government has a duty to protect its citizens from the potentially devastating effects of climate change, including by further cutting emissions.
Meanwhile, a similar case, La Rose v. Her Majesty the Queen, is awaiting a trial date at the federal level in Canada as 15 young people sue the Canadian government on similar grounds as the seven young people in Mathur v. Ontario.
The legal team in the Mathur case are in contact with those in the La Rose case, and any other lawyers working on similar cases, Hasan said.
"This is a David vs Goliath fight, so we have to collaborate," he said. "We have to co-operate and we have to share strategies."
With files from
Marco Chown Oved
CREDIT: Nathan Bawaan Toronto Star With files from Marco Chown Oved

PCs to fast-track highway projects: Bill sets penalties for holding up work on Hwy. 413, Bradford Bypass

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pcs-fast-track-highway-projects/docview/3117868804/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PCs+to+fast-track+highway+projects%3A+Bill+sets+penalties+for+holding+up+work+on+Hwy.+413%2C+Bradford+Bypass&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With an early provincial election looming, Premier Doug Ford's government wants to fast-track construction of Highway 413, the Bradford Bypass and a twin section of the Garden City Skyway in St. Catharines.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said legislation called the "Building Highways Faster Act" will be introduced after the legislature returns next week from an extended summer break to provide for "around-the-clock, 24/7 construction."
Taking aim at potential protests against the two highway projects which have been heavily opposed by environmentalists and others for using up prime Greenbelt farmland and threatening endangered or rare species, the bill would set "new penalties for obstructing access for field investigations" or "damaging equipment," Sarkaria said Thursday in Caledon.
It would also "accelerate" the process for buyouts or expropriations of farmland or other properties needed for the projects and "streamline" relocations of utility lines. There will also be legislation to establish an "accelerated environmental assessment process" for Highway 413, from Highway 401 at Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan, allowing the province to proceed with early work on the controversial roadway rejected by a previous Liberal government.
"Projects like this need to get built," Sarkaria said, citing "unprecedented growth" in Ontario with projections the population will increase two million by 2031 and put existing 400-series highways at or over capacity within a decade.
"Gridlock suffocates our roadways."
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre) said the proposed legislation is dangerous.
"Slashing environmental laws, paving over our Greenbelt, Ontarians have made it clear that they're not OK with this," she added in a statement, referring to the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal under investigation by the RCMP. "For the government to be trying it yet again with Highway 413 shows how they'll do anything to help their inner circle over the people of this province. We also need to build communities that don't require you to travel a great distance to get to work or school, by building more homes in existing neighbourhoods and especially around transit hubs."
The Bradford Bypass will stretch from Highway 400 in the Bradford area east to Highway 404 in a bid to ease congestion between there and Toronto. The Garden City Skyway traverses the Welland Canal on a busy stretch of the Queen Elizabeth Way. Plans call for a new four-lane, 2.2-kilometre bridge in addition to a rehabilitation of the deck on the existing bridge.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie reiterated her opposition to Highway 413 earlier this week.
"I have never supported the 413."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 7 of 500

Trudeau to shuffle ranks as ministers plan exit: Four more won't seek re-election as PM faces growing unrest within party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-shuffle-ranks-as-ministers-plan-exit/docview/3117868012/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+to+shuffle+ranks+as+ministers+plan+exit%3A+Four+more+won%27t+seek+re-election+as+PM+faces+growing+unrest+within+party&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will shuffle his cabinet in the coming weeks as four more ministers have told the Liberal leader they will not run again in the next campaign.
Over the last three months, a total of six of Trudeau's cabinet ministers, or 15 per cent, have bailed ahead of a federal election which polls suggest the Liberals will lose. The drip-drip of names has meant that the coming shuffle will be the third in as many months.
The shuffle is not expected to occur next week, contrary to early reports, according to a senior source, but could come quickly in the weeks following.
Parliament is in its Thanksgiving week recess but MPs return next week, when Trudeau will face a restive caucus, with reports of more than two dozen MPs - and possibly as many as 40 - getting ready to challenge Trudeau to step aside as Liberal leader.
Hamilton's Filomena Tassi, the minister for the federal economic development agency for southern Ontario, announced Thursday she won't run for reasons "that are deeply personal," adding, "It is time for me to be closer to home with my family."
Tassi follows National Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau, who is expected to run for mayor of Sherbrooke, Que., and two others who have advised Trudeau of their intention to leave politics: Northern Affairs Minister Dan Vandal, who is in charge of economic development for the Prairies and the North, and Sport Minister Carla Qualtrough. Their departures were first reported by Radio-Canada.
Last month Trudeau's Quebec lieutenant, Pablo Rodriguez, left the transport portfolio, and Liberal caucus, to make a run for the Liberal Party of Quebec leadership - a departure Trudeau filled in the interim with Treasury Board President Anita Anand. Likewise, he had a quick shuffle in July when Labour Minister Seamus O'Regan advised that he wouldn't run again, also for "personal reasons." Steven MacKinnon replaced O'Regan.
Trudeau has had a challenging fall, with polls continuing to show his party struggling.
After returning from a Francophonie summit in Paris where Israel's military engagement with Hezbollah in Lebanon dominated talks, and before leaving for the ASEAN summit in Laos last week, the prime minister called off a planned trip next week to Samoa to take part in the Commonwealth leaders summit.
Trudeau returned from Laos on the weekend, after a meeting in Germany on Ukraine was cancelled by U.S. President Joe Biden, only to confront new reports, including by the Star, of brewing caucus unrest.
All that comes amid a Canada-India diplomatic rupture as a result of the RCMP bombshell allegations against Indian diplomats and consular officials - and Trudeau's appearance at the public inquiry into foreign interference, where the prime minister accused his main rival, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, of irresponsibly refusing to obtain the required security clearance to get briefings on how his own party is at risk.
Some MPs who spoke to the Star this week defended Trudeau in the face of growing caucus dissent, arguing those calling for his resignation are helping Poilievre.
"We are wasting our energy by focusing inwards rather than focusing on the needs of our constituents," said Ottawa MP Yasir Naqvi.
"I respect Sean (Casey) but I disagree with him ... Justin Trudeau has more to offer." Naqvi was referring to the Prince Edward Island MP who went public this week saying it was time for Trudeau to step aside.
Another MP said it was "disappointing" to hear about these efforts in the media when those conversations should be held in private.
Others said they have not seen a letter reportedly calling for Trudeau's resignation and declined to take a stance on his leadership, but said they agree that conversations should be had about the future of the party. If even a small group of MPs sign a letter calling on the prime minister to resign, he will be put in a tough situation, they said.
With files from Mark Ramzy
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star With files from Mark Ramzy

Document 8 of 500

Doug Ford wants to buy your silence

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wants-buy-your-silence/docview/3118094519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+wants+to+buy+your+silence&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Some politicians try to buy votes.
Doug Ford wants to buy your silence. And acquiescence.
For a roughly $200 payout, to be handed out to everyone in Ontario, the premier hopes voters will forgive his political sins and forget his legal transgressions.
The planned cash bonanza, to be distributed early next year, is a calculated gamble. The goal is to clear the way for an early election before any bad news - legal and political - erodes the support Ford's Tories now enjoy in public opinion polls.
As my colleague Robert Benzie reported this week, the premier's sudden generosity will be made public later this month. In its annual fall economic statement, the government is dropping any pretence of fiscal probity as it invests heavily and recklessly in its own political survival.
Ford's PCs want an early election, no matter the cost. Never mind the unnecessary $155-million election expense - that's the least of it.
If they settle on the $200 figure for every adult and child in Ontario, that works out to as much as $1,000 for a family of five - and perhaps $3.2 billion out of the treasury in total. That money is badly needed to shore up our schools, our hospitals and our homeless, but the premier believes he needs it more desperately to soften up voters.
There are twin legal challenges facing Ford.
The first is deadly serious: potential violations of Ontario's criminal laws now being probed by a high-stakes RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt scandal.
The headlines have faded, but few have forgotten the $8.28-billion imbroglio over protected land made available to private developers on the premier's watch (until he reversed course under pressure). That police probe could be released sometime next year, delivering potentially bad news and a political death sentence.
Rather than wait for the police to rain on their re-election parade - scheduled for June 2026 under Ontario's fixed election law - the plan is to move the campaign up by more than a year to early 2025. But to bump up the date, the Tories would have to ignore the election law.
Fortuitously for Ford, that second potential legal peril is more symbolic than real. While the law mandates that any majority government should serve a full four-year term in power before triggering another, there is no legal penalty for flouting it - only a political price if the press and the people cry foul.
Hence the payout. The premier is banking on voters being more interested in money than morality.
It's an elegant, if expensive, election plan: a kickback for voters, gifted by a government accused of kickbacks from developers (despite those opposition allegations in the legislature, no criminality has been proven and the police aren't talking).
Why aim for a potential $200 giveaway? In the past, as a Toronto councillor, Ford famously doled out a wad of $20 bills to impoverished voters at a Toronto Community Housing building in 2013.
He later apologized for the spectacle. And subsequently opted for juicier giveaways that, historically, have hit a sweet spot with voters.
Ahead of the 2022 election, Ford's Tories cut cheques to rebate motorists for licence plate fees that the government cancelled, at a cost of more than $1 billion to the treasury. During the COVID pandemic, parents received as much as $250 per child. And the previous PC government of Mike Harris issued $200 "dividend" cheques.
How to justify such shameless pre-election (early election) vote-buying?
The unspoken reason is to satisfy the premier's lust for power. The official rationale is to support people's purchasing power.
The premier points to post-pandemic inflation raising the price of food and fuel, but it's not so simple. In fact, the inflation rate plunged to a remarkably low 1.6 per cent last month, thanks mostly to falling fuel prices, according to the latest Statistics Canada data.
And then there's the federal carbon levy, one of Ford's favourite targets. It is scheduled to increase again next spring, raising the price of gasoline.
Ford has fulminated over gasoline prices for years - even though the amount taken in by the federal government is fully rebated to the average family. Before the 2022 election, he used the federal carbon levy as a pretext for the province to reduce its own fuel tax in another vote-buying gambit.
For the Tories, the upcoming handout could be the gift that keeps on giving (if not voting). First, by soothing any public disgust over an early election; second, by serving as a counterpoint to the federal carbon levy, allowing Ford to fight an election on his perennial slogan, "Axe the tax."
In a preview of his campaign rhetoric, Ford complained Thursday about the "impact of inflation and high interest rates, alongside the growing cost of the federal carbon tax, which in my opinion is the worst tax you could ever put on the backs of people."
There you have it - an evil levy imposed by Ottawa (albeit rebated), versus a helpful handout from the province, pre-election. Ford has it all figured out.
But does he have voters figured out?
CAPTION: The unspoken reason Premier Doug Ford wants to send cheques to Ontarians is to satisfy the premier's lust for power. The official rationale is to support people's purchasing power, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Document 9 of 500

Doug Ford signals rebate cheques for all Ontarians will 'stimulate the economy'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-signals-rebate-cheques-all-ontarians/docview/3118094518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+signals+rebate+cheques+for+all+Ontarians+will+%27stimulate+the+economy%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Stressing if "you want to stimulate the economy, put money into people's pockets," Premier Doug Ford signalled the cheque will soon be in the mail.
In a speech to 1,200 people at an Empire Club luncheon at the Metro Toronto Convention, Ford gave the Star a grudging nod for revealing his finance minister would be announcing tax rebate cheques for 16 million Ontarians on Oct. 30.
"I risk stealing Peter Bethlenfalvy's thunder if I get into the fall economic statement," the premier said in a campaign-style address Thursday.
"I'm sure the Toronto Star knows it already," he added, wryly.
His comments came after the Star reported Wednesday that Ford is planning to send cheques to every Ontario adult and child as a possible pre-election goodie for voters.
The move will be formally unveiled in Bethlenfalvy's economic update.
"He's tight as skin on a grape," the premier joked about his more fiscally conservative treasurer.
Although the exact amount of the "tax rebate" cheques is still being finalized by government officials, it will likely be at least $200. The payments would be tax-free.
That suggests the scheme could cost the provincial treasury about $3.2 billion when the money is distributed in January or February.
Sources - speaking confidentially to discuss a plan that has not yet been shared with all of cabinet, let alone caucus - said the timing would help Ontarians when their pay packets are reduced due to employment insurance deductions and when post-Christmas credit card bills are due.
But the cash injection could also give Ford a political boost as he considers calling an election more than a year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
"Put money into people's pockets and they'll go out and do something as simple as going out for dinner, maybe buying your kids sneakers or clothes, or maybe putting a little renovation on your house," he told the Empire Club.
"And they might do that because they have money in their pockets. The same with businesses. If you put money into businesses, they'll invest into their people, into technology, into their infrastructure, and they grow. That's what creates economic strength."
In this month's Abacus Data tracking poll for the Star, the Tories were at 44 per cent while Bonnie Crombie's Liberals were at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats were at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens were at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians on Oct. 3-10 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
All three opposition leaders panned Ford's cheque gambit.
"Ford, if you really want to make life better, here's some advice: Build homes, hire doctors, fix schools, and give people a fair shot at planning for the future," said Stiles.
"Ontarians see right through these stunts from a government that's out of touch, out of gas, and clearly out of ideas."
Crombie, who attended Ford's speech, dismissed the plan as "another one-time gimmick from a premier who has promised and failed to cut taxes for more than six years."
"Rather than try to buy your vote in an election year, Ontario's Liberals will cut taxes for all middle-class families - permanently. More to come," she said, hinting at her party's election platform.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner suggested any such payouts should be means-tested.
"Imagine: Galen Weston getting a $200 cheque in the mail," said Schreiner, referring to the grocery tycoon.
"Ontarians need a government that's in their corner - not one that's trying to buy them out," said Schreiner.
"Some ways we could actually make life more affordable: build more homes, protect renters and invest in deeply affordable housing; stamp out oil and gas greed to bring down home energy costs; invest in public health care and education so Ontarians don't have to pay for things that should be covered publicly," he said.
While inflation has dipped to 1.6 per cent and interest rates are dropping, the Tories note that grocery prices remain high in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic and housing and energy costs continue to rise.
Ford's plan is a reprise of what former premier Mike Harris's Tories did in 2000 when then-finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques. Ford pointedly gave a shout-out from the podium to Harris, who was also in attendance Thursday.
That $200 payment a generation ago, which came when Eves announced a budget surplus, would be worth around $335 today, according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator.
Bethlenfalvy's update in a fortnight will not show a balanced budget, but is expected to signal the fiscal situation is much better than it was in his March 26 budget when he projected a surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25.
That was a $10-billion swing from the $200-million surplus the treasurer had forecast last year.

Document 10 of 500

Justin Trudeau to shuffle cabinet as four more ministers say they won't seek re-election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-shuffle-cabinet-as-four-more/docview/3118094457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+to+shuffle+cabinet+as+four+more+ministers+say+they+won%27t+seek+re-election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will shuffle his cabinet in the coming weeks as four more cabinet ministers have told the Liberal leader they will not run again in the next campaign.
Over the last three months, a total of six of Trudeau's cabinet ministers or 15 per cent have bailed ahead of a federal election which polls suggest the Liberals will lose. The drip-drip of names has meant that the coming shuffle will be the third in as many months.
The shuffle is not expected to occur next week, contrary to early reports, according to a senior source, but could come quickly in the weeks following that.
Parliament is in its Thanksgiving week recess, but MPs return next week, when Trudeau will face a restive caucus, with reports of more than two dozen, and possibly as many as 40 MPs, getting ready to challenge Trudeau to step aside as Liberal leader.
Hamilton's Filomena Tassi, the minister for the federal economic development agency for southern Ontario, announced Thursday she won't run for reasons "that are deeply personal. It is time for me to be closer to home with my family."
Tassi follows National Revenue Minister Marie-Claude Bibeau expected to run for mayor of Sherbrooke, and two others who have advised Trudeau of their intention to leave politics: Northern Affairs Minister Dan Vandal who is in charge of economic development for the Prairies and the North, and Sport Minister Carla Qualtrough. Their departures were first reported by Radio-Canada.
Last month Trudeau's Quebec lieutenant Pablo Rodriguez left the transport portfolio, and Liberal caucus, to make a run for the provincial Liberal Party of Quebec leadership - a departure Trudeau filled in the interim with Treasury Board President Anita Anand. Likewise, he had a quick shuffle in July when Labour Minister Seamus O'Regan advised he wouldn't run again, also for "personal reasons." Steven MacKinnon replaced O'Regan.
Trudeau has had a challenging fall, with polls continuing to show his party struggling.
After returning from a Francophonie summit in Paris where Israel's military engagement with Hezbollah in Lebanon dominated talks, and before leaving for the ASEAN summit in Laos last week, the prime minister called off a planned trip next week to Samoa to take part in the Commonwealth leaders' summit.
Trudeau returned from Laos on the weekend, after a meeting in Germany on Ukraine was cancelled by U.S. President Joe Biden, only to confront new reports, including by the Star, of brewing caucus unrest.
All that amid a Canada-India diplomatic rupture as a result of the RCMP bombshell allegations against Indian diplomats and consular officials - and Trudeau's appearance at the public inquiry into foreign interference where the prime minister accused his main rival, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, of irresponsibly refusing to get the required security clearance to get briefings on how his own party is at risk.
Some MPs who spoke to the Star this week defended Trudeau in the face of growing caucus dissent, arguing those calling for his resignation are helping Poilievre.
"We are wasting our energy by focusing inwards rather than focusing on the needs of our constituents," said Ottawa MP Yasir Naqvi.
"I respect Sean (Casey) but I disagree with him ... Justin Trudeau has more to offer." Naqvi was referring to the PEI MP who went public this week saying it was time for Trudeau to step aside.
Another MP said it was "disappointing" to hear about these efforts in the media when those conversations should be held in private.
Others said they have not seen a letter reportedly calling for Trudeau's resignation and declined to take a stance on his leadership, but said they agree that conversations should be had about the future of the party. If even a small group of MPs sign a letter calling on the prime minister to resign, he will be put in a tough situation, they said.
With files from Mark Ramzy
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appears as a witness at the federal inquiry into foreign interference in Ottawa on Oct. 16, 2024. Trudeau is expected to shuffle his cabinet in the coming weeks.

NDP calls on integrity commissioner to look into Doug Ford's Ontario Place spa deal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-calls-on-integrity-commissioner-look-into/docview/3117870059/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+calls+on+integrity+commissioner+to+look+into+Doug+Ford%27s+Ontario+Place+spa+deal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP Leader Marit Stiles is asking the province's integrity commissioner to look into the government's spa deal at Ontario Place.
Stiles has filed a complaint "calling for an investigation into the Therme deal," the NDP said in a written release. "The complaint includes evidence suggesting that Therme received preferential treatment from the Ford government and seeks an investigation into the minister of infrastructure's involvement."
The NDP says that "over the past two years, we have collected evidence suggesting a deeply troubling pattern of behaviour surrounding the Ford government's deal with Therme. We have reason to believe that Therme received preferential treatment from this government when they were awarded the 95-year-long lease to build a luxury spa at Ontario Place."
Grace Lee, Premier Doug Ford's director of media relations, called the move "nothing more than the NDP reaching for headlines with a frivolous complaint largely based on conspiracy theories. We look forward to making Ontario Place a world-class destination once again."
Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma, who was not minister at the time the deal was struck, said she looks forward "to assisting the integrity commissioner."
Unlike Stiles, she added "I have too much respect for the commissioner to play politics with his office. I will continue to focus my efforts on getting shovels in the ground to make Ontario Place a place that families can enjoy once again."
In its submission to the integrity commissioner, the NDP says the bidding process for Ontario Place development was not a typically competitive process and that, among other irregularities, did not follow the normal bidding rules for larger projects. It also says the deadline for applications was extended, during which time Therme submitted its bid.
Earlier this month, the Ford government released details of the 95-year lease with the Austrian firm, which includes 1,600 parking spots for the spa resort, and a clause that will force the province to pay up to $2.2 million a year if those spots aren't in place when it opens or by 2030, whichever comes first.
Plans for Ontario Place's redevelopment include the spa, a water park and a new home for the Ontario Science Centre.
Therme's lease is for 75 years with an option to renew for another 20.
Questions the NDP says it is hoping the Office of the Integrity Commissioner will answer include "was the procurement process fair? Why is the government using taxpayer money to build Therme a parking lot after telling other bidders it would not consider proposals with publicly funded facilities? Billions of dollars and a cherished public asset are at stake. We are bringing our case to the integrity commissioner so we can finally get some answers on this shady scheme."
CAPTION: The NDP want an investigation into the Ford government's deal made with Therme to build a spa at Ontario Place.

Document 12 of 500

Ford government aims to fast-track construction of Highway 413, Bradford Bypass and Garden City Skyway

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-aims-fast-track-construction/docview/3117593197/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+aims+to+fast-track+construction+of+Highway+413%2C+Bradford+Bypass+and+Garden+City+Skyway&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With an early provincial election looming, Premier Doug Ford's government wants to fast-track construction of Highway 413, the Bradford Bypass and a twin section of the Garden City Skyway in St. Catharines.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said legislation called the "Building Highways Faster Act" will be introduced after the legislature returns next week from an extended summer break to provide for "around-the-clock, 24/7 construction."
Taking aim at potential protests against the two highway projects which have been heavily opposed by environmentalists and others for using up prime Greenbelt farmland and threatening endangered or rare species, the bill would set "new penalties for obstructing access for field investigations" or "damaging equipment," Sarkaria said Thursday in Caledon.
It would also "accelerate" the process for buyouts or expropriations of farmland or other properties needed for the projects and "streamline" relocations of utility lines.
There will also be legislation to establish an "accelerated environmental assessment process" for Highway 413, from Highway 401 at Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan, allowing the province to proceed with early work on the controversial roadway rejected by a previous Liberal government.
"Projects like this need to get built," Sarkaria said, citing "unprecedented growth" in Ontario with projections the population will increase two million by 2031 and put existing 400-series highways at or over capacity within a decade.
"Gridlock suffocates our roadways."
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre) said the proposed legislation is dangerous.
"Slashing environmental laws, paving over our Greenbelt, Ontarians have made it clear that they're not OK with this," she added in a statement, referring to the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal under investigation by the RCMP.
"For the government to be trying it yet again with Highway 413 shows how they'll do anything to help their inner circle over the people of this province. We also need to build communities that don't require you to travel a great distance to get to work or school, by building more homes in existing neighbourhoods and especially around transit hubs."
The Bradford Bypass will stretch from Highway 400 in the Bradford area east to Highway 404 in a bid to ease congestion between there and Toronto. The Garden City Skyway traverses the Welland Canal on a busy stretch of the Queen Elizabeth Way. Plans call for a new four-lane, 2.2-kilometre bridge in addition to a rehabilitation of the deck on the existing bridge.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie reiterated her opposition to Highway 413 earlier this week.
"I have never supported the 413."
However, the highway promise is credited with helping Ford's PCs win all the Brampton ridings in the 2022 provincial election, taking three seats from the New Democrats.
The premier has repeatedly refused to rule out a snap election as early as March, instead of waiting until the next provincewide vote slated for June 2026. His government has also taken aim at bike lanes, pledging legislation that would require municipalities to get provincial approval before taking out any lanes of traffic on major roadways, and has stepped up feel-good government advertising about Ontario's economy.
As the Star's Robert Benzie reported, the province is planning to send cheques of at least $200 to 16 million Ontarians to offset the rising cost of living.
CAPTION: The government's legislation would"accelerate" the process for buyouts or expropriations of farmland or other properties needed for the projects.

Document 13 of 500

Appeal in landmark youth-led climate case gets go-ahead from Ontario court

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/appeal-landmark-youth-led-climate-case-gets-go/docview/3117592814/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Appeal+in+landmark+youth-led+climate+case+gets+go-ahead+from+Ontario+court&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a landmark case, Ontario's top court has ruled that an appeal from seven young Ontarians suing the provincial government over its new greenhouse gas emissions targets can proceed.
However, the Court of Appeal declined to rule on the case's substance, extending a legal battle in Canada's courts over their role in overseeing government climate action.
In a decision released Thursday, three judges - Lois B. Roberts, Steve A. Coroza and Sally A. Gomery - sided with the appellants in Mathur v. Ontario that a Superior Court of Justice judge "erred" in deciding the young people's case was a "positive rights case" in an April 2023 decision.
"This is not a positive rights case. The application does not seek to impose on Ontario any new positive obligations to combat climate change," Judges Roberts, Coroza and Gomery wrote. In legal studies and political theory, positive rights refers to rights that give people something, such as a right to have an education or a right to housing.
But the judges fell short of determining the merits of the case's arguments, writing the appeals court was "not well placed" to make such a ruling. Instead, they called for a new hearing, which could be overseen by the original judge or a different one.
Thursday's ruling gave the seven young appellants - lead applicant Sophia Mathur, Madison Dyck, Shelby Gagnon, Beze Gray, Zoë Keary-Matzner, Alex Neufeldt and Shaelyn Wabegijig - a small victory in their lawsuit against the provincial government that was first filed in 2019.
At issue - which lawyers for the seven young people argued before the Court of Appeal earlier this year - was Ontario's weakened 2030 emissions targets passed by Premier Doug Ford's then-newly elected Progressive Conservative government in 2018 that reduced goals from 37 per cent lower emissions below 2005 levels to 30 per cent. According to the appellants claim, this reduction violates the charter rights of current youth and future generations, specifically sections 7 and 15 that provide the right to life, liberty and security of the person and equal protection under the law respectively.
Mathur v. Ontario is the first case of its kind to be tried in Canada, and Thursday's ruling could shape decisions in other cases facing courts in Canada and internationally asking judiciaries to more actively oversee government climate plans.
In the U.S., judges have ruled in favour of young people from Montana and Hawaii challenging governments for their alleged climate in action. And in 2019, the top court in the Netherlands ruled the government has a duty to protect its citizens from the potentially devastating effects of climate change, including by further cutting emissions.
Meanwhile, a similar case, La Rose v. Her Majesty the Queen, is awaiting a trial date at the federal level in Canada as 15 young people sue the Canadian government on similar grounds as the seven young people in Mathur v. Ontario.
With files from Marco Chown Oved.
CAPTION: The seven appellants, centre, and their lawyers from the firm Ecojustice photographed together in 2020. They have been in the court system since 2019 making their case.

Subject: Court hearings & proceedings; Emissions; Climate change; Equal rights; Judges & magistrates

Document 14 of 500

Will Doug Ford's bike-lane legislation torpedo a plan to improve safety on this Toronto road?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-doug-fords-bike-lane-legislation-torpedo/docview/3117580926/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+Doug+Ford%27s+bike-lane+legislation+torpedo+a+plan+to+improve+safety+on+this+Toronto+road%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The fate of a years-long effort to improve safety on a dangerous stretch of Toronto road is up in the air, after Premier Doug Ford's government announced legislation that would require provincial approval for projects that replace car lanes with cycling routes.
This week, the city released its final study of proposed changes to Parkside Drive, a four-lane arterial on the eastern edge of High Park that's used as busy connector to the Gardiner Expressway, and which locals have long complained is unsafe. Council voted to explore additional safety measures there in 2021, after a chain-reaction crash killed two seniors.
The report states Parkside's current design doesn't meet city standards for sidewalk or car lane widths, or cyclist protection. It recommends a redesign to the roughly two-kilometre roadway. That redesign would include removing one of two southbound traffic lanes and replacing it with a two-way cycle track on the west side of Parkside, from Bloor Street West to Lake Shore Boulevard West.
According to the report, the overhaul would "improve the comfort and safety of all road users," particularly cyclists and pedestrians, and support Toronto's Vision Zero road safety goals.
But on Tuesday, the same day the city released the plan, Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria confirmed the province intends to introduce legislation that would require cities to get approval from Queen's Park for bike lane projects built at the expense of car lanes.
The Ford government said the "explosion of bike lanes" in Ontario cities has increased traffic congestion, and that the law would bring "informed decision-making" to the planning process.
Details of the legislation have yet to be revealed, but the province said in a press release that municipalities will be "required to demonstrate the proposed bike lanes won't have a negative impact on vehicle traffic."
On Parkside, a traffic analysis included in the city report predicted car travel times would increase by one to three minutes during peak periods after the redesign, while motor vehicle volumes would decrease by about 35 per cent.
Asked whether the province plans to intervene on Parkside, Dakota Brasier, a spokesperson for Sarkaria, said: "Our position is clear. We support a common-sense approach when it comes to bike lanes - which does not include removing a lane of traffic in the most congested city in North America.
"We will be reviewing the city's plan, as well as all other information from municipalities regarding bike lanes that have been installed in the past five years."
A city spokesperson declined to speculate on how the Parkside bike lanes could be affected by the new law, saying the municipality will wait until the government unveils the wording of its legislation.
Parkside resident Faraz Gholizadeh supports the city plan, and says it's already taken too long for the municipality to address high-speed traffic on the street. He wants the province to butt out.
"It's unfortunate that they're putting their nose into something that doesn't really involve them. This is a municipal issue," said Gholizadeh, who co-chairs the Safe Parkside group.
Gholizadeh argued the bike lanes won't just help cyclists, but will improve safety for all road users by slowing drivers. The report says the redesign will help "speed management" by narrowing car lane widths to city standards.
"It's never been a debate about bike lanes or no bike lanes. It's always been a debate about safety," Gholizadeh said. "Our provincial government seems to only be focused on people's commute times."
Parkside is used for about 21,000 motor vehicle trips per day, according to the city report. There have been almost 1,500 collisions on the street over the past decade, five of which resulted in serious injuries and two of which resulted in deaths. They included the multi-vehicle crash in October 2021 that killed Valdemar and Fatima Avila, whose car was crushed from behind.
In response to local residents' concerns, the city had already introduced "Watch Your Speed" signs, photo radar and new traffic signals, as well as reduced the speed limit from 50 to 40 km/h, among other changes.
In addition to the bike lanes, the new report recommends safety improvements at the Bloor and Lake Shore intersections, and enhanced connections to the Martin Goodman Trail. Where possible, left-turn lanes would be installed at intersections to allow for eastbound traffic flow. Parkside's two northbound lanes, one of which is already dedicated on-street parking, would be largely unchanged. Together, the measures would cost about $7.5 million, with installation scheduled to begin by 2026.
The report is scheduled to be debated at the city's infrastructure committee next Tuesday, before going to council for final approval.
The redesign has its critics. A city survey found 54 per cent of respondents were very supportive or supportive of the cycle track, while 42 per cent were opposed to some degree.
Local councillor Gord Perks (Ward 4, Parkdale-High Park), who has been working with staff and residents on the plan, conceded it would require "trade-offs." But he argued most people would be OK with adding a few minutes to their commute if it would prevent deadly collisions.
Perks warned it would be unwise for the province to torpedo the Parkside proposal. "Having a dangerous transportation system doesn't benefit anybody," he said.
CAPTION: Parkside Drive is a 1.9-kilometre roadway on the eastern edge of High Park that is used as busy connector to the Gardiner Expressway, and which local residents have long complained is unsafe.

Subject: Traffic flow; Legislation; Debates; Roads & highways; Traffic control; Bicycling

Title: Will Doug Ford's bike-lane legislation torpedo a plan to improve safety on this Toronto road?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 15 of 500

A bike lane in limbo + Doug Ford's pre-election cheques

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/bike-lane-limbo-doug-fords-pre-election-cheques/docview/3117580924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+bike+lane+in+limbo+%2B+Doug+Ford%27s+pre-election+cheques&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, Oct. 17 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on why a planned bike lane is in limbo, some pre-election cash from Doug Ford and a hint of Tory MPs tied to foreign meddling.
DON'T MISSA deadly Toronto road may not get its planned bike laneRoad safety measures proposed for a dangerous stretch of road on the eastern edge of High Park are in limbo, as city officials wait for more details on Doug Ford's new bike lane plan. Council voted to explore safety measures for Parkside Drive after a deadly crash that killed two seniors in 2021. Here's why a new provincial plan might stop any changes in their tracks.
The plan? The proposedredesign would include removing one traffic lane and replacing it with a two-way cycle track. A traffic analysis predicted car travel times would increase during peak periods and vehicle volumes would go down.
Bike lane battle: The back-and-forth over bike lanes is playing out in the streets, too. Residents and cyclists clashed outside a meeting about bike lanes on Bloor Street West hosted by Coun. Amber Morley last night.Doug Ford's pre-election plan includes cheques for millions of OntariansThe premier may soon send pre-election presents straight to your mailbox: a tax-free cheque of at least $200. Sources told the Star's Robert Benzie that the premier's gambit will officially be announced at the end of the month and could cost the provincial coffers about $3.2 billion early next year. Here's when - and more importantly, why - Ford is mailing out free money.
Déjà vu? Ford hasn't shied away from pre-election spending in the past. He eliminated licence plate sticker renewal fees and scrapped tolls on the 412 and 418 just before the 2022 election.Which MPs are connected to foreign meddling? Justin Trudeau knowsIn the hot seat of a foreign interference inquiry, the PM revealed that he knows which Conservative MPs have potential ties to foreign interference activity. Trudeau turned the tables on Pierre Poilievre, who has refused to obtain the security clearance needed to receive that intelligence. Here's what Poilievre had to say in response.
The matter at hand: Trudeau also revealed that he asked Canada's intelligence agencies to look into India's ties to a Canadian's murder.
Sending back up: U.S. and British allies want India to co-operate with the ongoing investigation into allegations of a co-ordinated intimidation campaign on Canadian soil.
Robin V. Sears's take: India will pay heavily for deliberately crossing a red line in nations across the globe.WHAT ELSE
