Document 16 of 500

Metrolinx shakeup was long overdue - just like its Eglinton Crosstown LRT

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/metrolinx-shakeup-was-long-overdue-just-like/docview/3117298073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Metrolinx+shakeup+was+long+overdue+-+just+like+its+Eglinton+Crosstown+LRT&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the news broke about a shakeup at Metrolinx this week, many Torontonians were likely shouting "it's about time."
The executive at the provincial transit agency who was in charge of "bringing new light rail lines into service" and the one in charge of planning have moved on (or been moved on) to pursue other opportunities. Given they haven't yet brought any new light rail lines into service, many will see this as overdue.
"Clearly heads are rolling because their vehicles haven't been," Toronto councillor Josh Matlow told the Star Tuesday. Amen, a lot of Torontonians will say.
Could this mean that finally something will get finished?
Earlier this month, I spent some time wandering out on Eglinton East near Warden Avenue, and it felt especially noteworthy that LRT trains were running every few minutes along the corridor. No one could board them, of course - this was still training and testing. But surely, I thought, the fact that it appears to be basically operating full steam means it's just about ready to open?
But then I remembered a day well over a year ago when I spent an afternoon watching teenagers play baseball at Eglinton West and Black Creek Drive, when those LRT trains were motoring along every few minutes just beyond the outfield fences ("Hit a train, win a steak," we shouted at the batters). And back then, I had thought the same.
And yet here we are today: there is still no opening date, and no hint at when one is coming beyond Premier Doug Ford's recent acknowledgment that it is looking less and less likely that it will be this year. It's been more than two years since I wrote a column exasperated that the opening date - then already two years later than once promised - had been kicked off into the indefinite future. Since then, the future has only grown to seem even more indefinite.
Literally, for years, we've seen that line in service, trains running empty for training and testing, a visible reminder of the ride across the centre of the city we cannot take, taunting us - haunting us, a ghost subway, the central character in a transit construction horror show.
So yeah, maybe it's long past time for a change of leadership among the folks who were supposed to be "bringing new light rail lines into service," especially now that it is clear that the Finch West LRT (originally proposed to open in 2013, and then 2020 and then 2023) is in a similar state of limbo, and beyond Toronto's borders, the Mississauga Hurontario LRT line that was supposed to open this fall has now also been placed into the no-opening-date-projected rip in the time-space continuum that has become Metrolinx's signature scheduling trick.
None of us wants to be that dude who celebrates people losing their jobs. But c'mon. A toast to something - anything - happening, some hint of accountability from an agency in charge of tens of billions of dollars of region-defining infrastructure projects, might feel appropriate.
But just as you're about to pop the cork, a further question occurs: does it seem likely that the person in charge of "bringing new light rail lines into service" would be replaced if a new light rail line coming into service was imminent? If the opening date of even one of these too-long-awaited projects was just weeks or even months away, wouldn't you the team that struggled through it all just show up to cut the ribbon, congratulate themselves, and then use the milestone as a perfect occasion to pass the torch and change the guard for the next set of projects?
More practically, would you put someone new in charge if their most immediate responsibility was going to be to declare victory and cut those ribbons? Or would you do it because there was some real, difficult work that needed to be done that required a new vision and voice and work plan?
Which is to say, is it possible that this shuffle indicates we are not all that close to seeing any of these LRT lines in operation?
God help us.
Many of us have been referring to the Eglinton Crosstown in the conditional tense for a while - you know: if it opens, rather than when. But we have been at least half-joking, right? Long experience has taught us to take nothing for granted and not let ourselves expect much. But surely we expected, even as Metrolinx head Phil Verster and Premier Doug Ford refused to speculate about dates, that we were looking at months rather than years.
We are years into that expectation. And now the GTA has at least three transit lines in the same Twilight Zone. A shakeup of the senior staff surely does seem long overdue. It's just far too late for it to offer any real sense hope.
CAPTION: Mount Dennis station on the new LRT line, showing construction for the new west extension.

Subject: Light rail transportation; Shakeups

Business indexing term: Subject: Shakeups

Company / organization: Name: Toronto Star; NAICS: 513110

Document 17 of 500

Justin Trudeau's point man on Canada's crumbling immigration consensus

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-point-man-on-canadas-crumbling/docview/3117298068/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+point+man+on+Canada%27s+crumbling+immigration+consensus&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In early September, the U.S. magazine Compact ran an article with the headline, "How Canada Turned against Immigration." The headline was wildly overstated. And yet, there has been a change - in policy, rhetoric and public mood - that has coincided, roughly, with the appointment of Marc Miller, a Montreal MP, as Minster of Immigration, Refugees, and Citizenship in July of 2023.
This year, Miller imposed a two-year cap on international-student visas, reducing the numbers by roughly 35 per cent. His office has pushed through changes to the temporary foreign worker program, and he has suggested that further changes are in the offing. The federal government has also indicated that it is reviewing its immigration target numbers for 2025 and 2026.
In a wide-ranging interview in early October, edited for space and clarity, Miller denied that the government is capitulating to anti-immigrant sentiment. The changes are motivated, he said, by an enduring belief in the value of immigration. To ensure that Canadians continue to look favourably on the immigration system, Miller argued, reform is necessary - even if it means making hard choices and acknowledging past mistakes.
In a survey from September 2022, just 27 per cent of Canadians agreed with the statement "there's too much immigration in Canada." A year later, 44 per cent agreed. What do you think is behind that change?
The consensus is still there around the value of immigration, but people want a sense that the system is under control. We've had some areas, like international students, where the system's gotten overheated. There's lots of fault to go around. But after identifying who's to blame, it's important to actually do something. That's where I play a role.
You say that people want a sense that the system is under control? What does "under control" mean?
An immigration system that makes sense is one where the volumes are acceptable to the population and they meet a number of needs: labour needs, family reunification objectives, and the kinds of lofty humanitarian goals that Canada has been proud of. But the economic motives have to be clear, and labour markets need to be properly aligned with immigration rates. The criticism is that we have been too slow to adapt. And that's something I take to heart.
It seems that one reason people are getting twitchy about immigration is that we've had a lot of it recently, particularly after COVID. In 2022, the Canadian population grew by more than a million people, and 96 per cent of them were newcomers. Why did the numbers tick up so much?
Our reaction after COVID was the right one: With severe market shortages, we chose to have higher permanent residency levels, to convert more people from temporary to permanent residents, and to take in temporary residents in areas like the low-income stream and international students, to the point where international students occupy half of the temporary resident spaces. That's historically high, and it's something we need to get control over.
So what you're seeking is an equilibrium?
We had a perfect storm with a hopefully once-in-a-lifetime pandemic, massive labour shortages, and the opportunity for a pro-immigration government to adjust nimbly. The IMF and Bank of Canada both said that we've avoided at least two recessions almost solely as a result of immigration. That's not something to spit at.
When were the two recessions?
Well, we avoided them so...
But when would those two recessions have happened?
In the last four years. And that is largely, according to the IMF, because of labour, because of the positive aspects of immigration.
What I'm getting from you is that you're always correcting for what recently happened.
We had the possibility of a COVID recession. We had these real labour market shortages, so we had to correct for that. Now we've got a lot of people coming in, and it's putting strain on housing prices, on infrastructure. People are getting anxious, so we're correcting for that. But there are also some long-term trends that are true. We do need to make the labour force younger overall. It's impossible to do that without a higher birth rate or immigrants. The math doesn't work.
Right. There are only two ways to increase the population.
The issue is having the proper intellectual debate and not turning it into value statements about the race, colour, or creed of the people coming in, but also without painting people as racist when they express views you disagree with.
How do you talk to people who are anxious about immigration in ways that neither demonize them nor concede every one of their points?
People want to hear what their politicians are thinking and draw their own conclusions. They don't want to be lectured at, and they don't want their fears dismissed. To the extent politicians can do this, they should just have chill one-on-one conversations with people about their apprehensions. I have relatives and friends who have different views than me on immigration. I try to not walk away from those conversations without at least understanding the other side.
In Canada, politicians often get defined by the zealots in their party, as opposed to the reasonable people. When you have a "big tent" party, you have a wide range of views, but you often get associated, publicly, with the most polarizing views.
What are the most polarizing views in your party?
I won't betray conversations I've had privately, but you could run the spectrum of people, from folks who want to slash immigration rates in half to folks who want to bring in more immigrants. And this is just my party, right? So think about the views that must exist in other parties.
Is it safe to say that immigration numbers are likely to come down in the near future?
I can't speak to that. But there's a conversation about it. There's a world in which it happens.
The immigration targets were originally set for 500,000 people in 2025 and 500,000 in 2026. Those figures are being potentially revised, yes?
Yeah. We have to be more nimble than we've been in the past. I don't think there's value in doubling down on assumptions that were true three or four years ago.
But the numbers aren't going up, right?
I certainly don't see it. But again, these are conversations happening in cabinet. They're happening in caucus. And the views there are wide ranging.
Reuters recently reported that Canada has been scrutinizing international travelers more carefully. Last June, the report said, we deemed more visa holders inadmissible than we have since COVID. Last July, we turned back more people at ports of entry than at any point since 2019. Are these changes happening in response to directives from your office?
The short answer is yes. I don't take any pleasure in denying visas, but this is something that had to be done. Going into the summer, I looked at the data I was getting from my department and realized that the system needed an injection of discipline.
Can you give me an example?
I can't, because then people will game the system. We did tighten the screws a bit, though. We saw a significant influx of people coming in from India and Bangladesh, and it was something we had to act on. We also looked at the quality of applicants that were coming before the Immigration and Refugee Board. When you see a number of rejections coming from one country that is different in scale from other countries, you realize something is amiss. That was the case with respect to applications from Mexico.
In 2016, the government relaxed the visa requirement for travelers from Mexico. This year, it reinstated the visa requirement. Was it a mistake to have relaxed that requirement in the first place?
I'm not arrogant enough to put myself in the shoes of whatever minister was in place at the time. But the policy worked for a while, and it wasn't working now. Once the visa requirement was re-imposed, we saw 80 per cent fewer asylum claims from Mexico.
Let's talk about international students. In theory, the reason we admit so many is because we want people from around the world to bring their smarts and work ethic to our universities. In practice, many international students are paying inflated prices for a tenth-rate education because they know that when their program of study is complete they can eventually apply for permanent residency.
The program had become a bit of a racket, and I probably should have acted sooner. My biggest mistake was to trust people for too long - to trust provinces and the institutions they regulate. Auditors general have said time and time again that Ontario and B.C. had to get a grip on things. Those are the two provinces where international-student placements had become a runaway train. And they were all promising something - permanent residency or Canadian citizenship - that was not guaranteed.
What should the provinces have been doing?
They should have regulated their designated learning institutions, put criteria as to the solvency of the potential students, put the infrastructure in place to house the students, and been more rigorous about which colleges get a designated-learning-institution badge to recruit international students.
It does seem to me that the federal government was in on the hustle too. I watched a video ad, targeted at prospective international students, that Immigration, Refugees, and Citizenship Canada was putting out, which basically said, "Come here to study. There's a good chance you'll get to stay."
We made things attractive. And things should be attractive. But when things get out of hand, you owe it to Canadians to get them under control.
But do you think the federal government should rein in the messaging that basically says "you can stay here"?
I think so. (Do you think) that message should not be implicit in the pull?
I think it was explicit sometimes.
I've been in government for 10 years. No one can ever say that government messaging is responsible for everything.
The temporary foreign worker program in Canada is also being revised. Can you say more about the kinds of revisions we can expect?
We have been heavily solicited by industry to make sure there was more access to low wage labour, including foreign labour. And so we made some changes. There was the 30 per cent change, to make sure that, in some industries, three out of ten people could be temporary foreign workers. But in areas where temporary foreign workers don't make as much sense, we saw a reduction to 10 per cent.
In which industries were the reductions?
Largely hospitality. Go into a Tim Hortons, and you will see a lot of temporary foreign workers. When you take a step back, the questions you need to pose are, 'How are we filling needs? Are we artificially depressing wages? How is an influx of temporary foreign workers contributing to that trend, particularly when you have a labour force already here?' There will need to be further adjustments to the program, including in the availability of asylum seekers who have work permits. But we also don't want to harm markets that are important to regional economies, like fish processing or food processing.
One of the criticisms of the temporary foreign worker program is that visas tend to be attached to a single employer. It gives the employer this incredible degree of leverage, which can lead to abuse. Is that policy of attaching temporary visas to an individual employer under revision?
We're looking at it. We're conscious of the fact that employers spend a lot of capital just to get people into the country, and so you wouldn't want them to just springboard immediately. Currently, there's a program in my department to ensure that, if a person leaves an abusive employer, they can have an open work permit or the ability to move. That program is increasingly being used, but it isn't a complete answer to the problem.
Is there a possibility of a ramped-up inspection regimen?
Yes, that work is under way.
Another criticism I've seen is that workplace inspections are often announced in advance, which means abuse of temporary foreign workers can be hidden. In the future, might you move toward unannounced inspections?
I can't speak to the specifics, but our work is ongoing.
I remember the 2019 election, when Maxime Bernier was running for the first time as leader of the People's Party of Canada. He felt his opponents, including in the Liberal Party, unfairly painted him as a racist. He was not in favour of banning immigration; he just wanted to reduce the overall target numbers. You've said that you too are open to reducing numbers. What's the difference between you and him?
People like him can't turn around and say, "I was right along," because they see something that they feel vindicates their positions. He's misused statistics, and he's headed up a party that's openly xenophobic. There are very few people who have ever accused our party, in its current iteration, of being xenophobic.
When you look at immigration rates throughout Canadian history, you see these peaks and troughs. You have a surge of immigration under Laurier, and then a decline under Borden and early King. Then you have another postwar surge, followed by a decline under Diefenbaker. And then you have this slow build, beginning with Pearson and lasting into the present day. Do you predict that, broadly, that surge is going to continue?
If you look strictly at the facts, we need immigration, barring some wonderful initiative that causes a baby boom. I have three kids. It costs a lot to have kids. Young people are refusing to have kids for affordability and a variety of other reasons. Our government has been pretty good with daycare affordability and supporting women who wish to enter the job market. We hope to create conditions that are favourable to people having families.
My hunch is that those initiatives, even if successful, won't dramatically increase the birth rate. Choices around kids are lifestyle related. I don't think you can legislate lifestyle choices.
As a party, we've been proud of saying, 'We don't tell people what to do in their bedrooms.' But the reality is that, absent a baby boom, we need immigration to keep our labour force young. Do I think the trend is always going to go upwards? I don't know. We still have a carefully built pro-immigration consensus among the major federal parties. As much as I criticize the Conservatives, as much as I accuse them of playing footsie with people with extreme thoughts, they have not undertaken yet to break the immigration consensus.
I have a non-policy question, one that I personally wrestle with. There's a strain of left-wing utopian thought that says, We should just have open borders. Obviously, no one's doing that.
You will find some folks in the NDP who favour open borders.
Okay, maybe you will. And, in their defence, I think that, on a purely ethical level, they're right. Look at me. I get all the privileges of being Canadian just because I was born here. And somebody else gets none of those privileges, just because they were born elsewhere. That just seems wrong, doesn't it?
That argument comes from the heart, and I respect it. I think we can all agree that those of us who were born here were really, really lucky. But it is naïve to think that we can have open borders. Control is important to the integrity of our immigration programs.
There's a critique out there - I associate it with the journalist David Frum - that too much immigration leads to destabilization, which leads to backlash and far-right nativism. Is that risk on your mind?
I think we do have to look at the impact that high volumes have on people's sense of fairness. I think Canadians are the most decent people in the world. But I am not naïve, and I'm not deaf to some of the things I'm hearing. Showing Canadians that you're reasonable, that you're willing to consider many options, without being cruel or inhumane, is an important thing to do.
CAPTION: Marc Miller,Canada's Minister of Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship, says his biggest mistake on international students was " to trust people too long."
CREDIT: Simon Lewsen, Contributor

Subject: Citizenship; Foreign students; Passports & visas; Politics; Politicians; Immigration; Labor market; Recessions

Title: Justin Trudeau's point man on Canada's crumbling immigration consensus

Document 18 of 500

Emotions high at bike lane meeting: Local councillor, community members discuss improving cycling infrastructure, traffic issues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/emotions-high-at-bike-lane-meeting/docview/3117296900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Emotions+high+at+bike+lane+meeting%3A+Local+councillor%2C+community+members+discuss+improving+cycling+infrastructure%2C+traffic+issues&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tensions ran high Wednesday night at an Etobicoke meeting held by Coun. Amber Morley to discuss bike lanes on Bloor Street West.
About 100 cyclists rolled in a half-hour before the 7 p.m. meeting at Etobicoke Collegiate Institute after rallying in High Park in support of the bike lanes.
They arrived blocking a lane of vehicular traffic, with a motorist stuck behind them leaning on the horn - a microcosm of the long-simmering strife between cyclists who support the lanes and residents who say the lanes are not well-used, are difficult to navigate for the disabled and the elderly, and have ruined the village atmosphere of the neighbourhood.
"It's great to see so many people on bikes come out to Coun. Morley's community meeting and show support for safe and protected cycling infrastructure like the Bloor-Street bikeways," said Michael Longfield, executive director of Cycle Toronto.
Inside the meeting, Morley said while she is committed to improving the bike lanes on Bloor West in Etobicoke, she does not support removing them.
"We acknowledge this is a very challenging issue and has had a lot of pain points for residents," said Morley (Ward 3 Etobicoke-Lakeshore), adding later that the bike lanes are a necessary part of the plan to keep people moving in the area, which is about to experience a steep increase in population.
"This is about building a sustainable city we can be proud of," she said.
Jacquelyn Hayward, the city's director of transportation project design and management, acknowledged it's taking longer for vehicular traffic moving through the area, citing an increase in travel times of between three-and-a-half and four-and-a-half minutes.
"We aren't satisfied with these numbers," said Hayward, saying the city is looking at changes such as syncing traffic lights to improve travel times.
Toronto Fire Deputy Chief Jim Jessop said emergency response times, based on two key performance indicators, have improved since the bike lanes were installed.
Yvan Baker, Liberal MP for Etobicoke-Centre, spoke out against the lanes, to loud applause. "It's had a devastating impact on traffic flow, with little upside benefit," Baker said.
Former Toronto city councillor John Campbell asked Morley and city officials to consider reducing the number of bike lanes to one instead of two. "These lanes are just not being used - they're empty," he said.
Some local residents also spoke out in favour of the lanes.
Emotions have been running high since the city began installing bike lanes along Bloor Street West from Runnymede Road to Aberfoyle Crescent in late summer of 2023, a few weeks after council approved a 4.7-kilometre complete street project in the neighbourhood.
Two vehicular traffic lanes were removed for bike lanes running on either side of the street, from Runnymede Aberfoyle. Phase 2, from Aberfoyle Crescent to Resurrection Road, is under construction.
Council approved the complete street project in June 2023.
Some local residents formed an organization, Balance on Bloor, to oppose the bike lanes in their current form. They have garnered more than 13,560 names on a petition.
Premier Doug Ford has said his government is preparing legislation that will require municipalities to get provincial support for bike lane projects that involve removing vehicular roadways.
"I understand and acknowledge that there are pain points with the project in the first year of installation, and I am fully committed to working collaboratively with Mayor (Olivia) Chow, city staff and local residents to improve the existing infrastructure," said Morley, in a statement issued in advance of the meeting.
In the past 10 years, 24,000 people have moved into the area, and there are about 70,200 new units in the development pipeline, according to data from Morley's office.
On Tuesday, provincial Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria provided more details about Ford's proposed legislation, saying that the goal is to keep bike lanes off main thoroughfares like Bloor Street West.
Sarkaria noted that many of the bike lanes were installed during the pandemic "when their impact on traffic was unclear."
The legislation is expected to be introduced after the legislature returns Monday from an extended summer break.
Sarkaria said existing bike lanes will stay, but he said the government will ask municipalities for all data on all bike lanes that have been installed in the last five years.
CAPTION: About 100 cyclists rode to a Wednesday evening meeting at Etobicoke Collegiate Institute to discuss Bloor Street West's bike lanes, which have generated controversy.Richard LautensToronto Star
CREDIT: Francine Kopun Senior Writer

Document 19 of 500

Electricity demand to soar by 75% in Ontario: Energy minister says nuclear will be 'anchor' for grid

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/electricity-demand-soar-75-ontario/docview/3117295395/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Electricity+demand+to+soar+by+75%25+in+Ontario%3A+Energy+minister+says+nuclear+will+be+%27anchor%27+for+grid&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new forecast shows Ontario's electricity needs surging 75 per cent by 2050, sparking the need for a more "ambitious" electricity generation plan to come soon, says Energy Minister Stephen Lecce.
"It is clear today Ontario needs more power," he told a news conference Wednesday, setting the stage for more generation, battery storage and conservation programs in addition to nuclear refurbishments and other expansions of the power system now underway.
The forecast released by the province's Independent Electricity System Operator marks a "quite significant" increase from the previous prediction of a 60 per jump in demand, said Chuck Farmer, the IESO's vice-president of planning, conservation and resource adequacy.
"Everybody is dealing with a sudden surge," Farmer said of agencies like the IESO in other jurisdictions facing higher demand because of electrification from cars to public transit, home heating, steelmaking as it transitions away from coal to arc furnaces, other industries such as electric vehicle battery plants and electricity-hungry data centres using artificial intelligence.
Ontario is expecting 16 more such data centres by 2035 and their forecast demand for power is six times higher than previously thought, said Lecce.
"This is a significant opportunity but it puts pressure on the grid," he added.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner urged Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government to avoid more natural gas-fired power plants, which cause carbon emissions that fuel climate change, in favour of renewable energy.
"My concern is the government's going to choose an expensive, dirty approach, which is ramping up gas plants, which is what they've been doing and makes absolutely no sense when wind, solar and battery storage and water power are far cheaper, far cleaner, can be deployed much faster," he said.
"Let's do it in the cheapest and cleanest way."
Lecce called natural gas plants, often used to meet demand at times of peak usage, "an insurance policy" to keep lights on and factories powered.
Nuclear, which supplies more than half the province's electricity, will remain the "anchor" of the system, he added, noting Ontario's grid is almost 90 per cent emission-free, mainly because of nuclear, hydroelectric power.
The previous energy minister, Todd Smith, signalled in January that the Ford government will seek approval from federal regulators to refurbish four aging 1980s Candu nuclear reactors at Pickering to get another 30 years of electricity out of the power plant just east of Toronto.
While no cost estimate was provided, the project - rejected 15 years ago by a previous Liberal government as too expensive - would cost billions of dollars and take a decade to complete.
Smith dismissed calls to pour that money into wind and solar as the world increasingly moves to renewable sources of electricity, calling nuclear "the responsible, pragmatic move forward."
The province is also planning a massive expansion of the Bruce Nuclear plant on Lake Huron, almost doubling its capacity by adding as many as 4,800 megawatts of electricity.
That is enough to power almost five million homes.
CAPTION: A data centre on Parliament Street, just north of the Distillery District. At least 16 large data centres are forecastto be in service by 2035 in Ontario and their estimated demand for power is six times higher than previously thought, said Energy Minister Stephen Lecce.Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 20 of 500

The fate of a years long effort to improve safety ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fate-years-long-effort-improve-safety-derived/docview/3117295342/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+fate+of+a+years+long+effort+to+improve+safety+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The fate of a years-long effort to improve safety on a dangerous stretch of Toronto road is up in the air, after Premier Doug Ford's government announced legislation that would require provincial approval for projects that replace car lanes with cycling routes.
This week, the city released its final study of proposed changes to Parkside Drive, a four-lane arterial road on the eastern edge of High Park that's used as a busy connector to the Gardiner Expressway, and which locals have long complained is unsafe. Council voted to explore additional safety measures there in 2021, after a chain-reaction crash killed two seniors.
The report states Parkside's current design doesn't meet city standards for sidewalk or car lane widths, or cyclist protection. It recommends a redesign to the roughly two-kilometre roadway.
That redesign would include removing one of two southbound traffic lanes and replacing it with a two-way cycle track on the west side of Parkside, from Bloor Street West to Lake Shore Boulevard West.
According to the report, the overhaul would "improve the comfort and safety of all road users," particularly cyclists and pedestrians, and support Toronto's Vision Zero road safety goals.
But on Tuesday, the same day the city released the plan, Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria confirmed the province intends to introduce legislation that would require cities to get approval from Queen's Park for bike lane projects built at the expense of car lanes.
The Ford government said the "explosion of bike lanes" in Ontario cities has increased traffic congestion, and that the law would bring "informed decision-making" to the planning process.
Details of the legislation have yet to be revealed, but the province said in a press release that municipalities will be "required to demonstrate the proposed bike lanes won't have a negative impact on vehicle traffic."
On Parkside, a traffic analysis included in the city report predicted car travel times would increase by one to three minutes during peak periods after the redesign, while motor vehicle volumes would decrease by about 35 per cent.
Asked whether the province plans to intervene on Parkside, Dakota Brasier, a spokesperson for Sarkaria, said: "Our position is clear. We support a common-sense approach when it comes to bike lanes - which does not include removing a lane of traffic in the most congested city in North America.
"We will be reviewing the city's plan, as well as all other information from municipalities regarding bike lanes that have been installed in the past five years."
A city spokesperson declined to speculate on how the Parkside bike lanes could be affected by the new law, saying the municipality will wait until the government unveils the wording of its legislation.
Parkside resident Faraz Gholizadeh supports the city plan, and says it's already taken too long for the municipality to address high-speed traffic on the street. He wants the province to butt out.
"It's unfortunate that they're putting their nose into something that doesn't really involve them. This is a municipal issue," said Gholizadeh, who co-chairs the Safe Parkside group.
Gholizadeh argued the bike lanes won't just help cyclists, but will also improve safety for all road users by slowing drivers.
The report says the redesign will help "speed management" by narrowing car lane widths to city standards.
"It's never been a debate about bike lanes or no bike lanes. It's always been a debate about safety," Gholizadeh said.
"Our provincial government seems to only be focused on people's commute times."
Parkside is used for about 21,000 motor vehicle trips per day, according to the city report. There have been almost 1,500 collisions on the street over the past decade, five of which resulted in serious injuries and two of which resulted in deaths. They included the multi-vehicle crash in October 2021 that killed Valdemar and Fatima Avila, whose car was crushed from behind.
In response to local residents' concerns, the city had already introduced "Watch Your Speed" signs, photo radar and new traffic signals, as well as reduced the speed limit from 50 to 40 kilometres per hour, among other changes.
In addition to the bike lanes, the new report recommends safety improvements at the Bloor and Lake Shore intersections, and enhanced connections to the Martin Goodman Trail. Where possible, left-turn lanes would be installed at intersections to allow for eastbound traffic flow. Parkside's two northbound lanes, one of which is already dedicated on-street parking, would be largely unchanged. Together, the measures would cost about $7.5 million, with installation scheduled to begin by 2026.
The report is scheduled to be debated at the city's infrastructure committee next Tuesday, before going to council for final approval.
The redesign has its critics. A city survey found 54 per cent of respondents were very supportive or supportive of the cycle track, while 42 per cent were opposed to some degree.
Local Coun. Gord Perks (Ward 4, Parkdale-High Park), who has been working with staff and residents on the plan, conceded it would require "trade-offs." But he argued most people would be OK with adding a few minutes to their commute if it would prevent deadly collisions.
Perks warned it would be unwise for the province to torpedo the Parkside proposal. "Having a dangerous transportation system doesn't benefit anybody," he said.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Document 21 of 500

Metrolinx shakeup was long overdue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/metrolinx-shakeup-was-long-overdue/docview/3117295306/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Metrolinx+shakeup+was+long+overdue&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the news broke about a shakeup at Metrolinx this week, many Torontonians were likely shouting "it's about time."
The executive at the provincial transit agency who was in charge of "bringing new light rail lines into service" and the one in charge of planning have moved on (or been moved on) to pursue other opportunities. Given they haven't yet brought any new light rail lines into service, many will see this as overdue.
"Clearly heads are rolling because their vehicles haven't been," Toronto Coun. Josh Matlow told the Star Tuesday. Amen, a lot of Torontonians will say.
Could this mean that finally something will get finished?
I spent some time this month wandering out on Eglinton Avenue East near Warden Avenue, and it felt especially noteworthy that LRT trains were running every few minutes along the corridor. No one could board them, of course - this was still training and testing. But surely, I thought, the fact that it appears to be basically operating full steam means it's just about ready to open?
But then I remembered a day well over a year ago when I spent an afternoon watching teenagers play baseball at Eglinton West and Black Creek Drive, when those LRT trains were motoring along every few minutes just beyond the outfield fences ("Hit a train, win a steak," we shouted at the batters). And back then, I had thought the same.
And yet here we are today: There is still no opening date, and no hint at when one is coming beyond Premier Doug Ford's recent acknowledgment that it is looking less and less likely that it will be this year. It's been more than two years since I wrote a column exasperated that the opening date - then already two years later than once promised - had been kicked off into the indefinite future. Since then, the future has only grown to seem even more indefinite.
Literally, for years, we've seen that line in service, trains running empty for training and testing, a visible reminder of the ride across the centre of the city we cannot take, taunting us - haunting us, a ghost subway, the central character in a transit construction horror show.
So yeah, maybe it's long past time for a change of leadership among the folks who were supposed to be "bringing new light rail lines into service," especially now that it is clear the Finch West LRT (originally proposed to open in 2013, and then 2020 and then 2023) is in a similar state of limbo, and beyond Toronto's borders, the Mississauga Hurontario LRT line that was supposed to open this fall has now also been placed into the no-opening-date-projected rip in the space-time continuum that has become Metrolinx's signature scheduling trick.
None of us wants to be that dude who celebrates people losing their jobs. But c'mon. A toast to something - anything - happening, some hint of accountability from an agency in charge of tens of billions of dollars of region-defining infrastructure projects, might feel appropriate.
But just as you're about to pop the cork, a further question occurs: Does it seem likely that the person in charge of "bringing new light rail lines into service" would be replaced if a new light rail line coming into service was imminent? If the opening date of even one of these too-long-awaited projects was just weeks or even months away, wouldn't you let the team that struggled through it all just show up to cut the ribbon, congratulate themselves, and then use the milestone as a perfect occasion to pass the torch and change the guard for the next set of projects?
More practically, would you put someone new in charge if their most immediate responsibility was going to be to declare victory and cut those ribbons? Or would you do it because there was some real, difficult work that needed to be done that required a new vision and voice and work plan?
Which is to say, is it possible that this shuffle indicates we are not all that close to seeing any of these LRT lines in operation?
God help us.
Many of us have been referring to the Eglinton Crosstown in the conditional tense for a while - you know: if it opens, rather than when. But we have been at least half-joking, right? Long experience has taught us to take nothing for granted and not let ourselves expect much. But surely we expected, even as Metrolinx head Phil Verster and Ford refused to speculate about dates, that we were looking at months rather than years.
We are years into that expectation. And now the GTA has at least three transit lines in the same Twilight Zone. A shakeup of the senior staff surely does seem long overdue. It's just far too late for it to offer any real sense hope.
CAPTION: A toast to some hint of accountability from Metrolinx over its long-delayed light rail transit projects might feel appropriate for some Torontonians, Edward Keenan writes.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Document 22 of 500

Cyclists clash with bike-lane opponents at Etobicoke meeting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cyclists-clash-with-bike-lane-opponents-at/docview/3117295246/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cyclists+clash+with+bike-lane+opponents+at+Etobicoke+meeting&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tensions ran high Wednesday night at an Etobicoke meeting held by Coun. Amber Morley to discuss bike lanes on Bloor Street West.
About 100 cyclists rolled in a half-hour before the 7 p.m. meeting at Etobicoke Collegiate Institute after rallying in High Park in support of the bike lanes.
They arrived blocking a lane of vehicular traffic, with a motorist stuck behind them leaning on the horn - a microcosm of the long-simmering strife between cyclists who support the lanes and residents who say the lanes are not well-used, are difficult to navigate for the disabled and the elderly, and have ruined the village atmosphere of the neighbourhood.
"It's great to see so many people on bikes come out to Coun. Morley's community meeting and show support for safe and protected cycling infrastructure like the Bloor-Street bikeways," said Michael Longfield, executive director of Cycle Toronto.
Inside the meeting, Morley, (Ward 3 Etobicoke-Lakeshore) said while she is committed to improving the bike lanes on Bloor West in Etobicoke, she does not support removing them.
"We acknowledge this is a very challenging issue and has had a lot of pain points for residents," said Morley, adding later that the bike lanes are a necessary part of the plan to keep people moving in the area, which is about to experience a steep increase in population.
"This is about building a sustainable city we can be proud of," she said.
Jacquelyn Hayward, the city's director of transportation project design and management, acknowledged it's taking longer for vehicular traffic moving through the area, citing an increase in travel times of between three-and-a-half and four-and-a-half minutes.
"We aren't satisfied with these numbers," said Hayward, saying the city is looking at changes such as syncing traffic lights to improve travel times.
Toronto Fire deputy chief Jim Jessop said emergency response times, based on two key performance indicators, have improved since the bike lanes were installed.
Yvan Baker, Liberal MP for Etobicoke-Centre spoke out against the lanes, to loud applause. "It's had a devastating impact on traffic flow, with little upside benefit," said Baker.
Former Toronto city councillor John Campbell asked Morley and city officials to consider reducing the number of bike lanes to one instead of two. "These lanes are just not being used - they're empty," he said.
Some local residents also spoke out in favour of the lanes.
Emotions have been running high since the city began installing bike lanes along Bloor Street West from Runnymede Road to Aberfoyle Crescent in late summer of 2023, a few weeks after council approved a 4.7-kilometre complete street project in the neighbourhood.
Two vehicular traffic lanes were removed for bike lanes running on either side of the street, from Runnymede Aberfoyle. Phase 2, from Aberfoyle Crescent to Resurrection Road, is under construction.
Council approved the complete street project in June 2023.
Some local residents formed an organization, Balance on Bloor, to oppose the bike lanes in their current form. They have garnered more than 13,560 names on a petition.
Premier Doug Ford has said his government is preparing legislation that will require municipalities to get provincial support for bike lane projects that involve removing vehicular roadways.
"I understand and acknowledge that there are pain points with the project in the first year of installation, and I am fully committed to working collaboratively with Mayor Chow, City Staff and local residents to improve the existing infrastructure," said Morley, in a statement issued in advance of the meeting.
In the past 10 years, 24,000 people have moved into the area, and there are about 70,200 new units in the development pipeline, according to data from Morley's office.
On Tuesday, provincial Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria provided more details about Ford's proposed legislation, saying that the goal is to keep bike lanes off main thoroughfares like Bloor Street West.
Sarkaria noted that many of the bike lanes were installed during the pandemic "when their impact on traffic was unclear."
The legislation is expected to be introduced after the legislature returns Monday from an extended summer break.
Sarkaria said existing bike lanes will stay, but he said the government will ask municipalities for all data on all bike lanes that have been installed in the last five years.
CAPTION: The cycling lanes on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke have sparked a backlash and involvement from Queen's Park.About 100 cyclists rode to the meeting at Etobicoke Collegiate Institute, after rallying in High Park in support of the bike lanes.CI-BIKELANES-OCT16 Dozens of cyclists gather on Wednesday night before a meeting to discuss bike lanes on Bloor Street West. Document 23 of 500

Province to send cheques to every Ontarian: Tax-free payments of at least $200 come as Ford eyes early election call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-send-cheques-every-ontarian/docview/3117295159/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+to+send+cheques+to+every+Ontarian%3A+Tax-free+payments+of+at+least+%24200+come+as+Ford+eyes+early+election+call&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is poised to send cheques to 16 million Ontarians to offset rising costs as a possible early election looms, the Star has learned.
Sources say the premier's gambit will be announced in Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement on Oct. 30.
While the precise amount of the rebate cheques is still being finalized, it should be at least $200 for every adult and child in the province. That means it could cost the provincial treasury about $3.2 billion when the cash flows out the door in January or February.
The payments would be tax-free. It could mean an additional $800 in household cash for a family of four at a time of year when paycheques are smaller due to employment insurance deductions and when holiday credit card bills are coming due.
Even though inflation has dropped to 1.6 per cent and mortgage rates are declining, Ford's Progressive Conservatives privately point to stubbornly high grocery prices and energy and housing expenses as the rationale for the payouts.
The move echoes what former premier Mike Harris's Tories did in 2000 when then finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques.
While Eves's budget that year was in surplus, Bethlenfalvy is not expected to balance the books until after the next election, which is scheduled for June 2026 but could come as soon as this March.
Still, the treasurer's fiscal update will show a significant improvement over his March 26 budget when he forecast a surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25.
That was a $10-billion swing from the $200-million surplus Bethlenfalvy had projected last year.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, noted Ford wants to get more money back in the pockets of Ontarians.
They pointed to his decision before the 2022 election to eliminate licence plate sticker renewal fees, which costs the treasury more than $1 billion annually but has been a popular measure with motorists.
Similarly, the premier scrapped tolls on highways 412 and 418.
As well, during the COVID-19 pandemic, the provincial government gave parents $200 per child under age 12 and $250 per child and youth under 21 with special needs to offset online educational expenses.
Speaking to reporters last Friday in Brockville, Ford, the multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family, insisted he understands how strapped families are feeling these days.
"We're looking at cutting costs every which way we possibly can," he said.
"We're the only government in the history of this province - think of this - that has never raised a tax. We reduced taxes. We got rid of the sticker fee that people were having to pay every single year."
Warning of the next increase in federal carbon pricing on April 1, but not mentioning the rebate, the premier predicted "the crunch is coming down."
"The feds are going to add to the worst tax ever, the carbon tax, so you're going to be paying 20 cents a litre (on gasoline). They already have 17 cents, another three cents on every litre of gas."
Bethlenfalvy's office said Wednesday it could not comment on what would or would not be in the fall economic statement.
In a video announcing the date of his fall update, the treasurer said "we're keeping costs down for hardworking families by fighting the carbon tax and cutting government taxes and fees."
"But there's more work to do, and we can't afford to go backwards," he said.
Even though the cost to the treasury would be at least $3.2 billion, government officials believe most of the money would be reinjected into the economy by cash-strapped Ontarians.
The payments come as Ford has been musing about calling an election a year ahead of schedule.
According to the most recent Abacus Data monthly tracking poll for the Star, the Tories are at 44 per cent while Bonnie Crombie's Liberals are at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles's New Democrats are at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens are at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians on Oct. 3-10 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 24 of 500

Alberta ad campaign aims to axe federal cap: Oil and gas emissions limit will kill jobs, province warns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alberta-ad-campaign-aims-axe-federal-cap/docview/3117295155/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+ad+campaign+aims+to+axe+federal+cap%3A+Oil+and+gas+emissions+limit+will+kill+jobs%2C+province+warns&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: EDMONTON Alberta's government has launched a national ad campaign targeting the federal government's incoming emissions cap for the oil and gas sector.
Premier Danielle Smith said Tuesday it's a de facto production cap that will stifle the economy.
"This, by extension, will cut jobs and revenues across the country, and that should concern every Canadian," Smith said at a news conference in Calgary.
Alberta Environment Minister Rebecca Schulz called the cap "wacko," saying it will lead the province and country into "economic and societal decline" and make every aspect of life less affordable.
The province's $7-million "Scrap the Cap" campaign claims the federal regulations, which could come in draft form later this year, would make "groceries, gas and all of life's necessities even more expensive."
Smith said the industry cannot absorb the costs of the cap, and if a product is made less available and the price increases, "it's going to increase the cost of everything."
In a statement, federal Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault and Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson accused Smith of using taxpayer money to spread disinformation.
"Repeating a lie does not make it true. Our government's commitment is to drive down pollution from the oil and gas sector, not to cut production," they said, adding the oil and gas industry recognizes reducing pollution is necessary for their own competitiveness.
"Canadians know that when oil and gas companies are making record profits, they should be doing their fair share and investing back in clean technologies that create good-paying, sustainable jobs for Albertans, all while protecting our environment," they said.
Alberta's campaign is to run television, print, social media and online video ads in Alberta, British Columbia, Ontario, New Brunswick and Nova Scotia until the end of November.
University of Calgary economist Trevor Tombe said he believes the emissions cap is bad policy, but the argument that it would drive up the price of gas - and therefore groceries - is misleading.
Tombe said gas prices are largely determined by taxes, retail markups and margins, and global oil prices.
"There hasn't been any credible analysis suggesting that Canada's emissions cap on oil and gas will affect world oil prices," said Tombe.
He said it's a fair argument to make that the cap would result in fewer jobs and lower wages in the sector, and he believes the federal policy takes a flawed approach.
Tombe said the most efficient way to lower emissions is by having an identical burden on all sectors, in all regions, so every tonne of emissions faces the same consequence.
At the same time, he said, the emissions cap won't necessarily force lower production.
"Even if costs rise and profits fall, the profit per barrel could still very well be positive, even with higher costs," said Tombe.
Alberta NDP Leader Naheed Nenshi said in a release that Smith's United Conservatives are spending taxpayer dollars to try and influence the next federal election instead of actually solving the problem.
"They got the federal government entrenched in a view that is bad for Alberta and we're now going to have an emissions cap that may well restrict our ability to increase production going forward," he said.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Lisa Johnson The Canadian Press

Document 25 of 500

Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-poised-send-out-pre-election-cheques-16/docview/3117699538/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+poised+to+send+out+pre-election+cheques+to+16+million+Ontarians&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is poised to send cheques to 16 million Ontarians to offset rising costs as a possible early election looms, the Star has learned.
Sources say the premier's gambit will be announced in Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy's fall economic statement on Oct. 30.
While the precise amount of the rebate cheques is still being finalized, it should be at least $200 for every adult and child in the province.
That means it could cost the provincial treasury about $3.2 billion when the cash flows out the door in January or February.
The payments would be tax-free. It could mean an additional $800 in household cash for a family of four at a time of year when paycheques are smaller due to employment insurance deductions and when holiday credit card bills are coming due.
Even though inflation has dropped to 1.6 per cent and mortgage rates are declining, Ford's Progressive Conservatives privately point to stubbornly high grocery prices and energy and housing expenses as the rationale for the payouts.
The move echoed what former premier Mike Harris's Tories did in 2000 when then-finance minister Ernie Eves announced $200 "dividend" cheques.
While Eves' budget that year was in surplus, Bethlenfalvy is not expected to balance the books until after the next election, which is scheduled for June 2026 but could come as soon as this March.
Still, the treasurer's fiscal update will show a significant improvement over his March 26 budget when he forecast a surprise $9.8-billion deficit for 2024-25.
That was a $10-billion swing from the $200-million surplus Bethlenfalvy had projected last year.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, noted Ford wants to get more money back in the pockets of Ontarians.
They pointed to his decision before the 2022 election to eliminate licence plate sticker renewal fees, which costs the treasury more than $1 billion annually but has been a popular measure with motorists.
Similarly, the premier scrapped tolls on Highways 412 and 418.
As well, during the COVID-19 pandemic, the provincial government gave parents $200 per child under age 12 and $250 per child and youth under 21 with special needs to offset online educational expenses.
Speaking to reporters last Friday in Brockville, Ford, the multimillionaire scion of a wealthy family, insisted he understands how strapped families are feeling these days.
"We're looking at cutting costs every which way we possibly can," he said.
"We're the only government in the history of this province - think of this - that has never raised a tax. We reduced taxes. We got rid of the sticker fee that people were having to pay every single year."
Warning of the next increase in federal carbon pricing on April 1, but not mentioning the rebate, the premier predicted "the crunch is coming down."
"The feds are going to add to the worst tax ever, the carbon tax, so you're going to be paying 20 cents a litre (on gasoline). They already have 17 cents, another three cents on every litre of gas."
Bethlenfalvy's office said Wednesday it could not comment on what would or would not be in the fall economic statement.
In a video announcing the date of his fall update, the treasurer said "we're keeping costs down for hardworking families by fighting the carbon tax and cutting government taxes and fees."
"But there's more work to do, and we can't afford to go backwards," he said.
Even though the cost to the treasury would be at least $3.2 billion, government officials believe most of the money would be re-injected into the economy by cash-strapped Ontarians.
The payments come as Ford has been musing about calling an election a year ahead of schedule.
According to the most recent Abacus Data monthly tracking poll for the Star, the Tories are at 44 per cent while Bonnie Crombie's Liberals are at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats are at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens are at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians on Oct. 3-10 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, the multi-millionaire scion of a wealthy family, insists he understands how strapped families are and says his government is "looking at cutting costs every which way we possibly can."

'Ontario needs more power:' Ford government wants to boost electricity expansion to meet surging demand

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-needs-more-power-ford-government-wants/docview/3117056222/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Ontario+needs+more+power%3A%27+Ford+government+wants+to+boost+electricity+expansion+to+meet+surging+demand&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A new forecast shows Ontario's electricity needs surging 75 per cent by 2050, sparking the need for a more "ambitious" electricity generation plan to come soon, says Energy Minister Stephen Lecce.
"It is clear today Ontario needs more power," he told a news conference Wednesday, setting the stage for more generation, battery storage and conservation programs in addition to nuclear refurbishments and other expansions of the power system now underway.
The forecast released by the province's Independent Electricity System Operator marks a "quite significant" increase from the previous prediction of a 60 per jump in demand, said Chuck Farmer, the IESO's vice-president of planning, conservation and resource adequacy.
"Everybody is dealing with a sudden surge," Farmer said of agencies like the IESO in other jurisdictions facing higher demand because of electrification from cars to public transit, home heating, steelmaking as it transitions away from coal to arc furnaces, other industries such as electric vehicle battery plants and electricity-hungry data centres using artificial intelligence.
Ontario is expecting 16 more such data centres by 2035 and their forecast demand for power is six times higher than previously thought, said Lecce.
"This is a significant opportunity but it puts pressure on the grid," he added.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner urged Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government to avoid more natural gas-fired power plants, which cause carbon emissions that fuel climate change, in favour of renewable energy.
"My concern is the government's going to choose an expensive, dirty approach, which is ramping up gas plants, which is what they've been doing and makes absolutely no sense when wind, solar and battery storage and water power are far cheaper, far cleaner, can be deployed much faster," he said.
"Let's do it in the cheapest and cleanest way."
Lecce called natural gas plants, often used to meet demand at times of peak usage, "an insurance policy" to keep lights on and factories powered.
Nuclear, which supplies more than half the province's electricity, will remain the "anchor" of the system, he added, noting Ontario's grid is almost 90 per cent emission-free, mainly because of nuclear, hydroelectric power.
The previous energy minister, Todd Smith, signalled in January that the Ford government will seek approval from federal regulators to refurbish four aging 1980s Candu nuclear reactors at Pickering to get another 30 years of electricity out of the power plant just east of Toronto.
While no cost estimate was provided, the project - rejected 15 years ago by a previous Liberal government as too expensive - would cost billions of dollars and take a decade to complete.
Smith dismissed calls to pour that money into wind and solar as the world increasingly moves to renewable sources of electricity, calling nuclear "the responsible, pragmatic move forward."
The province is also planning a massive expansion of the Bruce Nuclear plant on Lake Huron, almost doubling its capacity by adding as many as 4,800 megawatts of electricity. That is enough to power almost five million homes.
CAPTION: Darlington Nuclear Power Plant is currently ramping up to do a decade long refit of all four reactors.

Document 28 of 500

In Ontario's ERs we have allowed an assault on human dignity to fester

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-ers-we-have-allowed-assault-on-human/docview/3117055943/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+Ontario%27s+ERs+we+have+allowed+an+assault+on+human+dignity+to+fester&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A night at one of Toronto's busiest emergency rooms, Oct. 13
I'm not sure there could be a more damning indictment of the sad state in Ontario's ERs than that described in Francine Kopun's article. Crowding and human suffering have been detailed for more than two decades; what has received insufficient attention is the fundamental assault on human dignity that we have allowed to fester.
One of the core principles in the practice of medicine is a patient's fundamental right to confidentiality, the relief of their suffering and the dignity of the person. This has just become impossible in any Ontario ER as we examine people in hallways, chairs, and waiting rooms within earshot of other patients. We have normalized the unacceptable in the interests of shaving down an eight-hour ER wait to six or seven. This is made even worse in a rural ER where everybody knows everybody else and the discussion of an STD, alcohol abuse or marital infidelity will soon be news all over town. This is not OK, it never was OK and it's well past time emergency physicians reclaimed our historic right and duty to provide patients with the respect and privacy they deserve.
Alan Drummond MD, Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians Ottawa, Perth
Solution to family doctor shortage is already hereForeign doctors won't solve our family physician crisis. Here's what might, Oct. 13
Let's understand one thing: we are raising the next generation of doctors right under our noses, yet these wonderful young people are being barred from being part of our solution by an out-of-date hierarchy of wrong-minded old-school thinkers and a government that supports their ineptitude.
I know of this firsthand. My highly qualified and competent nephew, a high achiever in attaining his master's degree in anatomy and cellular biology with high honours and recognition, cannot gain acceptance to a medical school here and so we will lose this treasured and badly needed human asset to Australia where he will be welcomed. Something is very wrong here. Yet we are powerless to find a solution in a Doug Ford Ontario, intent on destroying our once-admired health-care system. A change in government must be affected as a first step to righting our health-care ship.
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge
Myopic vision on international studentsCanadian universities see international students' enrolment down 45 per cent after rule change. 'The system is just hanging on', Oct. 7
Cuts to international student programs at Ontario universities are hurting us all and diminishing our worldwide reputation.Foreign students do important research such as in stem cells advances that saves lives. We need to welcome foreign students and encourage them to remain in the province.
Katherine Borthwick, Hamilton
Courage, principles and the carbon tax'Too little, too late'? Trudeau government plans ad campaign to promote carbon pricing - and rebates, Sept. 8
Whether you like him or not or agree with him or not, you have to admire Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's commitment to the carbon price issue. He believes that placing a price on carbon is an effective way to reduce carbon emissions and that it is vital to do so. Trudeau also knows that he is losing support and perhaps the next election over the carbon tax. When someone commits to doing the right thing, takes a beating for it and then keeps on doing the right thing, that is evidence that the person has both principles and courage.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, on the other hand, distorts the issue of a carbon tax, exaggerates its costs, ignores its benefits but presents no viable alternatives. He ignores the fact that his party originated the idea of a tax on carbon and the fact that economists regard it as an effective and low-cost tool to reduce carbon dioxide emissions.
Poilievre knows that climate change is real, damages our health and our environment and is already costing billions of dollars in flood, forest fires, infrastructure damage from ever more frequent and violent storms, crop damage and increasing insurance costs. Also, he ignores that climate change is getting worse at an increasing rate and is an existential threat to life on earth as we know it.
A carbon tax alone will not solve the problem, but it will help. People make decisions every day which will affect their carbon footprint for many years into the future. The prospect of increasing fuel costs, according to economists, will drive most of us to choose less consumptive lifestyles which may mean more efficient transportation, shorter commutes, less extravagant homes. We owe that to future generations.
Dave Schwartz, Kenora
Percentage increases widen the gap between the rich and the poorAir Canada pilots could reject pay raise of nearly 40% on Thursday. Here's why, Oct. 9
The unhappiness of the junior pilots underscores the greatest failure of modern education, namely an understanding of percentages. The 40 per cent increase gives the junior pilots an increase of about $40,000 a year. The senior pilots could get $120,000. But the dollar increase in the cost-of-living, based on the standard basket of goods and services, is the same for both.
The failure to understand the "dilation" nature of percentages creates a percentage "fairness" concept in people's minds, a concept that is completely false. Fighting inflation requires equal monetary increases, not equal percentage increases. Percentage increases widen the gap between the rich, and the poor. Over the years, the application of percentage salary increases has made the gap of the 1920s into a bottomless abyss in the 2020s.
However, the rich benefit from this situation, as they do from the high interest rates that create inflation. The rich now enjoy a luxurious lifestyle based entirely on decisions made by governments. That is why we will never see a change. And that is why the social impacts of percentages will never be taught in our schools.
Peter Weygang, Bobcaygeon
Nine months to build a stadium, but housing takes forever
Race is on for new concert venue Oct. 13
It amazes me how easily this entertainment stadium gets approved, without delays and no issues about development fees. Compare this against the building of a few homes in Toronto in which everything gets to be a problem. It would seem that the entertainment stadium is more urgent than housing. A 50,000-capacity venue to be completed by next June, (nine months,) when a critical housing development for 50 families can't get done in two years, I guess there is no race for them.
Brian Mellor, Midland
CAPTION: It will take at least three hours and 23 minutes to see a doctor, according to the television monitor. It is 5:30 p.m. We have been warned.
