Document 29 of 500

Canadians are saving money with EVs and heat pumps. But only if they can afford the upfront cost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-are-saving-money-with-evs-heat-pumps/docview/3116960112/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+are+saving+money+with+EVs+and+heat+pumps.+But+only+if+they+can+afford+the+upfront+cost&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Over the last two years, Canadian households have been getting on board with the energy transition. The Greener Homes Grant helped Canadians from coast to coast to coast install heat pumps and retrofit their homes, while EV sales have risen rapidly thanks to growing model availability and purchase incentives.
Accordingly, thousands of Canadians have made a shift away from fossil fuels, reaping the affordability benefits. In fact, our latest analysis finds that a household in Toronto that switched out its gas cars for electric versions, ditched its natural gas appliances, installed a heat pump and made some modest energy efficiency upgrades would cut $550 off its monthly bill, even taking into account upfront costs.
And no wonder, Canadians' continued reliance on fossil fuels is costing them. In fact, a recent study found that energy prices are the most volatile component of inflation in the country.
But despite the savings benefits of clean technologies, upfront price can be a barrier to entry for many middle-income Canadians. And in many cases, these costs have been moving in the wrong direction - particularly for Ontarians who receive comparatively little provincial support.
Over the past year, the federal government's Greener Homes Grant (that offered up to $5,000 off the price of a heat pump and other energy-saving measures) was discontinued in favour of a program for lower income households.
Also, many of the most affordable and bestselling EVs in Canada have either increased in price or disappeared. Production of both the sub-$45,000 Chevrolet Bolt and the Kia Soul EV has been paused or discontinued, leaving gaps in the more affordable end of the market. And now the cheapest Tesla will no longer be available in Canada following new tariffs imposed on Chinese-made EVs.
Our evidence is clear: switching your fossil fuel-powered car for an EV saves money in every scenario, in every region of the country - even when upfront costs are included and even in Ontario. A driver opting for an electric Volkswagen ID.4 instead of a gas-powered Honda CR-V would save over $2,400 a year over the lifetime of the car. The problem is that not everyone can finance a pricier car that will start saving them money in the future.
Indeed, despite clear upsides, upfront cost remains the No. 1 concern for prospective EV buyers, despite EV sticker prices dropping in recent years. It needn't be the case.
Europeans can choose from at least 12 different fully electric options with a purchase price of less than $45,000, compared to just three in Canada. And the playing field isn't level across the country, either. Most provinces and territories offer some kind of rebate for buying a new or used EV. But Ontario isn't one of them.
The implications are written in the sales numbers: Ontario continues to trail the national average and has now even fallen behind the Yukon on electric market share. In fact, EVs now make up 32 per cent of new vehicle sales in Montreal and 25 per cent in Vancouver, compared to just 9 per cent in Toronto. Ontarians are missing out on EV options. The new Ontario-made electric Dodge Charger, for instance, will initially only be available in B.C. and Quebec.
This points to the critical importance of government policy, to both help cut the upfront cost via purchase incentives and encourage automakers to make more affordable models. But rebates are not universal and some key policies are at risk.
In fact, another recent report found that the federal Electric Vehicle Availability Standard (which requires automakers to make an increasing portion of EVs available for sale) will be key to incenting automakers to bring more affordable EVs to market. And yet, the future of the policy remains uncertain with the official opposition publicly stating they would repeal it if elected.
When it comes to home upgrades, many provinces stepped up after the federal government dropped its program earlier this year, retaining or expanding support for heat pumps and energy retrofits. But the result is a geographically unequal transition.
A median-income family in B.C. can receive up to $12,000 in government subsidies to switch from natural gas to a heat pump, while that same family in Ontario would receive no government support (and only up to $2,000 from the utility).
We are at a critical time in the energy transition. We have the solutions to lower energy bills and fight climate change, but it is vital that every Canadian can benefit. To that end, all levels of government must take action, offering rebates to cut upfront costs, improving charging access, simplifying financing and improving electricity rates to further incentivize cleaner options.
Anyone considering a clean energy shift can visit Clean Energy Canada's new online calculator, mycleanbill.ca, to get a better sense of potential savings based on your location, vehicle type, and home.
After all, the door to cleaner homes and lower bills must be open to all Canadians.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau look over a vehicle along an assembly line at an event announcing plans for a Honda electric vehicle battery plant in Alliston in April 2024.
CREDIT: Rachel Doran and Jana Elbrecht Contributors

Document 30 of 500

New plans must get provincial approval: Chow says she doesn't support Ontario limiting cities' power

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-plans-must-get-provincial-approval/docview/3116960096/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+plans+must+get+provincial+approval%3A+Chow+says+she+doesn%27t+support+Ontario+limiting+cities%27+power&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is hitting the brakes on new bike lanes, warning Toronto and other municipalities they'll need provincial approval before taking out lanes of traffic for cars and trucks.
The aim is to keep bike lanes off major thoroughfares like Bloor Street West, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria told reporters Tuesday as he previewed upcoming legislation from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Growth in bike lanes is "out of control," he charged at a pool hall and bar on Bloor near Islington, where local residents and businesses have complained new bike lanes are lightly used, particularly in cold or wet weather, and have slowed traffic.
"We're seeing an explosion in bike lanes, including many that were installed during the pandemic when their impact on traffic was unclear," said Sarkaria, who also announced plans to design new highways for speed limits above 120 km/h and to increase speed limits to 110 km/h on existing ones.
"While strategically placed bike lanes are a critical part of every city ... what cities should not be doing, however, is taking away lanes of traffic on our most congested roads," he added.
"Municipalities are still free to install bike lanes where they do not remove traffic."
There was quick pushback from Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and a prominent cycling lawyer as the Progressive Conservatives prepare to unveil the legislation when MPPs return from an extended summer break next Monday amid speculation about an early provincial election.
"I do not support limiting city powers," Chow said in a statement to the Star.
"It's always better when we work together to get things right," added the mayor, who last week proposed tweaking some bike lanes to ease concerns about overall traffic flow.
"To tackle congestion and keep people safe on city roads, we need all types of transportation. The province should concentrate on their job of finally getting the Eglinton Crosstown and Finch LRT open, which will have a huge impact on congestion."
The legislation would be a blow to Toronto city council's plan, passed over the summer, to add 100 kilometres of bike lanes to major routes over the next three years as part of efforts to reduce vehicle emissions.
Cycling lawyer David Shellnutt accused the province of starting an "us versus them campaign" dividing road users and making life more dangerous for cyclists in a year that has seen six killed on Toronto streets in addition to deaths in the Belleville, Guelph and Niagara areas.
"Ontarians must see these bike lane attacks from what they are: a tool to distract us from the real problems pressing this province," Shellnutt added in a statement hinting at the RCMP investigation of Ford's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"Even in Toronto, most will recognize that it is the sheer number of cars on the roads ... and maybe the baffling number of construction projects that are actually increasing gridlock."
The legislation, which government sources confirmed will be called the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," comes as Ford's Progressive Conservatives position themselves for an early election, possibly by next spring - a year ahead of the next scheduled provincewide vote in June 2026.
It's expected the bike lane plan, limiting municipal powers, would appeal to suburban voters who rely more heavily on cars to get around.
It's not clear how long it would take the province to render a decision on proposed new bike lanes. Cycling advocates have expressed concerns that the process could effectively halt new lanes.
Advocates maintain that bike lanes encourage people to get out of their cars and onto two wheels at a time of increasing concern about the impact of vehicle exhaust, climate change, the cost of commuting and congestion.
"More people biking, walking or taking public transit means more people not in cars and less gridlock," said Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre).
Sarkaria said existing bike lanes will stay but the government will ask municipalities for data on all bike lanes that have been installed in the last five years.
Toronto Coun. Brad Bradford (Beaches-East York) said bike lanes on Bloor Street West and Yonge Street should be "removed immediately" because they make it tougher for customers to patronize businesses and cause vehicular traffic to seek shortcuts through neighbourhoods.
Ford, a former city councillor who lives in Etobicoke, has frequently been critical of the expansion of bike lanes and faced criticism in 2018 for meddling in municipal powers by cutting the size of Toronto city council nearly in half in the midst of that year's local election campaign.
In contrast, the premier has repeatedly said municipalities are best placed to decide where to build new housing as the province reaches for a target of 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
With files from Ben Cohen
CAPTION: The province's "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act" aims to keep bike lanes off major thoroughfares like Bloor Street West, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said Tuesday, adding that the number of bike lanes is "out of control."Nick LachanceToronto Star
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau With files from Ben Cohen

Document 31 of 500

'Freedom Convoy' is finding new friends

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/freedom-convoy-is-finding-new-friends/docview/3116959265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Freedom+Convoy%27+is+finding+new+friends&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The greatest danger of the so-called "Freedom Convoy" was not to Ottawa. Yes, the convoy tortured the city for more than a month, and that shouldn't be forgotten, or downplayed. But it passed, even if the lonely leftovers that still hang around to harass politicians remain a smaller symptom of a wider ill.
No, the most dangerous part of the convoy was always its most farcical element, which is that some of the main organizers, who had a history of delusional and hateful comments, aimed to overthrow the government. No matter how supine Ottawa police were, no matter how absent Ontario Premier Doug Ford or then-mayor Jim Watson was, no matter the overlapping paralysis of jurisdictions, we all laughed at that.
Two-and-a-half years later, and elements of the movement are being embraced by politicians more than ever before. What if the convoy is succeeding at changing our governments for the worse?
You could see it coming at the time. Former Saskatchewan premier Brad Wall gave convoy leaders advice, and they met with former CPC leader Andrew Scheer in Regina, and various MPs cheered. Then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole took a half-hearted approach in meeting organizers, and was criticized for it. He was ejected for many reasons, after which interim CPC leader Candace Bergen saw an opportunity, and so did Pierre Poilievre. He glad-handed with convoy folks, and is now the leader of the Conservative party, and quite likely to be our next prime minister, at some point.
Poilievre has dutifully lined up with some of the most deluded members of the public: marching on the day before Canada Day in 2022 with anti-vaccine veteran James Topp, or his private member's bill that would have banned vaccine mandates for travellers and federal employees, marketed in anti-vaccine code words that implied more than COVID vaccines.
But it's worsening. In Alberta, Danielle Smith's government fired Alberta's then-chief medical officer of health Deena Hinshaw, which Smith justified by saying of public health, "they shut down the economy, they put on masks, they put on restrictions, and I thought, we're not going to let that happen."
The Alberta Human Rights Act was updated to include the right to refuse a vaccine or to not wear a mask, and Smith plans to do the same with the Alberta Bill of Rights. Her new deputy chief of staff, by the way, owns a restaurant that in 2022 accepted puppy pictures instead of vaccination passports for entry.
And in B.C., Conservative Leader John Rustad crossed into a different place. He has not only told an anti-vaccine group that he regretted getting vaccinated, but in a video unearthed by the indefatigable PressProgress, he was asked about a concept that the most angry and deluded anti-vaccine activists use: Nuremberg 2.0.
"Are you for or against a Nuremberg 2.0?" asked anti-vaccine activist Jedediah Ferguson, making it sound like "Newemberg."
"A do it bigger 2.0, sorry?" asked Rustad, confused.
"Nuremberg 2.0," repeated Ferguson, smiling.
"Nuremberg 2.0," said Rustad, a smile spreading across his face. "Ah, yes. That's probably something that's outside of my scope."
"I know I put you on the hot spot right there, for sure, but I had to ask," said Ferguson.
"No, no, it's fine," said Rustad. "I can say, that's something that's sort of outside the scope in terms of jurisdiction of British Columbia, but we would certainly be participating with other jurisdictions as we look at those sorts of issues."
Nuremberg 2.0 has long been a rallying cry among the most angry anti-vaccine activists: it is about show trials of doctors, politicians and journalists, and punishment ranging from prison to executions. Rustad later claimed to have misunderstood the question. The best you could probably say, based on his reaction and his answer, is that he was pandering to what he sees as his base. After all, in an election full of conspiracists, the most controversial candidate, Brent Chapman, has said there are "probably 45, maybe 50 Freedom people that are running for this party, and I know that some of our media says, oh, they're wack jobs."
Smith and Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe have played footsie with chemtrail conspiracists, but Rustad's answer was a new line crossed, in a party that is way out there. And he could win.
Radicalization tends to go in one direction. The convoy to Ottawa was, at its heart, divorced from reality, even if you could understand some of its energy: As a young mother named Dasha told me in January 2022 at a convoy rally north of Toronto, "I'm just a regular person that wants to go back to normal. Why can't we just call it a day and say this is a regular flu just like any other flu?" She also, as it happened, thought hospitals were lying about being full of patients.
She was wrong. The anti-vaccine radicals are wrong. It was sad, talking to Dasha: she had been fed disinformation under strenuous circumstances, and was clearly vulnerable. She was struggling with the world.
She's far from alone, and now the most unmoored reactions to the pandemic are leaching into governments across the country, and maybe into a federal government that has a habit of turning people who disagree with them into enemies.
The world of radical unreality is biting back, and we need conservative politicians with cognitive clarity and genuine principles beyond partisanship, who can transcend the angriest and most unhinged parts of their base. We face real-world problems, in the same way that we did under the COVID emergency, and we need people who deal with the world as it is.
If that doesn't happen, as under the COVID emergency, we will all suffer.
CAPTION: Police move in to remove so-called "Freedon Convoy" protesters near Parliament Hill in February 2022 after weeks of demonstrations. Two-and-a-half years later, elements of the movement are being embraced by politicians more than ever before, Bruce Arthur writes.Cole Burston The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Bruce Arthur Toronto Star OPINION

Document 32 of 500

Ford's move appears to be more about votes than gridlock: There's little evidence to show that protecting cyclists slows down drivers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-move-appears-be-more-about-votes-than/docview/3116941850/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+move+appears+to+be+more+about+votes+than+gridlock%3A+There%27s+little+evidence+to+show+that+protecting+cyclists+slows+down+drivers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's plan to poke a stick into the spokes of bike lane expansion is ostensibly about ending gridlock.
At the moment, however, there is little proof that bike lanes are a major cause of the aggravating, internationally infamous traffic migraine bedevilling Toronto, much less jams in the smaller Ontario cities that will also be affected by provincial legislation dictating municipal policy.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria on Tuesday announced that his government's first piece of business, when the legislature resumes sitting next week, will be forcing municipalities to seek provincial approval before installing new bike lanes that remove a vehicle lane.
Sarkaria said the Ford government will not demand that current bike lanes be removed, but will compel local governments to pause any plans on new lanes that could affect vehicle traffic, and try to make the case for their existence with data.
The Brampton South MPP made it clear he's not talking about how many cyclists are expected to use a new lane, or the potential safety benefits for bike riders who have seen a half-dozen fellow cyclists killed by drivers so far this year.
The "data is in commute times. I hear from every single person," Sarkaria said, citing conversations he's had with others about traffic on Bloor Street and University Avenue, and referencing his own personal commute from Brampton to Queen's Park.
The minister did not offer any data of his own, for example, quantifying delays due to bike lanes versus road construction, to justify the legislation. A Star request to his office Tuesday went unanswered.
Make no mistake - this is an unusual intervention. The Ontario government has a lot on its plate, from crises in housing and health care to the economy of Canada's most populous province. Adding two metres of asphalt on the side of a municipal road has not, over decades of bike lane construction, been considered a provincial priority.
But, as with the Ontario Place redevelopment and the 2018 cut to the size of Toronto city council, all roads lead back to Doug Ford, the Etobicoke business person, former Toronto city councillor and 2014 mayoral election loser who now calls the shots at Queen's Park.
Ford last month foreshadowed the coming legislation by taking aim at bike lanes installed on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke, citing "a petition just in Etobicoke for 50,000 people signing to get rid of these bike lanes," adding: "You might see four or five bikes as you see traffic lined up all the way down the road for kilometres and kilometres ... It's an absolute disaster."
There is a change.org petition with 13,514 names of people who aren't all from Etobicoke. Rather than scrapping the lanes, it calls for "a re-evaluation and balanced integration of bike lanes on Bloor St. in Etobicoke."
Ford's other objections reprise those he and his late brother, former mayor Rob Ford, made at city hall more than a decade ago. The premier cited bike lanes causing trouble for EMS vehicles, although the city's EMS bosses have told city hall they are not a problem. He also said cyclists have no business on busy main streets, echoing Rob Ford's line that bike lane users are "swimming with the sharks. Sooner or later, you're going to get bitten."
That view of cycling has mostly disappeared at city hall, with debate but little controversy in recent years over expansion of the network.
City data suggests the installation of protected bike lanes slows vehicle traffic, at least a bit, on some routes and not on others.
For example, morning rush-hour travel times on Bloor Street West eastbound between Runnymede Road and Aberfoyle Crescent helped add 2.8 minutes to the pre-bike lane average of 7.5 minutes for vehicles, and an extra 1.5 minutes for westbound drivers.
"Once signal timings are adjusted in fall 2024, impacts to travel times are expected to decrease," city staff say.
Increased congestion on University Avenue, city traffic experts say, is most likely due to Ontario Line construction.
The improved safety of cyclists on Bloor is not a talking point. Provincial officials, including Sarkaria, instead focus on driver sentiments, including that the University bike lanes are a gridlock trigger.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, a cycling advocate, has heard from unhappy Etobicoke residents including Ford - who now wields more control over Toronto than he ever did at city hall - and is promising changes for Bloor. She recently proposed "a compromise that would reopen some car lanes but keep the bike lanes and make the design work better."
Chow's office told the Star on Tuesday: "For all major cycling infrastructure projects on major roads the city undertakes ongoing review and modifications to ensure it works as planned. That's how we build sustainable cycling infrastructure."
All of this raises the question why Ford seems low-key obsessed with Toronto bike lanes when Ontarians can't find a GP to look after them and there's little evidence that his push to get housing built to alleviate the affordability crisis is actually working. Also, if bike lanes that took space from vehicle traffic are the problem, why not force municipalities to rip them all out?
After watching Ford professionally for 14 years, my best guess is that it's both intensely personal and shrewdly political.
He seems to have brought with him to Queen's Park a list of unfinished business. Slashing the size of council is one example. Also, it's difficult to watch Ford push the controversial spa-focused redevelopment of Ontario Place and not recall his glitzy, Ferris wheel dream of a "world class" waterfront.
But Ford has remained strong in opinion polls - despite scandals including the Greenbelt manoeuvres being probed by the RCMP - by governing with more than his gut. His government is known to do copious polling, with strategic interpretations of that data on how to leverage hot-button issues, to ensure that the premier's Teflon suit doesn't get sticky.
Toronto cyclists are not Ford's key constituency, so attacking bike lanes isn't losing him their votes. People in his suburban and rural base, however, are exasperated with gridlock. They can see bike lanes, so the impending legislation, whether or not there's data to support it, will invoke sighs of "At least somebody is doing something about it."
A provincial election is coming. You can feel it.
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Subject: Automobiles; Local elections; Legislation; Mayors; Traffic congestion; Redevelopment; Roads & highways; Vehicles; Bicycling

People: Ford, Rob

Title: Ford's move appears to be more about votes than gridlock:   There's little evidence to show that protecting cyclists slows down drivers

Document 33 of 500

Are you eligible, and for how much?: Everything to know as payments begin

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/are-you-eligible-how-much/docview/3116941803/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Are+you+eligible%2C+and+for+how+much%3F%3A+Everything+to+know+as+payments+begin&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians may have seen some extra cash in their bank accounts on Tuesday, thanks to legislation that has been the focal point of growing political debate.
Most Ontarians will receive at least $140 per quarter from the carbon price rebate, up from $122 last year thanks to a boost to the rebate in April.
The rebate is delivered every three months through deposits as a direct refund for the Liberal government's carbon levy - a program, often referred to by the opposition Conservatives as a "carbon tax," that has two components.
The scheme includes a pricing system for carbon-emitting heavy industries and a fuel charge on gasoline and home heating.
Here's what you need to know about the program and whether you're eligible.
What is the carbon tax rebate?
The credit is meant to help individuals and families offset the extra costs from the federal government's pollution pricing, according to the Canada Revenue Agency. In a review of the program, the parliamentary budget officer found in July 2023 that most Canadians in 2021 got more from the Canada Carbon Rebate (CCR) than they paid through the carbon levy. But, the budget watchdog, in a more expansive analysis, found that Canadians would see a "net loss" in the coming years from the carbon pricing scheme and rebate.
Another more recent analysis by the parliamentary budget officer, from Oct. 10, found that the industrial carbon price was more effective in slashing greenhouse gas emission than the consumer price.
The Chiefs of Ontario and the Attawapiskat First Nation called for a judicial review of the carbon levy in November 2023 over their concerns about the disproportionate impact of the pricing scheme.
The government made some changes to the carbon pricing system in late October 2023, doubling the top-up to the rebate for rural Canadians and temporarily pausing the fuel charge for heating oil.
In Ontario, the carbon pricing program is administered at a federal level, with the federal government returning 90 per cent of proceeds from those charges to eligible residents.
The CCR has gained attention not only for its rebrand - which the government implemented in an attempt to make its contentious climate policy more palatable - but also for resistance from provincial leaders.
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe in February said his province would not submit the money it owed for the carbon price on natural gas, leading to the federal government responding by threatening to deny payment of the rebate to Saskatchewan residents.
Several premiers across the country, including Ontario Premier Doug Ford, called on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to pause the carbon price increase on April 1. Trudeau did not oblige, accusing conservative premiers of "(making) political hay" out of the carbon price and challenging them to develop an alternative policy to reduce emissions.
In April, Jagmeet Singh backpedalled on his support for the consumer portion of the carbon levy, emphasizing the need for climate policies that target "big polluters."
Who is eligible for
the carbon tax rebate?
Residents of Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario were eligible when the program first rolled out and the program was expanded in July 2023 to include Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and New Brunswick.
To receive the benefit, you must be a resident of a CCR-eligible province on the first day of the payment month. You must also be at least 19 years old. If you are under that age, you must either have (or previously had) a spouse or common-law partner, or are (or previously were) a parent and live (or previously lived) with your child.
How much will I receive
in carbon tax rebate credits?
Ontarians eligible for the rebate will receive a base rebate of $140, with an additional $70 for a second adult in the family and $35 for each child under 19. A family of four in Ontario, according to the federal government, would receive $280.
The payments are universal and not dependent on income levels. Here's how it breaks down by province, for an individual, per quarter:
Alberta: $225
Manitoba: $150
New Brunswick: $95
Newfoundland and Labrador: $149
Nova Scotia: $103
Ontario: $140
Prince Edward Island: $110
Saskatchewan: $188
The CCR also includes a 20 per cent supplemental bump for residents in small and rural communities, which includes all eligible P.E.I. residents, according to the government. However, some rural communities have spoken out about the rules demarcating rural towns, saying the government has unfairly looped them in with nearby cities.
Recipients can also expect quarterly payments in January, April and July next year.
How do I receive
the carbon tax payment?
Eligible CCR recipients should see the credit in the form of a cheque or direct deposit automatically, without needing to apply. Canadians who file their taxes electronically before March 15 should receive their payment on the quarterly schedule, according to the government's website. Those filing later can expect their CCR payments six to eight weeks after their tax return is assessed.
If you have a spouse or common-law partner, the person whose tax return was assessed first will receive the total credit for the family. It doesn't matter which person receives the rebate as the total amount will be the same.
People with tax debt will see the credit automatically subtracted from the amount they owe.
The Canada Revenue Agency noted it may take up to 10 working days to receive a CCR payment.
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star

Trudeau government plans ad campaign to promote carbon pricing - and rebates, Sept. 8

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3116941349/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Re%3A+A+night+at+one+of+Toronto%27s+busiest+emergency+rooms%2C+Oct.+13+Re%3A+Foreign+doctors+won%27t+solve+our+family+physician+crisis.+Here%27s+what+might%2C+Oct.+13+Re%3A+Canadian+universities+see+international+students%27+enrolment+down+45+per+cent+after+rule+change.+%27The+system+is+just+hanging+on%27%2C+Oct.+7+Re%3A+%27Too+little%2C+too+late%27%3F+Trudeau+government+plans+ad+campaign+to+promote+carbon+pricing+-+and+rebates%2C+Sept.+8&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An assault on human dignity allowed to fester
Re: A night at one of Toronto's busiest emergency rooms, Oct. 13
I'm not sure there could be a more damning indictment of the sad state in Ontario's ERs than that described in Francine Kopun's article. Crowding and human suffering have been detailed for more than two decades; what has received insufficient attention is the fundamental assault on human dignity that we have allowed to fester. One of the core principles in the practice of medicine is a patient's fundamental right to confidentiality, the relief of their suffering and the dignity of the person. This has just become impossible in any Ontario ER as we examine people in hallways, chairs, and waiting rooms within earshot of other patients. We have normalized the unacceptable in the interests of shaving down an eight-hour ER wait to six or seven. This is made even worse in a rural ER where everybody knows everybody else and the discussion of an STD, alcohol abuse or marital infidelity will soon be news all over town. This is not OK, it never was OK and it's well past time emergency physicians reclaimed our historic right and duty to provide patients with the respect and privacy they deserve.
Alan Drummond MD, Canadian Association of Emergency
Physicians Ottawa, Perth, Ont.
The doctors we need are already here
Re: Foreign doctors won't solve our family physician crisis. Here's what might, Oct. 13
Let's understand one thing: we are raising the next generation of doctors right under our noses, yet these wonderful young people are being barred from being part of our solution by an out-of-date hierarchy of wrong-minded old-school thinkers and a government that supports their ineptitude. I know of this first-hand. My highly qualified and competent nephew, a high achiever in attaining his master's degree in anatomy and cellular biology with high honours and recognition, cannot gain acceptance to a medical school here and so we will lose this treasured and badly needed human asset to Australia where he will be welcomed. Something is very wrong here. Yet we are powerless to find a solution in a Doug Ford Ontario, intent on destroying our once-admired health-care system. A change in government must be affected as a first step to righting our health-care ship.
David Ottenbrite, Cambridge, Ont.
Myopic vision
on international students
Re: Canadian universities see international students' enrolment down 45 per cent after rule change. 'The system is just hanging on', Oct. 7
Cuts to international student programs at Ontario universities are hurting us all and diminishing our worldwide reputation. Foreign students do important research such as in stem cells advances that saves lives. We need to welcome foreign students and encourage them to remain in the province.
Katherine Borthwick, Hamilton
Courage, principles and the carbon tax
Re: 'Too little, too late'? Trudeau government plans ad campaign to promote carbon pricing - and rebates, Sept. 8
Whether you like him or not, or agree with him or not, you have to admire Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's commitment to the carbon price issue. He believes that placing a price on carbon is an effective way to reduce carbon emissions and that it is vital to do so. Trudeau also knows that he is losing support and perhaps the next election over the carbon tax. When someone commits to doing the right thing, takes a beating for it and then keeps on doing the right thing, that is evidence that the person has both principles and courage. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, on the other hand, distorts the issue of a carbon tax, exaggerates its costs, ignores its benefits but presents no viable alternatives. He ignores the fact that his party originated the idea of a tax on carbon and the fact that economists regard it as an effective and low-cost tool to reduce carbon dioxide emissions.
Poilievre knows that climate change is real, damages our health and our environment and is already costing billions of dollars in floods, forest fires, infrastructure damage from ever more frequent and violent storms, crop damage and increasing insurance costs. Also, he ignores that climate change is getting worse at an increasing rate and is an existential threat to life on Earth as we know it.
A carbon tax alone will not solve the problem, but it will help. People make decisions every day which will affect their carbon footprint for many years into the future. The prospect of increasing fuel costs, according to economists, will drive most of us to choose less consumptive lifestyles which may mean more efficient transportation, shorter commutes, less extravagant homes. We owe that to future generations.
Dave Schwartz, Kenora, Ont.
Percentage increases widen the income gap
Re: Air Canada pilots could reject pay raise of nearly 40% on Thursday. Here's why, Oct. 9
The unhappiness of the junior pilots underscores the greatest failure of modern education, namely an understanding of percentages. The 40 per cent increase gives the junior pilots an increase of about $40,000 a year. The senior pilots could get $120,000. But the dollar increase in the cost-of-living, based on the standard basket of goods and services, is the same for both.
The failure to understand the "dilation" nature of percentages creates a percentage "fairness" concept in people's minds, a concept that is completely false. Fighting inflation requires equal monetary increases, not equal percentage increases. Percentage increases widen the gap between the rich and the poor. Over the years, the application of percentage salary increases has made the gap of the 1920s into a bottomless abyss in the 2020s.
However, the rich benefit from this situation, as they do from the high interest rates that create inflation. The rich now enjoy a luxurious lifestyle based entirely on decisions made by governments. That is why we will never see a change. And that is why the social impacts of percentages will never be taught in our schools.
Peter Weygang,
Bobcaygeon, Ont.

Document 35 of 500

Ontario to invest $146M in life sciences: Province has goal of growing sector to employ 85,000

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-invest-146m-life-sciences/docview/3116941068/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+to+invest+%24146M+in+life+sciences%3A+Province+has+goal+of+growing+sector+to+employ+85%2C000&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is making what a key minister describes as a "hard pivot" to life sciences, as it looks to replicate its aggressive investment in the electric-vehicle sector in a bid to become a world leader in biomanufacturing and health sciences.
Premier Doug Ford outlined the second phase of the province's life sciences strategy Tuesday as he announced a $146-million investment in the industry.
"It'll be an all-of-government approach," Ford said at the MedTech Conference in Toronto. "We'll have everyone involved to enhance the province's biomanufacturing capacity, to unlock new streams of capital that help entrepreneurs turn their ideas into prototypes, into market-ready products, to improve the adoption and uptake of Ontario-made technologies in clinical settings and to continue to solidify our province as a premier destination for life sciences investments."
The province is putting $46 million into an infrastructure fund to boost research capacity at post-secondary schools and hospitals. It also expects to spend $5 million to speed up clinical trials, $36 million for several startup funds and $40 million in venture capital funds intended to spur growth and innovation.
The province is also expected to soon announce the details of a program to help companies find laboratory space.
Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli said the province has a "very ambitious" goal of growing employment in life sciences to 85,000 jobs by 2030, which would be a significant increase from the 72,000 jobs that currently exist.
"This first-of-a-kind-in-over-a-decade life sciences strategy sets a bold vision to establish Ontario as a biomanufacturing and life sciences hub," he said.
Last month, at a committee hearing going over cost estimates at Queen's Park, Fedeli spelled out the government's shifting focus.
After securing a number of high-profile, high-money investments from Volkswagen, Stellantis and others for the electric-vehicle supply chain, the province is looking to do the same with the life sciences industry.
"There's still more work to do in (the electric-vehicle sector), but for the most part, the companies that are in the sector pretty much all have a dance partner, so we are now pivoting, a very hard pivot, to life sciences," Fedeli told the committee.
"We have 70,000 STEM grads annually," he said, referring to graduates from science, technology, engineering and mathematics programs.
He added the province has a robust research and development ecosystem, along with 72,000 life science workers and 2,000 life sciences companies.
"We've landed over $4 billion in life sciences in the last four years," he went on, referring to companies' investments in the province.
Fedeli said Tuesday that Ontario also wants to become a leader in nuclear medicine, in part because of its 12 CANDU nuclear reactors.
"We have a unique opportunity to become the No. 1 North American supplier in the global nuclear medicines supply chain with critical investments in partnerships with leading Ontario universities and companies," Fedeli said.
"We will continue to support reliable access to cutting edge treatments for cancer patients and expand Ontario's nuclear medicine advantage."
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Document 36 of 500

Second Liberal calls for PM to resign: P.E.I. MP says he's supporting an overwhelming request from his constituents for Trudeau to go

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/second-liberal-calls-pm-resign/docview/3116940989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Second+Liberal+calls+for+PM+to+resign%3A+P.E.I.+MP+says+he%27s+supporting+an+overwhelming+request+from+his+constituents+for+Trudeau+to+go&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A second MP has gone public with a call for Justin Trudeau to step aside from the Liberal leadership ahead of the next election, while another MP says it's time for the caucus to have a tough conversation about leadership in private.
The day after the prime minister dismissed word of a brewing caucus revolt and vowed to lead his party into the campaign, Sean Casey, the member of Parliament for Charlottetown, told CBC's "Power and Politics" host David Cochrane that he supports an overwhelming call by his constituents for Trudeau to go.
"My job has always been to project the voice of the people I represent in Ottawa, to be Charlottetown's representative in Ottawa, and not the reverse. And the message that I've been getting loud and clear and more and more strongly as time goes by is that it's time for him to go. And I agree."
Meanwhile, Montreal MP Anthony Housefather told CTV's Power Play that he believes it is time for the Liberals to talk about who will lead the party when Canadians go to the polls in an election expected at the latest in October 2025, but it is a conversation he wishes would not play out in headlines.
"I support whoever is leader in my party at all times," he said. "But that doesn't mean there shouldn't be a robust caucus discussion about who the best person to lead us in the next election is, and that discussion should happen in caucus. It shouldn't happen in the media."
Housefather's comments came at the end of an unrelated interview, after he was asked if he still supports Trudeau.
Until now, only New Brunswick MP Wayne Long had publicly declared his belief it was time for Trudeau to go, although the Star has reported on widespread unease in Liberal ranks about the dismal polls, and lack of party readiness for the next campaign.
More than two dozen caucus members have discussed signing a letter pledging to be part of an effort to force the issue, with some suggesting between 30 and 40 could join the effort. Casey said he had not been a part of that effort.
After the devastating Liberal byelection loss on June 24 of the Toronto-St. Paul's seat, Trudeau made rounds of calls to calm caucus dissent. Casey said he told Trudeau directly on July 5 that while history would be kind to the prime minister, the electorate was no longer listening.
Then came another byelection loss on Sept. 16 in Montreal's LaSalle-Émard-Verdun - a riding the Liberals lost to the Bloc Québécois.
MPs had been primed to expect a close call in Montreal, with party officials at the Nanaimo caucus retreat just a week earlier previewing that results would be tight.
However, sources told the Star that MPs also expected a clear response from Trudeau and his team about how the party would recover, and no clear plan has emerged.
On Sunday, the party finally named a new campaign director, Ontario veteran Andrew Bevan, to replace Jeremy Broadhurst, who himself had told Trudeau in August that he did not believe the prime minister could lead the Liberals to a fourth victory.
Casey told CBC that leadership concerns about Trudeau have replaced health care as the number one issue he encounters with voters at the door. "People have had enough. They've tuned him out, and they want him to go. I can't tell you the number of people that have said to me, 'Sean, I'm afraid of Poilievre. I want to vote for you, but I can't.'"
Casey said he intends to run again even if Trudeau remains, but noted the Liberal leader has not yet signed his candidate nomination papers and "I think this interview may have an impact on that."
Casey denied he was lobbying for any other would-be aspirant to Trudeau's job.
"I'm advocating for a leadership change, not for a particular candidate," but Casey said he believes a leadership race would spark new ideas and energy in the party.
"Quite frankly, if Justin Trudeau was to leave, there would be risk," said Casey. "If there was a divisive leadership contest, there would be risk, but the upside of having a new leader is significantly higher than if he stays in my view."
Housefather was one of three Liberal MPs who voted against a contentious pro-Palestine NDP motion and previously threatened to leave the Liberal caucus over the governing party's stance on the Israel-Hamas war and rising antisemitism in Canada.
While Trudeau was away at an international summit last week, growing unease in Liberal ranks snowballed into an effort to formulate a way for Liberal MPs to push Trudeau to a decision to leave. The Liberal caucus never agreed to sign on to rules under the Reform Act that would have allowed a critical mass of MPs to force the question.
Trudeau on Monday responded tersely when asked if he intended to stay at the helm of the party, saying, "Oui. Yes."
The question was asked during an extraordinary news conference following Canada's expulsion of six Indian diplomats believed to be associated with intimidation and violence against Sikh Canadians.
"I had many conversations with members of my team and caucus members over the past number of days, and all those conversations were focused on the well-being and the safety of Canadians faced with these significant security concerns and allegations," Trudeau told reporters.
"The safety of Canadians is the number one thing that any government, that any parliamentarians should be preoccupied with, and that is exactly what we're focused on. There will be time to talk about internal party intrigue at another moment."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrives on Parliament Hill, Monday. When asked by CTV's "Power Play" if he still supports Trudeau, Montreal Liberal MP Anthony Housefather said he thinks it's time for the party to talk about a new leader in the next election.Justin Tang The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles and Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Document 37 of 500

The Freedom Convoy didn't die. It is finding friends among willing politicians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/freedom-convoy-didnt-die-is-finding-friends-among/docview/3117133342/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Freedom+Convoy+didn%27t+die.+It+is+finding+friends+among+willing+politicians&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The greatest danger of the so-called "Freedom Convoy" was not to Ottawa. Yes, the convoy tortured the city for more than a month, and that shouldn't be forgotten, or downplayed. But it passed, even if the lonely leftovers that still hang around to harass politicians remain a smaller symptom of a wider ill.
No, the most dangerous part of the convoy was always its most farcical element, which is that some of the main organizers, who had a history of delusional and hateful comments, aimed to overthrow the government. No matter how supine Ottawa police were, no matter how absent Ontario premier Doug Ford or mayor Jim Watson was, no matter the overlapping paralysis of jurisdictions, we all laughed at that.
Two-and-a-half years later, and elements of the movement are being embraced by politicians more than ever before. What if the convoy is succeeding at changing our governments for the worse?
You could see it coming at the time. Former Saskatchewan premier Brad Wall gave convoy leaders advice, and they met with former CPC leader Andrew Scheer in Regina, and various MPs cheered. Then-Conservative leader Erin O'Toole took a half-hearted approach in meeting organizers, and was criticized for it. He was ejected for many reasons, after which interim CPC leader Candace Bergen saw an opportunity, and so did Pierre Poilievre. He glad-handed with convoy folks, and is now the leader of the Conservative party, and quite likely to be our next prime minister, at some point.
Poilievre has dutifully lined up with some of the most deluded members of the public: marching on the day before Canada Day in 2022 with anti-vaccine veteran James Topp, or his private member's bill that would have banned vaccine mandates for travellers and federal employees, marketed in anti-vaccine code words that implied more than COVID vaccines.
But it's worsening. In Alberta, Danielle Smith's government fired Alberta's then chief medical officer of health Deena Hinshaw, which Smith justified by saying of public health, "they shut down the economy, they put on masks, they put on restrictions, and I thought, we're not going to let that happen."
The Alberta Human Rights Act was updated to include the right to refuse a vaccine or to not wear a mask, and Smith plans to do the same with the Alberta Bill of Rights. Her new deputy chief of staff, by the way, owns a restaurant that in 2022 accepted puppy pictures instead of vaccination passports for entry.
And in B.C., Conservative Leader John Rustad crossed into a different place. He has not only told an anti-vaccine group that he regretted getting vaccinated, but in a video unearthed by the indefatigable PressProgress, he was asked about a concept that the most angry and deluded anti-vaccine activists use: Nuremberg 2.0.
"Are you for or against a Nuremberg 2.0?" asked anti-vaccine activist Jedediah Ferguson, making it sound like "Newemberg."
"A do it bigger 2.0, sorry?" asked Rustad, confused.
"Nuremberg 2.0," repeated Ferguson, smiling.
"Nuremberg 2.0," said Rustad, a smile spreading across his face. "Ah, yes. That's probably something that's outside of my scope."
"I know I put you on the hot spot right there, for sure, but I had to ask," said Ferguson.
"No, no, it's fine," said Rustad. "I can say, that's something that's sort of outside the scope in terms of jurisdiction of British Columbia, but we would certainly be participating with other jurisdictions as we look at those sorts of issues."
Nuremberg 2.0 has long been a rallying cry among the most angry anti-vaccine activists: it is about show trials of doctors, politicians and journalists, and punishment ranging from prison to executions. Rustad later claimed to have misunderstood the question. The best you could probably say, based on his reaction and his answer, is that he was pandering to what he sees as his base. After all, in an election full of conspiracists, the most controversial candidate, Brent Chapman, has said there are "probably 45, maybe 50 Freedom people that are running for this party, and I know that some of our media says, oh, they're wack jobs."
Smith and Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe have played footsie with chemtrail conspiracists, but Rustad's answer was a new line crossed, in a party that is way out there. And he could win.
Radicalization tends to go in one direction. The convoy to Ottawa was, at its heart, divorced from reality, even if you could understand some of its energy: As a young mother named Dasha told me in January 2022 at a convoy rally north of Toronto, "I'm just a regular person that wants to go back to normal. Why can't we just call it a day and say this is a regular flu just like any other flu?" She also, as it happened, thought hospitals were lying about being full of patients.
She was wrong. The anti-vaccine radicals are wrong. It was sad, talking to Dasha: she had been fed disinformation under strenuous circumstances, and was clearly vulnerable. She was struggling with the world.
She's far from alone, and now the most unmoored reactions to the pandemic are leaching into governments across the country, and maybe into a federal government that has a habit of turning people who disagree with them into enemies.
The world of radical unreality is biting back, and we need conservative politicians with cognitive clarity and genuine principles beyond partisanship, who can transcend the angriest and most unhinged parts of their base. We face real-world problems, in the same way that we did under the COVID emergency, and we need people who deal with the world as it is.
If that doesn't happen, as under the COVID emergency, we will all suffer.
CAPTION: Police move in to remove protesters from downtown Ottawa near Parliament Hill on Feb. 19, 2022 following weeks of demonstrations.

Subject: Federal employees; COVID-19 vaccines; Political activism; Politicians

Document 38 of 500

Why Doug Ford hitting the brakes on bike lanes looks to be more about votes than gridlock

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-doug-ford-hitting-brakes-on-bike-lanes-looks/docview/3116942438/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Doug+Ford+hitting+the+brakes+on+bike+lanes+looks+to+be+more+about+votes+than+gridlock&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's plan to poke a stick into the spokes of bike lane expansion is ostensibly about ending gridlock.
At the moment, however, there is little proof that bike lanes are a major cause of the aggravating, internationally infamous traffic migraine bedevilling Toronto, much less jams in the smaller Ontario cities that will also be affected by provincial legislation dictating municipal policy.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria on Tuesday announced that his government's first piece of business, when the legislature resumes sitting next week, will be forcing municipalities to seek provincial approval before installing new bike lanes that remove a vehicle lane.
Sarkaria said the Ford government will not demand that current bike lanes be removed, but will compel local governments to pause any plans on new lanes that could affect vehicle traffic, and try to make the case for their existence with data.
The Brampton South MPP made it clear he's not talking about how many cyclists are expected to use a new lane, or the potential safety benefits for bike riders who have seen a half-dozen fellow cyclists killed by drivers so far this year.
The "data is in commute times. I hear from every single person," Sarkaria said, citing conversations he's had with others about traffic on Bloor Street and University Avenue, and referencing his own personal commute from Brampton to Queen's Park.
The minister did not offer any data of his own, for example, quantifying delays due to bike lanes versus road construction, to justify the legislation. A Star request to his office Tuesday went unanswered.
Make no mistake - this is an unusual intervention. The Ontario government has a lot on its plate, from crises in housing and health care to the economy of Canada's most populous province. Adding two metres of asphalt on the side of a municipal road has not, over decades of bike lane construction, been considered a provincial priority.
But, as with the Ontario Place redevelopment and the 2018 cut to the size of Toronto city council, all roads lead back to Doug Ford, the Etobicoke business person, former Toronto city councillor and 2014 mayoral election loser who now calls the shots at Queen's Park.
Ford last month foreshadowed the coming legislation by taking aim at bike lanes installed on Bloor Street West in Etobicoke, citing "a petition just in Etobicoke for 50,000 people signing to get rid of these bike lanes," adding: "You might see four or five bikes as you see traffic lined up all the way down the road for kilometres and kilometres. ... It's an absolute disaster."
There is a change.org petition with 13,514 names of people who aren't all from Etobicoke. Rather than scrapping the lanes, it calls for "a re-evaluation and balanced integration of bike lanes on Bloor St. in Etobicoke."
Ford's other objections reprise those he and his late brother, former mayor Rob Ford, made at city hall more than a decade ago. The premier cited bike lanes causing trouble for EMS vehicles, although the city's EMS bosses have told city hall they are not a problem. He also said cyclists have no business on busy main streets, echoing Rob Ford's line that bike lane users are "swimming with the sharks. Sooner or later, you're going to get bitten."
That view of cycling has mostly disappeared at city hall, with debate but little controversy in recent years over expansion of the network.
City data suggests the installation of protected bike lanes slows vehicle traffic, at least a bit, on some routes and not on others.
For example, morning rush-hour travel times on Bloor Street West eastbound between Runnymede Road and Aberfoyle Crescent helped add 2.8 minutes to the pre-bike lane average of 7.5 minutes for vehicles, and an extra 1.5 minutes for westbound drivers.
"Once signal timings are adjusted in fall 2024, impacts to travel times are expected to decrease," city staff say.
Increased congestion on University, city traffic experts say, is most likely due to Ontario Line construction.
The improved safety of cyclists on Bloor is not a talking point. Provincial officials, including Sarkaria, instead focus on driver sentiments, including that the University bike lanes are a gridlock trigger.
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, a cycling advocate, has heard from unhappy Etobicoke residents including Ford - who now wields more control over Toronto than he ever did at city hall - and is promising changes for Bloor. She recently proposed "a compromise that would reopen some car lanes but keep the bike lanes and make the design work better."
Chow's office told the Star on Tuesday: "For all major cycling infrastructure projects on major roads the city undertakes ongoing review and modifications to ensure it works as planned. That's how we build sustainable cycling infrastructure."
All of this raises the question why Ford seems low-key obsessed with Toronto bike lanes when Ontarians can't find a GP to look after them and there's little evidence that his push to get housing built to alleviate the affordability crisis is actually working. Also, if bike lanes that took space from vehicle traffic are the problem, why not force municipalities to rip them all out?
After watching Ford professionally for 14 years, my best guess is that it's both intensely personal and shrewdly political.
He seems to have brought with him to Queen's Park a list of unfinished business. Slashing the size of council is one example. Also, it's difficult to watch Ford push the controversial spa-focused redevelopment of Ontario Place and not recall his glitzy, Ferris wheel dream of a "world class" waterfront.
But Ford has remained strong in opinion polls - despite scandals including the Greenbelt manoeuvres being probed by the RCMP - by governing with more than his gut. His government is known to do copious polling, with strategic interpretations of that data on how to leverage hot-button issues, to ensure that the premier's Teflon suit doesn't get sticky.
Toronto cyclists are not Ford's key constituency, so attacking bike lanes isn't losing him their votes. People in his suburban and rural base, however, are exasperated with gridlock. They can see bike lanes, so the impending legislation, whether or not there's data to support it, will invoke sighs of "At least somebody is doing something about it." A provincial election is coming. You can feel it.
CAPTION: The Ford government said it will not demand existing bike lanes be removed but will compel local governments to pause any plans on new lanes that could impact vehicle traffic.

Subject: Automobiles; Local elections; Legislation; Mayors; Traffic congestion; Redevelopment; Roads & highways; Bicycling; Vehicles

People: Ford, Rob

Document 39 of 500

Second Liberal MP goes public with call for Justin Trudeau to resign: 'People have had enough'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/second-liberal-mp-goes-public-with-call-justin/docview/3116941886/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Second+Liberal+MP+goes+public+with+call+for+Justin+Trudeau+to+resign%3A+%27People+have+had+enough%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-A second MP has gone public with a call for Justin Trudeau to step aside from the Liberal leadership ahead of the next election, while another MP says it's time for the caucus to have a tough conversation about leadership in private.
The day after the prime minister dismissed word of a brewing caucus revolt and vowed to lead his party into the campaign, Sean Casey, the member of Parliament for Charlottetown, PEI, told CBC's Power and Politics host David Cochrane that he supports an overwhelming call by his constituents for Trudeau to go.
"My job has always been to project the voice of the people I represent in Ottawa, to be Charlottetown's representative in Ottawa, and not the reverse. And the message that I've been getting loud and clear and more and more strongly as time goes by is that it's time for him to go. And I agree."
Meanwhile, Montreal MP Anthony Housefather told CTV's Power Play that he believes it is time for the Liberals to talk about who will lead the party when Canadians go to the polls in an election expected at the latest in October 2025, but it is a conversation he wishes would not play out in headlines.
"I support whoever is leader in my party at all times," he said. "But that doesn't mean there shouldn't be a robust caucus discussion about who the best person to lead us in the next election is, and that discussion should happen in caucus. It shouldn't happen in the media."
Housefather's comments came at the end of an unrelated interview, after he was asked if he still supports Trudeau.
Until now, only New Brunswick MP Wayne Long had publicly declared his belief it was time for Trudeau to go, although the Star has reported on widespread unease in Liberal ranks about the dismal polls, and lack of party readiness for the next campaign.
More than two dozen caucus members have discussed signing a letter pledging to be part of an effort to force the issue, with some suggesting between 30 and 40 could join the effort. Casey said he had not been a part of that effort.
After the devastating Liberal byelection loss on June 24 of the Toronto-St. Paul's seat, Trudeau made rounds of calls to calm caucus dissent. Casey said he told Trudeau directly on July 5 that while history would be kind to the prime minister, the electorate was no longer listening.
Then came another byelection loss on Sept. 16 in Montreal's LaSalle-Émard-Verdun - a riding the Liberals lost to the Bloc Québécois.
MPs had been primed to expect a close call in Montreal, with party officials at the Nanaimo caucus retreat just a week earlier previewing that results would be tight.
However, sources told the Star that MPs also expected a clear response from Trudeau and his team about how the party would recover, and no clear plan has emerged.
On Sunday, the party finally named a new campaign director, Ontario veteran Andrew Bevan, to replace Jeremy Broadhurst, who himself had told Trudeau in August that he did not believe the prime minister could lead the Liberals to a fourth victory.
Casey told CBC that leadership concerns about Trudeau have replaced health care as the number one issue he encounters with voters at the door. "People have had enough. They've tuned him out, and they want him to go. I can't tell you the number of people that have said to me, 'Sean, I'm afraid of Poilievre. I want to vote for you, but I can't.'"
Casey said he intends to run again even if Trudeau remains, but noted the Liberal leader has not yet signed his candidate nomination papers and "I think this interview may have an impact on that."
Casey denied he was lobbying for any other would-be aspirant to Trudeau's job.
"I'm advocating for a leadership change, not for a particular candidate," but Casey said he believes a leadership race would spark new ideas and energy in the party.
"Quite frankly, if Justin Trudeau was to leave, there would be risk," said Casey. "If there was a divisive leadership contest, there would be risk, but the upside of having a new leader is significantly higher than if he stays in my view."
Housefather was one of three Liberal MPs who voted against a contentious pro-Palestine NDP motion and previously threatened to leave the Liberal caucus over the governing party's stance on the Israel-Hamas war and rising antisemitism in Canada.
While Trudeau was away at an international summit last week, growing unease in Liberal ranks snowballed into an effort to formulate a way for Liberal MPs to push Trudeau to a decision to leave. The Liberal caucus never agreed to sign on to rules under the Reform Act that would have allowed a critical mass of MPs to force the question.
Trudeau on Monday responded tersely when asked if he intended to stay at the helm of the party, saying, "Oui. Yes."
The question was asked during an extraordinary news conference following Canada's expulsion of six Indian diplomats believed to be associated with intimidation and violence against Sikh Canadians.
"I had many conversations with members of my team and caucus members over the past number of days, and all those conversations were focused on the well-being and the safety of Canadians faced with these significant security concerns and allegations," Trudeau told reporters.
"The safety of Canadians is the number one thing that any government, that any parliamentarians should be preoccupied with, and that is exactly what we're focused on. There will be time to talk about internal party intrigue at another moment."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau exits his vehicle as he arrives for a news conference on the investigative efforts related to violent criminal activity occurring in Canada with connections to India, on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, onOct. 14, 2024.

Document 40 of 500

Canada Carbon Rebate payments go out today. Here's how much you can get

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canada-carbon-rebate-payments-go-out-today-heres/docview/3116664612/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada+Carbon+Rebate+payments+go+out+today.+Here%27s+how+much+you+can+get&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians can expect some extra cash in their bank accounts on Tuesday, thanks to legislation that has been the focal point of growing political debate.
Most Ontarians will receive at least $140 per quarter from the carbon price rebate, up from $122 last year thanks to a boost to the rebate in April. The rebate is delivered every three months through deposits as a direct refund for the Liberal government's carbon levy - a program, often referred to by the opposition Conservatives as a "carbon tax," that has two components.
The scheme includes a pricing system for carbon-emitting heavy industries and a fuel charge on gasoline and home heating.
Here's what you need to know about the program and whether you're eligible.
What is the carbon tax rebate?
The credit is meant to help individuals and families offset the extra costs from the federal government's pollution pricing, according to the Canada Revenue Agency. In a review of the program, the parliamentary budget officer found in July 2023 that most Canadians in 2021 got more from the Canada Carbon Rebate (CCR) than they paid through the carbon levy. But, the budget watchdog, in a more expansive analysis, found that Canadians would see a "net loss" in the coming years from the carbon pricing scheme and rebate.
Another more recent analysis by the parliamentary budget officer, from Oct. 10, found that the industrial carbon price was more effective in slashing greenhouse gas emission than the consumer price.
The Chiefs of Ontario and the Attawapiskat First Nation called for a judicial review of the carbon levy in November 2023 over their concerns about the disproportionate impact of the pricing scheme.
The government made some changes to the carbon pricing system in late October 2023, doubling the top-up to the rebate for rural Canadians and temporarily pausing the fuel charge for heating oil.
In Ontario, the carbon pricing program is administered at a federal level, with the federal government returning 90 per cent proceeds from those charges to eligible residents.
The CCR has gained attention not only for its rebrand - which the government implemented in an attempt to make its contentious climate policy more palatable - but also for resistance from provincial leaders. Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe, in February, said his province would not submit the money it owed for the carbon price on natural gas, leading to the federal government responding by threatening to deny payment of the rebate to Saskatchewan residents.
Several premiers across the country, including Ontario Premier Doug Ford, called on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to pause the carbon price increase on April 1. Trudeau did not oblige, accusing conservative premiers of "(making) political hay" out of the carbon price and challenging them to develop an alternative policy to reduce emissions.
In April, Jagmeet Singh backpedaled on his support for the consumer portion of the carbon levy, emphasizing the need for climate policies that target "big polluters."
Who is eligible for the carbon tax rebate?
Residents of Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario were eligible when the program first rolled out and the program was expanded in July 2023 to include Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and New Brunswick.
To receive the benefit, you must be a resident of a CCR-eligible province on the first day of the payment month. You must also be at least 19 years old. If you are under that age, you must either have (or previously had) a spouse or common-law partner, or are (or previously were) a parent and live (or previously lived) with your child.
How much will I receive in carbon tax rebate credits?
Ontarians eligible for the rebate will receive a base rebate of $140, with an additional $70 for a second adult in the family and $35 for each child under 19. A family of four in Ontario, according to the federal government, would receive $280.
The payments are universal and not dependent on income levels. Here's how it breaks down by province, for an individual, per quarter:
Alberta: $225Manitoba: $150New Brunswick: $95Newfoundland and Labrador: $149Nova Scotia: $103Ontario: $140Prince Edward Island: $110Saskatchewan: $188
The CCR also includes a 20 per cent supplemental bump for residents in small and rural communities, which includes all eligible P.E.I. residents, according to the government. However, some rural communities have spoken out about the rules demarcating rural towns, saying the government has unfairly looped them in with nearby cities.
Recipients can also expect quarterly payments in January, April and July next year.
How do I receive the carbon tax payment?
Eligible CCR recipients should see the credit in the form of a cheque or direct deposit automatically, without needing to apply. Canadians who file their taxes electronically before March 15 should receive their payment on the quarterly schedule, according to the government's website. Those filing later can expect their CCR payments six to eight weeks after their tax return is assessed.
If you have a spouse or common-law partner, the person whose tax return was assessed first will receive the total credit for the family. It doesn't matter which person receives the rebate as the total amount will be the same.
People with tax debt will see the credit automatically subtracted from the amount they owe.
The Canada Revenue Agency noted it may take up to 10 working days to receive a CCR payment.
CAPTION: The carbon levy, a core part of the Liberal government's climate plan, has been the target of criticism from the Tory opposition.

Subject: Federal government; Canadians; Rural areas; Climate policy; Carbon; Judicial reviews; Electronic filing of tax returns; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Electronic filing of tax returns Environmental tax
