Document 41 of 500

Canada's rising tensions with India + reflecting on week one of Michael Thompson's trial

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-rising-tensions-with-india-reflecting-on/docview/3116663518/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+rising+tensions+with+India+%2B+reflecting+on+week+one+of+Michael+Thompson%27s+trial&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, October 15 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on rising Canada-India tensions, the first week of Michael Thompson's sex assault trial and the economic impact of Taylor Swift.
DON'T MISSCanada and India expelled each other's diplomats as tensions escalate
Canada-India relations took a dive Monday, after the RCMP accused Indian diplomats and consular officials of direct involvement in a rash of murders, violence and intimidation across Canada. India's top diplomat was identified as a person of interest in the assassination of a Sikh activist last year and was among the officials expelled Monday after New Delhi refused to co-operate with RCMP investigations. Take a closer look at what's happening.
Go deeper: So far, eight people have been charged with homicides in Canada while 22 stand charged with extortion linked to Indian government agents. Here's an inside look at how the RCMP unravelled the conspiracy.India's response: New Delhi denied responsibility, saying Canada provided no evidence of its alleged crimes. In a tit-for-tat that made the front page of Indian newspapers, six Canadian diplomats have been ordered to leave the country within a week.ICYMI: Monday's confrontation was sparked by the June 2023 killing of Sikh activist Hardeep Singh Nijjar. Here's a quick rundown of the history.What we learned in week one of Michael Thompson's sex assault trial
Although week one of the sitting Toronto councillor's trial ended without hearing from either complainant, there was no shortage of shocking allegations or embarrassing evidence. It's rare for an active Canadian politician to stand trial on such serious charges, Betsy Powell and Ben Spurr write; Thompson is accused of two sexual assaults, including forcing himself on a "very, very drunk" woman in 2022. Here's what you missed last week.
The first witness: A then-22-year-old student took the stand, alleging Thompson invited her to a Muskoka cabin under the guise of a networking event. Instead, a troubling series of events unfolded - here's what she said happened.
Who's Thompson? He was first elected to city council in 2003 and has represented Scarborough Centre ever since. By provincial law, he's entitled to keep his seat unless he's serving a prison sentence.Taylor Swift could be just what Toronto's economy needs
Tay Tay is coming to town and business owners are just as hyped as the Swifties. The superstar's blockbuster Eras Tour is expected to inject hundreds of millions of dollars into Toronto's economy, Mahdis Habibinia reports. So goes the "Swiftonomics" effect, which has seen the singer-songwriter bring a noticeable economic boost to every city she visits. Here's how much Toronto stands to gain.
By the numbers: A new report estimates Swift's two-week visit could have an economic impact of more than $282 million as approximately 240,000 concertgoers flock to her Toronto gigs.The countdown: We're just 30 days out from Swift touching down in Toronto to play six shows at Rogers Centre from Nov. 14 to 16 and Nov. 21 to 23. Are you ready for it?WHAT ELSE
Nearly two-thirds of Ontarians think Doug Ford must do more to deal with homeless encampments.Private colleges are often blamed for Canada's international student boom. The data shows a different reality.Economists anticipate lower inflation when September figures are released later today.Ontario's only science centre is 400 kilometres from Toronto. Here's why that's a problem.He helped make credit scores free for Canadians. Now he wants to make borrowing easier too.A woman is dead after being struck by a school bus driver in the Beaches.Cottage country? Calgary? Here's where renovators went when home flipping in Toronto got harder.Small business credit card transaction fees are about to drop - but not all businesses will benefit. Here's why.Out with Red Delicious, in with Snowflake? These tasty new apple breeds could be coming to stores near you. In defence of the community banquet hall: Why we must preserve this under appreciated part of suburbia.I watched the same concert at two well-known Toronto venues - and the music sounded bolder and more dynamic at one of them."Bigger, better, braver ... wiser." Celebrating a rock star chef and a Toronto fashion legend.POV
A fringe party packed with conspiracy theorists could soon lead one of Canada's largest provinces - and I'm not surprised.CLOSE-UP
CAPE CANAVERAL: A NASA spacecraft blasted off aboard a SpaceX Falcon Heavy rocket Monday, bound for Jupiter's mysterious moon Europa - on a mission to reveal whether its vast, hidden ocean might hold the keys to life. Take a closer look at what scientists are hoping to find.

Document 42 of 500

Ford government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-unveils-more-details-on-plan-curb/docview/3116663216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+unveils+more+details+on+plan+to+curb+new+bike+lanes+on+major+roads&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is hitting the brakes on new bike lanes, warning Toronto and other municipalities they'll need provincial approval before taking out lanes of traffic for cars and trucks.
The aim is to keep bike lanes off major thoroughfares like Bloor Street West, Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria told reporters Tuesday as he previewed upcoming legislation from Premier Doug Ford's government.
Growth in bike lanes is "out of control," he charged at a pool hall and bar on Bloor near Islington, where local residents and businesses have complained new bike lanes are lightly used, particularly in cold or wet weather, and have slowed traffic.
"We're seeing an explosion in bike lanes, including many that were installed during the pandemic when their impact on traffic was unclear," said Sarkaria, who also announced plans to design new highways for speed limits above 120 km/h and to increase speed limits to 110 km/h on existing ones.
"While strategically placed bike lanes are a critical part of every city ... what cities should not be doing, however, is taking away lanes of traffic on our most congested roads," he added.
"Municipalities are still free to install bike lanes where they do not remove traffic."
There was quick pushback from Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow and a prominent cycling lawyer as the Progressive Conservatives prepare to unveil the legislation when MPPs return from an extended summer break next Monday amid speculation about an early provincial election.
"I do not support limiting city powers," Chow said in a statement to the Star.
"It's always better when we work together to get things right," added the mayor, who last week proposed tweaking some bike lanes to ease concerns about overall traffic flow.
"To tackle congestion and keep people safe on city roads, we need all types of transportation. The province should concentrate on their job of finally getting the Eglinton Crosstown and Finch LRT open, which will have a huge impact on congestion."
The legislation would be a blow to Toronto city council's plan, passed over the summer, to add 100 kilometres of bike lanes to major routes over the next three years as part of efforts to reduce vehicle emissions.
Cycling lawyer David Shellnutt accused the province of starting an "us vs. them campaign" dividing road users and making life more dangerous for cyclists in a year that has seen six killed on Toronto streets in addition to deaths in the Belleville, Guelph and Niagara areas.
"Ontarians must see these bike lane attacks from what they are: a tool to distract us from the real problems pressing this province," Shellnutt added in a statement hinting at the RCMP investigation of Ford's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal.
"Even in Toronto, most will recognize that it is the sheer number of cars on the roads ... and maybe the baffling number of construction projects that are actually increasing gridlock."
The legislation, which government sources confirmed will be called the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," comes as Ford's Progressive Conservatives position themselves for an early election, possibly by next spring - a year ahead of the next scheduled provincewide vote in June 2026.
It's expected the bike lane plan, limiting municipal powers, would appeal to suburban voters who rely more heavily on cars to get around.
It's not clear how long it would take the province to render a decision on proposed new bike lanes. Cycling advocates have expressed concerns that the process could effectively halt new lanes.
Advocates maintain that bike lanes encourage people to get out of their cars and onto two wheels at a time of increasing concern about the impact of vehicle exhaust, climate change, the cost of commuting and congestion.
"More people biking, walking or taking public transit means more people not in cars and less gridlock," said Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre).
Sarkaria said existing bike lanes will stay but the government will ask municipalities for data on all bike lanes that have been installed in the last five years.
Toronto Coun. Brad Bradford (Beaches-East York) said the bike lanes on Bloor Street West and Yonge Street should be "removed immediately" because they make it tougher for customers to patronize businesses and cause vehicular traffic to seek shortcuts through neighbourhoods.
Ford, a former city councillor who lives in Etobicoke, has frequently been critical of the expansion of bike lanes and faced criticism in 2018 for meddling in municipal powers by cutting the size of Toronto city council nearly in half in the midst of that year's local election campaign.
In contrast, the premier has repeatedly said municipalities are best placed to decide where to build new housing as the province reaches for a target of 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
With files from Ben Cohen
CAPTION: The provincial government is moving to limit municipal powers when it comes to building bike lanes.

Subject: Roads & highways

Title: Ford government unveils more details on plan to curb new bike lanes on major roads

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 43 of 500

Cities can't be trusted to build the housing we so desperately need. Just look at this neighbourhood in Mississauga

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cities-cant-be-trusted-build-housing-we-so/docview/3116542019/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cities+can%27t+be+trusted+to+build+the+housing+we+so+desperately+need.+Just+look+at+this+neighbourhood+in+Mississauga&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Earlier this year, Premier Doug Ford told the public that he did not intend to legalize fourplex housing province-wide. Instead, he chose to leave it up to local city councils.
"Queens Park doesn't know best," he declared.
The PCs are opting for the "carrot" approach to getting housing built, hoping that a reward for municipalities from the $1.2B "Building Faster Fund" will coax them into hitting their housing targets.
What Ford did not anticipate was that most municipalities were simply not interested in building housing. Rather, some of Ontario's largest cities are stealthily preventing housing development by burying restrictions in local initiatives.
While there are innumerable examples, Mississauga's latest effort to block home building is particularly shameless. It is using the concept of heritage preservation to not only restrict new housing, but to also shirk its provincial commitments.
The "Streetsville Heritage Conservation District Plan" has been moving through internal reviews before it will eventually find its way before City Council for a vote. The plan is meant to designate a swath of the Streetsville community as "heritage land." Streetsville is known for a handful of 19th and 20th century-style sites which are part of the neighbourhood's charm.
But this is not merely a proposal to protect those sites. Instead, the city is claiming that the entire community - comprising multiple contemporary homes and businesses that do not meet the historical standard - should be marked as "heritage," and intends to subject all of those properties to new restrictions on everything from building additions to paint colour.
Heritage designations are not a new concept. The Ontario Heritage Act lays out guidelines for municipalities to assign this designation, and provides unique provisions to protect those lands. Mississauga has two Heritage Conservation Districts (HCDs), the second of which was designated 20 years ago.
But why, in the midst of a housing crisis, is the City of Mississauga suddenly eager to dust off this provincial tool which comes with specific restrictions on development?
The answer is available to those willing to sift through the 135-page plan. The Streetsville community is home to one of Mississauga's GO train stations, making it a "Major Transit Station Area" (MTSA). MTSAs are meant to prioritize density in areas that are heavily serviced by transit under provincial guidelines.
Currently, Streetsville is a "planned" MTSA, meaning that the intensification requirements have not yet come into effect. Once it converts to a "protected" MTSA, the city must focus housing development in that area. The Streetsville HCD Plan explicitly notes that "where there is conflict between intensification area policies and heritage resource policies, the heritage resource policies will take precedence."
In effect, the City of Mississauga is attempting to use a noble provincial designation as a trojan horse for restricting housing before MTSA requirements open it to growth.
The plan comes with myriad restrictions that make even the most minor construction impossible. Building heights are capped at 2.5 storeys, demolition on any property within the HCD (even if not on a heritage property) would be subject to city approval, and cosmetic changes would have to undergo a subjective assessment to determine whether it complements the neighbourhood "character."
This year, as Mississauga celebrates its 50th anniversary, it has achieved less than 22 per cent of its 2024 housing target.
Faced with city councils that are more invested in blocking housing than building it, the Ford government's incentives are not working. Worse, there is little to no oversight to prevent municipalities from weaponizing policy to block housing outside of the city planning process.
It is time to acknowledge that municipalities cannot be counted on to get it done on housing. Perhaps a "stick" approach from the provincial government will finally give them a compelling reason to build homes in a housing crisis. It's clear the crushing cost to families is not motivation enough.
CAPTION: Behind a suburban neighbourhood in Mississauga sits the city's more modern, dense skyline.
CREDIT: Kelly Singh, Contributor

Subject: Municipal government; Neighborhoods; Housing developments

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing developments

Title: Cities can't be trusted to build the housing we so desperately need. Just look at this neighbourhood in Mississauga

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 15, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Country of publication: Canada

Cities can't be trusted to build housing we need

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cities-cant-be-trusted-build-housing-we-need/docview/3116541974/se-2?accountid=15090

Kelly Singh is the executive
director of More Homes
Mississauga.

Document 45 of 500

Toronto wants European density. Let's see European transit

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-wants-european-density-lets-see-transit/docview/3116541916/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+wants+European+density.+Let%27s+see+European+transit&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto spent decades building a city for cars. This is what it's done to all the human beings who have to live here and get around, Oct. 5
I have shocking news for the urbanists of central Toronto. The suburbs are not actually full of ogres and trolls, Oct. 6
The Oct. 5 Opinion Weekly contained two perspectives on the suburbs. In contrast to Toronto suburbs, Julia Belluz touted the high quality of life, despite their density, in European metropolises, largely made possible by high-quality, high-frequency mass transit. Scott Stinson enjoys the space in the 'burbs, as long as you own a car. They are both correct.
What is NOT correct is the city of Toronto's proposal to allow no-appeal, as-of-right 12-storey apartments throughout newly-designated "avenues" in suburbs like Etobicoke. Such avenues in Vienna, Berlin, or Paris have extensive tram systems and frequent reliable buses. With few exceptions (Eglinton someday), Toronto's "avenues" do not.
Yet, while wringing their hands about traffic congestion, city council and city planners are proposing tens of thousands of new residents on Etobicoke roads like Bloor and Islington with no semblance or prospect of adequate transit.
This must be "if you build it, they will come" magical thinking. But we in Toronto have proof that such density in the suburbs does NOT mean adequate transit will come! Just ask the high-density residents of suburban Jane, Dufferin, and Weston Road as they shiver through long waits for jam-packed buses stuck in traffic. Forget bike lanes; if the city wants more highrises in the suburbs, why aren't we building dedicated bus-only lanes?
Of course, Toronto's suburbs can and should see more higher-density housing. As soon as we see the tramways and busways funded and under construction, we're on board! Until then, this ain't Vienna!
Bill Winegard, Etobicoke
Give us extensive, safe cycling infrastructure and more of us will leave our cars at home
After a holiday in the Netherlands, I'll never feel the same about cycling in Toronto. Dedicated bike lanes that went everywhere, side streets where cars were allowed but bikes had priority, massive, secure bike parking facilities, and a culture that takes cycling for granted. These blissful memories of cycling paradise were going through my mind on my most recent bike trip where I shared the road with cars, parked cars, trucks, streetcars and potholes.
Several times I had to detour to the centre of the road (between street car tracks) to avoid trucks taking up the entire curb lane or being doored and frequently cars drove much too close for comfort. To avoid this white knuckle cycling on my return trip, I went considerably out of my way to find a bike lane, and even then, had to manoeuvre around multiple streetcar tracks to get there and merge with traffic due to construction and illegally parked cars.
It boggles my mind and breaks my heart Premier Doug Ford wants to further restrict our meagre bike infrastructure. Please give us extensive, safe cycling infrastructure and many more of us will leave our cars at home.
Gabriela Byron, Toronto
Proroguing Parliament is not good for CanadiansPierre Poilievre's antics are making Parliament miserable for everyone. Give us a break, Oct. 10
I found the article written by Susan Delacourt to be very questionable. She seems to be paving the way for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's party to prorogue Parliament while suggesting that it would be good for Canadians. But the current issue in the House needs to be addressed, not swept under the rug as proroguing would allow.
Raymond Mercuri, Cocagne, N.B.
Remove bike lanes on Bloor Street West in EtobicokeMayor Olivia Chow considers changes to Etobicoke bike lanes, Oct. 10
Mayor Olivia Chow says she is looking for a compromise for the bike lanes on Bloor Street in Etobicoke. One already exists. In 2022 the city spent $4.3 million to reconstruct the bridge over Mimico Creek at Van Dusen Blvd parallel to Bloor just two blocks south. Everybody uses the bridge. That's why there are hardly any bikes in the bike lanes.
Meanwhile,drivers must open their doors into traffic to exit. This stops traffic as drivers wait for someone to get themselves and often their walker or cane (Etobicoke has more seniors than any other part of Toronto) safely out of their car.
Businesses are complaining of a significant drop in customers and at least two have closed. When I asked one business owner why, she said the bike lanes had reduced her business by more than 30 per cent. I asked where she was going. "Anywhere but Bloor" was the reply.
The city of Burlington removed its bike lanes from New Street in 2017 because of traffic congestion. Let's hope our council has the fortitude to do the same on Bloor Street.
Gail Rutherford, Etobicoke
Frustrated Liberal backbenchers can always support the Conservatives' confidence motionsBehind the scenes, anxious Liberals wonder if Justin Trudeau has a plan for re-election: 'The status quo is leading us to obliteration, Oct. 10
Tonda MacCharles and Alex Ballingall write: "MPs who believe (Prime Minister Justin) Trudeau should go feel 'powerless,' said one, because there is no mechanism to force the question of leadership: the party never adopted Reform Act rules that give caucus the power to remove a leader, as the Conservatives had before they toppled Erin O'Toole. 'You can talk 'til you're blue in the face, but if (Trudeau) chooses to ignore it, he can ignore it,' the source said."
Liberal backbenchers who feel this way can always vote in favour of one of the Conservative confidence motions. Trudeau will not ignore them and might even refuse to sign their election papers. They can then run as independent candidates in the ensuing election.
Patrick Cowan, North York
Letter writer offered good advice Opponents of MAID should mind their own business and allow others to make their own decisions, Oct. 10
I fully agree with the opinions expressed in reader Tony d'Andrea's letter. This idea reminded me of a billboard I saw in New York City many years ago. It referred to a specific controversy of that time and said in big bold letters: "DON'T LIKE GAY MARRIAGE? DON'T GET GAY MARRIED." Good advice in general.
Ross Gentleman, Toronto

Kudos to ParadkarStar columnist Shree Paradkar wins PEN Canada prize for advancing freedom of expression, Oct. 10
It was a pleasure to see Shree Paradkar's well-deserved win of the PEN Canada prize for her work on human rights and abuse in institutions and her reporting on Palestinian suffering. Paradkar has the journalistic decency, courage and humanity to write about the Palestinian catastrophe.
Rafat Khan, Mississauga
CAPTION: "The less I used my body to move, the less I felt the need for any physical activity. The more I had to rely on the car to get anywhere, the less I wanted to go anywhere," writes Julia Beluz on returning to Toronto.

Letters: Toronto, I love you but you're bringing me down, Opinion, Oct. 5 The suburbs are great, Opinion, Oct. 5 Chow mulls changes to bike lanes, Oct. 11 Anxious Liberals wonder: Where's the plan?, Oct. 11 Parliament has become a miserable place, Delacourt, Oct. 10 MAID opponents should mind their own business, Reader letter, Oct. 10 The Star's Paradkar wins PEN Canada prize, Oct. 10

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3116541866/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Toronto%2C+I+love+you+but+you%27re+bringing+me+down%2C+Opinion%2C+Oct.+5+The+suburbs+are+great%2C+Opinion%2C+Oct.+5+Chow+mulls+changes+to+bike+lanes%2C+Oct.+11+Anxious+Liberals+wonder%3A+Where%27s+the+plan%3F%2C+Oct.+11+Parliament+has+become+a+miserable+place%2C+Delacourt%2C+Oct.+10+MAID+opponents+should+mind+their+own+business%2C+Reader+letter%2C+Oct.+10+The+Star%27s+Paradkar+wins+PEN+Canada+prize%2C+Oct.+10&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: European-style transit is needed
Toronto, I love you but you're bringing me down, Opinion, Oct. 5
The suburbs are great,
Opinion, Oct. 5
The Oct. 5 Opinion Weekly contained two perspectives on the suburbs. In contrast to Toronto suburbs, Julia Belluz touted the high quality of life, despite their density, in European metropolises, largely made possible by high-quality, high-frequency mass transit. Scott Stinson enjoys the space in the 'burbs, as long as you own a car. They are both correct.
What is NOT correct is the City of Toronto's proposal to allow no-appeal, as-of-right 12-storey apartments throughout newly designated "avenues" in suburbs like Etobicoke. Such avenues in Vienna, Berlin or Paris have extensive tram systems and frequent buses.
Yet, while wringing their hands over traffic congestion, city council and city planners are proposing tens of thousands of new residents on Etobicoke roads like Bloor and Islington with no semblance or prospect of adequate transit.
This must be "if you build it, they will come" magical thinking. But we in Toronto have proof that such density in the suburbs does NOT mean adequate transit will come! Just ask the high-density residents of suburban Jane Street, Dufferin Street and Weston Road as they shiver through long waits for jam-packed buses stuck in traffic. Forget bike lanes; if the city wants more highrises in the suburbs, why aren't we building dedicated bus-only lanes?
Of course, Toronto's suburbs can and should see more higher-density housing. As soon as we see the tramways and busways funded and under construction, we're on board! Until then, this ain't Vienna!
Bill Winegard, Etobicoke
Give us extensive and safe cycling infrastructure
After a holiday in the Netherlands, I'll never feel the same about cycling in Toronto. Dedicated bike lanes that went everywhere, side streets where cars were allowed but bikes had priority, massive, secure bike-parking facilities, and a culture that takes cycling for granted. These blissful memories of cycling paradise were going through my mind on my most recent bike trip where I shared the road with cars, parked cars, trucks, streetcars and potholes.
Several times I had to detour to the centre of the road (between streetcar tracks) to avoid trucks taking up the entire curb lane or being doored and frequently cars drove much too close for comfort. To avoid this white knuckle cycling on my return trip, I went considerably out of my way to find a bike lane, and even then, had to manoeuvre around multiple streetcar tracks to get there and merge with traffic due to construction and illegally parked cars.
It boggles my mind and breaks my heart the Premier Doug Ford wants to further restrict our meagre bike infrastructure. Please give us extensive, safe cycling infrastructure and many more of us will leave our cars at home.
Gabriela Byron, Toronto
Axe bike lanes on Bloor Street West
Chow mulls changes to bike lanes, Oct. 11
Mayor Olivia Chow says she is looking for a compromise for the bike lanes on Bloor Street in Etobicoke. One already exists. In 2022 the city spent $4.3 million to reconstruct the bridge over Mimico Creek at Van Dusen Boulevard parallel to Bloor just two blocks south. Everybody uses the bridge. That's why there are hardly any bikes in the bike lanes.
Meanwhile, drivers must open their doors into traffic to exit, stopping traffic. This stops traffic as drivers wait for someone to get themselves and often their walker or cane (Etobicoke has more seniors than any other part of Toronto) safely out of their car.
Businesses are complaining of a significant drop in customers and at least two have closed. When I asked one business owner why, she said the bike lanes had reduced her business by more than 30 per cent. I asked where she was going. "Anywhere but Bloor" was the reply.
The City of Burlington removed its bike lanes from New Street in 2017 because of traffic congestion. Let's hope our council has the fortitude to do the same on Bloor Street.
Gail Rutherford, Etobicoke
Vote in favour of Tories' motions
Anxious Liberals wonder: Where's the plan?, Oct. 11
Tonda MacCharles and Alex Ballingall write: "MPs who believe (Prime Minister Justin) Trudeau should go feel 'powerless,' said one, because there is no mechanism to force the question of leadership: the party never adopted Reform Act rules that give caucus the power to remove a leader, as the Conservatives had before they toppled Erin O'Toole. 'You can talk 'til you're blue in the face, but if (Trudeau) chooses to ignore it, he can ignore it,' the source said." Liberal backbenchers who feel this way can always vote in favour of one of the Conservative confidence motions. Trudeau will not ignore them and might even refuse to sign their election papers. They can then run as independent candidates in the ensuing election.
Patrick Cowan, North York
Proroguing not good for Canada
Parliament has become a miserable place, Delacourt, Oct. 10
I found the article written by Susan Delacourt to be very questionable. She seems to be paving the way for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's party to prorogue Parliament while suggesting that it would be good for Canadians. But the current issue in the House needs to be addressed, not swept under the rug as proroguing would allow.
Raymond Mercuri, Cocagne, N.B.

Letter writer
gave good advice
MAID opponents should mind their own business, Reader letter, Oct. 10
I fully agree with the opinions expressed in reader Tony d'Andrea's letter. This idea reminded me of a billboard I saw in New York City many years ago. It referred to a specific controversy of that time and said in big bold letters: "DON'T LIKE GAY MARRIAGE? DON'T GET GAY MARRIED." Good advice in general.
Ross Gentleman, Toronto

The Star's Paradkar wins PEN Canada prize, Oct. 10

Rafat Khan, Mississauga

Document 47 of 500

Nearly two-thirds of Ontarians think Doug Ford must do more to deal with homeless encampments, survey finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nearly-two-thirds-ontarians-think-doug-ford-must/docview/3116324924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nearly+two-thirds+of+Ontarians+think+Doug+Ford+must+do+more+to+deal+with+homeless+encampments%2C+survey+finds&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The vast majority of Ontarians feel homeless encampments are a "problem" and most believe Premier Doug Ford is not doing enough to deal with them, a new poll suggests.
Abacus Data found 54 per cent think tents in municipal parks are a "big problem," while 30 per cent say they are a "minor problem" and 16 per cent insist they are "not really a problem."
"It is something that signals to people that things aren't working right," said Abacus president David Coletto.
Indeed, 62 per cent feel Ford isn't doing enough to help municipalities deal with encampments, while 17 per cent said the premier is doing enough, five per cent said he's doing "more than enough" and 15 per cent weren't sure.
"This chalks up to a real potential liability for the Progressive Conservatives and for Mr. Ford," said Coletto.
"The public doesn't get a sense that things are getting better," he added.
But Ford's Tories, who are considering an early election next year, remain popular with 44 per cent support - well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians from Oct. 3 through Thursday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The poll was conducted after Ford signalled on Oct. 2 that the province would be taking some unspecified action on homeless tent encampments.
"It's a big problem," conceded the premier, whose office at the legislature is 400 metres south of a small encampment in city-run Queen's Park.
"We've got to find homes for these folks as well, and that's our goal - all three levels of government. But I'll tell you, communities across the province, they're just beside themselves," he said that day.
In Toronto, Dufferin Grove Park, Allen Gardens, Bellevue Square and Clarence Square are among the city parks that are home to large encampments despite concerns from area residents about public safety.
Ford's comments came the week after he had chided tent dwellers to "get off your a-s-s and start working like everyone else," which sparked criticism.
Abacus found that 80 per cent of respondents think governments should "prioritize providing housing, mental health, and addiction services to help individuals transition out of homelessness and find long-term stability."
Only 20 per cent felt political leaders should "prioritize removing encampments and enforcing laws to address public safety and maintain order in communities."
There appears to be plenty of blame to go around - 63 per cent said Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is doing a poor job of handling the encampment crisis with 11 per cent saying he's doing a good job.
Fully 52 per cent felt Ford is doing a poor job on them, with 15 per cent saying he's doing a good job - while 48 per cent said Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow is doing a poor job on the encampments and 13 per cent saying she's doing a good job.
"The public wants to see a compassionate solution," said Coletto.
"There's clearly broad support for doing something."
Asked "which level of government is most responsible to deal with homeless encampments," 43 per cent said Queen's Park, 32 per cent said Ottawa and 25 per cent said municipalities.
Abacus also polled on Ford's proposals for dealing with traffic gridlock in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
While 67 per cent were aware of the premier's scheme to tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough - with 33 per cent unaware - 47 per cent said it was a bad idea while 35 per cent said it was a good idea and 18 per cent didn't know.
On the other hand, 39 per cent would like the province to buy back the long-term private lease on Highway 407 with 18 per cent opposing that and 21 per cent unsure.
"It demonstrates to that core base of supporters that he's listening to them because they are clearly telling him that traffic is a problem," said Coletto, noting respondents are divided on what can reasonably be done.
The premier's Sept. 25 tunnel idea could be a cornerstone pledge in a PC re-election campaign early next year.
Given Ford's current standing in the polls, Coletto said the Tories' plan for a vote one year early makes sense.
"There's no obvious headwind that's having an effect on holding his voting coalition together," the pollster said.
"The federal Liberal brand is not helping Bonnie Crombie," he said, referring to the persistent low polling of the Trudeau Liberals.
"And Marit Stiles just isn't breaking through."
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford has indicated that the province would be taking some unspecified action onhomeless tent encampments.

Document 48 of 500

He helped make credit scores free for Canadians. Now he wants to make borrowing easier too

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/he-helped-make-credit-scores-free-canadians-now/docview/3116324853/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=He+helped+make+credit+scores+free+for+Canadians.+Now+he+wants+to+make+borrowing+easier+too&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Andrew Graham helped bring Canada's financial sector into the digital age, but warns it's now falling behind.
As the co-founder and CEO of Borrowell, Graham helped give Canadians their first online loan product, access to credit data at no cost, and recently enabled them to use rent payments toward their credit scores.
At the same time, Graham warns that Canada's regulatory system is designed to optimize stability, often at the expense of innovation. As peer nations move forward with new services like open banking, he warns ongoing delays and regulatory burdens have put Canada behind.
"We have a big opportunity in this country to have more competition and more choice and lower costs," he says. "We've got a strong technology sector, we've got a strong financial services sector, we should be a home for globally competitive fintech companies; what we really need is the political will."
The Ottawa-born entrepreneur earned an economics degree from the University of Edinburgh in 2001, which included a year studying in the French Alps, followed by a summer as a reporter for the Economist in London and a stint in communications with the global non-profit CARE.
Graham eventually returned home to Ottawa to work as a political staffer but left again soon after to attend Harvard Business School in 2006.
Around that time Graham met fellow Canadian Harvard graduate Eva Wong and, after commiserating about their mutual struggles finding work back in their home country, the pair co-founded a non-profit called Toronto Homecoming in 2008 to help bridge the gap.
"I had just gone through that challenge of living abroad and trying to find a role in Toronto, and it took a lot of work, a lot of flights back to the city meeting with people," he says. "We sort of met and connected over this similar experience, where we had each been living abroad and had to navigate the world of searching for a job in Toronto."
Graham eventually did land a job in Toronto with Nortel in 2008 and, two years later, followed his then CFO to George Weston Ltd., which led to a role at PC Financial.
In 2014 he left to co-found Borrowell, one of the country's first fintechs, alongside Wong. In the decade since, Borrowell has become one of Canada's most widely used digital financial services, with more than three million users and one million app downloads.
Graham recently spoke with the Star from his office in downtown Toronto about the evolution of the fintech sector, the state of the country's balance sheet, the significance of adding rent to credit history, and why there's so much more the industry could offer Canadians under less restrictive regulatory conditions.
What inspired you to start a fintech?
I was working in the financial services industry and seeing some of the challenges that consumers face. If you're well off, you can hire financial advisers to help you optimize your finances, whereas if you're just a normal person it can be much harder to navigate. What's always been exciting to me is that technology can help everybody get personalized financial information at low cost.
I co-founded a not-for-profit in my spare time called Toronto Homecoming, and that entrepreneurial experience was a lot of fun and really gratifying. I liked building things and thought the financial services industry needed changing, so I decided to leave my corporate role and founded Borrowell.
How did Canadians access credit data at that time?
Very few Canadians had any access to their credit data. The only way to get it was to buy it through a credit monitoring service. You could go in person to Equifax or TransUnion kiosks, and you could request a paper copy, but it was certainly not widely available. As a result, very few people had any sense of what their credit score was or why it was what it was.
As you mentioned, Canadians faced many challenges related to personal finance. Why did you want to solve credit specifically?
I was really fascinated by credit cards, because they're not just payment vehicles, they can also be a way to borrow money. But for many people it ends up being a very expensive way to borrow money. If you went to a lender, you could get a loan at 10 per cent or a home line of credit at five per cent, but on a credit card you'd be paying multiples of that. I just thought that was strange. So solving that remains at the heart of what we do; helping people find the right portfolio of financial products for their needs.
How has that offering evolved over the last decade?
The first product that we had was a low-cost loan that you could apply for entirely online. It was 2015. We were the first to do that. Soon after we became the first company in Canada to offer credit scores for free and that was an important turning point. That's when things really took off.
You're no longer alone in providing that service, so what makes Borrowell unique?
We're the only Canadian company that is offering free credit score and reporting as a primary part of what we do. We have a couple of foreign competitors, and I think being headquartered here and being run by Canadians allows us to have a great understanding of this market. We've built deep relationships over the years with banks, with fintech companies - I'm now chair of the board of Fintechs Canada - so that gives us a really unique perspective.
What are some of the economic patterns you've seen amongst users recently?
One of the most interesting times for us was COVID. When the lockdowns first started lenders became hesitant to advance more credit, but as government programs came into effect the average indebtedness of our members went down because there was more money available. COVID gave us three years of relatively low indebtedness, missed payments and bankruptcies, but now we've gone back to where we might have been beforehand.
Are you concerned with the direction Canadians' finances are heading?
It's more concerning than where we were a couple of years ago, but I wouldn't say alarm bells are ringing. The biggest driver of missed payments or consumer bankruptcies is unemployment. When people start losing jobs, that's when really bad things happen in the world of credit. There's been some softening of the job market, but we as a country have weathered the last few years pretty well from a job's perspective.
Are Canadian households becoming more financially stable now that interest rates are declining?
Interest rates are still significantly higher than where they were during the COVID period, that's what's most important. We haven't really seen the effects of interest rates declining, and the reason is because they're still higher than they were a few years ago, and there's still a large volume of mortgages in Canada coming up for renewal at a higher rate.
Speaking of housing costs, how were you able to allow renters to use their payments toward their credit score?
That's one of the advances I'm most proud of. Traditionally, if you pay a mortgage, those on-time payments get counted in your credit history, whereas your next-door neighbour who's renting - who could be paying the same amount in rent as you're paying in mortgage every month - those payments wouldn't count for them.
We've got a long and positive relationship with Equifax - it's Equifax data that we're ultimately sharing with consumers - and they agreed with us that this is a problem. The challenge was getting the data into the system, because there's only a handful of mortgage lenders in this country, but many thousands of landlords.
We worked with Equifax to establish standards for data quality, so we help the consumer show their rental payments. We work with them to verify it and then we hand the data off to Equifax to include in the consumer's credit history. When we first launched the service, it was only forward looking. Now we can go back up to 24 months.
It's been a major win-win. Lenders get a better sense of credit risk and consumers get more access to credit; especially new Canadians, who often struggle to establish a Canadian credit score and often start in this country as renters.
Financial data is more readily available in other countries. Is our regulatory system slowing the industry down?
There's a belief that innovation comes at the expense of the stability that we take very seriously in the financial sector and I think that's just not right. You can have a financial sector that's both stable and secure while also having vigorous competition and choice. Virtually all the regulators that monitor the financial services industry have a mandate that focuses on stability, and there's no mention of competition or choice or driving down cost, and I think that's a missed opportunity. I think regulators could do both.
What would open banking mean for Borrowell?
With rent reporting, for example, the biggest challenge our consumers have is they have to show they paid their rent every month by connecting their bank account. Because we don't have open banking, that's a clunky process that often breaks. It's like if Netflix asked you to re-enter your payment details every month. That's the world we're living in today.
How do you see the business expending in the future?
I'm excited to continue helping Canadians assemble the right portfolio of financial products for wherever they are in their lives. New technologies like AI allow us to do that in a more powerful way, and there's never been more opportunities to make the complicated world of financial services more accessible.
This interview has been edited and condensed.
CAPTION: "We have a big opportunity in this country to have more competition and more choice and lower costs," says Borrowell co-founder Andrew Graham. "What we really need is the political will."

Document 49 of 500

Ontarians think Ford must do more to help municipalities: Survey findings a 'real potential liability' for the government, expert says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarians-think-ford-must-do-more-help/docview/3116182212/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontarians+think+Ford+must+do+more+to+help+municipalities%3A+Survey+findings+a+%27real+potential+liability%27+for+the+government%2C+expert+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The vast majority of Ontarians feel homeless encampments are a "problem" and most believe Premier Doug Ford is not doing enough to deal with them, a new poll suggests.
Abacus Data found 54 per cent think tents in municipal parks are a "big problem," while 30 per cent say they are a "minor problem" and 16 per cent insist they are "not really a problem."
"It is something that signals to people that things aren't working right," said Abacus president David Coletto.
Indeed, 62 per cent feel Ford isn't doing enough to help municipalities deal with encampments, while 17 per cent said the premier is doing enough, five per cent said he's doing "more than enough" and 15 per cent weren't sure.
"This chalks up to a real potential liability for the Progressive Conservatives and for Mr. Ford," said Coletto.
"The public doesn't get a sense that things are getting better," he added.
But Ford's Tories, who are considering an early election next year, remain popular with 44 per cent support - well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 24 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 22 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at seven per cent.
Abacus surveyed 997 Ontarians from Oct. 3 through Thursday using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.1 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
The poll was conducted after Ford signalled on Oct. 2 that the province would be taking some unspecified action on homeless tent encampments.
"It's a big problem," conceded the premier, whose office at the legislature is 400 metres south of a small encampment in city-run Queen's Park.
"We've got to find homes for these folks as well, and that's our goal - all three levels of government. But I'll tell you, communities across the province, they're just beside themselves," he said that day.
In Toronto, Dufferin Grove Park, Allen Gardens, Bellevue Square and Clarence Square are among the city parks that are home to large encampments despite concerns from area residents about public safety.
Ford's comments came the week after he had chided tent dwellers to "get off your a-s-s and start working like everyone else," which sparked criticism.
Abacus found that 80 per cent of respondents think governments should "prioritize providing housing, mental health, and addiction services to help individuals transition out of homelessness and find long-term stability."
Only 20 per cent felt political leaders should "prioritize removing encampments and enforcing laws to address public safety and maintain order in communities."
There appears to be plenty of blame to go around - 63 per cent said Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is doing a poor job of handling the encampment crisis with 11 per cent saying he's doing a good job.
Fully 52 per cent felt Ford is doing a poor job on them, with 15 per cent saying he's doing a good job - while 48 per cent said Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow is doing a poor job on the encampments and 13 per cent saying she's doing a good job.
"The public wants to see a compassionate solution," said Coletto.
Asked "which level of government is most responsible to deal with homeless encampments," 43 per cent said Queen's Park, 32 per cent said Ottawa and 25 per cent said municipalities.
Abacus also polled on Ford's proposals for dealing with traffic gridlock in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
While 67 per cent were aware of the premier's scheme to tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga to Scarborough - with 33 per cent unaware - 47 per cent said it was a bad idea while 35 per cent said it was a good idea and 18 per cent didn't know.
On the other hand, 39 per cent would like the province to buy back the long-term private lease on Highway 407 with 18 per cent opposing that and 21 per cent unsure.
"It demonstrates to that core base of supporters that he's listening to them because they are clearly telling him that traffic is a problem," said Coletto, noting respondents are divided on what can reasonably be done. The premier's Sept. 25 tunnel idea could be a cornerstone pledge in a PC re-election campaign early next year.
Given Ford's current standing in the polls, Coletto said the Tories' plan for a vote one year early makes sense.
"There's no obvious headwind that's having an effect on holding his voting coalition together," the pollster said.
"The federal Liberal brand is not helping Bonnie Crombie," he said, referring to the persistent low polling of the Trudeau Liberals.
"And Marit Stiles just isn't breaking through."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Nobel winner wants us to be wise about technology

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nobel-winner-wants-us-be-wise-about-technology/docview/3116182160/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nobel+winner+wants+us+to+be+wise+about+technology&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's something delightfully amusing about Geoffrey Hinton's handling of the media maelstrom after he was informed that he had been named co-recipient of the 2024 Nobel Prize in physics.
That he hesitated about answering the phone, because who calls at 1 a.m.?
That the world-famous University of Toronto computer scientist was in California staying, as he explained to the New York Times, in "a cheap hotel room with no internet."
That he told the BBC that he dropped out of physics after first year university.
That in considering a question posed by the Star's Victoria Gibson, who wondered whether the neural networks of artificial intelligence could be deployed to improve the city of Toronto, offered that he was "not quite sure how a neural network could get rid of Doug Ford."
Hinton, as the Star has reported, is a fierce critic of the premier's plans to build a downsized Ontario Science Centre on the waterfront.
And he's a passionate proponent of curiosity, from the spark that can ignite in the mind of a school child on a Science Centre outing, to the curiosity-driven research that has powered him across decades to global status as one of the godfathers of artificial intelligence.
What resonates is the way in which our newly minted Nobel laureate champions learning, including that of his own students who, as he has said, took his foundational research and "actually made things work."
Of course, most of us weren't paying attention in the early 1980s when Hinton and his fellow scientists experienced their first successes with computer algorithms. Nor would the vast majority have had a clue about his forbearance working in the field of brain-mimicking artificial neural networks, denounced as a waste of time by many in the scientific community.
But we're all awake now, eager, perhaps even desperate, to fully understand the positive potential of artificial intelligence - and its dangers.
Hinton has said in interviews that he always had his worries about AI, from job displacement to the proliferation of fake news. But the good works - he often emphasizes the benefits to health care in, say, the analysis of medical images, and huge improvements in productivity - are proving to be revolutionary.
A tipping point arrived in the spring of last year when Hinton announced that after a decade working with Google on its AI technology, he had quit, citing the existential risk of artificial intelligence outpacing the intelligence of mankind.
As he said to reporters this week, "We have no experience of what it's like to have things smarter than us."
How close are we to being outplayed by AI? Take a look at the advanced reasoning capabilities of OpenAI's GPT-4. Or, just for fun, check out its single sentence explanation of the plot of Cinderella - posted on OpenAI's website - where the artificial brain was commanded to start each word with the next letter of the alphabet.
It may be a fairytale. But it's also an example of the dizzying creative talents of AI.
Perhaps we should say "chillingly." Hinton has raised the spectre of a threat that could get out of control. He has made the urgent call for more research into AI safety. Systems more intelligent than us could "go rogue," he has said.
"That's not science fiction."
He doesn't have all the answers. He's not that smart. But perhaps in his wry, teacherly way he can again help lay a foundation stone for not just the mechanics of artificial intelligence, but the responsible way forward.
CAPTION: Geoffrey Hinton has raised the spectre of a threat that could get out of control. He has made the urgent call for more research into AI safety. Systems more intelligent than us could "go rogue," he has said. "That's not science fiction."Noah Bergerthe associated press file photo

Liberals name new campaign chair

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-name-new-campaign-chair/docview/3116182078/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+name+new+campaign+chair&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has announced the Liberal Party's new campaign chair, as some MPs reportedly band together to ask their leader to step down.
Trudeau and the party say Andrew Bevan, a longtime Liberal staff member and volunteer, is taking the reins ahead of the next general election.
Bevan most recently served as an adviser to Trudeau and chief of staff to Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland, which involved helping develop this past spring's budget.
He replaces Jeremy Broadhurst, who quit as national campaign director last month, shortly before the Liberals lost a critical byelection in Montreal.
Following a June byelection loss in Toronto, those developments fuelled calls among some Liberals for Trudeau to resign, which reportedly intensified last week as MPs pushed each other to commit to calling on Trudeau to step down.
The Liberals also announced a deputy campaign director, Marjorie Michel, who specializes in election outreach in Quebec.
Bevan previously served as chief of staff to former Liberal leader Stéphane Dion as well as former Ontario premier Kathleen Wynne.
The next federal election is set to take place within a year but could be called much sooner following the NDP's decision to withdraw from an agreement that supported the Liberals in confidence votes.
This past Friday, multiple media outlets reported efforts by Liberal MPs to band together to ask Trudeau to resign amid a sustained slump in the polls.
Details about the strategy and breadth of the bid were unclear, though one MP not involved in the revolt told The Canadian Press on Friday that the number of MPs involved was not insignificant.
The Canadian Press

Document 52 of 500

Community demands action after shots fired at Jewish girls school in North York

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/community-demands-action-after-shots-fired-at/docview/3116427910/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Community+demands+action+after+shots+fired+at+Jewish+girls+school+in+North+York&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One day after shots were fired outside a North York elementary school for Jewish girls, leaders demanded all levels of government do more to address the rise of antisemitism in Toronto and said their community would stand united against hate.
Speaking to reporters outside Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School on Sunday, school leaders said they were thankful there were no injuries following the shooting, which took place early Saturday morning, but stressed that students and their families should not have to face such a "tragic and horrible" incident.
It's the second time the North York school has been hit by gunfire in six months.
"No child should have fear coming to school. No child should ever hear that their school was shot at," said Rabbi Yaacov Vidal, principal at Bais Chaya Mushka.
That the gunfire occurred on Yom Kippur, the holiest day of the Jewish calendar, "makes this even a bigger tragedy," Vidal said. "That on such a holy day there could be such hate and negativity."
Vidal said students will return to school on Monday with "more enhanced security."
Toronto police confirmed on Saturday that a suspect or suspects in a motor vehicle discharged a firearm at the school, near Dufferin Street and Finch Avenue West earlier that morning. Police said they found evidence of gunfire and that there was damage to the school, including a shattered window at the front of the building. By Sunday, that window had been covered with a blue tarp.
Insp. Paul Krawczyk, the officer in charge of the investigation, told reporters on scene Saturday that police could not yet determine whether the incident was a hate crime. A key part of the investigation, police said, is to determine whether the two incidents are connected. Toronto police's Gun and Gang Task Force is leading the investigation, with support from members of the Hate Crime Unit.
Daniel Held, chief program officer at the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto, told reporters Sunday that parents in the Jewish community are raising their children in "circumstances we could never have imagined, certainly not in a country like Canada, where we pride ourselves on being a safe place for families of all faiths and cultures."
He called the Saturday shooting the "latest example of escalating antisemitism unlike anything we've seen in the GTA."
Earlier this year, Toronto Police Chief Myron Demkiw said that since the start of the Israel-Hamas war officers had been responding to a significantly higher number of hate crime calls, over half of which alleged antisemitism.
In an interview with the Star, Held said it's no longer enough for political leaders to offer "hopes and prayers" and said concrete actions are needed to counter hate in Canada. He said governments need to hold accountable groups that support terrorism and "invest in greater policing and prosecution resources" to protect communities.
Last May, shots were fired at Bais Chaya Mushka damaging the front of the school; no injuries were reported. At the time, Toronto police said its Hate Crime Unit would be supporting the Gun and Gang Task Force in that investigation.
Bais Chaya Mushka is an Orthodox Jewish girls' school that offers programs from junior kindergarten to Grade 8 with an enrolment of about 225 students.
Rabbi Nochum Sosover, Bais Chaya Mushka's executive director, told the Star that school leaders have sent statements to parents about the Saturday morning gunfire, which include strategies to help students cope with the incident. He said "everyone is shocked" and that police will be at the school Monday, along with full-time security.
Political leaders on Saturday condemned the shooting, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau saying in a statement on X (formerly Twitter) that he was "very disturbed" to hear of the incident as families marked Yom Kippur, adding that "antisemitism is a disgusting and dangerous form of hate - and we won't let it stand."
Speaking to reporters at a separate event Saturday, Premier Doug Ford called the incident "unacceptable" and said "these people need to be caught" and "thrown in jail." At the same event, Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow advised the suspects to "turn yourself in" and called the shooting "horrific."
Ya'ara Saks, York Centre MP and minister of mental health and addictions, on Sunday said she was "heartbroken" to be speaking out against the second shooting incident at the North York School.
"There is no place in this country where Canadians should not be able to be safe to express their faith, to worship where they want to worship, to live where they wish to live, to work where they want to thrive," said Saks, also associate minister of health. She said that it will take all levels of government to work together to stop rising antisemitism.
With files from Abhiraj Lamba and Sheila Wang

Document 53 of 500

Shots fired at Toronto school for Jewish girls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/shots-fired-at-toronto-school-jewish-girls/docview/3115921570/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Shots+fired+at+Toronto+school+for+Jewish+girls&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police are investigating after shots were fired outside a North York elementary school for Jewish girls early Saturday morning, an incident police described as "troubling." The shooting took place on Yom Kippur, the holiest day of the Jewish year.
A suspect or suspects in a motor vehicle discharged a firearm at the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, near Dufferin Street and Finch Avenue West, Toronto police said in a statement on X.
There are no reports of injuries, but police said evidence of gunfire has been found and damage to the school includes a shattered window at the front of the building.
Insp. Paul Krawczyk told reporters on scene at the private school that Toronto police received a call at 4 a.m. about a smashed window.
"Upon further investigation, it was determined that shots had been fired," said Krawczyk.
Toronto police's Gun and Gang Task Force is leading the investigation, with support from members of the Hate Crime Unit.
It's the second time the North York school has been hit by gunfire in six months. In May, shots were fired at the school - also early on a Saturday morning - damaging the front of the school; no injuries were reported.
Krawczyk said police could not yet determine whether the incident was a hate crime and did not confirm nor deny that the two shootings are related. Police did say that a key part of the investigation is to find out whether the incidents are connected.
He said police are also asking anyone with dash-cam or CCTV footage to come forward.
Toronto police on Saturday afternoon said in a post on X that it stands with Toronto's Jewish community.
"We understand the fear and pain this causes, especially on Yom Kippur," the post said. "To Toronto's Jewish community, we stand with you. Your safety is our priority. We are committed to finding those responsible."
On Saturday afternoon, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said in a statement on X that he was "very disturbed" to hear there were shots fired at a Jewish school in Toronto as families marked Yom Kippur.
"As we wait for more details, my heart goes out to the students, staff and parents who must be terrified and hurting today," Trudeau said. "Antisemitism is a disgusting and dangerous form of hate - and we won't let it stand."
Bais Chaya Mushka is an Orthodox Jewish girls' school that offers programs from junior kindergarten to Grade 8.
With an enrolment of about 225 students, the school caters to the Jewish Chabad community. Chabad is a major movement within mainstream Jewish tradition, with its roots in the Chassidic movement of the 18th century, according to Chabad.org.
In May, following the first time the school was hit by gunfire, Rabbi Yaacov Vidal, Bais Chaya Mushka's principal, told the Star it was "pretty shocking" to be told of the incident, which occurred on Shabbat, the Jewish Day of Rest.
"I think it's very sad that people target schools where children are innocent and just create this extra fear and chaos for no reason," Vidal said.
And while Toronto police at the time said it was too early to determine if the shooting was a hate crime, Vidal told the Star the incident was antisemitic.
"I think I would consider this a hate crime," Vidal said. "I don't see any other reason why this would occur."
Premier Doug Ford called the incident "unacceptable," while speaking to reporters at a separate event Saturday.
"We live in a peaceful society," Ford said. "We've never seen anything like this. We all grew up in Toronto, we all get along, and it's just unacceptable what we're seeing."
Ford added "these people need to be caught" and "thrown in jail."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow told reporters at the same event that she advised the suspects to "turn yourself in."
"Understand that this is a time of the highest holiday, and think about children, think about a time of worship," Chow said. "For someone to do something like that is just horrific."
With files from Abhiraj Lamba and Sheila Wang
CREDIT: Megan Ogilvie staff Reporter With files from Abhiraj Lamba and Sheila Wang
