Document 54 of 500

Is Justin Trudeau fated to lose just like Kathleen Wynne'? We asked MPs who were there for the last days of her government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-justin-trudeau-fated-lose-just-like-kathleen/docview/3115767652/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Justin+Trudeau+fated+to+lose+just+like+Kathleen+Wynne%27%3F+We+asked+MPs+who+were+there+for+the+last+days+of+her+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Is it déjà vu all over again?
That's a loaded question for the half dozen Liberal MPs who were part of the doomed Kathleen Wynne government that lost to Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives in 2018 and now sit in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's federal caucus facing Pierre Poilievre's surging Conservatives.
Two of those veteran politicians wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, their silence a telling indication of where things appear to be headed and how history could repeat itself.
Their more (self-described) "optimistic" colleagues prefer to emphasize - not the similarities of the electoral scenarios - but the differences between Queen's Park in 2018 and Ottawa now.
Of the six politicians now in the Commons, only two were able to hang onto their provincial seats in 2018 when the Ford juggernaut reduced an almost 15-year Ontario Liberal dynasty to a tiny rump without official party status in the legislature: community and social services minister Michael Coteau and solicitor general Marie-France Lalonde.
Charles Sousa, who had been finance minister, lost his seat as did attorney-general Yasir Naqvi, health minister Helena Jaczek, and Etobicoke Centre MPP Yvan Baker.
Jaczek - who sources said had been outspoken in Wynne's cabinet in the year leading up to the 2018 Liberal defeat and warned about the premier's unpopularity at the doorsteps due to soaring electricity bills - politely declined to comment when asked to reflect on what this period in federal politics feels like.
Jaczek was dropped from Trudeau's cabinet last summer after announcing she will not seek re-election in Markham-Stouffville whenever the next federal election is held.
Baker declined to weigh in, too.
But Coteau, Sousa, Naqvi and Lalonde insist the federal Liberals are not doomed to the same fate as the provincial party, stressing Trudeau is an effective campaigner and they still have time to turn things around.
In an interview, Coteau, who was one of Wynne's top ministers, said the comparisons between the former premier and the prime minister miss the mark.
"The players are different, the personalities are different, the time is different," he said, noting provincial and national issues also differ.
However, there is one key similarity, he said. After a certain time, all governments accumulate "challenges."
With Wynne - who had revived the provincial Grits after succeeding Dalton McGuinty in 2013 and won a surprise majority victory the following year - her predecessor's decision to cancel two gas-fired power plants in Oakville and Mississauga before the 2011 election ultimately hurt her in 2018, despite what her ex-cabinet colleagues say was a good governing record.
For Coteau, the weight of 15 years of Liberal rule bedevilled Wynne's second election bid from the get-go.
"A narrative hardened, and it was hard to change the talking points even when you did good things," said Coteau, pointing to Sousa's feat under Wynne of a balanced budget in 2017.
In contrast, he argued that the animosity to Trudeau is not so baked in that it can't be overcome with time, economic improvements, a good platform, and a strong campaign.
"I'm not saying the world is all rosy, but I think that the cement hasn't dried fully in regards to where Canadians want to go, what they're thinking about. I think there's still the ability to have a discussion," about "an alternative to the Pierre Poilievre approach," said Coteau, whose Don Valley East seat is disappearing in redistribution so he will be running in neighbouring Scarborough-Woburn.
Two of his former Ontario cabinet colleagues, not now in Ottawa, said they feel the antipathy toward Trudeau mirrors what they heard about Wynne six years.
"It may even be worse. You see these 'F--- Trudeau' signs everywhere; there were never 'F--- Wynne' signs and flags on people's trucks. People were mad at Kathleen about their hydro bills, but now it's unhinged toward the PM," said one ex-provincial minister who spoke on condition they not be named.
Another, whom the Star agreed not to identify so they could freely discuss internal deliberations, said the federal Liberals should be thinking of doing what the provincial Grits regret not doing: saving the furniture by switching leaders instead of allowing everyone to go down with the ship.
"Even if (Wynne) had stepped down (in 2017), we would have lost, but we wouldn't have gone down to seven or eight seats," said the retired second former minister. "We could have lived to fight another day. The OLP didn't do that; the LPC still could."
Wynne told the Star this summer the decision by a political leader to stay or go is a bruising and deeply personal one, and advised Trudeau to contemplate why he wanted to stay.
Naqvi said in an interview the federal Liberals' nine years in office is a big reason why "we are behind in the polls," but added, "I don't think it's lost."
"The next little bit matters a lot. The campaign matters a lot. I ran in 2011. It was my second election. I ran in 2018. Even in talking to people at the door, to me it feels more like 2011 than 2018," he said, referring to the McGuinty Liberals' third consecutive election victory.
By then, the premier had been in power eight years compared to Trudeau's nine.
McGuinty had weathered the 2008 economic crisis and recession and had brought in policies that Naqvi emphasized were good for the province - like the harmonized sales tax - but were "not popular" because some prices went up.
"We were able to pull that campaign and win that election, a minority government, by one seat," he said, referring to the Liberals winning 53 seats in what was then a 107-member legislature.
Debilitated by the gas-plants scandal, his popularity plummeting, McGuinty prorogued the legislature in October 2012 and resigned as leader, triggering the leadership race won by Wynne three months later.
Naqvi conceded he does hear similar comments at the door now as he heard when campaigning under McGuinty, when voters said "we love how you're doing but I think maybe it's time for you to change the leader."
Back then and now, Naqvi said, he shifts the focus onto the Liberals' opponent, insisting that in Ottawa Centre voters at the door are turned off by the Conservative leader.
As Ontario Liberal president, Naqvi oversaw the expedited leadership contest that replaced McGuinty. He said the federal party needs to do three things to turn the ship around: draw a clear contrast and show "how dangerous Poilievre is for the country"; produce a policy prescription on affordability that shows people "we get it," and "articulate a vision for the future."
Sousa said in an interview he sees "similarities" in that Ontario voters in 2018 faced a flood of "misinformation" in Conservative partisan rhetoric, including from groups like Canada Proud and Ontario Proud. Sousa argued Poilievre and his supporters are following the same playbook, "creating fear, division and promoting some of the hate that exists, especially in the toxic nature or the anonymity that exists, through social media. It feeds the beast."
He believes another "similarity" is that Poilievre, like Ford, cannot be trusted to leave social programs intact despite his pre-writ rhetoric. That is part of what Sousa said motivates him to run again despite the dismal poll showings for Trudeau. Sousa cited other personal priorities - to boost economic growth, protect national unity, and ensure Canada's voice is heard on the world stage - and why he believes there is a path to win under Trudeau.
"The prime minister is very competitive. He realizes that things could be in jeopardy - some of the programs have been put in place. And I believe he is trying to do what's best for the benefit of others, whereas Poilievre is using some of these issues as a platform to benefit himself."
Lalonde, in an interview, said she is "100 per cent" behind Trudeau and doesn't think there are parallels to be drawn between Wynne circa 2018 and Trudeau now.
"I don't think it's fair," she said. "For me, it's not about the polls. For me, it's about the delivery. My government wants to continue to support Canadians," she said. She intends to focus on the needs of her riding, but says she is not running a "local campaign. I'm fighting Pierre Poilievre."
All campaigns, she added, are "tough." In 2014, "no one thought I was going to win ... I won in 2018 ... And you know, (the) campaign matters."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau responds to a question during a news conference in Vientiane, Laos on Oct. 11, 2024.

Subject: Polls & surveys; Legislatures; Scandals; Voters; Political leadership; Prime ministers

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin; Wynne, Kathleen

Document 55 of 500

We are living through our neighbourhoods rather than in them

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-are-living-through-our-neighbourhoods-rather/docview/3115765576/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+are+living+through+our+neighbourhoods+rather+than+in+them&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Car Sick, Oct. 5
There is no war on cars in Toronto. Not yet at least.
Julia Belluz's commentary on life in car-dependent Toronto only briefly touched on the effect of extended car commutes on one's body and spirit. She instead focused on the many benefits of pedestrian-based living in cities like Paris, but I do believe that the negatives of car dependency need to be emphasized. An hour's commute every day, multiplied twice per day and repeated every workday year after year is a criminal waste of time and energy for the average citizen. And it's an offence to the communities that have to accommodate the cars passing them by in ever-amassing numbers. We are living through our neighbourhoods rather than in them, it seems.
Amsterdam was a tangle of car traffic in the 1970s, much like Toronto is today, and as a longtime resident I am hoping we are finally on the cusp of a similar revelation here in The Big Smoke. Back in 2010 Rob Ford put the brakes on his predecessor's Transit City LRT network construction, announcing "The war on the car is over." It was a ridiculous statement considering how the war had barely begun. And now Rob's brother Premier Doug Ford is toying with a multi-billion-dollar road tunnel under Highway 401.
Cities are not liveable if everything is built around roads that are congested with oversized single-occupant SUVs at every vantage. The cafes and artist neighbours that Belluz describes are a minor gain to one's lifestyle compared to the civic, economic, and emotional gains we could all enjoy if we could move away from the car-brained mentality that so many of us have and start working towards something better. One foot forward; one step at a time.
Dave McIntyre, Toronto
Why can't Toronto learn from the many successes of world class cities?
Recently we visited the beautiful city of Barcelona with its rich culture in the arts and iconic architecture. The old quarter of the city has narrow alleyways while the newer boulevards are wide with bus lanes, bicycle trails and easy to cross signalled pedestrian friendly roads.
Years ago, the city was enlarged by removing crumbling walls and dilapidated buildings to make room for multiple unit apartments and tree lined well designed boulevards. The city's subways and clean electric LRTs are efficient and easy to access, and there are wide pedestrian corridors that are covered by a healthy prolific tree canopy.
When touring this city of millions of residents, we were struck with the obvious fact that we didn't see any neighbourhoods with single family houses, only elegant apartment buildings where residents lived in units of up to four bedrooms. And there are endless blocks of these tall buildings of affordable housing.
I also read a local article that the Barcelona municipal government was about to ban all short-term rentals so that 10,000 additional residents would be able to reside there, relegating visitors and tourists to the many excellent hotels that are available. This is only one large European city that has gone through the growing pains and solved the problems of both creating more housing and reducing traffic issues by creating a streamlined public transportation system.
Why can't we learn from the many successes of world class cities and their expert teams of professional traffic engineers, instead of constantly floundering with Band-Aid approaches and crisis management solutions? Let's look beyond our borders to solve these challenges.
Marion Bartlett, Singhampton
CAPTION: Cities are not liveable if everything is built around roads that are congested with oversized single-occupant SUVs at every vantage, a reader writes.

Subject: Automobiles; Neighborhoods; Cities

Document 56 of 500

Trudeau, Ford and Chow condemn 'troubling' news that shots were fired outside North York school for Jewish girls on Yom Kippur

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-ford-chow-condemn-troubling-news-that/docview/3115765575/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%2C+Ford+and+Chow+condemn+%27troubling%27+news+that+shots+were+fired+outside+North+York+school+for+Jewish+girls+on+Yom+Kippur&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police are investigating after shots were fired outside a North York elementary school for Jewish girls early Saturday morning, an incident police described as "troubling." The shooting took place on Yom Kippur, the holiest day of the Jewish year.
A suspect or suspects in a motor vehicle discharged a firearm at the Bais Chaya Mushka Elementary School, near Dufferin Street and Finch Avenue West, Toronto police said in a statement on X (formerly Twitter).
There are no reports of injuries, but police said evidence of gunfire has been found and damage to the school includes a shattered window at the front of the building.
Insp. Paul Krawczyk, the officer in charge of the investigation, told reporters on scene at the private Jewish girls' school that Toronto police received a call at around 4 a.m. about a smashed window.
"Upon further investigation, it was determined that shots had been fired," said Krawczyk.
Toronto police's Gun and Gang Task Force is leading the investigation, with support from members of the Hate Crime Unit.
It's the second time the North York school has been hit by gunfire in six months. In May, shots were fired at the school - also early on a Saturday morning - damaging the front of the school; no injuries were reported. At the time, Toronto police also said its Hate Crime Unit would be supporting the Gun and Gang Task Force in that investigation.
Krawczyk said police could not yet determine whether the incident was a hate crime and did not confirm or discount that the two shootings are related. Police did say that a key part of the investigation is to find out whether the two incidents are connected.
He said police are also asking anyone with dash cam or CCTV footage to come forward.
Toronto police on Saturday afternoon said in a post on X that it stands with Toronto's Jewish community.
"We understand the fear and pain this causes, especially on Yom Kippur," Toronto police said. "To Toronto's Jewish community: we stand with you. Your safety is our priority. We are committed to finding those responsible."
On Saturday afternoon, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said in a statement on X that he was "very disturbed" to hear there were shots fired at a Jewish school in Toronto as families marked Yom Kippur.
"As we wait for more details, my heart goes out to the students, staff, and parents who must be terrified and hurting today," Trudeau said. "Antisemitism is a disgusting and dangerous form of hate - and we won't let it stand."
Bais Chaya Mushka is an Orthodox Jewish girls' school which offers programs from junior kindergarten to Grade 8.
With an enrolment of about 225 students, the school caters to the Jewish Chabad community. Chabad is a major movement within mainstream Jewish tradition with its roots in the Chassidic movement of the 18th century, according to Chabad.org.
In May, following the first time the school was hit by gunfire, Rabbi Yaacov Vidal, Bais Chaya Mushka's principal, told the Star it was "pretty shocking" to be told of the incident, which occurred on Shabbat, the Jewish Day of Rest.
"I think it's very sad that people target schools where children are innocent and just create this extra fear and chaos for no reason," Vidal said.
And while Toronto police at the time said it was too early to determine if the shooting was a hate crime, Vidal told the Star the incident was antisemitic.
"I think I would consider this a hate crime," Vidal said. "I don't see any other reason why this would occur."
Premier Doug Ford called the incident "unacceptable" while speaking to reporters at a separate event earlier Saturday.
"We live in a peaceful society," Ford said. "We've never seen anything like this. We all grew up in Toronto, we all get along, and it's just unacceptable what we're seeing."
Ford added "these people need to be caught" and "thrown in jail."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow told reporters at the same event that she advised the suspects to "turn yourself in."
"Understand that this is a time of the highest holiday, and think about children, think about a time of worship," Chow said. "For someone to do something like that is just horrific."
With files from Abhiraj Lamba and Sheila Wang

Michael Bublé, Martin Short celebrate Mount Sinai Hospital's 100th anniversary

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/michael-bublé-martin-short-celebrate-mount-sinai/docview/3115765512/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 58 of 500

Singh unclear on next move after pharmacare win: NDP leader says focus is on forcing Liberals to get provincial deals signed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-unclear-on-next-move-after-pharmacare-win/docview/3115717841/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+unclear+on+next+move+after+pharmacare+win%3A+NDP+leader+says+focus+is+on+forcing+Liberals+to+get+provincial+deals+signed&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh refused to say Friday whether his party will help the Liberal minority government survive long enough to ink pharmacare deals with the provinces, as Ontario indicated it is still waiting for "any details" about the promised program.
On Parliament Hill, Singh hailed how legislation underpinning a national pharmacare program became law Thursday night, describing it as a victory that New Democrats have fought to achieve for generations. Singh claimed the legislation never would have passed without his NDP using its influence in Parliament to push the Liberals to act through the parliamentary alliance that his party killed in early September.
But he was less clear about how the NDP would use that influence now, despite urging the government to sign deals to provide the coverage as soon as possible.
The Liberals have said the program - budgeted at almost $1.5 billion over five years - will provide government-paid coverage for diabetes medication and birth control, and could expand in the future.
"Our focus is on forcing this Liberal government to get those deals signed," Singh said.
He declined to say several times whether he would keep the government in power to make sure that happens or try and topple them if it doesn't. With no direct negotiations with Trudeau at the moment, Singh said he is simply making his desire public.
"I want to see people that have free medication when it comes to diabetes, and free birth control," he said.
Earlier Friday, federal Health Minister Mark Holland said that formal negotiations with provinces can start now that the pharmacare bill has passed, but that "informal" talks have already been going on.
Holland told reporters Friday that he's confident Ottawa can sign deals with all provinces by next spring, describing talks that have happened so far as "very" positive.
"It's time to start signing deals with provinces," said Holland.
"This is an important opportunity to make sure the people who need medication will get it.
One province - British Columbia - has already signed a memorandum of understanding with Ottawa, agreeing to work together on a program to cover diabetes medication, birth control and hormone replacement therapy for menopause.
Alberta and Quebec have said they want to opt out of the new program, but receive their share of the money. And Ontario is still waiting for details about the program from Ottawa, according to a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones.
Jones said in February that the province was "waiting for details" on the program and how it "matches or conflicts" with Ontario's existing coverage system.
"Ontario, like many provinces, has yet to receive any details on the federal government's proposal," said Jones's spokesperson Hannah Jensen by email on Friday.
"Instead of calling on the provinces to strike a deal with no detail, the federal government should focus on working collaboratively with provinces to adequately support health-care services across the country."
The future of the promised national pharmacare program is also unclear, since the Liberal minority government - without the guaranteed support of the New Democrats - could fall if the opposition Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and NDP band together to trigger an election.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has criticized the program, arguing it would be unfair to unions that fought for private coverage to have their prescription drug plans replaced by a government-paid "single-payer" alternative.
On Friday, Holland described the plan for government-paid coverage of diabetes medication and birth control as a "pilot" for single-payer pharmacare. The Liberals still haven't decided if the "end state" of a broader program will be a single-payer government scheme, he said.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh declined to say several times Friday whether he would keep the Liberal governmentin power long enough to ink pharmacare deals with the provinces or try and topple them if it doesn't.Justin Tang The Canadian Press

Document 59 of 500

Economy doesn't need populism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/economy-doesnt-need-populism/docview/3115717728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Economy+doesn%27t+need+populism&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's next election is a year away, or perhaps less now that the federal New Democratic Party has withdrawn its support from the governing Liberals.
In announcing the split, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh took aim not just at Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, but also at his alleged shadowy supporters, charging that the Liberal leader is "too beholden to corporate interests."
It was like watching satire, except that good satire requires a grain of truth. Here there was none.
In recent years, the Liberal government has received little applause from business. It hiked taxes on banks, insurance companies and tech companies. It banned the use of replacement workers during strikes in regulated industries, stoking business concern that labour disruptions would become both more likely and more costly. And business leaders raised alarms when this year's federal budget both increased the effective tax rate on capital gains and phased out some tax breaks for new business investment.
These policies came at a time when Canada desperately needs more entrepreneurship and more investment to reverse its declining productivity.
While the prime minister is usually respectful in conversations with business leaders, he's also no stranger to populism. He regularly invokes "fairness" - the idea that businesses and individuals should "pay a little more" to enable spending on housing and other priorities.
There are two problems with this reasoning.
First, he presents a false choice. There is no indication the government has carefully and comprehensively reviewed its spending priorities and its tax policies, and then made strategic choices to incentivize business investment and job creation while maximizing value for taxpayer dollars.
Second, while the government offers good programs to help small businesses grow, there's an attitude among many politicians that small businesses stop being worthy when they become too successful. Canada must make it easier, not harder, for businesses of all sizes to grow by attracting investment and retaining capital and talent.
Across the aisle, the Conservative party enjoys a commanding lead in the polls. Its leader, Pierre Poilievre, has shown a refreshing focus on engaging working-class voters, an area of historic weakness for his party.
On business, however, he has played the populist card as aggressively as the NDP. He argues corporate Canada has been too collegial in pursuing dialogue with government, rather than being more aggressively hostile or running national public relations and advertising campaigns.
It's time for all political leaders to recognize a reality: when it comes to fixing Canada's productivity crisis, we need more partnership and less populism.
The NDP should emulate its provincial cousins, who are far more open to dialogue with business. They have stronger economic policy platforms, which correlate with better electoral track records.
The Liberals badly need a fresh message that will win votes back from the Conservatives. How about a vision of rewarding risk-taking rather than punishing it? How about leaning into free trade and supporting Canada's exporters? How about a comprehensive review of all government spending and taxes to ensure a laserlike focus on investing in people and technology, while attracting and rewarding investment and the productivity, jobs and growth that come with it?
The Conservatives seem poised to form the next government, and they need a governing agenda. While of course Poilievre can keep hammering away at his political rivals, with everyone else he has an opportunity to shift from the clenched fist of opposition to the open hand of a prime minister in waiting. That's where he and his team will find a rich vein of ideas.
At a time when many Canadians are either anxious, angry or both, we need leaders who can calm things down. Listen with empathy. Understand the complexity of our challenges. Act with urgency. Unite us, rather than divide us.
That means working with the nation's business leaders, entrepreneurs, job creators and investors. They are the ones who will create the inclusive, sustainable growth Canadians want, need and deserve.
Daniel Tisch is president and CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is not shy about playing the populist card when it comes to business, writes Daniel Tisch. But it's time for all political leaders to recognize a reality: when it comes to fixing Canada's productivity crisis, we need more partnership and less populism.Christinne Muschi The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Daniel Tisch Contributor Daniel Tisch is president and CEO of the Ontario Chamber of Commerce.

Driving through life instead of enjoying it: Car Sick, Opinion, Oct. 5 Cities are not livable if everything is built around roads packed with oversized single-occupant SUVs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/driving-through-life-instead-enjoying/docview/3115717725/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Driving+through+life+instead+of+enjoying+it%3A+Car+Sick%2C+Opinion%2C+Oct.+5+Cities+are+not+livable+if+everything+is+built+around+roads+packed+with+oversized+single-occupant+SUVs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Car Sick, Opinion, Oct. 5
There is no war on cars in Toronto. Not yet at least.
Julia Belluz's commentary on life in car-dependent Toronto only briefly touched on the effect of extended car commutes on one's body and spirit. She instead focused on the many benefits of pedestrian-based living in cities like Paris, but I do believe that the negatives of car dependency need to be emphasized.
An hour's commute every day, multiplied twice per day and repeated every workday year after year is a criminal waste of time and energy for the average citizen. And it's an offence to the communities that have to accommodate the cars passing them by in ever-amassing numbers. We are living through our neighbourhoods rather than in them, it seems.
Amsterdam was a tangle of car traffic in the 1970s, much like Toronto is today, and as a longtime resident I am hoping we are finally on the cusp of a similar revelation here in The Big Smoke.
Back in 2010, Mayor Rob Ford put the brakes on his predecessor's Transit City LRT network construction, announcing "The war on the car is over." It was a ridiculous statement, considering how the war had barely begun. And now Rob's brother Premier Doug Ford is toying with a multibillion-dollar road tunnel under Highway 401.
Cities are not livable if everything is built around roads that are congested with oversized single-occupant SUVs. The cafes and artist neighbours that Belluz describes are a minor gain to one's lifestyle compared to the civic, economic and emotional gains we could all enjoy if we could move away from the car-brained mentality that so many of us have and start working towards something better. One foot forward; one step at a time.
Dave McIntyre, Toronto
CREDIT: ;

Document 61 of 500

The real problem with Doug Ford's lakefront parking palace

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/real-problem-with-doug-fords-lakefront-parking/docview/3115717678/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+real+problem+with+Doug+Ford%27s+lakefront+parking+palace&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There has been a lot of outrage, and rightly so, over the destruction of more than 800 trees at Ontario Place to make room for a private mega-spa. For my part, I have been fixating on a different environmentally disastrous detail in the recently released 95-year lease between Doug Ford's provincial government and Therme, the Austrian firm developing the spa.
The province is on the hook for building at least 1,800 parking spots for use between Therme and Live Nation. Infrastructure Ontario's Michael Lindsay told reporters that Ford plans to build a total of 2,500 new spots. If the province fails to build sufficient parking before the spa opens, taxpayers will be on the hook for up to $2.2 million per year. The structure itself will cost undisclosed millions.
The parkade, which will not only undoubtedly be a physical blight, is also a perfect symbol of Ford's inability to see more than five minutes into the future by committing the city further to a virtually irreversible vision of car dependency.
I have long been a critic of the abundance of free, cheap parking spots in North America, which gets me yelled at by people across the political spectrum and forces me to add, "Wait, let me finish!" The issue: For much of the last century, new construction was required to dedicate a certain amount of space to parking. These mandatory parking minimums were often arbitrarily decided, built around studies aligned to suburban developments and designed to accommodate visitors on the busiest days of the year. The result is an abundance of space used to store vehicles, and less space available for things like public parks, affordable housing and other amenities.
Parking, like all car-centric infrastructure, forces cities to sprawl, making neighbourhoods less dense and less efficiently serviced by public transit. Driving then becomes the most convenient option for people, inducing more parking spots, more highways and more sprawl.
Once this infrastructure is built, it is difficult to reverse course. Building alternatives, including better transit routes, bike lanes and dense, walkable neighbourhoods, requires taking space away from private vehicles. As we have seen again and again, drivers will fight back.
And still, the city is trying. Toronto has adopted a strategy to reduce gas emissions to net zero by 2040, a plan that cannot be feasible unless we reimagine our relationship to cars. In 2021, Toronto got rid of mandatory parking minimums for new developments, with exceptions for accessible spaces. Parking can still be built in abundance, but it isn't required by law. New housing only has to include the amount of spaces that residents will actually use.
This decision will affect future parking options, but managing existing lots is more challenging. Last spring, the city tried to introduce a stormwater charge. The tax would apply to the amount of hard surface area on a property, including parking lots, and would go towards offsetting some of the billions that the city has allocated for stormwater management. The plan was shelved after it received international derision, including from Donald Trump, Jr., who called it a "rain tax." The city has been reconsidering the plan with an increased focus on industrial and commercial properties after July's flash flooding cost over an estimated one billion dollars in damages. The rain tax debacle proved yet again that once expensive, unsustainable, car-centric infrastructure is put in place, it can't be removed or altered without resistance.
Which brings us back to Ontario Place, a location that is already served by the TTC and the GO Train, and is a planned subway stop on the upcoming Ontario Line. While environmentalists and public transit advocates have been working tirelessly to find solutions that reduce the amount of unnecessary parking in transit-rich areas, Ford's government is happily spending millions to turn a public greenspace into a parking lot with hundreds more spaces than its already bloated requirements are demanding. The trees have been razed, but the proposed parking structure has yet to be built. Once it has, there is no going back.
Anna Fitzpatrick is a Toronto-based writer.
She is the author of the novel "Good Girl" and
the picture book "Margot and the Moon Landing."
CAPTION: The Ontario government is set to build 2,500 new parking spots for the Therme Spa at Ontario Place.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star
CREDIT: Anna Fitzpatrick Contributor Anna Fitzpatrick is a Toronto-based writer. She is the author of the novel "Good Girl" and the picture book "Margot and the Moon Landing.";

Gala of the century

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gala-century/docview/3115714885/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gala+of+the+century&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mount Sinai Hospital celebrated its 100th anniversary last month with a Gala of the Century at the Four Seasons Centre, hosted by Martin Short. The star-studded evening featured a concert by Michael Bublé, along with performances by Divine Brown and Theo Tams, raising $12.5 million. Special guests included Premier Doug Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow, along with donors Ken and Jennifer Tanenbaum, along with Andrew Hoffman and Leslie Giller.

Document 64 of 500

Is Trudeau fated to end up like Wynne?: Liberal MPs who were once part of Queen's Park point out differences between 2018 and 2024

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-trudeau-fated-end-up-like-wynne/docview/3115714550/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Trudeau+fated+to+end+up+like+Wynne%3F%3A+Liberal+MPs+who+were+once+part+of+Queen%27s+Park+point+out+differences+between+2018+and+2024&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is it déjà vu all over again?
That's a loaded question for the half dozen Liberal MPs who were part of the doomed Kathleen Wynne government that lost to Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives in 2018 and now sit in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's federal caucus facing Pierre Poilievre's surging Conservatives.
Two of those veteran politicians wouldn't touch it with a 10-foot pole, their silence a telling indication of where things appear to be headed and how history could repeat itself.
Their more (self-described) "optimistic" colleagues prefer to emphasize - not the similarities of the electoral scenarios - but the differences between Queen's Park in 2018 and Ottawa now.
Of the six politicians now in the Commons, only two were able to hang onto their provincial seats in 2018 when the Ford juggernaut reduced an almost 15-year Ontario Liberal dynasty to a tiny rump without official party status in the legislature: community and social services minister Michael Coteau and solicitor general Marie-France Lalonde.
Charles Sousa, who had been finance minister, lost his seat, as did attorney-general Yasir Naqvi, health minister Helena Jaczek and Etobicoke Centre MPP Yvan Baker.
Jaczek - who sources said had been outspoken in Wynne's cabinet in the year leading up to the 2018 Liberal defeat and warned about the premier's unpopularity at the doorsteps due to soaring electricity bills - politely declined to comment when asked to reflect on what this period in federal politics feels like.
Jaczek was dropped from Trudeau's cabinet last summer after announcing she will not seek re-election in Markham-Stouffville whenever the next federal election is held.
Baker declined to weigh in, too.
But Coteau, Sousa, Naqvi and Lalonde insist the federal Liberals are not doomed to the same fate as the provincial party, stressing Trudeau is an effective campaigner and they still have time to turn things around.
Coteau, who was one of Wynne's top ministers, said the comparisons between the former premier and the prime minister miss the mark.
"The players are different, the personalities are different, the time is different," he said, noting provincial and national issues also differ.
However, there is one key similarity, he said. After a certain time, all governments accumulate "challenges."
With Wynne - who had revived the provincial Grits after succeeding Dalton McGuinty in 2013 and won a surprise majority victory the following year - her predecessor's decision to cancel two gas-fired power plants in Oakville and Mississauga before the 2011 election ultimately hurt her in 2018, despite what her ex-cabinet colleagues say was a good governing record.
For Coteau, the weight of 15 years of Liberal rule bedevilled Wynne's second election bid from the get-go.
"A narrative hardened, and it was hard to change the talking points even when you did good things," said Coteau, pointing to Sousa's feat under Wynne of a balanced budget in 2017.
In contrast, he argued that the animosity to Trudeau is not so baked in that it can't be overcome with time, economic improvements, a good platform and a strong campaign.
"I'm not saying the world is all rosy, but I think that the cement hasn't dried fully in regards to where Canadians want to go, what they're thinking about. I think there's still the ability to have a discussion" about "an alternative to the Pierre Poilievre approach," said Coteau, whose Don Valley East seat is disappearing in redistribution so he will be running in neighbouring Scarborough-Woburn.
Two of his former Ontario cabinet colleagues, not now in Ottawa, said they feel the antipathy toward Trudeau mirrors what they heard about Wynne six years ago.
"It may even be worse. You see these 'F- Trudeau' signs everywhere; there were never 'F- Wynne' signs and flags on people's trucks. People were mad at Kathleen about their hydro bills, but now it's unhinged toward the PM," said one ex-provincial minister who spoke on condition they not be named.
Another, whom the Star agreed not to identify so they could freely discuss internal deliberations, said the federal Liberals should be thinking of doing what the provincial Grits regret not doing: saving the furniture by switching leaders instead of allowing everyone to go down with the ship.
"Even if (Wynne) had stepped down (in 2017), we would have lost, but we wouldn't have gone down to seven or eight seats," said the retired second former minister. "We could have lived to fight another day. The OLP didn't do that; the LPC still could."
Wynne told the Star this summer the decision by a political leader to stay or go is a bruising and deeply personal one, and advised Trudeau to contemplate why he wanted to stay.
Naqvi said the federal Liberals' nine years in office is a big reason why "we are behind in the polls," but added, "I don't think it's lost."
"The next little bit matters a lot. The campaign matters a lot. I ran in 2011. It was my second election. I ran in 2018. Even in talking to people at the door, to me it feels more like 2011 than 2018," he said, referring to the McGuinty Liberals' third consecutive election victory.
By then, the premier had been in power eight years compared to Trudeau's nine.
McGuinty had weathered the 2008 economic crisis and recession and had brought in policies that Naqvi emphasized were good for the province - like the harmonized sales tax - but were "not popular" because some prices went up.
"We were able to pull that campaign and win that election, a minority government, by one seat," he said, referring to the Liberals winning 53 seats in what was then a 107-member legislature.
Debilitated by the gas-plants scandal, his popularity plummeting, McGuinty prorogued the legislature in October 2012 and resigned as leader, triggering the leadership race won by Wynne three months later.
Naqvi conceded he does hear similar comments at the door now as he heard when campaigning under McGuinty, when voters said, "We love how you're doing but I think maybe it's time for you to change the leader."
Back then and now, Naqvi said, he shifts the focus onto the Liberals' opponent, insisting that in Ottawa Centre voters at the door are turned off by the Conservative leader.
As Ontario Liberal president, Naqvi oversaw the expedited leadership contest that replaced McGuinty. He said the federal party needs to do three things to turn the ship around: draw a clear contrast and show "how dangerous Poilievre is for the country"; produce a policy prescription on affordability that shows people "we get it"; and "articulate a vision for the future."
Sousa said he sees "similarities" in that Ontario voters in 2018 faced a flood of "misinformation" in Conservative partisan rhetoric, including from groups like Canada Proud and Ontario Proud. Sousa argued Poilievre and his supporters are following the same playbook, "creating fear, division and promoting some of the hate that exists, especially in the toxic nature or the anonymity that exists, through social media. It feeds the beast."
He believes another "similarity" is that Poilievre, like Ford, cannot be trusted to leave social programs intact despite his pre-writ rhetoric. That is part of what Sousa said motivates him to run again despite the dismal poll showings for Trudeau. Sousa cited other personal priorities - to boost economic growth, protect national unity and ensure Canada's voice is heard on the world stage - and why he believes there is a path to win under Trudeau.
"The prime minister is very competitive. He realizes that things could be in jeopardy - some of the programs have been put in place. And I believe he is trying to do what's best for the benefit of others, whereas Poilievre is using some of these issues as a platform to benefit himself."
Lalonde said she is "100 per cent" behind Trudeau and doesn't think there are parallels to be drawn between Wynne circa 2018 and Trudeau now.
"I don't think it's fair," she said. "For me, it's not about the polls. For me, it's about the delivery. My government wants to continue to support Canadians."
She intends to focus on the needs of her riding, but says she is not running a "local campaign. I'm fighting Pierre Poilievre."
All campaigns, she added, are "tough." In 2014, "no one thought I was going to win ... I won in 2018 ... And you know, (the) campaign matters."
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Trudeau government's pharmacare bill is now law. What now?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/trudeau-governments-pharmacare-bill-is-now-law/docview/3115819502/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+pharmacare+bill+is+now+law.+What+now%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh refused to say Friday whether his party will help the Liberal minority government survive long enough to ink pharmacare deals with the provinces, as Ontario indicated it is still waiting for "any details" about the promised program.
On Parliament Hill, Singh hailed how legislation underpinning a national pharmacare program became law Thursday night, describing it as a victory that New Democrats have fought to achieve for generations. Singh claimed the legislation never would have passed without his NDP using its influence in Parliament to push the Liberals to act through the parliamentary alliance that his party killed in early September.
But he was less clear about how the NDP would use that influence now, despite urging the government to sign deals to provide the coverage as soon as possible.
The Liberals have said the program - budgeted at almost $1.5 billion over five years - will provide government-paid coverage for diabetes medication and birth control, and could expand in the future.
"Our focus is on forcing this Liberal government to get those deals signed," Singh said.
He declined to say several times whether he would keep the government in power to make sure that happens or try and topple them if it doesn't. With no direct negotiations with Trudeau at the moment, Singh said he is simply making his desire public.
"I want to see people that have free medication when it comes to diabetes, and free birth control," he said.
Earlier Friday, federal Health Minister Mark Holland said that formal negotiations with provinces can start now that the pharmacare bill has passed, but that "informal" talks have already been going on.
Holland told reporters Friday that he's confident Ottawa can sign deals with all provinces by next spring, describing talks that have happened so far as "very" positive.
"It's time to start signing deals with provinces," said Holland.
"This is an important opportunity to make sure the people who need medication will get it.
One province - British Columbia - has already signed a memorandum of understanding with Ottawa, agreeing to work together on a program to cover diabetes medication, birth control and hormone replacement therapy for menopause.
Alberta and Quebec have said they want to opt out of the new program but receive their share of the money. And Ontario is still waiting for details about the program from Ottawa, according to a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones.
Jones said in February that the province was "waiting for details" on the program and how it "matches or conflicts" with Ontario's existing coverage system.
"Ontario, like many provinces, has yet to receive any details on the federal government's proposal," said Jones's spokesperson Hannah Jensen by email on Friday.
"Instead of calling on the provinces to strike a deal with no detail, the federal government should focus on working collaboratively with provinces to adequately support health-care services across the country."
The future of the promised national pharmacare program is also unclear, since the Liberal minority government - without the guaranteed support of the New Democrats - could fall if the opposition Conservatives, Bloc Québécois and NDP band together to trigger an election.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has criticized the program, arguing it would be unfair to unions that fought for private coverage to have their prescription drug plans replaced by a government-paid "single-payer" alternative.
On Friday, Holland described the plan for government-paid coverage of diabetes medication and birth control as a "pilot" for single-payer pharmacare. The Liberals still haven't decided if the "end state" of a broader program will be a single-payer government scheme, he said.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh speaks at a news conference on pharmacare, in the Foyer of the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, on Oct. 11, 2024.

Toronto mom feared for her life after water taxi accident + what's Justin Trudeau's re-election plan?

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+mom+feared+for+her+life+after+water+taxi+accident+%2B+what%27s+Justin+Trudeau%27s+re-election+plan%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, October 11 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a nearly disastrous water taxi accident, Liberals anxious about their re-election prospects and the sex assault trial of Toronto Coun. Michael Thompson.
DON'T MISSA Toronto woman feared for her life after a water taxi accident
Toronto mom Brooke was nursing a broken arm when she set out for a picnic on Centre Island this summer, Francine Kopun writes. But as she was disembarking the taxi, one foot on the boat and the other on the dock, the captain suddenly reversed - sending her crashing into the water. Unable to swim with her injury, Brooke looked up to find the boat "inches from my face." Here's what happened next.
Word from the company: A spokesperson for the Art & Water branded taxis disputed the severity of the situation, saying: "At no point was the passenger in danger of being crushed between the boat and the dock."A wider issue: Exacerbating the incident were Centre Island's docking facilities, which do not move up and down with the water. This leads to a shifting distance between taxis and the dock - in Brooke's case, a gap of about two feet.Anxious Liberals are wondering if Justin Trudeau has a re-election plan
Liberal MPs are feeling the heat, but the party seems to be idling as the Tories enter "full campaign mode." That's what insiders tell Tonda MacCharles and Alex Ballingall, as they face down grim polling data with no new campaign director, no new ad campaign rollout and no major cabinet shuffle. "The status quo is leading us to obliteration," one MP said. What can be done?
Party sentiment: After speaking to more than 20 MPs and insiders, the Star heard there's no real threat to Justin Trudeau's leadership from within caucus - even though some believe the party would be better off without him.Meanwhile: The Grits' national campaign director Jeremy Broadhurst officially left his post Monday, after telling the Prime Minister he saw no real path to re-election under Trudeau's leadership. Take a closer look at his decision.A witness says Michael Thompson's friend acted like Ghislaine Maxwell
A 24-year-old witness in the sex assault trial of sitting Toronto councillor Michael Thompson said she felt "groomed" by his friend, Tracey, at the Muskoka cottage where Thompson allegedly sexually assaulted two other women, Betsy Powell reports. The 64-year-old Thompson suggested Tracey be her career mentor, but the witness felt she was being groomed for sex trafficking, she testified. Here's what you missed from the second day of Thompson's trial.
The allegations: Thompson has pleaded not guilty to two sexual assaults. Prosecutors alleged Thompson massaged a woman in a sexual manner without consent and "forced himself" on another who was "very, very drunk."Word from the defence: Lawyers asked why the witness kept communicating with Tracey in the weeks following the cottage trip if she felt she was being groomed. The witness replied she was testing if Tracey was sincere about being a mentor.WHAT ELSE
Pierre Poilievre has urged Justin Trudeau to list an Iran-allied group as a terrorist organization.Doug Ford "needs to come clean" about the Greenbelt scandal, opposition leaders are saying.TD Bank must pay $3 billion in a historic money-laundering settlement with the U.S. Justice Department.Air Canada pilots just ratified the richest contract offer in the airline's history. Check out what they got.Investors wreaked havoc on Toronto's condo market, leaving us with tiny units no one wants. Document 67 of 500

Toronto police to reveal details of new helicopters to combat auto theft this morning

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-police-reveal-details-new-helicopters/docview/3115487496/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+police+to+reveal+details+of+new+helicopters+to+combat+auto+theft+this+morning&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto police are about to announce details of new provincial air support to help combat surging automobile crime in the city.
Chief Myron Demkiw and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner will provide the update at 11 a.m. Friday at Downsview Airport. The conference will be streamed on Youtube.
Premier Doug Ford had previously pledged $134 million in funding for five new helicopters to patrol the GTA and Ottawa, in a bid to stem an escalating auto theft crisis in the cities.
According to his plan, two helicopters will be purchased for the Ontario Provincial Police to work with the Ottawa and Toronto police, and three more will be purchased and operated by Durham, Halton and Peel police.
The new Greater Toronto Area Joint Air Support Unit (JASU) will help hinder crimes including auto theft, carjackings, street racing and impaired driving, Ford said during the announcement in late September.
Ontario's spring budget had previously provided enough funding to lease four helicopters, but the new money will allow the province to outright buy five choppers and cover ongoing operating costs.
Experts previously told the Star's Queens Park reporter Kristin Rushowy the move is "not the end all, be all," and that little research has been done in Canada into whether helicopters actually deter theft.
But having eyes in the skies can help in tracking stolen cars or criminals without having to resort to dangerous high-speed chases.
These types of aircraft are used by police agencies in major cities around the world can be "a highly valuable asset to put at the disposal of law enforcement" - especially given Toronto's notorious gridlock, Christian Leuprecht, a professor at both Queen's University and Royal Military College of Canada in Kingston, told Rushowy.
For their part, GTA police rejoiced at the news.
"Equipped with the latest technology, the helicopters will help keep Toronto safe from violent carjackings," Demkiw said at the time.
Peel Regional Police Chief Nishan Duraiappah also praised the "monumental investment," adding that it will "provide much-needed support to our officers and help them do the difficult work they do every day to enhance community safety."
CAPTION: Toronto police are expected to announce details of new provincial helicopters Friday morning, after Doug Ford pledged $134 million for their purchase.

Nuit Blanche was a magical evening of unity

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/nuit-blanche-was-magical-evening-unity/docview/3115487491/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nuit+Blanche+was+a+magical+evening+of+unity&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nuit Blanche asks us to confront a timely paradox. Can we solve it? Oct. 6
What touched me Saturday night at Nuit Blanche wasn't so much the installations, but the buzz of excitement in the atmosphere. It was a magical evening of beautiful fall weather with thousands of Torontonians relaxing by the waterfront. It was an opportunity to stroll hand-in-hand with my husband, taking in the sights - adults in silly costumes, and a family pulling children in a wagon adorned with lights. We need more joy and a break amid our political challenges. Nuit Blanche provided it.
Monica Kavanagh, Thornhill
A slice of the healthier female perspective on the world
In Wednesday's Opinion pages a Star columnist argued that we stop shaming women who don't have children. A female rabbi argued that all peoples in the Middle East have shared humanity, while a female Palestinian author described her efforts to humanize her people in the eyes of her readers. Finally, there was a piece about Kamala Harris' battle to stop the Donald Trump/J.D. Vance stance on making abortions as difficult to obtain as possible.
These articles are all written from a healthier female perspective while wars are perpetrated by male autocrats who need conflict to boost their sagging popularity. Indeed, the mullahs of Iran need to deflect how unpopular they are with women, university students and secular Iranians by continuing to stoke the flames of war. Follow the money trail and you will see that trillions of dollars are being spent on weapons that have no concern for humanity and that despoil both neighbourhoods and the surrounding environment.
Saying that war is a "guy thing" is cold comfort for the millions of people paying the mortal price of these ghastly ventures. But it ought to make us vote against men like Trump/Vance, Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford who embrace narrowly conceived male priorities and to protest against the weapons makers and companies that trade their stock.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Canada's MAID program has given me incredible peace of mind
Canada shouldn't be the world's example of how not to implement MAID, Oct. 5
As a Canadian of mature years, I have to say that our MAID program has given me incredible peace of mind to know that if I developed a painful and life ending disease like ALS or some types of cancer, I could make the call about when I've had enough and want to end my life peacefully with dignity and respect. It's my opinion that an adult of sound mind should be allowed to decide when their suffering has become unbearable; no doubt that bar is different for everyone and that's OK. Who is anyone else to judge where that line is for another person? When the legislation was enacted, I felt an immediate sense of relief to know that it would be my decision to make as an adult when or if I felt that my suffering was unbearable. I'm proud to live in a country that allows me to make that final decision for myself. I've been very open with my children about this topic and would expect them to respect and understand my decision should the time come. I was shocked to find that the U.K. didn't have such a program and I hope they do engage in a robust discussion about what their people want.
Anne Sayers, Scarborough
I was fortunate to be able to attend the recent World Federation of Right To Die Societies conference in Dublin, Ireland. The continuously recycled negative media about Canada's medical assistance in dying (MAID) law was certainly a topic of discussion, but what quickly became evident is that our more liberal and equitable MAID program was what most countries aspire to. The U.K. will vote soon to legalize assisted dying for terminally ill people who aren't expected to live more than six months, similar to the criteria in other countries. What we learned in Canada after the adoption of Bill C-14 - initiating Canada's MAID law - is that suffering exists outside of the strict rule that a person must be nearing end of life. In Canada, our criteria and safeguards are rigorous, but our assisted dying legislation includes a broader scope of choice and suffering. For some, the short, six-month time frame and criteria that the medication must be self-administered result in many never accessing the assisted death they wanted. I believe that the intention of all assisted dying laws around the globe should be about compassion, the fundamental rights of an individual and avoiding suffering.
Helen Long, CEO, Dying With Dignity Canada, Toronto
Try this to relieve gridlock
Stop floating balloons to relieve highway gridlock - theoretical proposals are not solutions. What Torontonians need now are reasonable, cost-effective, sound solutions that can be implemented immediately. I propose a time-limited experiment in which the government pays for or heavily subsidized Highway 407 tolls for all transport trucks and restricts transports travelling on the 400 non-toll series to the right-hand lane. Let's see what impact this would have on gridlock.
Jerrold Lerman, Toronto
Missing email is one too many excuses from the mayorMayor Olivia Chow to apologize for not attending Oct. 7 vigil - blames missing email, Oct. 9
It is certainly interesting that Mayor Olivia Chow has now, for one reason or another, missed three events dedicated to supporting the Jewish community of Toronto in the one-year remembrance of the horrific events of Oct. 7. The excuse this time was that there was a "lost email." Numerous politicians and dignitaries (including some of her own council members) seemed to have received their emails and attended the vigil, but her email was apparently "lost!" There have now been one too many excuses for her disinterest and apathy. Shameful!
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
Canada is far from perfect but let us not forget the good we are already doing
Even though Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre would have us believe that our country is broken, let us stop and reflect at this time of Thanksgiving on all that is good in Canada: a health-care system that does not discriminate; an education system that ranks continuously in the top 10; freedom of speech that allows protests and rallies without fear of death or imprisonment; a wilderness that is second to none; our efforts to reconcile the wrongs experienced by the Indigenous people of this land and the incorporation of their art and beliefs into our daily lives; that we are not a melting pot but a truly multinational, all-embracing people open to welcome and respect our many differences ; one of the top countries to raise children; our attempts to deal with the environmental crisis and that most of us are ready and able to apologize and be civil among many other blessings. True, there is much to do, but let us not forget the good we are already doing and have done. Happy Thanksgiving.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora

Chow mulls changes to bike lanes: Bloor Street West corridors have drawn criticism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/chow-mulls-changes-bike-lanes/docview/3115312530/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chow+mulls+changes+to+bike+lanes%3A+Bloor+Street+West+corridors+have+drawn+criticism&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Olivia Chow is considering changes to bike lanes along Bloor Street West in Etobicoke that have drawn widespread criticism from residents, including Premier Doug Ford.
"I'm trying to come up with a compromise that would reopen some car lanes but keep the bike lanes and make the design work better," the mayor told Newstalk 1010 this week.
She said she's hoping her efforts will convince Ford - who last month announced plans for legislation limiting construction of new bike lanes in an effort to ease gridlock - that issues related to bike lanes can be resolved locally.
"I've asked the premier to give me some time to work on it, and I'm working flat out on it," she said.
A petition launched by Etobicoke resident Cody MacRae, advocating for a re-evaluation of the bike lanes installed last autumn between Runnymede Road and Aberfoyle Crescent, has garnered more than 13,000 signatures.
MacRae, founder of Balance on Bloor, said Thursday that residents "look forward to working with the mayor regarding a solution that serves the needs of residents."
Balance on Bloor has taken issue with the city's data and consultation process when it comes to bike lanes.
"Despite repeated requests, the city has yet to provide clarity on what defines a 'successful' bike lane implementation," MacRae said. "Local residents urge transparency and metrics to assess how the project impacts the community's safety, accessibility and local economy.
"The community looks forward to Mayor Chow's support in addressing these ongoing issues, urging a transparent and meaningful approach to transportation planning in the neighbourhood."
MacRae said the mayor met him and other members of Balance on Bloor, local business representatives, local Coun. Amber Morley and transportation staff - including Barbara Gray, general manager of transportation services - on Monday.
Chow did not respond to a request for comment from the Star late Thursday afternoon.
Michael Longfield, executive director of Cycle Toronto, said the mayor hasn't said she's going to remove bike lanes.
"It sounds like the mayor is open to considering options," said Longfield. "At this point it's looking at configuration changes."
Longfield said Ford's promise to regulate bike lanes is "overreach by the province."
The proposed legislation would override municipal powers by blocking construction of bike lanes if they lead to a loss of lanes to vehicular traffic.
The measure is one of several, including 24-7 construction for new highway projects like the 413 from Milton to Highway 400, in the proposed "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," sources have told the Star.
With files from Rob Ferguson

Ford urged to 'come clean' on land swap: RCMP's investigation enters its second year

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-urged-come-clean-on-land-swap/docview/3115312401/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+urged+to+%27come+clean%27+on+land+swap%3A+RCMP%27s+investigation+enters+its+second+year&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the RCMP's investigation into the Greenbelt scandal enters its second year, Premier Doug Ford's political rivals say he "needs to come clean about his role" in the $8.28-billion land swap scheme.
In the wake of revelations in the Star that at least eight of Ford's current and former aides have already been interviewed by the Mounties, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "there is still so much we don't know about this mess."
The premier, who has not yet met with police, has insisted "we have nothing to hide" about his aborted plan to open up 7,400 acres of protected Greenbelt land to housing development, and apologized for the fiasco last year.
Stiles isn't buying that.
"Reports that the RCMP is investigating potential bank accounts in Europe show that the extent of this corruption may be bigger than anyone thought," she said, citing an exclusive story in the Star to mark Thursday's first anniversary of the launch of the criminal probe.
"An apology is not enough, Doug Ford needs to come clean about his role in this scheme to rob Ontarians of the Greenbelt to enrich a wealthy few," the New Democrat said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "the RCMP is focused on the premier's inner sanctum because he has been at the heart of this scandal since day one.
"Doug Ford isn't in it for you. He's given away billions of your money to his rich buddies while people struggle to find a family doctor, classroom sizes balloon and the cost of living skyrockets," said Crombie. "I have the same question for Doug Ford that the RCMP has been asking ... Where did the direction come from to remove those lands from the Greenbelt?"
The Star reported Wednesday that members of the RCMP elite "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the branch that looks into political crimes and corruption, have been peppering interviewees with that query for months.
Armed with internal email and text messages they have gathered, the detectives have hinted that they may need to access banking records in Europe as part of their probe.
They have been interviewing current and former Progressive Conservative staffers and senior bureaucrats. The PC party is paying the Tories' legal fees and the tab is already approaching seven figures.
The RCMP's 2021 annual report noted the average length of time of a federal police investigation is 1.6 years, meaning the Mounties could wrap up their work next spring. That might impact Ford's timetable for a potential election a year or more ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to recount private conversations, say the RCMP has been concentrated on the premier's inner circle instead of the municipal affairs minister's office, which was initially blamed for the debacle.
The Mounties have not yet scheduled interviews with former municipal affairs minister Steve Clark or his then-chief of staff Ryan Amato, who left their posts in late summer 2023 over the imbroglio.
Both have denied any wrongdoing and are expected to meet with the RCMP later this year.
Thirteen months ago, Ford scrapped the Greenbelt land-swap scheme after separate reports by then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk and integrity commissioner J. David Wake concluded that Tory-connected developers were "favoured" in an unusual process supposedly led by Amato.
Interviewees say Wake's 166-page report appears to be serving as blueprint for the detectives.
The Star is not identifying all of those interviewed by the RCMP because they are not authorized to discuss what they are telling police.
So far, the line of questioning centres on the organizational reporting chart in the premier's office.
But investigators have also expressed interest in a 2020 trip to Las Vegas taken by Amin Massoudi, who was then Ford's principal secretary, with MPP Kaleed Rasheed, Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Jae Truesdell, who would later be hired as premier's housing policy adviser.
In September 2023, Rasheed left cabinet and the PC caucus after making what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner incorrect info about the date of the Vegas visit.
The well-liked Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP has yet to meet with the Mounties, but has maintained he did nothing wrong except inadvertently misleading Wake.
Truesdell, who was also forced from his government post despite being a private citizen at the time of the jaunt, has spoken to police. He's maintained he didn't do anything wrong. Rehmatullah, a guest at the premier's daughter's wedding, owned stakes in two parcels of land totalling about 23 acres that were removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief
