Tory leader backs mandatory drug rehab: Programs would apply to children, some prisoners

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-leader-backs-mandatory-drug-rehab/docview/3115312388/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+leader+backs+mandatory+drug+rehab%3A+Programs+would+apply+to+children%2C+some+prisoners&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Children and prison inmates who are "incapable of making decisions for themselves" should be subject to mandatory drug rehabilitation programs, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Thursday.
"I believe, for children and for prisoners who are behind bars, there should be mandatory drug treatment when they are found to be incapable of making decisions for themselves," Poilievre said Thursday during a campaign-style stop at a Toronto synagogue.
"For adults, again, I'm still doing a lot of research on how that would work."
Poilievre had previously raised the idea of involuntary treatment during a July news conference in London, Ont., although he acknowledged there that "I don't know if you can take someone off the street that has not committed a prison offence and successfully rehabilitate them."
While he expressed support Thursday for involuntary rehabilitation programs targeting some children and prison inmates, Poilievre did not say whether a Conservative government would attempt to pass legislation to set them up.
Health care, including most addiction and drug treatment services, is the domain of provincial and territorial governments, while the federal government provides funding.
Provincial law in Ontario requires medical or psychiatric assessment when a person doesn't consent to treatment, with reviews at regular intervals.
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms also contains legal protections for individuals against involuntary detention or treatment through clauses like Section 7, which guarantees "life, liberty and security of the person," and Section 15, which guarantees equal protection under the law.
Poilievre also used Thursday's news conference to blame Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for policies that he says are fuelling Canada's current drug crisis.
"We need to defund the unsafe government supply of drugs that the Trudeau and NDP regimes are pushing on the population," he said, referring to safe-supply services that Health Canada says "can help prevent overdoses, save lives, and connect people who use drugs to other health and social services."
At her own news conference in Toronto, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said "we need to take a science-based approach ... and we also need to take a public safety-based approach" when contemplating mandatory treatment policies.
"We have to be sure people get the treatment they need, and sometimes a person can be too sick to really recognize what treatment they need," she said, calling for an approach "led by compassion, led by a recognition that these are our sons and daughters, our brothers and sisters."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh echoed Freeland's calls for a compassionate, science-based approach to drug and addiction policy.
He said Thursday that the Conservatives have approached addiction policy with a "lack of awareness of the experts, a lack of recognition of the evidence."
Poilievre's latest comments did little to close a gap between the federal Tories and their provincial counterparts on addiction treatment policy.
"I have concerns that involuntary treatment would not lead to the outcomes we want," Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said following Poilievre's comments in July. The Ontario government recently banned safe injection sites within 200 metres of schools and daycares, which will result in the shuttering 10 of the 17 sites across the province.
In B.C., where voters head to the polls on Oct. 19, NDP Leader David Eby has committed to providing involuntary care for those struggling with overlapping addictions, mental illness and brain injuries. In New Brunswick, Progressive Conservative Leader Blaine Higgs said his party would table a bill on involuntary treatment if it is re-elected on Oct. 21. In Alberta, Premier Danielle Smith promised in 2023 to implement involuntary treatment.
With files from Mark Ramzy and Tonda MacCharles
CREDIT: Andy Takagi Toronto Star With files from Mark Ramzy and Tonda MacCharles

Document 72 of 500

Why isn't the city saving multiplexes from being turned into megahouses?: Toronto lost at least 200 units from 2017 to 2023, report says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-isnt-city-saving-multiplexes-being-turned/docview/3115312365/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+isn%27t+the+city+saving+multiplexes+from+being+turned+into+megahouses%3F%3A+Toronto+lost+at+least+200+units+from+2017+to+2023%2C+report+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A three-storey house on a quintessential west-end Toronto street offers "the pinnacle of modern living."
Spanning more than 3,500 square feet against a backdrop of marble and white oak, the home is characterized by its luxurious features: an imported Italian custom kitchen, noise-cancelling windows and a wireless home sound system.
According to its sales listing history, there used to be a five-plex in its place. But the structure, purchased in 2020 for $1.9 million, was flipped into a single-family home that's now on the market for $4.7 million.
Across Toronto, homeowners are swapping multi-unit houses with tenants for polished and palatial single-family homes.
According to an analysis published by University of Toronto's School of Cities in January, Toronto lost at least 200 units from 2017 to 2023 when multiplexes were converted into single-family homes. Most of the "reno-ductions" took place in the west end, clustering around Roncesvalles, Parkdale and Davenport, with some extending south of the Danforth. This "south-central" part of the city, historically zoned for multiplexes, is being gentrified and losing residents, the authors wrote.
The loss of older multiplexes comes at a cost for the city and for individuals living in those units. While the individuals lose rent-controlled, affordable housing and are left to face unaffordable market prices, the city loses the "gentle density" and the affordable housing that it's working in other ways to create.
In 2023, Toronto legalized duplexes, triplexes and fourplexes citywide, allowing the multiplexes to be built without special permissions across the city. Previously, roughly 70 per cent of Toronto had been zoned exclusively for single-family homes. (Multiplexes with five or more units still require special permissions in most parts of the city.)
The change was meant to increase housing supply, but experts say those new units will be expensive because building or converting a house into a multiplex is a complex, lengthy and pricey process - one that cannot currently create affordable housing.
"(Building or adapting a multiplex) is actually very hard to do and quite expensive," said Karen Chapple, director of the University of Toronto's School of Cities. "So in some ways, the multiplex solution may not be the solution for housing affordability, except in terms of preserving the older ones."
If that's the case, why isn't the city protecting them?

There are generally no barriers for homeowners to turn duplexes, triplexes or fourplexesinto megahouses.
Toronto doesn't require any special permissions to convert as-of-rightmultiplexes - which have a maximum of four units - into single-family homes, even though it does for the demolition of rental buildings with six or more units.
If a building with six or more rental units is slated for demolition or redevelopment, the owner must comply with Toronto's residential rental property demolition and conversion control bylaw, and provide tenants with replacement units at similar rents and assistance packages.
The only case in which replacements are not required is if all units have "above mid-range" rent; for example, if a one-bedroom unit costs more than $2,563.
Gord Perks, chair of Toronto's planning and housing committee, told the Star the provincial government has given the city a "very clear signal" that it shouldn't push harder on rental protections.
Recent amendments to Ontario's Planning Act gave the minister of municipal affairs and housing the authority to regulate the tools municipalities use to control rental replacement, Perks said.
And when the Ontario government signalled the rental replacement bylaw was under review, it sent a message that the city should "be careful" because it could lose its authority on the matter.
Plus, any provisions the city wants to change in its official plan have to be approved by Ontario's minister of municipal affairs and housing, Perks said.
"We're not being encouraged to expand our authority, we're actually being legislated to diminish it," Perks said. "So there's a hard calculation you have to make about whether the planning environment is such that the province is going to grant you more room, or whether we're in a position where they're going to keep taking it away."
Doug Ford's government has already diminished some of the city's powers, Perks said, for instance by changing rules around development charges and heritage designations.
Still, the Parkdale-High Park councillor said Toronto is ahead of any other Ontario municipality in protecting renters. It has taken steps in recent years to expand rental replacement rules, legalized multi-tenant housing (also known as rooming houses) citywide and is developing a "renoviction" bylaw to prevent bad-faith evictions related to renovations, he added.
The office of the Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing Paul Calandra did not respond to the Star's request for comment.

Although new multiplexes provide lowrise housing options, environmental benefits and gentle density in areas with shrinking populations, they don't provide the same level of affordability that old multiplexes do, Chapple explained.
"The issue is the loss of affordability, more than even the loss of density," Chapple said about the conversion of multiplexes into single-family homes.
According to a 2022 report by the Urban Land Institute (ULI) for the City of Toronto, the costs of building a multiplex are so high that a typical homeowner would need to charge market rent in order for the conversion to be financially feasible. Conversions where the units compete directly with condos would more likely be feasible, and conversions in areas with higher rents would be more attractive to homeowners, the report found.
The study provided examples of budgets for multiplex conversions, laying out scenarios in which a $2-million house cost $2.6 million to convert and a $1.5-million house costs another $1.5 million to convert.
Richard Joy, the executive director of ULI, Toronto, agreed new multiplexes would be good for density, but not necessarily for affordability.
"Not only is it the case that the market would probably drive the units up to a level that is not affordable, but even if you wanted to make them affordable, very generously, the cost of doing a multiplex is, unfortunately, a lot more expensive than I think a lot of people imagine," he said.
Given that new multiplexes are so expensive to build, Chapple said the city should try to preserve the existing stock.
She said the only scenario in which homeowners face an "onerous process" to convert a multiplex into a single-family-home is if the property is on the Heritage Register.
"What other cities have been doing, which I think works pretty well, is in certain zones to limit the total square footage that a single-family home can have," Chapple said.
Limiting the size of a single-family home to 3,000 square feet, for example, could prevent conversions of duplexes into "McMansions" while also preventing teardowns of smaller, more affordable homes, she explained.
Toronto could also apply rental replacement rules to multiplexes, Chapple said.
"From 2018 to 2022, the city approved nearly 2,500 replacement units at below-market rents, so it's a fairly successful program," she added.
While experts agree that new multiplexes can't provide affordable housing yet, they said governments can loosen requirements around development to make them more affordable to build and can provide incentives for developers to rent them out at affordable rates.
In Joy's view, there are many "archaic" building code and fire code barriers that the city could change to reduce costs. Simplifying requirements could reduce the costs of building a multiplex unit from $500,000, for example, to maybe $150,000, he said.
"There's huge potential in our single-family neighbourhoods across the GTA and Toronto," Joy said. "Literally hundreds and hundreds of condominiums worth of units that could be built without building condominiums."
Chapple pointed out that British Columbia recently allowed buildings of up to six storeys to have only one exit stairwell rather than two, something she said Toronto could do.
Michael Piper, an associate professor of urban design and architecture at the University of Toronto, said governments can make the process for these developments faster and cheaper by simplifying the approvals process.
He added that a recent, positive change is the removal of development charges for multiplexes.
Governments must also provide financial incentives to build multiplexes tied to affordability requirements, he said. For instance, they could provide a grant to builders with the requirement that recipients rent out the units at below-market rent.
It's particularly important to incentivize "citizen developers," he added, because they would be more likely than large developers to provide affordable prices.
"Owner-occupants have a different kind of economic pressure," Piper said. "They're just trying to make buying and owning a house more affordable."
CREDIT: Manuela Vega Housing Reporter

Document 73 of 500

Toronto's AI legend brought home the big award for smarts. Will we heed his call to use wisdom?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-ai-legend-brought-home-big-award-smarts/docview/3115312334/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+AI+legend+brought+home+the+big+award+for+smarts.+Will+we+heed+his+call+to+use+wisdom%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's something delightfully amusing about Geoffrey Hinton's handling of the media maelstrom after he was informed that he had been named co-recipient of the 2024 Nobel Prize in physics.
That he hesitated about answering the phone, because who calls at 1 a.m.?
That the world-famous University of Toronto computer scientist was in California staying, as he explained to The New York Times, in "a cheap hotel room with no internet."
That he told the BBC that he dropped out of physics after first year university.
That in considering a question posed by the Star's Victoria Gibson, who wondered whether the neural networks of artificial intelligence could be deployed to improve the city of Toronto, offered that he was "not quite sure how a neural network could get rid of Doug Ford."
Hinton, as the Star has reported, is a fierce critic of the premier's plans to build a downsized Ontario Science Centre on the waterfront.
And he's a passionate proponent of curiosity, from the spark that can ignite in the mind of a school child on a Science Centre outing, to the curiosity-driven research that has powered him across decades to global status as one of the godfathers of artificial intelligence.
What resonates is the way in which our newly minted Nobel laureate champions learning, including that of his own students who, as he has said, took his foundational research and "actually made things work."
Of course, most of us weren't paying attention in the early 1980s when Hinton and his fellow scientists experienced their first successes with computer algorithms. Nor would the vast majority have had a clue about his forbearance working in the field of brain-mimicking artificial neural networks, denounced as a waste of time by many in the scientific community.
But we're all awake now, eager, perhaps even desperate, to fully understand the positive potential of artificial intelligence - and its dangers.
Hinton has said in interviews that he always had his worries about AI, from job displacement to the proliferation of fake news. But the good works - he often emphasizes the benefits to health care in, say, the analysis of medical images, and huge improvements in productivity - are proving to be revolutionary.
A tipping point arrived in the spring of last year when Hinton announced that after a decade working with Google on its AI technology, he had quit, citing the existential risk of artificial intelligence outpacing the intelligence of mankind.
As he said to reporters this week, "We have no experience of what it's like to have things smarter than us."
How close are we to being outplayed by AI? Take a look at the advanced reasoning capabilities of OpenAI's GPT-4. Or, just for fun, check out its single sentence explanation of the plot of Cinderella - posted on OpenAI's website -- where the artificial brain was commanded to start each word with the next letter of the alphabet.
It may be a fairytale. But it's also an example of the dizzying creative talents of AI.
Perhaps we should say "chillingly." Hinton has raised the spectre of a threat that could get out of control. He has made the urgent call for more research into AI safety. Systems more intelligent than us could "go rogue," he has said. "That's not science fiction."
He doesn't have all the answers. He's not that smart. But perhaps in his wry, teacherly way he can again help lay a foundation stone for not just the mechanics of artificial intelligence, but the responsible way forward.
CAPTION: Geoffrey Hinton poses at Google's Mountain View, California headquarters in 2015.

Full text: Nuit Blanche was evening of unity in divided world
Nuit Blanche asks us to confront a timely paradox. Can we solve it? Oct. 6
What touched me Saturday night at Nuit Blanche wasn't so much the installations, but the buzz of excitement in the atmosphere. It was a magical evening of beautiful fall weather with thousands of Torontonians relaxing by the waterfront. It was an opportunity to stroll hand-in-hand with my husband, taking in the sights - adults in silly costumes, and a family pulling children in a wagon adorned with lights. We need more joy and a break amid our political challenges. Nuit Blanche provided it.
Monica Kavanagh, Thornhill
Chow's lost email excuse belongs
in the junk folder
Mayor Chow to apologize for not attending Oct. 7 vigil - blames missing email, Oct. 9
It is certainly interesting that Mayor Olivia Chow has now, for one reason or another, missed three events dedicated to supporting Toronto's Jewish community in the one-year remembrance of the horrific events of Oct. 7. The excuse this time was that there was a "lost email." Numerous politicians and dignitaries (including some of her own council members) seemed to have received their emails and attended the vigil, but her email was apparently "lost!" There have now been one too many excuses for her disinterest and apathy. Shameful!
Jeffrey Manly, Toronto
A slice of the healthier female perspective
In Wednesday's Opinion pages a Star columnist argued that we stop shaming women who don't have children. A female rabbi argued that all peoples in the Middle East have shared humanity, while a female Palestinian author described her efforts to humanize her people in the eyes of her readers. Finally, there was a piece about Kamala Harris's battle to stop the Donald Trump/JD Vance stance on making abortions as difficult to obtain as possible.
These articles are all written from a healthier female perspective while wars are perpetrated by male autocrats who need conflict to boost their sagging popularity. Indeed, the mullahs of Iran need to deflect how unpopular they are with women, university students and secular Iranians by continuing to stoke the flames of war. Follow the money trail and you will see that trillions of dollars are being spent on weapons that have no concern for humanity and that despoil both neighbourhoods and the surrounding environment.
Saying that war is a "guy thing" is cold comfort for the millions of people paying the mortal price of these ghastly ventures. But it ought to make us vote against men like Trump/Vance, Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford who embrace narrowly conceived male priorities and to protest against the weapons makers and companies that trade their stock.
Ron Charach, Toronto
MAID program has given me peace of mind
Canada shouldn't be the world's example of how not to implement MAID, Oct. 5
As a Canadian of mature years, I have to say that our MAID program has given me incredible peace of mind to know that if I developed a painful and life ending disease like ALS or some types of cancer, I could make the call about when I've had enough and want to end my life peacefully with dignity and respect.
It's my opinion that an adult of sound mind should be allowed to decide when their suffering has become unbearable; no doubt that bar is different for everyone and that's OK. Who is anyone else to judge where that line is for another person?
When the legislation was enacted, I felt an immediate sense of relief to know that it would be my decision to make as an adult when or if I felt that my suffering was unbearable. I'm proud to live in a country that allows me to make that final decision for myself. I've been very open with my children about this topic and would expect them to respect and understand my decision should the time come.
I was shocked to find that the U.K. didn't have such a program and I hope they do engage in a robust discussion about what their people want.
Anne Sayers, Scarborough
I was fortunate to be able to attend the recent World Federation of Right To Die Societies conference in Dublin, Ireland. The continuously recycled negative media about Canada's medical assistance in dying (MAID) law was certainly a topic of discussion, but what quickly became evident is that our more liberal and equitable MAID program was what most countries aspire to. The U.K. will vote soon to legalize assisted dying for terminally ill people who aren't expected to live more than six months, similar to the criteria in other countries.
What we learned in Canada after the adoption of Bill C-14 - initiating Canada's MAID law - is that suffering exists outside of the strict rule that a person must be nearing end of life. In Canada, our criteria and safeguards are rigorous, but our assisted dying legislation includes a broader scope of choice and suffering. For some, the short, six-month time frame and criteria that the medication must be self-administered result in many never accessing the assisted death they wanted. I believe that the intention of all assisted dying laws around the globe should be about compassion, the fundamental rights of an individual and avoiding suffering.
Helen Long, CEO, Dying With Dignity Canada, Toronto
Let's not forget the good we are already doing
Even though Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre would have us believe that our country is broken, let us stop and reflect at this time of Thanksgiving on all that is good in Canada: a health-care system that does not discriminate; an education system that ranks continuously in the top 10; freedom of speech that allows protests and rallies without fear of death or imprisonment; a wilderness that is second to none; our efforts to reconcile the wrongs experienced by the Indigenous people of this land and the incorporation of their art and beliefs into our daily lives; that we are not a melting pot but a truly multinational, all-embracing people open to welcome and respect our many differences; one of the top countries to raise children; our attempts to deal with the environmental crisis and that most of us are ready and able to apologize and be civil among many other blessings. True, there is much to do, but let us not forget the good we are already doing and have done. Happy Thanksgiving.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora

Document 75 of 500

Pierre Poilievre supports mandatory drug rehab for some children, prisoners

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-supports-mandatory-drug-rehab/docview/3115577205/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+supports+mandatory+drug+rehab+for+some+children%2C+prisoners&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Children and prison inmates who are "incapable of making decisions for themselves" should be subject to mandatory drug rehabilitation programs, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre said Thursday.
"I believe, for children and for prisoners who are behind bars, there should be mandatory drug treatment when they are found to be incapable of making decisions for themselves," Poilievre said Thursday during a campaign-style stop at a Toronto synagogue.
"For adults, again, I'm still doing a lot of research on how that would work."
Poilievre had previously raised the idea of involuntary treatment during a July news conference in London, Ont., although he acknowledged there that "I don't know if you can take someone off the street that has not committed a prison offence and successfully rehabilitate them."
While he expressed support Thursday for involuntary rehabilitation programs targeting some children and prison inmates, Poilievre did not say whether a Conservative government would attempt to pass legislation to set them up.
Health care, including most addiction and drug treatment services, is the domain of provincial and territorial governments, while the federal government provides funding.
Provincial law in Ontario requires medical or psychiatric assessment when a person doesn't consent to treatment, with reviews at regular intervals.
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms also contains legal protections for individuals against involuntary detention or treatment through clauses like Section 7, which guarantees "life, liberty and security of the person," and Section 15, which guarantees equal protection under the law.
Poilievre also used Thursday's news conference to blame Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for policies that he says are fuelling Canada's current drug crisis.
"We need to defund the unsafe government supply of drugs that the Trudeau and NDP regimes are pushing on the population," he said, referring to safe-supply services that Health Canada says "can help prevent overdoses, save lives, and connect people who use drugs to other health and social services."
At her own news conference in Toronto, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said "we need to take a science-based approach ... and we also need to take a public safety-based approach" when contemplating mandatory treatment policies.
"We have to be sure people get the treatment they need, and sometimes a person can be too sick to really recognize what treatment they need," she said, calling for an approach "led by compassion, led by a recognition that these are our sons and daughters, our brothers and sisters."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh echoed Freeland's calls for a compassionate, science-based approach to drug and addiction policy.
He said Thursday that the Conservatives have approached addiction policy with a "lack of awareness of the experts, a lack of recognition of the evidence."
Poilievre's latest comments did little to close a gap between the federal Tories and their provincial counterparts on addiction treatment policy.
"I have concerns that involuntary treatment would not lead to the outcomes we want," Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones said following Poilievre's comments in July.
The Ontario government recently banned safe injection sites within 200 metres of schools and daycares, which will result in the shuttering 10 of the 17 sites across the province.
In B.C., where voters head to the polls on Oct. 19, NDP Leader David Eby has committed to providing involuntary care for those struggling with overlapping addictions, mental illness and brain injuries. In New Brunswick, Progressive Conservative Leader Blaine Higgs said his party would table a bill on involuntary treatment if it is re-elected on Oct. 21. In Alberta, Premier Danielle Smith promised in 2023 to implement involuntary treatment.
Ontario's Big City Mayors caucus, a coalition of leaders from the province's largest municipalities cities, will vote Oct. 18 on whether to endorse a resolution that calls for involuntary treatment legislation.
With files from Mark Ramzy and Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a news conference at a synagogue in Toronto on Oct. 10, 2024.

Mayor Olivia Chow considers changes to Etobicoke bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mayor-olivia-chow-considers-changes-etobicoke/docview/3115312407/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+Olivia+Chow+considers+changes+to+Etobicoke+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Olivia Chow is considering changes to bike lanes along Bloor Street West in Etobicoke that have drawn widespread criticism from residents, including Premier Doug Ford.
"I'm trying to come up with a compromise that would reopen some car lanes but keep the bike lanes and make the design work better," the mayor told Newstalk 1010 this week.
She said she's hoping her efforts will convince Ford - who last month announced plans for legislation limiting construction of new bike lanes in an effort to ease gridlock - that issues related to bike lanes can be resolved locally.
"I've asked the premier to give me some time to work on it, and I'm working flat out on it," she said.
A petition launched by Etobicoke resident Cody MacRae, advocating for a re-evaluation of the bike lanes installed last autumn between Runnymede Road and Aberfoyle Crescent, has garnered more than 13,000 signatures.
MacRae, founder of Balance on Bloor, said Thursday that residents "look forward to working with the mayor regarding a solution that serves the needs of residents."
Balance on Bloor has taken issue with the city's data and consultation process when it comes to bike lanes.
"Despite repeated requests, the city has yet to provide clarity on what defines a 'successful' bike lane implementation," MacRae said. "Local residents urge transparency and metrics to assess how the project impacts the community's safety, accessibility and local economy.
"The community looks forward to Mayor Chow's support in addressing these ongoing issues, urging a transparent and meaningful approach to transportation planning in the neighbourhood."
MacRae said the mayor met him and other members of Balance on Bloor, local business representatives, local Coun. Amber Morley and transportation staff - including Barbara Gray, general manager of transportation services - on Monday.
Chow did not respond to a request for comment from the Star late Thursday afternoon.
Michael Longfield, executive director of Cycle Toronto, said the mayor hasn't said she's going to remove bike lanes.
"It sounds like the mayor is open to considering options," said Longfield. "At this point it's looking at configuration changes."
Longfield said Ford's promise to regulate bike lanes is "overreach by the province."
The proposed legislation would override municipal powers by blocking construction of bike lanes if they lead to a loss of lanes to vehicular traffic.
The measure is one of several, including 24-7 construction for new highway projects like the 413 from Milton to Highway 400, in the proposed "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," sources have told the Star.
With files from Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau
CAPTION: A cyclist using a bike lane on Bloor West this summer. The mayor is seeking "a compromise" on the contentious lanes.

Title: Mayor Olivia Chow considers changes to Etobicoke bike lanes

Section: news/gta

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Assessing Hurricane Milton's damages + What's happening with the RCMP's Greenbelt investigation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/assessing-hurricane-miltons-damages-whats/docview/3115067153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Assessing+Hurricane+Milton%27s+damages+%2B+What%27s+happening+with+the+RCMP%27s+Greenbelt+investigation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, October 10 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the aftermath of Hurricane Milton, the RCMP's Greenbelt investigation and the unpublicized plan to move Ontario Place's sewer.
DON'T MISSHurricane Milton cut a trail of destruction across Florida
The worst appears to be over. Storm-battered Floridians are assessing the damage after Milton made landfall as a Category 3 storm Wednesday night, heralded by a barrage of tornados and leaving three million homes and businesses without power. Tampa was spared from a direct hit, but 41 centimetres of rain and a deadly storm surge flooded the area. "We have lost some life," officials say. Here's the latest.
A waning disaster: By early Thursday, Milton had weakened to a Category 1 storm, although high winds and heavy rains continue. At its peak, winds of over 205 kilometres-per-hour were recorded.The future of storms: Meteorologists say that while the frequency of hurricanes aren't predicted to change in the future, storms are expected to become more severe.Here's what's been happening with the RCMP's Greenbelt investigation
"Where did the direction come from to remove those lands from the Greenbelt?" That's one question RCMP investigators are asking everyone they're interviewing about the $8.28-billion land swap scandal, the Star has learned. With the criminal probe marking its one-year anniversary today, Robert Benzie took a deep dive into the investigation and the secrets it's dredging up. Take an inside look at how it's going.
The interviewees: Insiders say at least eight of Doug Ford's current and former top aides have been interviewed so far. Ford himself has not, but the premier said his door is open to investigators.Unspooling the details: The detectives' questions indicate they've already obtained numerous internal email and text messages. Investigators have suggested they may need to access banking records in Europe - meaning the investigation could last many months or even years.The Ford government's plan to move Ontario Place's sewer has locals fuming
The Ford government wants to redirect sewer pipes spewing raw waste into Lake Ontario toward a channel used by swimmers and rowers. Why? To prevent pollution at a beach Austrian spa developer Therme is building at the new Ontario Place, David Rider reports. Local groups using the West Channel are panning the unpublicized plan as the "absolute worst place for an overflow sewage outlet." Here's the lowdown on the situation.
Word from officials: City staff say Infrastructure Ontario wants them to move, at provincial expense, the "combined sewer outflow" because its discharged waste could pollute Ontario Place's West Island after its shoreline is expanded with 10 acres of fill.Wait what? Residents suggested all these issues could be avoided by extending the pipe further out into the lake - but this option has been rejected by the province.WHAT ELSEPierre Poilievre was slammed by opponents after suggesting Israel should strike Iranian nuclear facilities.The Ford government revealed the details of its audit of Ontario's children's aid societies.Here's why city council voted to extend Toronto's Billy Bishop airport lease by as long as 12 years.Canadians watch too much American news and only get half the picture, the U.S. ambassador says.A mom is suing the police for a "negligent investigation and false imprisonment" after her disabled daughter's death.Olivia Chow has weighed in on Michael Thompson's city hall roles as details of his sex assault charges emerge.GTA food banks are facing a "tidal wave of demand" and struggling to meet Thanksgiving donation goals.Royal LePage has lowered its Toronto-area home price forecast amid a "soft" fall market.Canada is tightening its rules for multinational corporations transferring their employees here.A controversial contract with a U.S. tech company has cost Toronto taxpayers, the auditor general reports."The Drake featuring Drake": Does the Six God's appearance on the cover of a fly-fishing magazine signal a career pivot?He's the ultimate Canadian character actor. Now Victor Garber is getting his flowers at an Ontario film festival.POVPierre Poilievre's antics are making Parliament miserable for everyone. Give us a break.CLOSE-UP
ST. PETERSBURG: Fierce winds from Hurricane Milton tore the roof of Tropicana Field to shreds last night. The $138 million stadium, home of the Tampa Bay Rays, was reportedly being used as "a staging site for workers" who were brought to the area to deal with the storm's aftermath. Take a closer look at the damage.

Document 78 of 500

'Doug Ford needs to come clean' about Greenbelt scandal, opposition says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-needs-come-clean-about-greenbelt/docview/3115061911/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Doug+Ford+needs+to+come+clean%27+about+Greenbelt+scandal%2C+opposition+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the RCMP's investigation into the Greenbelt scandal enters its second year, Premier Doug Ford's political rivals say he "needs to come clean about his role" in the $8.28-billion land swap scheme.
In the wake of revelations in the Star that at least eight of Ford's current and former aides have already been interviewed by the Mounties, NDP Leader Marit Stiles said "there is still so much we don't know about this mess."
The premier, who has not yet met with police, has insisted "we have nothing to hide" about his aborted plan to open up 7,400 acres of protected Greenbelt land to housing development, and apologized for the fiasco last year.
Stiles isn't buying that.
"Reports that the RCMP is investigating potential bank accounts in Europe show that the extent of this corruption may be bigger than anyone thought," she said, citing an exclusive story in the Star to mark Thursday's first anniversary of the launch of the criminal probe.
"An apology is not enough, Doug Ford needs to come clean about his role in this scheme to rob Ontarians of the Greenbelt to enrich a wealthy few," the New Democrat said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said "the RCMP is focused on the premier's inner sanctum because he has been at the heart of this scandal since day one.
"Doug Ford isn't in it for you. He's given away billions of your money to his rich buddies while people struggle to find a family doctor, classroom sizes balloon and the cost of living skyrockets," said Crombie.
"I have the same question for Doug Ford that the RCMP has been asking ... Where did the direction come from to remove those lands from the Greenbelt?"
The Star reported Wednesday that members of the RCMP elite "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the branch that looks into political crimes and corruption, have been peppering interviewees with that query for months.
Armed with internal email and text messages they have gathered, the detectives have hinted that they may need to access banking records in Europe as part of their probe.
They have been interviewing current and former Progressive Conservative staffers and senior bureaucrats. The PC party is paying the Tories' legal fees and the tab is already approaching seven figures.
The RCMP's 2021 annual report noted the average length of time of a federal police investigation is 1.6 years, meaning the Mounties could wrap up their work next spring. That might impact Ford's timetable for a potential election a year or more ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to recount private conversations, say the RCMP has been concentrated on the premier's inner circle instead of the municipal affairs minister's office, which was initially blamed for the debacle.
The Mounties have not yet scheduled interviews with former municipal affairs minister Steve Clark or his then-chief of staff Ryan Amato, who left their posts in late summer 2023 over the imbroglio.
Both have denied any wrongdoing and are expected to meet with the RCMP later this year.
Thirteen months ago, Ford scrapped the Greenbelt land-swap scheme after separate reports by then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk and integrity commissioner J. David Wake concluded that Tory-connected developers were "favoured" in an unusual process supposedly led by Amato.
Interviewees say Wake's 166-page report appears to be serving as blueprint for the detectives.
The Star is not identifying all of those interviewed by the RCMP because they are not authorized to discuss what they are telling police.
So far, the line of questioning centres on the organizational reporting chart in the premier's office.
But investigators have also expressed interest in a 2020 trip to Las Vegas taken by Amin Massoudi, who was then Ford's principal secretary, with MPP Kaleed Rasheed, Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Jae Truesdell, who would later be hired as premier's housing policy adviser.
In September 2023, Rasheed left cabinet and the PC caucus after making what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner incorrect information about the date of the Vegas visit.
The well-liked Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP has yet to meet with the Mounties, but has maintained he did nothing wrong except inadvertently misleading Wake.
Truesdell, who was also forced from his government post despite being a private citizen at the time of the jaunt, has spoken to police. He's maintained he didn't do anything wrong.
Rehmatullah, a guest at the premier's daughter's wedding, owned stakes in two parcels of land totalling about 23 acres that were removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
It is not known whether Massoudi, now a private-sector lobbyist, has met with the RCMP yet.
He is a close friend and long-time adviser to the Ford family.
Last fall, the premier's office said it cut "formal" ties with him due to the Greenbelt affair.
In November 2022, a joint Star and Narwhal investigation found eight of the 15 parcels of Greenbelt land initially rezoned for development had been purchased in the four years after the Tories were elected in 2018.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Marit Stiles speaks in Welland, Ont., on Oct. 7, 2024.

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals; Interviews

Location: Europe

Document 79 of 500

Opponents of MAID should mind their own business and allow others to make their own decisions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/opponents-maid-should-mind-their-own-business/docview/3115061862/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Opponents+of+MAID+should+mind+their+own+business+and+allow+others+to+make+their+own+decisions&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada shouldn't be the world's example of how not to implement MAID, Oct. 8
Whether he intends to or not, Andrew Phillips is perpetuating the misrepresentation - now making headlines in the UK - that Canada's MAID (Medical Assistance in Dying) program is a compulsory state execution rather than a form of assisted dying for people seeking help from the state to alleviate their endless suffering.
The backlash against MAID is not proof of its failure to meet people's needs. Neither is expanding the list of eligible users a mistake that forces misuse. The increase in use reflects people exercising their need and rightful choice. Instead, the backlash has devolved into a "slippery slope" narrative, portraying MAID as a compulsory death sentence. The democratic question, then, is: why do people who don't want a legal medical service in dying get to override the will of those who do? Why should their views dictate how we die? They should mind their own business and allow others to make their own decisions. That is how democracy works. MAID exists so that patients can request it of their own free will, yet it is being distorted as something doctors prescribe without consent. This mischaracterization is driven by those who opposed the original premise: that a just society would assist those choosing to end their suffering.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Groups challenging MAID should be taken seriously
As the British Parliament again considers assisted suicide, Andrew Phillips' wise advice to learn the right lessons from Canada's experience is acutely perceptive. As he said, "there really is a slippery slope in this area, and it is still too easy to slide down without really noticing." The Supreme Court challenge by groups representing people with disabilities should be taken seriously. Canada has minimal provision of palliative care, and only poverty level support for most people living with disabilities. But the unquenchable insistence grows that any and every person with any physical or mental health issue - and honestly, that could be all of us - must be offered assisted suicide (MAID). I truly hope the people and politicians in the UK are not duped too.
Martha Crean, Toronto
Ford could help move the dial on homelessness by funding creative solutions
Doug Ford signals Ontario will take action on homeless tent encampments, Oct. 2
In regard to the increasing number of encampments in Toronto and in many other Ontario municipalities, Premier Doug Ford should pay attention to the recent comments of the co-founders of tech giant Shopify. Harvey Finkelstein, Spotify president, noted at last week's Elevate Tech conference, that there is a lack of ambition permeating the Canadian psyche and Shopify co-founder, Toby Lütke, noted a troubling deficiency in the larger project of Canada itself - namely the constraints holding back innovative ideas when people don't have the support to implement new ideas. "A lot of our success has been due to basically allowing everyone to go for world class. Everyone's like, 'Oh well, if we are allowed to do this, then let's go,'" Lütke said of granting permission to his engineers to be great.
Many of us who have been working for years to address the serious issues causing homelessness can identify with these remarks. We see governments spending hundreds of millions on expensive solutions while there are new technologies available and acres of office and retail buildings sitting empty in our city and yet we tolerate having people sleep outside - leading to the high costs of police, courts, hospitals, mental health, etc. Ford could help move the dial by funding creative solutions, such as culturally appropriate supportive housing and shelters for refugees that could free up to 50 per cent of shelter spaces in Toronto. There are many organizations - including Black-led and Indigenous - that can provide the leadership to help make this happen. Action on these opportunities will create many kinds of employment.
David Walsh, Toronto
Municipalities must insist builders meet the needs of the population
My grandparents owned a small two-bedroom bungalow in North Leaside when I was young. My understanding was these homes were built and priced to attract workers to the new factories, southeast of them, like Canada Wire. In other words, they were priced based on the wages being offered at the factories. Fast forward to today where city approved condominiums contain small units which are more attractive to investors than families wishing to purchase a new home. There has not been a requirement by the municipality for the type of units needed by the general population. Now consider those bungalows like my grandparents owned. The relative, who ended up living in it, sold it and it was torn down to build a large two-storey house selling for over $1 million more than it was purchased for. I believe that builders need to seek approval for the plans to meet the need of the population and the price point as part of the municipal approval in order to curtail the spiral of ever-increasing housing prices that seem to be occurring. We need to seek convergence of public housing prices versus private home construction prices.
Paul Naish, Markham
Cities require the revenue municipal development charges bring inIf Toronto wants an example of how to respond to a housing crisis, it should look at Calgary, Oct. 7
The housing crisis is a threat to our communities. It's time for the government to take bold action, Oct. 7
In their columns, David Olive and David Wilkes both lament municipal development charges. However, both fail to mention the inevitable alternative: either monster tax increases (or equivalent taxpayer support from feds or Province) or - more likely - simply not getting the essential city infrastructure that development charges pay for. Does anyone really think city councils will rocket up taxes to get the indispensable billions from those missing development charges? Not likely. Does anyone really think the province will reliably pony up those missing billions to the cities? Not likely. Already, cities are typically far too late providing the public transit, flood protection, or recreation facilities for a growing population. The result: more traffic congestion and a lower quality of life. Do we want worse? Olive hints at the folly of such cuts, questioning if housing will thus become more affordable and if developers will thus forgo any profits. Working for BILD, the developers' lobby, Wilkes is just doing his job. But from Olive, we expect the whole story.
Bill Winegard, Etobicoke
CAPTION: The Senate has passed a Liberal government bill delaying the expansion of MAID until 2027.

Subject: Mental health; Homeless people; Assisted suicide; Cities; Housing prices

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing prices

Document 80 of 500

The Ontario government has revealed its plans for an audit ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-government-has-revealed-plans-audit/docview/3114754975/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ontario+government+has+revealed+its+plans+for+an+audit+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has revealed its plans for an audit of children's aid societies that officials say will give them a comprehensive look at the state of the province's child welfare system, including the quality of child protection services and a financial review to pinpoint factors driving rising costs and deficits.
The audit, which ministry officials on Wednesday said will be conducted by an outside contractor with a final report expected next spring, will examine agencies' operational costs, including executive compensation and staffing.
The audit comes in the wake of Star reporting that revealed child welfare agencies are increasingly becoming a last-resort option for families of children with complex health needs unable to get care in their communities, and a separate investigation into the death of a four-year-old girl whose body was found in a Rosedale dumpster after children's aid closed her case.
The sector-wide review of 37 children's aid societies will also analyze funding spent on services for kids with complex special needs coming into the care of welfare agencies. The review does not include Indigenous agencies.
It also follows an announcement by Ontario ombudsman Paul Dubé that his office will investigate the practice of children's aid societies placing young people in unlicensed settings.
Child welfare leaders told the Star they are forced to house kids and teens with complex mental health and developmental needs in unlicensed settings, such as hotels, rental apartments, office buildings and Airbnbs due to a lack of treatment options, and say these emergency placements are driving up costs for their agencies and harming vulnerable children and youth.
In an interview, Michael Parsa, the minister of children, community and social services, said "some youth face challenges and that's why we want to make sure they have the supports to be able to continue to grow and succeed in their communities."
Parsa outlined ministry funding to children's aid societies, which he described as "record investments," including an additional $36.5 million in base funding this year to the province's 50 child welfare agencies. Parsa said investments in child welfare have increased over 10 years while the number of children and youth in care has gone down about 30 per cent.
When asked why child welfare agencies still rely on unlicensed placements for children with complex needs, despite "record investments," Parsa said answering that question "is part of the process" of the sector-wide review.
"We're looking to make sure that the investments we're making ... are being used appropriately," Parsa said.
Kiaras Gharabaghi, dean of the faculty of community services and a professor in child and youth care at Toronto Metropolitan University, called an audit of the child welfare system that focuses on finances "a distraction" that does not respond to the challenges faced by families in crisis.
"Children's aid societies may not always have been on the right side of this problem, but auditing their finances is not a response to the problem, accomplishes nothing for families who need support right now, and ultimately reinforces the idea that if only people spent their money right, we wouldn't have a problem," Gharabaghi said.
Details of the audit were outlined in a technical briefing for journalists led by two ministry officials. To attend the virtual briefing, reporters had to agree not to name the officials or quote them directly.
Ministry officials highlighted data showing a 49 per cent drop in open protection cases and a 30 per cent decrease in children in care in the last decade. They also said that despite a nine per cent increase in funding allocations, the ministry provided $55 million in additional funding over the last five years to manage deficits.
Focus areas of the audit include: regulatory compliance of child welfare services; how agencies use out-of-home placements; staffing and operational costs; and whether services are consistent across all CAS regions. The review will also look into how complex special needs services are contributing to agencies' costs.
Officials said the process to determine the third party that will conduct the review started Wednesday. A ministry spokesperson confirmed the review's findings and recommendations will be made public.
Questions about the audit remain unanswered, including whether the review will gather input from families and children in the care of welfare agencies. It's also unclear whether Premier Doug Ford directed the ministry to launch the review.
Last week, Ford pledged a "complete audit" of the child welfare sector, alleging misuse of taxpayer funds.
CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn, whose union represents roughly 5,000 child protection workers, said in a statement to the Star that the province should implement recommendations for child welfare reform already put forward by experts and advocates, while pointing to a system overhaul "four years in the making" and which has "produced absolutely no meaningful change."
"Another audit, investigation or review is not what families in Ontario need," Hahn said.
The province announced a complete overhaul of its child welfare system four years ago, at the time stating the redesign would focus on prevention and early intervention. Parsa said the new sector-wide audit is "part of our ongoing efforts" to improve the care of children and youth and said the review is another measure "to see how else we can improve services and support."
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email
adempsey@thestar.ca
and mogilvie@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Megan Ogilvie and Amy Dempsey Raven Toronto Star The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.

Document 81 of 500

MAID opponents should mind their own business Canada shouldn t ...[Derived Headline]: Canada shouldn't be the world's example of how not to implement MAID, Phillips, Oct. 8 Doug Ford signals Ontario will take action on homeless tent encampments, Oct. 2 If Toronto wants an example of how to respond to a housing crisis, it should look at Calgary, Olive, Oct. 7 The housing crisis is a threat to our communities. https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/maid-opponents-should-mind-their-own-business/docview/3114752960/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MAID+opponents+should+mind+their+own+business+Canada+shouldn+t+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Canada+shouldn%27t+be+the+world%27s+example+of+how+not+to+implement+MAID%2C+Phillips%2C+Oct.+8+Doug+Ford+signals+Ontario+will+take+action+on+homeless+tent+encampments%2C+Oct.+2+If+Toronto+wants+an+example+of+how+to+respond+to+a+housing+crisis%2C+it+should+look+at+Calgary%2C+Olive%2C+Oct.+7+The+housing+crisis+is+a+threat+to+our+communities.+It%27s+time+for+the+government+to+take+bold+action%2C+Wilkes%2C+Oct.+7&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MAID opponents should mind their own business
Canada shouldn't be the world's example of how not to implement MAID, Phillips, Oct. 8
Whether he intends to or not, Andrew Phillips is perpetuating the misrepresentation that Canada's MAID (Medical Assistance in Dying) program is a compulsory state execution, rather than a form of assisted dying for people seeking help from the state to alleviate their endless suffering.
The backlash against MAID is not proof of its failure to meet people's needs. Neither is expanding the list of eligible users a mistake that forces misuse.
The increase in use reflects people exercising their need and rightful choice. But the backlash has devolved into a "slippery slope" narrative, portraying MAID as a compulsory death sentence.
The democratic question, then, is: Why do people who don't want a legal medical service in dying get to override the will of those who do? Why should their views dictate how we die? They should mind their own business and allow others to make their own decisions. That is how democracy works.
MAID exists so that patients can request it of their own free will, yet it is being distorted as something doctors prescribe without consent. This mischaracterization is driven by those who opposed the original premise: that a just society would assist those choosing to end their suffering.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Groups fighting MAID should be taken seriously
As the British Parliament again considers assisted suicide, Andrew Phillips' wise advice to learn the right lessons from Canada's experience is acutely perceptive. As he said, "there really is a slippery slope in this area, and it is still too easy to slide down without really noticing."
The Supreme Court challenge by groups representing people with disabilities should be taken seriously. Canada has minimal provision of palliative care, and only poverty-level support for most people living with disabilities.
But the unquenchable insistence grows that any and every person with any physical or mental health issue - and honestly, that could be all of us - must be offered assisted suicide (MAID). I truly hope the people and politicians in the U.K. are not duped, too.
Martha Crean, Toronto
Ford could help move the dial
on homelessness
Doug Ford signals Ontario will take action on homeless tent encampments, Oct. 2
In regard to the increasing number of encampments in Toronto and many other Ontario municipalities, Premier Doug Ford should pay attention to recent comments by the co-founders of tech giant Shopify.
Company president Harvey Finkelstein said there is a lack of ambition permeating the Canadian psyche, and co-founder Toby Lütke, noted a troubling deficiency in the larger project of Canada itself - namely the constraints holding back innovative ideas when people don't have the support to implement new ideas.
"A lot of our success has been due to basically allowing everyone to go for world class. Everyone's like, 'Oh well, if we are allowed to do this, then let's go,'" Lütke said of granting permission to his engineers to be great.
Many of us who have been working for years to address the serious issues causing homelessness can identify with these remarks. We see governments spending hundreds of millions on expensive solutions, while there are new technologies available and acres of office and retail buildings sitting empty in our city. And yet we tolerate having people sleep outside - leading to the high costs of police, courts, hospitals, mental health, etc.
Ford could help move the dial by funding creative solutions, such as culturally appropriate supportive housing and shelters for refugees that could free up to 50 per cent of shelter spaces in Toronto. Action on these opportunities will create many kinds of employment.
David Walsh, Toronto
Builders must meet the needs
of the population
My grandparents owned a small two-bedroom bungalow in North Leaside when I was young. My understanding was these homes were built and priced to attract workers to the new factories, southeast of them, like Canada Wire. In other words, they were priced based on the wages being offered at the factories.
Fast forward to today, where city approved condominiums contain small units that are more attractive to investors than families wishing to purchase a new home. There has not been a requirement by the municipality for the type of units needed by the general population.
Now consider those bungalows like my grandparents owned. The relative who ended up living in it sold it, and it was torn down to build a large two-storey house selling for over $1 million more than it was purchased for.
I believe that builders need to seek approval for the plans to meet the need of the population and the price point as part of the municipal approval in order to curtail the spiral of ever-increasing housing prices that seem to be occurring. We need to seek convergence of public housing prices versus private home construction prices.
Paul Naish, Markham
Cities need funds that development charges provide
If Toronto wants an example of how to respond to a housing crisis, it should look at Calgary, Olive, Oct. 7
The housing crisis is a threat to our communities. It's time for the government to take bold action, Wilkes, Oct. 7
In their columns, David Olive and David Wilkes both lament municipal development charges. However, both fail to mention the inevitable alternative: either monster tax increases (or taxpayer support from feds or province) or - more likely - simply not getting the essential city infrastructure that development charges pay for.
Does anyone really think city councils will rocket up taxes to get the indispensable billions from those missing development charges? Not likely. Does anyone really think the province will reliably pony up those missing billions to the cities? Not likely.
Already, cities are far too late providing the public transit, flood protection or recreation facilities for a growing population. The result: more traffic congestion and a lower quality of life. Do we want worse?
Olive hints at the folly of such cuts. Working for BILD, the developers' lobby, Wilkes is just doing his job. But from Olive, we expect the whole story.
Bill Winegard, Etobicoke

Mayor sorry for missing vigil

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mayor-sorry-missing-vigil/docview/3114752457/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+sorry+for+missing+vigil&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Olivia Chow says a lost email meant for her inbox meant she didn't attend a vigil marking the anniversary of a Hamas attack in Israel that has led to rising death counts and war in the Middle East.
"I don't know the details, but my office did not get the email that was sent to us," Chow said, adding, "I regret that I missed ... the memorial," referring to Monday's anniversary vigil.
Several politicians, including other members of city council, such as Brad Bradford and Dianne Saxe, attended the event, including Premier Doug Ford.
The anniversary of the 2023 attack in Israel was sombrely marked across the city.

Document 83 of 500

Where did the direction come from to remove those lands ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/where-did-direction-come-remove-those-lands/docview/3114752454/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+did+the+direction+come+from+to+remove+those+lands+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Where did the direction come from to remove those lands from the Greenbelt?"
That's one question RCMP investigators probing the $8.28-billion land swap scandal have been asking the political aides and bureaucrats they have been interviewing these past few months, sources told the Star.
As the Mounties' criminal probe marks its first anniversary Thursday, insiders say at least eight current and former top aides to Premier Doug Ford have been interviewed - in the presence of lawyers being paid for by the Progressive Conservative party, which has already spent hundreds of thousands of dollars on legal fees and is bracing for a seven-figure tab.
Members of the RCMP "O" Division's sensitive and international investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that looks into political crimes and corruption, have been dropping hints to interviewees about where the probe is headed.
The detectives, whose questions indicate they have already obtained numerous internal email and text messages, have suggested they may need to access banking records in Europe, meaning the investigation could last many months or even years.
According to the RCMP's 2021 annual report, the average duration of a federal police investigation is 1.6 years.
That timing is significant because Ford is considering a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote. Any major developments in the Greenbelt scandal could derail that timetable.
So far, the RCMP's focus has been on the premier's inner sanctum - not the municipal affairs minister's office that was initially blamed for the imbroglio.
Ford himself has not been interviewed, but he has emphasized his door is open to investigators.
"We have nothing to hide," he said in August.
"Come in and do whatever you have to do ... but I want full co-operation - they know that - because there's nothing to hide there. Let's get on with it."
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, say the Mounties have not yet scheduled interviews with former municipal affairs minister Steve Clark or his then chief of staff, Ryan Amato.
Clark and Amato, who both left their posts in the wake of the debacle, have denied any wrongdoing and are eager to meet with the RCMP - possibly before Christmas.
In June, the premier appointed Clark as government house leader.
Ford abandoned the Greenbelt land swap scheme on Sept. 21, 2023, a month after separate reports by then auditor general Bonnie Lysyk and integrity commissioner J. David Wake found developers connected to the Tories had been "favoured" when the premier allowed homes to be built on the environmentally sensitive land.
Wake's 166-page report seems to be serving as something of a road map for the Mounties, according to those who have been interviewed at law firms and at an RCMP detachment in the Greater Toronto Area.
The Star is not identifying all of those who have met with the RCMP because they are not authorized to discuss what they are telling detectives.
Officially, the Mounties would only confirm their work is ongoing.
"The RCMP's sensitive and international investigations team takes these allegations seriously. We would like to reassure the residents of Ontario that we are conducting a thorough investigation," the force said in a statement.
So far, the police line of questioning seems to be about the organizational structure in the premier's office - who reports to whom, what is the role of the bureaucracy in political decisions and who has the final say on green-lighting government initiatives.
Detectives also appear intrigued by a 2020 trip to Las Vegas that a top Ford aide, then principal secretary Amin Massoudi, took with MPP Kaleed Rasheed, Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Jae Truesdell, at the time a private citizen who would later become the premier's housing policy adviser.
Rasheed was forced to step down as Ford's business minister and leave the PC caucus in September 2023, after making what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner wrong information about the date of the Vegas jaunt.
Friends confide he has yet to meet with the RCMP, noting the affable Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP feels like "collateral damage" because he lost his job over that vacation date inconsistency despite having no role in the Greenbelt decision.
Truesdell, forced from his position in Ford's office, was one of the first Tories to be interviewed by the Mounties. He has maintained he did nothing wrong.
Rehmatullah, who attended the premier's daughter's wedding, owned stakes in two small parcels totalling about 23 acres that were removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
It is unclear whether the RCMP has yet met with Massoudi, now a successful lobbyist working in the private sector.
The premier's office said it cut "formal" ties with Massoudi - long one of Ford's closest advisers - last fall due to fallout from the Greenbelt saga.
When Massoudi left Queen's Park after the 2022 election to launch his lobbying business, the premier said, "since the early days at city hall, Amin has been by my side and has become one of my most trusted advisers."
"He's been a steady hand in my office, first serving as deputy chief of staff and then principal secretary and chair of our successful re-election campaign, and has helped encourage many young people inside and outside government to get more involved in politics and public service," Ford said at the time.
"Amin is like family, and he'll continue to be a close adviser and friend. I wish him nothing but success in his next chapter."
Interviewees have told the Mounties the decision to open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area dates back to November 2021 - more than six months before the last election and one year before Clark's surprise announcement that the land would be developed.
A joint Star and Narwhal investigation in November 2022 found a majority of the areas slated for redevelopment had been bought up by developers when the land was still protected.
Of the 15 parcels of land initially opened up, eight were purchased in the four years after Ford's 2018 election.
The premier, who has set a target of building 1.5 million new homes by 2031, has said he only allowed Greenbelt development to tackle Ontario's fast-growing housing crisis.
According to the auditor general's report, 14 of the 15 parcels included in the land swap were selected by then minister Clark's chief of staff Amato - with input from some developers.
But Wake's report found Clark had violated the Members' Integrity Act by putting his "head in the sand" and not properly supervising his aide, who has always maintained he was just following orders.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief
