Document 84 of 500

Former MPP calls for inquiry into CSIS: Spy service accused of wrongfully pursuing official for political meddling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-mpp-calls-inquiry-into-csis/docview/3114752335/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+MPP+calls+for+inquiry+into+CSIS%3A+Spy+service+accused+of+wrongfully+pursuing+official+for+political+meddling&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA Markham deputy mayor Michael Chan is accusing Canada's spy service of wrongfully pursuing him in its efforts to confront foreign political interference, as he responded to media reports that he is the subject of a national security warrant under scrutiny at an ongoing public inquiry.
In a statement posted on social media Wednesday, Chan said he was the subject of a "wiretap" by the Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) that needed a government-approved warrant. Blaming "leaks" from security officials to the media, he also slammed recent media coverage as a "barrage" of falsehoods that implied he is "an 'agent' of the Chinese government."
Chan - a former Ontario MPP and Liberal cabinet minister - said the "wiretap" was related to allegations that he "engineered the removal" of former Liberal MP Geng Tan and pushed for former Liberal MPP Han Dong to replace him as the party candidate in the Toronto riding of Don Valley North. Dong is denying allegations of involvement in Chinese foreign interference.
Pointing to previous testimony and a report by former Gov. General David Johnston, Chan denied any involvement and said "nothing" has been found against him despite what he described as years of scrutiny by CSIS and the media, in particular by the Globe and Mail.
The Globe has reported that Chan is the subject of a CSIS warrant that took 54 days for a Trudeau cabinet minister to approve in 2021.
The federal government has not publicly identified the subject of the warrant.
"The failure to report in a fair and responsible manner has not only hurt me in unimaginable ways, it has gravely impacted by family and friends," Chan said in his statement, which called for a public inquiry into "the competency of CSIS."
Neither CSIS nor the Globe responded to requests from the Star for comment on Wednesday evening.
Shortly after Chan published his statement, former high-ranking Liberal staffer Zita Astravas testified at the inquiry, where she was questioned about how long it took for Bill Blair, then the public safety minister, to approve a CSIS warrant about an individual whose identity was kept secret during Wednesday's hearing.
Astravas, who was Blair's chief of staff at the time, acknowledged a timeline provided at the inquiry that showed it took 54 days for Blair to approve CSIS's request for the warrant, which the Federal Court approved three weeks later.
Blair told the inquiry in April that he signed off on the warrant within hours of being made aware of it.
On Wednesday, Astravas said the warrant was never "raised as a matter of urgency" and that she followed the usual process of putting things before her minister based on a "priority list" provided by CSIS. The warrant was never in danger of being denied, she said.
"As soon as the director had indicated that he would like to put this warrant on the agenda, it was arranged within days," Astravas said.
The inquiry has previously heard testimony that CSIS was frustrated by how long it took for Blair to approve the warrant.
Pressed by Gib van Ert, the lawyer for Conservative MP Michael Chong, Astravas said "there was an awareness" in Blair's office that the warrant was coming. But she refused to concede the Blair was aware of who the warrant targeted.
"He was aware of a warrant, but I do not have specific recollection if the name was included in that," she said.
Van Ert also suggested Astravas was wary of the warrant because it involved "high-ranking members of her party" and "people she had known for years."
"The accusation you're making is false," Astravas responded.
She also testified that she did not discuss the warrant with anyone in the Prime Minister's Office or outside the government.
The Hogue commission, which started exploring foreign meddling in Canada's 2019 and 2021 elections earlier this year, is in the midst of its second phase: a deep dive into Ottawa's ability to address foreign interference into the country's democratic processes.
The inquiry has frequently heard about one report prepared by CSIS and the Intelligence Assessment Secretariat (IAS) which summarized China's interference attempts abroad and in Canada. A document tabled with the inquiry on Wednesday showed that the IAS believed the report should be shared with some deputy and cabinet ministers.
On Wednesday, Hogue heard from Jody Thomas, who previously served as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's national security and intelligence adviser, and opted not to share the then-unfinished report with senior political figures because she believed the report failed to reach "new conclusions."
A second report, which looked at how China selects targets for influence operations, further highlights the lack of consensus over what information Trudeau and his cabinet should be shown.
Hogue has previously heard from CSIS that Trudeau should have seen the report. Thomas said the influence efforts outlined in the report did not necessarily constitute nefarious examples of interference.
Nathalie Drouin, the prime minister's current national security adviser, said Wednesday that another version of the report was made available to Trudeau after he appeared before Hogue in April. The prime minister is set to testify again on Oct. 16.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall and Raisa Patel Toronto Star

Mayor Olivia Chow to apologize for not attending Oct. 7 vigil - blames missing email

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mayor-olivia-chow-apologize-not-attending-oct-7/docview/3115173168/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mayor+Olivia+Chow+to+apologize+for+not+attending+Oct.+7+vigil+-+blames+missing+email&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Olivia Chow says a lost email meant for her inbox meant she didn't attend a vigil marking the anniversary of a Hamas attack in Israel that has led to rising death counts and war in the Middle East.
"I don't know the details, but my office did not get the email that was sent to us," Chow said, adding, "I regret that I missed ... the memorial," referring to the Oct. 7 anniversary vigil held earlier this week.
The North York event was organized by the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto, a Toronto-based Jewish organization with the mission "to preserve and strengthen the quality of Jewish life in Greater Toronto, Canada, Israel and around the world," according to its website. Several politicians, including other members of city council, such as Brad Bradford and Dianne Saxe, attended the event on the evening of Oct. 7, including Premier Doug Ford.
The anniversary of the 2023 attack in Israel, in which Hamas militants killed some 1,200 people and abducted roughly 240 others, was sombrely marked across the city. Since the initial attack by Hamas, Israel has responded with an ongoing bombardment of Gaza that local health officials say has killed roughly 42,000 Palestinians and displaced many more as the landscape of Gaza has been turned into rubble.
Chow said her staff were stretched thin Monday, responding to a spate of violence that occurred over the past week, a park maintenance report that highlighted shortcomings in the oversight of staff, the continued saga of the vacant home tax and the return of city council Wednesday.
"So myself and my officer were extensively ... we were stretched because this is council week and there was a substantial amount of work that was in front of us."
"I don't know at this point, it doesn't matter," Chow said after being pressed for details on why she didn't attend.
"I wasn't there," Chow said. "Let's be better."
The mayor said she would be meeting leaders from UJA and CIJA, the Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs, on Wednesday to apologize for not being able to make it to the vigil.
"I will apologize for not being able to make Monday evening's event."
With files from Ben Spurr
CAPTION: Mayor Olivia Chow said her office was stretched thin on Monday, when the Oct. 7 vigil was held.

Document 86 of 500

Cutting down 800 trees was bad enough. But there's a bigger problem with Ontario Place

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/cutting-down-800-trees-was-bad-enough-theres/docview/3115173099/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cutting+down+800+trees+was+bad+enough.+But+there%27s+a+bigger+problem+with+Ontario+Place&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There has been a lot of outrage, and rightly so, over the destruction of more than 800 trees at Ontario Place to make room for a private mega-spa. For my part, I have been fixating on a different environmentally disastrous detail in the recently released 95-year lease between Doug Ford's provincial government and Therme, the Austrian firm developing the spa.
The province is on the hook for building at least 1,800 parking spots for use between Therme and Live Nation. Infrastructure Ontario's Michael Lindsay told reporters that Ford plans to build a total of 2,500 new spots. If the province fails to build sufficient parking before the spa opens, the taxpayer will be on the hook for up to $2.2 million per year. The structure itself will cost undisclosed millions.
The parkade, which will not only undoubtedly be a physical blight, is also a perfect symbol of Ford's inability to see more than five minutes into the future by committing the city further to a virtually irreversible vision of car dependency.
I have long been a critic of the abundance of free, cheap parking spots in North America, which gets me yelled at by people across the political spectrum and forces me to add, "Wait, let me finish!" The issue: For much of the last century, new construction was required to dedicate a certain amount of space to parking. These mandatory parking minimums were often arbitrarily decided, built around studies aligned to suburban developments and designed to accommodate visitors on the busiest days of the year. The result is an abundance of space used to store vehicles, and less space available for things like public parks, affordable housing, and other amenities.
Parking, like all car-centric infrastructure, forces cities to sprawl, making neighbourhoods less dense and less efficiently serviced by public transit. Driving then becomes the most convenient option for people, inducing more parking spots, more highways, and more sprawl.
Once this infrastructure is built, it is difficult to reverse course. Building alternatives, including better transit routes, bike lanes, and dense, walkable neighbourhoods, requires taking space away from private vehicles. As we have seen again and again, drivers will fight back.
And still, the city is trying. Toronto has adopted a strategy to reduce gas emissions to net zero by 2040, a plan that cannot be feasible unless we reimagine our relationship to cars. In 2021, Toronto got rid of mandatory parking minimums for new developments, with exceptions for accessible spaces. Parking can still be built in abundance, but it isn't required by law. New housing only has to include the amount of spaces that residents will actually use.
This decision will affect future parking options, but managing existing lots is more challenging. Last spring, the city tried to introduce a stormwater charge. The tax would apply to the amount of hard surface area on a property, including parking lots, and would go towards offsetting some of the billions that the city has allocated for stormwater management. The plan was shelved after it received international derision, including from Donald Trump, Jr., who called it a "rain tax." The City has been reconsidering the plan with an increased focus on industrial and commercial properties after July's flash flooding cost over an estimated one billion dollars in damages. The rain tax debacle proved yet again that once expensive, unsustainable, car-centric infrastructure is put in place, it can't be removed or altered without resistance.
Which brings us back to Ontario Place, a location that is already served by the TTC and the GO Train, and is a planned subway stop on the upcoming Ontario Line. While environmentalists and public transit advocates have been working tirelessly to find solutions that reduce the amount of unnecessary parking in transit-rich areas, Ford's government is happily spending millions to turn a public greenspace into a parking lot with hundreds more spaces than its already bloated requirements are demanding. The trees have been razed, but the proposed parking structure has yet to be built. Once it has, there is no going back.
CAPTION: The Ontario government is set to build 2500 new parking spots at the Therme Spa at Ontario Place.
CREDIT: Anna Fitzpatrick, Contributor

Subject: Automobiles; Public transportation; Infrastructure; Stormwater management

Former Liberal MPP calls for public inquiry into CSIS amid warrant controversy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/former-liberal-mpp-calls-public-inquiry-into-csis/docview/3114755207/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+Liberal+MPP+calls+for+public+inquiry+into+CSIS+amid+warrant+controversy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Markham deputy mayor Michael Chan is accusing Canada's spy service of wrongfully pursuing him in its efforts to confront foreign political interference, as he responded to media reports that he is the subject of a national security warrant under scrutiny at an ongoing public inquiry.
In a statement posted on social media Wednesday, Chan said he was subject of a "wiretap" by the Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) that needed a government-approved warrant. Blaming "leaks" from security officials to the media, he also slammed recent media coverage as a "barrage" of falsehoods that implied he is "an 'agent' of the Chinese government."
Chan - a former Ontario MPP and Liberal cabinet minister - said the "wiretap" was related to allegations that he "engineered the removal" of former Liberal MP Geng Tan and pushed for former Liberal MPP Han Dong to replace him as the party candidate in the Toronto riding of Don Valley North. Dong is denying allegations of involvement in Chinese foreign interference.
Pointing to previous testimony and a report by former governor general David Johnston, Chan denied any involvement and said "nothing" has been found against him despite what he described as years of scrutiny by CSIS and the media, in particular by the Globe and Mail.
The Globe has reported that Chan is the subject of a CSIS warrant that took 54 days for a Trudeau cabinet minister to approve in 2021.
The federal government has not publicly identified the subject of the warrant.
"The failure to report in a fair and responsible manner has not only hurt me in unimaginable ways, it has gravely impacted by family and friends," Chan said in his statement, which called for a public inquiry into "the competency of CSIS."
Neither CSIS nor the Globe responded to requests from the Star for comment on Wednesday evening.
Shortly after Chan published his statement, former high-ranking Liberal staffer Zita Astravas testified at the inquiry, where she was questioned about how long it took for Bill Blair, then the public safety minister, to approve a CSIS warrant about an individual whose identity was kept secret during Wednesday's hearing.
Astravas, who was Blair's chief of staff at the time, acknowledged a timeline provided at the inquiry that showed it took 54 days for Blair to approve CSIS's request for the warrant, which the Federal Court approved three weeks later.
Blair told the inquiry in April that he signed off on the warrant within hours of being made aware of it.
On Wednesday, Astravas said the warrant was never "raised as a matter of urgency" and that she followed the usual process of putting things before her minister based on a "priority list" provided by CSIS. The warrant was never in danger of being denied, she said.
"As soon as the director had indicated that he would like to put this warrant on the agenda, it was arranged within days," Astravas said.
The inquiry has previously heard testimony that CSIS was frustrated by how long it took for Blair to approve the warrant.
Pressed by Gib van Ert, the lawyer for Conservative MP Michael Chong, Astravas said "there was an awareness" in Blair's office that the warrant was coming. But she refused to concede the Blair was aware of who the warrant targeted.
"He was aware of a warrant, but I do not have specific recollection if the name was included in that," she said.
Van Ert also suggested Astravas was wary of the warrant because it involved "high-ranking members of her party" and "people she had known for years."
"The accusation you're making is false," Astravas responded.
She also testified that she did not discuss the warrant with anyone in the Prime Minister's Office or outside the government.
The Hogue commission, which started exploring foreign meddling in Canada's 2019 and 2021 elections earlier this year, is in the midst of its second phase: a deep dive into Ottawa's ability to address foreign interference into the country's democratic processes.
The inquiry has frequently heard about one report prepared by CSIS and the Intelligence Assessment Secretariat (IAS) which summarized China's interference attempts abroad and in Canada. A document tabled with the inquiry on Wednesday showed that the IAS believed the report should be shared with some deputy and cabinet ministers.
On Wednesday, Hogue heard from Jody Thomas, who previously served as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's national security and intelligence adviser, and opted not to share the then-unfinished report with senior political figures because she believed the report failed to reach "new conclusions."
A second report, which looked at how China selects targets for influence operations, further highlights the lack of consensus over what information Trudeau and his cabinet should be shown.
Hogue has previously heard from CSIS that Trudeau should have seen the report. Thomas said the influence efforts outlined in the report did not necessarily constitute nefarious examples of interference.
Nathalie Drouin, the prime minister's current national security adviser, said Wednesday that another version of the report was made available to Trudeau after he appeared before Hogue in April. The prime minister is set to testify again on Oct. 16.
CAPTION: Former Markham-Unionville MPP and Liberal cabinet minister Michael Chan is shown at a meeting of Markham city council on Feb. 11, 2020.

Document 88 of 500

Canadians watch too much American news and only get half the picture, says U.S. ambassador

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-watch-too-much-american-news-only-get/docview/3114755149/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+watch+too+much+American+news+and+only+get+half+the+picture%2C+says+U.S.+ambassador&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are watching "far too much American cable news," says the U.S. ambassador to Canada, emphasizing democracy will thrive stateside regardless of who wins the presidential election.
But David Cohen conceded to the Star's Martin Regg Cohn at Toronto Metropolitan University's Democracy Forum "there's less polarization in Canada than there is in the United States" politically.
"Neither side may want to admit it, but there's stunningly broader agreement about the end state around some of these big issues - between or among the Conservative party, the NDP, the Liberal party - than there is disagreement," Cohen said Wednesday.
"What there is that is common between the United States and Canada - it's hard to say which one is better or worse - is a loss of civility in the dialogue that takes place around these important issues," he said.
"That's something that is hard for me to take because there's no reason for it."
The ambassador chided Canadians for their obsession with U.S. cable news channels like CNN, Fox News, and MSNBC where Democratic and Republican partisans performatively spar for the cameras.
"Canadians watch far too much American cable news. Canadians are watching this news without the context of living in the country," said Cohen, a former top executive at Comcast Corporation, which owns MSNBC among other broadcasters.
"Americans may watch too much of that news, too, but they live in the country, they're talking to other people, they're experiencing what the cable news is covering. They bring some American context to watching the cable news, Canadians don't have any of that."
Cohen's one-hour conversation with Cohn comes against the backdrop of a close election on Nov. 5 between Democrat Kamala Harris, departing President Joe Biden's vice-president, and Republican Donald Trump, the former president defeated by Biden in 2020.
While he was a major fundraiser for Biden and former presidents Barack Obama and Bill Clinton, the ambassador noted that under the U.S. Hatch Act he is prohibited from engaging in partisan political activities.
"I have unbelievable respect for the Hatch Act, but I hate to say this, I have even more respect for my reputation as a diplomat. When I was in the United States, I was extremely active in politics and a lot of people liked to hear me as a political commentator. In Canada, nobody's interested in their diplomats being a political commentator."
But Cohen stressed that even though "we have a turbulent decade of contention in our government systems and our government structures," U.S. democracy will survive as it did in the Civil War, the Great Depression, the McCarthy red scares of the 1950s and the upheaval of the civil rights movement.
"We've come out on the other side stronger than where we were before we went into those battles, our democracy stronger and more enduring."
Cohen, who meets with Canadian political leaders of all stripes, singled out Premier Doug Ford for praise.
"I like him a lot. He obviously has a huge affection for the United States," he said, adding Ford presented him with a signed CFL football that he now keeps in a glass case at the U.S. embassy in Ottawa.
Like Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz, who is Harris's vice-presidential running mate and famously played catch with Ford last June, the ambassador enjoyed tossing a football with the premier around his office at Queen's Park.
"We had a good time and neither one of us broke anything," said Cohen.
CAPTION: U.S. Ambassador to Canada David Cohen during a visit to Niagara Falls on May 12, 2023.

Ford government says audit of Ontario children's aid societies will look at care as well as finances

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-says-audit-ontario-childrens-aid/docview/3114752515/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+says+audit+of+Ontario+children%27s+aid+societies+will+look+at+care+as+well+as+finances&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has revealed its plans for an audit of children's aid societies that officials say will give them a comprehensive look at the state of the province's child welfare system, including the quality of child protection services and a financial review to pinpoint factors driving rising costs and deficits.
The audit, which ministry officials on Wednesday said will be conducted by an outside contractor with a final report expected next spring, will examine agencies' operational costs, including executive compensation and staffing.
The sector-wide review of 37 children's aid societies will also analyze funding spent on services for kids with complex special needs coming into the care of welfare agencies. The review does not include Indigenous agencies.
The audit comes in the wake of Star reporting that revealed child welfare agencies are increasingly becoming a last-resort option for families of children with complex health needs unable to get care in their communities, and a separate investigation into the death of a four-year-old girl whose body was found in a Rosedale dumpster after children's aid closed her case.
It also follows an announcement by Ontario Ombudsman Paul Dubé that his office will investigate the practice of children's aid societies placing young people in unlicensed settings.
Child welfare leaders told the Star they are forced to house kids and teens with complex mental health and developmental needs in unlicensed settings, such as hotels, rental apartments, office buildings and Airbnbs due to a lack of treatment options, and say these emergency placements are driving up costs for their agencies and harming vulnerable children and youth.
In an interview, Michael Parsa, the minister of children, community and social services, said "some youth face challenges and that's why we want to make sure they have the supports to be able to continue to grow and succeed in their communities."
Parsa outlined ministry funding to children's aid societies, which he described as "record investments," including an additional $36.5 million in base funding this year to the province's 50 child welfare agencies. Parsa said investments in child welfare have increased over 10 years while the number of children and youth in care has gone down about 30 per cent.
When asked why child welfare agencies still rely on unlicensed placements for children with complex needs, despite "record investments," Parsa said answering that question "is part of the process" of the sector-wide review.
"We're looking to make sure that the investments we're making ... are being used appropriately," Parsa said.
Kiaras Gharabaghi, dean of the Faculty of Community Services and professor in child and youth care at Toronto Metropolitan University, called an audit of the child welfare system that focuses on finances "a distraction" that does not respond to the challenges faced by families in crisis.
"Children's aid societies may not always have been on the right side of this problem, but auditing their finances is not a response to the problem, accomplishes nothing for families who need support right now, and ultimately reinforces the idea that if only people spent their money right, we wouldn't have a problem," Gharabaghi said.
Details of the audit were outlined in a technical briefing for journalists led by two ministry officials. To attend the virtual briefing, reporters had to agree not to name the officials or quote them directly.
Ministry officials highlighted data showing a 49 per cent drop in open protection cases and a 30 per cent decrease in children in care in the last decade. They also said that despite a nine per cent increase in funding allocations, the ministry also provided $55 million in additional funding over the last five years to manage deficits.
Focus areas of the audit include: regulatory compliance of child welfare services; how agencies use out-of-home placements; staffing and operational costs; and whether services are consistent across all CAS regions. The review will also look into how complex special needs services are contributing to agencies' costs.
Officials said the process to determine the third party that will conduct the review started Wednesday. A ministry spokesperson confirmed the review's findings and recommendations will be made public.
Questions about the audit remain unanswered, including whether the review will gather input from families and children in the care of welfare agencies. It's also unclear whether Premier Doug Ford directed the ministry to launch the review.
Last week, Ford pledged a "complete audit" of the child welfare sector, alleging misuse of taxpayer funds.
CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn, whose union represents roughly 5,000 child protection workers, said in a statement to the Star that the province should implement recommendations for child welfare reform already put forward by experts and advocates, while pointing to a system overhaul "four years in the making" and which has "produced absolutely no meaningful change."
"Another audit, investigation or review is not what families in Ontario need," Hahn said.
The province announced a complete overhaul of its child welfare system four years ago, at the time stating the redesign would focus on prevention and early intervention. Parsa told the Star that the new sector-wide audit is "part of our ongoing efforts" to improve the care of children and youth and said the review is another measure "to see how else we can improve services and support."
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca and mogilvie@thestar.ca
CAPTION: Michael Parsa, Ontario minister for children, community and social Services, saidinvestments in child welfare have increased over 10 years while the number of children and youth in care has gone down about 30 per cent.

Document 90 of 500

Here's who RCMP's Greenbelt investigators are interviewing - and the one question they're asking everyone

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-who-rcmps-greenbelt-investigators-are/docview/3114752511/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+who+RCMP%27s+Greenbelt+investigators+are+interviewing+-+and+the+one+question+they%27re+asking+everyone&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Where did the direction come from to remove those lands from the Greenbelt?"
That's one question RCMP investigators probing the $8.28-billion land swap scandal have been asking the political aides and bureaucrats they have been interviewing these past few months, sources told the Star.
As the Mounties' criminal probe marks its first anniversary Thursday, insiders say at least eight current and former top aides to Premier Doug Ford have been interviewed - in the presence of lawyers being paid for by the Progressive Conservative party, which has already spent hundreds of thousands of dollars on legal fees and is bracing for a seven-figure tab.
Members of the RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the elite Ottawa-based branch that looks into political crimes and corruption, have been dropping hints to interviewees about where the probe is headed.
The detectives, whose questions indicate they have already obtained numerous internal email and text messages, have suggested they may need to access banking records in Europe, meaning the investigation could last many months or even years.
According to the RCMP's 2021 annual report, the average length of time of a federal police investigation is 1.6 years.
That timing is significant because Ford is considering a spring election, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote. Any major developments in the Greenbelt scandal could derail that timetable.
So far, the RCMP's focus has been on the premier's inner sanctum - not the municipal affairs minister's office that was initially blamed for the imbroglio.
Ford himself has not been interviewed, but he has emphasized his door is open to investigators.
"We have nothing to hide," he said in August.
"Come in and do whatever you have to do ... but I want full co-operation - they know that - because there's nothing to hide there. Let's get on with it."
Sources, speaking confidentially in order to discuss internal deliberations, say the Mounties have not yet scheduled interviews with former municipal affairs minister Steve Clark or his then-chief of staff Ryan Amato.
Clark and Amato, who both left their posts in the wake of the debacle, have denied any wrongdoing and are eager to meet with the RCMP - possibly before Christmas. In June, the premier appointed Clark as government house leader.
Ford abandoned the Greenbelt land-swap scheme on Sept. 21, 2023, a month after separate reports by then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk and integrity commissioner J. David Wake found developers connected to the Tories had been "favoured" when the premier allowed homes to be built on the environmentally sensitive land.
Wake's 166-page report seems to be serving as something of a road map for the Mounties, according to those who have been interviewed at law firms and at an RCMP detachment in the Greater Toronto Area.
The Star is not identifying all of those who have met with the RCMP because they are not authorized to discuss what they are telling detectives.
Officially, the Mounties would only confirm their work is ongoing.
"The RCMP's Sensitive and International Investigations team takes these allegations seriously. We would like to reassure the residents of Ontario that we are conducting a thorough investigation," the force said in a statement.
So far, the police line of questioning seems to be about the organizational structure in the premier's office - who reports to whom, what is the role of the bureaucracy in political decisions and who has the final say on green-lighting government initiatives.
Detectives also appear intrigued by a 2020 trip to Las Vegas that a top Ford aide, then-principal secretary Amin Massoudi, took with MPP Kaleed Rasheed, Greenbelt developer Shakir Rehmatullah and Jae Truesdell, at the time a private citizen who would later become the premier's housing policy adviser.
Rasheed was forced to step down as Ford's business minister and leave the PC caucus in September 2023, after making what he called "an honest mistake" when he gave the integrity commissioner wrong information about the date of the Vegas jaunt.
Friends confide he has yet to meet with the RCMP, noting the affable Mississauga East-Cooksville MPP feels like "collateral damage" because he lost his job over that vacation date inconsistency despite having no role in the Greenbelt decision.
Truesdell, forced from his position in Ford's office, was one of the first Tories to be interviewed by the Mounties. He has maintained he did nothing wrong.
Rehmatullah , who attended the premier's daughter's wedding, owned stakes in two small parcels totalling about 23 acres that were removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
It is unclear whether the RCMP has yet met with Massoudi, now a successful lobbyist working in the private sector.
Long one of Ford's closest advisers, the premier's office said it cut "formal" ties with him last fall due to fallout from the Greenbelt saga.
When Massoudi left Queen's Park after the 2022 election to launch his lobbying business, the premier said "since the early days at city hall, Amin has been by my side and has become one of my most-trusted advisers."
"He's been a steady hand in my office, first serving as deputy chief of staff and then principal secretary and chair of our successful re-election campaign, and has helped encourage many young people inside and outside government to get more involved in politics and public service," Ford said at the time.
"Amin is like family, and he'll continue to be a close adviser and friend. I wish him nothing but success in his next chapter."
Interviewees have told the Mounties that the decision to open up 7,400 acres of the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area dates back to November 2021 - more than six months before the last election and one year before Clark's surprise announcement the land would be developed.
A joint Star and Narwhal investigation in November 2022 found a majority of the areas slated for redevelopment had been bought up by developers when the land was still protected. Of the 15 parcels of land initially opened up, eight were purchased in the four years after Ford's 2018 election.
The premier, who has set a target of building 1.5 million new homes by 2031, has said he only allowed Greenbelt development to tackle Ontario's fast-growing housing crisis.
According to the auditor general's report, 14 of the 15 parcels included in the land swap were selected by then-minister Clark's chief of staff Amato - with input from some developers.
But Wake's report found Clark had violated the Members' Integrity Act by putting his "head in the sand" and not properly supervising his aide, who has always maintained he was just following orders.
CAPTION: Steve Clark announced his resignation housing minister amid the Greenbelt controversy.Ryan Amato was chief of staff to Ontario Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing Steve Clark.Amin Massoudi and Doug Ford are shown in this 2014 file photo.Premier Doug Ford answers an opposition question at Queen's Park legislature on Sept. 25, 2023.Ford abandoned the Greenbelt land-swap scheme four days earlier.

Document 91 of 500

'The absolute worst place': Ford government's plan to move Ontario Place sewer away from Therme's planned beach has locals fuming

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/absolute-worst-place-ford-governments-plan-move/docview/3114490585/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+absolute+worst+place%27%3A+Ford+government%27s+plan+to+move+Ontario+Place+sewer+away+from+Therme%27s+planned+beach+has+locals+fuming&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government wants sewer pipes that send raw waste into Lake Ontario to be directed into a channel used by swimmers and rowers, the Star has learned.
The reason? To prevent pollution of a beach Therme is building at a redeveloped Ontario Place.
The unpublicized stopgap plan is taking flak from users of the West Channel, including a leader of a lake swimmers' group, the CNE and the local councillor, who wants wider public consultations on the plan.
"The absolute worst place for an overflow sewage outlet is in shallow (West Channel) water with no current," said Darrell Brown, chief executive of the Canadian National Exhibition Association. The Ex hosts water-skiing shows in the channel where Infrastructure Ontario wants the sewer redirected.
Four wastewater pipes currently go further out into open water at the west end of Ontario Place, discharging past the breakwall, which would put the waste stream in line with the planned public beach near the megaspa.
Combining the pipes into one aiming sewage into the West Channel, Brown said, is "like the Seine in the (Paris) Olympics" where some swimmers found themselves wading through concerns over E. coli bacteria levels. "If we've got a show where people are going in the water after a couple of storms - are they going to want to do that?"
City staff said in an email they are in talks with Infrastructure Ontario, the provincial agency leading the spa-focused redevelopment, which wants the city to move, at provincial expense, the "combined sewer outflow" because its current path for discharged feces, litter and other stormwater waste could pollute the West Island of Ontario Place after its shoreline is expanded with 10 acres of fill.
"In addition, a new beach is proposed on the western side of the West Island; with the combined sewer outfall in its proximity Infrastructure Ontario is investigating options for relocating the ... discharge away from the beach," to be built by Therme, the Austrian firm granted a 95-year lease by Premier Doug Ford's government to open a towering spa resort and waterpark.
Sensors, pumps and an underwater "turbidity curtain" would help "mitigate impacts during an overflow event" in the West Channel, part of an "interim solution" the city says would last as long as two years during West Island construction before permanent fixes that could include extending the outflow pipe around the expanded West Island and out into Lake Ontario, where waste would dissipate.
"Due to timing of the planned redevelopment, Infrastructure Ontario has advised that a relocated ... discharge location needs to be approved before spring 2025," the city said.
The province told the Star that damage to one pipe means some waste is already escaping into the channel after big rain events.
The CNE's Brown said Infrastructure Ontario should fix all the issues by extending the pipe further out into the lake now - an option that city staff told him has been rejected by the province.
City hall agreed last December to drop its opposition to the controversial Ontario Place revamp as part of the Ford government agreeing to a new fiscal deal for Toronto. However, Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, the Spadina-Fort York councillor whose ward includes the West Channel, said the city has a say in changes to its sewer system.
"From what we've seen so far with this (Ontario Place) redevelopment project, the provincial government has consistently avoided principles of good planning," Malik said. "They didn't complete a full environmental assessment for the West Island, and for the new lake fill, and they have to show the work now and prove that what they are looking for won't cause adverse impacts in the lake...
"Right now, the information that city staff have shared from Infrastructure Ontario is not satisfying," she said.
"Toronto Water has to approve the change and the new location - we need the full information for the protection and information of everyone, whether you're in the water or on the water or coming through the water."
Steve Mann, a year-round Lake Ontario swimmer, wrote in July 2023 that Ontario Place's old pebble beach is perfectly located to get the cleanest water, and warned that Therme's new beach on the west side of the island would be in the pathway of used condoms, tampons, cans, bottles and other detritus.
Told about the province's plan to aim the overflow sewer pipe into the channel, the U of T computer science professor said, "Rather than put the pipe out into open water, where the waste will dissipate, they want to put it right in the swimming and rowing channels where it will stink for a long time.
"That's our favourite swim spot; we swim in that channel almost every day. They should spend the money and fix the problem. Therme and (construction firm) Ellis Don have all the money, all the resources ...they should own the problem."
Andrew Liew of the Sunnyside Paddling Club said the Western Beaches Stakeholders' Association, which also includes the Argonaut Rowing Club, the Boulevard Club and Toronto Sailing and Canoe, is "aware of the issue and we have been in contact with Infrastructure Ontario."
Infrastructure Ontario spokesperson Ian McConachie said in an email that his agency "is in the process of assessing mitigation measures/phasing to ensure continued combined sewage outflow operations are maintained.
"The mitigation measures being considered will improve current environmental conditions for everyone, including other West Channel users. Longer-term infrastructure works are still under assessment. Currently, during severe weather events, there is discharge of municipal sewage into the inner channel as one of the four pipes that takes it out into the lake needs repairs."
McConachie did not answer Star questions, including what the total cost of the interim sewer fix and the long-term solution will be. The Ford government has touted Therme's promise to spend about $200 million to create 16 acres of new, public parkland space and $500 million on the glass-encased spa building, plus $84 million in rent and maintenance payments to the province from 2034 to 2044.
Critics, however, are questioning if the Therme lease is good for taxpayers in the long run, given unknown total provincial costs including site servicing and the creation of 2,500 new parking spaces to service the spa as well as an expanded year-round Budweiser Stage and a relocated Science Centre.
CAPTION: The province is looking at diverting a wastewater pipe that empties beyond the breakwall at Ontario Place's West Island into the West Channel that rowers and swimmers use.

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, October 9 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a warning from Canada's latest Nobel laureate, a woman accused of three murders and questions about Doug Ford's child welfare audit.
DON'T MISSToronto's new Nobel laureate has a dire warning for us
Canada's latest Nobel laureate has a grim message about his own creation. Geoffrey Hinton, the Nobel Prize-winning "godfather of AI," quit Google last year to warn the world about the technology he helped create. "These things could get smarter than us and might go rogue," he said earlier this year. "That's not science fiction...That's real." Kate Allen chronicles Hinton's fascinating life - and his urgent warning.
Go deeper: Hinton told reporters AI has great potential, like revolutionizing health care. But for all its virtues, the technology needs more guardrails against misuse. Immediate risks range from "fake videos corrupting elections" to sophisticated cyberattacks, he said.Meanwhile: Hinton also used his new platform to blast Doug Ford for his decision to close the Science Centre, which he once offered $1 million of his own money to repair. Here's what he had to say.A brother is shocked to learn his sister was accused of murder
Nicholas Kauldhar hasn't spoken to his older sister in over a decade. The Sabrina he knew stayed out of trouble as a teenager, he told Jason Miller - so he was appalled to learn the 30-year-old woman stands accused of murdering three people across three jurisdictions over the course of three days last week. "This is not something I would've ever imagined," he said. Take a closer look at the situation.
The allegations: Kauldhar faces one count of first-degree murder and two counts of second-degree murder. Investigators believe two of the killings were random; the third victim was allegedly known to her. None of the charges against her have been proven in court.What now? Kauldhar is expected in court today for her bail hearing. Doug Ford's child welfare audit is being panned as a 'smokescreen'
Ford had promised to audit Ontario's beleaguered child welfare system - but two unions doubt his intentions. The unions, representing more than 8,000 child protection workers, blasted the audit as a means to "silence critics" and a "smokescreen to distract from government underspending." Blaming individual children's aid societies for alleged financial mismanagement isn't the right approach, they tell Amy Dempsey Raven and Megan Ogilvie - here's what they say is really needed.
Word from Ontario: The Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services said the province is working to improve the system through standardizing safeguards across the sector, increasing societies' oversight over children in their care and more.ICYMI: The Star previously revealed that child welfare agencies are caring for a ballooning number of youth with complex health needs. Hundreds are being housed in unlicensed settings amid a lack of residential treatment options.WHAT ELSE

Document 93 of 500

New Nobel laureate rips Ford on Science Centre closure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-nobel-laureate-rips-ford-on-science-centre/docview/3114395247/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Nobel+laureate+rips+Ford+on+Science+Centre+closure&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Geoffrey Hinton, Toronto's new Nobel laureate in physics, has some choice words for Ontario Premier Doug Ford.
The University of Toronto professor emeritus and artificial intelligence pioneer appeared on CBC's "Metro Morning" on Tuesday, shortly after being awarded the prize, where he criticized Ford's handling of the Science Centre.
"Don't elect someone who's going to destroy the Science Centre."
That was Hinton's blunt answer when asked by host David Common what he has to say to young people interested in science, given what's happening to the centre.
The 76-year-old "godfather of AI" previously told reporters he would donate $1 million to repair the roof of the old Science Centre at Don Mills, after it was abruptly closed in late June following an engineering report that found structural issues with the building.
"The Science Centre engages children's natural curiosity about the world, which is the foundation of all science and engineering," Hinton told the Globe and Mail at the time. "It would be a tragedy to lose one of Ontario's jewels."
The computer scientist made it clear his donation would come only if Ford abandoned his plans to move the science museum to a smaller location at Ontario Place, scheduled to open in 2028.
However, the province did not respond when asked if it would accept Hinton's, and other private citizens', offers to pay for the repairs, according to the CBC.
Hinton doubled down on his criticism in a news conference later Tuesday. Asked how he would use a neural network - the technology he pushed to new heights - to improve his home city of Toronto, Hinton was again blunt: "I'm not quite sure how a neural network could get rid of Doug Ford," he said.
The new laureate again extolled the now-shuttered Science Centre as a place that encouraged "curiosity in young minds and curiosity in science."
"It could have been repaired, and it would have been much cheaper to repair it."
On Metro Morning,Hinton went on to encourage youth to "follow your curiosity," adding that "the best research comes out of being curious about how things work."
"Everybody's curious. And science is just being efficient about your curiosity so that you discover how things actually work ... That's how you get the best research done."
Hinton and Princeton University researcher John Hopfield were awarded the Nobel Prize in physics on Tuesday morning for their "foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks," according to the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences.
After 55 years of operation, the Ontario Science Centre was closed for good. And it happened years earlier than planned: "We had every intention on keeping the Science Centre open until the new Science Centre is built, but I cannot ignore the health and safety warning," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said in early July.
An engineering firm hired by provincial agency Infrastructure Ontario revealed problems with the site's concrete roof panels, which were at risk of failing. Water had infiltrated the panels, causing them to deteriorate and the internal reinforcing bar to rust. The report's authors recommended the faulty panels be replaced with steel decking, which would cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two to five years.
Officials instead opted to permanently close the facility.
With files from Victoria Gibson and the Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kevin Jiang Toronto Star With files from Victoria Gibson and the Canadian Press

Document 94 of 500

Two unions representing more than 8 000 child protection workers ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/two-unions-representing-more-than-8-000-child/docview/3114392422/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Two+unions+representing+more+than+8+000+child+protection+workers+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two unions representing more than 8,000 child protection workers across the province say Premier Doug Ford's plan to audit the child welfare system is a "smokescreen" meant to distract from Ontario's "failure" to help kids and teens in crisis.
Ford has pledged his government will do a complete audit of Ontario children's aid societies, telling reporters last week that he's heard "nightmare stories" of agencies paying rent for "Taj Mahal" office buildings and managers giving themselves bonuses "and not worrying about the kids."
His comments claiming misuse of taxpayer dollars came in response to Star reporting about kids and teens with complex needs in the care of children's aid being housed in unlicensed settings, including hotels, Airbnbs and office buildings.
On Tuesday, union leaders said Ford's comments were meant to "silence critics" about the mounting crisis and called on the government to adequately fund the social services system to ensure children can get the help they need.
JP Hornick, president of OPSEU/SEFPO, whose union represents 3,200 child protection workers at 13 welfare agencies, said blaming individual children's aid societies for alleged financial mismanagement is a "brazen lie."
"This audit is a smokescreen to distract from government underspending," Hornick said in a written statement to the Star.
"It's the eleventh hour of this crisis and this government has yet to do anything to solve it," CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn, whose union represents roughly 5,000 child protection workers at 27 children's aid society (CAS) agencies, said in a joint union statement released on Tuesday.
In a statement to the Star, the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services said the province has been working to improve the child welfare system, including by: passing legislation with "provisions that modernize and standardize important safeguards throughout the sector" and which strengthens oversight of foster care and group homes; amending regulations to "increase CAS oversight of children in their care"; and ensuring licensing information for out-of-care home providers is posted publicly online to allow CAS to make "informed decisions about where they are placing children and youth."
Reporting by the Star has revealed that child welfare agencies are caring for an increasing number of kids and teens with complex health needs, including hundreds who are being housed in unlicensed settings due to a lack of residential treatment options. Children with complex needs have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression, and families typically struggle to get timely and adequate help in their communities. Child welfare leaders say a growing number of parents are being forced to relinquish care of their children to CAS because they can no longer manage their complex needs at home.
Ontario ombudsman Paul Dubé is investigating the practice of children's aid societies placing young people in unlicensed settings. Child welfare agencies are pushing the ombudsman to examine the underlying issues that are leading them to use unlicensed placements as a last resort.
Ministry spokesperson Kristen Tedesco said the government has invested an additional $310 million over three years "to address increasing operational costs for community organizations that support vulnerable people across Ontario."
Tedesco also said the child welfare sector this year received a funding increase of $36.5 million - a base increase that "will be ongoing" - and that the children's ministry and the Ministry of Health "continue to increase funding" to support children with special needs.
Arlette Carrier, a child protection worker in southeastern Ontario and CUPE Local 2577 president, said workers across the province were frustrated by Ford's comments.
"I was hopeful and optimistic that the premier or Minister Michael Parsa would come out, show some compassion, some understanding, and come up with some real solutions." Instead, she said, the premier "doubled down."
Carrier said that her agency, the children's aid society that serves Lanark, Leeds and Grenville, near Ottawa, currently has two youth living in Airbnbs and one in a short-term rental apartment.
"It feels like the kids are just being abandoned by the government."
Andrew Harrigan, a child protection worker in York Region and OPSEU Local 304 president, said front-line staff have long warned that the province's health and social services systems are unable to meet the growing needs of vulnerable children and families.
"The system is not adequately funded to sustain what we're seeing in the community," said Harrigan.
Ruby Taylor, a child protection worker at the Kawartha-Haliburton Children's Aid Society and president of OPSEU Local 334, said her agency has seen a steady rise in the number of kids who come into care not because they need protection from abuse or neglect, but because they can't access the services they need and their parents can no longer safely care for them.
"We are worried about the kids in our community, and we don't know what else to do," Taylor said. "We've been rallying, we've been writing letters, we've been trying to advocate.
"We believe the children and youth in our communities deserve better."
CREDIT: Amy Dempsey Raven and Megan Ogilvie Toronto Star

Document 95 of 500

The Ford government wants sewer pipes that send raw waste ...[Derived Headline]

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-government-wants-sewer-pipes-that-send-raw/docview/3114391200/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+Ford+government+wants+sewer+pipes+that+send+raw+waste+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government wants sewer pipes that send raw waste into Lake Ontario to be directed into a channel used by swimmers and rowers, the Star has learned.
The reason? To prevent pollution of a beach Therme is building at a redeveloped Ontario Place.
The unpublicized stopgap plan is taking flak from users of the West Channel, including a leader of a lake swimmers' group, the CNE and the local councillor, who wants wider public consultations on the plan.
"The absolute worst place for an overflow sewage outlet is in shallow (West Channel) water with no current," said Darrell Brown, chief executive of the Canadian National Exhibition Association. The Ex hosts water-skiing shows in the channel where Infrastructure Ontario wants the sewer redirected.
Four wastewater pipes currently go farther out into open water at the west end of Ontario Place, discharging past the breakwall, which would put the waste stream in line with the planned public beach near the megaspa.
Combining the pipes into one aiming sewage into the West Channel, Brown said, is "like the Seine in the (Paris) Olympics" where some swimmers found themselves wading through concerns over E. coli bacteria levels. "If we've got a show where people are going in the water after a couple of storms - are they going to want to do that?"
City staff said in an email they are in talks with Infrastructure Ontario, the provincial agency leading the spa-focused redevelopment, which wants the city to move, at provincial expense, the "combined sewer outflow" because its current path for discharged feces, litter and other stormwater waste could pollute the West Island of Ontario Place after its shoreline is expanded with 10 acres of fill.
"In addition, a new beach is proposed on the western side of the West Island; with the combined sewer outfall in its proximity Infrastructure Ontario is investigating options for relocating the ... discharge away from the beach," to be built by Therme, the Austrian firm granted a 95-year lease by Premier Doug Ford's government to open a towering spa resort and waterpark.
Sensors, pumps and an underwater "turbidity curtain" would help "mitigate impacts during an overflow event" in the West Channel, part of an "interim solution" the city says would last as long as two years during West Island construction before permanent fixes that could include extending the outflow pipe around the expanded West Island and out into Lake Ontario, where waste would dissipate.
"Due to timing of the planned redevelopment, Infrastructure Ontario has advised that a relocated ... discharge location needs to be approved before spring 2025," the city said.
The province told the Star that damage to one pipe means some waste is already escaping into the channel after big rain events.
The CNE's Brown said Infrastructure Ontario should fix all the issues by extending the pipe further out into the lake now - an option that city staff told him has been rejected by the province.
City hall agreed last December to drop its opposition to the controversial Ontario Place revamp as part of the Ford government agreeing to a new fiscal deal for Toronto. However, Deputy Mayor Ausma Malik, the Spadina-Fort York councillor whose ward includes the West Channel, said the city has a say in changes to its sewer system.
"From what we've seen so far with this (Ontario Place) redevelopment project, the provincial government has consistently avoided principles of good planning," Malik said. "They didn't complete a full environmental assessment for the West Island, and for the new lake fill, and they have to show the work now and prove that what they are looking for won't cause adverse impacts in the lake.
"Right now, the information that city staff have shared from Infrastructure Ontario is not satisfying," she said.
"Toronto Water has to approve the change and the new location - we need the full information for the protection and information of everyone, whether you're in the water or on the water or coming through the water."
Steve Mann, a year-round Lake Ontario swimmer, wrote in July 2023 that Ontario Place's old pebble beach is perfectly located to get the cleanest water, and warned that Therme's new beach on the west side of the island would be in the pathway of used condoms, tampons, cans, bottles and other detritus.
Told about the province's plan to aim the overflow sewer pipe into the channel, the U of T computer science professor said, "Rather than put the pipe out into open water, where the waste will dissipate, they want to put it right in the swimming and rowing channels where it will stink for a long time.
"That's our favourite swim spot; we swim in that channel almost every day. They should spend the money and fix the problem. Therme and (construction firm) Ellis Don have all the money, all the resources ... they should own the problem."
Andrew Liew of the Sunnyside Paddling Club said the Western Beaches Stakeholders' Association, which also includes the Argonaut Rowing Club, the Boulevard Club and Toronto Sailing and Canoe, is "aware of the issue and we have been in contact with Infrastructure Ontario."
Infrastructure Ontario spokesperson Ian McConachie said in an email that his agency "is in the process of assessing mitigation measures/phasing to ensure continued combined sewage outflow operations are maintained.
"The mitigation measures being considered will improve current environmental conditions for everyone, including other West Channel users. Longer-term infrastructure works are still under assessment. Currently, during severe weather events, there is discharge of municipal sewage into the inner channel as one of the four pipes that takes it out into the lake needs repairs."
McConachie did not answer Star questions, including what the total cost of the interim sewer fix and the long-term solution will be. The Ford government has touted Therme's promise to spend about $200 million to create 16 acres of new, public parkland space and $500 million on the glass-encased spa building, plus $84 million in rent and maintenance payments to the province from 2034 to 2044.
Critics, however, are questioning whether the Therme lease is good for taxpayers in the long run, given unknown total provincial costs including site servicing and the creation of 2,500 new parking spaces to service the spa as well as an expanded year-round Budweiser Stage and a relocated Science Centre.
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Document 96 of 500

Doug Ford's plan to audit the child welfare system is a 'smokescreen,' unions say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-plan-audit-child-welfare-system-is/docview/3114395808/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+plan+to+audit+the+child+welfare+system+is+a+%27smokescreen%2C%27+unions+say&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Two unions representing more than 8,000 child protection workers across the province say Premier Doug Ford's plan to audit the child welfare system is a "smokescreen" meant to distract from Ontario's "failure" to help kids and teens in crisis.
Ford has pledged his government will do a complete audit of Ontario children's aid societies, telling reporters last week that he's heard "nightmare stories" of agencies paying rent for "Taj Mahal" office buildings and managers giving themselves bonuses "and not worrying about the kids."
His comments claiming misuse of taxpayer dollars came in response to Star reporting about kids and teens with complex needs in the care of children's aid being housed in unlicensed settings, including hotels, Airbnbs and office buildings.
On Tuesday, union leaders said Ford's comments were meant to "silence critics" about the mounting crisis and called on the government to adequately fund the social services system to ensure children can get the help they need.
JP Hornick, president of OPSEU/SEFPO, whose union represents 3,200 child protection workers at 13 welfare agencies, said blaming individual children's aid societies for alleged financial mismanagement is a "brazen lie."
"This audit is a smokescreen to distract from government underspending," Hornick said in a written statement to the Star.
"It's the 11th hour of this crisis and this government has yet to do anything to solve it," CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn, whose union represents roughly 5,000 child protection workers at 27 children's aid society (CAS) agencies, said in a joint union statement released on Tuesday.
In a statement to the Star, the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services said the province has been working to improve the child welfare system, including by: passing legislation with "provisions that modernize and standardize important safeguards throughout the sector" and which strengthens oversight of foster care and group homes; amending regulations to "increase CAS oversight of children in their care"; and ensuring licensing information for out-of-care home providers is posted publicly online to allow CAS to make "informed decisions about where they are placing children and youth."
Reporting by the Star has revealed that child welfare agencies are caring for an increasing number of kids and teens with complex health needs, including hundreds who are being housed in unlicensed settings due to a lack of residential treatment options. Children with complex needs have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression, and families typically struggle to get timely and adequate help in their communities. Child welfare leaders say a growing number of parents are being forced to relinquish care of their children to CAS because they can no longer manage their complex needs at home.
Ontario Ombudsman Paul Dubé is investigating the practice of children's aid societies placing young people in unlicensed settings. Child welfare agencies are pushing the ombudsman to examine the underlying issues that are leading them to use unlicensed placements as a last resort.
Ministry spokesperson Kristen Tedesco said the government has invested an additional $310 million over three years "to address increasing operational costs for community organizations that support vulnerable people across Ontario." Tedesco also said the child welfare sector this year received a funding increase of $36.5 million - a base increase that "will be ongoing" - and that the children's ministry and the Ministry of Health "continue to increase funding" to support children with special needs.
Arlette Carrier, a child protection worker in southeastern Ontario and CUPE Local 2577 president, said workers across the province were frustrated by Ford's comments.
"I was hopeful and optimistic that the premier or Minister Michael Parsa would come out, show some compassion, some understanding, and come up with some real solutions." Instead, she said, the premier "doubled down."
Carrier said that her agency, the children's aid society that serves Lanark, Leeds and Grenville, near Ottawa, currently has two youth living in Airbnbs and one in a short-term rental apartment.
"It feels like the kids are just being abandoned by the government."
Andrew Harrigan, a child protection worker in York Region and OPSEU Local 304 president, said front-line staff have long warned that the province's health and social services systems are unable to meet the growing needs of vulnerable children and families.
"The system is not adequately funded to sustain what we're seeing in the community," said Harrigan.
Ruby Taylor, a child protection worker at the Kawartha-Haliburton Children's Aid Society andpresident of OPSEU Local 334, said her agency has seen a steady rise in the number of kids who come into care not because they need protection from abuse or neglect, but because they can't access the services they need and their parents can no longer safely care for them.
"We are worried about the kids in our community, and we don't know what else to do," Taylor said. "We've been rallying, we've been writing letters, we've been trying to advocate.
"We believe the children and youth in our communities deserve better."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's comments claiming misuse of taxpayer dollars came in response to Star reporting about kids and teens with complex needs in the care of children's said being housed in hotels, Airbnbs and office buildings.Michael Parsa, Ontario minister for Children, Community and Social Services.

Another type of booze is coming to Ontario corner stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/another-type-booze-is-coming-ontario-corner/docview/3114394474/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+type+of+booze+is+coming+to+Ontario+corner+stores&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will introduce a minor regulation change that will allow convenience stores in the province to sell fortified wines such as port and vermouth, a senior government official told the Star.
Fortified wine is wine that has had distilled spirits, usually brandy, added to it.
The convenience stores can already sell wines with an alcohol content of 18 per cent or less and the change will just add a specific type of wine to the list, said a senior government official, who spoke on condition of anonymity because he wasn't authorized to make an official statement.
The change will come into effect Thursday, the official estimated, following the Ontario government's opening up of the alcohol retail market.
In May, Doug Ford announced they will hand over $225 million to The Beer Store to accelerate the expansion of alcohol sales.
Starting in September, more than 4,000 corner stores began offering Ontarians beer, wine, cider or ready-to-drink cocktails like hard seltzer.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford attends an press availability at a convenience store in Toronto, Thursday, Dec. 14, 2023. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Chris Young
