Document 98 of 500

Toronto's new Nobel laureate Geoffrey Hinton slams Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-new-nobel-laureate-geoffrey-hinton-slams/docview/3114142976/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+new+Nobel+laureate+Geoffrey+Hinton+slams+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Geoffrey Hinton, Toronto's new Nobel laureate in physics, has some choice words for Ontario premier Doug Ford.
The University of Toronto professor emeritus and artificial intelligence pioneer appeared on CBC's "Metro Morning" on Tuesday, shortly after being awarded the prize, where he criticized Ford's handling of the Science Centre.
"Don't elect someone who's going to destroy the Science Centre."
That was Hinton's blunt answer when asked by host David Common what he has to say to young people interested in science, given what's happening to the centre.
The 76-year-old "godfather of AI" previously told reporters he would donate $1 million to repair the roof of the old Science Centre at Don Mills, after it was abruptly closed in late June following an engineering report that found structural issues with the building.
"The Science Centre engages children's natural curiosity about the world, which is the foundation of all science and engineering," Hinton told the Globe and Mail at the time. "It would be a tragedy to lose one of Ontario's jewels."
The computer scientist made it clear his donation would come only if Ford abandoned his plans to move the science museum to a smaller location at Ontario Place, scheduled to open in 2028.
However, the province did not respond when asked if it would accept Hinton's, and other private citizens', offers to pay for the repairs, according to the CBC.
Geoffrey Hinton's advice to youth
On Metro Morning, Hinton went on to encourage youth to "follow your curiosity," adding that "the best research comes out of being curious about how things work.
"Everybody's curious. And science is just being efficient about your curiosity so that you discover how things actually work... That's how you get the best research done."
Hinton and Princeton University researcher John Hopfield were awarded the Nobel Prize in physics on Tuesday morning for their "foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks," according to the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences.
What's happening with the Ontario Science Centre?
After 55 years of operation, the Ontario Science Centre was closed for good. And it happened years earlier than planned: "We had every intention on keeping the Science Centre open until the new Science Centre is built, but I cannot ignore the health and safety warning," Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma said in early July.
An engineering firm hired by provincial agency Infrastructure Ontario revealed problems with the site's concrete roof panels, which were at risk of failing. Water had infiltrated the panels, causing them to deteriorate and the internal reinforcing bar to rust.
The report's authors recommended the faulty panels be replaced with steel decking, which would cost between $22 million and $40 million and take two to five years.
Officials instead opted to permanently close the facility, a decision that would be panned by municipal and provincial officials of nearly every stripe.
"Something smells bad here," Ontario NDP leader Marit Stiles told the Star shortly after the closure. "I believe this is just the latest in a long list of scandals this government has been involved in, and just like with the Greenbelt, it has mobilized people who are concerned."
With files from the Canadian Press
CAPTION: British-Canadian cognitive psychologist and computer scientist Geoffrey Hinton, known as the "godfather of AI."

Document 99 of 500

Build the high-speed rail service already!

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/build-high-speed-rail-service-already/docview/3114142973/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Build+the+high-speed+rail+service+already%21&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Is Canada finally ready to build high-speed rail? Oct. 7
Two items pop out from this story - the route and money. Toronto-Peterborough-Ottawa-Montreal-Quebec City. Who's it for? The shores of Lake Ontario and the St. Lawrence River hold the densest population east of Toronto, yet the proposed route is via Peterborough. It simply makes no sense.
The second item is money, which stands out all the more for the absence of the third item which is climate change. Canada is facing catastrophic climate change. We have experienced it already in its mildest form - floods, extreme heat, shorter and milder winters, and hurricane-like storms. Canada, a major contributor to climate change has done very little so far - we are producing record levels of fossil fuels, we still export coal, and our forests are burning at unheard-of rates.
So why are we arguing about the cost of high-speed rail while planes and cars belch vast quantities of greenhouse gases among those cities?
Canada likes to think that it is a world leader in so many ways. Not in this way. What do the countries of Europe, the Far East and South America know that we do not? They know that high speed rail is good for the environment and good for their economies. Enough already! Build a high-speed rail service from Quebec to Toronto, or Windsor, with a spur to Ottawa. And do it now.
David Kister, Kingston
The United Church of Canada wants to aid Indigenous families with their searches
Residential school survivors are still fighting for access to records. They can't heal, until they know, Sept. 30
Thank you for Joy SpearChief-Morris's excellent article on the role of institutional archives in facilitating reconciliation for Indigenous families across the country. There are two clarifications that are important for us to make:
Re: Laura Hallman's statement - "There's just so much hidden in the archives that we don't even know what we have."
The United Church archivists use detailed finding aids and indexes to ensure comprehensive knowledge of all of our collections, which are readily accessible through our catalogue. While we know what physical records we possess, much of the information still requires interpretation. That's what we mean when we refer to records being "hidden."
I also want to respond to a statement made by Kimberly Murray: "The churches are trying to hide behind the National Centre for Truth and Reconciliation, because many of them will say, when you go to the entity and say let me access the records, they'll say, 'We gave our records to the TRC. Go get them at the NCTR.'"
The United Church of Canada Archives understands the challenges faced by the NCTR in making records accessible to survivors and their families. We do not have the same constraints and try to share all the resources we have available. We also undertake research to locate materials that we do not hold in places such as Library and Archives Canada, or other denominational and provincial archives. We provide that information to inquirers to simplify this difficult process for survivors and families.
Through our Bringing the Children Home program, the United Church also provides packages of digitized material directly to communities to aid their searches.
Laura Hallman, Archives Manager, The United Church of Canada, Toronto
What are the Conservatives' plans for addressing the changing climate?
Patrick Corrigan: Climate change denial, Oct. 5
Patrick Corrigan's cartoon, of Justin Trudeau asking Pierre Pollievre "What's Your Plan?" captures an important truth. If there's to be no tax on carbon emissions, what is the Conservative plan to reduce carbon emissions in Canada's struggle to ameliorate the effects of climate change? We've already had disastrous forest fires, floods and severe heat events. Maybe Corrigan could illustrate an equivalency of the federal and Ontario Conservatives as neither seems to have a viable plan to move away from an unsustainable economy based on the use of fossil fuels.
Allan Baker, Scarborough
Read the fine print before you vote
In the run-up to the last two Ontario elections, especially the most recent one, those of us who predicted what Premier Doug Ford would do to the province were screaming from the rooftops that his party was a danger to Ontarians. But the polls said he'd still be elected, so out of a sense of powerlessness (and to be fair, weak leaders in the opposition parties), many people stayed home on voting day. Clearly, we were right, as evidenced by razing Ontario Place, closing the precious Ontario Science Centre, proposing to build highways and tunnels nobody wants, and encouraging the creeping privatization of health care.
We are now sounding the alarm about Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. While his party has not put out a platform despite two years of campaigning, his plans are easy to find. He voted against and has promised to cancel $10-a-day daycare, dental care, pharmacare, housing initiatives, and carbon rebates. He has stated publicly that he would do the exact opposite of everything the Liberals have done, so all the programs that families and seniors rely upon to help make ends meet are on the chopping block. But again, the polls say the Conservatives are leading.
Will citizens never learn that they should base their vote on achievements, commitments and issues. Appreciate Ford for his folksy ways and hate Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for his supposed elitism as much as you want but look carefully at the actions and policies of the parties before marking your ballot.
Patricia Wilmot, Toronto
Peace is possible with the right diplomacy
Peace is possible in the Middle East and in Russia-Ukraine with the right diplomacy. I think India could facilitate that. India has good relationships with the Middle Eastern countries and with Russia and Ukraine. If I am correct the president of Ukrainian requested India host a peace meeting in India. If it happens, Russia will be there. I say why not. Enough is enough with wars that achieve nothing but the loss of lives and leave countries in ruins. Yes, it might fail, but at least one could say India tried.
Anant Nagpur, Toronto
Rogers Stadium will cause confusion and maybe tearsOasis announce Toronto 2025 show, after dropping major concert hints, Sept. 30
For better or for worse, next summer's Oasis concert will see a huge influx of travellers from outside Canada and indeed Toronto. How many of them will miss the concert because they wind up downtown at the "Rogers Centre" instead of the new "Rogers Stadium" in Downsview Park?
Jeremy Greenberg, Toronto

Document 100 of 500

Toronto is losing multiplexes to megahouses. Why isn't the city protecting them?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-is-losing-multiplexes-megahouses-why-isnt/docview/3114090461/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+is+losing+multiplexes+to+megahouses.+Why+isn%27t+the+city+protecting+them%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A three-storey house on a quintessential west-end Toronto street offers "the pinnacle of modern living."
Spanning more than 3,500 square feet against a backdrop of marble and white oak, the home is characterized by its luxurious features: an imported Italian custom kitchen, noise-cancelling windows and a wireless home sound system.
According to its sales listing history, there used to be a five-plex in its place. But the structure, purchased in 2020 for $1.9 million, was flipped into a single-family home that's now on the market for $4.7 million.
Across Toronto, homeowners are swapping multi-unit houses with tenants for polished and palatial single-family homes.
According to an analysis published by University of Toronto's School of Cities in January, Toronto lost at least 200 units from 2017 to 2023 when multiplexes were converted into single-family homes. Most of the "reno-ductions" took place in the west end, clustering around Roncesvalles, Parkdale and Davenport, with some extending south of the Danforth. This "south-central" part of the city, historically zoned for multiplexes, is being gentrified and losing residents, the authors wrote.
The loss of older multiplexes comes at a cost for the city and for individuals living in those units. While the individuals lose rent-controlled, affordable housing and are left to face unaffordable market prices, the city loses the "gentle density" and the affordable housing that it's working in other ways to create.
In 2023, Toronto legalized duplexes, triplexes and fourplexes citywide, allowing the multiplexes to be built without special permissions across the city. Previously, roughly 70 per cent of Toronto had been zoned exclusively for single-family homes. (Multiplexes with five or more units still require special permissions in most parts of the city.)
The change was meant to increase housing supply, but experts say those new units will be expensive because building or converting a house into a multiplex is a complex, lengthy and pricey process - one that cannot currently create affordable housing.
"(Building or adapting a multiplex) is actually very hard to do and quite expensive," said Karen Chapple, director of the University of Toronto's School of Cities. "So in some ways, the multiplex solution may not be the solution for housing affordability, except in terms of preserving the older ones."
If that's the case, why isn't the city protecting them?
Why multiplexes are being razed for single-family homes
There are generally no barriers for homeowners to turn duplexes, triplexes or fourplexes into megahouses.
Toronto doesn't require any special permissions to convert as-of-right multiplexes - which have a maximum of four units - into single-family homes, even though it does for the demolition of rental buildings with six or more units.
If a building with six or more rental units is slated for demolition or redevelopment, the owner must comply with Toronto's residential rental property demolition and conversion control bylaw, and provide tenants with replacement units at similar rents and assistance packages.
The only case in which replacements are not required is if all units have "above mid-range" rent; for example, if a one-bedroom unit costs more than $2,563.
Gord Perks, chair of Toronto's planning and housing committee, told the Star the provincial government has given the city a "very clear signal" that it shouldn't push harder on rental protections.
Recent amendments to Ontario's Planning Act gave the minister of municipal affairs and housing the authority to regulate the tools municipalities use to control rental replacement, Perks said.
And when the Ontario government signalled the rental replacement bylaw was under review, it sent a message that the city should "be careful" because it could lose its authority on the matter.
Plus, any provisions the city wants to change in its official plan have to be approved by Ontario's minister of municipal affairs and housing, Perks said.
"We're not being encouraged to expand our authority, we're actually being legislated to diminish it," Perks said. "So there's a hard calculation you have to make about whether the planning environment is such that the province is going to grant you more room, or whether we're in a position where they're going to keep taking it away."
Doug Ford's government has already diminished some of the city's powers, Perks said, for instance by changing rules around development charges and heritage designations.
Still, the Parkdale-High Park councillor said Toronto is ahead of any other Ontario municipality in protecting renters. It has taken steps in recent years to expand rental replacement rules, legalized multi-tenant housing (also known as rooming houses) citywide, and is developing a "renoviction" bylaw to prevent bad-faith evictions related to renovations, he added.
The office of the Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing Paul Calandra did not respond to the Star's request for comment.
'The issue is the loss of affordability'
Although new multiplexes provide lowrise housing options, environmental benefits and gentle density in areas with shrinking populations, they don't provide the same level of affordability that old multiplexes do, Chapple explained.
"The issue is the loss of affordability, more than even the loss of density," Chapple said about the conversion of multiplexes into single-family homes.
According to a 2022 report by the Urban Land Institute (ULI) for the City of Toronto, the costs of building a multiplex are so high that a typical homeowner would need to charge market rent in order for the conversion to be financially feasible. Conversions where the units compete directly with condos would more likely be feasible, and conversions in areas with higher rents would be more attractive to homeowners, the report found.
The study provided examples of budgets for multiplex conversions, laying out scenarios in which a $2-million house cost $2.6 million to convert and a $1.5-million house costs another $1.5 million to convert.
Richard Joy, the executive director of ULI, Toronto, agreed new multiplexes would be good for density, but not necessarily for affordability.
"Not only is it the case that the market would probably drive the units up to a level that is not affordable, but even if you wanted to make them affordable, very generously, the cost of doing a multiplex is, unfortunately, a lot more expensive than I think a lot of people imagine," he said.
How Toronto can preserve density and affordabilityGiven that new multiplexes are so expensive to build, Chapple said the city should try to preserve the existing stock.
She said the only scenario in which homeowners face an "onerous process" to convert a multiplex into a single-family-home is if the property is on the Heritage Register.
"What other cities have been doing, which I think works pretty well, is in certain zones to limit the total square footage that a single-family home can have," Chapple said.
Limiting the size of a single-family home to 3,000 square feet, for example, could prevent conversions of duplexes into "McMansions" while also preventing teardowns of smaller, more affordable homes, she explained.
Toronto could also apply rental replacement rules to multiplexes, Chapple said.
"From 2018 to 2022, the city approved nearly 2,500 replacement units at below-market rents, so it's a fairly successful program," she added.
While experts agree that new multiplexes can't provide affordable housing yet, they said governments can loosen requirements around development to make them more affordable to build and can provide incentives for developers to rent them out at affordable rates.
In Joy's view, there are many "archaic" building code and fire code barriers that the city could change to reduce costs. Simplifying requirements could reduce the costs of building a multiplex unit from $500,000, for example, to maybe $150,000, he said.
"There's huge potential in our single-family neighbourhoods across the GTA and Toronto," Joy said. "Literally hundreds and hundreds of condominiums worth of units that could be built without building condominiums."
Chapple pointed out that British Columbia recently allowed buildings of up to six storeys to have only one exit stairwell rather than two, something she said Toronto could do.
Michael Piper, an associate professor of urban design and architecture at the University of Toronto, said governments can make the process for these developments faster and cheaper by simplifying the approvals process.
He added that a recent, positive change is the removal of development charges for multiplexes.
Governments must also provide financial incentives to build multiplexes tied to affordability requirements, he said. For instance, they could provide a grant to builders with the requirement that recipients rent out the units at below-market rent.
It's particularly important to incentivize "citizen developers," he added, because they would be more likely than large developers to provide affordable prices.
"Owner-occupants have a different kind of economic pressure," Piper said. "They're just trying to make buying and owning a house more affordable."
CAPTION: This Little Italy home was bought for $1.9 million in 2020. According to listing records, it used used to be a multiplex. It's now on sale as a single-family home for about $4.7 million.

Subject: Demolition; Costs; Affordable housing; Planning; Single family; Housing developments; Homeowners; Cities

Document 101 of 500

A year after the Oct. 7 attack, Canadians gather to mourn as politicians lay blame

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/year-after-oct-7-attack-canadians-gather-mourn-as/docview/3114090054/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+year+after+the+Oct.+7+attack%2C+Canadians+gather+to+mourn+as+politicians+lay+blame&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As faces flashed across a screen in North York on Monday night, a crowd of thousands fell quiet, with little more than weeping audible over the memorial to victims of the Oct. 7 attacks in Israel - one year after the shocking assault, which sparked a regionwide conflict.
In the crowd, emotions were raw, with onlookers clutching one another and video-calling loved ones, their phones raised to the large display screens above the stage at the Lipa Green Centre while photographs appeared one after another.
This sombre scene played out as Canada's top elected officials issued heated statements decrying the 2023 attack in Israel, in which Hamas gunmen killed some 1,200 civilians and soldiers and abducted roughly 250 others.
Israel responded with an ongoing bombardment of Gaza that local health officials say has killed roughly 41,000 Palestinians and displaced many more as the landscape of Gaza has been turned into rubble.
Earlier Monday, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, released a written statement condemning the Oct. 7 attack as having "set the region down a path of war and violence," as Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, in more graphic detail, censured the violence as an act perpetrated by "genocidal and sadistic terrorists."
"We unequivocally condemn Hamas for the terror they wrought," Trudeau said in his statement, which memorialized seven Canadians who lost their lives, as well as an eighth victim with ties to the country. "We mourn the Canadians and all civilians killed in the year that followed. We condemn Hamas, Hezbollah and Iran, whose senseless attacks lead to more death and instability. The scale of civilian casualties since Oct. 7 is heartbreaking and unacceptable, and all actors must comply with international law," he said.
Both leaders also addressed rising antisemitism across Canada. "We've seen synagogues and schools attacked, people taunted for wearing a Star of David or a Kippah, blatant antisemitic slurs becoming common, demonstrators glorifying terrorists, and denial of the horrific events of Oct. 7. This is all reprehensible and it must stop," Trudeau's statement read. Poilievre condemned such acts as "grotesque," lay blame at the feet of the Liberals.
Federal New Democrat leader Jagmeet Singh, meanwhile, issued a statement decrying the "brutal terror attacks" carried out on Oct. 7 in Israel as well as the "high number of civilian casualties and indiscriminate attacks" in Gaza in the year since.
Under the glow of tea lights, at street protests and in the halls of power, Monday's grief and anger reflected the deep anguish felt worldwide since the Hamas attack on Israel, and the ensuing Israeli retaliation in Gaza and elsewhere, the latest tragic chapter in a decades-long conflict.
The impact has been felt acutely in Canada. In Toronto alone, thousands of demonstrators have taken to the streets to express pain and frustration, as the city has seen a steep rise in hate crimes - including antisemitism, Islamophobia and anti-Palestinian racism.
Frightening incidents like shots fired at a Jewish girls' school in North York, along with a wave of security threats, have rattled people's sense of safety, and fierce debates emerged over the line between political speech and hate speech.
On Monday, the Star shared the stories of those whose lives were changed forever by the war, from Torontonian Maureen Leshem who still waits on news of her abducted cousin, to Mississauga resident Sam Ebid whose brother remains stuck in Gaza.
At the North York vigil, which was organized by the UJA Federation of Greater Toronto, signs of heightened security was evident, from police helicopters and drones overhead to concrete barriers erected along the sidewalks stretching several kilometres. Police officers, some carrying rifles and others with dogs in tow, were stationed throughout the event.
The vigil was among a series of memorials and protests planned around the world Monday to mark the grim anniversary, including in Ottawa where, as evening fell, demonstrators waving Palestinian flags gathered outside of the building that houses Israel's embassy.
At the North York memorial Ontario Premier Doug Ford stood onstage among a crowd of dozens of people as the ceremony began, but did not offer public remarks. In a statement earlier Monday, Ford expressed solidarity with Israel and Jews in Canada facing acts of antisemitism. Ford argued that the safe, immediate return of any remaining hostages from the Oct. 7 attack was "the best way to bring about lasting peace in the region."
In his statement, Trudeau expressed hope for a ceasefire.
"The suffering must end, and we must return to the path toward lasting peace and security for Israelis, Palestinians, Lebanese, and others across the region. We remain committed to working toward an irreversible path to achieving a two-state solution, where Israelis and Palestinians can live securely within internationally recognized borders," the prime minister's statement read.
Both Trudeau and Poilievre also expressed support for Israel - with Trudeau's statement backing the country's "right to exist in peace and security," and Poilievre stating his party would stand with Israel "through fire and water."
With files from Victoria Gibson and the Associated Press
CAPTION: Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly read out the names of seven Canadians killed in the Oct. 7 Hamas attack on Israel, promising on the anniversary of the attack that their memories will be honoured. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre says the government must condemn acts of antisemitism. Subject: Antisemitism; Prime ministers; Casualties; Truces & cease fires; Palestinian people

Document 102 of 500

Canada's bail system is under fire, but how does it actually work?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-bail-system-is-under-fire-how-does/docview/3114090052/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+bail+system+is+under+fire%2C+but+how+does+it+actually+work%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's bail system is once again being questioned after the revelation that the man accused of shooting a police officer in Toronto last Wednesday was out on bail at the time.
In January 2023 and again this summer, Canada's premiers sent letters to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau asking for the bail system to be reformed and strengthened to prevent people who have been accused of a crime and released on bail from committing more offences.
Despite the government making changes to the system at the beginning of this year, reoffending still seems to be a prevalent issue.
How does the bail system work in Canada?
In Canada, some people who have been charged with a criminal offence are allowed to be released from police custody while they wait for their trial.
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms says that all people have a right to liberty and to be assumed innocent until proven guilty. Access to "reasonable bail" is also recognized as a constitutional right in the Charter, meaning people can't be denied bail without "just cause."
According to the Canadian Department of Justice's website, when determining that someone should not be granted bail, finding just cause requires considering whether the accused offender needs to be detained to ensure they go to their scheduled court date, to keep the public - including victims - safe or to maintain public trust in the justice system.
Decisions made in the Supreme Court of Canada (SCC) within the last decade have set the precedent that for most alleged crimes, the accused should be released on bail at the earliest opportunity with as few restrictions as possible
Who's responsible for what?
The federal, provincial and territorial governments share responsibility for Canada's criminal justice system.
According to information on the Department of Justice website, the federal government is responsible for enacting criminal law and procedures, including those relating to bail.
On the other hand, provincial and territorial governments are responsible for prosecuting most criminal offences that happen in their jurisdiction, conducting bail hearings related to those offences and enforcing conditions on those who have been released on bail.
The Government of Canada made reforms to the bail system in collaboration with the provinces, law enforcement, community organizations and Indigenous partners that came into effect in January.
Some of the changes address serious and violent repeat offences and the risks posed by intimate partner violence by making it the accused's responsibility to prove that they should be released while they await trial instead of assuming they should be.
When asked to comment on the state of the system, Minister of Justice Arif Virani's spokesperson said in an email to the Star that there just isn't enough court space to hold contested bail hearings, "so (the courts) are consenting to bail."
"Ontario must step up and ensure their courts and prosecutors are well resourced, that provincially appointed Justices of the Peace are applying the law, and that bail reviews are considered when there are concerns that bail was granted improperly," the spokesperson said. "It's time to stop deflecting and start enforcing the laws that we passed in collaboration with the provinces and territories."
To address concerns about crime, Premier Doug Ford recently appointed Brampton North MPP Graham McGregor to a new role in Ontario's Cabinet called the Associate Minister of Auto Theft and Bail Reform.
"The federal government is responsible for the Criminal Code and while Ontario appoints the judges, they write the instruction manual," McGregor's spokesperson said in an email to the Star when asked to comment on calls for reform and how he plans to address the issue provincially.
"Their lenient system created the circumstances that resulted in an Ontario police officer getting shot in the stomach at five in the afternoon," the statement said. "Enough is enough."
Is reform working?
Part of the challenge in reforming bail and addressing reoffenders is that there is "a lot of misinformation right now about the state of the bail system," said Shakir Rahim, director of criminal justice at the Canadian Civil Liberties Association (CCLA).
"One of the narratives I think that has been pushed by some in the public sphere is that bail is somehow easy to get," said Rahim. But a CCLA report finding that 70 per cent of people held in provincial and territorial courts in 2022 were there pre-trial shows that's not true, he explained.
According to Statistics Canada, that number rose to around 80 per cent in Ontario for the same period.
Rahim doesn't deny that there are important conversations to be had around public safety and how the bail system contributes to it, but "a lot of different levels of government, or police forces, want to reduce the problem to something that is based on a false conclusion," he said.
When it comes to reforming the bail system, Rahim explained "you have to have the data to study it, and you have to invest in solutions that we know are actually effective for ensuring compliance with bail conditions."
Potential solutions could look like bail beds for those who could be released but have nowhere to stay, reducing adjournments in bail hearings to speed up the process and increasing funds for legal aid services.
Efforts Rahim feels "are just not being taken up by any level of government in a meaningful way."
CAPTION: Canada's premiers have written to the Prime Minister twice asking for changes to be made to the bail system, but one expert says misinformation makes addressing the issue a challenge.

Subject: Political appointments; Charter of Rights-Canada; Collaboration; False information; Prime ministers; Trials; Criminal law

Location: Canada

Document 103 of 500

Sorrow and solidarity: A year after Oct. 7 attack, Canadians gather to mourn as politicians lay blame

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sorrow-solidarity/docview/3114088779/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sorrow+and+solidarity%3A+A+year+after+Oct.+7+attack%2C+Canadians+gather+to+mourn+as+politicians+lay+blame&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

"The suffering must end, and we must return to the path toward lasting peace and security for Israelis, Palestinians, Lebanese, and others across the region. We remain committed to working toward an irreversible path to achieving a two-state solution, where Israelis and Palestinians can live securely within internationally recognized borders," the prime minister's statement read.
Both Trudeau and Poilievre also expressed support for Israel - with Trudeau's statement backing the country's "right to exist in peace and security," and Poilievre stating his party would stand with Israel "through fire and water."
With files from Victoria Gibson and the Associated Press
CREDIT: Calvi Leon Toronto Star Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star With files from Victoria Gibson and the Associated Press

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Build+high+speed+rail+service+now+Is+Canada+finally+ready+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Is+Canada+finally+ready+to+build+high-speed+rail%3F+Oct.+7+Residential+school+survivors+are+still+fighting+for+access+to+records.+They+can%27t+heal%2C+until+they+know%2C+Sept.+30+Patrick+Corrigan%3A+Climate+change+denial%2C+Oct.+5+Oasis+announce+Toronto+2025+show%2C+after+dropping+major+concert+hints%2C+Sept.+30&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Build high-speed rail service now
Is Canada finally ready to build high-speed rail? Oct. 7
Two items pop out from this story - the route and money. Toronto-Peterborough-Ottawa-Montreal-Quebec City. Who's it for? The shores of Lake Ontario and the St. Lawrence River hold the densest population east of Toronto, yet the proposed route is via Peterborough. It simply makes no sense.
The second item is money, which stands out all the more for the absence of the third item which is climate change. Canada is facing catastrophic climate change. We have experienced it already in its mildest form - floods, extreme heat, shorter and milder winters and hurricane-like storms. Canada, a major contributor to climate change has done very little so far - we are producing record levels of fossil fuels, we still export coal, and our forests are burning at unheard-of rates.
So why are we arguing about the cost of high-speed rail while planes and cars belch vast quantities of greenhouse gases among those cities? Canada likes to think that it is a world leader in so many ways. Not in this way. What do the countries of Europe, the Far East and South America know that we do not? They know that high-speed rail is good for the environment and good for their economies. Enough already. Build a high-speed rail service from Quebec to Toronto, or Windsor, with a spur to Ottawa. And do it now.
David Kister, Kingston
United Church wants to help with searches
Residential school survivors are still fighting for access to records. They can't heal, until they know, Sept. 30
Thank you for Joy SpearChief-Morris's excellent article on the role of institutional archives in facilitating reconciliation for Indigenous families across the country. There are two clarifications that are important for us to make:
Regarding Laura Hallman's statement - "There's just so much hidden in the archives that we don't even know what we have" - the United Church archivists use detailed finding aids and indexes to ensure comprehensive knowledge of all of our collections, which are readily accessible through our catalogue. While we know what physical records we possess, much of the information still requires interpretation. That's what we mean when we refer to records being "hidden."
I also want to respond to a statement made by Kimberly Murray: "The churches are trying to hide behind the National Centre for Truth and Reconciliation, because many of them will say, when you go to the entity and say let me access the records, they'll say, 'We gave our records to the TRC. Go get them at the NCTR.' "
The United Church of Canada Archives understands the challenges faced by the NCTR in making records accessible to survivors and their families. We do not have the same constraints and try to share all the resources we have available. We also undertake research to locate materials that we do not hold in places such as Library and Archives Canada, or other denominational and provincial archives. We provide that information to inquirers to simplify this difficult process for survivors and families.
Through our Bringing the Children Home program, the United Church also provides packages of digitized material directly to communities to aid their searches.
Laura Hallman, archives manager, The United Church of Canada, Toronto
What are Tories' climate plans?
Patrick Corrigan: Climate change denial, Oct. 5
Patrick Corrigan's cartoon of Justin Trudeau asking Pierre Poilievre "What's Your Plan?" captures an important truth. If there's to be no tax on carbon emissions, what is the Conservative plan to reduce carbon emissions in Canada's struggle to ameliorate the effects of climate change? We've already had disastrous forest fires, floods and severe heat events. Maybe Corrigan could illustrate an equivalency of the federal and Ontario Conservatives as neither seems to have a viable plan to move away from an unsustainable economy based on the use of fossil fuels.
Allan Baker, Scarborough
Read fine print before you vote
In the run-up to the past two Ontario elections, especially the most recent one, those of us who predicted what Premier Doug Ford would do to the province were screaming from the rooftops that his party was a danger to Ontarians. But the polls said he'd still be elected, so out of a sense of powerlessness (and to be fair, weak leaders in the opposition parties), many people stayed home on voting day. Clearly, we were right, as evidenced by razing Ontario Place, closing the precious Science Centre, proposing to build highways and tunnels nobody wants, and encouraging the privatization of health care.
We are now sounding the alarm about Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. While his party has not put out a platform despite two years of campaigning, his plans are easy to find. He voted against and has promised to cancel $10-a-day daycare, dental care, pharmacare, housing initiatives and carbon rebates. He has stated publicly that he would do the exact opposite of everything the Liberals have done, so all the programs that families and seniors rely upon to help make ends meet are on the chopping block. But again, the polls say the Conservatives are leading.
Will citizens never learn that they should base their vote on achievements, commitments and issues? Appreciate Ford for his folksy ways and hate Prime Minister Justin Trudeau for his supposed elitism as much as you want but look carefully at the actions and policies of the parties before voting.
Patricia Wilmot, Toronto
Peace viable with right diplomacy
Peace is possible in the Middle East and in Russia-Ukraine with the right diplomacy. I think India could facilitate that. India has good relationships with the Middle Eastern countries and with Russia and Ukraine. If I am correct the president of Ukraine requested India host a peace meeting in India. If it happens, Russia will be there. I say why not? Enough is enough with wars that achieve nothing but the loss of lives and leave countries in ruins. Yes, it might fail, but at least one could say India tried.
Anant Nagpur, Toronto
'Rogers Stadium' a confusing name
Oasis announce Toronto 2025 show, after dropping major concert hints, Sept. 30
For better or for worse, next summer's Oasis concert will see a huge influx of travellers from outside Canada and indeed Toronto. How many of them will miss it because they wind up downtown at the "Rogers Centre" instead of the new "Rogers Stadium" in Downsview Park?
Jeremy Greenberg, Toronto

Document 105 of 500

Metrolinx taken to court over trees: Group says removal amounts to 'environmental racism'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/metrolinx-taken-court-over-trees/docview/3114088523/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Metrolinx+taken+to+court+over+trees%3A+Group+says+removal+amounts+to+%27environmental+racism%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A community group is taking Metrolinx to court Tuesday to halt Eglinton LRT construction on the grounds that it is discriminatory.
The Save Eglinton Flats Coalition argues Metrolinx engaged in "environmental racism" by planning to clear 1,200 trees in Mount Dennis, a "lower socio-economic and culturally diverse" area.
Whereas LRT construction will tiptoe around more affluent neighbourhoods to the northwest, Mount Dennis, located around Jane Street and Eglinton Avenue West, will have its parkland ripped apart, said the coalition's spokesperson Neiland Brissenden.
"Those neighbourhoods with single-family homes worth over a million dollars aren't going to change because of this project," he said. "Whereas in Mount Dennis, they're going to be going right through our parks.
A further insult, Brissenden said, is that these areas received more substantial consultation from Metrolinx ahead of construction, leading, he believes, to their portion of the crosstown being built underground, away from important fixtures of the neighbourhoods.
"We know these communities, which are in the ridings of the Minister of Infrastructure Kinga Surma and Doug Ford, their residents were consulted in-depth," he said, noting that the residents of Mount Dennis "were not consulted at any point."
Metrolinx spokesperson Andrea Ernesaks said in a statement to the Star the transit agency has, in fact, had hundreds of interactions with the Mount Dennis community.
This, she said, included 150 stakeholder meetings to discuss the project, 27 public open houses, 182 community pop-ups and 142 local canvasses. As well, an environmental assessment was conducted, which "included public consultation and feedback that was incorporated into the final report," she said.
"We have been consulting directly with rights-holding First Nations communities and will continue to do so throughout the course of the project," said Ernesaks. "We are aware of the recent injunction request and are working with our legal team to ensure construction on this important project will not be delayed."
Brissenden said these meetings didn't start until a year after Metrolinx had already planned its route through Mount Dennis parkland. They weren't meant to consult with residents, he said, they were meant to manage anger.
"It was communicated as a done deal, not as a partnership with the community," he said. "Nobody in the community is against the government or the LRT. We understand how important it is for our community. We're against our parks being wiped out."
Brissenden said chopping down these trees will remove crucial flood, heat and noise protection for residents and leave local wildlife, including endangered species, destitute.
"Mount Dennis suffers some of the worst heat island effects in the city," he said. "Removing 1,200 trees will exacerbate that. Indigenous people also use the parkland for ceremony, healing and education. They're going to be negatively impacted."
Ernesaks said the transit agency's work will actually be a boon to the area. Half of those 1,200 trees, she said, are invasive species. Metrolinx wants to replace them with more suitable trees.
"We will only ever remove the trees that are absolutely necessary, and we plant more trees than we remove," she said. "Up to 3,500 plantings will take place in Mount Dennis area parkland."
Brissenden said he is skeptical about this. Where would these new trees go? Parts of the parkland where this urban forest grows will be paved.
"The problem with Mount Dennis is there's not a lot of room for other trees," he said. "We're not sure they could replant them because there just isn't the space."
Further, he doesn't believe Metrolinx is differentiating "invasive" species from "non-native" species in its tally, the latter of which are actually beneficial for the flats, he said.
Brissenden said he is "cautiously optimistic" a judge will grant an injunction Tuesday. Otherwise, chainsaws will hit trunks starting Thursday.
Still, Brissenden knows Save Eglinton Flats Coalition is following a path that has historically ended in frustration. Not even the Law Society of Ontario was able to prevent Metrolinx from cutting down some historic trees that were in their way last year.
"We know it's a tough battle," he said. "We tried to do everything that we possibly could before going the court route. The community of Mount Dennis, we don't have a lot of resources. We don't have a lot of star power. It was only after exhausting all the grassroots avenues we could that we started raising money to ask for the court's assistance."
Their saving grace may be Bill C-226, an act to "prevent and address environmental racism" that became law in June.
Lawyer Eric Gillespie, who is representing the coalition in court Tuesday, said he believes there is a "clear case" for environmental racism, per the bill. He also said he believes an injunction should be granted to prevent irreparable harm to endangered animals living in the park.
"The Mount Dennis community is significantly marginalized," he said. "Metrolinx is willing to work with all sorts of variables, but it's not willing to work with a seriously marginalized community. It's not meaningful consultation when you speak to people a year or more after the decision's already been made."
CREDIT: Ben Cohen Toronto Star

Document 106 of 500

Councillor forced himself on drunk woman, court told: Thompson denies allegations as sexual assault trial begins

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/councillor-forced-himself-on-drunk-woman-court/docview/3114088498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Councillor+forced+himself+on+drunk+woman%2C+court+told%3A+Thompson+denies+allegations+as+sexual+assault+trial+begins&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto city Coun. Michael Thompson was portrayed in court Monday as someone who duped a young woman into joining him at a friend's cottage with "false promises," then plied his guests with alcohol before allegedly sexually assaulting two of them.
Thompson, 64, has pleaded not guilty to two counts of sexual assault; his lawyer, Leora Shemesh, says he vigorously denies "any and all allegations."
On Monday, on the first day of trial at this cottage country courthouse, Crown attorney Mareike Newhouse gave Justice Philop Brissette an overview of the case - a rare criminal prosecution of a sitting Toronto councillor who remains active in municipal politics.
On Canada Day weekend in 2022, Newhouse said, Thompson massaged one woman in a sexual manner "to which she did not consent" and, hours later, "forced himself" on another woman who was "very, very drunk."
The second complainant "clearly remembers telling him no," Newhouse said; despite this, she will testify Thompson put his penis on her and ejaculated on her, she said.
The two complainants, whose identities are covered by a publication ban, are expected to testify at trial later this week.
Newhouse opened her case on Monday by calling to the witness stand a university student who also cannot be identified.
She testified she was 22 when she first met Thompson at a Yorkville art gallery, and initially had no idea who he was. "He approached me asking questions and insisted we have wine," mentioning he had "connections in the creative sphere," she said.
It was "pretty obvious the way I was dressed that I was a creative," the now 24-year-old said, explaining that she agreed to meet him again that night at a nearby restaurant. Once there, the six-term councillor, who represents Ward 21, Scarborough Centre, led her to believe he "has control over finances in these creative programs around the city," she said.
The encounter "seemed like a cool alignment of events," she said, testifying that Thompson seemed like he could be her "mentor" and that he presented himself as "someone who would look out for me."
He later drove her to her suburban home north of Toronto; before she got out of the car, she said he handed her two $50 bills and "insisted I take it," which she did. (During cross-examination, she rejected defence lawyer Shemesh's suggestion that Thompson gave her the money because she'd told him about the hardships she faced as a student.)
The woman testified that Thompson mentioned a "future weekend of networking" up at a cottage, where the guest list would include a mixed group of male and female professionals.
As the Canada Day 2022 weekend drew closer, she said she was concerned that "all of a sudden, the original people couldn't make it," but was glad to hear another woman with a professional background would be present.
Thompson picked her up at her home on Friday, July 1, and they drove to Port Carling, in Muskoka. They spoke about politics, and navigating the world as a Black person in "prominently white affluent spaces."
At one point, Thompson called Premier Doug Ford to wish him happy Canada Day. (The witness testified she felt he did it to impress her.) At the cottage - which she described as "relatively big" but older - she said Thompson "encouraged a certain flow of events" by offering drinks.
Still, she felt comfortable knowing that many of her friends from high school were also in nearby Muskoka for the long weekend.
Another woman, Thompson's friend Tracy - no last name was given - arrived within an hour, and they smoked some of her homegrown cannabis. At first, she testified thinking, "my mentors are chill," but her ease slowly dissipated.
The other woman invited her to "test the bounciness of his bed" and then, at some point - she didn't know if it was the first or second day - she thought she heard Tracy and Thompson "kissing or something," which made her confused, as Thompson had presented their relationship as "friendly."
At another point, while sitting outside the cottage, the witness said Thompson casually told her: "Feel free to get naked," to which she said, "No, I'm good."
Later, she said she noticed "red flags" as she chatted with the older woman, including that the other woman began pouring clear alcohol into her cup - "She was definitely trying to get me intoxicated," the witness testified.
"I started to get very iffy about her," she said, explaining that she began taking only sips from shot glasses and discreetly throwing the rest over her shoulder.
"In what way was he (Thompson) influencing the level of drinking?" Newhouse asked.
He "was always making sure I'm taken care of ... by (having) a drink in hand," the witness responded.
Shemesh challenged this during her cross-examination, pointing to her police statement where the woman described Thompson as "asking" her if she wanted a drink. She also played in court snippets of cellphone video - taken from the witness's phone - capturing her drinking wine and "cheering" with Tracy.
"You'll agree with me that he doesn't force you to drink alcohol?" Shemesh asked; the witness agreed, but said Thompson has a "coercive nature."
She recalled one occasion when she was sitting by the lake in her bikini coverup, wrapped in a blanket, when Thompson approached his friend Tracy - who was topless - and "started massaging her shoulders and whispering to her." She said Thompson suggested for a second time: "Feel free to get naked."
"I was really uncomfortable," she said, testifying she eventually went to bed.
The next day, the witness said she expected more people "to be flowing in." Instead, two women - the complainants - arrived with a dog.
The witness said she saw Thompson getting "feely" with the new arrivals, and saw him massaging one of the complainants.
By this time, she said, Tracy had left, and the witness said she bonded with the two new women as they processed "what was going on."
The next morning, the woman testified she spoke to the complainants. In court, the prosecutor told her not to repeat what they said, but asked her to describe their demeanour.
Both expressed shock, anger, confusion and feeling "taken advantage of," she testified.
At the end of the weekend, the three women left together. The witness had originally planned to return with Thompson, and when she told him she was leaving without him, he was visibly annoyed, she said.
Did you hear from him again, Newhouse asked?
"No, I was ghosted," the woman said, adding he never followed up on his "false promises."
Shemesh will resume her cross-examination when the trial resumes on Thursday.
CREDIT: Betsy Powell and Ben Spurr Toronto Star

Document 107 of 500

Justin Trudeau condemns Hamas on anniversary of Oct. 7 attacks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-condemns-hamas-on-anniversary-oct/docview/3114242375/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+condemns+Hamas+on+anniversary+of+Oct.+7+attacks&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Canadian political leaders marked the first anniversary Monday of the Hamas attacks on Israel that launched war in the Middle East, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau saying Hamas "set the region down a path of war and violence" that has since killed tens of thousands, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre blaming Iran and its terrorist proxies.
Premier Doug Ford echoed the two federal leaders in reiterating solidarity with Israel, and with Jews in Canada facing antisemitism, and called for the release of all hostages.
The federal Liberal and Conservative leaders displayed different approaches to ascribing blame and marking the toll of events that followed the initial deadly assault by Hamas on Israel.
"We unequivocally condemn Hamas for the terror they wrought," Trudeau said in a written statement released by his office.
"We mourn the Canadians and all civilians killed in the year that followed. We condemn Hamas, Hezbollah and Iran, whose senseless attacks lead to more death and instability. The scale of civilian casualties since Oct. 7 is heartbreaking and unacceptable, and all actors must comply with international law."
The prime minister mourned the loss of seven Canadian victims and an eighth who had ties to Canada and lamented the "terrifying surge in antisemitism" in Canada.
"We've seen synagogues and schools attacked, people taunted for wearing a Star of David or a Kippah, blatant antisemitic slurs becoming common, demonstrators glorifying terrorists, and denial of the horrific events of October 7. This is all reprehensible and it must stop," Trudeau said as he reiterated his government's solidarity with Jewish people, and its call for the release of remaining hostages, as well as a ceasefire to set the stage for peace talks and a two-state solution to the intractable Israel-Palestine question.
"To Jewish people and Israelis in Canada and around the world: we stand with you on this painful anniversary, and we won't relent until we see every last hostage returned home safely. We will keep working relentlessly with our allies until there is a ceasefire in the Middle East, and we will always defend Israel's right to exist in peace and security," the prime minister said.
Poilievre's statement, a more full-throated condemnation of the violence of that day a year ago, outlined in graphic detail that "genocidal and sadistic terrorists carried out the worst attack on Jews since the Holocaust."
"They burned people alive, tortured children in front of their parents, mutilated dead civilians and posted these demonic cruelties online for the world to see. All while their cowardly bosses and benefactors watched from Tehran and other faraway safe-havens. 101 hostages are still being held. Let them go."
Poilievre's statement also took aim at the Liberal government and Trudeau for the fallout Jews in Canada have faced.
"Meanwhile, Jews in Canada face grotesque antisemitism in the streets and by (sic) the two-faced and weak leadership of their Prime Minister placating lawless mobs."
He hailed Israel and the Jewish people who "live on and fight on. Western democratic values depend on destroying Tehran's proxy armies of Hamas and Hezbollah."
Alluding to the slogan "Never again," widely understood as a post-Second World War vow by the United Nations community to prevent another Holocaust when it passed international laws against genocide, Poilievre said that fight is the present one, as he stated his solidarity with the Jewish Zionist cause.
"Canada's Conservatives have not and will not waver. We won't bow to the radical, woke, anti-Zionist Jew haters. Because 'Never Again' is now, tomorrow and every day after. We unapologetically stand with Israel through fire and water., and unite with Jews to say, 'Am Yisrael Chai!" reads Poilievre's statement.
Poilievre's statement did not allude to the ensuing Palestinian casualty toll in Gaza as a result of Israel's bombardment campaign.
Palestinian health authorities say Israel has killed more than 42,000 Palestinians in the past year. It does not distinguish between militant and civilian deaths.
Violence in the region is escalating, as Israel engages with Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon. Iran has fired a barrage of missiles at Israel. On Monday, Israel's ambassador to the UN vowed again that Israel would retaliate against Iran, as the United States and other global leaders are urging restraint.
Trudeau and Poilievre are expected to attend an event in solidarity with the Jewish community in Ottawa to mark the anniversary late Monday.
Ford was expected to attend a similar event in Toronto.
In his statement, Ford underscored the deaths of nearly 1,200 people in the original Hamas attack and the kidnapping of around 240 more, and expressed sympathy with the "many people in Ontario's Jewish community who maintain deep and close ties with family and loved ones in Israel."
Ford called for the "safe and immediate return of the remaining hostages being held captive by Hamas, which is the best way to bring about lasting peace in the region."
And the premier restated his government's "strong opposition to antisemitism and hate and reiterate the need to support our Jewish friends and neighbours. We will continue to stand up against hate wherever it is found and work to foster understanding, unity and respect in our province and communities."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau holds a news conference at the Francophonie Summit in Paris on Oct. 5, 2024.

Document 108 of 500

We don't need tolls on Highway 401. We need to take back Highway 407

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-dont-need-tolls-on-highway-401-take-back-407/docview/3113724756/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+don%27t+need+tolls+on+Highway+401.+We+need+to+take+back+Highway+407&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Highway 407, transit would help congestion
Really want to fix Highway 401? You should have to pay every time you drive on it: Cohn, Oct. 3
Columnist Martin Regg Cohn suggests that instituting tolls on Highway 401 would relieve congestion. Maybe, but addicted drivers would find other secondary routes to congest and large commercial vehicles would chalk it up as just another increased cost of doing business. Taking back Highway 407 and making it free to all drivers sounds more like a revenge plot against former Premier Mike Harris, who handed it off years ago, than a real solution to the problem. Besides, it's way too expensive on the front end. So why not tap into both roadways, one with a toll and one without, but don't change the payment dynamic. Take the larger commercial vehicles off Highway 401 and make them use the 407 but have the province pay for their toll charges. Equip them with a special transponder linked directly to the province's bank account. These commercial vehicles are the biggest polluters when they're stuck in traffic and removing them from the heavily travelled 401 to the less busy 407 would spread out the traffic and ease both congestion and pollution. Win, win?
Michael Bines, Toronto
The government needs to "buy back" Highway 407, keep tolls on it and move truck traffic off Highway 401 and onto the 407. This would then eliminate bumper to bumper traffic on the 401 and reinstate it as an automobile freeway. It's a win-win solution with no digging required!
Belinda Hayes, Toronto
Whenever we talk about congestion and its corresponding frustrations, we usually forget why it exists at all. Some espouse congestion fees or road tolls to fix it, but this ignores the simple fact that in a city there are a lot of people who want to go somewhere and the easiest way to get there is usually the car. If the easiest way was by transit, then you can rest-assured that is how people would move. By imposing tolls or congestion fees, you are making the car a more expensive way. But if you consider the convenience, safety and time involved, it still remains the easiest way so people are unlikely to change their habits. Congestion is a fact of life for any thriving city. Would you prefer the empty streets of a dying city?
Paul Graham, Mississauga
Public vs. private interests
In 1999, Mike Harris' Progressive Conservative party sold Ontarians down the river by signing away Highway 407 on a 99-year lease to a private consortium. In the quarter-century since, Ontarians have looked longingly at this asset, which has expanded and runs across an increasingly busy part of the province, as it makes money for foreign shareholders, and costs the people of Ontario dearly in access to transportation options, exorbitant tolls and gridlock.
Last Thursday, Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government, having sold off Ontario Place for a private, foreign-owned spa on prime public land, watched as the urban forest of mature trees on the site was turned into wood chips to make way for the plans. Earlier this year, Ford paid off another private consortium (The Beer Store) to get out of a contract a year before it was to expire just to get beer into corner stores. Last year, instead of using taxes paid by Ontarians to improve public health care, Ford decided to promote private health care by encouraging use of for-profit clinics in the province.
Is it just me that feels that every time we give the Conservatives the keys to our province they insist on selling it off to private interests and stick it to the taxpayers who should have access to, control over and benefit from these assets?
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
Save the Jays
Shapiro doesn't even talk a good game. The Blue Jays are a disaster. And the boss has to go: DiManno, Oct. 2
President Mark Shapiro and General Manager Ross Atkins have had ten years at the helm and have proven they know nothing about building a playoff-contending baseball team. Shapiro should show a bit of dignity and veer off into construction management as renovating the baseball stadium seems to be his favourite thing. Atkins, on the other hand, has proven a deft hand at getting everything wrong at trades, purchases and building a strong minor league pipeline. I've given up. I no longer read about the team and will not watch the games on TV. It's all such a disappointment.
Ray Harvey, Vancouver, B.C.
If you watched the Blue Jays this year you would have seen they were a sombre bunch most of the time. The management team refused to make the required signings or trades and instead focused on a revised stadium. The day will come when they will no longer find it easy to sell tickets. Just because the Maple Leafs can sell out with a losing team doesn't mean the Jays will always be able to. Fans are fickle, not stupid. Rogers better wake up before all the money is spent on empty seats. Get rid of President Mark Shapiro and General Manager Ross Atkins, the two main problems.
Alan Rossell, Windsor
President Mark Shapiro and General Manager Ross Atkins have done precious little during their tenure except offer a lot of bafflegab and put some expensive lip stick on the Rogers Centre pig. When is Edward Rogers going to come to the realization that it is past time to send the not-so dynamic duo packing? It looks now like it might be up to the fans to get his attention and force his hand by refusing to fill the seats, purchase the merch and consume the overpriced food and drink. Cleveland seems to have done quite well without the pair. Toronto will too.
Bill Newman, Aurora
Now we watch the Raptors
What will the Toronto Raptors be this season? Here's the good, the bad and the unknown, Sept. 30
Columnist Doug Smith says that no one can foretell how the Raptors' season will unfold, and just how well or not the team will fit together this season. He claims this is cause for excitement. At best, fans can only feel trepidation. Current management is showing it may have only struck gold once and that a winning formula/combination may never occur again. When I played competitive sports, real excitement was when I stepped on the floor, field or ice knowing I was a member of a strong team that expected to win every time. Let's have that in Toronto sports!
Norm Ferguson, Richmond Hill, Ont.
CAPTION: Traffic jams on Highway 401 are no joke.

Subject: Automobiles; Consortia; Tolls; Traffic congestion

People: Shapiro, Mark

Title: We don't need tolls on Highway 401. We need to take back Highway 407

Document 109 of 500

Stronach to appear in Brampton court: Billionaire businessman stands accused of sexual assault by 13 women over a 50-year span

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stronach-appear-brampton-court/docview/3113724748/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stronach+to+appear+in+Brampton+court%3A+Billionaire+businessman+stands+accused+of+sexual+assault+by+13+women+over+a+50-year+span&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Frank Stronach will commence with his defence against the sexual assault charges that he is facing on Monday.
The billionaire businessman and founder of Magna International has been accused of sexually assaulting over a dozen women over the past 50 years and is scheduled to make his first appearance to face the charges in a Brampton Courthouse Monday morning. He may appear in court or just send a legal representative.
Stronach, 92, is facing allegations from 13 alleged victims, totalling 18 charges, many of them for historical sex assaults, that took place from 1977 to 2023.
Stronach has denied the allegations and none of the charges have been tested in court.
Stronach was initially charged in early June, by three accusers, that resulted in five charges, and authorities asked for more witnesses and more victims. Later that month, Peel Police added eight additional charges from seven more accusers. Last week, three additional victims and five more charges were laid against Stronach.
The charges include sexual assault, indecent assault, rape and forcible confinement. Because some are historical sexual assaults, they involve laws that no longer exist or have changed since the alleged incidents occurred.
"He was my abductor and rapist, and I feared for my life," is what one victim exclusively told the Star earlier this summer, of the alleged 1980 rape.
She claims he sexually assaulted her when she was employed as a worker at his Aurora farm, when she was 20 years old. The victim told the Star she reported the assault to the Toronto police in 2015, but little action was taken at the time.
"We have a lot of data which totally will prove those things are lies," Stronach said in a CBC Interview in August.
Stronach was born in Austria in 1932, and emigrated to Canada in 1954 with only a few hundred dollars. He arrived in Montreal before eventually settling in Ontario, and proceeding on his path to becoming one of Canada's richest men.
In 1958, he started Multimatic Investments, which eventually merged with Magna Electronics in 1969. The name was changed to Magna International in 1973, and by the 1990s, it grew into one of the largest auto parts manufacturers in the world. He was at the head of the company for decades and it led to him being considered a titan in the Canadian business world. Stronach was inducted the Canadian Business Hall of Fame in 1996. He also briefly wrote freelance opinion columns for the Star.
Stronach was bought out of his role in Magna at 2010, and, according to company spokespeople, has had no affiliation with the company since then. Despite that, Magna recently announced an internal probe into the company's conduct in the wake of the sexual assault charges.
His children and grandchildren have been involved in a long simmering dispute over the dispersal of assets of the family's fortune. This summer, his granddaughter, Selena Stronach, asked the court to compel The Stronach Group to disclose any documents relating to any settlements or payouts related to his sexual misconduct, but the court denied the request.
Throughout his life, Stronach has dabbled in different interests, like politics, where he ran as a candidate for the Liberals for the riding of York-Simcoe in 1988, but lost. He's also become more involved with business and politics in his native Austria, including starting a political party there in the 2010s. He also has been involved in thoroughbred horse racing.
CREDIT: Raju Mudhar Toronto Star

Subject: Sex crimes; Rape; Court hearings & proceedings; Disputes
