Document 110 of 500

Poilievre's ethnic media charm offensive: Conservative leader's strategy aims to reach diverse voters in Liberal-dominated areas like GTA

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievres-ethnic-media-charm-offensive/docview/3113724745/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre%27s+ethnic+media+charm+offensive%3A+Conservative+leader%27s+strategy+aims+to+reach+diverse+voters+in+Liberal-dominated+areas+like+GTA&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: He's railed against mainstream media he says is "bought-and-paid-for" by the Trudeau government. He routinely bickers with a parliamentary press gallery he accuses of being biased.
But on a warm June evening at his Stornoway residence in Ottawa, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was on a media charm offensive.
"We're meeting here today so that we can honour this incredible group of independent media that tell the stories of our communities and hold governments accountable," Poilievre, joined by about a dozen MPs and his wife Ana, said to an audience. "I told my team that I wanted to bring together the independent voices in the media, not those that are controlled by the government or the big corporations, that are controlled by Bell or Shaw or Rogers or some other big conglomerate connected to the government, but the independent and diverse voices that tell the real story."
That audience - journalists from so-called "ethnic" or third-language media whose readers and viewers come from various cultural communities in Canada - could be a pathway to political gold for the Conservative leader.
It's one feature of Poilievre's strategy to reach diverse voters in Liberal-dominated areas like the Greater Toronto Area and the Vancouver-Surrey region.
In that "informal" garden party gathering, Poilievre spent between 10 to 20 minutes speaking one-on-one with each invitee.
"I could see him absorbing a lot of information," said Darshan Maharaja, a political commentator who attended the event. "Maybe other leaders have done this, but the mileage that I saw there was tremendous."
Since taking leadership of the Conservative party in 2022, Poilievre has tried to make inroads in cultural communities by routinely speaking to ethnic media across Canada, in stark contrast with his frequently stated disdain for the mainstream press.
As major news networks' audiences shrink and more than 500 local papers have shut down since 2008, there are still at least 1,200 ethnic media publishers across Canada, according to the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada, although many of them switched to digital-only over the pandemic. As immigrants become a growing proportion of Canada's population, the audiences of ethnic media represent a vital target for all political parties.
On his cross-country tour this summer, Poilievre did more than 50 interviews and roundtables with local and ethnic media, his office told the Star, although it did not differentiate between the two.
The Star watched five such ethnic media roundtables and two interviews by the Conservative leader in recent months, and spoke to some of the journalists who took part and former Conservative insiders to understand what they reveal about Poilievre and his strategy.
The interviews and press conferences vary in duration but can include dozens of journalists from a wide range of groups in Canada - Punjabis, Ukrainians, Portuguese and more. In those appearances, Poilievre repeated talking points on affordability and crime, but also gave detailed answers to questions related to specific communities in Canada, delving more deeply into his views on cross-cultural relations across the country and his visions for immigration and foreign policy.
He vigorously defended Canadian immigrants and international students who he said are feeling the brunt of a mishandled system under the Trudeau government, but said his immigration policy would shift greater focus on high-skilled workers and crack down on fraudulent asylum claims.
He received several questions about the Indian government's treatment of Sikhs and its alleged foreign interference, in particular at a Punjabi Press Club of B.C. event in Surrey in August, and declared in several roundtables he's the only political leader fighting for a direct flight to Amritsar, a city in Punjab, India - a commitment the prime minister made to Sikh Canadians this spring, according to one media report.
"I would encourage the community to tell us where and when you need us," Poilievre told a journalist who asked about his engagement with Black Canadians at a Mississauga roundtable this month. "I will be there personally to show up for the events, the meetings and the engagements that matter most to you."
To another question on Hinduphobia in Canada, he said, "I just detest how the prime minister has divided our people."
"You know, we were getting along in this country. Now look - everybody's fighting," he said.
That Poilievre and the Conservatives are making this effort is not new. Jason Kenney, the minister for immigration and multiculturalism in Stephen Harper's government, was well-known for his success in courting newcomers - efforts that were widely seen, and credited by Harper himself, as vital to the Conservatives' 2011 majority victory.
But since Trudeau came to power in 2015, no Conservative leader had emulated that success, said Maharaja.
Poilievre, unlike past Conservative leaders, has taken it upon himself to lead that effort now, being present in multicultural communities - even before an election campaign - rather than delegating outreach to others around him.
That studies following the 2011 election later found that immigrant voters did not flock to the Conservatives in as large numbers as initially thought only goes to show how well Poilievre is doing. A Leger poll for OMNI news this week found the Tories are now the most popular political party among immigrants, with 44 per cent of respondents saying they would cast their ballot for Poilievre's Conservatives if an election were held today, compared to 26 per cent for the Liberals and 19 per cent for the NDP. The Conservatives and Liberals were, however, tied at 37 per cent apiece among newcomers who have been in Canada for less than six years.
Over the summer, the Tory leader appeared to spend more time in the GTA than both Trudeau and the NDP's Jagmeet Singh, visiting several cultural events like a Tamil festival in Scarborough and a centre for newcomers in North York. He is reaching out to a wider range of cultural groups than are the Liberals, according to a senior Liberal who is engaged in the governing party's efforts to reach the same communities.
Compared to past Tory leaders, "Pierre's style is a lot more intimate," Maharaja, an Indo-Canadian accountant who has written about politics for several years, told the Star.
The Tories have faced criticism in the past for targeted messaging and advertising in ethnic media that was controversial or misleading, or that spun a message that was different than that conveyed in mainstream media channels in English and French. In the 2015 election campaign, for example, the Tories dumped one GTA candidate after he wrote a homophobic editorial in a Punjabi newspaper.
Former Tory insiders who spoke to the Star said Poilievre's initiative in those settings shows message discipline and audience awareness that highlight his ability as a communicator and his desire to make inroads with those communities that have typically voted Liberal.
"He's building on what Conservative leaders have been trying to do for the last 20 years, which is to make many of these community members realize that they are actually small-c Conservatives," said Dimitri Soudas, a former director of communications for Harper. "Obviously, there's no absolute value to anything, but a good majority of those communities, regardless of where they come from in the world, believe in free enterprise, believe in freedom, low taxes and so on."
It also reflects a changing media landscape and a political environment that is becoming increasingly hostile to the Trudeau government.
Nitin Chopra, a journalist who has covered politics for a South Asian audience in Ontario since 2016, said Poilievre's message is resonating in many communities that have typically voted Liberal because it relates to issues people are concerned about, such as housing, immigration and crime.
Still, he wonders if this level of engagement will remain if Poilievre's Conservatives take power.
Some ethnic media journalists, many of whom run their own media channels where they are the reporter, the businessmen and the garbage men at the same time, can occasionally be explicit in their approval of Poilievre's message.
In one such press conference this summer, one journalist referred to a smiling Poilievre as the "future of Canada" and the "future prime minister."
"At the end of the day, this is part of the reality, and we as Conservatives, oftentimes feel, sometimes rightfully so ... that some traditional media are out to get us," Soudas said, referencing a recent CTV news story that altered Poilievre's remarks due to what it said was a "misunderstanding during the editing process." The Conservative leader vowed to blacklist CTV journalists when the editing error was revealed. Two journalists involved are no longer with the broadcaster, CTV said, and the broadcaster has apologized.
"I mean, you find me one politician that doesn't want ... the media outlet or the reporter to say, 'my goodness, you're the next prime minister,' " joked Soudas.
With files from Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Since taking over as leader of the federal Conservative party in 2022, Pierre Poilievre has tried to make inroads in cultural communities by routinely speaking to ethnic media across Canada, in stark contrast with his frequently stated disdain for the mainstream press.PATRICK DOYLE THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star With files from Tonda MacCharles

Subject: Journalism; Political leadership; Political parties; Community; Voters; Political advertising; Skepticism; Polls & surveys; Federal elections-Canada; Multiculturalism & pluralism; Media; Interviews

Maybe, but addicted drivers would find other secondary routes to congest and large commercial vehicles would chalk it up as just another increased cost of doing business. Michael Bines, Toronto
The government needs to "buy back" Highway 407, keep tolls on it and move truck traffic off Highway 401 and onto the 407. This would then eliminate bumper-to-bumper traffic on the 401 and reinstate it as an automobile freeway. It's a win-win solution with no digging required.
Belinda Hayes, Toronto
Whenever we talk about congestion and its corresponding frustrations, we usually forget why it exists at all. Some espouse congestion fees or road tolls to fix it, but this ignores the simple fact that in a city there are a lot of people who want to go somewhere and the easiest way to get there is usually the car. Paul Graham, Mississauga
Why are Tories always sticking it to Ontarians?
In 1999, Mike Harris' Progressive Conservative party sold Ontarians down the river by signing away Highway 407 on a 99-year lease to a private consortium. In the quarter-century since, Ontarians have looked longingly at this asset, which has expanded and runs across an increasingly busy part of the province, as it makes money for foreign shareholders, and costs the people of Ontario dearly in access to transportation options, exorbitant tolls and gridlock.
Last Thursday, Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government, having sold off Ontario Place for a private, foreign-owned spa on prime public land, watched as the urban forest of mature trees on the site was turned into wood chips to make way for the plans.
Earlier this year, Ford paid off another private consortium (the Beer Store) to get out of a contract a year before it was to expire just to get beer into corner stores.
Last year, instead of using taxes paid by Ontarians to improve public health care, Ford decided to promote private health care by encouraging use of for-profit clinics in the province.
Is it just me that feels that every time we give the Conservatives the keys to our province they insist on selling it off to private interests and stick it to the taxpayers who should have access to, control over and benefit from these assets?
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto
Save the Jays from the people in the front office
Shapiro doesn't even talk a good game. The Blue Jays are a disaster. And the boss has to go: DiManno, Oct. 2
President Mark Shapiro and general manager Ross Atkins have had 10 years at the helm and have proven they know nothing about building a playoff-contending baseball team. Shapiro should show a bit of dignity and veer off into construction management as renovating the baseball stadium seems to be his favourite thing. Atkins, on the other hand, has proven a deft hand at getting everything wrong at trades, purchases and building a strong minor league pipeline.
I've given up. I no longer read about the team and will not watch the games on TV. It's all such a disappointment.
Ray Harvey, Vancouver
If you watched the Blue Jays this year, you would have seen they were a sombre bunch most of the time.
The management team refused to make the required signings or trades and instead focused on a revised stadium. The day will come when they will no longer find it easy to sell tickets.
Just because the Maple Leafs can sell out with a losing team doesn't mean the Jays will always be able to.
Fans are fickle, not stupid. Rogers better wake up before all the money is spent on empty seats. Get rid of president Mark Shapiro and general manager Ross Atkins, the two main problems.
Alan Rossell, Windsor
President Mark Shapiro and general manager Ross Atkins have done precious little during their tenure except offer a lot of bafflegab and put some expensive lip stick on the Rogers Centre pig.
When is Edward Rogers going to come to the realization that it is past time to send the not-so dynamic duo packing? It looks now like it might be up to the fans to get his attention and force his hand by refusing to fill the seats, purchase the merch and consume the overpriced food and drink.
Cleveland seems to have done quite well without the pair. Toronto will, too.
Bill Newman, Aurora
Now we watch the Raptors
What will the Toronto Raptors be this season? Here's the good, the bad and the
unknown, Sept. 30
Columnist Doug Smith says that no one can foretell how the Raptors' season will unfold, and just how well or not the team will fit together this season.
He claims this is cause for excitement.
At best, fans can only feel trepidation.
Current management is showing it may have only struck gold once and that a winning formula/combination may never occur again. When I played competitive sports, real excitement was when I stepped on the floor, field or ice knowing I was a member of a strong team that expected to win every time. Let's have that in Toronto sports!
Norm Ferguson,
Richmond Hill

Document 112 of 500

Is Canada finally ready to build high-speed rail?: Key decision expected soon on who'll lead project

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-canada-finally-ready-build-high-speed-rail/docview/3113724694/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Canada+finally+ready+to+build+high-speed+rail%3F%3A+Key+decision+expected+soon+on+who%27ll+lead+project&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A key stop on the track to better passenger rail service that the Liberal government has promised is just around the bend - and with it comes a multibillion-dollar question that's still without an answer: If it really gets built, how fast will the trains go?
In the coming weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's cabinet is expected to choose which of three groups of private companies will get to help design and build a new passenger rail project that proponents bill as a game-changer for travel through the most populated part of the country. Running 1,000 kilometres from Toronto to Quebec City, the promised rail corridor is meant to slash journey times between major cities and could bring Canada - many say belatedly - into the modern era of high-speed rail travel.
"We are looking at all options," said Transport Minister Anita Anand, when asked whether the project will bring a dedicated line of trains that travel faster than 200 km/h, the standard definition of high-speed rail (HSR).
Vance Badawey, a Liberal MP who is Anand's parliamentary secretary, said the government is studying which areas might be best served by faster trains, and which could still rely on slower - but more frequent - rail service.
But, like Anand, he said the government has not yet decided what to do.
At an office at the federal Transport Department in downtown Ottawa this week, Martin Imbleau expressed high hopes for the project. As chief executive of the Crown corporation created to advance the project, Imbleau shared a vision for rail travel from Toronto to Quebec that rivals European countries like France.
That country's Train à Grande Vitesse system has successfully reduced travel times between cities over four decades of operation, Imbleau said, even though routes like the one from Paris to Amsterdam include stretches where trains run slower than 200 km/h.
He suggested the new rail project could look something like that: a corridor with trains that slow down when necessary, like when entering an urban centre, and speed up beyond 200 km/h along other parts of the route.
The idea now is to have a single new corridor with stops in Toronto, Peterborough, Ottawa, Laval, Montreal, Trois-Rivières and Quebec City.
But the final shape depends on which private group the government chooses, and how that group works with Imbleau's Crown corporation to design and build the project, he said, adding he expects the decision in the coming weeks. The three groups include more than 20 companies, with the likes of Air Canada, national rail companies from France, Spain and Germany, and British infrastructure investment firm John Laing.
Each group was asked to make two proposals to the government: one for trains that travel less than 200 km/h, and one for trains that travel faster. And, regardless of who wins the project, the designers will be allowed to pull from all the proposals to "mix and match" and meet their goals of faster, more frequent and reliable passenger service, Imbleau said.
Depending on what happens, he predicted train travel between Montreal and Toronto could be reduced to little more than three hours, compared to the current reality of more than five.
"We're really looking at reducing the journey time with those three factors," Imbleau said. "How fast remains to be seen."
The Liberals first floated the project more than five years ago as "high frequency rail."
The idea was to build new tracks dedicated exclusively to electrified passenger trains that would run more frequently. That way, passenger service wouldn't run on tracks owned by Canadian National and Canadian Pacific, whose freight traffic is prioritized over Via Rail trains carrying people, resulting in delays that drag out the time it takes to travel by rail.
But as one government source explained, the vision for the project shifted toward high speed rail, despite the "conventional wisdom" at Via and within the public service "just to do it at regular speed."
Part of that was a perception of public appetite, the source said. A public opinion survey completed in August for Imbleau's Crown corporation found a majority of respondents - 58 per cent of those from Quebec and 57 per cent from Ontario - said they preferred a faster but more expensive train over a slower, less costly option.
"Everybody wants high speed," said the source, who agreed to speak on condition of anonymity.
Of course, HSR is more expensive to build, in part because it requires special infrastructure to make it safe to travel at high speeds, like grade separation.
In 2021, then transport minister Omar Alghabra floated a rough cost estimate for the project at between $6 billion and $12 billion.
However, the real cost might actually be more than double that, the government source said, depending on how much of the project is built for high speed, where the tracks end up getting built, and because of factors like inflation that have driven up construction costs in recent years.
A 2017 assessment of a possible "ultra-high speed" rail project from Portland, Ore., to Vancouver estimated such a project - with up to 800 kilometres of track - could cost between $32 billion and $57 billion.
"The truth of the matter is the cost is going to go up," the source said.
Imbleau told the Star it's too early to talk about costs, since they still don't know exactly what they're going to build. But he stressed that the rail project would ultimately bring in revenue from ticket sales.
"If it costs more but it brings in more value, maybe there's a business case there," he said, adding that he sees the project as an "investment" to help Canada catch up with other countries that have had rapid rail service for decades. And, although he noted there are still many uncertainties, he predicted a first section of the rail line could be finished within about 12 years.
"I will let the government make their decision, and I will respect that," Imbleau said. "What we're developing is a project that is required, and it's an investment for future generations."
Paul Langan, a longtime advocate for HSR in Canada, said the project appears to be veering toward high speed.
"Just how high is the question," he said.
His big concern is, given the political situation that finds the governing Liberals in a minority Parliament and trailing badly in the polls, what will happen to the project if a new party - namely the Conservatives - takes power after the next election.
In an emailed statement this week, Conservative MP and transport critic Philip Lawrence did not say what his party would do with the rail project if it came to power. Instead, he said the public should doubt whether the Liberals will actually get it built.
"Like every photo op and every big promise from Justin Trudeau, Canadians can't trust that he will deliver on anything," the statement said.
According to the government source, the proposals from the private groups are in the "final stages" of assessment by the public service, and cabinet could decide by November which one is going to take part in the project.
"It was a big mistake we didn't do this 30 years ago," the source said.
"These types of project are, in my opinion, non-negotiable," the source added.
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star

Document 113 of 500

Pierre Poilievre has a plan to attract very specific voters. Here's how he is doing it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievre-has-plan-attract-very-specific/docview/3113527248/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre+has+a+plan+to+attract+very+specific+voters.+Here%27s+how+he+is+doing+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-He's railed against mainstream media he says is "bought-and-paid-for" by the Trudeau government. He routinely bickers with a parliamentary press gallery he accuses of being biased.
But on a warm June evening at his Stornoway residence in Ottawa, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was on a media charm offensive.
"We're meeting here today so that we can honour this incredible group of independent media that tell the stories of our communities and hold governments accountable," Poilievre, joined by about a dozen MPs and his wife Ana, said to an audience. "I told my team that I wanted to bring together the independent voices in the media, not those that are controlled by the government or the big corporations, that are controlled by Bell or Shaw or Rogers or some other big conglomerate connected to the government, but the independent and diverse voices that tell the real story."
That audience - journalists from so-called "ethnic" or third-language media whose readers and viewers come from various cultural communities in Canada - could be a pathway to political gold for the Conservative leader.
It's one feature of Poilievre's strategy to reach diverse voters in Liberal dominated areas like the Greater Toronto Area and the Vancouver-Surrey region.
In that "informal" garden party gathering, Poilievre spent between 10 to 20 minutes speaking one-on-one with each invitee.
"I could see him absorbing a lot of information," said Darshan Maharaja, a political commentator who attended the event.
"Maybe other leaders have done this, but the mileage that I saw there was tremendous."
Since taking leadership of the Conservative Party in 2022, Poilievre has tried to make inroads in cultural communities by routinely speaking to ethnic media across Canada, in stark contrast with his frequently-stated disdain for the mainstream press.
As major news networks' audiences shrink and more than 500 local papers have shut down since 2008, there are still at least 1,200 ethnic media publishers across Canada, according to the National Ethnic Press and Media Council of Canada, although many of them switched to digital-only over the pandemic. As immigrants become a growing proportion of Canada's population, the audiences of ethnic media represent a vital target for all political parties.
On his cross-country tour this summer, Poilievre did more than 50 interviews and roundtables with local and ethnic media, his office told the Star, although it did not differentiate between the two.
The Star watched five such ethnic media roundtables and two interviews by the Conservative leader in recent months, and spoke to some of the journalists who took part and former Conservative insiders to understand what they reveal about Poilievre and his strategy.
The interviews and press conferences vary in duration but can include dozens of journalists from a wide range of groups in Canada - Punjabis, Ukrainians, Portuguese and more. In those appearances, Poilievre repeated talking points on affordability and crime, but also gave detailed answers to questions related to specific communities in Canada, delving more deeply into his views on cross-cultural relations across the country and his visions for immigration and foreign policy.
He vigorously defended Canadian immigrants and international students who he said are feeling the brunt of a mishandled system under the Trudeau government, but said his immigration policy would shift greater focus on high-skilled workers and crack down on fraudulent asylum claims.
He received several questions about the Indian government's treatment of Sikhs and its alleged foreign interference, in particular at a Punjabi Press Club of B.C. event in Surrey in August, and declared in several roundtables he's the only political leader fighting for a direct flight to Amritsar, a city in Punjab, India - a commitment the prime minister made to Sikh Canadians this spring, according to one media report.
"I would encourage the community to tell us where and when you need us," Poilievre told a journalist who asked about his engagement with Black Canadians at a Mississauga roundtable earlier this month. "I will be there personally to show up for the events, the meetings and the engagements that matter most to you."
To another question on Hinduphobia in Canada, he said, "I just detest how the prime minister has divided our people."
"You know, we were getting along in this country. Now look - everybody's fighting," he said.
That Poilievre and the Conservatives are making this effort is not new. Jason Kenney, the minister for immigration and multiculturalism in Stephen Harper's government, was well-known for his success in courting newcomers - efforts that were widely seen, and credited by Harper himself, as vital to the Conservatives' 2011 majority victory.
But since Trudeau came to power in 2015, no Conservative leader had emulated that success, said Maharaja.
Poilievre, unlike past Conservative leaders, has taken it upon himself to lead that effort now, being present in multicultural communities - even before an election campaign - rather than delegating outreach to others around him.
That studies following the 2011 election later found that immigrant voters did not flock to the Conservatives in as large numbers as initially thought only goes to show how well Poilievre is doing. A Leger poll for OMNI news this week found the Tories are now the most popular political party among immigrants, with 44 per cent of respondents saying they would cast their ballot for Poilievre's Conservatives if an election were held today, compared to 26 per cent for the Liberals and 19 per cent for the NDP. The Conservatives and Liberals were, however, tied at 37 per cent apiece among newcomers who have been in Canada for less than six years.
Over the summer, the Tory leader appeared to spend more time in the GTA than both Trudeau and the NDP's Jagmeet Singh, visiting several cultural events like a Tamil festival in Scarborough and a centre for newcomers in North York. He is reaching out to a wider range of cultural groups than are the Liberals, according to a senior Liberal who is engaged in the governing party's efforts to reach the same communities.
Compared to past Tory leaders, "Pierre's style is a lot more intimate," Maharaja, an Indo-Canadian accountant who has written about politics for several years, told the Star.
The Tories have faced criticism in the past for targeted messaging and advertising in ethnic media that was controversial or misleading, or that spun a message that was different than that conveyed in mainstream media channels in English and French. In the 2015 election campaign, for example, the Tories dumped one GTA candidate after he wrote a homophobic editorial in a Punjabi newspaper.
Former Tory insiders who spoke to the Star said Poilievre's initiative in those settings shows message discipline and audience awareness that highlight his ability as a communicator and his desire to make inroads with those communities that have typically voted Liberal.
"He's building on what Conservative leaders have been trying to do for the last 20 years, which is to make many of these community members realize that they are actually small-c Conservatives," said Dimitri Soudas, a former director of communications for Harper. "Obviously, there's no absolute value to anything, but a good majority of those communities, regardless of where they come from in the world, believe in free enterprise, believe in freedom, low taxes and so on."
It also reflects a changing media landscape and a political environment that is becoming increasingly hostile to the Trudeau government.
Nitin Chopra, a journalist who has covered politics for a South Asian audience in Ontario since 2016, said Poilievre's message is resonating in many communities that have typically voted Liberal because it relates to issues people are concerned about, such as housing, immigration and crime.
Still, he wonders if this level of engagement will remain if Poilievre's Conservatives take power.
Some ethnic media journalists, many of whom run their own media channels where they are the reporter, the businessmen and the garbage men at the same time, can occasionally be explicit in their approval of Poilievre's message.
In one such press conference this summer, one journalist referred to a smiling Poilievre as the "future of Canada" and the "future prime minister."
"At the end of the day, this is part of the reality, and we as Conservatives, oftentimes feel, sometimes rightfully so ... that some traditional media are out to get us," Soudas said, referencing a recent CTV news story that altered Poilievre's remarks due to what it said was a "misunderstanding during the editing process." The Conservative leader vowed to blacklist CTV journalists when the editing error was revealed. Two journalists involved are no longer with the broadcaster, CTV said, and the broadcaster has apologized.
"I mean, you find me one politician that doesn't want ... the media outlet or the reporter to say, 'my goodness, you're the next prime minister,'" joked Soudas.
Independent media, which is crucial to the country's diversity and serves its smaller communities, often doesn't have the business scale, resources or audience size to hold powerful federal and provincial political leaders to account.
Back in 2021, Maharaja had sharply criticized the Conservatives, arguing in a blog piece that the party had failed to make inroads with many cultural communities because of insufficient outreach to ethnic media.
He contended that if the Conservatives "wish to improve their electoral prospects in the backdrop of an electorate that is increasingly foreign-born and not native speaker of English, they must improve their ethnic media outreach, otherwise their messaging would just amount to sounds of silence," which would be "a fatal flaw" in the Conservatives' communications strategy, he wrote.
Now, he said in an interview, it's clear the Conservative party, which leads all major polls under Poilievre, has taken that advice.
With files from Tonda MacCharles
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre rises during question period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Oct. 3, 2024.

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Document 114 of 500

Frank Stronach to face sex assault charges in Brampton court on Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/frank-stronach-face-sex-assault-charges-brampton/docview/3113527095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Frank+Stronach+to+face+sex+assault+charges+in+Brampton+court+on+Monday&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Frank Stronach will commence with his defence against the sexual assault charges that he is facing on Monday.
The billionaire businessman and founder of Magna International has been accused of sexually assaulting over a dozen women over the past 50 years and is scheduled to make his first appearance to face the charges in a Brampton Courthouse Monday morning. He may appear in court or just send a legal representative.
Stronach, 92, is facing allegations from 13 alleged victims, totalling 18 charges, many of them for historical sex assaults, that took place from 1977 to 2023.
Stronach has denied the allegations and none of the charges have been tested in court.
Stronach was initially charged in early June, by three accusers, that resulted in five charges, and authorities asked for more witnesses and more victims. Later that month, Peel Police added eight additional charges from seven more accusers. Last week, three additional victims and five more charges were laid against Stronach.
The charges include sexual assault, indecent assault, rape and forcible confinement. Because some are historical sexual assaults, they involve laws that no longer exist or have changed since the alleged incidents occurred.
"He was my abductor and rapist, and I feared for my life," is what one victim exclusively told The Star earlier this summer, of the alleged 1980 rape.
She claims he sexually assaulted her when she was employed as a worker at his Aurora farm, when she was 20 years old. The victim told the Star she reported the assault to the Toronto police in 2015, but little action was taken at the time.
"We have a lot of data which totally will prove those things are lies," Stronach said in a CBC Interview in August.
Stronach was born in Austria in 1932, and emigrated to Canada in 1954 with only a few hundred dollars. He arrived in Montreal before eventually settling in Ontario, and proceeding on his path to becoming one of Canada's richest men.
In 1958, he started Multimatic Investments, which eventually merged with Magna Electronics in 1969. The name was changed to Magna International in 1973, and by the 1990s, it grew into one of the largest auto parts manufacturers in the world. He was at the head of the company for decades and it led to him being considered a titan in the Canadian business world. Stronach was inducted the Canadian Business Hall of Fame in 1996. He also briefly wrote freelance opinion columns for the Star.
Stronach was bought out of his role in Magna at 2010, and, according to company spokespeople, has had no affiliation with the company since then. Despite that, Magna recently announced an internal probe into the company's conduct in the wake of the sexual assault charges.
His children and grandchildren have been involved in a long simmering dispute over the dispersal of assets of the family's fortune. This summer, his granddaughter, Selena Stronach, asked the court to compel The Stronach Group to disclose any documents relating to any settlements or payouts related to his sexual misconduct, but the court denied the request.
Throughout his life, Stronach has dabbled in different interests, like politics, where he ran as a candidate for the Liberals for the riding of York-Simcoe in 1988, but lost. He's also become more involved with business and politics in his native Austria, including starting a political party there in the 2010s. He also has been involved in thoroughbred horse racing.
CAPTION: Billionaire businessman Frank Stronach leaves Peel Regional Police 22 Division in Brampton, July 2, 2024.Frank Stronach is facing new sexual assault charges involving additional victims and is set to appear in Brampton Court on Oct. 7.

Document 115 of 500

Money for Ford's tunnel would be much better spent on health care, schools

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/money-fords-tunnel-would-be-much-better-spent-on/docview/3113119927/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Money+for+Ford%27s+tunnel+would+be+much+better+spent+on+health+care%2C+schools&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford should listen to Elliott
Ford's 401 tunnel fantasy is a distraction from what Ontario should be spending money on: Elliott, Oct. 1
Contributor Matt Elliot's recent article listed many services and programs that are currently underfunded, including nurses, family doctors, hospitals, teachers, educational workers, schools in need of repairs, mental health and addiction services, support for children with complex needs, and colleges and universities. Pick any government service and you can see a need for more funding. Elliott makes the argument that the money proposed for the Highway 401 tunnel would be much better spent on these underfunded services. Government spokespersons insist funding for these services has increased in recent years. This may be true though the increased funding is nowhere near enough. Perhaps Ford could bring Elliott onto his team to get it done. Now that would be common sense.
Rick Foren, Ajax
The column mentioned the tunnel money may be better used towards the installation of subway platform edge doors. Toronto Public Health and CAMH studies have suggested they could reduce subway suicides by 89 per cent. The cost would be $4.1 billion. Before such a spend, I would like to see a study of the subway trains reducing speed entering the stations to that of an average walk. They could make up the lost seconds with speed in between the stations. If it saves one life, some might think it worthwhile. If suicides are entirely eliminated, then $4.1 billion was saved.
Jim Skeels, Toronto
Research shows that ventilation in long tunnels is a huge problem, so perhaps only allowing electric vehicles and hydrogen vehicles to use the tunnel would solve the ventilation concern while encouraging the use of those vehicles.
Janet King, Markham, Ont.
Conservatives love simplified messaging, so let's keep it simple. More highways equals more traffic equals more congestion. Paved green space leads to more flooding. A 55-kilometre tunnel under a highway creates a flooding risk, a security threat, and a bottomless money pit. Consider what the people of Ontario really need, which is a modern, efficient transportation system and well-funded public health care. Let's bring fiscal responsibility back in the next election.
Teresa Ganna Porter, Newmarket
Other 'ridiculous' ideas
Further to Premier Doug Ford's ludicrous proposal to build a tunnel under Highway 401, I offer the following equally ridiculous ideas for Ford: relocate the Science Centre to Ontario Place and move Ontario Place to the Science Centre site, change safe injection sites to beer kiosks, change numbers and letters on licence plates to pictures, open beer sales outlets in emergency rooms where patients have hours to kill, close the Gardiner Expressway and use ferries for east-west traffic, build a second parallel tunnel under Highway 401 for pedestrians only, add scenic pullovers in the proposed tunnel, ban pedestrians and bikes from the downtown core, and buy back Highway 407 and sell the 401.
Maurice Sacco, Toronto
I have the perfect name for the tunnel. It should be called Ford's Folly. A folly is something that lacks good sense or normal prudence and foresight. Also, an excessively costly or unprofitable undertaking or the state or quality of being foolish. Thank goodness I don't live in Toronto, yet my taxes will be paying for it.
Terry Middlemiss, Hamilton
We have a duty to preserve
Farewell to the Eastern Avenue Bridge, a bit of public heritage that evoked an industrial past: Micallef, Sept. 29
Reading contributor Shawn Micallef's piece, I was struck by the disregard with which our city is treating the space. The old Eastern Avenue bridge has been neglected by the city, but was well used until the closure of the lower Don trail. Some folks put in work to install a small concrete BMX and skate park, others have made artwork on the deck of the bridge, and a local band recorded a music video there. The city and its agencies have a duty to preserve what is special about our city's neighbourhoods. So far, it has a bad track record; the gardens that made Regent Park special have largely disappeared, and in the spring, the city tried to demolish a community-built skate park in Christie Pits. Each time one of these spaces is threatened, the city loses the trust of engaged and passionate community members.
Liam Nunn, Toronto
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford attends the US-Canada Summit in Toronto on Tuesday, June 11, 2024. Ford has ruled out calling an election in 2024, but has left the door open to calling one in 2025. THE CANADIAN PRESS/Chris Young

Subject: Public health; Skateboarding; Funding; Ventilation; Roads & highways

Document 116 of 500

Architect mourns destruction of tree grove: Crews cut down more than 800 trees without any notice

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/architect-mourns-destruction-tree-grove/docview/3113047009/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Architect+mourns+destruction+of+tree+grove%3A+Crews+cut+down+more+than+800+trees+without+any+notice&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government's quick destruction of more than 800 trees on the West Island of Ontario Place is "shameful," says a prominent landscape architect who quit the controversial spa-focused redevelopment project.
"It is shocking to me that we are destroying our own unique, sensitive habitat that was home to coyotes, fox, raccoons and birds," Walter Kehm, known for designing the Trillium and Tommy Thompson parks, told the Star Friday. "We've lost our cathedral grove that was overlooking the lake, where you could see the sunset through the trees - it was magical."
Crews with chainsaws and backhoes apparently started the removal operation, with no public notice, on Wednesday evening, shocking many people who woke up Thursday to see much of the miniforest reduced to stumps and brush. By Friday afternoon it was a waste removal operation.
A video posted on social media Thursday showed birds circling above a tangle of felled 20-metre Norway spruce trees, as well as oak, green ash and birch, while a backhoe dumped debris into a dumpster.
The thicket was cleared to make way for a towering spa resort and waterpark to be built near the water's edge by Austrian company Therme, a centrepiece of the Premier Doug Ford government's remake of the site that was home to a provincial theme park that closed in 2012.
On Thursday, taking reporters' questions while making public the province's 95-year lease deal with Therme, Infrastructure Ontario chief executive Michael Lindsay defended the deforestation, saying it was timed to avoid bird and bat nesting times. The lost trees will be replaced by many more, including by a six-to-one ratio for the mature ones, he said.
Kehm resigned in 2023 as a senior principal at Toronto-based LANDinc, one of two firms under contract to help design and construct the new Ontario Place's "public realm," citing opposition to the deforestation plan.
He is accusing the Ford government of making the lease public on Thursday to divert media and public attention away from the clear-cut, calling it a cynical move that is typical of the government's approach to the waterfront revamp that will also feature an expanded, year-round Budweiser Stage and a relocated Ontario Science Centre.
Ian McConachie, an Infrastructure Ontario spokesperson, said Friday that site preparation had been underway for a year and that replacement trees will increase biodiversity.
"The province is rebuilding Ontario Place into a world-class, year-round destination with expanded parks and public spaces, increased waterfront access, a new public marina and family-friendly entertainment," he said in an email. "The redeveloped Ontario Place will create thousands of new jobs and attract an estimated four to six million visitors each year."
Advocacy group Ontario Place Protectors filed paperwork for an injunction to stop the tree destruction but, given that the trees are all down, the group "is currently trying to determine if that is still something that should proceed," said lawyer Eric Gillespie.
Ontario Place Protectors has been granted leave to appeal a July court decision that upheld the province's right to redevelop the site without doing a full environmental assessment, with a court date yet to be set, Gillespie said.
The lease details reveal that the Ford government is under pressure to create a huge number of new parking spots within 650 metres of the spa entrance by the time the spa opens or 2030, whichever comes first - or pay significant financial penalties to Therme.
The lease requires creation of spots for 1,800 vehicles but the province says it plans to build 2,500 spaces to accommodate visitors to other site attractions including the Science Centre and parkland.
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Subject: Court decisions; Deforestation; Architects; Redevelopment

Business indexing term: Subject: Architects

Document 118 of 500

Cop shooting renews bail debate: Suspect charged with attempted murder of Toronto officer already facing several theft charges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cop-shooting-renews-bail-debate/docview/3113045721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cop+shooting+renews+bail+debate%3A+Suspect+charged+with+attempted+murder+of+Toronto+officer+already+facing+several+theft+charges&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The police officer who was shot and seriously injured in Toronto's midtown on Wednesday was released from hospital the next day, a spokesperson for the Toronto Police Association confirmed Friday.
The shooting in the Yonge and Eglinton neighbourhood fuelled concerns about a recent spate of Toronto gun violence and recharged political debates about Canada's bail system, given the suspected shooter was apparently out on bail.
It happened at about 5:30 p.m. on Wednesday, when police who were conducting a robbery investigation stopped a vehicle. Two sources with knowledge of the investigation say the plainclothes officer was shot in the abdomen by a man who got out of the vehicle.
Ontario's police watchdog says an officer fired a gun in the underground parking garage where it happened as the suspect fled, but no one was hit.
The suspected shooter was later arrested at an east-end hospital. Police say 21-year-old Tibor Organa faces several charges, including attempted murder, unauthorized possession of a firearm and three counts of robbery.
Two others were also arrested in the robbery investigation, police said, a 22-year-old woman and a 15-year-old.
Court records indicate the suspected shooter had been out on bail after being charged with multiple counts of theft and breach of probation among other offences in King Township last May.
The bail system has repeatedly come under the scrutiny of politicians and law enforcement representatives who levy accusations of overly lenient conditions and releases.
It caught the ire of Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who said during question period in Ottawa on Thursday that the government's "hug-a-thug" system is putting people at risk.
Government House leader Karina Gould said no police officer should be hurt in the line of duty, but Poilievre's comments showed he was "trampling" over Canadians' rights.
Studies and prisoners' rights advocates have been critical of the argument that bail should be more restrictive, saying that erodes a fundamental principle of the justice system - the presumption of innocence.
Nonetheless, after provincial premiers including Ontario's Doug Ford pleaded with the federal government to fix what they deemed a broken system, Ottawa legislated to tighten bail rules late last year. Local authorities are ultimately responsible for managing the system governed by those laws.
Gun violence in Toronto was under the spotlight this week as police responded to two deadly shootings less than 24 hours apart on Monday.
The first nine months of 2024 have already seen more shootings, and more deaths due to firearm violence, than in each of the past three years.
Police Chief Myron Demkiw said the force had upped its presence in neighbourhoods most affected by shootings, and police had made 746 firearm-related arrests so far this year.
He said police data indicate this year's figures are on par with pre-pandemic rates.
CAPTION: A planclothes Toronto police officer was shot while conducting a robbery investigation near Yonge and Eglinton on Wednesday. The officer was released from hospital Thursday.Jennifer Pagliaro Toronto Star
CREDIT: The Canadian Press

Full text: The Ford government's quick destruction of more than 800 trees on the West Island of Ontario Place is "shameful", says a prominent landscape architect who quit the controversial spa-focused redevelopment project.

"The redeveloped Ontario Place will create thousands of new jobs and attract an estimated four to six million visitors each year."

The lease requires creation of spots for 1,800 vehicles but the province says it plans to build 2,500 spaces to accommodate visitors to other site attractions including the Science Centre and parkland.
Ford originally said his government would build an underground lot at Ontario Place. During negotiations with city hall on a new fiscal deal for Toronto he agreed to consider building it, below ground or above, at Exhibition Place.
The city and province will only say that negotiations continue between them but the Star has learned of one option on the table.
Some officials would like to see a three-storey underground lot built on the site of the Better Living Centre, which would be razed and replaced with a 4.5-acre "festival plaza" that could be made appealing for events and the walk to the west bridge to Ontario Place, according to a city hall source not authorized to discuss details of the discussions.
Ford, however, would have to be convinced to spend more to bury the garage rather than building a cheaper above-ground option. The Exhibition Place board has said it will not support an above-ground parkade on the city-owned site.
CAPTION: Crews began removing trees at Ontario Place's West Island on Wednesday.

Document 120 of 500

New details about Ford's Ontario Place plans + high-speed rail finally coming to Canada?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/new-details-about-fords-ontario-place-plans-high/docview/3112846075/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+details+about+Ford%27s+Ontario+Place+plans+%2B+high-speed+rail+finally+coming+to+Canada%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, October 4 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on new details emerging about Ford's plans for Ontario Place, Canada's first high-speed rail line and a teenager charged with first-degree murder.
DON'T MISSThe Ford government dropped new details about its Ontario Place plans
After years of secrecy, more details are emerging about Ontario's 95-year lease with Therme for a towering spa resort at the new site of Ontario Place, David Rider reports. Notably, the contract requires at least 1,600 parking spots for Therme and another 200 for an expanded Budweiser Stage - or taxpayers could be on the hook for millions in damages to the Austrian firm. Take a closer look.
Word from officials: Infrastructure Ontario's Michael Lindsay described the parking provision as "reasonable" and said the government plans to build a total of 2,500 parking spots for visitors to the Science Centre, new parkland and other attractions.Parking plans: Appearing to have ruled out an underground parking lot for being too pricey, Ford floated the idea of a towering, above-ground parkade near the waterfront that some activists have slammed as an "absolute blight at Ontario Place."Canada might finally be ready to build a high-speed rail line

That's how long one transportation executive expects the journey to take, should a new 1,000-kilometre rail corridor between Toronto and Quebec City usher Canada into the modern era of high-speed rail travel, Alex Ballingall reports. But questions are swirling around the project, which is still in its infancy - namely, how fast will the trains actually go?
All aboard: Ottawa is expected to choose between three groups of private companies in the coming weeks to help design and build the project. These include more than 20 businesses, from Air Canada to national rail companies from France, Spain and Germany.Next stop: The idea is to build a single rail corridor with stops at Toronto, Peterborough, Ottawa, Laval, Montreal, Trois-Rivières and Quebec City. A 13-year-old boy has been charged with first-degree murder
Officers were called to a parking lot near Jane Street and St. Clair Avenue West just after midnight Tuesday, where a boy allegedly stabbed a 54-year-old man to death. Life-saving measures were attempted, but the yet-unidentified victim was pronounced dead at the scene. He is Toronto's 67th murder victim this year, Abby O'Brien reports. What's going on?
Context: While not unprecedented, a Star analysis shows it's extremely uncommon for one so young to be charged with murder. Twelve is the youngest age one can be held legally accountable for criminal behaviour. The last time a 13-year-old faced first-degree murder charges was in 2018.The youth murders: Statistics show it's becoming increasingly common for youth to be implicated in murder in Canada. In 2022 alone, 90 youth were accused of homicide - a five-decade record.WHAT ELSE

Document 121 of 500

Ford government falls woefully short of balanced budget promises

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ford-government-falls-woefully-short-balanced/docview/3112845743/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+government+falls+woefully+short+of+balanced+budget+promises&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government recently published Ontario's official public accounts for the 2023/24 fiscal year, which show that the province wound up with a deficit of $647 million. According to Caroline Mulroney, the government's Treasury Board president, this is very good news. "After 15 years of a Liberal government," she said. "I don't think people can even imagine getting this close to a balanced budget."
But in reality, the Ford government has yet again moved the goalposts it uses to measure fiscal success.
Let's first address the suggestion that, when Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government left office in 2018, it was difficult to "imagine" getting close to a balanced budget in 2023/24. While releasing the Ford government's first fiscal update in November 2018, Finance Minister Vic Fedeli spoke passionately about the need to eliminate the deficit and reduce provincial debt. "This government believes balancing the budget and reducing Ontario's debt burden is not only a fiscal imperative," he said, "it is a moral one." That update didn't include a clear path to balance but it - along with campaign rhetoric - gave a clear impression the government sought a quick return to balance.
However, by the spring of 2019 - before the pandemic - the Ford government's urgency had already started to cool and the 2019 budget included a five-year fiscal plan that balanced the budget in 2023/24. So, contrary to Mulroney's claim, a relatively small deficit in 2023/24 doesn't represent a fiscal achievement that would have been hard to "imagine" when the Liberals left office. Rather it's a failure. The Ford government has failed to achieve a budget balance despite the leisurely five-year path laid out in its 2019 budget.
The Ford government now says it will balance the budget by 2026/27, but given past experience it's reasonable to be skeptical, especially if the government encounters any unexpected bumps in the road such as an economic slowdown. And to be clear, the government can't blame COVID because annual provincial revenues have by now recovered from that shock.
And crucially, we're only talking about the government's "operating" deficits, driven by annual expenditures on day-to-day items such as employee salaries and debt interest. When you look at the bigger picture, which includes spending on long-term capital projects, Mulroney's claim grows more dubious. The government's net debt burden this year will reach a projected $408 billion - or 37.4 per cent relative to the size of the provincial economy, down slightly from 39.2 per cent in 2017/18, the Liberal government's final full year in office.
This slight improvement since the Wynne years does not seem to reflect a government that believes debt reduction is a "moral" imperative.
To be sure, it's good news that the deficit for 2023/24 is a little smaller than the government previously thought. However, contrary to claims from Mulroney and others in the Ford government, it's nothing to celebrate. Far from exceeding expectations or simply delivering on its promises, the Ford government has fallen well short of its deficit-reduction pledges. Will they break their new promises and move the goalposts once again? That's not hard to imagine.
Ben Eisen is a senior fellow at the Fraser Institute.
CAPTION: Transportation Minister Caroline Mulroney answers questions at Queen's Park in Toronto as Premier Doug Ford watches on June 29, 2020.
CREDIT: Ben Eisen, Contributor

Subject: Budgets; Debt restructuring

Poilievre's 'common sense' narrative is 'reheated Conservative coffee'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/poilievres-common-sense-narrative-is-reheated/docview/3112845738/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre%27s+%27common+sense%27+narrative+is+%27reheated+Conservative+coffee%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Common sense narrative is familiar
Justin Trudeau dishes with Liberal MP on where he went wrong, what he did right and why he's the one to beat Pierre Poilievre, Oct. 2
We have seen the "Common Sense Revolution" before. It took us by storm and fooled us under former Ontario premier Mike Harris. We are still feeling the effects many years later. Now federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has picked up the same script, using the same language, and if we don't watch out, the results will be the same. Common sense appears to mean looking after the top 20 per cent of income earners. Simple answers and throwing around blame don't cut it in a complicated world. I don't buy "common sense" that prioritizes dollars over people, wants over needs and individuals over society at large.
Peter Rapsey, Toronto
For all those who continually revert to "common sense" to justify their arguments and decisions, remember the point from Dr. Keith Stanovich, one of Canada's leading psychologists: beware today's "common sense" because it is not difficult to show that yesterday's "common sense" often turns into today's "nonsense." When Pierre Poilievre uses the term, he really means Conservative Sense. Let's not be fooled.
John Cousins, Bowmanville, Ont.
With or without glasses and a makeover Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre remains the same right-wing bully he's always been, stoking anger and chaos for his own political gain is his platform and his common sense blather is just reheated Conservative coffee.
Kat Duffy, Mississauga
The next time Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre runs a TV ad trying to look like a normal Canadian building a jigsaw puzzle with his kid, he might consider removing the luxurious and expensive Breitling watch from his wrist. Man of the people? I don't think so.
Malcolm Lowe, Unionville, Ont.
I am so tired of Pierre Poilievre, the leader of the Conservative party, wasting time in the House being a drama king. He picks fights unnecessarily and comes up with gimmicky rhyming and alliterate slogans that he thinks are so clever. He should be known as "PP the GG" (Pierre Poilievre, the Gimmick Guy). Enough, PP, get to work serving Canadian citizens.
Laura Fehr, Mississauga
"Common sense" no doubt is something we all appreciate, but I have yet to hear the Conservative leader explain what it means to him other than cutting things that matter to Canadians. I wish his voting base questioned that.
Rachel Griffin, Ancaster
Abuse of food banks
Doug Ford's inaction has left Ontario's most vulnerable without a fighting chance, Sept. 30
I am an almost 80-year-old food bank volunteer and donor, and can attest that food banks are suffering abuse at the hands of our provincial government. We volunteers offer our time, energy and money in an attempt to help our neighbours, both children and adults, through lean times. I have been doing this since the Bill Davis years, a kinder time. At that time we food bankers respectfully made a decision to not accept funding from Frank Drea, then minister of community and social services. Today, the Ford government treats us volunteers like employees. The premier expects us to be there as Band-Aids for his obvious failure as government-funded agencies send us their hungry. Ford expects volunteers and private donors to feed chronically hungry children, children our provincial government seems to care little about. I am not a wealthy man but this year I have already given $5,000 and many hours to our local food bank. Local people are very generous. We do what we can, but our business model is unsustainable as an irresponsible government takes advantage of us enabling do-gooders. Food bank abuse does exist but the abuser is the premier.
Gus Sandusky, Norwood, Ont.
Danger zone
Doug Ford raising speed limit to 110 km/h on all 400-series highways 'where it is safe to do so,' Oct. 2
A UBC report prepared for the Ministry of Transportation and Infrastructure of British Columbia found an 18 per cent increase in fatal collisions after raising the speed limit by 10 km/h. In addition faster speeds will cause more fuel consumption and thus carbon dioxide and other pollutants. It isn't safe anywhere in Ontario to raise speed limits.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
We need to vote for a progressive party
Trudeau government survives another Conservative non-confidence vote, Oct. 2
In the face of Parliamentary turmoil, given the daily threats of a non-confidence motion, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's reluctance to quit has thrown Liberal supporters into confusion. There is the divide between sheer loyalty to the brand on the one hand and negative poll numbers on the other. Undoubtedly Trudeau's gambit is to repeat the tried and true and once again call on supporters and those intimidated by Conservative rhetoric to thwart the purported Conservative onslaught. This historical ping-ponging between Liberals and Conservatives does not do justice to the reality that Canadians have a valid and clear choice in voting for the NDP. It is time for Canada to grow up and move from the Liberal-Conservative electoral monopoly, and vote for a progressive party with people-oriented policies, a party bringing a new voice and new ideas to the debate.
Carl Hager, Gatineau, Que.
Rethinking Charlie Hustle
Pete Rose's life was always a hustle. His nickname was perfect: Arthur, Oct. 1
Charlie Hustle has died. Pete Rose died Sept. 30 at the age of 83 after a career that spanned 24 seasons and two World Series titles with his hometown Cincinnati Reds. He was one of the game's greatest players with more hits than any other player ever. In 1986 Rose unofficially retired as a player and when managing the Reds in 1989, there were allegations about his betting habits, though he never admitted to betting on his team. Unfortunately, Rose received a lifetime ban from Major League Baseball for betting on baseball while he was still an active manager. The hype about the Rose issue seems very hypocritical. Today any TV baseball game is rife with betting advertisements between each inning encouraging wagering on the game. Given today's betting climate, perhaps Charlie Hustle's banishment is questionable.
Gary Megaffin, Kitchener

Document 123 of 500

PM defends his leadership on MP's podcast: Trudeau says attack ads against Poilievre years before election would look 'petty'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-defends-his-leadership-on-mps-podcast/docview/3112758255/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+defends+his+leadership+on+MP%27s+podcast%3A+Trudeau+says+attack+ads+against+Poilievre+years+before+election+would+look+%27petty%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Under siege in the polls but fighting for a shot at a fourth term, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took to a backbench MP's podcast to riff on why he thinks he deserves it.
In a nearly hour-long interview recorded Friday and published Tuesday, Trudeau gave more revealing answers to fellow political traveller Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith than he has to any journalist in months.
The prime minister conceded a few mistakes. But only a few. And he expressed little regret.
Trudeau conceded: the Liberal party has given Pierre Poilievre two years to build his political brand instead of immediately running ads against the Conservative leader; that as prime minister he failed to deliver on his promise of electoral reform; that the Liberal party bears some responsibility for two bad byelection losses in Toronto and Montreal strongholds; and that the federal government should have acted sooner on housing.
Yet his abstract responses to Erskine-Smith's challenge on other questions - on what policies he wants to see through in a fourth term, on why he's running despite anonymous backbench naysayers and bad polls, or on how he thinks Liberals can win - underscored a bigger issue.
Can the Liberal leader, who has been on the Canadian and American podcast circuit in an attempt to broaden the reach of his message, get through to those he needs to persuade - not least, other Liberal MPs, party volunteers and those voters he's lost since 2015 that he hopes to bring back onside.
First, the mistakes:
Trudeau defended the decision not to run political ads against Poilievre as a tactical consideration, that years before an election was too soon and that he'd rebuffed a similar attempt by Conservatives to frame him too early, and more importantly, that he felt it would look petty to voters if the Liberals took their eye off the bigger economic and financial woes then facing Canadians. When Erskine-Smith begged to differ, Trudeau insisted there is still time to paint Poilievre as the risk to the environment, equality rights and social programs that the Liberals believe he is.
On the broken promise of electoral reform, Trudeau wished he'd had a do-over. He said he should have immediately shut down talk about proportional representation as a viable option. He repeated his oft-expressed view it would have been too divisive and spliced the Canadian electorate into ever smaller pies, but confessed Liberals were deliberately vague in order to appeal to Fair Vote Canada advocates. Trudeau said he wished he'd used his majority to bring in the ranked ballot model he preferred, saying it would not have changed ridings or even the ballot that much - suggesting instead of a check, voters would rank their preferences in order - and would force parties to appeal beyond their bases. But Trudeau said he did not feel there was a broad level of support in the House for what he called "an irreversible change," so he pulled the plug. Erskine-Smith said the broken promise was his most difficult day in nine years in Trudeau's caucus. Trudeau said it was "a gut-wrenching day for me."
On the byelection losses in Toronto-St. Paul's and LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, Trudeau said there were "a lot of factors" in each but the Liberal candidates did not have enough time on the ground to campaign ahead of the byelection dates, noting he'd had a year to campaign before winning Papineau. However Trudeau's answer did not reflect the fact that Leslie Church had been campaigning for nearly a year in the riding before she was appointed, and it did not address complaints that the appointments of her and Laura Palestini as candidate in the Montreal-area riding prevented competitive nomination races and angered some in those ridings. Nor did Trudeau directly address Erskine-Smith's question about what those losses told him about the bigger changes that are needed.
On housing and immigration, Trudeau acknowledged that the massive post-pandemic influx of temporary residents - foreign workers and international students - had an impact on housing shortages. But he said his government should have "leaned harder" on provinces and territories to address "NIMBYism" (not-in-my-backyard pushback) in order to build more housing.
But it was the prime minister's responses to Erskine-Smith's probing about why he is still running and how he thinks he can reverse the Liberals' fortunes that underscored the big challenge.
"Obviously your brain didn't melt on national television the way that Biden's did," Erskine-Smith said, but Kamala Harris has given that party a better shot at winning. He asked Trudeau to explain why he thinks he's the best person to lead the Liberals' next fight.
Trudeau's answer was, basically, that he is the one who knows better than anyone "exactly how hard this fight is going to be," that it is "fundamental to how Canadians come out of or come through" the past few difficult years, and it is "exactly why I got into politics."
He touted progressive policies that delivered for people and will last over the long term: the Canada Child Benefit, standing up a national child care program, fighting climate change including with carbon pricing, and efforts to tackle housing shortages he said will pay off years from now.
Trudeau pivoted to note, "Nobody is asking Poilievre what ... he is fighting for ... he hasn't even begun to articulate what he's fighting for ... other than himself and his desire to be in power."
However, Trudeau effectively said that there is no new vision for the coming campaign, rather that Liberals are fighting for the same vision he fought for in 2015, to protect the environment and the economy, to lift up ordinary Canadians and improve people's resilience for the future.
"Yeah, it's going to be a change election," he said.
"Everything has changed, not just climate change, but the way we work, the way AI works, the way geopolitics happens, the pressures on everything. The world is in a massive pivot moment right now, and we don't know what the biggest issue is going to be."
To confront that kind of change, Trudeau insisted that Canadians understand what his "values" are, and what he brings to a crisis, pointing out that the crises he confronted, from the COVID-19 pandemic to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, were not foreseen in any campaign.
Values, he said, are "ultimately the only thing."
Except in Poilievre, the Liberals are facing a political rival who is promising practical benefits for individual households, with slogan-based promises to "axe the tax, build the homes," and "stop the crime" and is laying claim to represent "common sense" Canadian values.
Erskine-Smith rightly called Trudeau out on all the high-level talk, and said Poilievre is a better communicator than any Conservative leader in the past nine years. He urged Trudeau to be better at telling the Liberals' story.
On Wednesday, after it aired, Erskine-Smith said his personal decision not to run in the next election is because he needs to spend more time with his young children, but he still supports Trudeau, at least for now.
CAPTION: During an appearance on Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith's podcast, released this week, Justin Trudeau admitted Liberals were deliberately vague about election reform, and said he should have shut down talk about proportional representation as a viable option.After the podcast aired, Erskine-Smith, who is not running for re-election, said he still supports Trudeau.Spencer Colby The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star
