Document 124 of 500

Canadians favour Harris over Trump

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadians-favour-harris-over-trump/docview/3112756407/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+favour+Harris+over+Trump&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are three times more positive about U.S. presidential candidate Kamala Harris than they are about Donald Trump, a new Pollara poll suggests.
"If Canada was the 51st state, it would be a state that no one would bother to campaign in because it would be a landslide for the Democrats," said Dan Arnold, chief strategy officer for Pollara Strategic Insights.
Indeed, 62 per cent of respondents had positive feelings about Harris with only 18 per cent having negative feelings toward the current Democratic vice-president.
In comparison, 66 per cent had negative feelings about Trump with 20 per cent having positive feelings toward the former Republican president.
"There's still apprehension that Trump might win - people are much more positive about the Democrats."
As part of Pollara's "Rage Index" polling that examines the mood of Canadians, the firm surveyed 1,500 people on their thoughts about the Nov. 5 U.S. election.
The poll was conducted between Sept. 11 - the day after the Harris-Trump debate - and Sept. 19 using online panels. While such opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Arnold said Canadians appear slightly more optimistic about the Democrats' chances since President Joe Biden stepped aside on July 21 following his disastrous June 27 debate performance against Trump.
In June, six per cent said they were "happy" about the U.S. election while 53 per cent were "angry." Last month, 10 per cent were happy and 46 per cent were angry.
But Harris's strong showing in the Sept. 10 debate against Trump has helped her standing among voters of all three major Canadian political parties.
Seven out of eight Liberal supporters - 87 per cent - felt positively toward her with only seven per cent feeling negative. Similarly, 83 per cent of Liberals were negative toward Trump with 10 per cent positive.
Almost half of Conservative voters - 45 per cent - had positive feelings about Harris with 37 per cent negative. That's greater than the 39 per cent of Tories who held a positive view toward Trump. Nearly half - 46 per cent - had a negative sentiment about him.
Among New Democratic voters, 83 per cent were positive about Harris with six per cent negative, while 88 per cent of NDP supporters had a negative view of Trump with six per cent positive.
Pollara also tested the views Canadians have toward the current president and about the two running mates.
Only one-third - 34 per cent - were positive about Biden with 36 per cent negative.
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz - Harris's vice-presidential pick and a favourite of Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who has worked closely with him on trade issues - was viewed positively by 32 per cent of respondents and negatively by 10 per cent.
Ohio Sen. JD Vance, Trump's running mate, fared worse than Harris, Trump, Walz or Biden in terms of the "positive" metric.
Just 12 per cent of those surveyed viewed Vance positively, while 36 per cent had negative feelings about him.
The survey was completed before Tuesday's vice-presidential debate.
Arnold noted awareness of the vice-presidential candidates is high among Canadians - 63 per cent knew who Vance was while 57 per cent knew who Walz was.
That's greater than those who could identify Canada's longest-serving premier - Saskatchewan's Scott Moe at 35 per cent - or federal Health Minister Mark Holland at 31 per cent.
"It's been more exciting than a typical Canadian election. People here are following it very closely."
Harris has a strong connection to Canada, where she spent most of her teenage years when her mother worked as a researcher at the Jewish General Hospital and taught at McGill University. She graduated from Montreal's Westmount High School in 1981 then returned stateside for university.
CAPTION: A new Pollara poll suggests 62 per cent of Canadian respondents had positive feelings about Kamala Harris as compared to 20 per cent for Donald Trump.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 125 of 500

Ford has failed to balance the budget

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-has-failed-balance-budget/docview/3112756269/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+has+failed+to+balance+the+budget&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ford government recently published Ontario's official public accounts for the 2023/24 fiscal year, which show that the province wound up with a deficit of $647 million. According to Caroline Mulroney, the government's Treasury Board president, this is very good news. "After 15 years of a Liberal government," she said. "I don't think people can even imagine getting this close to a balanced budget."
But in reality, the Ford government has yet again moved the goalposts it uses to measure fiscal success.
Let's first address the suggestion that, when Kathleen Wynne's Liberal government left office in 2018, it was difficult to "imagine" getting close to a balanced budget in 2023/24. While releasing the Ford government's first fiscal update in November 2018, then-finance minister Vic Fedeli spoke passionately about the need to eliminate the deficit and reduce provincial debt. "This government believes balancing the budget and reducing Ontario's debt burden is not only a fiscal imperative," he said, "it is a moral one." That update didn't include a clear path to balance but it - along with campaign rhetoric - gave a clear impression the government sought a quick return to balance.
However, by the spring of 2019 - before the pandemic - the Ford government's urgency had already started to cool and the 2019 budget included a five-year fiscal plan that balanced the budget in 2023/24. So, contrary to Mulroney's claim, a relatively small deficit in 2023/24 doesn't represent a fiscal achievement that would have been hard to "imagine" when the Liberals left office. Rather it's a failure. The Ford government has failed to achieve a budget balance despite the leisurely five-year path laid out in its 2019 budget.
The Ford government now says it will balance the budget by 2026/27, but given past experience it's reasonable to be skeptical, especially if the government encounters any unexpected bumps in the road such as an economic slowdown. And to be clear, the government can't blame COVID because annual provincial revenues have by now recovered from that shock.
And crucially, we're only talking about the government's "operating" deficits, driven by annual expenditures on day-to-day items such as employee salaries and debt interest. When you look at the bigger picture, which includes spending on long-term capital projects, Mulroney's claim grows more dubious. The government's net debt burden this year will reach a projected $408 billion - or 37.4 per cent relative to the size of the provincial economy, down slightly from 39.2 per cent in 2017/18, the Liberal government's final full year in office.
This slight improvement since the Wynne years does not seem to reflect a government that believes debt reduction is a "moral" imperative. To be sure, it's good news that the deficit for 2023/24 is a little smaller than the government previously thought. However, contrary to claims from Mulroney and others in the Ford government, it's nothing to celebrate.
Far from exceeding expectations or simply delivering on its promises, the Ford government has fallen well short of its deficit-reduction pledges. Will they break their new promises and move the goalposts once again? That's not hard to imagine.
Ben Eisen is a senior fellow at the Fraser Institute.
CREDIT: Ben Eisen Contributor Ben Eisen is a senior fellow at the Fraser Institute.;

Subject: Debt restructuring; Premier-Canada; State budgets

Business indexing term: Subject: Debt restructuring State budgets

People: Ford, Doug

Company / organization: Name: Fraser Institute; NAICS: 541720

Document 126 of 500

Charging drivers only way to fix Hwy. 401

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/charging-drivers-only-way-fix-hwy-401/docview/3112756243/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Charging+drivers+only+way+to+fix+Hwy.+401&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's tunnel vision has everyone focusing on his blind spots.
Too bad most motorists refuse to look at the congestion solution in plain sight.
For all the public mockery of the premier's plan to dig under Highway 401, which came out of nowhere last week, traffic jams are no joke. But the bitter truth is that almost everyone is guilty of closing their eyes to the economics of commuting and congestion.
The critics claim to have a cleverer, better way: Why not buy back Highway 407 (technically it's been leased, not sold) for an estimated $30 billion, remove its exorbitant tolls and take the pressure off the 401?
In fact, Ford says he hasn't ruled it out. After all, he has cheerfully cancelled tolls in the past and could well do it again in future to win more votes.
In truth, removing those tolls would be a false economy - leaving us no further ahead in time or money spent. Just further behind fiscally and environmentally.
In reality, the only lasting solution would be the complete opposite:
Keep those existing tolls on the 407.
And go one step further by adding new tolls to the old 401 - not to mention all other major highways and parkways encircling and choking the GTA.
What? Tolls on the 401?
We all know that toll roads are the third rail of Ontario politics.
Yes, it's unthinkable. But try this thought experiment.
Forget the pocketbook politics for a moment. Never mind the populist impulses that everyone falls for.
Not just the premier but almost everyone - politicians, pundits and the public - has become avowedly, adamantly anti-tax and anti-toll. Of course everyone instinctively dislikes tolls, but that distaste won't get us where we need to go.
There is no such thing as a free lunch. Or a free highway.
A toll-free highway still exacts a heavy price in lost time if you're stuck in traffic, because time is money. The cost has been estimated in the billions of dollars annually in delayed deliveries and lost productivity, not to mention people cheated out of personal time - and family time.
In a free market, everything has a price - and it's never free. Highways cost big money to build, but road tolls (unlike bridge tolls) are no longer about cost recovery.
Now, road tolls (and congestion charges for crowded downtowns) are a price signal - a way of flagging for drivers that they must choose carefully whether or when to travel, not on a whim or caprice.
Economics is all about scarcity. Pricing is way of allocating or rationing - rationally - a commodity that others are also competing for, in this case highway space.
In the real world of economics, prices are typically set at levels that the market will bear, based on the fundamentals of supply and demand.
Building (or opening) more toll-free highways tends to encourage more cars, creating greater congestion until we end up right back where we started - in bumper-to-bumper equilibrium again. Economists and planners call it "induced demand" - a consequence of increasing the supply of free freeways.
Raise prices (tolls) higher? Demand will decrease and traffic congestion will decline.
Reduce road tolls to zero, while building or tunnelling new roads? Demand from cars will increase as more motorists clog the roads - at an enormous price in lost time.
Digging a tunnel replicates the same dynamic: If you build it - or dig it - they will come.
People will drive on and under the 401 without limits as soon as you allow unlimited access. That's the inevitably of induced demand.
Ultimately, when the flow of traffic stalls, motorists will search for alternatives: rapid transit, remote work, off-peak commutes, or toll roads that represent value for money (savings in time).
To be clear, congestion is out of control. The question is how to win the congestion battle, not an election battle.
The reality is that road tolls are time-savers, and time is money. The problem is that voters don't want to hear about it.
The premier's pie-in-the-sky proposal to tunnel across much of the GTA is not only a road map to ruin, it is a teachable moment. But the opposition's counterproposal for a free Highway 407, which would create a free-for-all, is merely a diversion on the road to induced demand.
Many are snickering at Ford's tunnel vision. But the joke is on us if we keep looking away from economic realities.
This thought experiment on road tolls might read like a political dead end. But more of the same wishful thinking about free highways won't get us anywhere.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's plan to dig under Highway 401 has drawn snickers, but the joke is on us if we keep ignoring the economic realities of congestion, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Christopher KatsarovThe Canadian press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Toll roads; Commuting; Automobile drivers; Cost control; Cost recovery; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Economics; Premier-Canada; Tunnels; Proposals

Full text: Poilievre's common sense narrative familiar
Justin Trudeau dishes with Liberal MP on where he went wrong, what he did right and why he's the one to beat Pierre Poilievre, Oct. 2
We have seen the "Common Sense Revolution" before. It took us by storm and fooled us under former Ontario premier Mike Harris. We are still feeling the effects many years later. Now federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has picked up the same script, using the same language, and if we don't watch out, the results will be the same. Common sense appears to mean looking after the top 20 per cent of income earners. Simple answers and throwing around blame don't cut it in a complicated world. I don't buy "common sense" that prioritizes dollars over people, wants over needs and individuals over society at large.
Peter Rapsey, Toronto
For all those who continually revert to "common sense" to justify their arguments and decisions, remember the point from Dr. Keith Stanovich, one of Canada's leading psychologists: beware today's "common sense" because it is not difficult to show that yesterday's "common sense" often turns into today's "nonsense." When Pierre Poilievre uses the term, he really means Conservative Sense. Let's not be fooled.
John Cousins, Bowmanville, Ont.
With or without glasses and a makeover, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre remains the same right-wing bully he's always been. Stoking anger and chaos for his own political gain is his platform and his common sense blather is just reheated Conservative coffee.
Kat Duffy, Mississauga
The next time Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre runs a TV ad trying to look like a normal Canadian building a jigsaw puzzle with his kid, he might consider removing the luxurious and expensive Breitling watch from his wrist. Man of the people? I don't think so.
Malcolm Lowe, Unionville, Ont.
I am so tired of Pierre Poilievre wasting time in the House being a drama king. He picks fights unnecessarily and comes up with gimmicky rhyming and alliterate slogans that he thinks are so clever. He should be known as "PP the GG" (Pierre Poilievre, the Gimmick Guy). Enough, PP, get to work serving Canadian citizens.
Laura Fehr, Mississauga
"Common sense" no doubt is something we all appreciate, but I have yet to hear the Conservative leader explain what it means to him other than cutting things that matter to Canadians. I wish his voting base questioned that.
Rachel Griffin, Ancaster
Food banks suffering abuse under Ford
Ford's inaction has left Ontario's most vulnerable without a fighting chance, Sept. 30
I am an almost 80-year-old food bank volunteer and donor, and can attest that food banks are suffering abuse at the hands of our provincial government. We volunteers offer our time, energy and money in an attempt to help our neighbours, both children and adults, through lean times. I have been doing this since the Bill Davis years, a kinder time. At that time we food bankers respectfully made a decision to not accept funding from Frank Drea, then minister of community and social services. Today, the Ford government treats us volunteers like employees. The premier expects us to be there as Band-Aids for his obvious failure as government-funded agencies send us their hungry. Ford expects volunteers and private donors to feed chronically hungry children, children our provincial government seems to care little about. I am not a wealthy man but this year I have already given $5,000 and many hours to our local food bank. Local people are very generous. We do what we can, but our business model is unsustainable as an irresponsible government takes advantage of us enabling do-gooders. Food bank abuse does exist but the abuser is the premier.
Gus Sandusky, Norwood, Ont.
Unsafe to raise speed limits
in Ontario
Doug Ford raising speed limit to 110 km/h on all 400-series highways 'where it is safe to do so,' Oct. 2
A UBC report prepared for the Ministry of Transportation and Infrastructure of British Columbia found an 18 per cent increase in fatal collisions after raising the speed limit by 10 km/h. In addition, faster speeds will cause more fuel consumption and thus carbon dioxide and other pollutants. It isn't safe anywhere in Ontario to raise speed limits.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
Canadians need to vote for a progressive party
Trudeau government survives another Conservative non-confidence vote, Oct. 2
In the face of parliamentary turmoil, given the daily threats of a non-confidence motion, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's reluctance to quit has thrown Liberal supporters into confusion.
There is the divide between sheer loyalty to the brand on the one hand and negative poll numbers on the other. Undoubtedly Trudeau's gambit is to repeat the tried and true and once again call on supporters and those intimidated by Conservative rhetoric to thwart the purported Conservative onslaught.
This historical ping-ponging between Liberals and Conservatives does not do justice to the reality that Canadians have a valid and clear choice in voting for the NDP.
It is time for Canada to grow up and move from the Liberal-Conservative electoral monopoly, and vote for a progressive party with people-oriented policies, a party bringing a new voice and new ideas to the debate.
Carl Hager, Gatineau, Que.
Would Rose
be banned in today's world?
Pete Rose's life was always a hustle. His nickname was perfect: Arthur, Oct. 1
Charlie Hustle has died. Pete Rose died Sept. 30 at the age of 83 after a career that spanned 24 seasons and two World Series titles with his hometown Cincinnati Reds.
He was one of the game's greatest players with more hits than any other player ever. In 1986 Rose unofficially retired as a player and when managing the Reds in 1989, there were allegations about his betting habits, though he never admitted to betting on his team.
Unfortunately, Rose received a lifetime ban from Major League Baseball for betting on baseball while he was still an active manager.
The hype about the Rose issue seems very hypocritical. Today any TV baseball game is rife with betting advertisements between each inning encouraging wagering on the game.
Given today's betting climate, perhaps Charlie Hustle's banishment is questionable.
Gary Megaffin, Kitchener

Document 128 of 500

Province on hook for parking at private spa: Ontario's 95-year lease with Austrian firm stipulates penalties if parking not ready in time for opening

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-on-hook-parking-at-private-spa/docview/3112756102/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+on+hook+for+parking+at+private+spa%3A+Ontario%27s+95-year+lease+with+Austrian+firm+stipulates+penalties+if+parking+not+ready+in+time+for+opening&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's 95-year lease with Therme, the Austrian firm building a controversial spa resort at redeveloped Ontario Place, puts pressure on the provincial government to get moving on a new parking structure or risk paying Therme millions of dollars per year in damages.
Premier Doug Ford's government released details of the lease Thursday after years of public concern, secrecy and controversy over plans to replace trees and other natural features with a towering indoor spa and water park on the mostly shuttered theme park's West Island, along with an expanded Budweiser Stage and relocated Ontario Science Centre.
The lease requires at least 1,600 parking spots for Therme and another 200 for Live Nation, which is expanding Budweiser Stage on the waterfront site. However, Infrastructure Ontario's Michael Lindsay told reporters that the Ford government plans to build a total of 2,500 new spots so people can visit by car to also enjoy the Science Centre, new parkland and other attractions.
If the province fails to meet its parking obligations before the spa resort opens, or by 2030, whichever comes first, the lease compels taxpayers to give Therme $5 per spot per day for a portion of the unbuilt spots, which Lindsay said could total $2.2 million per year.
He called the provision "reasonable" and noted that the province could use surface lots as "bridging measures" to meet the obligation, but "we're hoping ... to land on a more permanent solution" such as a parking structure either at Ontario Place or city-owned Exhibition Place on the north side of Lake Shore Boulevard.
Lindsay rebuffed a reporter's question about how much the province is budgeting for a structure that could cost hundreds of millions of dollars if it is built below ground - an option the Ford government appears to have ruled out as too pricey.
The province is in talks with city officials on parking options at Exhibition Place. "Negotiations are ongoing as to the shape and location of the parking structure" that could be built there, Shirven Rezvany, a spokesperson for Mayor Olivia Chow, told the Star in an email.
Benefits of the redevelopment plan, Infrastructure Ontario said in briefing documents, "include, at a minimum, nearly $2 billion in estimated revenue contributions from Therme Canada to the province over the duration of the lease and $700 million in upfront capital investments from Therme Canada.
"Of these upfront capital investments, $500 million will go to build the Therme Canada facility and $200 million will support approximately 16 acres of freely accessible public space" around the spa building, according to the provincial agency.
"The province will maintain ownership of the lands and has not provided or committed to any operational subsidy or capital contributions to the Therme Canada facility," it added.
Therme's facility at Ontario Place cannot, under lease terms, be a casino, shopping mall or condominium. The lease starts in 2025 and runs for 75 years with a 20-year extension clause.
The lease says the province has the right to early termination, with five years' notice, after the 10th year of operation. That could, however, trigger provincial damages paid to the spa operator. If Therme fails to achieve key lease obligations, it could be on the hook for damages to the province.
Lindsay spoke as opponents of the redevelopment plan mourned the loss of hundreds of mature trees on Ontario Place's West Island, felled to make way for new construction.
After Lindsay said the deforestation was timed to ensure birds and bats are not in nesting season, and that new plantings will replace more trees than were cut, a communications aide ordered reporters to ask no more questions about trees.
While emphasizing that the province won't be paying Therme's construction or operating costs, Lindsay acknowledged that provincial taxpayers have so far spent "hundreds of millions" of dollars on site servicing to get all of Ontario Place ready for redevelopment.
Ontario Place for All, an advocacy group fighting the plan, said in an email that the 95-year lease "attempts to lock Ontarians in for generations.
"The best result for Ontarians is for the government to come to its senses and stop this MegaSpa from being built," the group said.
"Despite the loss of many trees today on West Island, it's still a public space that should be preserved for the public for generations to come."
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter

Document 129 of 500

Doug Ford's government releases details of Ontario Place lease with Therme spa

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-releases-details-ontario/docview/3112921113/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+releases+details+of+Ontario+Place+lease+with+Therme+spa&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's 95-year lease with Therme, the Austrian firm building a controversial spa resort at redeveloped Ontario Place, puts pressure on the provincial government to get moving on a new parking structure or risk paying Therme millions of dollars per year in damages.
Premier Doug Ford's government released details of the lease Thursday after years of public concern, secrecy and controversy over plans to replace trees and other natural features with a towering indoor spa and water park on the mostly shuttered theme park's West Island, along with an expanded Budweiser Stage and relocated Ontario Science Centre.
The lease requires at least 1,600 parking spots for Therme and another 200 for Live Nation, which is expanding Budweiser Stage on the waterfront site. However, Infrastructure Ontario's Michael Lindsay told reporters that the Ford government plans to build a total of 2,500 new spots so people can visit by car to also enjoy the Science Centre, new parkland and other attractions.
If the province fails to meet its parking obligations before the spa resort opens, or 2030, whichever comes first, the lease compels taxpayers to give Therme $5 per spot per day for a portion of the unbuilt spots, which Lindsay said could total $2.2 million per year.
He called the provision "reasonable" and noted that the province could use surface lots as "bridging measures" to meet the obligation, but "we're hoping ... to land on a more permanent solution" such as a parking structure either at Ontario Place or city-owned Exhibition Place on the north side of Lake Shore Boulevard.
Lindsay rebuffed a reporter's question about how much the province is budgeting for a structure that could cost hundreds of millions of dollars if it is built below ground - an option the Ford government appears to have ruled out as being too pricey.
The province is in talks with city officials on parking options at Exhibition Place. "Negotiations are ongoing as to the shape and location of the parking structure," that could be built there, Shirven Rezvany, a spokesperson for Mayor Olivia Chow, told the Star in an email.
Benefits of the redevelopment plan, Infrastructure Ontario said in briefing documents, "include, at a minimum, nearly $2 billion in estimated revenue contributions from Therme Canada to the province over the duration of the lease and $700 million in upfront capital investments from Therme Canada.
"Of these upfront capital investments, $500 million will go to build the Therme Canada facility and $200 million will support approximately 16 acres of freely accessible public space," around the spa building, according to the provincial agency.
"The province will maintain ownership of the lands and has not provided or committed to any operational subsidy or capital contributions to the Therme Canada facility," it added.
Therme's facility at Ontario Place cannot, under lease terms, be a casino, shopping mall or condominium. The lease starts in 2025 and runs for 75 years with a 20-year extension clause.
The lease says the province has the right to early termination, with five years' notice, after the tenth year of operation. That could, however, trigger provincial damages paid to the spa operator. If Therme fails to achieve key lease obligations, it could be on the hook for damages to the province.
Lindsay spoke as opponents of the redevelopment plan mourned the loss of hundreds of mature trees on Ontario Place's West Island, felled to make way for new construction.
After Lindsay said the deforestation was timed to ensure birds and bats are not in nesting season, and that new plantings will replace more trees than were cut, a communications aide ordered reporters to ask no more questions about trees.
While emphasizing that the province won't be paying Therme's construction or operating costs, Lindsay acknowledged that provincial taxpayers have so far spent "hundreds of millions" of dollars on site servicing to get all of Ontario Place ready for redevelopment.
Ontario Place for All, an advocacy group fighting the plan, said in an email that the 95-year lease "attempts to lock Ontarians in for generations.
"The best result for Ontarians is for the government to come to its senses and stop this MegaSpa from being built," the group said.
"Despite the loss of many trees today on West Island, it's still a public space that should be preserved for the public for generations to come."
CAPTION: The release of the Therma spa lease on Thursday by Doug Ford's government follows years of public concern over the controversial plans to redevelop the now-shuttered waterfront park.

Canadians three times more positive about Kamala Harris than Donald Trump, survey says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadians-three-times-more-positive-about-kamala/docview/3112920674/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadians+three+times+more+positive+about+Kamala+Harris+than+Donald+Trump%2C+survey+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians are three times more positive about U.S. presidential candidate Kamala Harris than they are about Donald Trump, a new Pollara poll suggests.
"If Canada was the 51st state, it would be a state that no one would bother to campaign in because it would be a landslide for the Democrats," said Dan Arnold, chief strategy officer for Pollara Strategic Insights.
Indeed, 62 per cent of respondents had positive feelings about Harris with only 18 per cent having negative feelings toward the current Democratic vice-president.
In comparison, 66 per cent had negative feelings about Trump with 20 per cent having positive feelings toward the former Republican president.
"There's still apprehension that Trump might win - people are much more positive about the Democrats."
As part of Pollara's "Rage Index" polling that examines the mood of Canadians, the firm surveyed 1,500 people on their thoughts about the Nov. 5 U.S. election.
The poll was conducted between Sept. 11 - the day after the Harris-Trump debate - and Sept. 19 using online panels. While such opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 2.5 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Arnold said Canadians appear slightly more optimistic about the Democrats' chances since President Joe Biden stepped aside on July 21 following his disastrous June 27 debate performance against Trump.
In June, six per cent said they were "happy" about the U.S. election while 53 per cent were "angry." Last month, 10 per cent were happy and 46 per cent were angry.
But Harris's strong showing in the Sept. 10 debate against Trump has helped her standing among voters of all three major Canadian political parties.
Seven out of eight Liberal supporters - 87 per cent - felt positively toward her with only seven per cent feeling negative. Similarly, 83 per cent of Liberals were negative toward Trump with 10 per cent positive.
Almost half of Conservative voters - 45 per cent - had positive feelings about Harris with 37 per cent negative. That's greater than the 39 per cent of Tories who held a positive view toward Trump. Nearly half - 46 per cent - had a negative sentiment about him.
Among New Democratic voters, 83 per cent were positive about Harris with six per cent negative, while 88 per cent of NDP supporters had a negative view of Trump with six per cent positive.
Pollara also tested the views Canadians have toward the current president and about the two running mates.
Only one-third - 34 per cent - were positive about Biden with 36 per cent negative.
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz - Harris's vice-presidential pick and a favourite of Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who has worked closely with him on trade issues - was viewed positively by 32 per cent of respondents and negatively by 10 per cent.
Ohio Sen. JD Vance, Trump's running mate, fared worse than Harris, Trump, Walz or Biden in terms of the "positive" metric.
Just 12 per cent of those surveyed viewed Vance positively, while 36 per cent had negative feelings about him.
The survey was completed before Tuesday's vice-presidential debate.
Arnold noted awareness of the vice-presidential candidates is high among Canadians - 63 per cent knew who Vance was while 57 per cent knew who Walz was.
That's greater than those who could identify Canada's longest-serving premier - Saskatchewan's Scott Moe at 35 per cent - or federal Health Minister Mark Holland at 31 per cent.
"It's been more exciting than a typical Canadian election. People here are following it very closely."
Harris has a strong connection to Canada, where she spent most of her teenage years when her mother worked as a researcher at the Jewish General Hospital and taught at McGill University. She graduated from Montreal's Westmount High School in 1981 then returned stateside for university.
"Canadians always like 'Canadians' who make it big in the U.S.," said Arnold, noting that is despite the fact the vice-president seldom publicly mentions her time in Montreal.
"She doesn't talk about it because it doesn't help her politically," he added.
But 77 per cent of Quebecers polled viewed her positively with just eight per cent having negative feelings.
"So the 'hometown' effect might be a bit in play," said Arnold.
CAPTION: Republican presidential candidate Donald Trump giving a speech on Aug. 20, 2024, about immigration and crime i in Howell, Michigan.

Document 131 of 500

Toronto to Montreal in 3 hours? Canada might be finally ready to build a high speed rail line - but how fast it will be remains an open question

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-montreal-3-hours-canada-might-be-finally/docview/3112756376/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto+to+Montreal+in+3+hours%3F+Canada+might+be+finally+ready+to+build+a+high+speed+rail+line+-+but+how+fast+it+will+be+remains+an+open+question&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A key stop on the track to better passenger rail service that the Liberal government has promised is just around the bend - and with it comes a multibillion-dollar question that's still without an answer:
If it really gets built, how fast will the trains go?
In the coming weeks, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's cabinet is expected to choose which of three groups of private companies will get to help design and build a new passenger rail project that proponents bill as a game-changer for travel through the most populated part of the country. Running 1,000 kilometres from Toronto to Quebec City, the promised rail corridor is meant to slash journey times between major cities and could bring Canada - many say belatedly - into the modern era of high speed rail travel.
"We are looking at all options," said Transport Minister Anita Anand, when asked whether the project will bring a dedicated line of trains that travel faster than 200 km/h, the standard definition of high speed rail (HSR).
Vance Badawey, a Liberal MP who is Anand's parliamentary secretary, said the government is studying which areas might be best served by faster trains, and which could still rely on slower - but more frequent - rail service. But, like Anand, he said the government has not yet decided what to do.
At an office at the federal Transport Department in downtown Ottawa this week, Martin Imbleau expressed high hopes for the project. As chief executive officer of the Crown corporation created to advance the project, Imbleau shared a vision for rail travel from Toronto to Québec that rivals European countries like France.
That country's Trains à Grande Vitesse (TGV) system has successfully reduced travel times between cities over four decades of operation, Imbleau said, even though routes like the one from Paris to Amsterdam include stretches where trains run slower than 200 km/h.
He suggested the new rail project could look something like that: a corridor with trains that slow down when necessary, like when entering an urban centre, and speed up beyond 200 km/h along other parts of the route. The idea now is to have a single new corridor with stops in Toronto, Peterborough, Ottawa, Laval, Montreal, Trois-Rivières and Quebec City.
But the final shape depends on which private group the government chooses, and how that group works with Imbleau's Crown corporation to design and build the project, he said, adding that he expects the decision in the coming weeks. The three groups include more than 20 companies, with the likes of Air Canada, national rail companies from France, Spain and Germany, and British infrastructure investment firm John Laing.
Each group was asked to make two proposals to the government: one for trains that travel less than 200 km/h, and one for trains that travel faster. And regardless of who wins the project, the designers will be allowed to pull from all the proposals to "mix and match" and meet their goals of faster, more frequent and reliable passenger service, Imbleau said.
Depending on what happens, he predicted train travel between Montreal and Toronto could be reduced to little more than three hours, compared to the current reality of more than five.
"We're really looking at reducing the journey time with those three factors," Imbleau said. "How fast remains to be seen."
The Liberals first floated the project more than five years ago as "high frequency rail." The idea was to build new tracks dedicated exclusively to electrified passenger trains that would run more frequently. That way, passenger service wouldn't run on tracks owned by Canadian National (CN) and Canadian Pacific (CPKC), whose freight traffic is prioritized over Via Rail trains carrying people, resulting in delays that drag out the time it takes to travel by rail.
But as one government source explained, the vision for the project shifted toward high speed rail, despite the "conventional wisdom" at Via and within the public service "just to do it at regular speed."
Part of that was a perception of public appetite, the source said. A public opinion survey completed in August for Imbleau's Crown corporation found a majority of respondents - 58 per cent of those from Quebec and 57 per cent from Ontario - said they preferred a faster but more expensive train over a slower, less costly option.
"Everybody wants high speed," said the source, who agreed to speak on condition of anonymity.
Of course, HSR is more expensive to build, in part because it requires special infrastructure to make it safe to travel at high speeds, like grade separation. In 2021, then-transport minister Omar Alghabra floated a rough cost estimate for the project at between $6 billion and $12 billion. But the real cost might actually be more than double that, the government source said, depending on how much of the project is built for high speed, where the tracks end up getting built, and because of factors like inflation that have driven up construction costs in recent years.
A 2017 assessment of a possible "ultra-high speed" rail project from Portland, Oregon to Vancouver, B.C. estimated such a project - with up to 800 kilometres of track - could cost between $32 billion and $57 billion.
"The truth of the matter is the cost is going to go up," the source said.
Imbleau told the Star it's too early to talk about costs, since they still don't know exactly what they're going to build. But he stressed that the rail project would ultimately bring in revenue from ticket sales.
"If it costs more but it brings in more value, maybe there's a business case there," he said, adding that he sees the project as an "investment" to help Canada catch up with other countries that have had rapid rail service for decades. And, although he noted there are still many uncertainties, he predicted a first section of the rail line could be finished within about 12 years.
"I will let the government make their decision, and I will respect that," Imbleau said. "What we're developing is a project that is required, and it's an investment for future generations."
Paul Langan, a long-time advocate for HSR in Canada, said the project appears to be veering towards high speed. "Just how high is the question," he said.
His big concern is, given the political situation that finds the governing Liberals in a minority Parliament and trailing badly in the polls, what will happen to the project if a new party - namely the Conservatives - takes power after the next election.
In an emailed statement this week, Conservative MP and transport critic Philip Lawrence did not say what his party would do with the rail project if it came to power. Instead, he said the public should doubt whether the Liberals will actually get it built.
"Like every photo op and every big promise from Justin Trudeau, Canadians can't trust that he will deliver on anything," the statement said.
According to the government source, the proposals from the private groups are in the "final stages" of assessment by the public service, and cabinet could decide by November which one is going to take part in the project.
"It was a big mistake we didn't do this 30 years ago," the source said. "These types of project are, in my opinion, non-negotiable."
CAPTION: A train travels on France's high-speed rail network, in Connerre, western France, on Aug. 29, 2024.

Subject: Construction costs; Design; High speed; Public opinion surveys; Cost estimates

Business indexing term: Subject: Construction costs Public opinion surveys Cost estimates

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Quebec City Quebec Canada; Canada; France

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: Toronto to Montreal in 3 hours? Canada might be finally ready to build a high speed rail line - but how fast it will be remains an open question

Document 132 of 500

Doug Ford's tunnel won't fix Highway 401. Charging drivers is the only way

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tunnel-wont-fix-highway-401-charging/docview/3112756345/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+tunnel+won%27t+fix+Highway+401.+Charging+drivers+is+the+only+way&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford's tunnel vision has everyone focusing on his blind spots.
Too bad most motorists refuse to look at the congestion solution in plain sight.
For all the public mockery of the premier's plan to dig under Hwy. 401, which came out of nowhere last week, traffic jams are no joke. But the bitter truth is that almost everyone is guilty of closing their eyes to the economics of commuting and congestion.
The critics claim to have a cleverer, better way: Why not buy back Hwy. 407 (technically it's been leased, not sold) for an estimated $30 billion, remove its exorbitant tolls and take the pressure off the 401?
In fact, Ford says he hasn't ruled it out. After all, he has cheerfully cancelled tolls in the past and could well do it again in future to win more votes.
In truth, removing those tolls would be a false economy - leaving us no further ahead in time or money spent. Just further behind fiscally and environmentally.
In reality, the only lasting solution would be the complete opposite:
Keep those existing tolls on the 407.
And go one step further by adding new tolls to the old 401 - not to mention all other major highways and parkways encircling and choking the GTA.
What? Tolls on the 401?
We all know that toll roads are the third rail of Ontario politics.
Yes, it's unthinkable. But try this thought experiment.
Forget the pocketbook politics for a moment. Never mind the populist impulses that everyone falls for.
Not just the premier but almost everyone - politicians, pundits and the public - has become avowedly, adamantly anti-tax and anti-toll. Of course everyone instinctively dislikes tolls, but that distaste won't get us where we need to go.
There is no such thing as a free lunch. Or a free highway.
A toll-free highway still exacts a heavy price in lost time if you're stuck in traffic, because time is money. The cost has been estimated in the billions of dollars annually in delayed deliveries and lost productivity, not to mention people cheated out of personal time - and family time.
In a free market, everything has a price - and it's never free. Highways cost big money to build, but road tolls (unlike bridge tolls) are no longer about cost recovery.
Now, road tolls (and congestion charges for crowded downtowns) are a price signal - a way of flagging for drivers that they must choose carefully whether or when to travel, not on a whim or caprice.
Economics is all about scarcity. Pricing is way of allocating or rationing - rationally - a commodity that others are also competing for, in this case highway space.
In the real world of economics, prices are typically set at levels that the market will bear, based on the fundamentals of supply and demand.
Building (or opening) more toll-free highways tends to encourage more cars, creating greater congestion until we end up right back where we started - in bumper-to-bumper equilibrium again. Economists and planners call it "induced demand" - a consequence of increasing the supply of free freeways.
Raise prices (tolls) higher? Demand will decrease and traffic congestion will decline.
Reduce road tolls to zero, while building or tunnelling new roads? Demand from cars will increase as more motorists clog the roads - at an enormous price in lost time.
Digging a tunnel replicates the same dynamic: If you build it - or dig it - they will come.
People will drive on and under the 401 without limits as soon as you allow unlimited access. That's the inevitably of induced demand.
Ultimately, when the flow of traffic stalls, motorists will search for alternatives: rapid transit, remote work, off-peak commutes, or toll roads that represent value for money (savings in time).
To be clear, congestion is out of control. The question is how to win the congestion battle, not an election battle.
The reality is that road tolls are time-savers, and time is money. The problem is that voters don't want to hear about it.
The premier's pie in the sky proposal to tunnel across much of the GTA is not only a road map to ruin, it is a teachable moment. But the opposition's counterproposal for a free Hwy. 407, which would create a free-for-all, is merely a diversion on the road to induced demand.
Many are snickering at Ford's tunnel vision. But the joke is on us if we keep looking away from economic realities.
This thought experiment on road tolls might read like a political dead end. But more of the same wishful thinking about free highways won't get us anywhere.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford has people snickering with his suggestion we build a highway tunnel across GTA, but the joke is on us, Martin Regg Cohn writes.

Subject: Commuting; Toll roads; Automobile drivers; Cost recovery; Cost control; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Economics

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost recovery Cost control Economics

Title: Doug Ford's tunnel won't fix Highway 401. Charging drivers is the only way

Document 133 of 500

Would Trudeau stepping down trigger another Liberal civil war?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/would-trudeau-stepping-down-trigger-another/docview/3112504433/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Would+Trudeau+stepping+down+trigger+another+Liberal+civil+war%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau's determination to stay the leader of the federal Liberal Party - and, of course, prime minister - is more than ego. He is probably worried about the eruption of a second Liberal civil war.Until the sudden explosion of Pierre Trudeau into leadership in the 1960s, the party was a model of unity. For more than 50 years, Liberals presented a smiling portrait of a party united. That ended with the first Trudeau's election.Liberals were always far from homogenous ideologically. They understood, however, the electoral power of a publicly united family. Blue Liberals on Bay Street and parts of Ontario would tolerate unemployment insurance and better pensions and so on, so long as Canadian corporate interests were served. More progressive Liberals, centred in Toronto, Vancouver, Quebec and Atlantic Canada, demanded a broader and deeper social safety net and were dismissive of Bay Street. Western Liberals, then as now, did not count.in 1968, Blue Grits were confident that corporate icon Bob Winters would serve their best interests. When Trudeau crushed him by a 10-point margin in the leadership contest, the first cracks in Liberal unity slowly began to emerge.One of Justin Trudeau's greatest achievements is unknown to most Canadians. He salvaged Liberal unity after an often bloody three decade civil war and rebuilt the party under a new very centrist regime. He will fight hard to prevent the party sliding back into factional strife when he has gone.John Turner, the next corporate Liberal prince, fired off monthly diatribes against his former boss from the safety of his Bay Street law office for years after his "retirement" from Cabinet. Corporate Canada, meanwhile, whispered against Trudeau from behind the curtains.Pierre Trudeau was never "a party guy." His obsession was Canadian unity and the Liberals were merely his vehicle. His son is very different. He learned the lessons of a fractious party. Turner's disastrous performance led to Brian Mulroney's landslide victory. Jean Chrétien's wilful baiting of the blue Liberals first ended with Martin's victory, but was soon followed by his defeat. A defeated party then chose two of the most inept party leaders Canada has seen, Stéphane Dion and Michael Ignatieff.Justin Trudeau does indulge the nearly universal instinct of long-term leaders: undermine every prospective successor. As a result, Liberals today have no one to succeed him. No minister has favourable voter recognition , let alone Trudeau-like charisma. So Trudeau, worried not to repeat his father's failure, is trapped.Part of Justin Trudeau's sometimes-brutal tactics to prevent the civil war was to remove its main players. Many Chrétien/Martin-era party elders are still enraged at Trudeau and his enforcer, Gerry Butts; slamming the door in their faces. After a defeat, expect many of them to go public. They have the potential to wreak havoc, especially the corporate Liberals among them.The parallels between 1968 and today are eerie. A rich banker, Mark Carney, whose political experience is non-existent, is, like Bob Winters, the blue Liberals' current messiah. And progressive Liberals will probably squabble among themselves about which second stringer they should rally behind - absent a new Trudeau.Trudeau's calculus for staying - beyond ego and hubris - must therefore be this. If he loses to Poilievre, he will still leave the Liberals after a hard-fought and competent campaign and probably save more of the furniture than anyone else could. More importantly, though, he will still have the power to crush faction fighting. If he leaves before the next election, a Liberal Kim Campbell will lead the party to greater ruin.The prime minister will retire, but not remain silent, taking comfort in a 10-year premiership and some significant policy achievements. But also he will get history's credit as the leader who helped the Liberals move from defeat to rebuilding not once, but twice, and successfully blocked a new civil war.Trudeau understands the value of a long game he controls. He will leave Pierre Poilievre shouting pathetically after his sixth or 16th failed effort to defeat his government. Then Trudeau will call an election on his timing, and campaign hard, as if his legacy depends on it - and it does.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau meets with then Bank of England governor Mark Carney at the G20 Summit in Buenos Aires, Argentina on Friday, Nov. 30, 2018. Carney is seen by blue Liberals as the successor to Trudeau as Liberal leader.

Illegal truck yards popping up across the GTA + another Toronto cop shot

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, October 3 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on illegal truck yards in the GTA, another Toronto police officer shot and Doug Ford's "complete audit" of Ontario children's aid.
DON'T MISSIllegal truck yards are popping up across the GTA
Hundreds of illicit truck depots across the GTA are bedevilling residents with a constant barrage of noise and exhaust smell at all hours, endangering environmentally sensitive farmland and rapidly deteriorating highways, David Rider and Noor Javed report. They're being fuelled by a burgeoning e-commerce industry that demands an ever-increasing tide of tractor trailers to ferry goods. Residents and elected officials feel helpless against their spread - so what can be done?
A plea for help: Local leaders are pleading with Doug Ford's government for more tools to combat the lots, rather than relying on municipal charges that trigger years-long - and often fruitless - court battles.Why it matters: Many of the lots might look legit with proper signage and 24/7 security but they're operating without adequate approvals - causing serious environmental, health, safety and quality of life issues for nearby residents.A Toronto police officer was shot during a traffic stop
A 29-year-old plainclothes police officer is recovering in hospital after being shot in the abdomen Wednesday evening, Jennifer Pagliaro and Daniel Ramos report. They'd stopped a vehicle near Yonge and Eglinton during a robbery investigation when the shot rang out; another officer fired back and police cruisers soon swarmed the residential Lillian Street. Three people, including the suspected shooter, have been arrested. Take a closer look at what transpired.
The aftermath: The officer, who'd been on the force for five-and-a-half years, is "doing well" and is expected to make a full recovery, Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw told reporters.A dangerous duty: This marks the 637th time a Toronto police officer had been injured this year and the second officer to have been shot in the last 10 weeks. "This is unprecedented as far as what's going on in the city and we need it to stop," said the president of the Toronto Police Association.Doug Ford pledged to launch a "complete audit" of children's aid
In the wake of the Star's extensive reporting on the issue, Ford has vowed his government will do a "complete audit" of Ontario's children's aid agencies, Megan Ogilvie, Kristin Rushowy and Amy Dempsey Raven report. "We're hearing nightmare stories about the abuse of taxpayers' money" by the agencies, he told reporters yesterday. But can an audit actually fix a broken system?
ICYMI: Ford's comments followed Star reporting about kids and teens with complex health needs being housed in hotels, Airbnbs and office buildings because they are unable to get the proper care they need.Word from advocates: "To think that this crisis across the sectors is in any way about the mismanagement of money - and will be solved by addressing the mismanagement, if it exists - is dangerous and puts children at risk," said Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child and youth advocate from 2008 to 2019.WHAT ELSE

Document 135 of 500

'We can't take it anymore': Unchecked spread of illegal truck yards across the GTA angers residents, councils

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-cant-take-anymore-unchecked-spread-illegal/docview/3112504397/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+can%27t+take+it+anymore%27%3A+Unchecked+spread+of+illegal+truck+yards+across+the+GTA+angers+residents%2C+councils&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ayesha Faruq Ahmad lives in the Highlands of Castlemore, a Brampton neighbourhood billed as a "coveted" community noted for its quiet, leafy streets and spacious homes.
Yet for years, she and her neighbours in this community on the border with Caledon have been bedevilled by a constant barrage of noise and smell at all hours of the day and night. The land next to her home is home to a truck depot where dozens of tractor trailers have rumbled in and out around the clock, spewing diesel fumes, spilling oil and gas and shining headlights into homes.
"We are so tired, we can't take it anymore," Ahmad told a recent Caledon council meeting, describing the effects of the truck yard over her back fence.
The lot is an illicit operation that's one of hundreds sprouting up across the GTA - ranging from a handful of tractor trailers tucked next to a farmhouse to huge, gated operations with 24/7 security and constant traffic lumbering in and out - all fuelled by the burgeoning e-commerce that has helped fill Ontario highways with a growing number of tractor-trailers that need to be kept somewhere between hauls.
More than just a quality-of-life issue for a growing number of GTA residents like Ahmad, the proliferation of such lots - by some accounts hundreds near Caledon alone - has also increased the risk to drivers on local roads, potentially damaged environmentally sensitive farmland and caused the rapid deterioration of the region's public highways.
Local leaders plead for help
They have also become a source of frustration among local leaders, who have pleaded with Premier Doug Ford's government to give them more tools to close the illicit lots, rather than just rely on municipal charges that trigger years-long, often fruitless, court battles.
"I'm at my wit's end," said Caledon Mayor Annette Groves, "because we just aren't getting anywhere."
Her town, with rolling green hills and about 80,000 people, has an estimated 300 sites being used illegally, mostly for truck parking, the regional epicentre of a crisis that she says is nearing a "tipping point."
"We have illegal yards popping up in our community and the legal system doesn't permit us to do certain things," Groves said. "If I am put in a position that I have to start barricading entrances to the lots myself, that's what I am going to do."
Elected officials in municipalities across the 905 with large rural areas share Groves's frustration with seemingly unstoppable rise of huge truck lots established on agricultural and environmentally sensitive land not zoned or serviced for commercial activities.
Politicians from places such as Halton Hills, Milton and Whitchurch-Stouffville say it's become difficult to track new lots, much less initiate long, expensive legal efforts to try to shut them down.
Ahmad noted that the 12-acre lot that opened in 2017 near her home is still operating after a court ordered it shut down last year, following a years-long legal battle launched by the town and in which she testified. After a temporary drop in activity, the lot has only gotten busier, with up to 35 trucks a day, rolling in and out.
"We thought it would get better and yet we are suffering every day," she told the Star.
Peel is a trucking hub
Peel's central location near the 400-series highways has made it a warehouse hub for Ontario as online, quick-delivery shopping has flourished. Pressure for space to leave rigs and trailers between hauls has newly formed companies buying up agricultural and rural land to host trucks, many with more than 100 spots, with each renting for between $300 and $600 per month.
Many of the illegal enterprises look as if they are legit with signage and security apparatus, but they are operating without adequate approvals - causing serious environmental, health, safety and quality of life issues for residents living next door.
Milton councillor Colin Best took the Star on a quick tour of five allegedly illegal lots, all near Trafalgar Road and Highway 401. In some cases, a cluster of trucks could be seen in the distance tucked beside a farmhouse. Others are not hiding at all.
On agriculture-zoned land on Auburn Road, two lots side by side have tall fences, speed limit signs, security gates, signs advertising rental spaces and a constant rumbling parade of trucks in and out, on a rural road visibly crumbling at the edges.
"They are snubbing their noses at the municipalities," said Best, adding that realtors from Brampton and Mississauga are finding agricultural landowners willing to sell between five and 20 acres and advertising the lots as business sites.
Next to one allegedly illegal lot, a Mississauga realtors' sign touts "Future development employment zoning land for sale."
A 25-acre lot at 7499 Auburn Rd. is owned by a numbered company that paid $4.35 million for it in 2019. Corporate records list as directors Paramjit S. Sandhu and Balwinder Thandi, a Burlington realtor.
Thandi told the Star in an email: "We were advised by the seller that the property had been used for these parking purposes since before the bylaw came into effect and that it is therefore a legal nonconforming use. Our solicitor provided the Town of Milton with the requested documents."
Town officials confirmed that lot, along with the one at 7539 Auburn, owned by a different numbered company, are on land zoned A1 agriculture with no provisions for any commercial use. "A transportation terminal and/or truck parking lot are not permitted uses and no determination has been made with respect to any legal non-conforming use," according to an email from Caledon staff.
Douglas Allen, a paralegal representing Gurjinder Singh Brar of Brampton, a director of the firm that paid $3.51 million for 7539 Auburn in 2016, declined to comment, saying matters are before the courts: "Your questions involve complex events and circumstances, many of which my clients have no involvement in."
Bill Oughtred, a planning and development consultant engaged by the site's owner, emailed the Star, apparently intending to reply to Allen: "I would not give the reporter the report. Furthermore just give him limited information with respect to it being before the courts," he wrote.
"There are likely around 100 illegal truck parking locations through Caledon, Halton Hills and King. I would not encourage the reporter to single out this particular site by being given too much detail."
Councils have limited resources
In fact, Caledon, which has been at the forefront of tackling the illegal parking issue for several years, has identified some 300 lots being used illegally. Of those, 207 were investigated as illegal truck depots, triggering 61 completed prosecutions and 25 in progress.
"This has become a law-and-order issue, a planning issue, an environmental issue, a public health and safety issue," said Doug Maskell, one of the councillors on Caledon's illegal land use task force.
"We have been in a long-term battle," said Maskell, adding that he's met with provincial government officials several times. "We are doing what we can, with the powers that we have under the legislation passed by the province. And we have run out of ammunition."
Municipalities can lay various types of charges under the Planning Act, the Municipal Act and the Provincial Offences Act. They say, however, that prosecutions can drag on for years and, if successful, generate fines seen by illegal operators as a cost of doing business that won't deter them from continuing to operate.
Under the current rules, municipalities can fine individual landowners up to $25,000, plus $10,000 a day until they conform with the municipal law. If the property is owned by a corporation, those amounts are $50,000 and $25,000 a day.
Though provincial offences legislation allows for fines as high as $100,000, "fines never go that high," community services director Catherine McLean told Caledon council this month, adding that even repeated convictions may only result in levies as high as $70,000. "The fines are relatively low."
Doug Ford government responds
In August, Caledon and other municipalities asked the Ford government for help at an all-party provincial standing committee, which held a one-day hearing on ways the province can support municipalities trying to crack down on the illegal sites.
An aide for Municipal Affairs Minister Paul Calandra said there is no deadline for a report from the committee. If it isn't issued before the next provincial election, there may not ever be one.
Earlier this month, Groves sent Deputy Premier Sylvia Jones, MPP for Dufferin-Caledon, a letter demanding urgent action to "strengthen municipal enforcement powers." They include doubling the maximum fines, letting municipalities physically bar property entrances, and giving them the ability to put fines and orders on property titles, potentially impacting financing.
"For over a decade we've been exercising every tool we have," Groves wrote. "Without provincial intervention, there is the risk that this issue will grow to an unmanageable state and continue to spread throughout the province."
The Star's request for an interview with Calandra about the truck lot issue triggered an email response: "Municipalities have the authority to pass and amend zoning bylaws that regulate the use of land and contain specific requirements that are legally enforceable, including land use permissions and performance standards." Calandra's response pointed to the current fines.
For Caledon's Maskell, that simply isn't enough to halt the rising tide. "More stringent laws could be passed by the province in an afternoon," he said. "It's a no-brainer."
Meanwhile, court delays are also helping to keep these truck lots open in Halton Hills, according to Mayor Ann Lawlor, noting that a 2023 request by her town for an injunction against one operator wasn't heard until 2024.
Lawlor said that her mostly rural community has about 50 properties with illegal land uses, mostly truck lots.
"Some of our prime agricultural land is being polluted," she said. "People are buying up agricultural properties, which are somewhat cheaper because of their zoning than industrial and commercial properties for the same use."
The "cowboys," as she calls them, build secondary entrances, erect buildings without permits or inspection, truck in dirty fill for the tractor trailers to drive on and change environmental features on the land.
"The cowboys who flout the bylaws simply don't care," she said.
Even after charging the companies under municipal bylaws, cities say they have no way to enforce the orders.
In January 2023, Caledon won a court order against two trucking companies operating illegally, ordering them to "cease operations" according to a town press release. More than a year later, Falcon Xpress - the operator of the truck lot next to Ahmad's home - continues to operate in plain sight.
In an email, Falcon said its company formed to operate the lot "has been complying with the order that was issued on consent after it cooperated with the Town of Caledon with respect to zoning issues that were raised.
Falcon said "we will continue to work with all stakeholders, including the Town of Caledon and its residents, to ensure harmony in the community in which we all work and live."
A Caledon spokesperson, in a statement to the Star on Wednesday, said: "The town considers that 6086 Mayfield remains in violation of the court order from 2023 and staff are working towards a court date as soon as possible to have the matter return to court."
Multiple tractor-trailers and buildings were visible on the Falcon site as recently as Tuesday morning, including regular truck traffic in and out of the yard after stops at a building near the entrance.
"If this fails," said Ahmad, "they can't remove a single truck yard from Caledon."
Groves shares Ahmad's frustration, including her fears about road safety, with the increase in large trucks on local roads, noting several road fatalities that involved large trucks in the region in recent weeks.
"It makes me furious that these bad actors are here making millions at our residents' expense," said Groves.
The Ontario Trucking Association has urged all levels of government to work together to address the lack of available truck terminals, but agrees that "illegal truck parking should not be tolerated and all levels of government need to enforce the law on these practices," according to association spokesperson Marcho Beghetto.
Mark Sraga, who retired last year as Caledon's head of municipal law enforcement, said that demand for inexpensive spots to park rigs near Mississauga and Brampton is only going to increase.
Municipalities along the 401 need to rezone more land for industrial use including legal truck lots, he said, so operators and drivers can be forced to follow the normal rules, including having owners pay to service the land and pay commercial property taxes rather than the much lower agricultural rate.
At the same time, the Ontario government needs to quickly give local officials stronger measures, such as the power to block entrances to illegal sites - just as they were allowed to do with illegal cannabis shops, he said.
"There is growing demand and trying to fight it is a losing battle," Sraga said. "It's like whack-a-mole - you shut one down and it pops up somewhere else because these trucks have to go somewhere."
CAPTION: Some illegal truck yards consist of a few tractor trailers tucked against a farmhouse. Others are 24/7 operations with round-the-clock security.A driver prepares to enter a large truck yard built on agricultural land on Auburn Road in Milton."We have run out of ammunition," says Caledon councillor Doug Maskell of his municipality's fight to shutdown illegal truck yards.The growth in e-commerce has helped fuel demand for trucking and is expected to only grow in the future.Ayesha Faruq Ahmad's home is across the street from an illegal truck yard on Mayfield Road that continues to operate despite a court ordering it to shut down.Ayesha Faruq is photographed in a subdivision off of Mayfield Road. Across the road is Falcon Transport, an illegal truck lot in Caledon East. Brampton, ON. September 26, 2024.

Subject: Agriculture; Landowners; Rural areas; Roads & highways; Trucks

Title: 'We can't take it anymore': Unchecked spread of illegal truck yards across the GTA angers residents, councils
