How do we stop gun violence in Toronto?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/how-do-we-stop-gun-violence-toronto/docview/3112504325/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+do+we+stop+gun+violence+in+Toronto%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: How do we stop it?
Family devastated after man shot dead at plaza in Weston, Sept. 30
The Star's headline asked how we stop gun violence. The gun-related violence and deaths in our city are shocking and tragic. Yes, the root causes need addressing and fixing, and so does the relentless flow of illegal guns. But those are longer-term solutions. We need a more immediate solution and it's available right in our community. Let's end the silence and encourage people to come forward, use Crime Stoppers and make the anonymous calls to inform authorities on criminal activity. Someone always knows something. Family members, partners, friends and associates can all help solve this by not staying silent. Don't wait for a tragedy; call in when you know of a gun. Silence is only helping the criminals. Don't want to feel like a snitch? Think of that poor father of 16 who was killed. He didn't have to die. Someone knew something.
Eugene Bolvary, Toronto
Minister and community advocate Rev. Sky Starr is right. All levels of government must deal with the root causes of the gun violence that plagues racialized communities. However, systemic racism gets in the way of really ambitious action. If it were mainly white people victimized in the over 230 shootings, more would be done. We need programs to lure young men away from gang membership. We need unrelenting ad campaigns in these neighbourhoods that stigmatize gun ownership and target their macho appeal. Similar ad campaigns against Big Tobacco and drunk driving saved countless lives. Members of these communities must be consulted every step of the way. After all, those restive males who carry guns truly terrorize their communities and limit the safe mobility of residents. Is there a role for our armed forces, in combination with police, to sweep whole neighbourhoods for guns if gun amnesties fail? Would local residents be willing to see such drastic measures taken? The federal government must continuously lobby the U.S. and at the UN against the American overproduction of rapid-fire guns destined for civilian sale, since many such guns are involved in shootings that destabilize societies, as we've seen in many U.S. cities, Mexico and Central America. It should be illegal to own stocks in companies that manufacture prohibited firearms. Pro-gun politicians must be pressured to reverse their unhelpful stances on gun control, since the measures they support are reactive in nature, rather than preventive.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Joly's speech to the UN
Mélanie Joly warns UN about leaders who undermine democracy, Sept. 30
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly's brilliant speech at the UN struck a chord with me. She talked about the weaponization of the word "freedom" to suit political purposes. Instead, she urged global leaders to reclaim freedom, in all its forms, as a basic human right. After her thoughtful observations on this subject, Joly ended her speech by addressing the unacceptable male-only leadership of the UN for the last 80 years! Sadly, this is a fact that appears to overlook the reality that women are the finest negotiators on the planet! Without the physicality or propensity of men to fight, women must negotiate during their whole lives. Also, as bearers of the entire human race, they value all lives versus seeing lives as expendable to accomplish certain leaders' goals. Peace and security have always been our mantra. My vote goes to Joly to lead us in that direction as head of the UN!
Aileen Pollock, Toronto
For the first time in a long time I was uplifted reading the morning Star. I was surprised to read Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly stood at the UN and spoke like a Liberal. I'm unsure how she got through the party gatekeepers with the content but it made me proud of Canadian Liberal values. In front of the usual audience of power brokers and enabling war mongers, she called for the liberation of the word "freedom" and an end of the politics of division. Take note, right-wing trolls, liberalism in Canada is not dead yet.
Ken Pyette, Toronto
Ford reaction distracts from Grassy Narrows
Student field trip to protest 'disgusting,' says Doug Ford, promising those responsible will be 'held accountable,' Sept. 23
It is very disappointing that the only response of Premier Doug Ford has been to speak about the few pro-Palestinian demonstrators at the 8,000-strong rally in support of the people of Grassy Narrows. I was there. There were a few pro-Palestinian demonstrators in little groups of three or four scattered among thousands of people. Students involved were not being led by teachers or anyone else. Now, rather than using the events to help educate students about Indigenous issues, Ford and his ilk have planted in their minds that participation is dangerous. This is to the detriment of reconciliation. Ford's only public response to this huge event completely ignored the main issue.
Lynda Lange, Toronto
Happy birthday, Jimmy Carter
Jimmy Carter and his hometown of Plains celebrate the 39th president's 100th birthday, Oct. 1
Former U.S. president Jimmy Carter will not escape history. Not only is he the oldest living U.S. president, he is the first U.S. president to reach the age of 100 - a tough act to follow. Whatever Carter lacked in his one presidential term, he earned accolades in his personal life. This legend in his own time has been an exceptional humanitarian and the epitome of a good Christian. He is an exemplary human being and just as he wanted, Carter has made his life count for something. As a humble servant to God, Carter never stopped loving and caring for others. God bless America and God bless Jimmy Carter.
JoAnn Lee Frank, Clearwater, Fla.
Keep Vladdy and Bichette together
Vladimir Guerrero Jr. is often the only reason to watch the Blue Jays. Somebody tell the front office: DiManno, Sept. 30
The vast majority of the Blue Jays faithful fans are waiting for the dynamic duo from Cleveland to wake up and put a well earned, huge long-term contract in front of Vladimir Guerrero Jr. It would not surprise me if Jr. responds by telling them he will sign when Bo Bichette is signed. These two have played together throughout the Jays' minor system and the bond between them is deep. Vladdy has witnessed Teoscar Hernandez, Lourdes Gurriel Jr. and Gabriel Moreno among others cast aside in poor trades. He must be worried about Bo. So are we. Perhaps it is time to jettison Ross Atkins and Mark Shapiro and bring in solid baseball executives to take charge of the rebuild.
David Maki, Toronto
CAPTION: Women cry at the scene where a man was shot at a strip mall at the northwest corner of Jane Street and Lawrence Avenue.

Document 137 of 500

Ford's bungling of Ontario's nursing shortage is aimed at undermining public health care

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/fords-bungling-ontarios-nursing-shortage-is-aimed/docview/3112504319/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+bungling+of+Ontario%27s+nursing+shortage+is+aimed+at+undermining+public+health+care&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Figuring out how to undermine support for public health care isn't rocket science. All you have to do is destroy the public system, and then people will be willing to accept private health care.This appears to be Doug Ford's strategy for weakening the strong attachment Ontarians feel for public health care, thereby enabling his government to plow ahead with plans to further open up lucrative profit opportunities for private health-care businesses.This would explain, for instance, Ford's bizarre handling of the nursing shortage in Ontario.Despite nurses being underpaid, exhausted and demoralized by the pandemic, Ford kept a one per cent per year cap on their wage increases, even as inflation spiked to eight per cent.This angered nurses and prompted them to leave the public system in large numbers, generating chaos and longer wait times throughout the system - and creating a situation where the province is now paying hundreds of millions of dollars extra per year as Ontario's hospitals are forced to hire nurses from private agencies at much higher rates.This bungled handling of the nursing shortage makes no sense, unless Ford was trying to undermine the public's confidence in our health care system - in which case he appears to have succeeded.A poll released last week found Canadians feel their access to medical care has worsened in the last few years and that wait-lists are now too long.As the Globe and Mail reported on its front page, the poll found that, "as a result, 73 per cent now say they're open to major health-care reform, including a larger role for the private sector."This finding was no doubt sweet music for Premier Ford, since it suggests his plan to further privatize Ontario's health-care system - allowing private clinics to do knee and hip replacements, cataract surgery, colonoscopies, etc. - is in line with what people want.But on closer examination, the poll presents little evidence that Canadians favour more private health care.Rather the poll, conducted by the public relations firm Navigator - which is, among other things, a registered lobbyist for the private health-care company Medcan - seems largely aimed at steering respondents away from solutions that would involve strengthening the public system.Participants were asked which of two statements most closely resembles their view: "Canada's health-care system is one of the best in the world and we should keep it as it is." Or: "Canada's health-care system does not deliver on its promises and is in need of a major reform."Given only these two choices, 73 per cent of respondents favoured major reform over doing nothing.But what we need to know is what kind of reform people want - do they, for instance, want more privatization, or do they want reform within the public system?This question is never asked.When asked what their concerns were with the public system, respondents clearly indicated their main concerns were staff shortages and long wait times.The poll asks them whether, given the long wait times for surgery, they would support some surgeries being done by the private sector? Respondents largely supported this, particularly if the private surgeries were publicly funded.What does this tell us, however, other than that people are very concerned about long surgery wait-lists? And if they're only offered one option for solving the problem - private surgery clinics - they'll support that option.Why not give them another option such as would they support our public hospitals offering additional surgeries on weekends and evenings to reduce long wait-lists (as suggested by some Ontario doctors)?Interestingly, both staff shortages and long wait-lists in Ontario are problems that were greatly exacerbated by Ford's mishandling of the nursing crisis.Could it be that the dissatisfaction with our health-care system may be best solved - not by introducing a lot of private, profit-making clinics - but simply by paying nurses good wages within the public system?
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford attends an announcement at Seneca College, in King City, Ont., Friday, Feb. 9, 2024.

Document 138 of 500

Ford boosts limits on 400-series highways

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-boosts-limits-on-400-series-highways/docview/3112401876/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+boosts+limits+on+400-series+highways&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford feels the need for speed.
Against the backdrop of a potential spring provincial election, Ford on Wednesday said the speed limit on all 400-series highways will be raised from 100 km/h to 110 km/h "where it is safe to do so."
Speaking to reporters at a campaign-style announcement in East York to tout construction on the Ontario Line subway, the premier said he was hitting the gas pedal to keep motorists moving.
"Earlier this year, we raised the speed limit on 10 additional sections of 400-series highways and today I've directed the minister to raise the speed limit to 110 kilometres on all 400 series highways where it is safe to do so," he said.
"I'm going to repeat that: where it is safe to do. So we're leaving no stone unturned to help fight gridlock on our roads, reduce travel times and allow people to spend less time commuting and more time at home with their families, and that includes the Ontario Line, which is going to be a game changer for Toronto and the entire region."
Ford, who last week promised a feasibility study into a potential 60-kilometre tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga and Brampton to Scarborough that could cost tens of billions of dollars, stressed "everything's on the table right now" to reduce gridlock.
The Toronto Region Board of Trade has estimated that traffic snarls cost the provincial economy $11 billion annually in lost productivity.
Asked about proposals from Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles to eliminate tolls on the privately owned Highway 407 so more cars and trucks could use it, Ford conceded such a move could be in the works.
"We'll look at the 407 and see exactly which way are we going. We own about 22 kilometres on the far (eastern) end of the 407 and there's other opportunities along the 407," he said, noting motorists pay about $1.4 billion in tolls annually.
"All options are on the table, maybe both options. Let's see. But we know one thing is costing our economy $11 billion a year. This is costing people over 100 hours of sitting in traffic on the 401," he said.
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener-Centre) said the speed limit gambit proves "the Ford government doesn't have a serious plan to make it easier for Ontarians to get around."
"The Eglinton LRT looks like it won't open until at least 2025, the Finch West LRT is now delayed too, and gridlock on the 401 has become so bad that it's almost unbearable," said Clancy.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Speed limits; Automobile drivers; Roads & highways; Premier-Canada

People: Ford, Doug

Document 139 of 500

Tent encampments are 'big problem,' Ford says: Premier signals Ontario will take action to find homes for those living in public areas

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tent-encampments-are-big-problem-ford-says/docview/3112401677/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tent+encampments+are+%27big+problem%2C%27+Ford+says%3A+Premier+signals+Ontario+will+take+action+to+find+homes+for+those+living+in+public+areas&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is signalling the province will take action to deal with homeless tent encampments in Toronto parks and other communities across Ontario.
"It's a big problem," said Ford, whose office at the Ontario legislature is about 400 metres south of an encampment at city-run Queen's Park.
"We've got to find homes for these folks as well, and that's our goal - all three levels of government. But I'll tell you, communities across the province, they're just beside themselves," the premier said Wednesday at an unrelated announcement in East York.
"I got a call yesterday. There's needles in the park right beside a place (with) encampments all over the place. It's unacceptable, and we have to all work together to find them shelter and get them out of the public spaces. You just can't be doing it."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who was also at the announcement, said the housing crisis is compounded by the city's increasing population, and "it's hard to find affordable housing for everyone, which is why you see all our shelters are full. We now have 12,000 people in our shelters; half of them are refugees claimants. So the situation is really difficult."
She acknowledged concerns raised by area residents about the encampments, saying "in big parks, small parks - especially in small parks ... (the tents) are right beside playgrounds, and it's just really difficult for families and the kids, because then they have no room for the kids to play. So, we're working hard."
She said a Canada-Ontario rent supplement, a program that will resume this fall, helps moves families out of shelters "which means that we can then make room for people that are living in tents, as the winter arrives in a few months - we can now get them from the encampments into shelters so that they're indoors and don't have to endure the outdoor environment."
Chow noted that shelter spots and housing were found for people living in tents in Grange Park, and many at Allan Gardens.
Last week, Ford said homeless people in encampments need to "get off your a-s-s and start working like everyone else," though was criticized for not considering how the cost of food or housing impacts people, especially those in low-wage jobs or on social assistance.
The premier has said he's heard from a number of Ontarians and municipal officials who are "rightly frustrated" with encampments.
Encampments are an issue jurisdictions across North America are struggling with post-pandemic as homelessness, mental health issues and substance abuse are on the rise.
In 2021, Toronto began enforcing the no-camping rules in some parks, clearing out homeless campers, but has since backed off that approach.
Hamilton is now allowing encampments with small cabins, making social supports available, until housing is found for them.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy and Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau

Full text: How do we stop gun violence
in Toronto?
Family devastated after man shot dead at plaza in Weston, Sept. 30
The gun-related violence and deaths in our city are shocking and tragic. Yes, the root causes need addressing and fixing, and so does the relentless flow of illegal guns. But those are longer-term solutions. We need a more immediate solution and it's available right in our community.
Let's end the silence and encourage people to come forward, use Crime Stoppers and make the anonymous calls to inform authorities on criminal activity. Someone always knows something. Family members, partners, friends and associates can all help solve this by not staying silent.
Eugene Bolvary, Toronto

Minister and community advocate Rev. Sky Starr is right. All levels of government must deal with the root causes of the gun violence that plagues racialized communities.
We need programs to lure young men away from gang membership. We need unrelenting ad campaigns in these neighbourhoods that stigmatize gun ownership and target their macho appeal. Similar ad campaigns against Big Tobacco and drunk driving have saved countless lives.
Members of these communities must be consulted every step of the way. After all, those restive males who carry guns truly terrorize their communities and limit the safe mobility of residents.
Is there a role for our armed forces, in combination with police, to sweep whole neighbourhoods for guns if gun amnesties fail? Would local residents be willing to see such drastic measures taken?
The federal government must continuously lobby the U.S. and at the UN against the American overproduction of rapid-fire guns destined for civilian sale, since many such guns are involved in shootings that destabilize societies, as we've seen in many U.S. cities, Mexico and Central America. It should be illegal to own stocks in companies that manufacture prohibited firearms.
Pro-gun politicians must be pressured to reverse their unhelpful stances on gun control, since the measures they support are reactive in nature, rather than preventive.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Joly's speech
to UN strikes feminist chord
Mélanie Joly warns UN about leaders who undermine democracy, Sept. 30
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly's brilliant speech at the UN struck a chord with me. She talked about the weaponization of the word "freedom" to suit political purposes. Instead, she urged global leaders to reclaim freedom, in all its forms, as a basic human right.
After her thoughtful observations on this subject, Joly ended her speech by addressing the unacceptable male-only leadership of the UN for the past 80 years! Sadly, this is a fact that appears to overlook the reality that women are the finest negotiators on the planet. Without the physicality or propensity of men to fight, women must negotiate during their whole lives.
Also, as bearers of the entire human race, they value all lives versus seeing lives as expendable to accomplish certain leaders' goals. Peace and security have always been our mantra.
My vote goes to Joly to lead us in that direction as head of the UN.
Aileen Pollock, Toronto

For the first time in a long time I was uplifted reading the Star. I was surprised to read Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly stood at the UN and spoke like a Liberal. I'm unsure how she got through the party gatekeepers with the content, but it made me proud of Canadian Liberal values.
In front of the usual audience of power brokers and enabling war mongers, she called for the liberation of the word "freedom" and an end of the politics of division. Take note, right-wing trolls, liberalism in Canada is not dead yet.
Ken Pyette, Toronto
Ford reaction distracts from Grassy Narrows
Student field trip to protest 'disgusting,' says Doug Ford, promising those responsible will be 'held accountable,' Sept. 23
It is very disappointing that the only response of Premier Doug Ford has been to speak about the few pro-Palestinian demonstrators at the 8,000-strong rally in support of the people of Grassy Narrows.
I was there. There were a few pro-Palestinian demonstrators in little groups of three or four scattered among thousands of people. Students involved were not being led by teachers or anyone else.
Now, rather than using the events to help educate students about Indigenous issues, Ford and his ilk have planted in their minds that participation is dangerous. This is to the detriment of reconciliation.
Ford's only public response to this huge event completely ignored the main issue.
Lynda Lange, Toronto
Jimmy Carter
became a legend
in his own time
Jimmy Carter and his hometown of Plains celebrate the 39th president's 100th birthday, Oct. 1
Former U.S. president Jimmy Carter will not escape history. Not only is he the oldest living U.S. president, he is the first U.S. president to reach the age of 100 - a tough act to follow.
Whatever Carter lacked in his one presidential term, he earned accolades in his personal life. This legend in his own time has been an exceptional humanitarian and the epitome of a good Christian.
He is an exemplary human being and, just as he wanted, Carter has made his life count for something.
JoAnn Lee Frank,
Clearwater, Fla.

Document 141 of 500

Ford undermines system by bungling

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-undermines-system-bungling/docview/3112401454/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+undermines+system+by+bungling&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Figuring out how to undermine support for public health care isn't rocket science. All you have to do is destroy the public system, and then people will be willing to accept private health care.
This appears to be Doug Ford's strategy for weakening the strong attachment Ontarians feel for public health care, thereby enabling his government to plow ahead with plans to further open up lucrative profit opportunities for private health-care businesses.
This would explain, for instance, Ford's bizarre handling of the nursing shortage in Ontario. Despite nurses being underpaid, exhausted and demoralized by the pandemic, Ford kept a one per cent a year cap on their wage increases, even as inflation spiked to eight per cent.
This angered nurses and prompted them to leave the public system in large numbers, generating chaos and longer wait times throughout the system - and creating a situation where the province is now paying hundreds of millions of dollars extra per year as Ontario's hospitals are forced to hire nurses from private agencies at much higher rates.
This bungled handling of the nursing shortage makes no sense, unless Ford was trying to undermine the public's confidence in our health care system - in which case he appears to have succeeded.
A poll released last week found Canadians feel their access to medical care has worsened in the last few years and that wait-lists are now too long. As the Globe and Mail reported on its front page, the poll found that, "as a result, 73 per cent now say they're open to major health-care reform, including a larger role for the private sector."
This finding was no doubt sweet music for Premier Ford, since it suggests his plan to further privatize Ontario's health-care system - allowing private clinics to do knee and hip replacements, cataract surgery, colonoscopies, etc. - is in line with what people want.
But on closer examination, the poll presents little evidence that Canadians favour more private health care. Rather the poll, conducted by the public relations firm Navigator - which is, among other things, a registered lobbyist for the private health-care company Medcan - seems largely aimed at steering respondents away from solutions that would involve strengthening the public system.
Participants were asked which of two statements most closely resembles their view: "Canada's health-care system is one of the best in the world and we should keep it as it is." Or: "Canada's health-care system does not deliver on its promises and is in need of a major reform."
Given only these two choices, 73 per cent of respondents favoured major reform over doing nothing.
But what we need to know is what kind of reform people want - do they, for instance, want more privatization, or do they want reform within the public system?
This question is never asked.
When asked what their concerns were with the public system, respondents clearly indicated their main concerns were staff shortages and long wait times.
The poll asks them whether, given the long wait times for surgery, they would support some surgeries being done by the private sector? Respondents largely supported this, particularly if the private surgeries were publicly funded.
What does this tell us, however, other than that people are very concerned about long surgery wait-lists? And if they're only offered one option for solving the problem - private surgery clinics - they'll support that option.
Why not give them another option such as would they support our public hospitals offering additional surgeries on weekends and evenings to reduce long wait-lists (as suggested by some Ontario doctors)?
Interestingly, both staff shortages and long wait-lists in Ontario are problems that were greatly exacerbated by Ford's mishandling of the nursing crisis.
Could it be that the dissatisfaction with our health-care system may be best solved - not by introducing a lot of private, profit-making clinics - but simply by paying nurses good wages within the public system?
Linda McQuaig is an author, journalist and a freelance
contributing columnist for
The Star.
CAPTION: Despite nurses being underpaid, exhausted and demoralized by the pandemic, Doug Ford kept a one per cent a year cap on their wage increases, Linda McQuaig writes, even as inflation spiked to eight per cent.Chris YoungThe Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Linda McQuaig OPINION Linda McQuaig is an author, journalist and a freelance contributing columnist for The Star.;

Document 142 of 500

Ford pledges 'complete audit' of children's aid sector: Groups 'disappointed' by premier's response to Star series on kids with complex needs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-pledges-complete-audit-childrens-aid-sector/docview/3112401222/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+pledges+%27complete+audit%27+of+children%27s+aid+sector%3A+Groups+%27disappointed%27+by+premier%27s+response+to+Star+series+on+kids+with+complex+needs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has pledged his government will do a "complete audit" of Ontario children's aid societies, saying that he's heard "nightmare stories" about the misuse of taxpayer dollars by the agencies.
His comments Wednesday came in response to Star reporting that child welfare leaders are sounding the alarm about kids and teens with complex health needs being housed in hotels, Airbnbs and office buildings because they are unable to get the medical help and specialized treatments they need.
Ford said his government has increased funding to children's aid societies, but alleged some are abusing taxpayer money rather than properly spending those funds on kids.
"We're hearing nightmare stories about the abuse of taxpayers' money - I've heard stories of some of these agencies working in Taj Mahals. They're paying rent - $100,000 for rent," Ford said when asked about the issue at an unrelated announcement in East York.
The Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services confirmed in a written statement to the Star that it is "continuing with a review and audit of the sector to better understand how children and youth are being served by the child welfare system," with more details to come.
The organization that represents most of the province's children's aid societies said in a statement to the Star that Ford's comments do not address the lack of supports, services and "appropriate placements" for children and youth with complex needs, calling his response "disappointing."
"Addressing the inadequate access to community-based and out-of-home care is a responsibility that cuts across all levels of government and provincial partners, community-based organizations, and service providers," said Solomon Owoo, chief executive of the Ontario Association of Children's Aid Societies (OACAS).
Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child and youth advocate from 2008 to 2019, said he was angered by the premier's comments Wednesday.
"To think that this crisis across the sectors is in any way about the mismanagement of money - and will be solved by addressing the mismanagement, if it exists - is dangerous and puts children at risk. Pure and simple," Elman said.
A Star investigation has revealed that hundreds of Ontario children with complex health needs have been living in hotels, Airbnbs, rental apartments, offices and even camping trailers because the province has no live-in treatment options for them. New figures from OACAS show the number of kids and teens in the care of child welfare agencies and living in unlicensed settings has tripled since 2021.
Children with complex needs often have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression, or substance abuse and self-harm, and their families typically struggle to get them timely and adequate medical care and supports.
Child welfare leaders across the province are reporting an increasing number of desperate families relinquishing care of their kids because they've been unable to get help in the community. But because children's aid societies do not have faster access to scarce treatment beds or intensive services, the agencies are forced to house young people with complex mental health and developmental needs in unlicensed settings, which in turn leads to high costs for agencies.
It's unclear how much is being spent across the province on housing kids in makeshift settings. In the year ending in March 2024, there were 354 children whose care had each cost child welfare agencies more than $200,000 per year; the Star has learned of at least four children and youth for whom the annual cost of housing and supervision is roughly $1 million each.
In the wake of Star reporting, child advocates and opposition MPPs are calling on the Ford government to launch an emergency response and say the province can no longer "look away" from the crisis.
On Wednesday, Ford said the province has boosted funding over the past decade and will do "a complete audit" on children's aid societies, adding: "If we see funds not being spent properly on the kids, guess what? You're looking for another job. Simple as that."
The total number of children in the care of child welfare agencies has gone down by about a third over the past decade. Yet the agencies are still "running non-stop deficits," he added. "Not all, but of a lot of them, and we've increased (funding) ... It's just unacceptable."
Child welfare leaders previously told the Star that deficits stem from creating emergency placements for children with complex needs. The number of children and youth needing high-cost care has doubled in the past three years, according to OACAS data.
This week, OACAS sent an open letter to Ford calling on the province to take "immediate action" to ensure community-based organizations and service providers have enough funding and staff to "truly respond to the needs of children, youth and families."
The organization pointed to a June report from the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario that found the government had underfunded the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services by an estimated $3.7 billion less than required to fulfil existing programs and announced commitments.
Close to 30 agencies and associations, including municipalities, non-profits, and organizations in mental health care, immigration and refugee services, and intellectual and developmental disabilities, among others, signed the OACAS letter pushing for greater investments in community-based organizations that serve children and youth.
CREDIT: Megan Ogilvie, Kristin Rushowy and Amy Dempsey Raven Toronto Star

Document 143 of 500

Is Trudeau preventing a Liberal civil war?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-trudeau-preventing-liberal-civil-war/docview/3112401212/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Trudeau+preventing+a+Liberal+civil+war%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Justin Trudeau's determination to stay the leader of the federal Liberal Party - and, of course, prime minister - is more than ego. He is probably worried about the eruption of a second Liberal civil war.
Until the sudden explosion of Pierre Trudeau into leadership in the 1960s, the party was a model of unity. For more than 50 years, Liberals presented a smiling portrait of a party united. That ended with the first Trudeau's election.
Liberals were always far from homogenous ideologically. They understood, however, the electoral power of a publicly united family. Blue Liberals on Bay Street and parts of Ontario would tolerate unemployment insurance and better pensions and so on, so long as Canadian corporate interests were served. More progressive Liberals, centred in Toronto, Vancouver, Quebec and Atlantic Canada, demanded a broader and deeper social safety net and were dismissive of Bay Street. Western Liberals, then as now, did not count.
In 1968, Blue Grits were confident that corporate icon Bob Winters would serve their best interests. When Trudeau crushed him by a 10-point margin in the leadership contest, the first cracks in Liberal unity slowly began to emerge.
One of Justin Trudeau's greatest achievements is unknown to most Canadians. He salvaged Liberal unity after an often bloody three decade civil war and rebuilt the party under a new very centrist regime. He will fight hard to prevent the party sliding back into factional strife when he has gone.
John Turner, the next corporate Liberal prince, fired off monthly diatribes against his former boss from the safety of his Bay Street law office for years after his "retirement" from cabinet. Corporate Canada, meanwhile, whispered against Trudeau from behind the curtains.
Pierre Trudeau was never "a party guy." His obsession was Canadian unity and the Liberals were merely his vehicle. His son is very different. He learned the lessons of a fractious party. Turner's disastrous performance led to Brian Mulroney's landslide victory. Jean Chrétien's wilful baiting of the blue Liberals first ended with Martin's victory, but was soon followed by his defeat. A defeated party then chose two of the most inept party leaders Canada has seen, Stéphane Dion and Michael Ignatieff.
Justin Trudeau does indulge the nearly universal instinct of long-term leaders: undermine every prospective successor. As a result, Liberals today have no one to succeed him. No minister has favourable voter recognition, let alone Trudeau-like charisma. So Trudeau, worried not to repeat his father's failure, is trapped.
Part of Justin Trudeau's sometimes-brutal tactics to prevent the civil war was to remove its main players. Many Chrétien/Martin-era party elders are still enraged at Trudeau and his enforcer, Gerry Butts; slamming the door in their faces. After a defeat, expect many of them to go public. They have the potential to wreak havoc, especially the corporate Liberals among them.
The parallels between 1968 and today are eerie. A rich banker, Mark Carney, whose political experience is non-existent, is, like Bob Winters, the blue Liberals' current messiah. And progressive Liberals will probably squabble among themselves about which second stringer they should rally behind - absent a new Trudeau.
Trudeau's calculus for staying - beyond ego and hubris - must therefore be this. If he loses to Poilievre, he will still leave the Liberals after a hard-fought and competent campaign and probably save more of the furniture than anyone else could. More importantly, though, he will still have the power to crush faction fighting. If he leaves before the next election, a Liberal Kim Campbell will lead the party to greater ruin.
The prime minister will retire, but not remain silent, taking comfort in a 10-year premiership and some significant policy achievements. But also he will get history's credit as the leader who helped the Liberals move from defeat to rebuilding not once, but twice, and successfully blocked a new civil war.
Trudeau understands the value of a long game he controls. He will leave Pierre Poilievre shouting pathetically after his sixth or 16th failed effort to defeat his government. Then Trudeau will call an election on his timing, and campaign hard, as if his legacy depends on it - and it does.
Robin V. Sears was an NDP strategist for 20 years and later served as a communications adviser to businesses and governments on three continents. He is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.
CREDIT: Robin Sears OPINION Robin V. Sears was an NDP strategist for 20 years and later served as a communications adviser to businesses and governments on three continents. He is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.;

Eglinton LRT unlikely to open this year: Province will get involved as 'last resort,' premier says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/eglinton-lrt-unlikely-open-this-year/docview/3112397965/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Eglinton+LRT+unlikely+to+open+this+year%3A+Province+will+get+involved+as+%27last+resort%2C%27+premier+says&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown LRT may not open until the new year, the premier of Ontario said on Wednesday.
"Are we going to hit the next few months? Hopefully. If not, it will be in the new year," Doug Ford told reporters at a press conference about the Ontario Line.
Provincial agency Metrolinx's CEO Phil Verster has previously said the public will be given three months' advance notice when the transit agency is ready to open the Eglinton LRT. With less than three months left in 2024, the troubled light rail line does not appear to be on schedule to open this year.
Andrea Ernesaks, a Metrolinx spokesperson, confirmed with the Star on Wednesday that three-month promise is still applicable but would not give an opening date.
"Other than sighing, I don't know how to respond to that," said Mayor Olivia Chow, following a sigh and long pause, at an unrelated press conference later on Wednesday. "Please just get it done."
The 19-kilometre Eglinton LRT was originally supposed to open in 2020, with the Finch West LRT slated to be running in 2023. The Finch light rail line has also been delayed.
Chow went on to describe conversations she's had with residents living near the Eglinton LRT who "are ready," but can't use the transit line in a city that made it clear during budget consultations that mobility is among the top priorities.
"It's disappointing. It's frustrating," she said.
At his press conference Wednesday, Ford said that all the construction is done, but operators still need to be trained and trains need to be tested. "My number one concern is ... making sure it's safe," he said.
When asked what indicators the premier uses to measure Verster's success, Ford said, "We hold him and Metrolinx accountable all the time. But I'm a fan of Phil."
Verster, who made $838,097 last year according to Ontario's Sunshine List, became CEO of Metrolinx in 2017 and pledged transparency around transit decisions while at the helm.
The Star revealed this year that Metrolinx was on the verge of revealing the expected opening date of the Eglinton Crosstown in 2023, but Ford's office ordered it be kept secret. Verster held a press conference in September last year at which he declared he had a good idea of when the line would be done, but had decided not to announce it. The agency has yet to publish a timeline for when either LRT will take its first passengers.
The consortium contracted to design, build and maintain the Finch Avenue light rail, Mosaic Transit Partners, launched a court battle this week, as first reported by Global News, claiming Metrolinx had entered into a deal with the TTC that is interfering with the completion of the Finch West LRT. Last year, the group building the Eglinton Crosstown LRT filed a similar complaint about that project.
"Eglinton compared to Finch is like night and day ... The two sides need to sort out their differences," Ford said, noting that as "a last resort" his government will get involved but stopped short of detailing what that means.
CREDIT: Mahdis Habibinia Toronto Star

Document 145 of 500

In for the long haul: Residents and local leaders face an uphill battle against hundreds of illicit truck lots that operate in the GTA, posing environmental and safety risks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/long-haul/docview/3112397953/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+for+the+long+haul%3A+Residents+and+local+leaders+face+an+uphill+battle+against+hundreds+of+illicit+truck+lots+that+operate+in+the+GTA%2C+posing+environmental+and+safety+risks&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ayesha Faruq Ahmad lives in the Highlands of Castlemore, a Brampton neighbourhood billed as a "coveted" community noted for its quiet, leafy streets and spacious homes.
Yet for years, she and her neighbours in this community on the border with Caledon have been bedevilled by a constant barrage of noise and smell at all hours of the day and night. The land next to her home is the site of a truck depot where dozens of tractor-trailers have rumbled in and out around the clock, spewing diesel fumes, spilling oil and gas and shining headlights into homes.
"We are so tired, we can't take it anymore," Ahmad told a recent Caledon council meeting, describing the effects of the truck yard over her back fence.
The lot is an illicit operation that's one of hundreds sprouting up across the GTA - ranging from a handful of tractor-trailers tucked next to a farmhouse to huge, gated operations with 24-7 security and constant traffic lumbering in and out - all fuelled by the burgeoning e-commerce that has helped fill Ontario highways with a growing number of tractor-trailers that need to be kept somewhere between hauls.
More than just a quality-of-life issue for a growing number of residents like Ahmad, the proliferation of such lots - by some accounts hundreds near Caledon alone - has also increased the risk to drivers on local roads, potentially damaged environmentally sensitive farmland and caused the rapid deterioration of the region's public highways.
They have also become a source of frustration for local leaders, who have pleaded with Premier Doug Ford's government to give them more tools to close the illicit lots, rather than relying on municipal charges that trigger years-long, often fruitless, court battles.
"I'm at my wit's end," said Caledon Mayor Annette Groves, "because we just aren't getting anywhere."
Her town, with rolling green hills and about 80,000 people, has an estimated 300 sites being used illegally, mostly for truck parking, the regional epicentre of a crisis that she says is nearing a "tipping point."
"We have illegal yards popping up in our community and the legal system doesn't permit us to do certain things," Groves said. "If I am put in a position that I have to start barricading entrances to the lots myself, that's what I am going to do."
Elected officials in municipalities across the 905 region with large rural areas share Groves's frustration with the seemingly unstoppable rise of huge truck lots established on agricultural and environmentally sensitive land not zoned or serviced for commercial activities.
Politicians from places such as Halton Hills, Milton and Whitchurch-Stouffville say it's become difficult to track new lots, much less initiate long, expensive legal efforts to try to shut them down.
Ahmad noted the 12-acre lot that opened in 2017 near her home is still operating after a court ordered it shut down last year, following a years-long legal battle launched by the town and in which she testified. After a temporary drop in activity, the lot has only grown busier, withup to 35 trucks a day rolling in and out.
"We thought it would get better and yet we are suffering every day," she told the Star.
Peel is a trucking hub
Peel Region's central location near the 400-series highways has made it a warehouse hub for Ontario as online, quick-delivery shopping has flourished. Pressure for space to leave rigs and trailers between hauls has newly formed companies buying up agricultural and rural land to host trucks, many with more than 100 spots, each renting for between $300 and $600 per month.
Many of the illegal enterprises appear legitimate, with signage and security apparatuses, but they are operating without adequate approvals - causing serious environmental, health, safety and quality-of-life issues for residents living next door.
Milton Coun. Colin Best took the Star on a quick tour of five allegedly illegal lots, all near Trafalgar Road and Highway 401. In some cases, a cluster of trucks could be seen in the distance tucked beside a farmhouse. Others are not hiding at all.
On agriculture-zoned land on Auburn Road, two lots side by side have tall fences, speed limit signs, security gates, signs advertising rental spaces and a constant parade of trucks in and out, on a rural road visibly crumbling at the edges.
"They are snubbing their noses at the municipalities," said Best, adding that realtors from Brampton and Mississauga are finding agricultural landowners willing to sell between five and 20 acres and advertising the lots as business sites.
Next to one allegedly illegal lot, a Mississauga realtors' sign touts "future development employment zoning land for sale."
A 25-acre lot at 7499 Auburn Rd. is owned by a numbered company that paid $4.35 million for it in 2019. Corporate records list as directors Paramjit S. Sandhu and Balwinder Thandi, a Burlington realtor.
Thandi told the Star in an email: "We were advised by the seller that the property had been used for these parking purposes since before the bylaw came into effect and that it is therefore a legal nonconforming use. Our solicitor provided the Town of Milton with the requested documents."
Town officials confirmed that the lot, along with the one at 7539 Auburn Rd., owned by a different numbered company, are on land zoned A1 agriculture with no provisions for any commercial use. "A transportation terminal and/or truck parking lot are not permitted uses and no determination has been made with respect to any legal nonconforming use," according to an email from Caledon staff.
Douglas Allen, a paralegal representing Gurjinder Singh Brar of Brampton, a director of the firm that paid $3.51 million for 7539 Auburn Rd. in 2016, declined to comment, saying matters are before the courts: "Your questions involve complex events and circumstances, many of which my clients have no involvement in."
Bill Oughtred, a planning and development consultant engaged by the site's owner, emailed the Star, apparently intending to reply to Allen: "I would not give the reporter the report. Furthermore just give him limited information with respect to it being before the courts," he wrote.
"There are likely around 100 illegal truck parking locations through Caledon, Halton Hills and King. I would not encourage the reporter to single out this particular site by being given too much detail."
Councils have limited resources
In fact, Caledon, which has been at the forefront of tackling the illegal parking issue for several years, has identified about 300 lots being used illegally. Of those, 207 were investigated as illegal truck depots, triggering 61 completed prosecutions and 25 in progress.
"This has become a law-and-order issue, a planning issue, an environmental issue, a public health and safety issue," said Doug Maskell, one of the councillors on Caledon's illegal land use task force.
"We have been in a long-term battle," said Maskell, adding that he's met with provincial government officials several times. "We are doing what we can, with the powers that we have under the legislation passed by the province. And we have run out of ammunition."
Municipalities can lay various types of charges under the Planning Act, the Municipal Act and the Provincial Offences Act. They say, however, that prosecutions can drag on for years and, if successful, generate fines seen by illegal operators as a cost of doing business that won't deter them from continuing to operate.
Under the current rules, municipalities can fine individual landowners up to $25,000, plus $10,000 a day until they conform with municipal law. If the property is owned by a corporation, those amounts are $50,000 and $25,000 a day.
Although provincial offences legislation allows for fines as high as $100,000, "fines never go that high," community services director Catherine McLean told Caledon council this month, adding that even repeated convictions may only result in levies as high as $70,000. "The fines are relatively low."
Ford government responds
In August, Caledon and other municipalities asked the Ford government for help at an all-party provincial standing committee, which held a one-day hearing on ways the province can support municipalities trying to crack down on the illegal sites.
An aide for Municipal Affairs Minister Paul Calandra said there is no deadline for a report from the committee. If it isn't issued before the next provincial election, there may not ever be one.
This month, Caledon mayor Groves sent Deputy Premier Sylvia Jones - who is MPP for Dufferin-Caledon - a letter demanding urgent action to "strengthen municipal enforcement powers."
The demands include doubling the maximum fines, letting municipalities physically bar property entrances, and giving them the ability to put fines and orders on property titles, potentially impacting financing.
"For over a decade we've been exercising every tool we have," Groves wrote. "Without provincial intervention, there is the risk that this issue will grow to an unmanageable state and continue to spread throughout the province."
The Star's request for an interview with Calandra about the truck lot issue was met with an email response: "Municipalities have the authority to pass and amend zoning bylaws that regulate the use of land and contain specific requirements that are legally enforceable, including land use permissions and performance standards." Calandra's response pointed to the current fines.
For Caledon's Maskell, that simply isn't enough to halt the rising tide. "More stringent laws could be passed by the province in an afternoon," he said. "It's a no-brainer."
Meanwhile, court delays are also helping to keep these truck lots open in Halton Hills, according to Mayor Ann Lawlor, noting that a 2023 request by her town for an injunction against one operator wasn't heard until 2024.
Lawlor said her mostly rural community has about 50 properties with illegal land uses, mostly truck lots.
"Some of our prime agricultural land is being polluted," she said. "People are buying up agricultural properties, which are somewhat cheaper because of their zoning than industrial and commercial properties for the same use."
The "cowboys," as she calls them, build secondary entrances, erect buildings without permits or inspection, truck in dirty fill for the tractor-trailers to drive on and change environmental features on the land.
"The cowboys who flout the bylaws simply don't care," she said.
Even after charging the companies under municipal bylaws, cities say they have no way to enforce the orders.
In January 2023, Caledon won a court order against two trucking companies operating illegally, ordering them to "cease operations," according to a town press release. More than a year later, Falcon Xpress - the operator of the truck lot next to Ahmad's home - continues to operate in plain sight.
In an email, Falcon said its company formed to operate the lot "has been complying with the order that was issued on consent after it co-operated with the Town of Caledon with respect to zoning issues that were raised.
Falcon said: "We will continue to work with all stakeholders, including the Town of Caledon and its residents, to ensure harmony in the community in which we all work and live."
A Caledon spokesperson, in a statement to the Star on Wednesday, said: "The town considers that 6086 Mayfield remains in violation of the court order from 2023 and staff are working towards a court date as soon as possible to have the matter return to court."
Multiple tractor-trailers and buildings were visible on the Falcon site as recently as Tuesday morning, including regular truck traffic in and out of the yard after stops at a building near the entrance.
"If this fails," said Ahmad, "they can't remove a single truck yard from Caledon."
Groves shares Ahmad's frustration, including her fears about road safety with the increase in large trucks, noting several fatalities that involved large trucks in the region in recent weeks.
"It makes me furious that these bad actors are here making millions at our residents' expense," said Groves.
The Ontario Trucking Association has urged all levels of government to work together to address the lack of available truck terminals, but agrees that "illegal truck parking should not be tolerated and all levels of government need to enforce the law on these practices," according to association spokesperson Marcho Beghetto.
Mark Sraga, who retired last year as Caledon's head of municipal law enforcement, said demand for inexpensive spots to park rigs near Mississauga and Brampton is only going to increase.
Municipalities along the 401 need to rezone more land for industrial use, including legal truck lots, he said, so operators and drivers can be forced to follow the normal rules, including having owners pay to service the land and pay commercial property taxes rather than the much lower agricultural rate.
At the same time, the Ontario government needs to quickly give local officials stronger measures, such as the power to block entrances to illegal sites - just as they were allowed to do with illegal cannabis shops, he said.
"There is growing demand and trying to fight it is a losing battle," Sraga said. "It's like whack-a-mole - you shut one down and it pops up somewhere else because these trucks have to go somewhere."
CREDIT: David Rider Senior Politics Reporter Noor Javed Toronto Star

Doug Ford signals Ontario will take action on homeless tent encampments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-signals-ontario-will-take-action-on/docview/3112599881/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+signals+Ontario+will+take+action+on+homeless+tent+encampments&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is signalling the province will take action to deal with homeless tent encampments in Toronto parks and other communities across Ontario.
"It's a big problem," said Ford, whose office at the Ontario legislature is about 400 metres south of an encampment at city-run Queen's Park.
"We've got to find homes for these folks as well, and that's our goal - all three levels of government. But I'll tell you, communities across the province, they're just beside themselves," the premier said Wednesday at an unrelated announcement in East York.
"I got a call yesterday. There's needles in the park right beside a place (with) encampments all over the place. It's unacceptable, and we have to all work together to find them shelter and get them out of the public spaces. You just can't be doing it."
Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, who was also at the announcement, said the housing crisis is compounded by the city's increasing population, and "it's hard to find affordable housing for everyone, which is why you see all our shelters are full. We now have 12,000 people in our shelters; half of them are refugees claimants. So the situation is really difficult."
She acknowledged concerns raised by area residents about the encampments, saying "in big parks, small parks - especially in small parks ... (the tents) are right beside playgrounds, and it's just really difficult for families and the kids, because then they have no room for the kids to play. So, we're working hard."
She said a Canada-Ontario rent supplement, a program that will resume this fall, helps moves families out of shelters "which means that we can then make room for people that are living in tents, as the winter arrives in a few months - we can now get them from the encampments into shelters so that they're indoors and don't have to endure the outdoor environment."
Chow noted that shelter spots and housing were found for people living in tents in Grange Park, and many at Allan Gardens.
"People don't want to be camping, they don't want to be outside," the mayor also said. " ... And some of the neighbourhoods are being impacted tremendously ... It's in the best interest for us to deal with this crisis."
Last week, Ford said homeless people in encampments need to "get off your a-s-s and start working like everyone else," though was criticized for not considering how the cost of food or housing impacts people, especially those in low-wage jobs or on social assistance.
The premier has said he's heard from a number of Ontarians and municipal officials who are "rightly frustrated" with encampments.
Encampments are an issue jurisdictions across North America are struggling with post-pandemic as homelessness, mental health issues and substance abuse are on the rise.
In 2021, Toronto began enforcing the no-camping rules in some parks, clearing out homeless campers, but has since backed off that approach.
Hamilton is now allowing encampments with small cabins, making social supports available, until housing is found for them.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford.

Document 147 of 500

Doug Ford pledges 'complete audit' of Ontario children's aid 'nightmare stories' in wake of Star investigation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-pledges-complete-audit-ontario/docview/3112398038/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+pledges+%27complete+audit%27+of+Ontario+children%27s+aid+%27nightmare+stories%27+in+wake+of+Star+investigation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford has pledged his government will do a "complete audit" of Ontario children's aid societies, alleging that he's heard "nightmare stories" about the misuse of taxpayer dollars by the agencies.
His comments, made Wednesday, came in response to Star reporting that child welfare leaders are sounding the alarm about kids and teens with complex health needs being housed in hotels, Airbnbs and office buildings because they are unable to get the medical help and specialized treatments they need.
Ford said his government has increased funding to children's aid societies, but alleged some are abusing taxpayer money rather than properly spending those funds on kids.
"We're hearing nightmare stories about the abuse of taxpayers' money - I've heard stories of some of these agencies working in Taj Mahals. They're paying rent - $100,000 for rent," Ford said when asked about the issue at an unrelated announcement Wednesday morning in East York.
And any managers "giving yourselves a bonus - not worrying about the kids - I'm coming for you," the premier said.
The Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services confirmed in a written statement to the Star that it is "continuing with a review and audit of the sector to better understand how children and youth are being served by the child welfare system," with more details to come.
The organization that represents most of the province's children's aid societies said in a statement to the Star that Ford's comments do not address the lack of supports, services and "appropriate placements" for children and youth with complex needs, calling his response "disappointing."
"Addressing the inadequate access to community-based and out-of-home care is a responsibility that cuts across all levels of government and provincial partners, community-based organizations, and service providers," said Solomon Owoo, chief executive of the Ontario Association of Children's Aid Societies (OACAS).
Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child and youth advocate from 2008 to 2019, said he was angered by the Premier's comments Wednesday.
"To think that this crisis across the sectors is in any way about the mismanagement of money - and will be solved by addressing the mismanagement, if it exists - is dangerous and puts children at risk. Pure and simple," Elman said.
A Star investigation has revealed that hundreds of Ontario children with complex health needs have been living in hotels, Airbnbs, rental apartments, offices and even camping trailers because the province has no live-in treatment options for them. New figures from OACAS show the number of kids and teens in the care of child welfare agencies and living in unlicensed settings has tripled since 2021.
Children with complex needs often have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression, or substance abuse and self-harm, and their families typically struggle to get them timely and adequate medical care and supports.
Child welfare leaders across the province are reporting an increasing number of desperate families relinquishing care of their kids because they've been unable to get help in the community. But because CAS does not have faster access to scarce treatment beds or intensive services, the agencies are forced to house young people with complex mental health and developmental needs in unlicensed settings, which in turn leads to high costs for agencies.
It's unclear how much is being spent across the province on housing kids in makeshift settings. In the year ending in March 2024, there were 354 children whose care had each cost child welfare agencies more than $200,000 per year; the Star has learned of at least four children and youth for whom the annual cost of housing and supervision is roughly $1 million each.
In the wake of Star reporting, child advocates and opposition MPPs are calling on the Ford government to launch an emergency response and say the province can no longer "look away" from the crisis.
On Wednesday, Ford said the province has boosted funding over the last decade and will do "a complete audit" on children's aid societies, adding: "If we see funds not being spent properly on the kids, guess what? You're looking for another job. Simple as that."
The total number of children in the care of child welfare agencies has gone down by about a third over the last decade. Yet the agencies are still "running non-stop deficits," he added. "Not all, but of a lot of them, and we've increased (funding) ... It's just unacceptable."
Child welfare leaders previously told the Star that deficits stem from creating emergency placements for children with complex needs. The number of children and youth needing high-cost care has doubled in the past three years, according to OACAS data.
This week, OACAS sent an open letter to Ford calling on the province to take "immediate action" to ensure community-based organizations and service providers have enough funding and staff to "truly respond to the needs of children, youth and families."
The organization pointed to a June report from the Financial Accountability Office of Ontario that found the government had underfunded the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services by an estimated $3.7 billion less than required to fund existing programs and announced commitments.
Close to 30 agencies and associations, including municipalities, non-profits, and organizations in mental health care, immigration and refugee services and intellectual and developmental disabilities, among others, signed the OACAS letter pushing for greater investments in community-based organizations that serve children and youth.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford, seen here in a July 10, 2024, file photo, has pledged a "complete audit" of Ontario children's aid societies.

Document 148 of 500

Justin Trudeau dishes with Liberal MP on where he went wrong, what he did right and why he's the one to beat Pierre Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-dishes-with-liberal-mp-on-where-he/docview/3112397669/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+dishes+with+Liberal+MP+on+where+he+went+wrong%2C+what+he+did+right+and+why+he%27s+the+one+to+beat+Pierre+Poilievre&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Under siege in the polls but fighting for a shot at a fourth term, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau took to a backbench MP's podcast to riff on why he thinks he deserves it.
In a nearly hour-long interview recorded Friday and published Tuesday, Trudeau gave more revealing answers to fellow political traveller Liberal MP Nate Erskine-Smith than he has to any journalist in months.
The prime minister conceded a few mistakes. But only a few. And he expressed little regret.
Trudeau conceded: the Liberal party has given Pierre Poilievre two years to build his political brand instead of immediately running ads against the Conservative leader; that as prime minister he failed to deliver on his promise of electoral reform; that the Liberal party bears some responsibility for two bad byelection losses in Toronto and Montreal strongholds; and that the federal government should have acted sooner on housing.
Yet his abstract responses to Erskine-Smith's challenge on other questions - on what policies he wants to see through in a fourth term, on why he's running despite anonymous backbench naysayers and bad polls, or on how he thinks Liberals can win - underscored a bigger issue.
Can the Liberal leader, who has been on the Canadian and American podcast circuit in an attempt to broaden the reach of his message, get through to those he needs to persuade - not least, other Liberal MPs, party volunteers and those voters he's lost since 2015 that he hopes to bring back onside.
First, the mistakes:
 Trudeau defended the decision not to run political ads against Poilievre as a tactical consideration, that years before an election was too soon and that he'd rebuffed a similar attempt by Conservatives to frame him too early, and more importantly, that he felt it would look petty to voters if the Liberals took their eye off the bigger economic and financial woes then facing Canadians. When Erskine-Smith begged to differ, Trudeau insisted there is still time to paint Poilievre as the risk to the environment, equality rights and social programs that the Liberals believe he is.
 On the broken promise of electoral reform, Trudeau wished he'd had a do-over. He said he should have immediately shut down talk about proportional representation as a viable option. He repeated his oft-expressed view it would have been too divisive and spliced the Canadian electorate into ever smaller pies, but confessed Liberals were deliberately vague in order to appeal to Fair Vote Canada advocates. Trudeau said he wished he'd used his majority to bring in the ranked ballot model he preferred, saying it would not have changed ridings or even the ballot that much - suggesting instead of a check, voters would rank their preferences in order - and would force parties to appeal beyond their bases. But Trudeau said he did not feel there was a broad level of support in the House for what he called "an irreversible change," so he pulled the plug. Erskine-Smith said the broken promise was his most difficult day in nine years in Trudeau's caucus. Trudeau said it was "a gut-wrenching day for me."
 On the byelection losses in Toronto-St. Paul's and LaSalle-Emard-Verdun, Trudeau said there were "a lot of factors" in each but the Liberal candidates did not have enough time on the ground to campaign ahead of the byelection dates, noting he'd had a year to campaign before winning Papineau. However Trudeau's answer did not reflect the fact that Leslie Church had been campaigning for nearly a year in the riding before she was appointed, and it did not address complaints that the appointments of her and Laura Palestini as candidate in the Montreal-area riding prevented competitive nomination races and angered some in those ridings. Nor did Trudeau directly address Erskine-Smith's question about what those losses told him about the bigger changes that are needed.
 On housing and immigration, Trudeau acknowledged that the massive post-pandemic influx of temporary residents - foreign workers and international students - had an impact on housing shortages. But he said his government should have "leaned harder" on provinces and territories to address "NIMBYism" (not-in-my-backyard pushback) in order to build more housing.
But it was the prime minister's responses to Erskine-Smith's probing about why he is still running and how he thinks he can reverse the Liberals' fortunes that underscored the big challenge.
"Obviously your brain didn't melt on national television the way that Biden's did," Erskine-Smith said, but Kamala Harris has given that party a better shot at winning. He asked Trudeau to explain why he thinks he's the best person to lead the Liberals' next fight.
Trudeau's answer was, basically, that he is the one who knows better than anyone "exactly how hard this fight is going to be," that it is "fundamental to how Canadians come out of or come through" the past few difficult years, and it is "exactly why I got into politics."
He touted progressive policies that delivered for people and will last over the long term: the Canada Child Benefit, standing up a national child care program, fighting climate change including with carbon pricing, and efforts to tackle housing shortages he said will pay off years from now.
Trudeau pivoted to note, "Nobody is asking Poilievre what ... he is fighting for ... he hasn't even begun to articulate what he's fighting for ... other than himself and his desire to be in power."
However, Trudeau effectively said that there is no new vision for the coming campaign, rather that Liberals are fighting for the same vision he fought for in 2015, to protect the environment and the economy, to lift up ordinary Canadians and improve people's resilience for the future.
"Yeah, it's going to be a change election," he said. "Everything has changed, not just climate change, but the way we work, the way AI works, the way geopolitics happens, the pressures on everything. The world is in a massive pivot moment right now, and we don't know what the biggest issue is going to be."
To confront that kind of change, Trudeau insisted that Canadians understand what his "values" are, and what he brings to a crisis, pointing out that the crises he confronted, from the COVID-19 pandemic to Russia's invasion of Ukraine, were not foreseen in any campaign.
Values, he said, are "ultimately the only thing."
Except in Poilievre, the Liberals are facing a political rival who is promising practical benefits for individual households, with slogan-based promises to "axe the tax, build the homes," and "stop the crime" and is laying claim to represent "common sense" Canadian values.
Erskine-Smith rightly called Trudeau out on all the high-level talk, and said Poilievre is a better communicator than any Conservative leader in the past nine years. He urged Trudeau to be better at telling the Liberals' story.
On Wednesday, after it aired, Erskine-Smith said his personal decision not to run in the next election is because he needs to spend more time with his young children, but he still supports Trudeau, at least for now.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau rises during question period in the House of Commons on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, onOct. 2, 2024.

Subject: Electoral reform; Canadians; Voters; Climate change; Prime ministers; Political advertising; Pandemics; Elections

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Title: Justin Trudeau dishes with Liberal MP on where he went wrong, what he did right and why he's the one to beat Pierre Poilievre
