Document 149 of 500

Eglinton Crosstown LRT may not open this year: Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/eglinton-crosstown-lrt-may-not-open-this-year/docview/3112112850/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Eglinton+Crosstown+LRT+may+not+open+this+year%3A+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The long-delayed Eglinton Crosstown LRT may not open until the new year, the premier of Ontario said on Wednesday.
"Are we going to hit the next few months? Hopefully. If not, it will be in the new year," Doug Ford told reporters at a press conference about the Ontario Line.
Provincial agency Metrolinx's CEO Phil Verster has previously said the public will be given three months' advance notice when the transit agency is ready to open the Eglinton LRT. With less than three months left in 2024, the troubled light rail line does not appear to be on schedule to open this year.
Andrea Ernesaks, a Metrolinx spokesperson, confirmed with the Star on Wednesday that three-month promise is still applicable but would not give an opening date.
"Other than sighing, I don't know how to respond to that," said Mayor Olivia Chow, following a sigh and long pause, at an unrelated press conference later on Wednesday. "Please just get it done."
The 19-km Eglinton LRT was originally supposed to open in 2020, with the Finch West LRT slated to be running in 2023. The Finch light rail line has also been delayed.
Chow went on to describe conversations she's had with residents living near the Eglinton LRT who "are ready," but can't use the transit line in a city that made it clear during budget consultations that mobility is among the top priorities.
"It's disappointing. It's frustrating," she said, adding the city's put aside money so that it can operate "the minute" those rail lines are ready.
At his press conference Wednesday, Ford said that all the construction is done, but operators still need to be trained and trains need to be tested. "My number one concern is ... making sure it's safe," he said.
When asked what indicators the premier uses to measure Verster's success, Ford said, "We hold him and Metrolinx accountable all the time. But I'm a fan of Phil."
Verster, who made $838,097 last year according to Ontario's Sunshine List, became CEO of Metrolinx in 2017 and pledged transparency around transit decisions while at the helm.
The Star revealed this year that Metrolinx was on the verge of revealing the expected opening date of the Eglinton Crosstown in 2023, but Ford's office ordered it be kept secret. Verster held apress conference in September last year at which he declared he had a good idea of when the line would be done, but had decided not to announce it.
The agency has yet to publish a timeline for when either LRT will take its first passengers.
"We need to have confidence that key milestones are being met," Ernesaks said.
The consortium contracted to design, build and maintain the Finch Avenue light rail, Mosaic Transit Partners, launched a court battle this week, as first reported by Global News, claiming Metrolinx had entered into a deal with the TTC that is interfering with the completion of the Finch West LRT. Last year, the group building the Eglinton Crosstown LRT filed a similar complaint about that project.
"Eglinton compared to Finch is like night and day ... The two sides need to sort out their differences," Ford said, noting that as "a last resort" his government will get involved but stopped short of detailing what that means.
CAPTION: The 19-km Eglinton Crosstown LRT was originally supposed to open in 2020.

Document 150 of 500

Public education in Ontario has always been political

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/public-education-ontario-has-always-been/docview/3112112787/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+education+in+Ontario+has+always+been+political&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In response to Toronto District School Board (TDSB) students attending a rally in support of the Grassy Narrows First Nation, where they also encountered pro-Palestinian protesters, Premier Doug Ford has once again accused teachers of trying to "indoctrinate our kids."
Ford admonished teachers to "stick with your knitting, stick with teaching the kids the geography, history, math, spelling."
Knitting aside, Ford's comments reflect a growing sentiment in that some educators have unduly politicized schools to promote a progressive social agenda. What is needed, according to these critics, is a return to "the basics" and a time when schools did not try to influence what kids think about social issues. The problem with this vision of value-neutral schooling is that it has never existed and never will.
Since its inception, one of the core functions of mass public schooling has been to impart certain values onto children. Since 1897, Ontario legislation has commanded schools to "inculcate" students with certain Christian values.
In its current form, the Ontario Education Act states that it is the duty of every teacher to "inculcate by precept and example respect for religion and the principles of Judeo-Christian morality and the highest regard for truth, justice, loyalty, love of country, humanity, benevolence, sobriety, industry, frugality, purity, temperance and all other virtues."
In 1980, Toronto Board of Education trustee Michael Walker cited this section of the legislation in explaining his vote against the board's decision to establish partnership with gay rights groups. Walker went on to say, "I most definitely think homosexual views and values should not be promoted as an acceptable alternative lifestyle in any school system.
By acceding to the demands of homosexuals, the Toronto Board of Education has for all practical purposes given their cause a legitimacy in the schools which it does not deserve." Was Walker right? Was the Toronto board inappropriately indoctrinating students?
Fast forward a few decades to 2023 and we have Premier Ford's former Minister of Education Stephen Lecce explicitly supporting the raising of Pride flags in all Ontario schools, saying that it is "a signal of respect and inclusion."
Most people would view going from Walker to Lecce as a sign of social progress. But how did this happen? Magic? Knitting? No, it is because institutions like the Toronto Board took a political stance in support of progressive causes like gay rights and in doing so helped to change mainstream views.
The same thing applies to truth and reconciliation. Amidst the current controversy, most people agree that what has happened to the Grassy Narrows First Nation is a grave injustice that students should learn about, especially since schools now regularly teach about Indigenous issues.
But it was not always this way. In 2007, right before I became a high school teacher, I spent a month completing an internship with TDSB central staff. During this time, the board organized a fair where students from across the city presented posters on different social problems.
Several judges wanted to give one of the top awards to a student who had presented on the myriad issues faced by Canada's Indigenous Peoples. But instead, the head of the board department overseeing the competition rejected it and criticized the student for injecting "the politics of the Assembly of First Nations" into our schools.
Is this what we want to go back to?
It is important to keep in mind that we operate in a context of compulsory education, where we force kids to go to school. Given that context, special attention is always warranted to what values are being imparted to our children. And it is perfectly legitimate for people to try to influence what is being taught in schools through our democratically elected school boards and provincial legislature.
But beware when someone tells you they are going to depoliticize schools. There is no such thing as value-neutral schooling. It is just a matter of what and whose values will be taught.
CAPTION: Thousands marched through the streets of Toronto on Sept. 18 to protest the situation in the Grassy Narrows First Nations and arrived at Queen's Park where they unfurled a 1,600 square foot banner calling for justice. TDSB students attended the protest on a field trip, which became controversial when Palestinian protestors also took part in the event.
CREDIT: Sachin Maharaj Contributor

Subject: Students; Needlework; LGBTQ rights; Education; Teachers; Native North Americans; School boards

Calls grow for Ford government to take emergency action to help kids with complex needs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/calls-grow-ford-government-take-emergency-action/docview/3112112714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Calls+grow+for+Ford+government+to+take+emergency+action+to+help+kids+with+complex+needs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Child advocates and opposition MPPs are calling on the province to launch an emergency response to address the high numbers of children with complex needs ending up in the care of child welfare agencies, calling the government's failure to act a "tragedy."
They point to a report commissioned by the Ford government on the crisis three years ago - and first made public by the Star on Monday - and say its recommendations must be acted upon to ensure vulnerable children get the help they need.
"To have a report with recommendations from experts sitting on the shelf for two years collecting dust while our systems are failing and crumbling around families is absolutely shameful and heartbreaking," said New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor (Hamilton Mountain), the critic for children, community and social services since 2011.
She said the government can no longer "look away" from the crisis and must act on its recommendations now that the report has been made public.
"They have to take them seriously - they're from the experts within the system - and get moving on them as quickly as possible," Taylor said.
A Toronto Star investigation revealed that hundreds of Ontario children with complex special needs have been living in hotels, Airbnbs, rental apartments, offices and even camping trailers because the province has no live-in treatment options for them. The number of kids and teens in the care of child welfare agencies and living in these unlicensed settings has tripled since 2021, according to figures the Star obtained from the organization that represents Ontario children's aid societies. The Ombudsman's office is investigating this practice.
Children with complex needs often have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, and their families typically struggle to get them timely and adequate medical care and supports. Families and experts who spoke to the Star said children face years-long wait lists and an acute lack of residential treatment options. In many cases, they said exhausted parents cannot access specialized care because their child is considered "too high-needs." When families break down, they are forced to relinquish care of their kids to children's aid societies because they've been unable to get help.
Sara Austin, founder and chief executive officer of Children First Canada, said there has been recognition for years - by child welfare leaders, advocates, families and even the government - that the systems of care meant to serve children and youth are broken, but now kids are being put in such "gravely concerning circumstances" that "we're past the tipping point."
Austin said it concerns her that the government's only response so far has been calling for an audit of child welfare agencies. While an audit may be warranted, she said, it's action that's needed.
"There have been roundtables. There have been expert opinions on this. A lot of people have weighed in on what needs to change. And what we're seeing is that this change isn't happening," Austin said. "It's certainly not happening at the rate that is truly warranted for the crisis that exists."
Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child and youth advocate from 2008 to 2019, said the province is in a "dangerous" crisis and the government "must act to quell the emergency it has created."
"When this government talks about audits and special reports and pilot projects, they are talking about where change goes to die," Elman said. "It is particularly unconscionable when parents and children are struggling so hard, bruised and battered in the face of government's inaction."
Elman called on the government to do whatever is necessary to get these families urgent help.
In response to questions, the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services sent a statement that said the government "has been working over several years to refocus the child welfare system on high-quality and culturally appropriate services that prioritize safety, protection and the needs and circumstances of children, youth and families."
The ministry responsible for children and youth and the Ministry of Health "continue to increase funding to support children with special needs and are working together to support easier, more co-ordinated access to services, and more seamless transitions within and across child-serving sectors," the statement said.
Children's aid societies "must follow the law and ensure that placements for children are safe, appropriate and meet the child's needs," the ministry said.
The two ministries say the 2022 report provided them "with insight and recommendations to improve the lives of children and youth."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles called the situation "concerning."
"Families and kids with complex needs deserve all our support, and if the government isn't providing that, then shame on us," Stiles said, while speaking to reporters Tuesday in Lindsay, Ont.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner also called on the government to bolster support for families with children with complex needs.
"To know the government has an advisory committee that's put forward recommendations and the government is not moving on those recommendations is just a tragedy," he said.
Ottawa South Liberal MPP John Fraser, critic for children, community and social services, said the government must take immediate action and "find more appropriate spaces" for these vulnerable children, suggesting the issue "isn't a priority" for the Ford government.
"How are we housing kids with complex needs in places where none of us would expect to live in or sleep in?"
With files from Kristin Rushowy
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca and mogilvie@thestar.ca
CAPTION: Irwin Elman, Ontario's former child and youth advocate, says that "when this government talks about audits and special reports and pilot projects, they are talking about where change goes to die."Sara Austin, founder and CEO of Children First Canada, said kids are being put in such "gravely concerning circumstances" that "we're past the tipping point."

Document 152 of 500

We should be taking a scalpel, not a machete, to the international student program

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-should-be-taking-scalpel-not-machete/docview/3112112699/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+should+be+taking+a+scalpel%2C+not+a+machete%2C+to+the+international+student+program&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians love a good pasta restaurant, but of all the workforce challenges we face as a nation, Kitchener's Conestoga College should not be wasting limited international student visas on an "Italian Culinary Arts" course in September 2025.
Canada desperately needs more post-secondary graduates in applied biotechnology, computer science, construction engineering, early childhood education, machining and practical nursing, among other key skills.
Given the lack of affordable housing, and a local unemployment rate of more than seven per cent, why ever would Conestoga recruit students - primarily from India and China - for a one or two-year course in Charcuterie and Artisanal Foods, Interior Design, Public Relations or Social Media Marketing?
I'll risk a shortage of future osso bucco sous-chefs if that means more nurses and daycare instructors.
On the heels of broad-based pressure, effectively encapsulated by Ontario Conservative MP Arpan Khanna, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government is now rushing to reduce the number of student visas being granted. But broad-based cuts don't take into account Canada's needs - that requires a scalpel, not a machete.
Given their focus on messaging, I'm not surprised that the Liberals put social media courses on the same pedestal as construction engineering, but the downside is obvious. Ontario's Metrolinx, for example, requires a future pool of trained young professionals to deliver a 20-year pipeline of multi-faceted construction projects on behalf of Premier Doug Ford's government.
Ottawa didn't prioritize the skills that will help bring these generational infrastructure projects to fruition.
Western University President Alan Shepard put it succinctly: "The post-secondary system is bulging with offerings to entice students from around the world. But not all offerings are equal, and the students they attract are not supported equally when they arrive."
That's my alma mater boss politely reminding politicians that when Western or the University of Toronto offer a first-year student a spot, they're guaranteed a residence room. Other institutions have no such concern, as Brampton's bylaw department discovered when it found 25 students living in an illegal basement apartment.
I'd have thought that Ontario's newly-appointed Minister of Colleges and Universities, Nolan Quinn, could bring order to this chaos. That the minister might roll back the excesses, including the new $495,000 salary that Conestoga's Board of Governors recently awarded President John Tibbits (a figure that's higher than the presidential salaries at McMaster or Queen's, for example).
Not likely, according to one of Quinn's predecessors.
"(Post secondary institutions) are ungovernable," according to Glen Murray, who held the Training, Colleges and Universities cabinet portfolio under former Liberal Premier Dalton McGuinty. They may be our tax dollars, but Ontario's funding formula gives each institution wide latitude to run their own show.
Colleges and Institutes Canada president Pari Johnston claims that Trudeau's visa cuts will cause "significant harm ... to local businesses that rely on the schools to supply graduates to the labour force."
I asked her team for the underlying analysis, but was told "no one data set" supported their assertion.
Johnston's spokesperson says the "core mission" of community colleges "is to serve their community needs." That includes Charcuterie and Artisanal Foods, apparently. Exemplifying why Murray finds our higher-education system "aimless, missionless, and wandering."
The last time Gallup checked, 74 million people wanted to immigrate to Canada. That luxury means we can "craft a careful and co-ordinated strategy that makes the best use of top global talent," as Shepard argues.
One former Ontario community college instructor told me she quit in disgust when she realized that most of her international students "had zero interest in the class. It was all about getting permanent residency."
Murray and I are in violent agreement when he says that "our national interest is to have the most highly educated, skilled and civil society in the world. To reinforce our democracy and strengthen our economy and productivity."
That requires choices: "Who do we want to come here to be educated? Who do we want to come and be educated and go back to their country? Who do we want to retain here?"
These essential questions are neither partisan nor anti-immigrant and should guide an international student strategy that reflects our national interest.
CAPTION: Canada desperately needs more post-secondary graduates in applied biotechnology, computer science, construction engineering, early childhood education, machining and practical nursing, sowhy are colleges such as Kitchener's Conestoga College allowing limited international students visas to be used in other programs, asks Mark McQueen.

A mixed view on field trips

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/mixed-view-on-field-trips/docview/3112112691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+mixed+view+on+field+trips&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mixed view of student field trips
TDSB cancels Truth and Reconciliation Day field trips to Nathan Phillips Square citing 'abundance of caution,' Sept. 30
I am in agreement that visits by school-aged children to the Truth and Reconciliation Day ceremony would have been beneficial. The students would learn about tolerance, inclusivity and acceptance of all people, as worthy Canadians, regardless of race and religion. The ceremony was extremely important for everyone to understand the crimes committed via residential schools.
Eric Bornstein, Toronto
It is great that TDSB is putting a hold on field trips but did they not communicate this to their system? Allegedly two schools still attended the event that could have had political protests, but fortunately did not. This was the same type of event that attracted protests last time. Did it not reach the teachers from the two schools that field trips were on pause? Why did the superintendents not step in or know about the pause? TDSB is a huge board, but surely their internal communications must improve.
Joel Hertz, former YRDSB vice-chair, Toronto
As one who has taught for 35 years, I was disgusted to hear about the Toronto District School Board school protest trip. What were those teachers thinking? The answer, of course, is they were not thinking. If 15 schools were involved, this must have been the idea of one or more consultants from the board office. Most teachers know that a lot of these people are really not grounded in reality. The lesson here for teachers is simple. Don't be afraid to stand up to board office people and their ideas. I did it in my day! Be the leaders you are supposed to be. Put the well-being of your students first! Finally, there must be consequences for those who suggested and planned this trip. Discipline or dismissal from the Ontario College of Teachers is essential. As for the teachers' federations, stay out of politics. You are being paid to negotiate good contracts, salaries and working conditions and to protect members. That's it.
John Sudak, Hamilton, Ont.
Nonsense in Parliament
Poilievre acts as if there's power in being unlikeable. It's not a good look: Delacourt, Sept. 26
Why would anyone who cares about Canada want these adolescent men representing us, not just in this country but on the world stage? If a person doesn't know the basics of good manners and how to conduct themselves with some form of decorum, they should not be allowed to represent our country either at home or abroad. I would hate to think they allowed their children to carry on in such a way in their classrooms or anywhere else.
Angela Ingram, Tiny, Ont.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's recent behaviour in the House of Commons was immature and unbecoming someone who aspires to be prime minister. His childish recital of meaningless slogans followed by a silly grin was inappropriate and contributes to the low level of discourse in the House. He has demonstrated that he is not ready to be the leader of the country and I hope the voters he has attracted with his slogans will wake up to the fact that there is no substance to his utterings.
Gordon Simpson, Toronto
Beware political promises
If the Trudeau Liberals have fulfilled 90 per cent of their political promises, yet owning or renting housing are unaffordable, food banks are overwhelmed, and finding a family doctor is almost impossible, they were clearly making the wrong promises. Premier Doug Ford wasted millions fulfilling promises to cancel wind turbine contracts and get beer in corner stores, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will cripple our economy's transition to a low carbon future if he fulfils his promise to "axe the tax."
Rob Strang, Orangeville, Ont.
As I contemplate the next federal election, I find I have no choice but to vote for Justin Trudeau. Better the devil you know than the one you don't. I don't like Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre with his nasty, aggressive manner, and while he brags that he will axe the tax, fix the budget, build the homes and fight crime, I've seen no details. How would he propose to deal with climate change once the carbon "tax" is gone? What unique measures would he take to ensure millions of affordable homes are built nationwide? What services and programs would be cut to fix the budget? Is crime so high that it requires electing him to fight it? While I like NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, I don't see him and his party as strong enough to lead the country. Poilievre does not have the personality to represent me in the nation's highest office.
Bob Coupland, Oakville
A smart business person would do test run Why doesn't the Ford government pay for Highway 407 tolls for a week or month and see how that alleviates traffic on Highway 401 instead of spending billions on a new highway or tunnel? See what the impact of another highway would do to daily traffic jams. A smart business person would test first before you spend unnecessary money on something that might not be needed.
Patti Pokorchak, Toronto
Premier Doug Ford has proposed a tunnel underneath Highway 401. This has got to be one of the worst schemes I have ever heard. To start with, there would have to be a feasibility study, which would cost mega bucks. I could do it for a few dollars on one page and it would read "forget it," thereby saving the province billions of dollars.
Carl Irwin, Flesherton, Ont.
Thank you, Ellie
After 40 years at the Star, Ellie Tesher bids her readers a very fond farewell, Sept. 28
I have read the Star almost every day since I moved here from Montreal in the late 1980s and luckily discovered Eliie Tesher's advice column some 23 years ago. As a teacher in different capacities for more than 45 years, Ellie's advice helped me to cultivate a better relationship with some of my students who struggled to find a path in life. On a personal level, Ellie points out that travelling is one of the most fascinating life lessons, broadening our minds while making us aware of the world around us. My travels to different parts of the world where some of the great monuments were built was an eye opener helping to reinforce my faith in humanity. Congratulations on an amazing professional journey. Happy retirement.
Robert Ariano, Scarborough
CAPTION: The City of Toronto opened the new Spirit Garden at Nathan Phillips Square on Monday, National Day for Truth and Reconciliation.

Document 154 of 500

War breaks out in the Middle East + Trudeau survives another non-confidence vote

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/war-breaks-out-middle-east-trudeau-survives/docview/3112112690/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=War+breaks+out+in+the+Middle+East+%2B+Trudeau+survives+another+non-confidence+vote&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, October 2 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on war in the Middle East, Justin Trudeau surviving a second non-confidence vote and another bloody night in the GTA.
DON'T MISSWar has begun in the Middle East, says Mélanie Joly
Canada's foreign affairs minister said the nation "unequivocally" condemns Iran's missile attacks on Israel, and urged "all parties" to agree to a ceasefire to avoid devastating civilian casualties, Tonda MacCharles reports. "We need to make sure that there is no escalation," Joly said. "Of course, Israel needs to be able to protect itself and that's why we'll continue to support its security." Here's what comes next.
An escalating crisis: Iran launched at least 180 missiles at Israel yesterday, most of which were intercepted, following Israel-led ground incursions into Lebanon. Israel has vowed retaliation against Iran; here's what we know so far.Canadians abroad: Joly urged Canadians to leave Lebanon - a warning some Lebanese-Canadians have ignored as they plan trips into the embattled country. There are an estimated 40,000 to 45,000 Canadians still in Lebanon.Martin Regg Cohn's take: Sheikh Hassan Nasrallah, leader of millitant group Hezbollah, was revered almost as a living god by his followers until he was assassinated by Israel in Lebanon. But when Regg Cohn met the man, he appeared little more than a religious warlord.Justin Trudeau survives a second Conservative non-confidence vote
The Liberals are still kicking after weathering a second attempt by Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives this fall to topple his government, Mark Ramzy reports. The Bloc Québécois and NDP came to the Grits' defence, voting down a Tory non-confidence motion 207 to 121 yesterday. Trudeau's government is expected to face several more confidence tests in the coming months. Here's what they're up against.
Under pressure: The Liberals could soon lose support from the Bloc Québécois should it not pass a $16 billion bill to boost seniors' pension payments. But Trudeau's government gave no indication it's planning to give the Bloc what it wants.Incoming: The Tories have three more opposition days this season, and have already presented one more confidence motion. The Liberals will face another confidence test in the coming weeks over a motion on its capital gains tax increase.Three are dead after yet another violent night in the GTA
Another spate of violence tore across the GTA early Tuesday morning, leaving three men dead and others injured - the latest in an exceptionally bloody year for the city, Abby O'Brien and Calvi Leon report. One advocate said they can hardly kee up. Take a look at what can be done about the surging homicides in the city.
The details: Just after midnight Tuesday, a shooting left a man in his mid-20s dead in a shopping centre parking lot. A stabbing took the life of another soon after. Hours later, an incident just outside the city left another man dead and two injured.A bloody year: Shootings are up nearly 40 per cent this year over the last, including 338 shootings and "firearm related discharges" - the highest since 2020. Thirty-five people have died in shootings so far this year.WHAT ELSE

Doug Ford raising speed limit to 110 km/h on all 400-series highways 'where it is safe to do so'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-raising-speed-limit-110-km-h-on-all-400/docview/3112112687/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+raising+speed+limit+to+110+km%2Fh+on+all+400-series+highways+%27where+it+is+safe+to+do+so%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford feels the need for speed.
Against the backdrop of a potential spring provincial election, Ford on Wednesday said the speed limit on all 400-series highways will be raised from 100 km/h to 110 km/h "where it is safe to do so."
Speaking to reporters at a campaign-style announcement in East York to tout construction on the Ontario Line subway, the premier said he was hitting the gas pedal to keep motorists moving.
"Earlier this year, we raised the speed limit on 10 additional sections of 400-series highways and today I've directed the minister to raise the speed limit to 110 kilometres on all 400 series highways where it is safe to do so," he said.
"I'm going to repeat that: where it is safe to do. So we're leaving no stone unturned to help fight gridlock on our roads, reduce travel times and allow people to spend less time commuting and more time at home with their families, and that includes the Ontario Line, which is going to be a game changer for Toronto and the entire region."
Ford, who last week promised a feasibility study into a potential 60-kilometre tunnel under Highway 401 from Mississauga and Brampton to Scarborough that could cost tens of billions of dollars, stressed "everything's on the table right now" to reduce gridlock.
The Toronto Region Board of Trade has estimated that traffic snarls cost the provincial economy $11 billion annually in lost productivity.
Asked about proposals from Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles to eliminate tolls on the privately owned Highway 407 so more cars and trucks could use it, Ford conceded such a move could be in the works.
"We'll look at the 407 and see exactly which way are we going. We own about 22 kilometres on the far (eastern) end of the 407 and there's other opportunities along the 407," he said, noting motorists pay about $1.4 billion in tolls annually.
"All options are on the table, maybe both options. Let's see. But we know one thing is costing our economy $11 billion a year. This is costing people over 100 hours of sitting in traffic on the 401," he said.
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener-Centre) said the speed limit gambit proves "the Ford government doesn't have a serious plan to make it easier for Ontarians to get around."
"The Eglinton LRT looks like it won't open until at least 2025, the Finch West LRT is now delayed too, and gridlock on the 401 has become so bad that it's almost unbearable," said Clancy.
"If the premier thinks increasing the speed limit on some sections of some highways is going to fix the problem, he just doesn't get it," she said, urging improved all-day GO train and bus service, dedicated transit lanes and "more bike-share programs and e-bike infrastructure."
While NDP premier Bob Rae built the 407, his Progressive Conservative successor Mike Harris sold a 99-year lease to operate it to a private consortium for $3.1 billion.
Ford has repeatedly criticized that sell-off - even though his father, Doug Ford, Sr., was an MPP in that Tory government and Harris's son, Mike Harris, Jr., is a cabinet minister in the premier's government.
"I would have never sold it," he said during his successful 2022 re-election campaign.
Wednesday's changes come as the premier is considering an election next spring, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford takes part in the parade as he attends the International Ploughing Match in Lindsay, Ont., on Oct. 1, 2024.

Document 156 of 500

Farmers protest PCs during plowing event

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/farmers-protest-pcs-during-plowing-event/docview/3111857783/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Farmers+protest+PCs+during+plowing+event&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A group of farmers came to the plowing match here hoping to get Premier Doug Ford to start paying a little less attention to tunnelling under Highway 401 and a little more to what's going on above ground.
With about 770 acres of farmland in the Waterloo area under threat of expropriation, the farmers wanted to gain the support of other farmers as well as politicians on Tuesday at the annual event.
"We've come from Wilmot to put our voices forward, to help people understand what the issues are with the land acquisition that's proposed," said Alfred Lowrick, who lives beside the properties the region wants to obtain, and whose mother-in-law received an expropriation notice.
While the region is the expropriating authority, there's confusion and secrecy around the province's role and what it is funding, he added, "so there's a bit of a quandary of who we should be talking to and what we should be addressing."
The region has acquired one of seven properties identified for future, unspecified industrial use "and the rest of the farmers don't want to sell. They're not interested in doing this, and the community isn't," he added, referring to support for a local lawn-sign campaign.
The Wilmot farmers were handing out stickers in support of their cause to those who attended opening day of the 105th annual International Plowing Match and Rural Expo, held this year at the Lindsay Fairgrounds.
Every fall, it is a major weeklong event for rural Ontario - one that draws cabinet ministers, MPPs and the party leaders, who take part in a plowing contest.
Asked about the Wilmot issue by reporters, Ford said, "I just want the region to treat them fairly."
But Waterloo MPP Catherine Fife, a New Democrat, said "there has not been one public meeting, not one fiscal report to the general public and now there's just a finger-pointing exercise between the regional government and the PC government - they're just blaming each other." Fife charged that the provincial government is "driving the whole process," and said the public deserves answers.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, taking part in her first plowing match as head of the provincial party, said the Wilmot farmers "know that we've been with them."
Crombie said farmers also have the same concerns as the rest of Ontario's residents - such as trying to find a family doctor or keeping the emergency room open at their local hospital.
Ford also told reporters that while there are a lot of opinions about tunnelling under Highway 401, a proposal he announced last week, "we're just doing a feasibility study. Anyone that's a driver on the 401 on a daily basis knows we're just jammed."
The congestion "takes $11 billion a year out of our economy - not to mention all the time you can either spend at home with your family, have some fun in the sun or whatever you want to do."
Lowrick said that while tunnelling would cost billions of dollars for "a nice little hole they want to drill" to address heavy traffic in the Greater Toronto Area, the Ford government is "missing the boat" by not also addressing the concerns of farmers.
CAPTION: A protester opposed to the development of farmland in Wilmot County holds up a sign in front of Premier Doug Ford and members of the PC caucus who were taking part in Monday's International Plowing Match in Lindsay, Ont.Chris Young The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 157 of 500

Calls grow for launch of emergency response: Advocates, MPPs say Ford government's failure to help children with complex needs a 'tragedy'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/calls-grow-launch-emergency-response/docview/3111856658/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Calls+grow+for+launch+of+emergency+response%3A+Advocates%2C+MPPs+say+Ford+government%27s+failure+to+help+children+with+complex+needs+a+%27tragedy%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Child advocates and opposition MPPs are calling on the province to launch an emergency response to address the high numbers of children with complex needs ending up in the care of child welfare agencies, calling the government's failure to act a "tragedy."
They point to a report commissioned by the Ford government on the crisis three years ago - and first made public by the Star on Monday - and say its recommendations must be acted upon to ensure vulnerable children get the help they need.
"To have a report with recommendations from experts sitting on the shelf for two years collecting dust while our systems are failing and crumbling around families is absolutely shameful and heartbreaking," said New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor (Hamilton Mountain), the critic for children, community and social services since 2011.
She said the government can no longer "look away" from the crisis and must act on its recommendations now that the report has been made public.
"They have to take them seriously - they're from the experts within the system - and get moving on them as quickly as possible," Taylor said.
A Toronto Star investigation revealed that hundreds of Ontario children with complex special needs have been living in hotels, Airbnbs, rental apartments, offices and even camping trailers because the province has no live-in treatment options for them. The number of kids and teens in the care of child welfare agencies and living in these unlicensed settings has tripled since 2021, according to figures the Star obtained from the organization that represents Ontario children's aid societies. The Ombudsman's office is investigating this practice.
Children with complex needs often have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, and their families typically struggle to get them timely and adequate medical care and supports. Families and experts who spoke to the Star said children face years-long wait-lists and an acute lack of residential treatment options. In many cases, they said exhausted parents cannot access specialized care because their child is considered "too high-needs." When families break down, they are forced to relinquish care of their kids to children's aid societies because they've been unable to get help.
Sara Austin, founder and chief executive officer of Children First Canada, said there has been recognition for years - by child welfare leaders, advocates, families and even the government - that the systems of care meant to serve children and youth are broken, but now kids are being put in such "gravely concerning circumstances" that "we're past the tipping point."
Austin said it concerns her that the government's only response so far has been calling for an audit of child welfare agencies. While an audit may be warranted, she said, it's action that's needed.
"There have been roundtables. There have been expert opinions on this. A lot of people have weighed in on what needs to change. And what we're seeing is that this change isn't happening," Austin said. "It's certainly not happening at the rate that is truly warranted for the crisis that exists."
Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child and youth advocate from 2008 to 2019, said the province is in a "dangerous" crisis and the government "must act to quell the emergency it has created."
"When this government talks about audits and special reports and pilot projects, they are talking about where change goes to die," Elman said.
"It is particularly unconscionable when parents and children are struggling so hard, bruised and battered in the face of government's inaction."
Elman called on the government to do whatever is necessary to get these families urgent help.
In response to questions, the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services sent a statement that said the government "has been working over several years to refocus the child welfare system on high-quality and culturally appropriate services that prioritize safety, protection and the needs and circumstances of children, youth and families."
The ministry responsible for children and youth and the Ministry of Health "continue to increase funding to support children with special needs and are working together to support easier, more co-ordinated access to services, and more seamless transitions within and across child-serving sectors," the statement said.
Children's aid societies "must follow the law and ensure that placements for children are safe, appropriate and meet the child's needs," the ministry said.
The two ministries say the 2022 report provided them "with insight and recommendations to improve the lives of children and youth."
NDP Leader Marit Stiles called the situation "concerning."
"Families and kids with complex needs deserve all our support, and if the government isn't providing that, then shame on us," Stiles said, while speaking to reporters Tuesday in Lindsay, Ont.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner also called on the government to bolster support for families with children with complex needs.
"To know the government has an advisory committee that's put forward recommendations and the government is not moving on those recommendations is just a tragedy," he said.
Ottawa South Liberal MPP John Fraser, critic for children, community and social services, said the government must take immediate action and "find more appropriate spaces" for these vulnerable children, suggesting the issue "isn't a priority" for the Ford government.
"How are we housing kids with complex needs in places where none of us would expect to live in or sleep in?"
With files from Kristin Rushowy
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca and mogilvie@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Megan Ogilvie Toronto Star Amy Dempsey Raven Senior Writer With files from Kristin Rushowy The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story

Document 158 of 500

Public education has always been political

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/public-education-has-always-been-political/docview/3111855934/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Public+education+has+always+been+political&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In response to Toronto District School Board (TDSB) students attending a rally in support of the Grassy Narrows First Nation, where they also encountered pro-Palestinian protesters, Premier Doug Ford has once again accused teachers of trying to "indoctrinate our kids."
Ford admonished teachers to "stick with your knitting, stick with teaching the kids the geography, history, math, spelling."
Knitting aside, Ford's comments reflect a growing sentiment in that some educators have unduly politicized schools to promote a progressive social agenda. What is needed, according to these critics, is a return to "the basics" and a time when schools did not try to influence what kids think about social issues. The problem with this vision of value-neutral schooling is that it has never existed and never will.
Since its inception, one of the core functions of mass public schooling has been to impart certain values onto children. Since 1897, Ontario legislation has commanded schools to "inculcate" students with certain Christian values.
In its current form, the Ontario Education Act states that it is the duty of every teacher to "inculcate by precept and example respect for religion and the principles of Judeo-Christian morality and the highest regard for truth, justice, loyalty, love of country, humanity, benevolence, sobriety, industry, frugality, purity, temperance and all other virtues."
In 1980, Toronto Board of Education trustee Michael Walker cited this section of the legislation in explaining his vote against the board's decision to establish partnership with gay rights groups. Walker went on to say, "I most definitely think homosexual views and values should not be promoted as an acceptable alternative lifestyle in any school system.
By acceding to the demands of homosexuals, the Toronto Board of Education has for all practical purposes given their cause a legitimacy in the schools which it does not deserve." Was Walker right? Was the Toronto board inappropriately indoctrinating students?
Fast forward a few decades to 2023 and we have Premier Ford's former Minister of Education Stephen Lecce explicitly supporting the raising of Pride flags in all Ontario schools, saying that it is "a signal of respect and inclusion."
Most people would view going from Walker to Lecce as a sign of social progress. But how did this happen? Magic? Knitting? No, it is because institutions like the Toronto board took a political stance in support of progressive causes like gay rights and in doing so helped to change mainstream views.
The same thing applies to truth and reconciliation. Amidst the current controversy, most people agree that what has happened to the Grassy Narrows First Nation is a grave injustice that students should learn about, especially since schools now regularly teach about Indigenous issues.
But it was not always this way. In 2007, right before I became a high school teacher, I spent a month completing an internship with TDSB central staff. During this time, the board organized a fair where students from across the city presented posters on different social problems.
Several judges wanted to give one of the top awards to a student who had presented on the myriad issues faced by Canada's Indigenous Peoples. But instead, the head of the board department overseeing the competition rejected it and criticized the student for injecting "the politics of the Assembly of First Nations" into our schools.
Is this what we want to go back to?
It is important to keep in mind that we operate in a context of compulsory education, where we force kids to go to school. Given that context, special attention is always warranted to what values are being imparted to our children. And it is perfectly legitimate for people to try to influence what is being taught in schools through our democratically elected school boards and provincial legislature.
But beware when someone tells you they are going to depoliticize schools. There is no such thing as value-neutral schooling. It is just a matter of what and whose values will be taught.
Sachin Maharaj is an assistant professor of educational leadership, policy and program evaluation at the University of Ottawa.
CREDIT: Sachin Maharaj Contributor Sachin Maharaj is an assistant professor of educational leadership, policy and program evaluation at the University of Ottawa.;

Subject: Students; Educational leadership; School boards; Public schools; Social conditions & trends; Social justice; Demonstrations & protests; Indoctrination

People: Ford, Doug

Company / organization: Name: Toronto District School Board; NAICS: 611110

Document 159 of 500

Foreign student strategy needed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/foreign-student-strategy-needed/docview/3111855812/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Foreign+student+strategy+needed&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians love a good pasta restaurant, but of all the workforce challenges we face as a nation, Kitchener's Conestoga College should not be wasting limited international student visas on an "Italian Culinary Arts" course in September 2025.
Canada desperately needs more post-secondary graduates in applied biotechnology, computer science, construction engineering, early childhood education, machining and practical nursing, among other key skills.
Given the lack of affordable housing, and a local unemployment rate of more than seven per cent, why ever would Conestoga recruit students - primarily from India and China - for a one or two-year course in Charcuterie and Artisanal Foods, Interior Design, Public Relations or Social Media Marketing?
I'll risk a shortage of future osso bucco sous-chefs if that means more nurses and daycare instructors.
On the heels of broad-based pressure, effectively encapsulated by Ontario Conservative MP Arpan Khanna, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government is now rushing to reduce the number of student visas being granted. But broad-based cuts don't take into account Canada's needs - that requires a scalpel, not a machete.
Given their focus on messaging, I'm not surprised that the Liberals put social media courses on the same pedestal as construction engineering, but the downside is obvious. Ontario's Metrolinx, for example, requires a future pool of trained young professionals to deliver a 20-year pipeline of multi-faceted construction projects on behalf of Premier Doug Ford's government.
Ottawa didn't prioritize the skills that will help bring these generational infrastructure projects to fruition.
Western University President Alan Shepard put it succinctly: "The post-secondary system is bulging with offerings to entice students from around the world. But not all offerings are equal, and the students they attract are not supported equally when they arrive."
That's my alma mater boss politely reminding politicians that when Western or the University of Toronto offer a first-year student a spot, they're guaranteed a residence room. Other institutions have no such concern, as Brampton's bylaw department discovered when it found 25 students living in an illegal basement apartment.
I'd have thought that Ontario's newly appointed Minister of Colleges and Universities, Nolan Quinn, could bring order to this chaos. That the minister might roll back the excesses, including the new $495,000 salary that Conestoga's Board of Governors recently awarded President John Tibbits (a figure that's higher than the presidential salaries at McMaster or Queen's, for example).
Not likely, according to one of Quinn's predecessors.
"(Post secondary institutions) are ungovernable," according to Glen Murray, who held the Training, Colleges and Universities cabinet portfolio under former Liberal Premier Dalton McGuinty. They may be our tax dollars, but Ontario's funding formula gives each institution wide latitude to run their own show.
Colleges and Institutes Canada president Pari Johnston claims that Trudeau's visa cuts will cause "significant harm ... to local businesses that rely on the schools to supply graduates to the labour force."
I asked her team for the underlying analysis, but was told "no one data set" supported their assertion.
The last time Gallup checked, 74 million people wanted to immigrate to Canada. That luxury means we can "craft a careful and co-ordinated strategy that makes the best use of top global talent," as Shepard argues.
One former Ontario community college instructor told me she quit in disgust when she realized that most of her international students "had zero interest in the class. It was all about getting permanent residency."
Murray and I are in violent agreement when he says that "our national interest is to have the most highly educated, skilled and civil society in the world. To reinforce our democracy and strengthen our economy and productivity."
That requires choices: "Who do we want to come here to be educated? Who do we want to come and be educated and go back to their country? Who do we want to retain here?"
These essential questions are neither partisan nor anti-immigrant and should guide an international student strategy that reflects our national interest.
Mark McQueen is a Toronto-based entrepreneur who has worked on Bay Street for more than 30 years. He is the former board chair of two federal agencies and was an adviser in the Brian Mulroney PMO. He is a freelance contributing columnist for the Star.
CAPTION: Canada desperately needs more graduates in biotechnology, engineering, and nursing. So why are schools recruiting international students for courses in public relations and interior design, Mark McQueen asks.Conestoga College

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+TDSB+cancels+Truth+and+Reconciliation+Day+field+trips+to+Nathan+Phillips+Square+citing+%27abundance+of+caution%2C%27+Sept.+30+Poilievre+acts+as+if+there%27s+power+in+being+unlikeable.+It%27s+not+a+good+look%3A+Delacourt%2C+Sept.+26+After+40+years+at+the+Star%2C+Ellie+Tesher+bids+her+readers+a+very+fond+farewell%2C+Sept.+28&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Truth and Reconciliation too important
TDSB cancels Truth and Reconciliation Day field trips to Nathan Phillips Square citing 'abundance of caution,' Sept. 30
I am in agreement that visits by school-aged children to the Truth and Reconciliation Day ceremony would have been beneficial. The students would learn about tolerance, inclusivity and acceptance of all people, as worthy Canadians, regardless of race and religion. The ceremony was extremely important for everyone to understand the crimes committed via residential schools.
Eric Bornstein, Toronto
School board right to put pause on field trips
It is great that the TDSB is putting a hold on field trips but did they not communicate this to their system? Allegedly two schools still attended the event that could have had political protests, but fortunately did not. This was the same type of event that attracted protests last time. Did it not reach the teachers from the two schools that field trips were on pause? Why did the superintendents not step in or know about the pause? TDSB is a huge board, but surely their internal communications must improve.
Joel Hertz, Toronto,
former York Region District School Board vice-chair
As one who has taught for 35 years, I was disgusted to hear about the TDSB school protest trip. What were those teachers thinking? The answer, of course, is that they weren't thinking. If 15 schools were involved, this must have been the idea of one or more consultants from the board office. Most teachers know that a lot of these people are really not grounded in reality. The lesson here for teachers is simple. Don't be afraid to stand up to board office people and their ideas. I did it in my day! Be the leaders you are supposed to be. Put the well-being of your students first! Finally, there must be consequences for those who suggested and planned this trip. Discipline or dismissal from the Ontario College of Teachers is essential. As for teachers' federations, stay out of politics. You're being paid to negotiate good contracts, salaries and working conditions and to protect members. That's it.
John Sudak, Hamilton
Nonsense in Parliament
Poilievre acts as if there's power in being unlikeable. It's not a good look: Delacourt, Sept. 26
Why would anyone who cares about Canada want these adolescent men representing us, not just in this country but on the world stage? If a person doesn't know the basics of good manners and how to conduct themselves with some form of decorum, they should not be allowed to represent our country either at home or abroad. I would hate to think they allowed their children to carry on in such a way in their classrooms or anywhere else.
Angela Ingram, Tiny, Ont.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's recent behaviour in the House of Commons was immature and unbecoming someone who aspires to be prime minister. His childish recital of meaningless slogans followed by a silly grin was inappropriate and contributes to the low level of discourse in the House. He has demonstrated that he is not ready to be the leader of the country and I hope the voters he has attracted with his slogans will wake up to the fact that there is no substance to his utterings.
Gordon Simpson, Toronto
Beware promises by politicians
If the Trudeau Liberals have fulfilled 90 per cent of their political promises, yet owning or renting housing are unaffordable, food banks are overwhelmed, and finding a family doctor is almost impossible, they were clearly making the wrong promises. Premier Doug Ford wasted millions fulfilling promises to cancel wind turbine contracts and get beer in corner stores, and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre will cripple our economy's transition to a low carbon future if he fulfils his promise to "axe the tax."
Rob Strang, Orangeville
As I contemplate the next federal election, I find I have no choice but to vote for Justin Trudeau. Better the devil you know than the one you don't. I don't like Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre with his nasty, aggressive manner, and while he brags that he will axe the tax, fix the budget, build the homes and fight crime, I've seen no details. How would he propose to deal with climate change once the carbon "tax" is gone? What unique measures would he take to ensure millions of affordable homes are built nationwide? What services and programs would be cut to fix the budget? Is crime so high that it requires electing him to fight it? While I like NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, I don't see him and his party as strong enough to lead the country. Poilievre does not have the personality to represent me in the nation's highest office.
Bob Coupland, Oakville
A smart business person would do a test run first
Why doesn't the Ford government pay for Highway 407 tolls for a week or month and see how that alleviates traffic on Highway 401 instead of spending billions on a new highway or tunnel? See what the impact of another highway would do to daily traffic jams. A smart business person would test first before you spend unnecessary money on something that might not be needed.
Patti Pokorchak, Toronto
Premier Doug Ford has proposed a tunnel underneath Highway 401. This has got to be one of the worst schemes I have ever heard. To start with, there would have to be a feasibility study, which would cost mega bucks. I could do it for a few dollars on one page and it would read "forget it," thereby saving the province billions of dollars.
Carl Irwin, Flesherton, Ont.
Thank you, Ellie
After 40 years at the Star, Ellie Tesher bids her readers a very fond farewell, Sept. 28
I have read the Star almost every day since the late 1980s and luckily discovered Eliie Tesher's advice column some 23 years ago. As a teacher in different capacities for more than 45 years, Ellie's advice helped me to cultivate a better relationship with some of my students who struggled to find a path in life. On a personal level, Ellie points out that travelling is one of the most fascinating life lessons, broadening our minds while making us aware of the world around us. My travels to different parts of the world where some of the great monuments were built was an eye opener helping to reinforce my faith in humanity. Congratulations on an amazing professional journey. Happy retirement.
Robert Ariano, Scarborough

Document 161 of 500

Protesting farmers greet Doug Ford at International Plowing Match

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/protesting-farmers-greet-doug-ford-at/docview/3112186146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protesting+farmers+greet+Doug+Ford+at+International+Plowing+Match&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LINDSAY, ONT. - A group of farmers came to the plowing match here hoping to get Premier Doug Ford to start paying a little less attention to tunnelling under Highway 401 and a little more to what's going on above ground.
With about 770 acres of farmland in the Waterloo area under threat of expropriation for unknown reasons, the farmers wanted to gain the support of other farmers as well as politicians on Tuesday at the annual event.
"We've come from Wilmot to put our voices forward, to help people understand what the issues are with the land acquisition that's proposed," said Alfred Lowrick, who lives beside the properties the region wants to obtain, and whose mother-in-law received an expropriation notice.
While the region is the expropriating authority, there's confusion and secrecy around the province's role and what it is funding, he added, "so there's a bit of a quandary of who we should be talking to and what we should be addressing."
The region has acquired one of seven properties identified for future, unspecified industrial use "and the rest of the farmers don't want to sell. They're not interested in doing this, and the community isn't," he added, referring to support for a local lawn-sign campaign.
The Wilmot farmers were handing out stickers in support of their cause to those who attended opening day of the 105th annual International Plowing Match and Rural Expo, held this year at the Lindsay Fairgrounds.
Every fall, it is a major weeklong event for rural Ontario - one that draws cabinet ministers, MPPs and the party leaders, who take part in a plowing contest.
Asked about the Wilmot issue by reporters, Ford said, "I just want the region to treat them fairly."
But Waterloo MPP Catherine Fife, a New Democrat, said "there has not been one public meeting, not one fiscal report to the general public and now there's just a finger-pointing exercise between the regional government and the PC government - they're just blaming each other."
Fife charged that the provincial government is "driving the whole process," and said the public deserves answers.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, taking part in her first plowing match as head of the provincial party, said the Wilmot farmers "know that we've been with them."
Crombie said farmers also have the same concerns as the rest of Ontario's residents - such as trying to find a family doctor or keeping the emergency room open at their local hospital.
Ford also told reporters that while there are a lot of opinions about tunnelling under Highway 401, a proposal he announced last week, "we're just doing a feasibility study. Anyone that's a driver on the 401 on a daily basis knows we're just jammed."
The congestion "takes $11 billion a year out of our economy - not to mention all the time you can either spend at home with your family, have some fun in the sun or whatever you want to do."
Lowrick said that while tunnelling would cost billions of dollars for "a nice little hole they want to drill" to address heavy traffic in the Greater Toronto Area, the Ford government is "missing the boat" by not also addressing the concerns of farmers.
CAPTION: A protester opposed to development of farmland in Wilmot County holds a sign as Premier Doug Ford takes part in the parade at the International Plowing Match in Lindsay, Ont., on Oct. 1, 2024.

Take-off at Billy Bishop airport in jeopardy + family reeling after Toronto's latest homicide

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/take-off-at-billy-bishop-airport-jeopardy-family/docview/3111599726/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, October 1 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the fate of Billy Bishop airport, aftershocks from Toronto's latest homicide and why TDSB cancelled Truth and Reconciliation Day field trips.
DON'T MISSCity staff recommend against extending Billy Bishop airport's lease
Billy Bishop airport is "disappointed" after city staff recommended against a multidecade extension to its lease. Without it, the airport cannot finance the federally mandated runway extensions it must build by 2027, Ben Cohen reports. Mayor Olivia Chow's executive committee will mull the recommendation today as the airport's fate hangs in the balance. Here's what you need to know.
Go deeper: To meet federal requirements, Billy Bishop proposed three runway safety projects, of which city staff chose the smallest and cheapest. The proposed 54 metres long, 145 metres wide addition to the runway would cost $64 million, to be covered entirely by PortsToronto, the airport's owner.Why it matters: Billy Bishop is the ninth busiest airport in Canada, having served 2.8 million passengers in 2019. PortsToronto says it generates $2.1 billion in economic output and employs more than 2,000 people.A family was devastated by Toronto's latest homicide
In an especially deadly year for gun violence, Anthony Dowayne McBean became Toronto's 66th homicide victim on Sunday night, Calvi Leon reports. McBean's father, Anthony, said this was the second time he's lost a son to gun violence in this city: "Now, my first-born; it's the same thing again... How do we stop it?"
Remembrance: A well-known figure in his community, McBean is survived by his 16 kids and five grandchildren. "He messaged me yesterday, and his last words were, 'I love you,'" one of his daughters said.A day earlier: Hundreds of people took to the streets on Saturday demanding an end to gun violence on the National Day of Remembrance for Victims of Homicide. Shootings have spiked this year, up approximately 40 per cent from 2023.Why TDSB cancelled Truth and Reconciliation Day field trips
Toronto's public school board cancelled field trips to a ceremony celebrating a new monument to residential school survivors on Truth and Reconciliation Day, over the "potential risk" of it turning into a political protest, Morgan Sevareid-Bocknek, Isabel Teotonio and Reagan McSwain report. Here's why six schools - including at least one focusing on Indigenous studies - cancelled their trips to Nathan Phillips Square yesterday.
ICYMI: TDSB is under investigation by the province after, roughly two weeks ago, students attended a rally for Indigenous rights that attracted pro-Palestinian protesters. Until the probe is complete, TDSB has banned all field trips to protests.Word from teachers: Some teachers reported feeling "devastated" about the cancelled event: "It seems like (the TDSB) is very worried about anything that might be political - but by its nature reconciliation is political."WHAT ELSE
Mélanie Joly warned the UN about leaders who undermine democracy. Take a look at her dire message.A Liberal MP said he shouldn't be silenced in the House of Commons for linking the Conservatives to Putin.Israel assassinated Hezbollah's chief. Here's who they may target next.Why this Ontario foreign worker suddenly had her OHIP coverage revoked.Conservatives maintain their lead as the NDP take second place outside Quebec. Check out our latest poll.JD Vance and Tim Walz will battle in the first U.S. vice-presidential debate tonight. Here's a quick primer.Another round of GST/HST credit payments go out this week. Here's how much you could expect.Oasis is coming to Toronto. Given the band's history of drama, do you need ticket insurance?Tributes pour in for NBA Hall of Famer Dikembe Mutombo, dead at 58 from brain cancer.Shaq tried to go unnoticed in downtown Toronto. It didn't last very long.This goat runner won hearts and even a medal after crashing a Newfoundland marathon.These are the most popular baby names in Canada over the last year.POV
The Bloc Québécois is trying to get the Trudeau government to give me more money. Here's why I don't want it.CLOSE-UP
NATHAN PHILLIPS SQUARE: Talon White-Eye dances on stage at a ceremony marking Truth and Reconciliation Day in Toronto yesterday. The city also unveiled a new, permanent spirit garden at Nathan Phillips Square commemorating residential school survivors and the children who never returned home, Joy SpearChief-Morris writes. Take a look.
