Document 163 of 500

Doug Ford's 401 tunnel fantasy is a distraction from what Ontario should be spending money on

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-401-tunnel-fantasy-is-distraction-what/docview/3111599722/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+401+tunnel+fantasy+is+a+distraction+from+what+Ontario+should+be+spending+money+on&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Watching Premier Doug Ford stand in Etobicoke last Wednesday and announce that he would somehow build a tunnel underneath Highway 401 that would somehow reduce traffic in the GTA, I let my mind drift to thoughts of alternate timelines. Timelines with different Doug Fords making different announcements in different places.
Oh, what could have been.
For instance, instead of standing in Etobicoke to announce an idea of a concept of a plan to build a ludicrously expensive highway tunnel, in another timeline Ford spent the day standing in Northeast Scarborough to announce that his government would put up the $4.5 billion needed to build the Eglinton East LRT.
Unlike the tunnel, the LRT is a transportation plan that, well, exists. It's a 27-stop 18.6-kilometre line that would link Kennedy Station to Malvern Town Centre, passing through the University of Toronto Scarborough campus. There's a business case and an environmental project report. It's just missing funding.
In another, pretty similar timeline, the premier might have stood down near the waterfront, to announce $2.7 billion in funding for the Waterfront East LRT. It's an important transit link that was promised to the businesses and institutions that invested in the burgeoning eastern waterfront. And heck, provincial funding for the transit line could even be conditional on city hall adding more density to their plans for the port lands, helping Ford hit provincial housing targets.
If neither of those realities are to your liking, there's a timeline where Ford could have stationed himself at Yonge-Bloor subway station, perhaps flanked by representatives from Toronto Public Health and CAMH, to announce the needed long-term investment of $4.1 billion to install platform edge doors in Toronto's subway stations. Studies suggest platform doors can reduce deaths by suicide by as much as 89 per cent.
And, of course, there's a timeline where he could announce something about Highway 407. Even if buying back the toll highway is deemed unworkable, a 2023 study by British consulting firm Eunomia estimated that for about $4 billion, the province could cover the tolls for all commercial truck traffic on the 407 for 30 years. While it would be pretty frustrating to see the government subsidize tolls on a highway it should never have sold in the first place, getting all those trucks off the 401 seems like it would help a fair bit.
Not every alternative timeline has a transportation announcement, though. A different Ford could have announced $3.7 billion annually over the next 10 years to bring Ontario's per-capita spending on hospitals in line with the national average. Or he could have announced the $16.8 billion needed for schools to tackle their repair bills. Or he could have announced the $44 billion his own Financial Accountability Office projects could be needed - at minimum - through 2030 to proactively strengthen municipal and provincial infrastructure to better withstand the effects of climate change.
Or, in maybe the best of these alternate timelines, a different version of the premier could have announced literally all of this. Everything.
Because the figures I've given you in this column total about $113 billion. The Ford government hasn't offered any official cost estimates yet, but, for comparison, my colleague Edward Keenan, using figures from Boston's "Big Dig" tunnel project, estimated the cost of Ford's 401 tunnel from Brampton to Scarborough at somewhere north of $150 billion.
If that holds, it means for about the same amount of money, Ontario could theoretically fix every school, significantly improve the hospital system for the next decade, build two new major transit projects, shift truck traffic off the 401, protect our infrastructure from climate change, and literally save lives in our subway stations by installing platform doors. Oh, and there might even be enough left over to cut every person living in Ontario a $2,000 cheque.
Alternatively, Ontario could maybe spend decades building a tunnel under the 401.
While it's somewhat laudable that Ford is willing to spend money on the province's future, it is ridiculous that the premier's tunnel vision can't let him see how much more he could do with this kind of spending. It's doubly ridiculous that Ford's support for bad ideas continues to take time and resources away from good ones. This may or may not be the darkest timeline, but I've got no doubt it's the silliest.
CAPTION: Doug Ford's fantasy about building a tunnel under the 401 would take money away from fixes for a lot of pressing problems.

Subject: Funding; Cost estimates

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost estimates

Title: Doug Ford's 401 tunnel fantasy is a distraction from what Ontario should be spending money on

Document 164 of 500

Doug Ford's tunnel fantasy gives politics a bad name

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-tunnel-fantasy-gives-politics-bad-name/docview/3111599685/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+tunnel+fantasy+gives+politics+a+bad+name&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's fantasy tunnel will never be built. Let's start there. There will never be a 50-km tunnel connecting Markham to Brampton. There will be no shorter version linking Scarborough to Etobicoke. Not with roads. Not with trains. Not at all.
Put aside the cost. (North of $60 billion according to some estimates.) Put aside the logistics. (Imagine the Eglinton crosstown debacle times 10.) The fact is that even if the tunnel was built it wouldn't do much to solve the GTA's very real and very pressing traffic woes. It would induce more traffic, bring in more cars, cause even more gridlock.
Perhaps for Ford that's neither here nor there. His tunnel gambit, after all, was politically effective, goading his opponents into shooting down a plan, however far-fetched, to address the congestion crisis that is top of mind for so many in this city.
Never mind that this kind of politicking encourages people to be even more cynical about politics than they already are. Never mind that it is not a realistic proposal to ease the GTA's traffic nightmares, a far cry from congestion pricing, road tolls, major new transit investments or any number of other ideas pushed by policy wonks. Instead, the premier is choosing easy politics rather than doing the hard work of dealing with the actual problems this province faces.
Just think of all the things Ford wasn't talking about last week when he was talking about digging a tunnel about as long as the distance from the CN Tower to St. Catharines. He wasn't talking about long-term care, where, the Star reported last week, seniors in one Scarborough home are being warehoused next to a lobby all day. He wasn't talking about children in government custody being shipped to hotels, Airbnbs and even office buildings because the province can't care for them properly. He wasn't talking about parents giving up their own children with complex needs because they simply can't get the supports they require. He wasn't talking about the plan to deal with that crisis his own government commissioned three years ago and then shelved.
He wasn't talking about kids being strip-searched in provincial jails. He wasn't talking about school test scores - math, reading and writing are all down in Toronto. He wasn't talking about the crisis in primary care, about hallway medicine, about housing costs, or about the giant fiscal cliff colleges and universities are facing in this province as international student numbers decline.
He wasn't even really talking about improving traffic. Not in a serious way. Which is a shame because traffic requires our serious attention. Toronto's traffic problems are among the world's worst. Many of us know all too well the profound frustrations of endless commutes that corrode our quality of life, harm the environment and hurt our city's competitiveness. Of course, there are no panaceas, in the sky or underground. The real solutions are politically challenging, logistically complicated and often dull. Few politicians want to talk about working with cities on moving to 24-hour construction scheduling, which would require municipalities and their construction partners to coordinate in ways that would be new to both. Few want to talk about moving to a world with fewer cars on the road. That stuff is boring, complicated and hard work compared to a giant tunnel.
At his best, Ford is an empathetic and flexible leader in touch with the concerns of many in this province. We often disagree with him on policy. But we believe he shares the Star's commitment to a stronger, better and even faster Ontario. That's why this kind of naked politicking is so disappointing.
After more than six years in power, we expect better - and we deserve better, too.
CAPTION: Ontario Premier Doug Ford attends the US-Canada Summit in Toronto in June.

Title: Doug Ford's tunnel fantasy gives politics a bad name

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 1, 2024

Section: opinion/editorials

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 165 of 500

I love my EV and the carbon tax rebate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-love-my-ev-carbon-tax-rebate/docview/3111599322/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+love+my+EV+and+the+carbon+tax+rebate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The rest of the world knows the best tool to fight climate change. Canada is abandoning it, Sept. 24
While others badmouth the carbon tax, my wife and I love it, and cheer all the way to the bank when we receive our rebate. As owners of one vehicle, an EV, we smile as we pass gas stations, and grin at how little it costs to charge our car at home. Fortunately, a couple of years ago, our forward-looking condo corporation installed fifteen level 2 charging stations in the underground parking, allowing us to charge while we sleep. We are seniors on a fixed income and far from wealthy, but we can easily afford our EV. The federal EV purchase rebate helped with the purchase, and the carbon tax rebate helps cover the small operating cost. In addition, we find pleasure in knowing that we are doing something to reduce our carbon footprint. This is how the carbon tax is supposed to work: reduce your carbon footprint and reap the benefits. But that aside, let me say more about EV ownership. Driving an EV is fun. The car is quiet and instantly responsive, and the technology, with its safety features, driving aids and entertainment, remains endlessly fascinating. Our daily trips around town use very little battery, and long trips, even to Florida and back, have proved easy, uneventful, inexpensive and a joy. The media, and our provincial government have not done what they should to promote the carbon tax or EVs. Subsidizing battery factories and car manufacturers is not enough. We need more charging infrastructure and positive reporting about EVs. Imagine the clean air and quiet roads if there were more EVs, not just cars and SUVs, but trucks and buses.
Bob Coupland, Oakville
Poilievre's slogan should read 'Reject Your Carbon Tax Rebate'
Reading Andrew Phillips's opinion piece caused me to think about Pierre Poilievre's slogan "Axe the Tax." For truth in advertising, or political slogans, Poilievre's slogan should read "Reject Your Carbon Tax Rebate."
David B. Clemens, Toronto
Carbon pricing plan is not a tax
In this almost excellent article by Andrew Phillips regarding the carbon conundrum, I fear he has caused a measure of misunderstanding. Throughout the piece, he too often uses the term "carbon tax," rather than "carbon pricing." For the slow among us, this creates bewilderment. What needs to be explained, in big bold print, is that the carbon pricing plan is not a tax, in any way, shape or form.
Jack Drury, Toronto
Tunnel under Highway 401 is absurd
Doug Ford announces proposal for tunnel under Highway 401 to carry more traffic, Sept. 27
Although one should no longer be surprised by Premier Doug Ford's numerous ideas to kowtow to motorists, his rich so-called friends, and his meddling into Toronto's jurisdictional matters, his latest plan to build a tunnel under Highway 401 to ease traffic congestion is nothing short of absurd. The cost alone is astronomical and would be far better spent were it invested in education and health care for all people living in Ontario. Instead, the number of years spent on the tunnel construction, the increase in traffic once completed, and the devastating effects on our environment could all be avoided by encouraging travel on Highway 407. The reimbursement of the tolls paid by these 407 motorists (to a private consortium rather than to the government, thanks to a former Conservative government sell-off) could be used to offset their provincial taxes which may in fact prove to be a lower expenditure than Ford's latest inane solution to ease traffic congestion that, in the long run, would only cause the present problem to be exacerbated.
Barbara Saul, Toronto
Neveah is the result of underfunding children's aid societiesBefore the girl was found in a Rosedale dumpster, Toronto Children's Aid had closed her case. Turns out, they had a history of closing cases too early, Sept. 29
Kudos to the Star for once again breaking open a story that is of dire consequence to individuals in our society and to society overall when social needs are not met leading to consequences including crime, substance abuse, ill health, violence and sometimes death. Neveah Tucker (body found in dumpster) was just the tip of the iceberg when it comes to the results of underfunding children's aid societies.
Brian O'Sullivan, Stouffville
Puffin festival celebrated since 1874In Iceland, doing Puffin stuff, celebrating a truly puffling experience, Sept. 28
The very interesting report on the Puffin Festival in Iceland brought back some happy memories as I worked in a fish processing factory on Heimaey (the largest of the Westmann Islands) for two summers while at university in the early 1960s. In those days there was no fast ferry so getting to Heimaey usually involved a very scary flight in a tiny plane that landed on a runway that was probably too short, but as it ran from one side of the island to the other, there was little choice. On one trip the pilot touched down several times before the plane finally managed to land safely, within about five feet of a cliff edge.
The local population had told us about the Þjóðhátíð ("National Festival") which has been celebrated on the first Monday in August since 1874 - when the millennium of Icelandic settlement was celebrated. In the 1960s it was obviously much more of a local festival than it is today, and we heard about and saw the pufflings mentioned in the article. However, in those days one of the culinary highlights of the festival was "roast puffling on a stick." Yes, I tried it (once). It tasted very fishy. It's good to hear that they are now more carefully conserved.
David S. Crawford, Toronto
Disability parking permit not honoured at hospital
As a disabled person I feel very fortunate to have a disability parking permit when parking in Toronto.
However, on a recent visit to Humber Hospital where I parked in an area with a disability parking spot, I was shocked to find out that disability permits are not honoured. I received a $70 parking ticket. There were no signs stating that disability permits were not acceptable. Using my walker to pay and then returning to my car to put the payment voucher in my windshield is not easy, especially when the payment machine is not located close to the disability parking area.
It's inconceivable that a place dealing with health issues would not allow disability permits.
When I called to complain, I was told that hospitals are private property. Am I wrong to think that hospitals are public property funded by taxpayers and shouldn't hospitals provide free parking since they fall under the umbrella of health care? Hopefully this issue will be investigated and disabled patients not penalized.
Mari Carlo, Toronto
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre signs an "Axe the Tax" rally sign in Iqaluit on Sept. 9.

Subject: Carbon footprint; Children & youth; Automobile drivers; Permits; Disability; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Festivals; Environmental tax; Factories

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax Factories

Document 166 of 500

Doug Ford's tunnel fantasy gives politics a bad name

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/doug-fords-tunnel-fantasy-gives-politics-bad-name/docview/3111422090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+tunnel+fantasy+gives+politics+a+bad+name&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's fantasy tunnel will never be built. Let's start there.

Document 167 of 500

What Ontario should be spending on

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/what-ontario-should-be-spending-on/docview/3111422086/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+Ontario+should+be+spending+on&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Watching Premier Doug Ford stand in Etobicoke last Wednesday and announce that he would somehow build a tunnel underneath Highway 401 that would somehow reduce traffic in the GTA, I let my mind drift to thoughts of alternate timelines. Timelines with different Doug Fords making different announcements in different places.
Oh, what could have been.
For instance, instead of standing in Etobicoke to announce an idea of a concept of a plan to build a ludicrously expensive highway tunnel, in another timeline Ford spent the day standing in Northeast Scarborough to announce that his government would put up the $4.5 billion needed to build the Eglinton East LRT.
Unlike the tunnel, the LRT is a transportation plan that, well, exists. It's a 27-stop 18.6-kilometre line that would link Kennedy Station to Malvern Town Centre, passing through the University of Toronto Scarborough campus. There's a business case and an environmental project report. It's just missing funding.
In another, pretty similar timeline, the premier might have stood down near the waterfront, to announce $2.7 billion in funding for the Waterfront East LRT. It's an important transit link that was promised to the businesses and institutions that invested in the burgeoning eastern waterfront. And heck, provincial funding for the transit line could even be conditional on city hall adding more density to their plans for the port lands, helping Ford hit provincial housing targets.
If neither of those realities are to your liking, there's a timeline where Ford could have stationed himself at Yonge-Bloor subway station, perhaps flanked by representatives from Toronto Public Health and CAMH, to announce the needed long-term investment of $4.1 billion to install platform edge doors in Toronto's subway stations. Studies suggest platform doors can reduce deaths by suicide by as much as 89 per cent.
And, of course, there's a timeline where he could announce something about Highway 407. Even if buying back the toll highway is deemed unworkable, a 2023 study by British consulting firm Eunomia estimated that for about $4 billion, the province could cover the tolls for all commercial truck traffic on the 407 for 30 years. While it would be pretty frustrating to see the government subsidize tolls on a highway it should never have sold in the first place, getting all those trucks off the 401 seems like it would help a fair bit.
Not every alternative timeline has a transportation announcement, though. A different Ford could have announced $3.7 billion annually over the next 10 years to bring Ontario's per-capita spending on hospitals in line with the national average. Or he could have announced the $16.8 billion needed for schools to tackle their repair bills. Or he could have announced the $44 billion his own Financial Accountability Office projects could be needed - at minimum - through 2030 to proactively strengthen municipal and provincial infrastructure to better withstand the effects of climate change.
Or, in maybe the best of these alternate timelines, a different version of the premier could have announced literally all of this. Everything.
Because the figures I've given you in this column total about $113 billion. The Ford government hasn't offered any official cost estimates yet, but, for comparison, my colleague Edward Keenan, using figures from Boston's "Big Dig" tunnel project, estimated the cost of Ford's 401 tunnel from Brampton to Scarborough at somewhere north of $150 billion.
If that holds, it means for about the same amount of money, Ontario could theoretically fix every school, significantly improve the hospital system for the next decade, build two new major transit projects, shift truck traffic off the 401, protect our infrastructure from climate change, and literally save lives in our subway stations by installing platform doors. Oh, and there might even be enough left over to cut every person living in Ontario a $2,000 cheque.
Alternatively, Ontario could maybe spend decades building a tunnel under the 401.
While it's somewhat laudable that Ford is willing to spend money on the province's future, it is ridiculous that the premier's tunnel vision can't let him see how much more he could do with this kind of spending. It's doubly ridiculous that Ford's support for bad ideas continues to take time and resources away from good ones. This may or may not be the darkest timeline, but I've got no doubt it's the silliest.
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Subject: Cost estimates; Tunnels; Federal funding; Government spending; Provincial government

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost estimates Federal funding Government spending

People: Ford, Doug

Corrections

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/corrections/docview/3111422046/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Corrections&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A Sept. 28, 2024 article about Premier Doug Ford and Hwy. 407 incorrectly stated that his government never considered buying the highway before announcing the 401 tunnel proposal. In fact, the government had previously thought about buying back the 407.A Sept. 28 opinion article about Justin Trudeau's carbon pricing plan incorrectly stated that his government suspended the carbon price on heating oil in Atlantic Canada. In fact, the change was implemented across the country. In addition, Gerald Butts is vice chair at Eurasia Group, not vice president, as the article said.

Document 169 of 500

TDSB cancels Truth and Reconciliation Day field trips to Nathan Phillips Square citing 'abundance of caution'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tdsb-cancels-truth-reconciliation-day-field-trips/docview/3111421883/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TDSB+cancels+Truth+and+Reconciliation+Day+field+trips+to+Nathan+Phillips+Square+citing+%27abundance+of+caution%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's public school board says it made the "difficult" decision to cancel field trips to a ceremony marking a new monument to honour residential school survivors because of the possibility it could turn into a political protest.
About six schools called off their planned trip to the Indigenous Legacy Gathering on Monday, the National Day for Truth and Reconciliation. At least one of those schools focuses on Indigenous studies.
The Toronto District School Board says it made the move "out of an abundance of caution" because roughly two weeks ago students attended a rally about Indigenous rights that attracted pro-Palestinian protesters.
In a statement to the Star, TDSB spokesperson Ryan Bird said the board-wide decision to cancel the trips Monday "was a difficult one."
"Staff was very concerned that this event taking place at Nathan Phillips Square, although highly educational and in line with our commitment to Truth and Reconciliation, posed a potential risk," he said by email.
The concern, he noted, was that staff and organizers "could not provide assurance" that the event would not become a site for a political protest, march or rally.
The province is currently investigating the board for the rally students attended mid-September and until that probe is complete the TDSB has cancelled field trips that could involve a protest, march or rally.
One teacher, who asked not to be identified because she was not authorized to speak, said she was "devastated" about having to cancel the trip, which marked the official opening of the Spirit Garden to honour residential school survivors. (It's in response to the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's Call to Action 82, which calls on each capital city in Canada to install a residential school monument.)
"To not be able to bring children, both Indigenous and non-Indigenous, down for this really, really important day is really upsetting to me," she told the Star. "It's some sort of strange reaction to the Grassy Narrows" event.
"It seems like (the TDSB) is very worried about anything that might be political, but by its nature reconciliation is political."
On Sept. 18, students from about 15 schools attended the Grassy Narrows River Run, billed as a community event to support the Grassy Narrows First Nation and their efforts to address mercury contamination. It started downtown at Grange Park, then demonstrators marched to Queen's Park.
Parents were told it was an "educational opportunity" and that students wouldn't participate in the rally. But many students and teachers joined the solidarity march for Indigenous rights, which attracted thousands. Controversy erupted when videos posted on social media showed some students and staff alongside pro-Palestinian protesters, and joining in chats, such as "From Turtle Island to Palestine, Occupation is a Crime."
Some parents cited concerns about students being exposed to antisemitic chants, and the board apologized "for the harm that some students may have experienced." However, some community members have called the apology an example of anti-Palestinian racism.
The Ministry of Education has ordered a provincial investigation into the Sept. 18 trip. Premier Doug Ford has criticized the TDSB, saying it must be "held accountable" for taking kids to a rally intended to be about Indigenous rights but that included pro-Palestinian protesters, calling it "disgraceful."
In a statement, the ministry said it wasn't involved in the decision to cancel Monday's trips and directed all inquiries to the TDSB.
Monday's event attracted politicians, student groups and families, with events spread across the square showcasing traditional Indigenous speakers, singers and dancers while unveiling the new addition of the Spirit Garden to the public.
Sources said they saw students and staff from at least two TDSB schools. Asked about this, the TDSB replied that last week, direction was given to schools to let their superintendent know if they had plans to attend, and all trips that they were made aware of were cancelled.
More than 100 residential school survivors were present; some spoke about how much it meant to see so many children there.
Jay Young, a parent of an 11-year-old student at D. A. Morrison Middle School, said his daughter's field trip to the event had been in the works for several weeks. Last Friday, a PA day, he said he received an email from the principal announcing the cancellation of the trip, "out of an abundance of caution." The Star has not seen a copy of the email.
Young said he was disappointed his daughter wouldn't hear from Indigenous voices on the National Day of Truth and Reconciliation, and would have taken her out of school himself if he did not have a previous commitment. He said the possibility of protests exists at any public event, and would become a teaching moment for his child, he said.
It's a day of sorrow, where unfortunate truths need to be communicated, he said.
"I think people really need to give children credit, even children who are younger than my daughter, that they understand and that they can learn."
In an interview, a TDSB teacher said Monday's event would have been key for students of all backgrounds to attend "because there are so few Indigenous teachers in the board."
"Teachers rely on field trips to have authentic, decolonial education," said the teacher, who asked not to be identified because they weren't authorized to speak.
"In particular, any Indigenous student in the TDSB would have benefited from being able to attend."
The event, however, did attract many community members and school groups.
Teacher Vikkie Fung of YMCA Academy, which isn't part of the TDSB, said it was important to take her students so they could "learn more about truth and reconciliation, about our history, about our heritage.
"We always have to reflect on the past to build the future."
The TDSB says all of its 582 schools marked the day in multiple ways, including assemblies, activities and wearing orange.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+The+rest+of+the+world+knows+the+best+tool+to+fight+climate+change.+Canada+is+abandoning+it%2C+Sept.+24+Doug+Ford+announces+proposal+for+tunnel+under+Highway+401+to+carry+more+traffic%2C+Sept.+27+Before+the+girl+was+found+in+a+Rosedale+dumpster%2C+Toronto+Children%27s+Aid+had+closed+her+case.+Turns+out%2C+they+had+a+history+of+closing+cases+too+early%2C+Sept.+29+In+Iceland%2C+doing+Puffin+stuff%2C+celebrating+a+truly+puffling+experience%2C+Sept.+28&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I love my EV
and my carbon tax rebate
The rest of the world knows the best tool to fight climate change. Canada is abandoning it, Sept. 24
While others badmouth the carbon tax, my wife and I love it, and cheer all the way to the bank when we receive our rebate. As owners of one vehicle, an EV, we smile as we pass gas stations, and grin at how little it costs to charge our car at home. Fortunately, a couple of years ago, our forward-looking condo corporation installed 15 level 2 charging stations in the underground parking, allowing us to charge while we sleep. We are seniors on a fixed income and far from wealthy, but we can easily afford our EV. The federal EV purchase rebate helped with the purchase, and the carbon tax rebate helps cover the small operating cost. In addition, we find pleasure in knowing that we are doing something to reduce our carbon footprint. This is how the carbon tax is supposed to work: reduce your carbon footprint and reap the benefits. But that aside, let me say more about EV ownership. Driving an EV is fun. The car is quiet and instantly responsive, and the technology, with its safety features, driving aids and entertainment, remains endlessly fascinating. Our daily trips around town use very little battery, and long trips, even to Florida and back, have proved easy, uneventful, inexpensive and a joy. The media, and our provincial government have not done what they should to promote the carbon tax or EVs. Subsidizing battery factories and car manufacturers is not enough. We need more charging infrastructure and positive reporting about EVs. Imagine the clean air and quiet roads if there were more EVs, not just cars and SUVs, but trucks and buses.
Bob Coupland, Oakville
Tunnel under Highway 401
is absurd
Doug Ford announces proposal for tunnel under Highway 401 to carry more traffic, Sept. 27
Although one should no longer be surprised by Premier Doug Ford's numerous ideas to kowtow to motorists, his rich so-called friends, and his meddling into Toronto's jurisdictional matters, his latest plan to build a tunnel under Highway 401 to ease traffic congestion is nothing short of absurd. The cost alone is astronomical and would be far better spent were it invested in education and health care for all people living in Ontario. Instead, the number of years spent on the tunnel construction, the increase in traffic once completed, and the devastating effects on our environment could all be avoided by encouraging travel on Highway 407. The reimbursement of the tolls paid by these 407 motorists (to a private consortium rather than to the government, thanks to a former Conservative government sell-off) could be used to offset their provincial taxes which may, in fact, prove to be a lower expenditure than Ford's latest inane solution to ease traffic congestion that, in the long run, would only cause the present problem to be exacerbated.
Barbara Saul, Toronto
Neveah tragedy holds lessons
Before the girl was found in a Rosedale dumpster, Toronto Children's Aid had closed her case. Turns out, they had a history of closing cases too early, Sept. 29
Kudos to the Star for once again breaking open a story that is of dire consequence to individuals in our society and to society overall when social needs are not met leading to consequences including crime, substance abuse, ill health, violence and sometimes death. Neveah Tucker (body found in dumpster) was just the tip of the iceberg when it comes to the results of underfunding children's aid societies.
Brian O'Sullivan, Stouffville
Puffin festival celebrated
since 1874
In Iceland, doing Puffin stuff, celebrating a truly puffling experience, Sept. 28
The very interesting report on the Puffin Festival in Iceland brought back some happy memories as I worked in a fish processing factory on Heimaey (the largest of the Westmann Islands) for two summers while at university in the early 1960s. In those days there was no fast ferry, so getting to Heimaey usually involved a very scary flight in a tiny plane that landed on a runway that was probably too short, but as it ran from one side of the island to the other, there was little choice. On one trip, the pilot touched down several times before the plane finally managed to land safely, within about five feet of a cliff edge. The local population had told us about the "National Festival" which has been celebrated on the first Monday in August since 1874 - when the millennium of Icelandic settlement was celebrated. In the 1960s, it was obviously much more of a local festival than it is today, and we heard about and saw the pufflings mentioned in the article. However, in those days one of the culinary highlights of the festival was "roast puffling on a stick." Yes, I tried it (once). It tasted very fishy. It's good to hear that they are now more carefully conserved.
David S. Crawford, Toronto
Carbon pricing plan is not a tax
In this almost excellent article by Andrew Phillips regarding the carbon conundrum, I fear he has caused a measure of misunderstanding. Throughout the piece, he too often uses the term "carbon tax," rather than "carbon pricing." For the slow among us, this creates bewilderment. What needs to be explained, in big bold print, is that the carbon pricing plan is not a tax, in any way, shape or form.
Jack Drury, Toronto
Disability parking permit not honoured
As a disabled person I feel very fortunate to have a disability parking permit when parking in Toronto. However, on a recent visit to Humber Hospital where I parked in an area with a disability parking spot, I was shocked to find out that disability permits are not honoured. I received a $70 parking ticket. There were no signs stating that disability permits were not acceptable. Using my walker to pay and then returning to my car to put the payment voucher in my windshield is not easy, especially when the payment machine is not located close to the disability parking area. It's inconceivable that a place dealing with health issues would not allow disability permits. When I called to complain, I was told that hospitals are private property. Am I wrong to think that hospitals are public property funded by taxpayers and shouldn't hospitals provide free parking since they fall under the umbrella of health care? Hopefully this issue will be investigated and disabled patients not penalized.
Mari Carlo, Toronto

Full text: A Sept. 28 article about Premier Doug Ford and Highway 407 incorrectly stated that his government never considered buying the highway before announcing the 401 tunnel proposal. In fact, the government had previously thought about buying back the 407.
A Sept. 28 opinion article about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's carbon pricing plan incorrectly stated that his government suspended the carbon price on heating oil in Atlantic Canada. In fact, the change was implemented across the country. In addition, Gerald Butts is vice-chair at Eurasia Group, not vice-president, as the article said.

TDSB cancels Truth and Reconciliation Day field trips to Nathan Phillips Square citing 'abundance of caution'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tdsb-cancels-truth-reconciliation-day-field-trips/docview/3111696437/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TDSB+cancels+Truth+and+Reconciliation+Day+field+trips+to+Nathan+Phillips+Square+citing+%27abundance+of+caution%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Toronto's public school board says it made the "difficult" decision to cancel field trips to a ceremony marking a new monument to honour residential school survivors because of the possibility it could turn into a political protest.
About six schools called off their planned trip to the Indigenous Legacy Gathering on Monday, the National Day for Truth and Reconciliation. At least one of those schools focuses on Indigenous studies.
The Toronto District School Board says it made the move "out of an abundance of caution" because roughly two weeks ago students attended a rally about Indigenous rights that attracted pro-Palestinian protesters.
In a statement to the Star, TDSB spokesperson Ryan Bird said the board-wide decision to cancel the trips Monday "was a difficult one."
"Staff was very concerned that this event taking place at Nathan Phillips Square, although highly educational and in line with our commitment to Truth and Reconciliation, posed a potential risk," he said by email.
The concern, he noted, was that staff and organizers "could not provide assurance" that the event would not become a site for a political protest, march or rally.
The province is currently investigating the board for the rally students attended mid-September and until that probe is complete the TDSB has cancelled field trips that could involve a protest, march or rally.
One teacher, who asked not to be identified because she was not authorized to speak, said she was "devastated" about having to cancel the trip, which marked the official opening of the Spirit Garden to honour residential school survivors. (It's in response to the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's Call to Action 82, which calls on each capital city in Canada to install a residential school monument.)
"To not be able to bring children, both Indigenous and non-Indigenous, down for this really, really important day is really upsetting to me," she told the Star. "It's some sort of strange reaction to the Grassy Narrows" event.
"It seems like (the TDSB) is very worried about anything that might be political, but by its nature reconciliation is political."
On Sept. 18, students from about 15 schools attended the Grassy Narrows River Run, billed as a community event to support the Grassy Narrows First Nation and their efforts to address mercury contamination. It started downtown at Grange Park, then demonstrators marched to Queen's Park.
Parents were told it was an "educational opportunity" and that students wouldn't participate in the rally. But many students and teachers joined the solidarity march for Indigenous rights, which attracted thousands. Controversy erupted when videos posted on social media showed some students and staff alongside pro-Palestinian protesters, and joining in chats, such as "From Turtle Island to Palestine, Occupation is a Crime."
Some parents cited concerns about students being exposed to antisemitic chants, and the board apologized "for the harm that some students may have experienced." However, some community members have called the apology an example of anti-Palestinian racism.
The Ministry of Education has ordered a provincial investigation into the Sept. 18 trip. Premier Doug Ford has criticized the TDSB, saying it must be "held accountable" for taking kids to a rally intended to be about Indigenous rights but that included pro-Palestinian protesters, calling it "disgraceful."
In a statement, the ministry said it wasn't involved in the decision to cancel Monday's trips and directed all inquiries to the TDSB.
Monday's event attracted politicians, student groups and families, with events spread across the square showcasing traditional Indigenous speakers, singers and dancers while unveiling the new addition of the Spirit Garden to the public.
Sources said they saw students and staff from at least two TDSB schools. Asked about this, the TDSB replied that last week, direction was given to schools to let their superintendent know if they had plans to attend, and all trips that they were made aware of were cancelled.
More than 100 residential school survivors were present; some spoke about how much it meant to see so many children there.
Jay Young, a parent of an 11-year-old student at D. A. Morrison Middle School, said his daughter's field trip to the event had been in the works for several weeks. Last Friday, a PA day, he said he received an email from the principal announcing the cancellation of the trip, "out of an abundance of caution." The Star has not seen a copy of the email.
Young said he was disappointed his daughter wouldn't hear from Indigenous voices on the National Day of Truth and Reconciliation, and would have taken her out of school himself if he did not have a previous commitment. He said the possibility of protests exists at any public event, and would become a teaching moment for his child, he said.
It's a day of sorrow, where unfortunate truths need to be communicated, he said.
"I think people really need to give children credit, even children who are younger than my daughter, that they understand and that they can learn."
In an interview, a TDSB teacher said Monday's event would have been key for students of all backgrounds to attend "because there are so few Indigenous teachers in the board."
"Teachers rely on field trips to have authentic, decolonial education," said the teacher, who asked not to be identified because they weren't authorized to speak.
"In particular, any Indigenous student in the TDSB would have benefited from being able to attend."
The event, however, did attract many community members and school groups.
Teacher Vikkie Fung of YMCA Academy, which isn't part of the TDSB, said it was important to take her students so they could "learn more about truth and reconciliation, about our history, about our heritage.
"We always have to reflect on the past to build the future."
The TDSB says all of its 582 schools marked the day in multiple ways, including assemblies, activities and wearing orange.

Should September 30th be a statutory holiday in Ontario?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/should-september-30th-be-statutory-holiday/docview/3111694327/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Should+September+30th+be+a+statutory+holiday+in+Ontario%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While the National Day for Truth and Reconciliation has been a federal statutory day since 2021, most workers and students in Ontario don't get the day off. But an Ontario MPP is trying to change that.
Deputy Leader of Ontario NDP Sol Mamakwa, the only First Nation representative at Queen's Park, is planning proposed legislation to have Sept. 30 declared as a paid provincial holiday. Mamakwa said he wants not just Indigenous people, but also other Ontarians to have the day off to reflect, mourn and learn of the history of residential schools.
There are precedents in other provinces that the MPP may be looking at. If the legislation is passed, Ontario will be the fifth province to observe the day as a provincial holiday.
Here's what you need to know about how other parts of Canada are observing.
Who has Sept. 30 off in Ontario?
Because Truth and Reconciliation Day is a federal holiday, it applies to employees in federally regulated industries across Canada such as Canada Post, banks and air transportation - but not to provincially regulated industries.
Ontario employers may choose to give staff the day off and some of them are required to do so by a collective agreement. For example, Sept. 30 is deemed as a holiday for Ontario Public Service employees in their collective agreement after all five unions filed formal policy disputes with the employer in 2022. The arbitrator found in favour of the bargaining agents.
Ontario's Courts limit operations on this day, closing all counter services, only operating Weekend and Statutory Holiday courts and hearing urgent matters in the Ontario Superior Court of Justice.
Which province gets Sept. 30 off?
British Columbia, Manitoba, Prince Edward Island, New Brunswick, Yukon and Northwest Territories are the only six jurisdictions where Truth and Reconciliation Day is a paid holiday.
B.C. workers are able to observe Sept. 30 with a paid day off or receive payment at premium rates if required to work since the province passed legislation in 2023.
While Nova Scotia does not officially recognize this day as a holiday, provincial government offices, public schools, and regulated child care are closed on Truth and Reconciliation Day.
Should Sept. 30 be a paid holiday in Ontario?
Mamakwa, a residential school survivor, plans to introduce a private member's bill in November to push for a provincial holiday and discuss it in the legislature in Anishininiimowin, known in English as Oji-Cree.
Greg Rickford, Ontario's minister of Indigenous affairs, said he was "not entirely persuaded" and more consultation with the Indigenous community is needed.
The minister said some First Nations leaders have said a statutory holiday is not the appropriate approach at this time, rather than educating about the legacy of residential schools and working to bring home children who did not return.
Jonathan Hamilton-Diabo, an associate professor at Victoria University with expertise in the history of residential schools and a Mohawk from Kahnawake, supports the idea of making Truth and Reconciliation Day a provincial holiday in Ontario.
"I think it could be a good way of encouraging all people to be involved. If it does result that way. Sometimes what happens is when we give a holiday, then people literally take the holiday," said Hamilton-Diabo.
He added he appreciated the proposal came from a residential school survivor and the Ontario government should do a thorough consultation with Indigenous people, leaders, communities and survivors.
"That's essential for them to hear the different perspectives on this because I don't believe it's going to be uniform."
Hamilton-Diabo said more emphasis needs to be put on developing strategies so people want to participate in reconciliation, rather than being taught to do it.
Annie Korver, the founder of Rise Consulting, an Indigenous-owned firm that helps companies develop reconciliation action plans, also agrees that Sept. 30 should be a provincial statutory holiday, but she said people "have the opportunity to move away from the word 'holiday.'"
"Today is not a holiday, today is a day for purposeful reflection on Truth," said Korver in a statement.
Korver added, that if setting aside an entire day is too big of a step, companies can share teachings or plan activities for team members to participate in.
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: People march during the "Every Child Matters: A Day of Action for Truth and Reconciliation" rally in Montreal, Canada on Monday. Across Canada, September 30 is the National Day for Truth and Reconciliation, a time to commemorate the victims of the residential school system for Indigenous kids, also known as Orange Shirt Day. The last residential school shuttered in 1996.

Underfunded fire services on reserves + Ontario's escalating crisis in child services

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/underfunded-fire-services-on-reserves-ontarios/docview/3111188659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Underfunded+fire+services+on+reserves+%2B+Ontario%27s+escalating+crisis+in+child+services&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, September 30 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Today is National Day for Truth and Reconciliation; check out what's open and closed in the Six.
Here's the latest on underfunded fire services on reserves, the escalating crisis in child services and Ontario's auditor general speaking out on the girl found in a dumpster.
DON'T MISSIndigenous people are perishing in fires. A new lawsuit demands change
The underfunding of fire protection services on Indigenous reserves has resulted in residents dying in blazes at far higher rates than the rest of Canada, a new lawsuit from two Ontario First Nations alleges. It also claims the government has been aware of the issue for the last two decades and has done "nothing to address it." Take a look at what needs to change.
By the numbers: A recent report found 20 per cent of 700 Canadian fire deaths in the last decade were First Nations, Métis or Inuit - four times greater than the proportion of Indigenous people in Canada.Wait, what? To date, the government invested nearly $31 million into fire protection on reserves. But after interviewing a dozen First Nations fire chiefs, many say they see no evidence of the investment - and that they were not consulted on what they need.There's a plan to help kids in crisis - but nothing gets done
A roundtable of experts, child welfare leaders and parents met in 2021 to concoct a plan to revolutionize Ontario's health and social services system - one that has too-often left vulnerable children in the shadows. Two and a half years later, that plan has collected dust while the provincial government twiddled its thumbs and watched the crisis worsen, Amy Dempsey Raven and Megan Ogilvie report. What's it waiting for?
A possible solution: The roundtable devised 32 recommendations to help pull families out of crisis and bring cohesion to a fragmented system. But they've been ghosted by the province and none of their recommendations have been implemented.An unfolding crisis: Systems are crumbling, kids are facing years-long waitlists for treatment, and scores of children are living out of hotels and office buildings while under the care of Ontario child welfare agencies.Neveah's death laid bare the cracks in Ontario's child welfare system
Toronto's children's aid society had a documented history of closing child-protection cases too early, Ontario's Auditor General revealed in an exclusive interview with the Star. Nearly a decade since seven child-welfare agencies were audited and in the wake of a four-year-old girl being found dead in a Rosedale dumpster in 2022, Shelley Spence says it may be time for a new probe. Here's what Spence says about the state of the system.
A lingering problem: In a 2015 audit of seven children's aid societies, Spence's predecessor found the agencies "may be closing cases prematurely, risking the well-being of children." Nearly half of their cases were closed, despite ongoing child safety concerns.Down the line: CAS Toronto did not act on its premature case closures until 2022. Even then, they implemented just 57 per cent of the audit's recommendations: "Not what we would call a passing grade at school," Spence said.WHAT ELSE
Residential school survivors are still fighting for access to records. They can't heal until they know.It's high time a reckoning came for the people and systems enabling those accused of sexual assault.Health care for Indigenous people in Canada is failing. It's time to give the power back.How we can change a system that puts too many Indigenous people in jail.A third NDP MPP is hoping to swap Queen's Park for Ottawa in the next federal election.A woman is dead after a stabbing in a Scarborough home on Sunday.Donald Trump escalated his attacks on Kamala Harris' mental fitness and suggested she should be prosecuted.Joe Biden says he'll speak with Israel's prime minister, saying an all-out war in the Middle East must be avoided.Is it perimenopause or do I just want to get a divorce? Experts break down its most confusing symptoms.Beloved singer-songwriter and actor Kris Kristofferson has died at 88.Don't miss this week's top Toronto culture events, from a Q&A with Guillermo del Toro to this bizarre and brilliant comedy show.As cars turn into computers and repair costs rise, here's how to beat the system and fix your ride for less.POV
When I heard my grandparents' Indigenous language on the radio, it felt like home. Here's what stopped me from learning it myself.CLOSE-UP
FORT ERIE: The basketball program at Fort Erie International Academy has produced one NBA player and 13 NCAA Division 1 players in just three years - including Ishan Sharma, who is set to hit the court at the University of Virginia this fall. Libaan Osman chronicles how this tiny Ontario town of 30,000 became an unlikely hotspot for Canada's rising basketball stars.
Thank you for reading First Up. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca
Sign up here to get this newsletter in your inbox.

Chief Randall Phillips said home fires and inadequate housing are an endemic problem on his and many other indigenous communities.

Ontario had a plan to solve the growing kids crisis. It sat on the shelf for more than two years

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-had-plan-solve-growing-kids-crisis-sat-on/docview/3111069628/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+had+a+plan+to+solve+the+growing+kids+crisis.+It+sat+on+the+shelf+for+more+than+two+years&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was supposed to be the plan that finally made a difference.
At least, that's what Karen Wilson believed when she agreed to join a group of experts, child welfare leaders and parents Doug Ford's government asked to find ways to improve treatment and care for children with complex mental health needs.
This was in 2021, when healthcare leaders were sounding the alarm about a youth mental health crisis coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic.
To Wilson, a parent and child advocate, it seemed like the provincial government was finally ready to take action - to transform a health and social services system that had long left Ontario's most vulnerable children in the shadows.
"This wasn't going to be another report that sat collecting dust on a shelf," Wilson said. "That's what I thought."
Two-and-a-half years after the roundtable completed its plan to address a systemwide crisis, the province has not acted - and the situation has gotten worse.
Kids are facing years-long waitlists for treatment. Exhausted families are being told their child is "too high needs" for specialized care. Scores of vulnerable children with complex needs are living out of hotels and office buildings while under the care of Ontario child welfare agencies. And hundreds of Ontario parents have been forced to relinquish care of their kids to children's aid societies because they've been unable to get help.
The Star has obtained a copy of the roundtable report, which had not previously been made public, and spoke to six former table members who confirmed the province has not been in touch for follow-up discussions or to take the lead on any of the 32 recommendations meant to help pull families out of crisis and bring cohesion to a fragmented system.
Meanwhile, several members of the roundtable who spoke to the Star say the group has received little more than a 'thank you' for their work.
In March 2022, the roundtable submitted a report that included 32 recommendations to help pull families out of crisis. In a letter introducing their findings, the leaders made it clear they expected a response.
"To remove any doubt regarding our future expectations, we, along with all collaboration table members, declare - unequivocally - that we await with great interest the response from (the ministries)," they wrote, noting they were committed to working together on the solutions.
Wilson, 55, was hopeful. When the roundtable began, she had gone to some of the struggling families she worked with in the Windsor area and urged them to share their stories.
This is our chance, she told them. "We're going to make a difference."
She herself had raised a son with mental health and developmental needs and had then become a child and youth advocate, guiding desperate families through the same challenges she'd navigated as the mother of a boy with what the province calls "complex needs." Children like her son typically have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression, and are united by the fact that Ontario's health and social services struggle to meet their needs.
The roundtable met more than a dozen times over 12 months. It felt like a good sign that representatives from two Ontario ministries - the Ministry of Health and the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) - attended the meetings. They heard parents speak about living in crisis.
"I asked these families to step up and share their stories, to bare their hearts," said Wilson. "And then literally nothing came of it."
In response to questions from the Star, the two ministries sent a statement that said the report provided them "with insight and recommendations to improve the lives of children and youth."
The statement did not respond directly to questions about which recommendations, if any, have been acted upon, but cited several recent investments in child and youth health, including a $97 million pilot program for kids with complex special needs at three children's hospitals, funding to "expand access to community-based intensive services" and 10 new complex care beds.
A plan for a growing crisisThe roundtable recognized a host of long-standing challenges.
Its final report proposed recommendations that are "balanced, achievable, and essential for meaningful change," co-chairs Patrick Dion, the former executive director of a mental health services agency, and Mark Kartusch, a child welfare leader, told the Star in a statement.
"We remain ready to support the implementation of these recommendations and are confident that our colleagues share this commitment to progress."
Some members of the roundtable who viewed the ministries' statement to the Star said that while investments are appreciated and will help children and families in some regions, the funding announcements noted by the government don't address the group's recommendations, which were meant to improve the system's overall effectiveness.
Derrick Drouillard, executive director of the Windsor-Essex Children's Aid Society and member of the 2021 roundtable, said it's notable that the report's first recommendation - to create a standing committee at the deputy ministerial level between the MCCSS and the ministries of Health and Education - has not yet been acted upon.
"Never have we been able to sit and say, at the highest ministry levels, let's sit together and unpack these recommendations," said Drouillard.
In an interview, Kartusch, now a director at an Indigenous child welfare agency in southern Ontario, acknowledged the report "has sat" and said its status is "something you'd have to ask the ministry about." He added that he remains "hopeful" the report will move forward, noting the province needs "a broad system solution, otherwise, we're just band-aiding it."
Among the recommendations: create and appropriately fund a new "Escalated Service" for young people in crisis; implement an "accelerated response mechanism" to overcome structural barriers for Indigenous and young people who face inequities; build capacity to ensure families and children get early intervention, minimizing their need for a crisis response; and boost the availability of live-in treatment options and access to respite services.
Drouillard said an "Escalated Service" - essentially a crisis response team - for each region is critical, noting that child welfare staff need to have confidence they can call a children's mental health provider or community service and quickly get a residential treatment spot for the child or teen who has come through their doors.
"We don't have that right now," he said. "We'll have a meeting and we'll hear that it's going to take three months or six months and we are just waiting and waiting and waiting."
'I'm desensitized to nothing happening'Panel member Tanya Stack was raising a 14-year-old son with autism, anxiety and other complex needs when she agreed to join the roundtable to share her experience. Stack, an Oshawa bookkeeper with two younger children, said she was disappointed but not surprised the government hasn't taken action. Parents of children with complex needs are used to wasting their time pushing for change that never comes, she said. "That's our life."
"This is just another one of the things that I put my heart and soul into, all that effort, and it goes to the back burner," she said. "I'm desensitized to nothing happening."
Stack and her spouse have endured the cycle familiar to parents of children with high needs: traumatic police calls and emergency room trips that ended with their son waiting for many hours and then being released; getting turned down for services because of his dual diagnosis or high-functioning autism; unpredictable access to respite services.
"We're doing everything that we can do to try to survive day to day," Stack said.
One year after the report was submitted to the government, Karen Wilson wrote to the ministry staff members who'd sat with her on the panel, saying she felt "sick" knowing that nothing had come of it.
"Can it really be true that not one of the recommendations has been acted upon to better the lives of families and youth in our communities ... even though we gave them the information they needed to begin the necessary changes to make things better?" she wrote.
Wilson, who works for a family support network in Windsor, said she continues to hear from parents who see no help coming and feel like they are "drowning."
"The family members are becoming physically ill, marriages are falling apart, and young people ... are continuing to spiral, taking their parents and siblings with them."
Things have gotten much worse for Ontario families since Wilson was raising her own son, Robert, who is now 28, she said. Wilson considers herself fortunate: she managed to get residential care for her child beginning at age 13 and educational supports when he was in school. "Although it was extremely challenging parenting a complex child with disabilities, we didn't face the same horrible journey that the families coming up behind us are."
In 2005, Wilson was told that the only way to get appropriate treatment for her then-nine-year-old child was to give him up to CAS under a temporary care agreement. Appalled, she phoned the office of the provincial minister. Soon after, the government, then led by Dalton McGuinty's Liberal Party, made a public promise to stop putting families in that position.
"No family in this province, in this day and age, should have to consider giving up their children to get the help" they need, then-minister Sandra Pupatello, said in the legislature.
But that's exactly what families are now faced with, Wilson said.
"The support these kids need is not available. It doesn't exist today," Wilson said. "Or if it does, it's hidden."
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca and mogilvie@thestar.ca
CAPTION: Karen Wilson holds a photo of her son, Robert, at age two. Wilson says she was thrilled to sit on a roundtable aimed to address the growing crisis facing Ontario families of kids with complex needs.

National Day for Truth and Reconciliation is a day for teaching

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/national-day-truth-reconciliation-is-teaching/docview/3111069596/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=National+Day+for+Truth+and+Reconciliation+is+a+day+for+teaching&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Where truth and reconciliation begins
The National Day for Truth and Reconciliation was established as an annual opportunity for all Canadians to explore and learn the early history of our country that was concealed from us. As a fifth generation descendant of an early settler family who chose this country for a better life and as a retired teacher/principal, I am doubly annoyed at all levels of government under which I have lived for the past 80 years who have furthered this concealment. We, teachers included, did not know about it or deliberately overlooked it. It was never included in our provincial curriculum and obviously, we did not teach it. Shameful! However, the ice is slowly breaking and we must seize the opportunity our government is now providing on Sept. 30 to be more informed. In Burnt River, Ont., one can take a motor trip within our local community to Somerville Centennial Park on the banks of the beautiful river to see the land acknowledgment plaque honouring the First Nations peoples who originally inhabited this area. Read Anishinaabe author, journalist and lecturer Tanya Talaga's new book, "The Knowing," which brings to life her research and discovery of what actually happened to her great-great-grandmother who mysteriously disappeared. It reveals the disturbing, colonial bigotry of the business, political and religious elite of the day. You can also tune in to an upcoming new series on the "The Knowing" being aired on CBC Gem. Seize the opportunity to be more informed. That is where truth and reconciliation actually begin.
Hugh Armstrong, Burnt River, Ont.
The challenge, or not, of who to vote for
I've voted Liberal my entire life. Trudeau has made that impossible now, Sept. 26
The article expresses my feelings exactly. I am so disappointed with our federal government and confused as to who I can vote for. There is no clear alternative. Not one of the federal parties offers convincing solutions to Canada's issues. Each of them inspires a non-confidence motion and all inspire an incompetence motion.
Brian Mellor, Midland, Ont.
If retired journalist Janice Kennedy is going to base her voting decision on the way a leader smiles, his speech tics or his socks, those of us who care about policies would prefer she stay away on voting day. Independent research has shown that the Trudeau government has fulfilled more than 90 per cent of its promises. Perhaps not the ones Kennedy cared most about, but still, it's a very good record, especially considering the pandemic, wars and the obstructionism of Conservative premiers determined to undermine initiatives like $10-a-day daycare.
Patricia Wilmot, Toronto
Who are we supposed to vote for in the next federal election? Of the two viable parties, Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau refuses to resign so the party can take new directions. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is not a leader, he's an attack dog career politician and his policies are not palatable. Climate change is real and too important of an issue to be used for political purposes as Poilievre does. The focus should be on renewable energy, not attacking the program in place. Besides, I like the deposits in my bank account. Also, going after CTV politically is the wrong way for Poilievre to address his grievance. Political attacks on media are an attack on democracy. The foul wind has blown north in Canada's politics.
C.E. McCaig, Kitchener, Ont.
It seems the writer and I have a lot in common. I have also voted Liberal federally my entire life. I too cast my first vote for Pierre Trudeau, who was my MP at the time. With respect, I'm not on board with the "anti-Trudeau" bandwagon so common in the media. Amidst all the noise, I think we lose track of some to the accomplishments of his tenure in office. Whether Justin Trudeau should stay or go is a different issue altogether. I for one think that 10 years of one prime minister is about all Canadians can stomach. But I have no doubt which party I will vote for in the next election. Voting for Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives is a hard no.
Gary Moriarty, Stephenville Crossing, Nfld.
Basic math is a struggle
EQAO test results show TDSB students struggling in math and literacy, Sept. 26
Recently I made a retail purchase and the total price came to $171.58. I gave the cashier $200. After she rang it in, I told her I would give her change, fished $1.60 out from my pocket and gave it to her. She held the change in one hand and the two hundred dollar bills in the other, and looked at me with this really puzzled look. She didn't have a clue. I told her to give me $30 back, but still no clue. I told her it was simple high school math. "Well, I am in high school and I didn't take math yet," she replied. This is not an isolated case. It happens frequently when I try to provide exact change.
Paul Leduc, Fenelon Falls, Ont.
'Journalists are vital'
In today's world, journalists need to double down on the truth, Sept. 27
More than ever journalists are needed to convey truth. Politicians like Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speak in Trump-like clichés about the government being "deep state" and only offer caustic taunts and insults. Premier Doug Ford has recently insulted the homeless with callous remarks. Neither politician serves democracy. Poilievre has threatened the CBC and CTV, which sets a dangerous precedent regarding respecting the institutions that hold government to account. Our provincial legislature is not even in session until Oct. 21, which does not afford the Opposition an occasion to challenge the government on its policies. The government wants to control the message and avert any discussion or criticism. This does not bode well for democracy. Journalists are vital in their role in seeking the truth amidst the bluster.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
A plea to parents
My husband is a school bus driver. Every September, he and his fellow drivers try to get your children to stop standing, jumping, yelling and screaming on the bus so that he can drive safely without being distracted. And every September parents complain to the school board about buses. That is not the solution. Instead, please teach your children the rules of riding a school bus. We all want our loved ones to arrive home safely at the end of the day.
Carole O'Reilly, Mississauga
CAPTION: Author and documentary filmmaker Tanya Talaga reconnects with her cousin, Victor Chapais, in "The Knowing."
