Liberals roll out details on plan to fight hate: Strategy includes $65 million in funding to help religious centres install cameras, hire security guards

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-roll-out-details-on-plan-fight-hate/docview/3111069323/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+roll+out+details+on+plan+to+fight+hate%3A+Strategy+includes+%2465+million+in+funding+to+help+religious+centres+install+cameras%2C+hire+security+guards&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberal government is announcing more details on its existing strategy and funding to fight a rise in hate crimes targeting multiple communities.
Diversity Minister Kamal Khera released the Action Plan on Combatting Hate last week, which aims to co-ordinate how various departments promote diversity and prevent violent incidents and speech online targeting minorities.
Khera said the funding is needed because Statistics Canada is reporting an increase in hate crimes involving Jews, Muslims, LGBTQ people and other communities.
"Whether it is online or on our streets, hateful words (and) actions are having a devastating impact on our communities and our entire country, whether it is our mosques being attacked, communities being divided or even losing loved ones," she said outside a mosque in Brampton.
She noted the arrest last week of a London, Ont., man whom police say had verbally harassed a woman wearing an Islamic headscarf and brandished a knife.
A self-described white nationalist in that same southwestern Ontario city murdered four members of a Muslim family in 2021 in what a judge ruled to be an act of terrorism.
"We cannot allow hate to go unchecked; the cost of inaction is far too great," Khera said.
The action plan released last Tuesday details how Ottawa intends to spend the $273.6 million the Liberals allocated in this April's budget for various programs, over the course of six years.
Khera said that allocation includes a $65 million top-up to a fund that helps community institutions and religious centres cover the cost of installing cameras or hiring security guards.
She noted Ottawa has increased the annual amount of cash institutions can apply for, such as those who feel it's necessary to get round-the-clock security.
The Centre for Israel and Jewish Affairs said the extra cash for security is "very welcome as many institutions have been stretched beyond capacity," noting Jewish schools have been shot at, synagogues have been vandalized and Jewish businesses have been set ablaze.
Tuesday's action plan largely reiterates work that federal departments and agencies are already doing, with the idea of creating consultation panels that can spot gaps in laws and programs or address barriers to implementing an existing Anti-Racism Strategy. It listed various programs helping anti-racism organizations to monitor and combat online hatred, as well as training for Crown prosecutors on "the unique dynamics of hate crimes."
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Dylan Robertson The Canadian Press

Document 178 of 500

Inaction harming Ontario's vulnerable

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/inaction-harming-ontarios-vulnerable/docview/3111069316/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inaction+harming+Ontario%27s+vulnerable&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: October 1 could have been a celebratory day for Ontarians concerned with the struggles of low-income residents. After all, it's the day the minimum wage will nudge up - following behind inflation, sure - but up nonetheless. But if you're living on Ontario Works, or what used to be called welfare, the date will carry a more bitter significance. It will have been a full six years since any increase in the amounts for basic needs and shelter.
In that time, inflation has worked its ugly powers and the price of everything has gone up. There has been a 20 per cent hike in the cost of living for the typical Ontario family, hitting the basics like food and housing especially hard.
If you happen to be surviving on an Ontario Works (OW) income, you've felt that sting deeper than most. The cost of living at the provincial poverty line has gone up 22 per cent. This isn't just inflation doing its usual damage. No, the source of the pain runs deeper, rooted in a 1995 decision when Mike Harris slashed welfare by 22 per cent. Now Doug Ford has done the same, just more slowly and quietly. No need for a flashy cut when the grind of inaction does the job for you.
By freezing Ontario Works at its 2018 levels, Ford's government has delivered a stealthier, yet no less painful blow. A single recipient in Toronto is now $435 further below the poverty line than they were six years ago. You don't need to squint to see the impact: Food insecurity has shot up and food bank usage is skyrocketing. Homelessness and pressures on an overburdened shelter system continue to mount. Since 2018, social assistance indexation - increasing OW benefits to increase along with price inflation - would have meant a combined basic needs and shelter allowance of $882 a month. This would be $149 more than the current amounts - money that would surely make a big difference in the lives of many of the poorest people in the province.
The real kicker is that this didn't need to happen. Before Ford, the Liberal governments never found the resolve to restore the original cuts to OW, but they did tie increases to inflation, the kind of basic economic policy that, like progressive income taxation, everyone agrees on.
The province, however, has ignored Ontario Works, while continuing to index many other things such as child benefits, income taxes and the minimum wage, for example. It also increased Ontario Disability Support Program amounts in 2022 and commenced indexing in 2023. The Guaranteed Annual Income System - a program aimed at low-income seniors - started indexing in 2023. These are positive moves that ensure that some of the poorest didn't slide backwards with every price hike.
Yet here we are with OW recipients suffering under a rising cost of living while the government watches from a distance.
As if that weren't enough, the rules around working while on Ontario Works haven't changed either. You can earn up to $200 a month before the benefits start getting clawed back. But that $200 has been frozen since 2013. Back then, that threshold got you nearly 20 hours of work in a month at minimum wage. After Oct. 1, one can only work 12 hours before exceeding the limit. If that amount had risen with the times, it would be $332 now - a change that would let people work more and live better without losing benefits. But again, nothing moves, nothing adjusts, and the so-called incentives to work become more inadequate every year.
What we're left with is a situation in which life is harder for the poorest citizens as the province cuts their safety net inch by inch.
The Ford government can still change course. The province has recently come around to indexation for low-income seniors and people with disabilities. It is time to extend this good policy to Ontario Works recipients and give those struggling Ontarians a fighting chance.
Brian Lewis is a Senior Fellow with the CD Howe Institute and Munk School for Global Affairs and Public Policy. He is the former provincial Chief Economist.
CAPTION: Ontario has recently come around to indexation for low-income seniors and people with disabilities. It's time to extend this to Ontario Works recipients.Paige Taylor WhiteToronto Star
CREDIT: Brian Lewis Contributor Brian Lewis is a Senior Fellow with the CD Howe Institute and Munk School for Global Affairs and Public Policy. He is the former provincial Chief Economist.;

Document 179 of 500

Doug Ford's inaction has left Ontario's most vulnerable without a fighting chance

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-inaction-has-left-ontarios-most/docview/3111069296/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+inaction+has+left+Ontario%27s+most+vulnerable+without+a+fighting+chance&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: October 1 could have been a celebratory day for Ontarians concerned with the struggles of low-income residents. After all, it's the day the minimum wage will nudge up - following behind inflation, sure - but up nonetheless. But if you're living on Ontario Works, or what used to be called welfare, the date will carry a more bitter significance. It will have been a full six years since any increase in the amounts for basic needs and shelter.
In that time, inflation has worked its ugly powers and the price of everything has gone up. There has been a 20-per-cent hike in the cost of living for the typical Ontario family, hitting the basics like food and housing especially hard.
If you happen to be surviving on an Ontario Works (OW) income, you've felt that sting deeper than most. The cost of living at the provincial poverty line has gone up 22 per cent. You don't need to squint to see the impact: Food insecurity has shot up and food bank usage is skyrocketing. Homelessness and pressures on an overburdened shelter system continue to mount. Since 2018, social assistance indexation - increasing OW benefits to increase along with price inflation - would have meant a combined basic needs and shelter allowance of $882 a month. This would be $149 more than the current amounts - money that would surely make a big difference in the lives of many of the poorest people in the province.
The real kicker is that this didn't need to happen. Before Ford, the Liberal governments never found the resolve to restore the original cuts to OW, but they did tie increases to inflation, the kind of basic economic policy that, like progressive income taxation, everyone agrees on.

It also increased Ontario Disability Support Program amounts in 2022 and commenced indexing in 2023. The Guaranteed Annual Income System - a program aimed at low-income seniors - started indexing in 2023. These are positive moves that ensure that some of the poorest didn't slide backwards with every price hike.
Yet here we are with OW recipients suffering under a rising cost of living while the government watches from a distance.
As if that weren't enough, the rules around working while on Ontario Works haven't changed either. You can earn up to $200 a month before the benefits start getting clawed back. But that $200 has been frozen since 2013. Back then, that threshold got you nearly 20 hours of work in a month at minimum wage. After October 1, one can only work 12 hours before exceeding the limit. If that amount had risen with the times, it would be $332 now - a change that would let people work more and live better without losing benefits. But again, nothing moves, nothing adjusts, and the so-called incentives to work become more inadequate every year.
What we're left with is a situation in which life that is harder for the poorest citizens as the province cuts their safety net inch by inch.
The Ford government can still change course. The province has recently come around to indexation for low-income seniors and people with disabilities. It is time to extend this good policy to Ontario Works recipients and give those struggling Ontarians a fighting chance.
CAPTION: People line up outside Allan Garden during a time of record inflation.
CREDIT: Brian Lewis, Contributor

Letters: I've voted Liberal my entire life. Trudeau has made that impossible now, Sept. 26 EQAO test results show TDSB students struggling in math and literacy, Sept. 26 In today's world, journalists need to double down on the truth, Sept. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3111069208/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+I%27ve+voted+Liberal+my+entire+life.+Trudeau+has+made+that+impossible+now%2C+Sept.+26+EQAO+test+results+show+TDSB+students+struggling+in+math+and+literacy%2C+Sept.+26+In+today%27s+world%2C+journalists+need+to+double+down+on+the+truth%2C+Sept.+27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Where truth and reconciliation actually begins
The National Day for Truth and Reconciliation was established as an annual opportunity for all Canadians to explore and learn the early history of our country that was concealed from us. As a fifth-generation descendant of an early settler family who chose this country for a better life and as a retired teacher/principal, I am doubly annoyed at all levels of government under which I have lived for the past 80 years who have furthered this concealment.
We, teachers included, did not know about it or deliberately overlooked it. It was never included in our provincial curriculum and obviously, we did not teach it. Shameful! However, the ice is slowly breaking and we must seize the opportunity our government is now providing on Sept. 30 to be more informed. In Burnt River, Ont., one can take a motor trip within our local community to Somerville Centennial Park on the banks of the beautiful river to see the land acknowledgment plaque honouring the First Nations peoples who originally inhabited this area.
Read Anishinaabe author, journalist and lecturer Tanya Talaga's new book, "The Knowing," which brings to life her research and discovery of what actually happened to her great-great-grandmother who mysteriously disappeared. It reveals the disturbing, colonial bigotry of the business, political and religious elite of the day.
You can also tune in to an upcoming new series on the "The Knowing" being aired on CBC Gem. Seize the opportunity to be more informed. That is where truth and reconciliation actually begin.
Hugh Armstrong, Burnt River, Ont.
The challenge,
or not, of who
to vote for
I've voted Liberal my entire life. Trudeau has made that impossible now, Sept. 26
The article expresses my feelings exactly. I am so disappointed with our federal government and confused as to who I can vote for. There is no clear alternative. Not one of the federal parties offers convincing solutions to Canada's issues. Each of them inspires a non-confidence motion and all inspire an incompetence motion.
Brian Mellor, Midland, Ont.
If retired journalist Janice Kennedy is going to base her voting decision on the way a leader smiles, his speech tics or his socks, those of us who care about policies would prefer she stay away on voting day. Independent research has shown that the Trudeau government has fulfilled more than 90 per cent of its promises. Perhaps not the ones Kennedy cared most about, but still, it's a very good record, especially considering the pandemic, wars and the obstructionism of Conservative premiers determined to undermine initiatives like $10-a-day daycare.
Patricia Wilmot, Toronto
Who are we supposed to vote for in the next federal election? Of the two viable parties, Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau refuses to resign so the party can take new directions. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is not a leader, he's an attack dog career politician and his policies are not palatable. Climate change is real and too important of an issue to be used for political purposes as Poilievre does. The focus should be on renewable energy, not attacking the program in place. Besides, I like the deposits in my bank account. Also, going after CTV politically is the wrong way for Poilievre to address his grievance. Political attacks on media are an attack on democracy. The foul wind has blown north in Canada's politics.
C.E. McCaig, Kitchener, Ont.
It seems the writer and I have a lot in common. I have also voted Liberal federally my entire life. I too cast my first vote for Pierre Trudeau, who was my MP at the time. With respect, I'm not on board with the "anti-Trudeau" bandwagon so common in the media.
Amidst all the noise, I think we lose track of some to the accomplishments of his tenure in office. Whether Justin Trudeau should stay or go is a different issue altogether. I for one think that 10 years of one prime minister is about all Canadians can stomach. But I have no doubt which party I will vote for in the next election. Voting for Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives is a hard no.
Gary Moriarty, Stephenville Crossing, Nfld.
High school math can be a struggle in world of retail
EQAO test results show TDSB students struggling in math and literacy, Sept. 26
Recently I made a retail purchase and the total price came to $171.58. I gave the cashier $200. After she rang it in, I told her I would give her change, fished $1.60 out from my pocket and gave it to her. She held the change in one hand and the two $100 bills in the other, and looked at me with this really puzzled look.
She didn't have a clue. I told her to give me $30 back, but still no clue. I told her it was simple high school math. "Well, I am in high school and I didn't take math yet," she replied.
This is not an isolated case. It happens frequently when I try to provide exact change.
Paul Leduc, Fenelon Falls, Ont.
Journalists play
a vital role in pursuit of truth
In today's world, journalists need to double down on the truth, Sept. 27
More than ever journalists are needed to convey truth. Politicians like Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speak in Trump-like clichés about the government being "deep state" and only offer caustic taunts and insults.
Premier Doug Ford has recently insulted the homeless with callous remarks. Neither politician serves democracy. Poilievre has threatened the CBC and CTV, which sets a dangerous precedent regarding respecting the institutions that hold government to account.
Our provincial legislature is not even in session until Oct. 21, which does not afford the Opposition an occasion to challenge the government on its policies.
The government wants to control the message and avert any discussion or criticism. This does not bode well for democracy. Journalists are vital in their role in seeking the truth amidst the bluster.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
Parents must teach kids about bus decorum
My husband is a school bus driver. Every September, he and his fellow drivers try to get your children to stop standing, jumping, yelling and screaming on the bus so that he can drive safely without being distracted. And every September, parents complain to the school board about buses. That is not the solution. Instead, please teach your children the rules of riding a school bus. We all want our loved ones to arrive home safely at the end of the day.
Carole O'Reilly, Mississauga

Document 181 of 500

Blueprint to address kids' needs left sitting on shelf: Province fails to act on plan to improve treatment and care, advocates say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/blueprint-address-kids-needs-left-sitting-on/docview/3111069205/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Blueprint+to+address+kids%27+needs+left+sitting+on+shelf%3A+Province+fails+to+act+on+plan+to+improve+treatment+and+care%2C+advocates+say&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was supposed to be the plan that finally made a difference.
At least, that's what Karen Wilson believed when she agreed to join a group of experts, child welfare leaders and parents Doug Ford's government asked to find ways to improve treatment and care for children with complex mental-health needs.
This was in 2021, when health-care leaders were sounding the alarm about a youth mental-health crisis coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic.
To Wilson, a parent and child advocate, it seemed like the provincial government was finally ready to take action - to transform a health and social services system that had long left Ontario's most vulnerable children in the shadows.
"This wasn't going to be another report that sat collecting dust on a shelf," Wilson said. "That's what I thought."
Two-and-a-half years after the roundtable completed its plan to address a systemwide crisis, the province has not acted - and the situation has gotten worse.
Kids are facing years-long wait-lists for treatment. Exhausted families are being told their child is "too high needs" for specialized care. Scores of vulnerable children with complex needs are living out of hotels and office buildings while under the care of Ontario child welfare agencies. And hundreds of Ontario parents have been forced to relinquish care of their kids to children's aid societies because they've been unable to get help.
The Star has obtained a copy of the roundtable report, which had not previously been made public, and spoke to six former table members who confirmed the province has not been in touch for followup discussions or to take the lead on any of the 32 recommendations meant to help pull families out of crisis and bring cohesion to a fragmented system.
Meanwhile, several members of the roundtable who spoke to the Star say the group has received little more than a "thank you" for their work.
In March 2022, the roundtable submitted a report that included 32 recommendations to help pull families out of crisis. In a letter introducing their findings, the leaders made it clear they expected a response.
"To remove any doubt regarding our future expectations, we, along with all collaboration table members, declare - unequivocally - that we await with great interest the response from (the ministries)," they wrote, noting they were committed to working together on the solutions.
Wilson, 55, was hopeful. When the roundtable began, she had gone to some of the struggling families she worked with in the Windsor area and urged them to share their stories.
This is our chance, she told them. "We're going to make a difference."
She herself had raised a son with mental-health and developmental needs and had then become a child and youth advocate, guiding desperate families through the same challenges she'd navigated as the mother of a boy with what the province calls "complex needs." Children like her son typically have two or more mental health, developmental or behavioural conditions, such as autism and depression, and are united by the fact that Ontario's health and social services struggle to meet their needs.
The roundtable met more than a dozen times over 12 months. It felt like a good sign that representatives from two Ontario ministries - the Ministry of Health and the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) - attended the meetings. They heard parents speak about living in crisis.
"I asked these families to step up and share their stories, to bare their hearts," said Wilson.
"And then literally nothing came of it."
In response to questions from the Star, the two ministries sent a statement that said the report provided them "with insight and recommendations to improve the lives of children and youth."
The statement did not respond directly to questions about which recommendations, if any, have been acted upon, but cited several recent investments in child and youth health, including a $97-million pilot program for kids with complex special needs at three children's hospitals, funding to "expand access to community-based intensive services" and 10 new complex care beds.
A plan for a growing crisis
The roundtable recognized a host of long-standing challenges.
Its final report proposed recommendations that are "balanced, achievable, and essential for meaningful change," co-chairs Patrick Dion, the former executive director of a mental-health services agency, and Mark Kartusch, a child welfare leader, told the Star in a statement.
"We remain ready to support the implementation of these recommendations and are confident that our colleagues share this commitment to progress."
Some members of the roundtable who viewed the ministries' statement to the Star said that while investments are appreciated and will help children and families in some regions, the funding announcements noted by the government don't address the group's recommendations, which were meant to improve the system's overall effectiveness.
Derrick Drouillard, executive director of the Windsor-Essex Children's Aid Society and member of the 2021 roundtable, said it's notable that the report's first recommendation - to create a standing committee at the deputy ministerial level between the MCCSS and the ministries of Health and Education - has not yet been acted upon.
"Never have we been able to sit and say, at the highest ministry levels, let's sit together and unpack these recommendations," said Drouillard.
In an interview, Kartusch, now a director at an Indigenous child welfare agency in southern Ontario, acknowledged the report "has sat" and said its status is "something you'd have to ask the ministry about." He added that he remains "hopeful" the report will move forward, noting the province needs "a broad system solution, otherwise, we're just band-aiding it."
Among the recommendations: create and appropriately fund a new "Escalated Service" for young people in crisis; implement an "accelerated response mechanism" to overcome structural barriers for Indigenous and young people who face inequities; build capacity to ensure families and children get early intervention, minimizing their need for a crisis response; and boost the availability of live-in treatment options and access to respite services.
Drouillard said an "Escalated Service" - essentially a crisis response team - for each region is critical, noting that child welfare staff need to have confidence they can call a children's mental-health provider or community service and quickly get a residential treatment spot for the child or teen who has come through their doors.
"We don't have that right now," he said. "We'll have a meeting and we'll hear that it's going to take three months or six months and we are just waiting and waiting and waiting."
'I'm desensitized to
nothing happening'
Panel member Tanya Stack was raising a 14-year-old son with autism, anxiety and other complex needs when she agreed to join the roundtable to share her experience. Stack, an Oshawa bookkeeper with two younger children, said she was disappointed but not surprised the government hasn't taken action. Parents of children with complex needs are used to wasting their time pushing for change that never comes, she said. "That's our life."
"This is just another one of the things that I put my heart and soul into, all that effort, and it goes to the back burner," she said. "I'm desensitized to nothing happening."
Stack and her spouse have endured the cycle familiar to parents of children with high needs: traumatic police calls and emergency room trips that ended with their son waiting for many hours and then being released; getting turned down for services because of his dual diagnosis or high-functioning autism; unpredictable access to respite services.
"We're doing everything that we can do to try to survive day to day," Stack said.
One year after the report was submitted to the government, Wilson wrote to the ministry staff members who'd sat with her on the panel, saying she felt "sick" knowing that nothing had come of it.
"Can it really be true that not one of the recommendations has been acted upon to better the lives of families and youth in our communities ... even though we gave them the information they needed to begin the necessary changes to make things better?" she wrote.
Wilson, who works for a family support network in Windsor, said she continues to hear from parents who see no help coming and feel like they are "drowning."
"The family members are becoming physically ill, marriages are falling apart, and young people ... are continuing to spiral, taking their parents and siblings with them."
Things have gotten much worse for Ontario families since Wilson was raising her own son, Robert, who is now 28, she said. Wilson considers herself fortunate: she managed to get residential care for her child beginning at age 13 and educational supports when he was in school. "Although it was extremely challenging parenting a complex child with disabilities, we didn't face the same horrible journey that the families coming up behind us are."
In 2005, Wilson was told that the only way to get appropriate treatment for her then-nine-year-old child was to give him up to CAS under a temporary care agreement. Appalled, she phoned the office of the provincial minister. Soon after, the government, then led by Dalton McGuinty's Liberal Party, made a public promise to stop putting families in that position.
"No family in this province, in this day and age, should have to consider giving up their children to get the help" they need, then-minister Sandra Pupatello said in the legislature.
But that's exactly what families are now faced with, Wilson said.
"The support these kids need is not available. It doesn't exist today," Wilson said. "Or if it does, it's hidden."
The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca and
mogilvie@thestar.ca
CREDIT: Amy Dempsey Raven and Megan Ogilvie Toronto Star The Toronto Star is investigating how and why children with complex special needs are not getting the treatment, care and support they need. If you have a story or tip to share, email adempsey@thestar.ca

Title: Blueprint to address kids' needs left sitting on shelf:   Province fails to act on plan to improve treatment and care, advocates say

Document 182 of 500

Rogers did not buy naming rights for this op-ed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/rogers-did-not-buy-naming-rights-this-op-ed/docview/3110897045/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rogers+did+not+buy+naming+rights+for+this+op-ed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you peel this pungent onion ever so slightly, it quickly becomes apparent that the new Rogers Stadium scheme for Downsview is something akin to a monopolist's fever dream.
The just-announced live music venue, with capacity for 50,000 people, will sit temporarily (or so we're told) in the middle of what's to become a sprawling medium-density community where the decommissioned Downsview airport long operated.
It is a joint venture between Live Nation and Rogers, which is described as "the naming rights partner and exclusive telecommunications and entertainment sponsor."
Like many big cities, Toronto is not lacking in entertainment venues that double as billboards for whichever corporation acquired the naming rights. But there's more to this particular deal than just branding.
Let's start with Live Nation. The California-based entertainment superpower owns Ticketmaster, and is currently the target of a long-anticipated anti-trust lawsuit by the U.S. Federal Trade Commission, which wants the company broken up for allegedly "monopolizing markets across the live concert industry." Live Nation already owns the soon-to-be expanded Budweiser Stage at the imminently-to-be-denuded Ontario Place.
Ticketmaster, in turn, helps itself to a generous portion of almost all your live entertainment spend because of its exclusive ticketing deals with all manner of acts and venues, from Scotiabank Arena to TIFF, which has allowed itself - there was likely no choice - to embrace the highly anti-competitive practice of "dynamic pricing".
For its part, Rogers recently spent $4.7 billion to acquire Bell's stake in Maple Leaf Sports and Entertainment, which puts the telecom giant in the delicious position of co-owning not just something like a monopoly, but a vertically integrated one!
The company now either owns or co-owns Toronto's four major league sports teams, the venues in which those teams play, in addition to the concession rights in the venues, a piece of the merchandising rights, and a portion of the broadcast rights.
How sweet is that?
Rogers, which built its fortune on CRTC radio licenses, monopoly cable franchises and cell service, didn't need to acquire the naming rights to the Rogers Centre because Rogers bought Skydome in a fire sale in 2004 for about $25 million, an exquisitely remunerative deal considering the Province of Ontario (i.e. you) invested some half a billion dollars in the 1980s building it.
I'm guessing Rogers won't be in a hurry to name BMO Field or ScotiaBank arena after itself because to do so means forgoing enormously lucrative naming rights. But who knows?
The point of this rant is to note how a handful of corporate behemoths have become so deeply embedded in our recreational and cultural lives that they're almost impossible to avoid. This situation didn't happen overnight. But it is likely to become ever more consolidated as consumer choice disappears and the opportunities to squeeze even more money out of your pocket multiply, with little in the way of countervailing regulatory or competitive pressure.
Consider this: will we, as consumers of live entertainment, know if any backroom deals will be struck to ensure that this new Rogers Stadium doesn't cannibalize business at, say, the Budweiser Stage, ScotiaBank Arena, or the Rogers Centre? Of course not. But that there is reason to wonder at all is concerning enough.
In a saner and more competitive universe, the arrival of a new live music venue at Downsview would force rival venues like Rogers Centre and the Scotiabank Arena to up their game and book shows that are equally appealing, or would entice consumers with more attractive ticket prices. But there's no universe in which this kind of rivalry can take hold anymore because, well, all the players are related through cross-ownership or partnerships. None of them have any incentive to act on behalf of anyone except their own shareholders.
As if all that weren't enough, there is one more layer to this story. Some developers in recent years have taken to setting up quirky pop-up markets on their sites in advance of construction, partly to generate some income but also to create a sense of place. The Rogers Stadium turbocharges that tactic. One could reasonably ask how the presence of a sprawling entertainment venue will affect the mega-project planned for Downsview which will play out over three decades. Here again, we only have a single player: Northcrest, the development arm of the public sector pension plan that bought the entire 370-acre airport in 2018.
For some reason, 21st century tech-infused capitalism seems built to foster and then reinforce the existence of monopolies or corporate oligopolies. Canada is no stranger to such forces, as the supermarket, airline and telecom sectors can all attest.
Yet there's something especially grating about the way these dynamics have colonized our off-the-clock lives. The press release that accompanied this week's unveiling of the new Rogers Stadium included upbeat quotes by everyone from Rogers and Live Nations execs to Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford -- an impressively monolithic expression of support.
My question is why no one's calling on the competition regulators to join this party.
CAPTION: Telecommunications company Rogers Communications signage is pictured in Ottawa.
CREDIT: John Lorinc, Contributor

Subject: Consumers; Monopolies; Airports

Business indexing term: Subject: Consumers Monopolies Airports

Company / organization: Name: Bank of Nova Scotia; NAICS: 522110; Name: Ticketmaster Entertainment Inc; NAICS: 561599

Document 183 of 500

Before the girl was found in a Rosedale dumpster, Toronto Children's Aid had closed her case. Turns out, they had a history of closing cases too early

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/before-girl-was-found-rosedale-dumpster-toronto/docview/3110897035/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Before+the+girl+was+found+in+a+Rosedale+dumpster%2C+Toronto+Children%27s+Aid+had+closed+her+case.+Turns+out%2C+they+had+a+history+of+closing+cases+too+early&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The children's aid society responsible for closing the case of the four-year-old girl who was later found dead in a Rosedale dumpster failed, for years, to address the problem of shuttering child-protection cases too early, according to the province's Auditor General's office.
And before Neveah went missing, Children's Aid Society (CAS) Toronto was one of only two of the audited child-welfare agencies in the province that had not addressed concerns they were prematurely ending their supervision of vulnerable children - putting girls and boys still in need of protection at risk.
Now the provincial watchdog says it may be time for a new audit of the child-welfare system - saying she has no information about a "review and audit" Premier Doug Ford's government says it is undertaking.
"We are talking about the most vulnerable people in our society that are needing protection from their environment," Shelley Spence, who was appointed earlier this year, said in an exclusive interview with the Star this week. "So it is very disappointing."
Of Neveah, she said: "I can't get a picture of that dumpster out of my head."
Problem raised nearly decade ago
In 2015, Spence's predecessor, Bonnie Lysyk, published an audit of seven children's aid societies, examining whether they had "effective policies and procedures" in place and the value for money of the funding provided for those services.
Each CAS in the province is responsible for investigating concerns about children, such as neglect or abuse. When necessary, they seek out court orders for child-protection workers to monitor kids while they are cared for by their parents, or, when more extreme risks exist, in foster care.
The agencies are then responsible for deciding when they no longer need to supervise a child - and provincial standards say cases can only be closed when a case worker and their supervisor are satisfied there are no "current or imminent" safety threats to a child.
Among the 2015 audit's major findings: agencies "may be closing cases prematurely, risking the well-being of children."
That concern was raised after the office reviewed CAS cases that were re-opened - and discovered nearly half had been shuttered despite ongoing child-safety concerns.
The very risk factors that prompted the agencies to step back in, they found, had been present when they deemed there were no more safety concerns and ended involvement.
On average, cases were re-opened after just 68 days. In some cases, the society had to intervene again within a week of closure, the auditor's office found.
CAS Toronto initially failed to take action
In that 2015 audit, Lysyk recommended agencies ensure all initial risk factors are appropriately addressed before case closure and that there be an annual review of all reopened cases.
But by 2020, in a followup on whether the societies had acted, the auditor's office found two CAS organizations had not taken action on premature case closures.
One of them was CAS Toronto.
CAS Toronto did not take action on the issue of premature case closures until 2022, according to the auditor's office - seven years after the audit. By then, the child-protection agency had implemented just 57 per cent of the overall recommendations from the 2015 audit.
"Fifty-seven per cent is not what we would call a passing grade at school," Spence said.
"We'd hope for some progress. We do encourage the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) to work with their organizations to figure out the barriers."
Neveah's disappearance came after case closed
Neveah went missing after CAS Toronto requested supervision for her and her younger brother be ended. A judge made that order in March 2021.
One day after the CAS supervision order ended, neither Neveah nor her brother showed up at daycare, as had been arranged. They would never attend.
Neveah was last seen just months later, while in her mother's care, and police believe she died later that year.
Though CAS Toronto applied to re-open the case in 2023, it was already too late. Neveah's unidentified remains were found in a construction dumpster on Dale Avenue in 2022. It would take Toronto police investigators more than a year to discover who she was, using advanced DNA technology. To date, no one has been charged in her death, the cause of which remains undetermined.
Child-protection advocates have since questioned the decision to end CAS Toronto supervision of Neveah, knowing her mother had struggled with substance use and child-protection workers had identified previous concerns about neglect, unstable housing and inadequate supervision.
CAS Toronto did not directly respond to a question asking whether they'd addressed the premature case closure recommendation prior to Neveah going missing.
The Star is not publishing Neveah's last name to protect the identity of her siblings in CAS care.
Why cases were being closed early
Though Spence said she couldn't comment on the specifics of Neveah's time in CAS care, she said it's important to understand why cases were being closed early.
"I do think that if they fully implemented our recommendations and analyzed and assessed what those factors are, that could help with those problems," she said.
Reached by the Star this month, Lysyk - who conducted the 2015 audit - said she believes the time for another CAS audit has come, since it's been nearly a decade since the last one and "implementation rates were low."
"I think that there would be value for the Office of the Auditor General to conduct another audit of the CASs, if it so chooses," Lysyk said.
She noted, however, that it's the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services that oversees child-protection agencies. She called on the ministry to work more closely with CAS agencies to ensure audit recommendations - and other improvements needed to protect vulnerable children - are "properly and fully actioned."
In a statement Friday, a spokesperson for the ministry said it expects child-welfare societies to meet provincial requirements, including child-protection standards, saying the "safety and well-being of every child is a priority."
"Ministry standards are intended to guide child protection workers through each phase of service delivery including the closing of a child protection case," the statement said, adding that CAS reviews and annual reporting are some of the tools used to monitor compliance.
CAS Toronto, in a response to questions about their progress on the audit recommendations, said they have followed up and reported back to the auditor but did not say why their progress was less than 60 per cent, nearly nine years later.
"Like all child welfare agencies in Ontario, our Agency adheres to the Ontario Child Protection Standards (2016) and their mechanisms in place, including provincial reporting and quality improvement plans, to track performance," the emailed statement said.
"Further, our staff take part in comprehensive training on regulatory and standard requirements, and we have internal monitoring measures in place to ensure compliance and manage gaps. We continuously strive to implement sustainable and impactful processes that support the children and youth that our agency serves."
Though the audit didn't look specifically at child deaths while in or shortly following CAS supervision, Spence said it would be worthwhile to look at the connection, if any, between premature closure and child deaths.
Spence said she's aware of the planned "audit" the Ford government says is in the "early stages" but that her office is not involved. She said the province's standing committee of public accounts can request she investigate.
"If the standing committee wanted a special audit done, we would be more than happy to," Spence said, adding they will consider a new audit as part of their future work plan.
"Certainly it's on the radar ... in the coming years for a revisit."
CAPTION: Shelley Spence, Ontario's Auditor General, says it may be time for a new audit of the child welfare system.In November 2023,Neveah was given a municipal burial at Thornton Cemetery in Oshawa in a quiet plot courtesy of the cemetery.Shelley Spence, Ontario's Auditor General,says it may be time for a new audit of the child welfare system.Shelley Spence, Ontario's Auditor General,says it may be time for a new audit of the child welfare system.The remains of a four-year-old girl named Neveah were found in a dumpster about a year after her death.

Third NDP MPP hopes to swap Queen's Park for Ottawa in the next federal election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/third-ndp-mpp-hopes-swap-queens-park-ottawa-next/docview/3110897020/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Third+NDP+MPP+hopes+to+swap+Queen%27s+Park+for+Ottawa+in+the+next+federal+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - Jagmeet Singh's NDP could be adding a third member of the Ontario legislature to its roster of candidates, with Parkdale-High Park MPP Bhutila Karpoche announcing her intention to seek the party's federal nomination in that riding, the Star has learned. "Our country is at a crossroads and what we decide to do next will shape all our lives for a long time. It is not the time to simply accept things the way they are or cross our fingers that things will get better. It is a time to stand up and make the changes we want to see happen," Karpoche wrote in a statement to the Star."We must refuse the politics of cynicism and division. We must care for each other, not lose our sense of humanity. We must build a Canada where everyone, especially young people, can feel that our future is bright and secure." Karpoche becomes the third member of Ontario New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles's caucus who hopes to run under the NDP's federal banner when the next election is called - which could happen at any time, now that Singh has ended his parliamentary alliance that propped up the minority Liberal government.Hamilton Mountain MPP Monique Taylor and Ottawa Centre MPP Joel Harden have also mounted bids to secure the federal nominations in their ridings, both of which are currently held by Justin Trudeau's Liberals but have previously been won by the NDP. The same is true of Toronto's Parkdale-High Park, a riding that was previously won twice by the NDP's Peggy Nash, but has been home to Liberal MP - and now federal justice minister - Arif Virani since 2015. Despite Virani's current grip on the riding, Parkdale-High Park has produced close races between the Liberals and New Democrats in the past.The three MPPs are not yet official candidates for the federal New Democrats, who have already nominated 59 candidates but have yet to schedule nomination contests in their ridings. And while all three can remain in Stiles's caucus until a federal campaign is triggered, their potential departures would represent a blow for the Ontario NDP leader, who has ejected two MPPs - Sarah Jama and Mike Mantha - from her caucus, both of whom now sit as Independents. A loss of numerous incumbents could put the Ontario NDP on the back foot at a time when Premier Doug Ford has not ruled out calling an early provincial election.But should Karpoche win the nomination, her candidacy would be key to the federal NDP's hopes of wresting a number of Toronto ridings away from the Liberals amid waning support for the Trudeau government. The federal New Democrats have not held a riding in Toronto since 2015, when they lost their eight Toronto-area seats to the Liberals as Trudeau won a majority government. The party believes that the ascension of Olivia Chow - who was an NDP MP in the former riding of Trinity-Spadina from 2006 to 2014 - to Toronto's mayoralty last summer has given it an opening to reclaim long-lost turf. Karpoche, who also considered running in the mayoral race last year, has represented Parkdale-High Park at Queen's Park since 2018, and sailed easily to re-election in 2022 with 54 per cent of the vote. She is currently the Ontario NDP's affordability and GTA issues critic, and was the first Tibetan-Canadian elected to public office in North America.
CAPTION: Parkdale-High Park MPP Bhutila Karpochespeaks at a rally in Toronto protesting the closure of the Ontario Science Centre on July 14, 2024.

NDP MPP hopes to swap Queen's Park for Ottawa: Bhutila Karpoche third member of Ontario caucus to seek federal bid

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-mpp-hopes-swap-queens-park-ottawa/docview/3110871063/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+MPP+hopes+to+swap+Queen%27s+Park+for+Ottawa%3A+Bhutila+Karpoche+third+member+of+Ontario+caucus+to+seek+federal+bid&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jagmeet Singh's NDP could be adding a third member of the Ontario legislature to its roster of candidates, with Parkdale-High Park MPP Bhutila Karpoche announcing her intention to seek the party's federal nomination in that riding, the Star has learned.
"Our country is at a crossroads and what we decide to do next will shape all our lives for a long time. It is not the time to simply accept things the way they are or cross our fingers that things will get better. It is a time to stand up and make the changes we want to see happen," Karpoche wrote in a statement to the Star.
"We must refuse the politics of cynicism and division. We must care for each other, not lose our sense of humanity. We must build a Canada where everyone, especially young people, can feel that our future is bright and secure."
Karpoche becomes the third member of Ontario New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles's caucus who hopes to run under the NDP's federal banner when the next election is called - which could happen at any time, now that Singh has ended his parliamentary alliance that propped up the minority Liberal government.
Hamilton Mountain MPP Monique Taylor and Ottawa Centre MPP Joel Harden have also mounted bids to secure the federal nominations in their ridings, both of which are currently held by Justin Trudeau's Liberals but have previously been won by the NDP.
The same is true of Toronto's Parkdale-High Park, a riding that was previously won twice by the NDP's Peggy Nash, but has been home to Liberal MP - and now federal justice minister - Arif Virani since 2015. Despite Virani's current grip on the riding, Parkdale-High Park has produced close races between the Liberals and New Democrats in the past.
The three MPPs are not yet official candidates for the federal New Democrats, who have already nominated 59 candidates but have yet to schedule nomination contests in their ridings.
And while all three can remain in Stiles's caucus until a federal campaign is triggered, their potential departures would represent a blow for the Ontario NDP leader, who has ejected two MPPs - Sarah Jama and Mike Mantha - from her caucus, both of whom now sit as Independents.
A loss of numerous incumbents could put the Ontario NDP on the back foot at a time when Premier Doug Ford has not ruled out calling an early provincial election.
But should Karpoche win the nomination, her candidacy would be key to the federal NDP's hopes of wresting a number of Toronto ridings away from the Liberals amid waning support for the Trudeau government.
The federal New Democrats have not held a riding in Toronto since 2015, when they lost their eight Toronto-area seats to the Liberals as Trudeau won a majority government.
The party believes that the ascension of Olivia Chow - who was an NDP MP in the former riding of Trinity-Spadina from 2006 to 2014 - to Toronto's mayoralty last summer has given it an opening to reclaim long-lost turf.
Karpoche has represented Parkdale-High Park at Queen's Park since 2018, and sailed easily to re-election in 2022 with 54 per cent of the vote. She is currently the Ontario NDP's affordability and GTA issues critic, and was the first Tibetan-Canadian elected to public office in North America.

Document 186 of 500

Colonizing our downtime consolidates their power

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/colonizing-our-downtime-consolidates-their-power/docview/3110871055/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Colonizing+our+downtime+consolidates+their+power&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you peel this pungent onion ever so slightly, it quickly becomes apparent that the new Rogers Stadium scheme for Downsview is something akin to a monopolist's fever dream.
The just-announced live music venue, with capacity for 50,000 people, will sit temporarily (or so we're told) in the middle of what's to become a sprawling medium-density community where the decommissioned Downsview airport long operated.
It is a joint venture between Live Nation and Rogers, which is described as "the naming rights partner and exclusive telecommunications and entertainment sponsor."
Like many big cities, Toronto is not lacking in entertainment venues that double as billboards for whichever corporation acquired the naming rights. But there's more to this deal than just branding.
Let's start with Live Nation. The California-based entertainment superpower owns Ticketmaster, and is currently the target of a long-anticipated anti-trust lawsuit by the U.S. Federal Trade Commission, which wants the company broken up for allegedly "monopolizing markets across the live concert industry." Live Nation already owns the soon-to-be expanded Budweiser Stage at the imminently-to-be-denuded Ontario Place.
Ticketmaster, in turn, helps itself to a generous portion of almost all your live entertainment spend because of its exclusive ticketing deals with all manner of acts and venues, from Scotiabank Arena to TIFF, which has allowed itself - there was likely no choice - to embrace the highly anti-competitive practice of "dynamic pricing." For its part, Rogers recently spent $4.7 billion to acquire Bell's stake in Maple Leaf Sports and Entertainment, which puts the telecom giant in the delicious position of co-owning not only something like a monopoly, but a vertically integrated one!
The company now either owns or co-owns Toronto's four major league sports teams, the venues in which those teams play, in addition to the concession rights in the venues, a piece of the merchandising rights, and a portion of the broadcast rights. How sweet is that?
Rogers, which built its fortune on CRTC radio licenses, monopoly cable franchises and cell service, didn't need to acquire the naming rights to the Rogers Centre because Rogers bought Skydome in a fire sale in 2004 for about $25 million, an exquisitely remunerative deal considering the Province of Ontario (i.e. you) invested some half a billion dollars in the 1980s building it.
I'm guessing Rogers won't be in a hurry to name BMO Field or ScotiaBank arena after itself because to do so means forgoing enormously lucrative naming rights. But who knows?
The point of this rant is to note how a handful of corporate behemoths have become so deeply embedded in our recreational and cultural lives that they're almost impossible to avoid. This situation didn't happen overnight. But it is likely to become ever more consolidated as consumer choice disappears and the opportunities to squeeze even more money out of your pocket multiply, with little in the way of countervailing regulatory or competitive pressure.
Consider this: Will we, as consumers of live entertainment, know if any backroom deals will be struck to ensure that this new Rogers Stadium doesn't cannibalize business at, say, the Budweiser Stage, Scotiabank Arena, or the Rogers Centre? Of course not. But that there is reason to wonder at all is concerning enough.
In a saner and more competitive universe, the arrival of a new live music venue at Downsview would force rival venues like Rogers Centre and the Scotiabank Arena to up their game and book shows that are equally appealing, or would entice consumers with more attractive ticket prices. But there's no universe in which this kind of rivalry can take hold anymore because, well, all the players are related through cross-ownership or partnerships. None of them have any incentive to act on behalf of anyone except their own shareholders.
As if all that weren't enough, there is one more layer to this story. Some developers in recent years have taken to setting up quirky pop-up markets on their sites in advance of construction, partly to generate some income but also to create a sense of place. The Rogers Stadium turbocharges that tactic. One could reasonably ask how the presence of a sprawling entertainment venue will affect the mega-project planned for Downsview which will play out over three decades. Here again, we only have a single player: Northcrest, the development arm of the public sector pension plan that bought the entire 370-acre airport in 2018.
For some reason, 21st century tech-infused capitalism seems built to foster and then reinforce the existence of monopolies or corporate oligopolies. Canada is no stranger to such forces, as the supermarket, airline and telecom sectors can all attest.
Yet there's something especially grating about the way these dynamics have colonized our off-the-clock lives. The press release that accompanied this week's unveiling of the new Rogers Stadium included upbeat quotes by everyone from Rogers and Live Nations execs to Mayor Olivia Chow and Premier Doug Ford - an impressively monolithic expression of support.
My question is why no one's calling on the competition regulators to join this party.
John Lorinc is a Toronto journalist and editor, and a Toronto Star contributor.
CAPTION: Rogers now controls Toronto's four major sports teams, their venues and concession, merchandising and broadcast rights.Sean Kilpatrick THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

Document 187 of 500

Drug disasters bring Ford and Eby closer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/drug-disasters-bring-ford-eby-closer/docview/3110870937/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Drug+disasters+bring+Ford+and+Eby+closer&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: We know drug addiction takes a heavy toll on individuals and communities.
We also know it's a deeply divisive issue, pitting individuals against communities.
What's less well known is how two premiers from disparate provinces and distinct parties are slowly converging on substance abuse.
As Doug Ford flirts with pre-election rhetoric in Ontario, and David Eby faces re-election realities in B.C., their similarities in substance - albeit not style - are hard to ignore.
To be sure, Ford's Tories and Eby's New Democrats are polar opposites. Ford is all about bombast; Eby is all empathy.
Despite that duality, they are increasingly aligned - if not in their rhetoric, then in the realities of governing. The lessons from Lotus Land, as West Coast politics are often described, are worth studying in Ontario.
On the eve of his campaign launch, B.C.'s NDP premier seemed to acknowledge that the prerequisite to winning re-election was admitting defeat on the decriminalization of drugs. For years, his New Democrats led the way on harm reduction, needle exchanges and gaining a special exemption from federal law on consumption of illegal hard drugs.
For British Columbians, it was a bridge too far. What once seemed worth trying to stop addicts from dying - if only by default because nothing else seemed to work - now seems only to have made matters worse for everyone.
The dying is ongoing, while the enabling of open drug use - once seen as a stopgap - seems only to be normalizing and multiplying the problem. Politically, the plight of individuals has become a blight on communities as unregulated and uninhibited drug use drags down neighbourhoods.
The experiment continued for years, but the clock was ticking for B.C. New Democrats. In prison, nothing focuses the mind like a hanging in the morning; in politics, nothing focuses the fight for hearts and minds like an election in the offing.
Eby's backtracking began even before he hit the campaign trail. He first retreated from decriminalization, then called for compelled treatment of people with severe addictions who are mentally ill. With more constraints to come, it was a massive climbdown for the NDP.
"This announcement is the beginning of a new phase of our response to the addiction crisis," the B.C. premier promised. "We are taking action to get them the care they need to keep them safe, and in doing so, keep our communities safe, too."
Keep our communities safe?
Whenever Ford uses words like that, Ontario's NDP - which forms the Official Opposition - accuses him of sacrificing the lives of addicts while opening up a political wedge. But in B.C., a different New Democratic Party - accustomed to governing - is closing the political wounds.
Uncontrolled encampments have created no-go zones in Vancouver and Victoria. A series of attacks by people who are mentally ill created a public outcry.
The open sale of drugs and implements outside harm reduction sites seemed only to spread the harm. Crack pipes started appearing in vending machines outside needle exchanges.
At the Vancouver announcement, Mayor Ken Sim saluted Eby's belated political U-turn. Unsurprisingly, a chorus of mental health experts condemned the NDP's about-face, saying involuntary treatment lacks scientific evidence.
Yet as we learned during the pandemic, the science isn't quite so simple - and political science is a lot harder than it looks. It's easy to be bold in opposition, but governments of all parties are ultimately accountable to voters at election time.
Experts insist that politicians must follow the science - such as it is - but getting the people to follow the politicians is the challenge. In B.C. as in Ontario, broad swaths of the public are compassionate and concerned about the epidemic of drug abuse, but after several years of wide open experimentation, the margin of manoeuvre is shrinking.
For all the cheap talk of harm reduction, rehabilitation and decriminalization, the hard political truth is that it is far more expensive than politicians ever bargained on. Beyond the political costs, the clinical costs of creating holistic treatment cycles that shepherd addicts from needle exchanges into treatment are daunting.
Almost inevitably, governments in both Ontario and B.C., municipally and provincially, defaulted to the path of least resistance - harm reduction without the rehabilitation that is so much more comprehensive and expensive.
For his part, Ford seems willing to make the war on drugs part of the culture wars in Ontario. But his announcement last month shutting down many of the province's safe injection sites - by declaring their locations unsafe - has come and gone without a major public fight.
As politicians, both Ford and Eby can read public opinion. But as premiers who aspire to govern, it's not enough to shut down failed experiments and shrug their shoulders.
The challenge will be living up to their promises to stop the dying, by making the bigger investments in the broader treatment cycles that are required for deadly addictions. That's the difference between sloganeering politics and serious governing.
CAPTION: As David Eby, left, faces re-election realities in B.C. and Doug Ford flirts with pre-election rhetoric in Ontario, their similarities in substance - albeit not style - are hard to ignore, Martin Regg-Cohn writes.THE CANADIAN PRESS file photos
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 188 of 500

Indigenous leaders say more needs to be done: Some highlight challenges working with Ottawa, seek 'willing partner' to build better relations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/indigenous-leaders-say-more-needs-be-done/docview/3110870923/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Indigenous+leaders+say+more+needs+to+be+done%3A+Some+highlight+challenges+working+with+Ottawa%2C+seek+%27willing+partner%27+to+build+better+relations&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberal party of today is not quite the same as the one elected in 2015 promising to foster new paths and nation-to-nation relationships with Indigenous Peoples, the leaders of the three national Indigenous organizations said as they look ahead to the fourth National Day for Truth and Reconciliation on Monday.
"Our reconciliation moment that started in 2015 really had, in the beginning, this blue-sky hope of a changed Canada," said Natan Obed, the president of Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami, an organization that serves as the national voice for 70,000 Inuit in Canada.
"Now, in many cases, we're trying to figure out how to implement our clear positions - the things that we hope to do to implement our rights or to build a better relationship with this country. But we're seeing the challenges in either working with the federal government to do that, or even between Indigenous Peoples."
Cassidy Caron, president of the Métis National Council representing Métis in Alberta, Ontario and British Columbia, says there was a seismic shift in the government's agenda around the time of the COVID-19 pandemic.
The housing crisis and the increased cost of living took over headline after headline, but there was a lack of acknowledgment from politicians that Indigenous Peoples have been at the front line of those crises long before they became political talking points, she said.
"With one year left before a federal election, there's still significant work that needs to be done, and we have the ability to do it in partnership," she said. "But we need a willing partner on the other side."
Cindy Woodhouse Nepinak, the national chief of the Assembly of First Nations representing some 630 chiefs across the country, said the lives of Indigenous Peoples are literally on the line of that partnership.
She pointed to the police-involved killings of nine Indigenous people in recent weeks.
"If that was done to the same proportions on another community somewhere, it would be alarming," she said.
"The whole of government is responsible for this."
The ministers of Indigenous Services and Crown-Indigenous Relations acknowledge that progress may not be as swift as Indigenous Peoples may wish to see but insist their determination - and that of their government - has never wavered.
Still, agenda items remain unfulfilled, namely around the recognition of rights, child welfare reforms, the ever-growing infrastructure gap and clean drinking water.
Perhaps the most fraught discussion is about who recognized as Indigenous.
That issue came to a head after the Liberals introduced Bill C-53, a mechanism to formally recognize Métis governments in Ontario, Alberta and Saskatchewan.
It was meant to be a means of creating a new relationship between Métis and the federal government, but soon devolved into questions about who should be considered Métis after a pressure campaign from First Nations who took issue with the Ontario group.
The future of that legislation is uncertain with a legal challenge and without unanimous support for the bill by Métis nation organizations included in the bill.
"The federal government has a responsibility to find a way to make (self-government) happen, working alongside our Métis governments," said Caron.
Obed has been begging the federal government to have a conversation about identity for years over a group he says is fraudulently claiming to be Inuit.
He says the federal government is being overly risk-averse about wanting to determine who is Indigenous, despite mounting pressure from Indigenous leaders for the government to follow their lead and their understanding of histories.
"This conversation is defining the future of Canada, and I don't say this in a way that's meant to be overstated," he said.
"We're in for another wave of dispossession based on non-Indigenous Canadians choosing to be Indigenous to take what they feel is theirs."
Crown-Indigenous Relations Minister Gary Anandasangaree said confirming Indigenous rights is an "arduous process."
"Make no mistake, the role of the Canadian government is not to be the arbiter of Indigenous identity," he said.
"Rather, the work that I'm trying to do is to ensure that anyone who's asserting that identity gets a fair process, one that is guided by Section 35 (of the Charter) but also historical records that can confirm their identity."
Child welfare is another major area of contention.
The federal government triumphantly announced in July that it had reached a $47.8-billion agreement with First Nations to reform the on-reserve child welfare system. The settlement came after years of litigation at the Canadian Human Rights Tribunal over chronic underfunding of those systems.
Woodhouse Nepinak billed it as a win for the federal government in the effort to materially change the lives of First Nations kids.
But the victory is not certain: The deal has caused fractures among chiefs, some of whom think it doesn't go far enough. It's set to go to a vote at a special AFN assembly in October.
Another piece of legislation chiefs say could change the lives of First Nations is Bill C-61, which was co-developed with First Nations to ensure communities have clean water and can protect the source-water on their territories.
But the bill has stalled at a House of Commons committee, and many chiefs have questioned whether Indigenous Services Minister Patty Hajdu believes First Nations have a right to clean drinking water.
She didn't answer that question directly in an interview, but said the committee has heard "a variety of different amendments" along that line. She said she is open to adopting any changes that strengthen the goal of the bill, which is "to make sure that First Nations have access to clean drinking water" and control over that water.
Asked whether she has confidence that bill will be made law before the next election, Hajdu lamented the fact that the NDP ended the supply-and-confidence agreement that has kept her minority government in power for over two years.
"My goal is to have this, hopefully, to Senate by December," she said. "That will entirely be dependent on the speed of the opposition parties and whether or not they're going to play games in the House, as they did last spring, which delayed the debate on the legislation."
The government did pass a bill creating a National Council for Reconciliation earlier this year but Obed wishes it had not become law.
The council, which is intended to fulfil a call to action from the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's final report, will monitor and report on the federal government's progress toward reconciliation. It has seats for all three national Indigenous organizations.
But Obed, who has billed the council as "toothless," said his organization is debating whether to put forward a name for it at all.
"We feel that this particular body may actually cause a threat to our continued work on reconciliation based on who may or may not be appointed, and based on what the government of Canada chooses to do with the recommendations and reports that are given back," he said.
He said he worries the council could be "weaponized to be the authoritative position of Indigenous Peoples that the government of Canada then says it is working on or has achieved reconciliation from."
Anandasangaree defended the council, saying it will not replace current accountability structures between national Indigenous organizations and the federal government.
"Reconciliation is hard, and reconciliation is not a passive exercise," Anandasangaree said.
He said it's going to take time and a significant amount of effort. "And I've said it before many times: it's going to take every successive government coming forward from now onward to be on this path."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Métis National Council president Cassidy Caron said "there's still significant work that needs to be done" to foster new paths and relationships with Indigenous Peoples.Adrian WyldTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Alessia Passafiume The Canadian Press
