Document 189 of 500

Local media strengthens connections and safeguards democracy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/local-media-strengthens-connections-safeguards/docview/3110722041/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Local+media+strengthens+connections+and+safeguards+democracy&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: This World News Day, we're reminded that supporting local media and journalism isn't just a civic duty - it's also a smart business decision for advertisers. Strengthening connection with Canadian audiences while safeguarding our democracy? That's a win-win.
On Tuesday, the Canadian Media Directors' Council (CMDC) board gathered at the Toronto Star's newsroom with editor-in-chief Nicole MacIntyre to reaffirm our commitment to the Canadian Media Manifesto. Seeing the newsroom in action was nothing short of inspiring. Journalists tackling the day's most pressing issues from hard-hitting investigative reports that hold the powerful accountable to real-time challenges like climate change to our cities' infrastructure. Watching them work reminded us just how essential their role is in keeping society informed and connected to what's really happening in the world around us.
Recent research by CMDC in partnership with classified ad site Kijiji and media buying agency GroupM sheds light on the crucial role local media plays to newcomers. Seventy-six per cent of newcomers engage with Canadian media and news before stepping foot in this country. Local news builds bridges, fostering integration and community spirit. Yet, we must recognize another truth: while 74 per cent of Canadians depend on local news, distinguishing fact from fiction is becoming an increasing challenge. With more than 700 AI-generated news sites emerging online, our trusted media ecosystem is under threat. These sites often feature mostly AI-generated content, lack human oversight, are designed to mimic authentic journalism, and fail to clearly disclose that their material is AI-produced.
The democracy we cherish relies on three foundational pillars - strong institutions, shared stories, and social capital. More than half of the world's population will have voted in this election year 2024. Conflicts are rising and technological advancements like AI are reshaping society. Yet, quality news and journalism remain underfunded, making it even more difficult to combat the spread of misinformation. In the last decade, the rise of "citizen journalism" has only intensified the spread of misinformation and incendiary content. At the same time, audiences have grown accustomed to consuming content for free or at minimal cost.
This is why we launched the Canadian Media Manifesto on World News Day in 2021 - to spark vital conversations about the need for advertisers to invest in local media. Setting an ambitious goal to allocate 25 per cent of digital media investments to local news by 2025 ($380 million), we have already made progress, reaching 19.8 per cent with more than 1,500 pledges from marketing, media and business leaders committed to this mission. In conversations with some brand marketers, they understand local media's power in shaping mindsets, influencing culture and driving effective advertising and brand growth.
This September, the Ontario provincial government mandated that government agencies, boards and commissions spend at least 25 per cent of their advertising budget with Ontario-based news organizations. As Premier Doug Ford stated, "By ensuring that a minimum of 25 per cent of their advertising budgets are reserved for Ontario-based publishers, the government is helping to support these publishers and their workers, who are creating local news content for people across the province." We urge the federal government to follow suit and encourage businesses and their leaders to further contribute to a healthy and prosperous Canadian media ecosystem.
Supporting local media is more than simply making a difference; it tangibly fuels our economy and deepens our connection with Canada's diverse audiences.
So, on this World News Day, let's reaffirm our commitment to local media. Let's invest in a better future.
CAPTION: Members of theCanadian Media Directors' Councilboard toured the Toronto Star newsroom on Sept. 24.
CREDIT: Shannon Lewis, Contributor

Subject: Journalism; Media planning & buying; False information; Advertising; Audiences; Democracy

Business indexing term: Subject: Media planning & buying Advertising

Document 190 of 500

Some good comes from conflict

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/some-good-comes-conflict/docview/3110722001/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+good+comes+from+conflict&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Leave room for differing opinions
On U of T's campus everyone is shouting and no one is being heard. I'm the hopeless optimist trying to fix it, Sept. 21
In assessing a discourse between opposing parties, civil or otherwise, it is important to understand whether they wish to change the views of the other side or influence the views of a third party such as the university president or governing council. In the debate over Hamas' holding hostages and Israel's invasion of Gaza, my sense is that neither side has any possibility of changing the views of the other side. This differs from many debates in universities where discussion can cause the other side to refine, clarify or even modify its position. On this topic perhaps the best that can be hoped for is to prevent each side from making insults or ad hominem attacks against other groups of students.
Bruce Couchman, Ottawa
Conflict is unavoidable; however, much good has come of it. Think of the civil rights movement. Good conflict is fuelled by respect for one's opponent. It understands and holds complexity and allows for dialogue to learn from one another. On the other hand, high conflict is driven by "us" versus "them," devolving into stereotypes and dehumanization. It is seductive, exhausting, dangerous and very difficult to stop despite a desire to do so. I agree, we all must push for better.
Cheryl McNamara, Toronto
Want better discourse? Start by checking facts
I've watched us descend into conflicting realities - here's how we get out, Sept. 19
Writer Carol Off poses the question, "how does the news media regain the attention and trust of the public?" One way is fact-checking politicians. During the Harris/Trump debate, there was simultaneous fact-check of Trump's statements and immediate feedback about his sensational claims. We need that more often. The public is distrustful when the whole truth is hidden. How much money was really spent by taxpayers in speeding up Premier Doug Ford's beer plan or the deals surrounding the Greenbelt? Why does it take so long for the RCMP to report on that situation? It takes the deeper digging that journalists do to bring to light the backroom "deals" made by politicians. No wonder Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre wants to get rid of the CBC. He fears journalists like Off as they challenge the truth of his claims.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
We need better from our leaders
The acrimony between Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Jagmeet Singh is on full display as return to Parliament looms, Sept. 11
I am so tired of the bullying, name-calling and finger pointing nonsense that seems to be happening among our elected leaders. They all look and sound so ridiculous, not to mention very foolish. It is as though they are putting on a performance in a reality show and are forgetting that they have been elected to actually do a job. Please, for the sake of our country, can elected officials all focus on the horrific issues that Canadians are facing today?
Melanie Kohek, Whitby, Ont.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, left, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh have all "claimed their main concern is bettering the lot of Canadians, as they traded accusations of pettiness, childishness and peddling in falsehoods," the Star's Ottawa bureau reported.

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Some good comes from conflict

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 28, 2024

Section: opinion/letters-to-the-editor

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document 191 of 500

There are two lanes in the bike lane debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-are-two-lanes-bike-lane-debate/docview/3110721976/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+are+two+lanes+in+the+bike+lane+debate&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In defense of bike lanes
Ford shoots down criticism of controversial plan to limit bike lanes, Sept. 23
The "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," which would prohibit the construction of new bike lanes in the instance that they remove a vehicular traffic lane, is an unsustainable, dangerous and regressive idea put forth by Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives. Toronto is growing in population every day, and everyone has somewhere they need to be. Traffic will only get worse if unsustainable car-dependent tendencies are not addressed. The GTA's continued reliance on cars is the cause of gridlock and congestion, not the city's newly implemented bike lanes. Toronto's bike lanes alleviate car traffic on the road and will lead to more sustainable transportation practices in the city long term.
Protected and separated bike lanes have also been shown to make transportation safer for all road users. A 2019 study published in the Journal of Transport & Health noted that 12 cities in the U.S. saw fatal crash rates decrease by as much as 75 per cent following an increase in the creation of cycling infrastructure. Toronto and the GTA will not become safer if bike lane creation is hindered.
This new act is also extremely regressive in its ideology. Perhaps in the 1970s, the novelty of a personal automobile was still exciting and futuristic. But in 2024 it is paramount that Toronto and the province work to build diversified transportation infrastructure to ensure future generations get to experience the joys of mobility. The future of Toronto and the GTA should be cycling and other forms of active transportation, not cars endlessly stuck in gridlock on Highway 401.
Olivia Wiper, Newmarket
We close curb lanes for months to accommodate an outdoor patio, and for years to accommodate construction of any kind of building. We close curb lanes all day long for parcel and takeout deliveries. A single car can close hundreds of feet of a curb lane by parking midblock, forcing everyone else to merge in the centre lane. We close big chunks of a lane when an inconsiderate driver wants to grab a coffee, or when they wait for someone to emerge from a building or just need to pull over to make a call. If Premier Doug Ford has his way, a bike lane will soon be the only thing you can't use a curb lane for.
Doug Earl, Toronto
As much as Premier Doug Ford tries to distance himself from the divisive antics of federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, he has obviously taken note of the effect of Poilievre's tactic of pitting groups against each other. In pandering to his car-driving base, and with no regard for climate change or the safety of vulnerable road users, Ford has chosen to pit those who pedal against those who guzzle gas, instead of improving and expanding public transit. Does he not realize that the cycling community can become a formidable lobby when poked?
Judith Butler, Toronto
There are impacts from bike lanes
As a resident of Woodbine Avenue, where bike lanes were imposed a couple of years ago, the most immediate impacts were twofold: first, as the major artery for people heading to Scarborough along Kingston Road, Gerrard Street East or O'Connor Drive, traffic jams on Woodbine grew exponentially; and second, hoping to escape the gridlock, motorists began turning off Woodbine onto the nearby residential streets. The city has even recognized the problem by altering some traffic rules, but it hasn't helped much. All of this chaos could possibly be justified if the bike lanes, which are cleared of snow in the winter before Woodbine is, were busy. But they're not. The vast majority of the time there are no bikes to be seen.
Claire Hoy, Toronto
No doubt downtown cycling advocates come from a good spot, and many of them, to their credit, are progressive and honestly advocate for the inclusion of all. However, they do not realize how exclusionary they sound to those who live in areas like Rexdale, Jane-Finch, Black Creek, Crescent Town, Thorncliffe Park or Scarborough. Those residents who live far from downtown do not welcome lectures about how bike lanes that hinder their driving are good for them, or how cars, and, more particularly, the people in them, need to have roadblocks put in their way.
Roman Botiuk, Etobicoke
I couldn't agree more with the province's position on bike lanes in Toronto. Do not add any more if it means reducing current vehicle lanes. I have witnessed first-hand the traffic on streets that have had vehicle lanes eliminated to add bike lanes. It's made it almost impossible for emergency vehicles to get where they need to go in a timely manner. Fire trucks, ambulances and police cars get stuck, with only a single lane of traffic in each direction, all the time. It is a real safety issue!
Larry Proctor, Caledon East, Ont.
CAPTION: The Ford provincial government wants to stop construction of bike lanes that take space away from cars.

Document 192 of 500

'We got outgunned:' The inside story of how Justin Trudeau lost the battle over his most important climate plan

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-got-outgunned-inside-story-how-justin-trudeau/docview/3110721966/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We+got+outgunned%3A%27+The+inside+story+of+how+Justin+Trudeau+lost+the+battle+over+his+most+important+climate+plan&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "When it starts to fall apart," Gord Downie once sang. "Man, it really falls apart."Canada's once-lauded carbon pricing program began falling apart soon after it was set up in 2019. Today, thanks to government incompetence and a relentless, bad-faith campaign to destroy it, it is really falling apart.I've followed the promise of and backlash to the Liberals' deeply flawed, largely successful and seemingly doomed carbon pricing plan from the beginning. In recent months, I've spoken to architects of the regime, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, his first environment minister, Catherine McKenna, and his long-time right-hand man Gerald Butts about what went wrong and why. What I heard was the story of a good policy undermined by a cynical conservative campaign, sold badly by the Trudeau government and eventually failed by a government that couldn't be bothered justifying one of its own marquee initiatives.If nothing changes, the story will end in tragedy. "Globally, people will think we are bonkers" if the carbon pricing system dies, McKenna told me last week. And they'd be right. But it doesn't have to be that way. There are tangible ideas on the table now that could make the system more palatable to voters, while beefing up Canada's economy, lightening the load on taxpayers and still reducing Canada's carbon emissions. The only question is: Will Trudeau's government embrace them before it's too late? ***Gerald Butts, Trudeau's former senior adviser, remembers offering a prescient warning in the early years of the Liberals' mandate.The federal government was negotiating with the provinces on how to implement a national price on carbon. Most of the premiers around the table already had some version of the same policy in place, or were at least receptive to the idea, even the conservative-leaning ones. But there was one naysayer: Saskatchewan premier Brad Wall. Butts pointed to Wall as the ghost of conservative future. "That's the base (the national party) will build back from," Butts told his colleagues. "So listen to what Brad Wall is saying." Some in Trudeau's government were convinced they didn't need to listen. They were so buoyed by polls showing widespread support for climate action that they were ignoring the more sobering data underneath: Canadians wanted climate action, sure, but they were loath to pay more in taxes to make it happen. McKenna says she knew all that going in. Carbon pricing "was never a political winner," she told me. Her mission was to make the plan politically palatable, yet she found herself facing others in cabinet who wanted to use the tax revenues to finance new projects. "I had to fight very, very hard internally to return the revenue (to taxpayers)," she says. It's a fight she won, but it was clear to her, even then, that some of her colleagues were wearing green-tinted glasses.Parliament adopted the final version of the plan in 2018. Ottawa would set out baseline standards: A minimum price on gasoline paid by consumers at the pump; and a second price covering what industrial emitters belch out. The provinces could adopt their own systems, as long as they met those baselines. If they failed or refused, Ottawa would step in and install a carbon tax, returning the revenue directly to taxpayers.The political winds began shifting even before the carbon price took effect. Wall's backup arrived via Alberta Premier Jason Kenney and Ontario's Doug Ford: "The resistance," as Maclean's called them. They dismantled their predecessors' carbon pricing schemes, forcing Ottawa to impose the federal carbon tax, then marched into court to challenge it on constitutional grounds.Still, the Liberals were optimistic. Most low-income and middle-class Canadians stood to actually earn money through the federal tax. But McKenna was still worried. She wanted to return the tax as a quarterly rebate, the "Climate Action Incentive," so Canadians would be clear that they were coming out ahead. But the Canada Revenue Agency nixed that idea. "We were told 'nope, I'm sorry,'" she says. Instead, the rebate was folded into Canadians' yearly tax returns."We needed to do a sales job on this," McKenna says. It didn't happen. "The government moved on." McKenna ultimately quit politics in 2021.The Supreme Court finally rendered its verdict on the carbon pricing plan in 2021, deeming it constitutional and warning of "irreversible harm" to Canada if the federal government was blocked from fighting a threat as existential as climate change. It turned out to be a pyrrhic victory. For Canada to meet its climate targets, the minimum prices established in the original plan had to keep rising, year after year. That was fine in a booming economy, but when inflation spiked post COVID the public mood began to change. In 2022, things got even worse for the plan. When staff at the independent Parliamentary Budget Office crunched the numbers, they found a key Liberal selling point was no longer true. Most households under the federal carbon tax were going to pay more than they received in the rebate. As the pandemic faded from view and the fiscal picture worsened, people from coast-to-coast-to-coast grew increasingly pissed. The anti-vaccine Freedom Convoy, originally born out of anti-carbon tax activism, descended on Ottawa in early 2022. Erin O'Toole, a soft environmentalist, was ousted in favour of the pugnacious Pierre Poilievre. In Alberta, Kenney was replaced with Danielle Smith - who bemoaned "Trudeau's anti-carbon-dioxide obsession" and warned that Ottawa planned to use the environment as an excuse to implement dystopian social controls. Liberals today love to blame misinformation for derailing their climate plans. McKenna is more blunt: We got "outgunned," she said. As opposition grew, the Liberals only fuelled the idea that the price was partisan and political, when they exempted home heating oil in Liberal-red Atlantic Canada.Despite all the noise, backlash and messaging missteps, though, one fact is often ignored: The carbon price worked. Independent modeling shows Canada's CO2 emissions falling to 50-year lows. The Canadian Climate Institute's analysis suggests that, without carbon pricing and other measures, Canada's emissions would be up to 40 per cent higher than they are today. Canada is not quite on track to hit its climate targets, but it is nearly there.At least for now. Poilievre is promising a "carbon tax election," and Canadians look set to endorse his plan to "axe the tax." NDP leader Jagmeet Singh hastily distanced himself from carbon pricing this month. Even eco-friendly B.C. may elect a climate skeptic. I've been speaking to Liberal MPs who are exasperated that one of the party's signature policies, a real legacy initiative, is falling apart. "How are you f-ing up something that people actually care about?" one MP asked. The fight isn't over, the same MP said. "There is a way to thread the needle." There are good ideas on the table. They could start by taxing the $40 billion in annual profits made by oil and gas companies in Canada - the Parliamentary Budget Officer assessed a modest version of such a tax could raise $4 billion in revenue over five years.That kind of money could allow the Liberals to switch from defence to offence. The Liberals allowed "the climate plan to be defined by its enemies as just the carbon tax," Butts told me. More money in the system could allow for a "national climate plan," styled after Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act. That could mean tax breaks, better carbon tax rebates, and a suite of green investment.The Liberals could also consider a strategic retreat from the consumer carbon tax, instead making the supply-side industrial levy more stringent. The industrial carbon price has been the single most effective carbon-reduction policy, although there is room for improvement.Ottawa could be even more bold. The European Union is running a massive, successful carbon market. Ditching the federal carbon tax and joining the European pricing scheme could come with huge benefits and efficiencies. "It's not a crazy idea!" Butts, now a vice president at the Eurasia Group, assures me.Even if it is, the time might be right for some crazy ideas.Unfortunately, the Trudeau government looks like it does on so many files, paralyzed with indecision. When I spoke to the prime minister about this in March, he didn't seem compelled to change much. "People can vote the way they want to," he shrugged.Right now, thanks to Trudeau's personal unpopularity, Canadians look set to vote for a party that will dismantle his most ambitious climate plans. If they do, the country will miss its climate targets by a mile.McKenna is out of politics now, but she hasn't given up the fight. "I'm not going to stop defending carbon pricing because Pierre Poilievre lies about it, and the NDP are a joke," she said. "I can't help it if politicians are weak."
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau speaks to supporters during a Liberal party fundraiser in Montreal on July 3, 2024.

Subject: Canadians; Climate action; Emissions; Carbon; Politics; Climate change; Prime ministers; Tax elections; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax elections Environmental tax

Document 193 of 500

A history of closing cases too early: Auditor general says it may be time for a new audit of the provincial child-welfare system

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/history-closing-cases-too-early/docview/3110653099/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+history+of+closing+cases+too+early%3A+Auditor+general+says+it+may+be+time+for+a+new+audit+of+the+provincial+child-welfare+system&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The children's aid society responsible for closing the case of the four-year-old girl who was later found dead in a Rosedale dumpster failed, for years, to address the problem of shuttering child-protection cases too early, according to the province's auditor general's office.
And before Neveah went missing, Children's Aid Society (CAS) Toronto was one of only two of the audited child-welfare agencies in Ontario that hadn't addressed concerns they were prematurely ending their supervision of vulnerable children - putting girls and boys still in need of protection at risk.
Now the provincial watchdog says it may be time for a new audit of the child-welfare system - saying she has no information about a "review and audit" Premier Doug Ford's government says it is undertaking.
"We are talking about the most vulnerable people in our society that are needing protection from their environment," Shelley Spence, who was appointed earlier this year, said this week. "So it is very disappointing."
In 2015, her predecessor, Bonnie Lysyk, published an audit of seven children's aid societies, examining whether they had "effective policies and procedures" in place and the value for money of the funding provided for those services.
Each CAS in the province is responsible for investigating concerns about children, such as neglect or abuse. When necessary, they seek out court orders for child-protection workers to monitor kids while they are cared for by their parents, or, when more extreme risks exist, in foster care.
The agencies are then responsible for deciding when they no longer need to supervise a child - and provincial standards say cases can only be closed when a case worker and their supervisor are satisfied there are no "current or imminent" safety threats to a child.
Among the 2015 audit's major findings: agencies "may be closing cases prematurely, risking the well-being of children."
That concern was raised after the office reviewed CAS cases that were reopened - and discovered nearly half had been shuttered despite ongoing child-safety concerns.
The very risk factors that prompted the agencies to step back in, they found, had been present when they deemed there were no more safety concerns and ended involvement.
On average, cases were reopened after just 68 days. In some cases, the society had to intervene again within a week of closure, the auditor's office found.
In that 2015 audit, Lysyk recommended agencies ensure all initial risk factors are appropriately addressed before case closure and that there be an annual review of all reopened cases.
But by 2020, in a followup on whether the societies had acted, the auditor's office found two CAS organizations had not taken action on premature case closures.
One of them was CAS Toronto.
CAS Toronto did not take action on the issue of premature case closures until 2022, according to the auditor's office - seven years after the audit. By then, the child-protection agency had implemented just 57 per cent of the overall recommendations from the 2015 audit.
"Fifty-seven per cent is not what we would call a passing grade at school," Spence said. "We'd hope for some progress. We do encourage the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services (MCCSS) to work with their organizations to figure out the barriers."
Neveah went missing after CAS Toronto requested supervision for her and her younger brother be ended. A judge made that order in March 2021. One day after the CAS supervision order ended, neither Neveah nor her brother showed up at daycare, as had been arranged. They would never attend. Neveah was last seen just months later, while in her mother's care, and police believe she died later that year.
Though CAS Toronto applied to reopen the case in 2023, it was already too late. Neveah's unidentified remains were found in a construction dumpster on Dale Avenue in 2022. It would take Toronto police investigators more than a year to discover who she was, using advanced DNA technology. To date, no one has been charged in her death, the cause of which remains undetermined.
Child-protection advocates have since questioned the decision to end CAS Toronto supervision of Neveah, knowing her mother had struggled with substance use and child-protection workers had identified previous concerns about neglect, unstable housing and inadequate supervision.
CAS Toronto did not directly respond to a question asking whether they'd addressed the premature case closure recommendation prior to Neveah going missing.
Though Spence said she couldn't comment on the specifics of Neveah's time in CAS care, she said it's important to understand why cases were being closed early.
Reached by the Star this month, Lysyk - who conducted the 2015 audit - said she believes the time for another CAS audit has come, since it's been nearly a decade since the last one and "implementation rates were low."
She noted, however, that it's the Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services that oversees child-protection agencies. She called on the ministry to work more closely with CAS agencies to ensure audit recommendations - and other improvements needed to protect vulnerable children - are "properly and fully actioned."
In a statement Friday, a spokesperson for the ministry said it expects child-welfare societies to meet provincial requirements, including child-protection standards, saying the "safety and well-being of every child is a priority."
CAS Toronto, in a response to questions about their progress on the audit recommendations, said they have followed up and reported back to the auditor but did not say why their progress was less than 60 per cent, nearly nine years later.
Though the audit didn't look specifically at child deaths while in or shortly following CAS supervision, Spence said it would be worthwhile to look at the connection, if any, between premature closure and child deaths.
Spence said she's aware of the planned "audit" the Ford government says is in the "early stages" but that her office is not involved. She said the province's standing committee of public accounts can request she investigate.
"If the standing committee wanted a special audit done, we would be more than happy to," Spence said, adding they will consider a new audit as part of their future work plan.
"Certainly it's on the radar ... in the coming years for a revisit."
CREDIT: Jennifer Pagliaro and Wendy Gillis Toronto Star

Document 194 of 500

Ford critics say he should buy back the 407: Premier's opponents say using existing highway would be an easier way to ease gridlock in GTA

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-critics-say-he-should-buy-back-407/docview/3110653082/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+critics+say+he+should+buy+back+the+407%3A+Premier%27s+opponents+say+using+existing+highway+would+be+an+easier+way+to+ease+gridlock+in+GTA&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's political opponents say a more prudent means for the province to relieve congestion on the 401 is to buy back the underused 407.
Leaders of the three other major political parties in Ontario agree it would be far cheaper, quicker and simpler to purchase the private toll road than what Ford proposed - digging what could be the world's largest tunnel beneath the world's busiest highway.
While the 18-lane behemoth that is the 401 is perpetually packed, the 407, which runs parallel to it, resorted to a promotional campaign earlier this year to entice drivers to take it.
Despite this, the Ford administration never considered buying the 407 before announcing the 401 tunnel proposal, a senior government source at Queen's Park told the Star.
The reason being that if population growth trends in Ontario hold, all of the 400-series highways in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area will hit or exceed capacity within the decade, according to the province's traffic modelling.
"The 407 will be at or over capacity in 10 years anyway," said the source, speaking privately on background to discuss internal deliberations.
Ford said as much on Wednesday in unveiling the tunnel plan.
"We can't just sit by and let this happen," he told reporters, noting Ontario's population increased by 800,000 last year.
The premier has made no secret that he is not a fan of the tolls motorists pay on 407, and has said former Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris made a wrong turn in 1999 when he decided to privatize the highway to raise money to lower his government's deficit.
But tolls can be tools to fight congestion, said Matti Siemiatycki, professor of geography and planning and director of the infrastructure institute at the University of Toronto.
"Tolls encourage people to use other modes of transportation," he said. "Or to carpool or to go during times when tolls are lower. That's what we've seen in other regions."
Had the 407 not been privatized in a 99-year lease for $3.1 billion, the initial cost of building the highway would have long ago been paid off and tolls eliminated. More recent estimates of 407's value have come in at around $30 billion or more.
Arguments in favour of buying the 407 over building the tunnel hinge on the former seeming like it would be significantly cheaper. But the province has not released a cost estimate for the dig and experts say they don't have enough information yet to make their own calculations.
Siemiatycki's best guess, at this stage, is "mega billions."
"We don't know the length, we don't know how many lanes we're talking about here, this is really early days," he said. "The premier also talked about building transit under there, so that's another huge cost."
The Star reached out to 407 ETR, the company that operates the highway, to ask whether it would sell to the province, and if so, for how much. It declined to answer.
"407 ETR is a private road operator and we're not involved in provincial transportation policy discussions beyond our responsibilities under the concession agreement," said Christina Basil, 407 ETR's vice-president of communications and government relations. "Shareholder transactions are commercially sensitive, and as such, we are unable to comment on valuations."
Either way, tunnel or 407 acquisition, you can't defeat congestion for good with more lanes, transit experts say. This is explained by the concept of induced demand. More space for cars encourages more drivers. If you build it, they will come.
University of Toronto professor Steven Farber, a transportation geographer and spatial analyst, has a more sophisticated solution to gridlock than either proposal.
Instead, Ontario needs to develop a "holistic strategy" for tolling the 400-series highways, he said.
Single-occupancy vehicles are the largest contributor to congestion, he said. A "modest investment" in high-occupancy vehicle lanes could reduce their concentration.
"I would also restrict truck use of the 401 during peak periods to encourage logistics companies to pay for 407 use out of pocket," said Farber.
Ontarians should give up on the idea that roadways will become less congested, he added. Instead, efforts should go toward making other forms of transportation more accessible.
"The best we can do is provide more people opportunities to use other modes so that they don't have to be part of the congestion," he said. "But that's easy for me. I live about five kilometres from my work and there are good transit options. We need to create those conditions for more people in more parts of the city."
With files from Rob Ferguson
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Ben Cohen Toronto Star

Subject: Transportation planning; Roads & highways; Cost estimates; Motorcycles; Politicians; Traffic congestion; Political parties

Business indexing term: Subject: Cost estimates

People: Ford, Doug

Company / organization: Name: University of Toronto; NAICS: 611310

Title: Ford critics say he should buy back the 407:   Premier's opponen ts say using existing highway would be an easier way to ease gridlock in GTA

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Document 195 of 500

How Justin Trudeau fumbled Canada's best-ever climate plan: The carbon pricing scheme is in tatters. Can the Liberals pivot before it's too late?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-justin-trudeau-fumbled-canadas-best-ever/docview/3110653006/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+Justin+Trudeau+fumbled+Canada%27s+best-ever+climate+plan%3A+The+carbon+pricing+scheme+is+in+tatters.+Can+the+Liberals+pivot+before+it%27s+too+late%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "When it starts to fall apart," Gord Downie once sang, "man, it really falls apart."
Canada's once-lauded carbon pricing program began falling apart soon after it was set up in 2019. Today, thanks to government incompetence and a relentless, bad-faith campaign to destroy it, it is really falling apart.
I've followed the promise of and backlash to the Liberals' deeply flawed, largely successful and seemingly doomed carbon pricing plan from the beginning. In recent months, I've spoken to architects of the regime, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, his first environment minister, Catherine McKenna, and his longtime right-hand man Gerald Butts about what went wrong and why.
What I heard was the story of a good policy undermined by a cynical conservative campaign, sold badly by the Trudeau government and eventually failed by a government that couldn't be bothered justifying one of its own marquee initiatives.
If nothing changes, the story will end in tragedy. "Globally, people will think we are bonkers" if the carbon pricing system dies, McKenna told me last week.
And they'd be right.
But it doesn't have to be that way. There are tangible ideas on the table now that could make the system more palatable to voters, while beefing up Canada's economy, lightening the load on taxpayers and still reducing Canada's carbon emissions. The only question is: Will Trudeau's government embrace them before it's too late?
Gerald Butts, Trudeau's former senior adviser, remembers offering a prescient warning in the early years of the Liberals' mandate.
The federal government was negotiating with the provinces on how to implement a national price on carbon. Most of the premiers around the table already had some version of the same policy in place, or were at least receptive to the idea, even the conservative-leaning ones.
But there was one naysayer: Saskatchewan premier Brad Wall. Butts pointed to Wall as the ghost of conservative future. "That's the base (the national party) will build back from," Butts told his colleagues. "So listen to what Brad Wall is saying."
Some in Trudeau's government were convinced they didn't need to listen. They were so buoyed by polls showing widespread support for climate action that they were ignoring the more sobering data underneath: Canadians wanted climate action, sure, but they were loath to pay more in taxes to make it happen.
McKenna says she knew all that going in. Carbon pricing "was never a political winner," she told me. Her mission was to make the plan politically palatable, yet she found herself facing others in cabinet who wanted to use the tax revenues to finance new projects. "I had to fight very, very hard internally to return the revenue (to taxpayers)," she says. It's a fight she won, but it was clear to her, even then, that some of her colleagues were wearing green-tinted glasses.
Parliament adopted the final version of the plan in 2018. Ottawa would set out baseline standards: A minimum price on gasoline paid by consumers at the pump; and a second price covering what industrial emitters belch out. The provinces could adopt their own systems, as long as they met those baselines. If they failed or refused, Ottawa would step in and install a carbon tax, returning the revenue directly to taxpayers.
The political winds began shifting even before the carbon price took effect. Wall's backup arrived via Alberta premier Jason Kenney and Ontario's Doug Ford: "The resistance," as Maclean's called them. They dismantled their predecessors' carbon pricing schemes, forcing Ottawa to impose the federal carbon tax, then marched into court to challenge it on constitutional grounds.
Still, the Liberals were optimistic. Most low-income and middle-class Canadians stood to actually earn money through the federal tax. But McKenna was still worried. She wanted to return the tax as a quarterly rebate, the "Climate Action Incentive," so Canadians would be clear that they were coming out ahead. But the Canada Revenue Agency nixed that idea. "We were told 'nope, I'm sorry,'" she says. Instead, the rebate was folded into Canadians' yearly tax returns.
"We needed to do a sales job on this," McKenna says. It didn't happen. "The government moved on." McKenna ultimately quit politics in 2021.
The Supreme Court finally rendered its verdict on the carbon pricing plan in 2021, deeming it constitutional and warning of "irreversible harm" to Canada if the federal government was blocked from fighting a threat as existential as climate change.
It turned out to be a pyrrhic victory. For Canada to meet its climate targets, the minimum prices established in the original plan had to keep rising, year after year. That was fine in a booming economy, but when inflation spiked post-COVID the public mood began to change. In 2022, things got even worse for the plan. When staff at the independent Parliamentary Budget Office crunched the numbers, they found a key Liberal selling point was no longer true. Most households under the federal carbon tax were going to pay more than they received in the rebate.
As the pandemic faded from view and the fiscal picture worsened, people from coast-to-coast-to-coast grew increasingly pissed. The anti-vaccine Freedom Convoy, originally born out of anti-carbon tax activism, descended on Ottawa in early 2022. Erin O'Toole, a soft environmentalist, was ousted in favour of the pugnacious Pierre Poilievre. In Alberta, Kenney was replaced with Danielle Smith - who bemoaned "Trudeau's anti-carbon-dioxide obsession" and warned that Ottawa planned to use the environment as an excuse to implement dystopian social controls.
Liberals today love to blame misinformation for derailing their climate plans. McKenna is more blunt: We got "outgunned," she said. As opposition grew, the Liberals only fuelled the idea that the price was partisan and political, when they exempted home heating oil in Liberal-red Atlantic Canada.
Despite all the noise, backlash and messaging missteps, though, one fact is often ignored: The carbon price worked.
Independent modelling shows Canada's CO2 emissions falling to 50-year lows. The Canadian Climate Institute's analysis suggests that, without carbon pricing and other measures, Canada's emissions would be up to 40 per cent higher than they are today. Canada is not quite on track to hit its climate targets, but it is nearly there.
At least for now. Poilievre is promising a "carbon tax election," and Canadians look set to endorse his plan to "axe the tax." NDP leader Jagmeet Singh hastily distanced himself from carbon pricing this month. Even eco-friendly B.C. may elect a climate skeptic.
I've been speaking to Liberal MPs who are exasperated that one of the party's signature policies, a real legacy initiative, is falling apart. "How are you f-king up something that people actually care about?" one MP asked. The fight isn't over, the same MP said. "There is a way to thread the needle."
There are good ideas on the table. They could start by taxing the $40 billion in annual profits made by oil and gas companies in Canada - the Parliamentary Budget Officer assessed a modest version of such a tax could raise $4 billion in revenue over five years.
That kind of money could allow the Liberals to switch from defence to offence. The Liberals allowed "the climate plan to be defined by its enemies as just the carbon tax," Butts told me. More money in the system could allow for a "national climate plan," styled after Joe Biden's Inflation Reduction Act. That could mean tax breaks, better carbon tax rebates, and a suite of green investment.
The Liberals could also consider a strategic retreat from the consumer carbon tax, instead making the supply-side industrial levy more stringent. The industrial carbon price has been the single most effective carbon-reduction policy, although there is room for improvement.
Ottawa could be even more bold. The European Union is running a massive, successful carbon market. Ditching the federal carbon tax and joining the European pricing scheme could come with huge benefits and efficiencies. "It's not a crazy idea!" Butts, now a vice-president at the Eurasia Group, assures me.
Even if it is, the time might be right for some crazy ideas.
Unfortunately, the Trudeau government looks like it does on so many files, paralyzed with indecision. When I spoke to the prime minister about this in March, he didn't seem compelled to change much. "People can vote the way they want to," he shrugged.
Right now, thanks to Trudeau's personal unpopularity, Canadians look set to vote for a party that will dismantle his most ambitious climate plans. If they do, the country will miss its climate targets by a mile.
McKenna is out of politics now, but she hasn't given up the fight. "I'm not going to stop defending carbon pricing because Pierre Poilievre lies about it, and the NDP are a joke," she said. "I can't help it if politicians are weak."
Justin Ling is an independent investigative journalist based in Montreal and a contributing columnist for the Star.
Reach him by email:
justinling@proton.me
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals today love to blame misinformation for derailing their climate plans, Justin Ling writes.Christinne Muschi The Canadian Press file photo

In praise of bike lanes Ford shoots down criticism of ...[Derived Headline]: Ford shoots down criticism of controversial plan to limit bike lanes, Sept. 23

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/praise-bike-lanes-ford-shoots-down-criticism/docview/3110652715/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+praise+of+bike+lanes+Ford+shoots+down+criticism+of+...%5BDerived+Headline%5D%3A+Ford+shoots+down+criticism+of+controversial+plan+to+limit+bike+lanes%2C+Sept.+23&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In praise
of bike lanes
Ford shoots down criticism of controversial plan to limit bike lanes, Sept. 23
The "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act," which would prohibit the construction of new bike lanes in the instance that they remove a vehicular traffic lane, is an unsustainable, dangerous and regressive idea put forth by Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservatives. Traffic will only get worse if unsustainable car-dependent tendencies are not addressed. GTA's continued reliance on cars is the cause of gridlock and congestion, not the city's newly implemented bike lanes. Toronto's bike lanes alleviate car traffic on the road and will lead to more sustainable transportation practices in the city long term.
Protected and separated bike lanes have also been shown to make transportation safer for all road users. A 2019 study published in the Journal of Transport & Health noted that 12 cities in the U.S. saw fatal crash rates decrease by as much as 75 per cent following an increase in the creation of cycling infrastructure. Toronto will not become safer if bike lane creation is hindered.
This new act is also extremely regressive in its ideology. Perhaps in the 1970s, the novelty of a personal automobile was still exciting and futuristic. But in 2024 it is paramount that Toronto and the province work to build diversified transportation infrastructure to ensure future generations get to experience the joys of mobility. The future of the GTA should be cycling and other forms of active transportation, not cars endlessly stuck in gridlock on Highway 401.
Olivia Wiper, Newmarket
We close curb lanes for months to accommodate an outdoor patio, and for years to accommodate construction of any kind of building. We close curb lanes all day long for parcel and takeout deliveries. A single car can close hundreds of feet of a curb lane by parking midblock, forcing everyone else to merge in the centre lane. We close big chunks of a lane when an inconsiderate driver wants to grab a coffee, or when they wait for someone to emerge from a building or just need to pull over to make a call. If Premier Doug Ford has his way, a bike lane will soon be the only thing you can't use a curb lane for.
Doug Earl, Toronto
As much as Premier Doug Ford tries to distance himself from the divisive antics of federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, he has obviously taken note of the effect of Poilievre's tactic of pitting groups against each other. In pandering to his car-driving base, and with no regard for climate change or the safety of vulnerable road users, Ford has chosen to pit those who pedal against those who guzzle gas, instead of improving and expanding public transit. Does he not realize that the cycling community can become a formidable lobby when poked?
Judith Butler, Toronto
Disdain for
bike lanes
As a resident of Woodbine Avenue, where bike lanes were imposed a couple of years ago, the most immediate impacts were twofold: first, as the major artery for people heading to Scarborough along Kingston Road, Gerrard Street East or O'Connor Drive, traffic jams on Woodbine grew exponentially; and second, hoping to escape the gridlock, motorists began turning off Woodbine onto the nearby residential streets. The city has even recognized the problem by altering some traffic rules, but it hasn't helped much. All of this chaos could possibly be justified if the bike lanes, which are cleared of snow in the winter before Woodbine is, were busy. But they're not. The vast majority of the time there are no bikes to be seen.
Claire Hoy, Toronto
No doubt downtown cycling advocates come from a good spot, and many of them, to their credit, are progressive and honestly advocate for the inclusion of all. However, they do not realize how exclusionary they sound to those who live in areas like Rexdale, Jane-Finch, Black Creek, Crescent Town, Thorncliffe Park or Scarborough. Those residents who live far from downtown do not welcome lectures about how bike lanes that hinder their driving are good for them, or how cars, and, more particularly, the people in them, need to have roadblocks put in their way.
Roman Botiuk, Etobicoke
I couldn't agree more with the province's position on bike lanes in Toronto. Do not add any more if it means reducing current vehicle lanes. I have witnessed first-hand the traffic on streets that have had vehicle lanes eliminated to add bike lanes. It's made it almost impossible for emergency vehicles to get where they need to go in a timely manner. Fire trucks, ambulances and police cars get stuck, with only a single lane of traffic in each direction, all the time.
Larry Proctor, Caledon East, Ont.

Document 197 of 500

After 40 years at the Star, it's finally time to say goodbye

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/after-40-years-at-star-finally-time-say-goodbye/docview/3110652655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+40+years+at+the+Star%2C+it%27s+finally+time+to+say+goodbye&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=L.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I remember when I was lying in bed as a child, listening to the radio and hearing the moving song, "Thanks for the Memories," sung by the late comedian Bob Hope.
Today, I'm feeling much deeper nostalgia for the remarkable 40 years of my lifetime career working for the Toronto Star.
Now, I'm retiring.
It's been fulsome and fantastic years of engaging with Toronto Star readers. It became a mutual relationship when I wrote my advice column for the last 23 of those years. Oddly enough, it was because the famous American-based advice columnist, Ann Landers, had died.
I'd read every column of hers in the Star since I was an early teen, fascinated by her readers' personal stories and Landers' insights.
After she passed on, I called the Star's then-publisher, John Honderich, and asked him, "What about a made-in-Canada relationship adviser?" He said "Lunch!" And the deal was done. I called it, simply, "Ellie." It was syndicated across Canada.
Honderich immediately launched a $1-million campaign to promote my new column. Billboards blasted my face on the sides and back of every bus in Toronto (like past billboard ads for the American TV Series "Sex in the City.")
I became the Star's relationship advice columnist, answering thousands of letters and emails over the years. I'm grateful for the loyalty and trust of every reader who sought my advice, even those who sometimes disagreed. I was learning all sides of people's personal situations.
So how did I land in the field of professional journalism?
Back when I was a young mother with two school-age children, I wrote freelance articles at home, sending them to magazines and newspapers. To my surprise, all were published.
Next came an unexpected phone call from the Star's then-managing editor, Mark Harrison, who invited me to join the newspaper staff as a reporter. It was the chance of a lifetime. I became a staff reporter writing daily news. Next, the Family Section editors put me in charge of the Food, Fashion and Home sections.
With the fashion season in full bloom, I was sent to cover the Paris "showings." While interviewing Karl Lagerfeld, the creative director for Chanel, about his latest fashion coup presenting "the maxi skirt," he called over an employee to deliver my skirt size.
I carefully said, "It's lovely." He said, "It's yours." I said, "I can't take it." He tried once more to give me the skirt, but I refused. That's when Lagerfeld, smiling, said, "The New York Times doesn't take freebies either."
It was a proud moment for me.
But Paris' excess was in full mode at the following after-party. The smoke-filled room boasted fashion models and celebrities alike. Suddenly, a woman pulled me to her lap. It was Iman, the Somalian-born beauty, married to the late singer-songwriter David Bowie for 24 years. We chatted briefly and I left.
During that same time, the Star boasted some fearless female reporters including the late, sadly missed Christie Blatchford, plus the remarkable Rosie DiManno, whose hard-hitting truths are still regularly delivered by her on page two, with the intellectual force of a wakeup punch!
Then I proudly became the Star's first-ever female Sunday Editor. It was a big title for a very popular weekend paper. I remained as the editor throughout the astonishing baseball World Series of both 1992 and 1993!
And those were only the early years of my working for the Toronto Star.
I was next assigned to the Star's investigative unit, writing feature stories ... and everything changed.
I consider my biggest, most significant story was about the Dionne quintuplets and how I became involved with one of Canada's most fascinating yet shocking tales.
They were the first five identical newborn girls, hailed as worldwide wonders ever to survive beyond a few days, especially as they were so tiny, and then exploited for years. Ontario's then-Liberal government recognized the babies' enormous political and economic value. They were placed under guardianship of four provincial appointees with three acts granting control until the quintuplets were age 21.
All other power grabs and exploitations soon became highly possible, including a specially built "tourist attraction" named Quintland, where the very young girls were displayed like caged animals in a zoo. Money from countless visiting American and world-travelling tourists poured into the government's "guardians," but not to the Quintuplets themselves.
Years later, I followed closely the pursuit by the surviving three adult quintuplets (Cecile, Annette and Yvonne, after the earlier deaths of Emilie and Marie), for fair compensation from their trust funds.
Extravagant expenses and dubious billing practices had bled the women's trusts by half. Finally, the three sisters, urged on by Cecile's son Bertrand, connected with a Montreal public relations adviser, who told me, "I was meeting a part of Canadian history."
I felt the same way.
I wrote daily Toronto Star accounts about the women's determined efforts to receive fair compensation from the province. An avalanche of 6,000 faxes and letters of support arrived at my newsroom desk.
Readers expressed their great empathy for the women and outrage over years of their being exploited.
I packed boxes with the many letters delivered to me, got help carrying them to Premier Mike Harris' office and handed them over to him.
Finally, after negotiations between two high-powered Toronto lawyers, the women received a total of $4 million in compensation. It was a joyous highlight of my time at the Star.
Soon after, some remarkably big investigative stories followed with journalists pursuing them, across the globe.
Luckily, I'd found access through a Toronto woman to the story of Raoul Wallenberg, a Swedish diplomat based in Budapest, Hungary, during the Second World War amid Nazi terror. At the neutral Swedish legation, he was implored for help by Jewish residents living in Hungary and hiding for their lives. Wallenberg designed a self-styled "protection passport" providing Swedish immigration permits which he handed out.
He personally sheltered people for safety in his own apartment, including a baby born there (maternity hospitals were closed to Jews). He'd protected the delivering mother by remaining outside his own door, holding a gun.
He named the baby Yvonne after one of his relatives and considered her his goddaughter. She grew up to attend a ceremony years later in 1997, along with Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of England. I accompanied Yvonne Singer to the unveiling of the Wallenberg Monument by the Queen, at Western Marble Arch Synagogue. It honoured Wallenberg's selfless bravery.
Meanwhile, my Toronto Star work-related connections were expanding. A Canadian-based TV show called "Outlaw In-Laws," started in 2007, hired me to be "the talent," guiding warring in-laws towards some mutual understanding.
It was long gruelling work, travelling across Ontario in the middle of winter with a TV crew. But it was rewarding, fun and a new adventure for me. And throughout, those TV scripts were a basis for helping families heal their differences.
Several years ago, the #MeToo period began, with people of all ages and backgrounds speaking out about having experienced sexual abuse. I asked readers to send me, anonymously, their own stories. Most were heartbreaking.
Older women who'd been raped when young, said they'd never fully recovered from it. One man disclosed when, as a boy, he'd been tied to a tree and abused by older boys. Their letters committed me firmly to my work.
Now, years later and having reached retirement, I'm delighted that my daughter Lisi has also chosen to do the important work of offering relationship advice ... in the tradition of the daughters of Ann Landers and her twin sister, Dear Abby.
Thanks to all my readers for sharing these memories with me. I loved the varied jobs and learned enormously from special colleagues who'd accepted a "newbie."
Thanks especially for the past 26 years since I met and married a colleague there and, while loving the newspaper, I've loved him too.
To work at something deeply meaningful is a gift.
I hope all who've read this, have also achieved personal satisfaction in life.
And that you still find and maintain it, whenever possible.
CAPTION: The Dionne siblings, five identical newborn girls, were the first quintuplet to survive beyond a few days. Hailed as worldwide wonders, the children were exploited for years.Ellie Tesher holds some of the hundreds of letters that arrived at the Star in support of the Dionne quintuplets, a story she worked on and considers the biggest of her career.EI Scan photo by Fred Davis from Helen DafoeSteve Russell Toronto star file photo

Document 198 of 500

Why is everything we do now wrong?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-is-everything-we-do-now-wrong/docview/3110652476/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+is+everything+we+do+now+wrong%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=C.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Please be aware: whatever you're doing, you're doing it wrong.
I was researching drones this summer and was struck by the number of expert posts about how doing this or that was wrong. You're using the RC all wrong. You're changing the blades all wrong. Takeoff is wrong. Landing is wrong.
It freaked me out. So now I'm sticking with paper planes.
Cutting an avocado? Congratulations, you're doing it wrong. Vacuuming? You're doing it wrong. Laundry? You're doing it wrong. Taking supplements? You're doing it wrong. Arts and crafts? Wrong and wrong. Negotiating a raise? You're doing it wrong. Charging your phone? You're doing it wrong. Scrubbing grout? Your technique is so hilariously wrong.
Building a tunnel under the 401? You're definitely doing it wrong. Look, I'm all for easing traffic congestion. Our commuting times couldn't be longer if the streets were coated with molasses. But if a city needs six weeks to fill a pothole, eight months to fix a bridge and 13 years (and counting) to construct an LRT line, excavating a subterranean motorway from Brampton to Scarborough will take about 900 years.
Premier, put down the imaginary shovel. If you can't fortify the Science Centre, the world's longest tunnel is the world's biggest pipe-dream. Supersonic flying buses driven by robot monkeys are more feasible. Teleportation will exist long before that tunnel opens.
Doug Ford, you're doing it wrong. Not that you should take offence.
According to the fastest growing segment of service journalism, it seems we are all doing everything wrong all the time. Culture has turned efficiency and perfectionism into twin virtues and now the internet is chockablock with glib scolds who are keen to point out why your pillow fluffing is all wrong.
I assume you're breathing right now? You're doing it wrong. Use your nose and engage your diaphragm. Inhale. Exhale. Pull out your hair.
Last year, WebMD noted, "10 Ways You're Drinking Water Wrong." Too cold. Gulping too fast. Drinking out of a stream (?). When my daughters were young, they loved when I made them "spa water." All I did was chuck a few lemon and cucumber slices into the pitcher and stir dramatically. Their eyes got big. It was like I was doing a magic trick.
Unfortunately, I was also increasing their exposure to salmonella and E. coli.
Now I know why Drake wrote a song called, "Doing It Wrong."
A story this week noted how we are pouring champagne wrong. You shouldn't be using a flute. A universal wine glass is a better bubbly vessel. Luckily, I work at a newspaper and can't afford champagne. But I was horrified by another emphatic headline: "How To Make a Dirty Martini - You're Doing It Wrong All Wrong."
You know what else I've doing wrong? Tying my shoes. Yeah. My parents taught me to make two loops, tuck one under the other and pull. Wrong. They didn't teach me any Lifehacker methods, including "Bunny Ears," "Big Loops," "Cheerio" and "Ian knot." Once at Home Depot, my laces came undone and I suddenly lurched toward a circular saw display.
Mom? Dad?Doing it wrongcould have slashed my throat.
The Doing It Wrong Movement is now criss-crossing the globe. In India, they are cleaning air conditioners wrong. In Sweden, they are making coffee wrong. In Japan, they are making eye contact wrong. In Singapore, the men are having sex wrong. In Ireland, they are getting into the back of cabs wrong.
And you don't want to know how wrong you are while peeling a banana.
So today I raise an anti-wrong fist and shout, "Enough!"
I am taking a stand against the Do Righters. I don't have time to worry about optimized posture. Or the correct way to squeegee a windshield. I'm scraping off the bird poop and beating rush hour. Related, I try to stay clear of the scatological, but we are also wiping wrong?
What's that, Popular Science? I'm mowing my lawn wrong? I should be following the "One-Third Rule" - whatever that is - and be aware if the grass variant is Bermuda, Zoysia or Fescue? Listen to me. You lunatics are now engaged in unpopular science.
You want to help me out? Send over an intern to mow my lawn.
You do you, Do Righters. Leave the rest of us alone. I don't care if there is a more efficient way to pack a suitcase when I'm running late. Enough with the shaming. Enough with the annoying advice. If it works, it's fine. Am I brushing my teeth too aggressively and risking gum damage? Possibly. But my mouth feels minty fresh, so go suck a lemon out of the spa water and think about what might happen when your engine conks out in that 401 tunnel.
But you're showering wrong! You're stretching wrong! You're enunciating wrong!
Fine, how's this: Shut. Up. Already.
CAPTION: Building a tunnel under the 401? You're definitely doing it wrong. If a city needs six weeks to fill a pothole, excavating a subterranean motorway from Brampton to Scarborough will take about 900 years, writes Vinay Menon.R.J. Johnston Toronto Star file photo

Some good can come from conflict: On U of T's campus everyone is shouting and no one is being heard. I'm the hopeless optimist trying to fix it, Sept. 21 I've watched us descend into conflicting realities - here's how we get out, Sept. 19 The acrimony between Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Jagmeet Singh is on full display as return to Parliament looms, Sept. 11 But readers warn against debates devolving into the name calling and hostility seen right now in Parliament

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/some-good-can-come-conflict/docview/3110652441/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+good+can+come+from+conflict%3A+On+U+of+T%27s+campus+everyone+is+shouting+and+no+one+is+being+heard.+I%27m+the+hopeless+optimist+trying+to+fix+it%2C+Sept.+21+I%27ve+watched+us+descend+into+conflicting+realities+-+here%27s+how+we+get+out%2C+Sept.+19+The+acrimony+between+Justin+Trudeau%2C+Pierre+Poilievre+and+Jagmeet+Singh+is+on+full+display+as+return+to+Parliament+looms%2C+Sept.+11+But+readers+warn+against+debates+devolving+into+the+name+calling+and+hostility+seen+right+now+in+Parliament&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=OP.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On U of T's campus everyone is shouting and no one is being heard. I'm the hopeless optimist trying to fix it, Sept. 21
In assessing a discourse between opposing parties, civil or otherwise, it is important to understand whether they wish to change the views of the other side or influence the views of a third party such as the university president or governing council. In the debate over Hamas' holding hostages and Israel's invasion of Gaza, my sense is that neither side has any possibility of changing the views of the other side. This differs from many debates in universities where discussion can cause the other side to refine, clarify or even modify its position. On this topic perhaps the best that can be hoped for is to prevent each side from making insults or ad hominem attacks against other groups of students.
Bruce Couchman, Ottawa
Conflict is unavoidable; however, much good has come of it. Think of the civil rights movement. Good conflict is fuelled by respect for one's opponent. It understands and holds complexity and allows for dialogue to learn from one another. On the other hand, high conflict is driven by "us" versus "them," devolving into stereotypes and dehumanization. It is seductive, exhausting, dangerous and very difficult to stop despite a desire to do so. I agree, we all must push for better.
Cheryl McNamara, Toronto
I've watched us descend into conflicting realities - here's how we get out, Sept. 19
Writer Carol Off poses the question, "how does the news media regain the attention and trust of the public?" One way is fact-checking politicians. During the Harris/Trump debate, there was simultaneous fact-check of Trump's statements and immediate feedback about his sensational claims. We need that more often. The public is distrustful when the whole truth is hidden. How much money was really spent by taxpayers in speeding up Premier Doug Ford's beer plan or the deals surrounding the Greenbelt? Why does it take so long for the RCMP to report on that situation? It takes the deeper digging that journalists do to bring to light the backroom "deals" made by politicians. No wonder Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre wants to get rid of the CBC. He fears journalists like Off as they challenge the truth of his claims.
Diane Sullivan, Toronto
The acrimony between Justin Trudeau, Pierre Poilievre and Jagmeet Singh is on full display as return to Parliament looms, Sept. 11
I am so tired of the bullying, name-calling and finger pointing nonsense that seems to be happening among our elected leaders. They all look and sound so ridiculous, not to mention very foolish. It is as though they are putting on a performance in a reality show and are forgetting that they have been elected to actually do a job. Please, for the sake of our country, can elected officials all focus on the horrific issues that Canadians are facing today?
Melanie Kohek, Whitby, Ont.
