Document 254 of 500

Doug Ford is slapping Toronto in the face, again. But this time he's also shooting himself in the foot

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-slapping-toronto-face-again-this/docview/3108516340/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+slapping+Toronto+in+the+face%2C+again.+But+this+time+he%27s+also+shooting+himself+in+the+foot&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Doug Ford government is reportedly planning to introduce legislation that would ban municipalities from building new bicycle lanes that reduce the space for motor vehicles.
To which one must say: Sigh. It's a shame the Premier doesn't have some developer friends who engaged in a bit of bike lane speculation.
In truth, even that probably wouldn't have mattered. If there is one north star that has guided the Premier in his policy-making, it is an absolute devotion to the automobile. Road tolls removed. Licence-plate fees waived. Highways widened.
Four wheels good, two wheels bad.
And yet, even amid Ford's relentlessly pro-car agenda, this is something else. Leaving aside the heavy-handedness of Queen's Park dictating to municipalities what they are allowed to do with their own road infrastructure, as though the Premier's Office knows better than the elected officials of towns and cities that each have unique travel and commuting patterns, a ban on new bike lanes will literally have the opposite of the desired effect.
Bike lanes do not increase traffic congestion, they reduce it. Full stop. That is, and it feels kind of obvious to say it, the point.
It's not as though there isn't real-word evidence of this. Major cities that have committed to bike-lane expansion - Paris, London, Madrid, Seoul - have reduced vehicular traffic over time, for the dead simple reason that more humans can fit on a bike lane than in a lane of traffic while in their cars. Some studies estimate that 10 times as many people can commute on a protected bike lane as compared to a typical lane of motor-vehicle traffic, since the majority of those vehicles will have a single occupant - the least efficient mode of travel in terms of sharing the road.
That's not to say there won't be complaints, as it takes time for commuting behaviours to change after bike lanes are implemented. Motorists grumble about the reduced space for their unnecessarily large vehicles, bringing negative headlines. This seems to be basically what the Premier is responding to: traffic congestion in Toronto is bad, and also some bike lanes were implemented, so therefore bike lanes must be to blame.
But just because a certain segment of the Tory base thinks bikes are too woke doesn't mean they are the problem. This is government by vibes.
Ford could look, for example, to the east, where Montreal has been expanding dedicated bike lanes for years now in an effort to reduce vehicular traffic on the island. It has worked as designed. In one example, the installation of protected bike lanes along historic Saint-Denis Street has helped transform it from a thoroughfare for commuting motorists into something closer to its original purpose as a commercial centre. Vehicular traffic is down, bike trips have skyrocketed, pedestrian traffic is up and the commercial occupancy rate has steadily increased.
And again, this isn't some kind of weird one-off. Studies around the world have shown that cyclists and pedestrians are more likely to stop and shop than just-passing-through drivers. This seems like a pretty good outcome for Premier Open for Business.
Achieving those kinds of results, by transforming cities away from car dependency, of course takes time and patience. It's much easier to grouse about bikes taking away space from those precious cars, and politically expedient because more people drive than cycle.
But you know what encourages people to switch from car to bike? Better bicycle lanes, especially those that are protected from vehicular traffic. Having to hug the shoulder of a road or threading the space between moving vehicles on one side and parked cars on the other, can be uncomfortable and dangerous. Separating riders from cars has had a transformative effect wherever it is tried: it's much safer, it moves people out of cars and on to bikes, which is better for public health and the environment.
Despite all that, this feels like one of those Ford initiatives that is impervious to facts. He's heard people complain about bike lanes, so he has concerns about bike lanes. The motorists are unhappy? Can't have that. And so the blame for congestion will fall on the bike lanes that haven't even been built yet.
Passing a law to prohibit them, after all, won't cost a thing. Except in the long run.
CAPTION: The Ford government wants to stop construction of city bike lanes that take space away from cars.
CREDIT: Scott Stinson, Contributor

Subject: Automobiles; Bans; Commuting; Bicycles; Automobile drivers; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Bicycling

Title: Doug Ford is slapping Toronto in the face, again. But this time he's also shooting himself in the foot

Document 255 of 500

Electoral ballots aren't a joke. Pranking makes our democracy less credible, not more

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/electoral-ballots-arent-joke-pranking-makes-our/docview/3108516219/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Electoral+ballots+aren%27t+a+joke.+Pranking+makes+our+democracy+less+credible%2C+not+more&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When did election ballots become a staging ground for political stunts?In two of the three recent federal by-elections - in Toronto, Winnipeg and Montreal - self-styled electoral system reformers figured out how to load up the actual ballot with dozens of names of unaffiliated candidates to produce outsized and difficult-to-use ballots. These need to be laboriously hand-counted, meaning that results come in extraordinarily late by Canadian election standards.Not to be confused with the joke parties of the past, this tactic depends on large numbers of people registering as independent candidates, with neither the desire nor the chance to win, in order to make a point about why Canada's election system needs an overhaul.The organizers, who call themselves the Longest Ballot Committee and have not revealed their identities publicly, declared victory after the second such race: "We can say without a doubt our campaign was a success because we had so much fun and sparked important conversations about our shared democracy," they said in a statement reported by the CBC.Their campaign is about pressuring the federal government to enact the ranked ballot reforms promised and then dismissed by Justin Trudeau's Liberals in 2015. But finding a way to make the actual ballot cumbersome and confusing risks infecting Canada's electoral system with the cynicism that has disabled its U.S. counterpart. Indeed, one has to ask why is it a good idea to confuse voters in the ballot box, a place that should be literally beyond the reach of politics?There's a strand of populist ideology that elections should be as open as possible. But it doesn't follow that election administration should be vulnerable to an activity that makes a mockery of the voting process and renders the task of determining a winner exceedingly difficult. I don't buy the argument that Canada's first-past-the-post system is hopelessly tainted because the winners don't necessarily represent the majority of votes cast. The entire history of post-war Canada, which is what we're talking about, can't simply be dismissed as an eight-decade-long disaster. What's more, no electoral system is ideal; even those that seem to be the most democratic - e.g., proportional representation - are vulnerable to abuse. Just look at Israel's fatally-fractured parliament, where tiny extremist parties control the balance of power. It's also important to acknowledge that different Canadian jurisdictions have very different rules about how individuals can enlist as candidates.At the federal level, a prospective candidate must collect 100 signatures of electors, which can now be done very easily using electronic forms. The refundable deposit used to be $1,000, but Alberta's court of appeal struck down that requirement in 2017, deeming it unconstitutional.Deposits, however, still exist in provincial and municipal elections.In Ontario, candidates for the legislature, as well as municipal councils and school boards in jurisdictions with over 4,000 residents, have to find 25 signatures for their nomination papers and pay a $200 deposit. B.C. requires 75 signatures and a $250 deposit. Alberta, in turn, wants 25 signatures and a $500 deposit.Which of these represents the most optimally democratic approach? The one with the least required names? The one with no deposit? There is no right answer, because these rules are obviously arbitrary.What we do now know is that it's become easy to punk the federal races and there's every reason to think these tactics will spread. After the past week's by-elections, Canada's chief electoral officer Stéphane Perrault said he'll look at tightening the rules, citing the difficulties that voters with accessibility issues experienced when presented with metre-long ballots containing dozens of names.I'm glad Perrault is advocating on behalf of a marginalized group that came face-to-face with the potential disenfranchisement posed by Longest Ballot's stunt. But I'd argue that Elections Canada can broaden its scope of concern to everyone who wants to cast a vote without worrying that some anonymous gadfly group is having fun at their expense.If the Longest Balloteers think there's appetite for reform, let them seek a space within the parties and then expend some energy making their case. But leave the election process alone. After all, we know by now what happens when people start to sow doubt about a system that demands air-tight credibility to remain legitimate.
CAPTION: Voters arrive to cast their ballot in the federal byelection for the riding of Lasalle-Emard-Verdun, in Montreal, Monday, Sept. 16, 2024.
CREDIT: John Lorinc, Contributor

Subject: Local elections; Election results; Candidates; Democracy; Voters; Special elections

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Title: Electoral ballots aren't a joke. Pranking makes our democracy less credible, not more

Document 256 of 500

Student field trip to protest 'disgusting,' says Doug Ford, promising those responsible will be 'held accountable'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/student-field-trip-protest-disgusting-says-doug/docview/3108515889/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Student+field+trip+to+protest+%27disgusting%2C%27+says+Doug+Ford%2C+promising+those+responsible+will+be+%27held+accountable%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Taking students to a rally intended to be about Indigenous rights but which included pro-Palestinian protesters is "disgraceful" and "disgusting," said Premier Doug Ford, promising those behind it will be "held accountable."
It's a controversy the Toronto District School Board will be dealing with at a special board meeting Wednesday evening, where trustees can discuss what went wrong and who's responsible.
"It's unacceptable," Ford said when asked about the incident, at an unrelated event in Cobourg on Monday afternoon.
"There needs to be an investigation, and we'll be all over this and make sure people are held accountable. They (need to) think twice about bringing young, little kids - without the parents' permission - on these school trips. It's disgusting."
Last Wednesday, students from about 15 TDSB schools - a mix of elementary, middle and secondary - went on a field trip to the Grassy Narrows River Run, billed as a community event supporting the Grassy Narrows First Nation and their efforts to address mercury contamination. It began at Grange Park and then protesters marched to the Ontario Legislature.
Parents were told it was an educational opportunity for students to observe speakers, and that they wouldn't participate in the rally. But videos posted to social media showed kids marching and chanting, "From Turtle Island to Palestine, occupation is a crime," which sparked public outcry.
The board has since apologized "for the harm that some students may have experienced," saying it's taking concerns "very seriously" and prioritizing an investigation into the matter.
The premier said students "should be in the classroom learning about reading, writing, spelling, arithmetic - the whole shebang," but instead, the TDSB and teachers "want to bring them down to a rally, a Palestinian rally, and it's ridiculous."
"I've said this is indoctrination for years," he added, blaming a small minority of "bad actors."
"Number one, you shouldn't be teaching our kids," the premier added. "But number two, stick with your knitting - stick with teaching the kids the geography, history, math, spelling, that's what they need."
In a statement to the Star, Education Minister Jill Dunlop said she was concerned that student safety was put at risk.
"Our government firmly believes that publicly funded schools and activities should never be used as vehicles for political protests. This principle was clearly violated last week when several activist teachers took groups of young children, on a TDSB-sanctioned field trip, to a demonstration. This action compromised the safety of our children and violated the trust of parents," she said.
"Some teachers, along with others associated with this protest, have a history of promoting inflammatory, discriminatory and hateful content. There is no place for this type of teaching in our classrooms. I expect the TDSB's investigation to be thorough, transparent and to conclude as quickly as possible."
Wednesday's special board meeting will also discuss how to mitigate any harm that was caused and if policies about field trips need to be changed.
In her weekly email update to constituents, Trustee Shelley Laskin said she became "deeply upset and angered" by what she saw on social media and in emails from parents who expressed "serious concerns" they weren't fully informed about what the trip would entail.
"Reports of what children experienced before, during and after the River Run are deeply disturbing," she wrote. Among them, she notes, were an eight-year-old child who came home with a sticker that read "Zionism Kills" and Jewish and other students who expressed discomfort at hearing antisemitic slogans being told they would "get over it."
"Somehow a trip to learn what happened at Grassy Narrows and the environmental impact on the Indigenous people who live there turned into a situation where there was Pro-Palestinian chanting and antisemitic rhetoric. How did this situation happen?" she asked.
The Star has not been able to independently verify these reports and the TDSB says it's still investigating.
Officials at the board, and province, have been receiving emails of frustration from the public, citing concerns about both antisemitism and anti-Palestinian racism.
Tamara Gottlieb, of Jewish Educators and Families Association of Canada, told the Star that parents raised concerns with school administrators before the trip because they wanted details about the speakers and felt uneasy about children attending a political protest that could get unruly.
She said parents were assured that speakers would stick to the issue of mercury contamination and students would only be observing, not participating, in the protest.
"This is really a collapse of confidence in the TDSB," says Gottlieb, who is calling on both the province and the Ontario College of Teachers to investigate.
In a statement to the Star, Zaid Zawaideh, founder of Toronto Palestinian Families, called the board's apology "yet another example of systemic anti-Palestinian racism."
"It is deeply concerning that the TDSB issued an apology for a field trip to the Grassy Narrows River Run ... Palestinians, who know full well the impact of settler colonialism, have the right to be there, just as any other community does."
When contacted by the Star, the OCT, which regulates the teaching profession, said it's unable to comment on any individual complaints or investigations.
CAPTION: Thousands participated in the protest Wednesday about the decades-long mercury poisonings in the Grassy Narrows community.

Document 257 of 500

Bike lane ban will make traffic worse

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bike-lane-ban-will-make-traffic-worse/docview/3108515854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bike+lane+ban+will+make+traffic+worse&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Doug Ford government is reportedly planning to introduce legislation that would ban municipalities from building new bicycle lanes that reduce the space for motor vehicles.
To which one must say: Sigh. It's a shame the premier doesn't have some developer friends who engaged in a bit of bike lane speculation.
In truth, even that probably wouldn't have mattered. If there is one north star that has guided the premier in his policy-making, it is an absolute devotion to the automobile. Road tolls removed. Licence-plate fees waived. Highways widened.
Four wheels good, two wheels bad.
And yet, even amid Ford's relentlessly pro-car agenda, this is something else. Leaving aside the heavy-handedness of Queen's Park dictating to municipalities what they are allowed to do with their own road infrastructure, as though the Premier's Office knows better than the elected officials of towns and cities that each have unique travel and commuting patterns, a ban on new bike lanes will literally have the opposite of the desired effect.
Bike lanes do not increase traffic congestion, they reduce it. Full stop. That is, and it feels kind of obvious to say it, the point.
It's not as though there isn't real-word evidence of this. Major cities that have committed to bike-lane expansion - Paris, London, Madrid, Seoul - have reduced vehicular traffic over time, for the dead simple reason that more humans can fit on a bike lane than in a lane of traffic while in their cars. Some studies estimate that 10 times as many people can commute on a protected bike lane as compared to a typical lane of motor-vehicle traffic, since the majority of those vehicles will have a single occupant - the least efficient mode of travel in terms of sharing the road.
That's not to say there won't be complaints, as it takes time for commuting behaviours to change after bike lanes are implemented. Motorists grumble about the reduced space for their unnecessarily large vehicles, bringing negative headlines. This seems to be basically what the premier is responding to: traffic congestion in Toronto is bad, and also some bike lanes were implemented, so therefore bike lanes must be to blame.
But just because a certain segment of the Tory base thinks bikes are too woke doesn't mean they are the problem. This is government by vibes.
Ford could look, for example, to the east, where Montreal has been expanding dedicated bike lanes for years now in an effort to reduce vehicular traffic on the island. It has worked as designed. In one example, the installation of protected bike lanes along historic Saint-Denis Street has helped transform it from a thoroughfare for commuting motorists into something closer to its original purpose as a commercial centre. Vehicular traffic is down, bike trips have skyrocketed, pedestrian traffic is up and the commercial occupancy rate has steadily increased.
And again, this isn't some kind of weird one-off. Studies around the world have shown that cyclists and pedestrians are more likely to stop and shop than just-passing-through drivers. This seems like a pretty good outcome for Premier Open for Business.
Achieving those kinds of results, by transforming cities away from car dependency, of course takes time and patience. It's much easier to grouse about bikes taking away space from those precious cars, and politically expedient because more people drive than cycle.
But you know what encourages people to switch from car to bike? Better bicycle lanes, especially those that are protected from vehicular traffic. Having to hug the shoulder of a road or threading the space between moving vehicles on one side and parked cars on the other, can be uncomfortable and dangerous. Separating riders from cars has had a transformative effect wherever it is tried: it's much safer, it moves people out of cars and on to bikes, which is better for public health and the environment.
Despite all that, this feels like one of those Ford initiatives that is impervious to facts. He's heard people complain about bike lanes, so he has concerns about bike lanes. The motorists are unhappy? Can't have that. And so the blame for congestion will fall on the bike lanes that haven't even been built yet.
Passing a law to prohibit them, after all, won't cost a thing. Except in the long run.
Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.
CAPTION: Just because a certain segment of the Tory base thinks bikes are too woke doesn't mean they are the problem, Scott Stinson writes.Steve Russell Toronto Star
CREDIT: Scott Stinson Contributor Scott Stinson is a journalist based in suburban Toronto.;

Subject: Automobiles; Bans; Commuting; Bicycles; Automobile drivers; Traffic congestion; Roads & highways; Bicycling; Premier-Canada; Legislation

Document 258 of 500

Ford defends controversial plan to limit bike lanes: MPP says premier putting 'wedge politics over real solutions'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-defends-controversial-plan-limit-bike-lanes/docview/3108515851/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+defends+controversial+plan+to+limit+bike+lanes%3A+MPP+says+premier+putting+%27wedge+politics+over+real+solutions%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is defending his government's push to limit bike lanes in Ontario municipalities, saying cars and trucks should have full priority on major roads like Bloor Street and University Avenue.
"We just want to get traffic moving ... that's what it comes down to, making sure you aren't putting bike lanes in the middle of some of the busiest streets in the country," Ford told reporters Monday.
"Put them on secondary roads."
The controversial plan - which government sources have confirmed is in upcoming legislation titled the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act" - would block construction of new bike lanes if they result in the loss of other lanes to cars, buses, trucks and other traffic.
It quickly sparked concern when details leaked last week. Six cyclists have been killed on Toronto streets this year.
"This is politics that plays with people's lives," said New Democrat MPP Joel Harden (Ottawa Centre), his party's transportation critic.
"This is putting wedge politics over real solutions."
University of Toronto geography and planning professor Matti Siemiatycki called the plan to limit bike lanes "ineffective and dangerous" as people increasingly take to bicycles to get around.
Toronto city council recently voted in favour of adding 100 kilometres of bike lanes to major routes within three years to reduce vehicle emissions and combat climate change.
But Ford was not backing down during a water and sewer funding announcement for a new housing development in Cobourg, an hour's drive east of Toronto.
He bristled at notions that removing motor vehicle lanes in favour of bike lanes doesn't increase congestion.
"That's a bunch of hogwash," Ford said, insisting the reduction of lanes on University Avenue's "hospital row" south of College Street to make way for bike lanes has made that stretch "jammed like crazy."
He also cited Bloor Street, claiming bike lanes have "disrupted all the businesses there."
"We need to focus on transportation routes.
"They get people ... from point A to point B."
Ford evaded a question about why he repeatedly says municipalities "know best" where housing is needed as the province battles a shortage of affordable new homes, but now wants to override municipal powers on where bike lanes should be located.
Critics said there is an inconsistency there as the premier apparently looks to court support from motorists and suburbanites as he mulls whether to call an early election next spring instead of waiting until the next slated provincewide vote in June 2026.
"Nobody asked for the government to dictate people's transportation choices or get in the way of local communities doing what works best for them," said Harden.
Ford was criticized for meddling in municipal affairs shortly after he was elected premier in 2018 and passed legislation cutting the size of Toronto city council nearly in half during the city's election campaign.
An Etobicoke resident, he has frequently slammed the expansion of bike lanes in Toronto. City staff have found bike ridership increases when new protected bike lanes are installed.
The premier also sounded off Monday about environmental assessments for Highway 413, which would link Highway 401 at Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan.
"Let's build the damn highway. There's hundreds of thousands of people stuck in our cars backed up from here to Timbuktu, and you're worried about a grasshopper jumping across the highway."
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson and Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 259 of 500

The new Bonnie Crombie sounds like the old Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-bonnie-crombie-sounds-like-old-doug-ford/docview/3108515843/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+new+Bonnie+Crombie+sounds+like+the+old+Doug+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Liberals had billed it as a weekend policy conference to brainstorm for a few good ideas on good governance.
But when Bonnie Crombie finally took the podium, she skipped the (policy) platform. Pondering her path to power, Crombie chose a shortcut.
No policy bible, no fine print, no road map to reviving and revivifying the moribund Liberals. While it's still early days, the party leader detoured from any details for the 1,000 party delegates meeting in London.
Henceforth, her provincial Liberals will build their campaign all around Doug Ford. And downplay their ties to Justin Trudeau's unpopular federal Liberals.
To be clear, she won't just focus on Ford's faults. Crombie's Liberals are also stealing the premier's proven playbook - battle-tested in two winning elections - and turning it against his governing Tories with a vengeance.
Remember Ford's winning campaign in 2018, when he badmouthed Kathleen Wynne's Liberals as corrupt parasites allegedly enriching their "insider" friends? Remember the attacks against "hallway health care" and the cheesy slogans promising "buck a beer?"
Now, Crombie wants to flip Ford's script, by turning his own words against him. If you can't beat them, borrow their sloganeering.
Which is why the new Crombie sounds increasingly like the old Ford, circa 2018. Policy light, rhetoric heavy.
At her final pep rally Saturday evening, the culmination of all those hours of deep thought by the party faithful, there was barely a peep about policy. Just chapter headings on health care and education, with a promise of more to come - hopefully before Ford springs an early election on the opposition parties next spring.
"So how will we do all of this?" she asked rhetorically, before retreating from her own challenge.
"We start by doing less for the rich insiders - the people who are getting ahead while everyone else is falling behind. Less for Doug's buddies. Less for the people who are under criminal investigation."
She complained about "hospital hallways" that are crammed, echoing Ford's old complaints. And then Crombie delivered her carefully prepared punch line, that she hopes will shake up Ontario politics:
"We are going to do less for them, so we can do more for you."
In case you missed it, that phrase, first heard at London's convention centre, will resound and redound across the province if the Liberals have their way. On cue, as Crombie led the chant, her followers shouted it out:
"Less for them. More for you."
As slogans go, it's not merely clunky but cheesy.
It encapsulates both the politics of envy and the pocketbook politics of our era.
It frames politics as entirely transactional, not transformational. It is anti-tax and all about affordability.
Will it sell?
To be sure, people are feeling besieged by economic disparities. But the solutions are not as simple as politicians pretend.
Affordability and tax-cutting won't pay the bills. Sustainability and efficiency are part of the equation.
Without sustainable tax revenues, you can't sustain the education and health services that people count on, and that public sector workers bargain on. If you can't drive efficiencies in an $85-billion health-care budget (without reflexively invoking privatization sloganeering and scaremongering), you won't be able to fix overflowing hospital hallways.
On the environment, Crombie says her provincial Liberals want nothing to do with any carbon tax. But she hasn't even hinted at her own alternative, beyond saying she wants to "protect our planet."
For his part, Ford never presented any serious policies in his last two campaigns, other than his perennial "axe the tax" attack on any federal carbon levy. Nor has NDP Leader Marit Stiles come forth with serious new policies since taking over her party.
Meanwhile, the Tories are spending big money on ads that frame and defame Crombie as a posh and pampered politician who is out of touch with ordinary Ontarians. Now, the Liberal leader is recasting Ford as the real rich kid who grew up with a silver spoon in his father's Etobicoke "mansion," heir to a family firm and political dynasty, while she is the hard scrabble survivor who grew up in a "rooming house" raised by a single mother.
Will this battle of personal narratives speak to voters? Is it a substitute for the detailed policy wars of the past, when all three major parties offered substantive platforms instead of placeholders for personal brands?
Voters hungry for substance will have to content themselves with a content-free pitch from Crombie (and Stiles), each insisting that corruption has insinuated itself into every sinew of Ford's being. The challenge is that Ford remains far ahead in the polls and keeps winning byelections, most recently by promising to "Get it done!"
Can Crombie outdo Ford in slogans? That depends on how - or whether - she ever answers her own question in her weekend speech:
"How will we do all of this?"
Good question.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at the policy convention in London on Saturday. Crombie is stealing Premier Doug Ford's proven playbook and turning it against his governing Tories with a vengeance, Martin Regg Cohn writes.SophieBouquillonfor thetoronto star
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

To change a tire? The Ford government wants your opinion, Sept. 9 Poilievre poisoned Canadians against the most effective tool we have for fighting climate change, Sept. 17

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3108515685/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ford+government+to+restrict+new+bike+lanes+in+bid+to+ease+gridlock%2C+Sept.+20+The+Rogers+way+suggests+no+Toronto+team+president+is+safe%3A+Feschuk%2C+Sept.+21+Should+Ontario+students+learn+how+to+sew%3F+To+change+a+tire%3F+The+Ford+government+wants+your+opinion%2C+Sept.+9+Poilievre+poisoned+Canadians+against+the+most+effective+tool+we+have+for+fighting+climate+change%2C+Sept.+17&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fighting the
'war on cars'
Ford government to restrict new bike lanes in bid to ease gridlock, Sept. 20
Premier Doug Ford has done nothing more than reveal his general lack of knowledge or understanding and his bias toward prioritizing motorist convenience over vulnerable road user safety. Any infrastructure which prioritizes motorist convenience over vulnerable user safety should be held as a crime against the citizenry. I'm willing to bet that infrastructure design always plays a significant and critical role in the death of a pedestrian or cyclist.
Peter Lehman, Waterloo, Ont.
So, Premier Doug Ford wants to forbid cities to build bike lanes if they take away a lane of car traffic. No surprise! Former mayor Rob Ford, his brother, insisted there was a "war on cars." The Fords don't care about people who don't own cars. Does Doug Ford think that cyclists and bikes will disappear without bike lanes? Nope. We'll clog up the streets and slow traffic. Would drivers rather have me in bike lanes, where they don't have to worry about hitting me, or sharing the car lanes, slowing them down and competing with them for space?
Elizabeth Block, Toronto
Nowhere have I seen any mention of the disabled community and what all this means to them. Getting into the city if you require transit with supports is an excruciating journey. We need door to door public transit. Bike lanes are potential death traps because of the many cyclists who blow through intersections without dismounting and walking their bike through the intersection. If you require the use of a service dog, sidewalks are a nightmare. People either crash into you or your dog because they're not paying attention. Many of us drive modified vehicles using hand controls with side or back ramps. However, parking is practically non-existent. Street parking has been lost to bike lanes. Surface lots don't have wide enough disabled spaces for side ramps to extend. So please have a think about how adding more bike lanes impacts infrastructure required for those with disabilities.
Lilian Hulme-Smith,
Richmond Hill
Too much
Rogers control
The Rogers way suggests no Toronto team president is safe: Feschuk, Sept. 21
The insatiable appetite of Edward Rogers to control the sports scene in Toronto has taken another step. Rogers' domination of the sports market in the city is evident with his recent purchase of Bell's investment in Maple Leaf Sports & Entertainment for $4.7 billion. Already having Rogers Centre, Sportsnet and Blue Jays in hand, Rogers will own the majority of the Toronto Raptors, the Toronto Blue Jays, the Toronto Argonauts and Toronto FC soccer club. That just leaves MLSE chair Larry Tanenbaum with his 20 per cent and five per cent owned by OMERS, a Canadian pension fund. It's only time before all of Toronto's sports teams are neglected the way the Jays have been. It's all about Rogers' bottom line and no more. What this city lacks is a sports-minded organization, not a profit-obsessed corporation that doesn't know a hockey puck from a catcher's mitt, and could care less.
Gary Megaffin, Kitchener, Ont.
Teaching life skills at school
Should Ontario students learn how to sew? To change a tire? The Ford government wants your opinion, Sept. 9
I totally disagree with the letters that say teaching of life skills at school is useless. If not for home economics class, I would never have learned to operate a sewing machine. My mother did not own one. Being taught this skill, as well as cooking, has been immensely important in my life. Maybe it's the era I grew up in but these skills totally had a positive impact on my life and have saved me a lot of money in clothing repairs over the years.
Marla Switzer, Thornhill
Clarity on
carbon tax
Poilievre poisoned Canadians against the most effective tool we have for fighting climate change, Sept. 17
This article is one of the very few over the years of the so-called "carbon tax" that even comes close to the truth. Over 200 global economists agree that carbon pricing is the best way to reduce the burning of fossil fuels contributing to climate change. The media has been generally quiet on the fact that the rebate component of the tax leaves most households better off financially, leaving Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre free rein to spread misinformation about the tax. Poilievre carrying on about it for years makes one worry about what else he has in store for Canada if he becomes prime minister.
Jim Griffin, Markdale, Ont.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre compares the effect of carbon pricing to an economic "nuclear winter." He ignores the undeniable fact that carbon pollution is already producing "nuclear summers" in our country. Think of the economic (and human) costs of catastrophic forest fires, heat domes, droughts and flooding. To avoid these devastating effects of climate change, Canada and other countries must immediately implement measures to reduce carbon pollution. The federal Liberals' carbon pricing policy has been shown to be a very effective carbon reduction policy (granted, the policy can and should be better communicated).
Jeffrey Levitt, Toronto
You can't make people disappear
Closing safe injection sites without alternative supports in place is another example that the little guy does not matter to the provincial Progressive Conservatives. As with dismantling encampments, it is as if the government's solution is to remove something it finds embarrassing or inconvenient instead of finding a solution. It's an important reminder of the stakes of the upcoming federal election; I worry about universal health care if the federal Conservatives are elected. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is no good, but a Conservative would be much worse. It would seem that the only safe option in the coming election is a minority government; then at least the damage can be minimized.
Rick Lockman, Orillia, Ont.

Document 261 of 500

Ford calls trip to protest 'disgusting': Premier vows to hold to account those responsible for taking students to pro-Palestinian event

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-calls-trip-protest-disgusting/docview/3108515634/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+calls+trip+to+protest+%27disgusting%27%3A+Premier+vows+to+hold+to+account+those+responsible+for+taking+students+to+pro-Palestinian+event&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Taking students to a rally intended to be about Indigenous rights but which included pro-Palestinian protesters is "disgraceful" and "disgusting," said Premier Doug Ford, promising those behind it will be "held accountable."
It's a controversy the Toronto District School Board will be dealing with at a special board meeting Wednesday evening, where trustees can discuss what went wrong and who's responsible.
"It's unacceptable," Ford said when asked about the incident, at an unrelated event in Cobourg on Monday afternoon.
"There needs to be an investigation, and we'll be all over this and make sure people are held accountable. They (need to) think twice about bringing young, little kids - without the parents' permission - on these school trips. It's disgusting."
Last Wednesday, students from about 15 TDSB schools - a mix of elementary, middle and secondary - went on a field trip to the Grassy Narrows River Run, billed as a community event supporting the Grassy Narrows First Nation and their efforts to address mercury contamination. It began at Grange Park and then protesters marched to the Ontario Legislature.
Parents were told it was an educational opportunity for students to observe speakers, and that they wouldn't participate in the rally. But videos posted to social media showed kids marching and chanting, "From Turtle Island to Palestine, occupation is a crime," which sparked public outcry.
The board has since apologized "for the harm that some students may have experienced," saying it's taking concerns "very seriously" and prioritizing an investigation into the matter.
The premier said students "should be in the classroom learning about reading, writing, spelling, arithmetic - the whole shebang," but instead, the TDSB and teachers "want to bring them down to a rally, a Palestinian rally, and it's ridiculous."
"I've said this is indoctrination for years," he added, blaming a small minority of "bad actors."
"Number one, you shouldn't be teaching our kids," the premier added. "But number two, stick with your knitting - stick with teaching the kids the geography, history, math, spelling, that's what they need."
In a statement to the Star, Education Minister Jill Dunlop said she was concerned that student safety was put at risk.
"Our government firmly believes that publicly funded schools and activities should never be used as vehicles for political protests. This principle was clearly violated last week when several activist teachers took groups of young children, on a TDSB-sanctioned field trip, to a demonstration. This action compromised the safety of our children and violated the trust of parents," she said.
"Some teachers, along with others associated with this protest, have a history of promoting inflammatory, discriminatory and hateful content. There is no place for this type of teaching in our classrooms. I expect the TDSB's investigation to be thorough, transparent and to conclude as quickly as possible."
Wednesday's special board meeting will also discuss how to mitigate any harm that was caused and if policies about field trips need to be changed.
In her weekly email update to constituents, Trustee Shelley Laskin said she became "deeply upset and angered" by what she saw on social media and in emails from parents who expressed "serious concerns" they weren't fully informed about what the trip would entail.
"Reports of what children experienced before, during and after the River Run are deeply disturbing," she wrote. Among them, she notes, were an eight-year-old child who came home with a sticker that read "Zionism Kills" and Jewish and other students who expressed discomfort at hearing antisemitic slogans being told they would "get over it."
"Somehow a trip to learn what happened at Grassy Narrows and the environmental impact on the Indigenous people who live there turned into a situation where there was pro-Palestinian chanting and antisemitic rhetoric. How did this situation happen?" she asked.
The Star has not been able to independently verify these reports and the TDSB says it's still investigating.
Officials at the board, and province, have been receiving emails of frustration from the public, citing concerns about both antisemitism and anti-Palestinian racism.
Tamara Gottlieb, of Jewish Educators and Families Association of Canada, told the Star that parents raised concerns with school administrators before the trip because they wanted details about the speakers and felt uneasy about children attending a political protest that could get unruly.
She said parents were assured that speakers would stick to the issue of mercury contamination and students would only be observing, not participating, in the protest.
"This is really a collapse of confidence in the TDSB," says Gottlieb, who is calling on both the province and the Ontario College of Teachers to investigate.
In a statement to the Star, Zaid Zawaideh, founder of Toronto Palestinian Families, called the board's apology "yet another example of systemic anti-Palestinian racism."
"It is deeply concerning that the TDSB issued an apology for a field trip to the Grassy Narrows River Run ... Palestinians, who know full well the impact of settler colonialism, have the right to be there, just as any other community does."
When contacted by the Star, the OCT, which regulates the teaching profession, said it's unable to comment on any individual complaints or investigations.
CAPTION: Thousands participated in the protest last Wednesday about the decades-long mercury poisonings in the Grassy Narrows community, including students from about 15 Toronto District School Board schools.Richard LautensToronto Star
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau Isabel Teotonio Toronto Star

Ballot pranks hurt our democracy's credibility

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ballot-pranks-hurt-our-democracys-credibility/docview/3108515621/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ballot+pranks+hurt+our+democracy%27s+credibility&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When did election ballots become a staging ground for political stunts?
In two of the three recent federal by-elections - in Toronto, Winnipeg and Montreal - self-styled electoral system reformers figured out how to load up the actual ballot with dozens of names of unaffiliated candidates to produce outsized and difficult-to-use ballots. These need to be laboriously hand-counted, meaning that results come in extraordinarily late by Canadian election standards.
Not to be confused with the joke parties of the past, this tactic depends on large numbers of people registering as independent candidates, with neither the desire nor the chance to win, in order to make a point about why Canada's election system needs an overhaul.
The organizers, who call themselves the Longest Ballot Committee and have not revealed their identities publicly, declared victory after the second such race: "We can say without a doubt our campaign was a success because we had so much fun and sparked important conversations about our shared democracy," they said in a statement reported by the CBC.
Their campaign is about pressuring the federal government to enact the ranked ballot reforms promised and then dismissed by Justin Trudeau's Liberals in 2015. But finding a way to make the actual ballot cumbersome and confusing risks infecting Canada's electoral system with the cynicism that has disabled its U.S. counterpart. Indeed, one has to ask why is it a good idea to confuse voters in the ballot box, a place that should be literally beyond the reach of politics?
There's a strand of populist ideology that elections should be as open as possible. But it doesn't follow that election administration should be vulnerable to an activity that makes a mockery of the voting process and renders the task of determining a winner exceedingly difficult.
I don't buy the argument that Canada's first-past-the-post system is hopelessly tainted because the winners don't necessarily represent the majority of votes cast. The entire history of postwar Canada, which is what we're talking about, can't simply be dismissed as an eight-decade-long disaster. What's more, no electoral system is ideal; even those that seem to be the most democratic - e.g., proportional representation - are vulnerable to abuse. Just look at Israel's fatally-fractured parliament, where tiny extremist parties control the balance of power.
It's also important to acknowledge that different Canadian jurisdictions have very different rules about how individuals can enlist as candidates.
At the federal level, a prospective candidate must collect 100 signatures of electors, which can now be done very easily using electronic forms. The refundable deposit used to be $1,000, but Alberta's court of appeal struck down that requirement in 2017, deeming it unconstitutional.
Deposits, however, still exist in provincial and municipal elections.
In Ontario, candidates for the legislature, as well as municipal councils and school boards in jurisdictions with over 4,000 residents, have to find 25 signatures for their nomination papers and pay a $200 deposit. B.C. requires 75 signatures and a $250 deposit. Alberta, in turn, wants 25 signatures and a $500 deposit.
Which of these represents the most optimally democratic approach? The one with the least required names? The one with no deposit? There is no right answer, because these rules are obviously arbitrary.
What we do now know is that it's become easy to punk the federal races and there's every reason to think these tactics will spread. After the past week's byelections, Canada's chief electoral officer Stéphane Perrault said he'll look at tightening the rules, citing the difficulties that voters with accessibility issues experienced when presented with metre-long ballots containing dozens of names.
I'm glad Perrault is advocating on behalf of a marginalized group that came face-to-face with the potential disenfranchisement posed by Longest Ballot's stunt. But I'd argue that Elections Canada can broaden its scope of concern to everyone who wants to cast a vote without worrying that some anonymous gadfly group is having fun at their expense.
If the Longest Balloteers think there's appetite for reform, let them seek a space within the parties and then expend some energy making their case. But leave the election process alone. After all, we know by now what happens when people start to sow doubt about a system that demands airtight credibility to remain legitimate.
John Lorinc is a Toronto journalist and editor. He is a contributor for the Star.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: John Lorinc Contributor John Lorinc is a Toronto journalist and editor. He is a contributor for the Star.;

Document 263 of 500

A traffic cure that Toronto won't touch

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/traffic-cure-that-toronto-wont-touch/docview/3108515474/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+traffic+cure+that+Toronto+won%27t+touch&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Big number: 27%, the reduction in traffic attributed to the congestion charge in London, England, according to a report by the Canadian Automobile Association
The most interesting thing about Toronto's new traffic-fighting congestion management plan isn't anything in the plan itself. Instead, the most interesting thing is what got left out.
With this plan, Toronto city hall's transportation department has now, rather emphatically, slammed the brakes on any notion of charging drivers for using downtown streets.
Journalists attending a briefing at city hall last Friday were given a Q&A package with background information about the new traffic plan. Under a section titled "Is the city considering a congestion charge on motorists?" the transportation department said no, definitely not - and offered a surprising reason why.
A congestion charge - a road toll charged to drivers who enter the downtown core during business hours - the document said, "likely would not reduce or deter traffic from coming into Toronto, and could have adverse impacts on people who must travel by car for work, child care, health reasons, etc."
The surprise comes because the notion that a congestion charge wouldn't reduce the amount of traffic in the city seems to be countered by data from, well, every city with a congestion charge.
Cities like London, Stockholm and Singapore all saw immediate and lasting traffic reductions after implementing congestion charges. A recent study by the Canadian Automobile Association - not exactly a bunch of radical anti-car pinkos - reported a 27 per cent drop in traffic from the implementation of the London charge, noting that "traffic levels have declined and transit use has increased since the scheme was implemented."
They also note a major reduction in car crashes and an associated increase in cycling.
So, um, why would Toronto be different? When I asked about it, Roger Browne, the city's director of traffic management, offered an interesting explanation.
"The driving culture in North America is very different than other parts of the world," Browne said. He also cited a lack of alternatives for people to get into the city, calling it a potential "equity issue."
"At the end of the day, it doesn't matter how much you charge. If you're going to take an approach of drawing a box around the city and then imposing that sort of charge, anyone that has to do business or anyone that has to bring goods or services into the city, they still have to do that. They would just pass that charge onto the consumer," he explained.
Call me skeptical. I suspect there are still a lot of people driving downtown who don't really need to be driving downtown. Like, for example, some of the more than 50,000 licensed ride-share drivers for apps like Uber and Lyft who spend a lot of time just waiting for their next fare. Or people coming downtown in groups who have done the math and realized it's still inexplicably cheaper to drive and park then pay for multiple transit fares. Or stubborn people who are set in their ways, but who might consider alternatives like transit if given a little economic push.
And if those transit alternatives are lacking, then it seems incumbent on city hall to make them better. Revenue from a congestion charge would probably help do that.
Look, I don't have any illusions that Mayor Olivia Chow and Toronto council could actually implement a congestion charge right now.
Premier Doug Ford seems poised to deny city hall the basic ability to install bike lanes. In that context, the notion that his driver-loving government might let Toronto implement any kind of road toll is pure fantasy.
So had Browne and transportation general manager Barbara Gray cited the political and legislative barriers as the city's reasons for not pursuing a congestion charge, I would have understood. But by offering a more robust argument against the effectiveness of road pricing, this transportation department has signalled that they're unlikely to ever really shift their traffic-fighting efforts into high gear.
Because the data seems clear. Congestion pricing has proven to be an effective strategy for cities to reduce traffic. In fact, it might be the only effective strategy.
Certainly none of the other measures announced at city hall last week have much chance of being as effective.
Don't get me wrong. I'm a big fan of the other traffic-fighting measures announced by Browne and Gray, like a stronger push for automated enforcement, higher fines for blocking bike lanes and new fees for construction companies who block traffic lanes. But even Browne admitted he expects these measures will mostly just keep things from getting worse. The city is planning to launch a dashboard that measures how long trips are taking - a "travel time index." As long as that index isn't increasing, the transportation department will be happy.
"For us, success is when we see that the travel time index remains relatively constant, despite the fact that we're seeing significant increases in terms of volumes of traffic. That is our key measure of success," Browne said.
OK, but it does seem relevant that there's another level of success available to cities via congestion charges - a move that can actually reduce traffic. You could call it an extra strength remedy to the congestion that ails us. Toronto, it seems, isn't anywhere near ready to swallow that pill.
CAPTION: Congestion pricing has proven to be an effective strategy for cities to reduce traffic, writes Matt Elliott. Even so, Toronto has explicitly said it is not considering a congestion charge.Steve Russell Toronto Star
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Document 264 of 500

Here's why the new Bonnie Crombie sounds like the old Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/heres-why-new-bonnie-crombie-sounds-like-old-doug/docview/3108821182/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Here%27s+why+the+new+Bonnie+Crombie+sounds+like+the+old+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's Liberals had billed it as a weekend policy conference to brainstorm for a few good ideas on good governance.
But when Bonnie Crombie finally took the podium, she skipped the (policy) platform. Pondering her path to power, Crombie chose a shortcut.
No policy bible, no fine print, no road map to reviving and revivifying the moribund Liberals. While it's still early days, the party leader detoured from any details for the 1,000 party delegates meeting in London.
Henceforth, her provincial Liberals will build their campaign all around Doug Ford. And downplay their ties to Justin Trudeau's unpopular federal Liberals.
To be clear, she won't just focus on Ford's faults. Crombie's Liberals are also stealing the premier's proven playbook - battle-tested in two winning elections - and turning it against his governing Tories with a vengeance.
Remember Ford's winning campaign in 2018, when he badmouthed Kathleen Wynne's Liberals as corrupt parasites allegedly enriching their "insider" friends? Remember the attacks against "hallway health care" and the cheesy slogans promising "buck a beer?"
Now, Crombie wants to flip Ford's script, by turning his own words against him. If you can't beat them, borrow their sloganeering.
Which is why the new Crombie sounds increasingly like the old Ford, circa 2018. Policy light, rhetoric heavy.
At her final pep rally Saturday evening, the culmination of all those hours of deep thought by the party faithful, there was barely a peep about policy. Just chapter headings on health care and education, with a promise of more to come - hopefully before Ford springs an early election on the opposition parties next spring.
"So how will we do all of this?" she asked rhetorically, before retreating from her own challenge.
"We start by doing less for the rich insiders - the people who are getting ahead while everyone else is falling behind. Less for Doug's buddies. Less for the people who are under criminal investigation."
She complained about "hospital hallways" that are crammed, echoing Ford's old complaints. And then Crombie delivered her carefully prepared punch line, that she hopes will shake up Ontario politics:
"We are going to do less for them, so we can do more for you."
In case you missed it, that phrase, first heard at London's convention centre, will resound and redound across the province if the Liberals have their way. On cue, as Crombie led the chant, her followers shouted it out:
"Less for them. More for you."
As slogans go, it's not merely clunky but cheesy. It encapsulates both the politics of envy and the pocketbook politics of our era.
It frames politics as entirely transactional, not transformational. It is anti-tax and all about affordability.
Will it sell?
To be sure, people are feeling besieged by economic disparities. But the solutions are not as simple as politicians pretend.
Affordability and tax-cutting won't pay the bills. Sustainability and efficiency are part of the equation.
Without sustainable tax revenues, you can't sustain the education and health services that people count on, and that public sector workers bargain on. If you can't drive efficiencies in an $85-billion health-care budget (without reflexively invoking privatization sloganeering and scaremongering), you won't be able to fix overflowing hospital hallways.
On the environment, Crombie says her provincial Liberals want nothing to do with any carbon tax. But she hasn't even hinted at her own alternative, beyond saying she wants to "protect our planet."
For his part, Ford never presented any serious policies in his last two campaigns, other than his perennial "axe the tax" attack on any federal carbon levy. Nor has NDP Leader Marit Stiles come forth with serious new policies since taking over her party.
Meanwhile, the Tories are spending big money on ads that frame and defame Crombie as a posh and pampered politician who is out of touch with ordinary Ontarians. Now, the Liberal leader is recasting Ford as the real rich kid who grew up with a silver spoon in his father's Etobicoke "mansion," heir to a family firm and political dynasty, while she is the hard scrabble survivor who grew up in a "rooming house" raised by a single mother.
Will this battle of personal narratives speak to voters? Is it a substitute for the detailed policy wars of the past, when all three major parties offered substantive platforms instead of placeholders for personal brands?
Voters hungry for substance will have to content themselves with a content-free pitch from Crombie (and Stiles), each insisting that corruption has insinuated itself into every sinew of Ford's being. The challenge is that Ford remains far ahead in the polls and keeps winning byelections, most recently by promising to "Get it done!"
Can Crombie outdo Ford in slogans? That depends on how - or whether - she ever answers her own question in her weekend speech:
"How will we do all of this?"
Good question.
CAPTION: Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at the party's gathering in London, Ont. on Sept. 21, 2024.

Subject: Voters

Title: Here's why the new Bonnie Crombie sounds like the old Doug Ford

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 265 of 500

'Put them on secondary roads:' Doug Ford shoots down criticism of controversial plan to limit bike lanes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/put-them-on-secondary-roads-doug-ford-shoots-down/docview/3108515845/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Put+them+on+secondary+roads%3A%27+Doug+Ford+shoots+down+criticism+of+controversial+plan+to+limit+bike+lanes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is defending his government's push to limit bike lanes in Ontario municipalities, saying cars and trucks should have full priority on major roads like Bloor Street and University Avenue.
"We just want to get traffic moving ... that's what it comes down to, making sure you aren't putting bike lanes in the middle of some of the busiest streets in the country," Ford told reporters Monday.
"Put them on secondary roads."
The controversial plan - which government sources have confirmed is in upcoming legislation titled the "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act" - would block construction of new bike lanes if they result in the loss of other lanes to cars, buses, trucks and other traffic.
It quickly sparked concern when details leaked last week. Six cyclists have been killed on Toronto streets this year.
"This is politics that plays with people's lives," said New Democrat MPP Joel Harden (Ottawa Centre), his party's transportation critic.
"This is putting wedge politics over real solutions."
University of Toronto geography and planning professor Matti Siemiatycki called the plan to limit bike lanes "ineffective and dangerous" as people increasingly take to bicycles to get around.
Toronto city council recently voted in favour of adding 100 kilometres of bike lanes to major routes within three years to reduce vehicle emissions and combat climate change.
But Ford was not backing down during a water and sewer funding announcement for a new housing development in Cobourg, an hour's drive east of Toronto.
He bristled at notions that removing motor vehicle lanes in favour of bike lanes doesn't increase congestion.
"That's a bunch of hogwash," Ford said, insisting the reduction of lanes on University Avenue's "hospital row" south of College Street to make way for bike lanes has made that stretch "jammed like crazy."
He also cited Bloor Street, claiming bike lanes have "disrupted all the businesses there."
"We need to focus on transportation routes. They get people ... from point A to point B."
Ford evaded a question about why he repeatedly says municipalities "know best" where housing is needed as the province battles a shortage of affordable new homes, but now wants to override municipal powers on where bike lanes should be located.
Critics said there is an inconsistency there as the premier apparently looks to court support from motorists and suburbanites as he mulls whether to call an early election next spring instead of waiting until the next slated provincewide vote in June 2026.
"Nobody asked for the government to dictate people's transportation choices or get in the way of local communities doing what works best for them," said Harden.
Ford was criticized for meddling in municipal affairs shortly after he was elected premier in 2018 and passed legislation cutting the size of Toronto city council nearly in half during the city's election campaign.
An Etobicoke resident, he has frequently slammed the expansion of bike lanes in Toronto. City staff have found bike ridership increases when new protected bike lanes are installed.
The premier also sounded off Monday about environmental assessments for Highway 413, which would link Highway 401 at Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan.
"Let's build the damn highway. There's hundreds of thousands of people stuck in our cars backed up from here to Timbuktu, and you're worried about a grasshopper jumping across the highway."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces a $25-million investment in water infrastructure to help enable the construction of new homes in Cobourg on Sept. 23, 2024.

Document 266 of 500

The scary truth behind this viral video of Jagmeet Singh

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/scary-truth-behind-this-viral-video-jagmeet-singh/docview/3108250379/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+scary+truth+behind+this+viral+video+of+Jagmeet+Singh&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's something cathartic about watching bullies get put in their place.
That's likely a big reason why a video showing NDP leader Jagmeet Singh confronting and schooling a far-right heckler is resonating with so many people after going viral last week.
The video starts with Singh being followed around Parliament Hill by a group of hecklers filming him on their phones. The hecklers are part of a spin-off group that has been gathering daily outside Parliament since the end of the Freedom Convoy two years ago - a number of them say they are banned from entering the House of Commons for security reasons.
After one man affiliated with the group cursed at Singh, the NDP leader stopped, turned around and asked him: "You got something to say?"
"It wasn't me," the man stuttered. "It was the gentleman behind me, I guess." He twirled around but there was no such gentleman to be seen.
"You're a coward," Singh told the man, standing chest to chest. "You're not going to say it to my face."
Most who saw the video took delight in watching a man behaving like an Internet troll melt faster than a snowflake on a sunny day when confronted in real life. Others seemed relieved to see a political leader finally stand up to the obnoxious convoy crowd.
Only a few hours earlier that day, members of that same group disrupted a ceremony honouring Terry Fox, shouting "traitors," "liars" and "f-k Trudeau."
Other members of the group have been following MPs around downtown Ottawa screaming "treason" and "criminals." They claim these confrontations are justified because they are holding elected officials "accountable."
In our democracy, the public has a right to question elected politicians. In fact, the public has a democratic duty to hold them accountable. But this group is confusing public accountability with personal abuse.
Watching their videos, it's hard not to wonder how any normal person would react to a group of men following them down the street screaming profanities while broadcasting their abusive behaviour live on social media.
Singh himself has spoken openly about his experiences with childhood bullying. His brother Gurratan, a former Ontario NDP MPP, reacted to the video by pointing out his older brother has "stood up to bullies his whole life and we all know bullies fold when someone stands up to them."
This is undeniably true. And yet this is Parliament Hill, not a schoolyard. Why should elected officials be subjected to behaviour considered dangerously inappropriate for seven-year-olds?
This exact kind of confrontation could easily escalate and spiral out of control, dragging innocent bystanders into a dangerous mess. It's only a matter of time before an MP, a random pedestrian or a tourist gets hurt.
Still there is something about this viral moment that felt inevitable.
Elected officials have been dealing with these volatile confrontations more and more since the end of the pandemic. Last year, Chrystia Freeland was aggressively ambushed by a local conspiracy theorist in Grande Prairie, Alberta. Jagmeet Singh and a Radio-Canada journalist were harassed by a man who tried to place them under "citizen's arrest."
We know national security agencies have been monitoring threats targeting cabinet ministers and MPs, particularly women, from convoy-adjacent extremists.
Make no mistake, this is happening across the political spectrum. People affiliated with these groups have been charged for criminal harassment outside the home of Newfoundland and Labrador's Liberal premier, Andrew Fury. Others have staged protests outside the homes of Ontario Premier Doug Ford and former Progressive Conservative Health Minister Christine Elliot. Liberal, Conservative or NDP, they don't seem to care.
It's happening at every level of government too. In Pickering, Ontario, local city councillors say they are being overwhelmed by out-of-town far-right conspiracy groups.
It's too simplistic to describe this phenomenon as a decline in decorum and civility. The far-right is engaging in tactics targeted at Canada's democratic institutions.
These groups will claim their abusive and harassing behaviours are justified in the name of patriotism, accountability or even free speech. But unless we see concrete steps to address this scourge and create safe workplaces for public officials, more and more Canadians will simply opt-out of public life.
Whatever you think of his politics, Singh was right about one thing. We can't let the bullies win. Our democracy is at stake.
CAPTION: Protestors linked to the so-called Freedom Convoy have been accused of harassing and even abusing politicians across Canada.Luke LeBrun is the editor PressProgress.
CREDIT: Luke LeBrun

Subject: Extremism; Conspiracy; Democracy; Public officials; Accountability

Location: Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet

Where are aging Torontonians supposed to go? + a grieving father wants justice from Kenneth Law

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/where-are-aging-torontonians-supposed-go-grieving/docview/3108250278/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Where+are+aging+Torontonians+supposed+to+go%3F+%2B+a+grieving+father+wants+justice+from+Kenneth+Law&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, Sept. 23 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on a housing strategy for seniors, the father of one of Kenneth Law's alleged victims speaks out and make-or-break time for Jagmeet Singh.
DON'T MISSA housing strategy to help seniors downsize
Torontonians are aging and the housing crunch is hitting them hard. Most new builds lack the accessibility features to attract seniors, but some developers are making headway in creating more accessible homes incorporating Universal Design. Here's what that is and what it means for Toronto's housing market.
Staying put: Many boomers and aging Canadians aren't leaving their homes - that's a problem for millennials hoping to move into houses and lowrise homes.As Kenneth Law faces a trial, this New Zealand man wants justice for his daughter
Kenneth Law will be in court in Canada, but his alleged victims are all over the world. A 21-year-old New Zealand woman was one of them. She allegedly received a suicide kit from Law and died shortly after. Her father, a retired police officer, wants to see "the full force of the law" come down on Law. Here's what he had to say.
Who is Kenneth Law? The 58-year-old Mississauga man is facing 14 counts of second-degree murder and is accused of aiding or abetting 14 people to die by suicide. Media reports have linked him to more than 100 deaths in three countries.The stakes have never been higher for Jagmeet Singh
After eight years and three elections, it's make or break for Jagmeet Singh. He has barely grown his New Democrats' seat count in the past two elections and the party is currently stagnant in the polls. But with the Liberals in decline and a resurgent Conservative party, can Singh seize a chance to jump into the fray? Raisa Patel takes a look at the monumental task ahead for Singh's NDP.
The "say it to my face" video: While the clip of Singh confronting hecklers has gone viral, there's scary truth behind it.Susan Delacourt and Matt Gurney's take: It's just a matter of time before we're in an election. Here's where all the parties stand.WHAT ELSE

Here's what you need to know.There are pros and cons that come with long-term-care insurance. Thank you for reading First Up. You can reach me and the First Up team at firstup@thestar.ca

The scary truth in Singh's viral video

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/scary-truth-singhs-viral-video/docview/3107938349/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+scary+truth+in+Singh%27s+viral+video&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Luke LeBrun is the editor of PressProgress. His reporting focuses on federal politics, right-wing media and far-right extremism.
