Document 269 of 500

Liberals, teachers starting to mend fences: Unions attend party meeting after years of strained relations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-teachers-starting-mend-fences/docview/3107938236/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals%2C+teachers+starting+to+mend+fences%3A+Unions+attend+party+meeting+after+years+of+strained+relations&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After a decade-long rift, the Ontario Liberals and the province's teacher unions are starting to play nice in the sandbox.
The party's annual meeting here on the weekend featured a 90-minute education policy session led by the presidents of all four major teacher unions - something that has not happened in recent memory - who received an enthusiastic standing ovation at the end.
"It will be a co-operative collaboration ... There's no payoff when there's friction, only the kids suffer," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie told the Star in an interview, acknowledging the party learned the hard way when a decade of strong ties abruptly ended in 2012 after the McGuinty government imposed contracts on teachers as part of an austerity push, sparking 10-plus years of strained relations.
"We've all acknowledged that we need to invest in our educators," Crombie said. "We (are) serious about fixing education and training our kids for success."
On Saturday, Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario president Karen Brown told the Star she believes the PC government has failed educators and students "and we're looking at parties that have platforms that support public education. They're seeing us as key stakeholders ... and we're here to give our expertise."
The Liberals "want to listen, and we're hoping to see some of that expertise embedded in their policy," she said after the panel discussion.
But, she added, it won't be easy, as "there are some things that our members are going to have a difficult time still with reconciling," including their long-standing resentment of the McGuinty government's Bill 115, legislation that was ultimately ruled unconstitutional by the courts.
"But we need to look forward and move ahead," Brown said. "So this is part of building on our commonalities and repairing those relationships."
In 2018, ETFO - the country's largest such union - took the unusual step of endorsing the NDP in the provincial election.
Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation, said "we don't have the kind of relations we'd want to have with the Conservatives, and we've been on-again, off-again with the Liberals. They are at least saying the right things right now" and consulting with the unions.
"The trust level still remains to be rebuilt with our members because I participate in an event like this, and I will hear from people who will be very angry, but we can't disagree with everyone," said Littlewood. "At some point in time, we have to have alliances ... what we need are parties that are going to listen to what's happening in education and are actually going to do something about it."
Experts say both sides benefit from improved relations.
The unions "recognize that if there is going to be a change in government, it's going to be Liberal," said Nelson Wiseman, a longtime political expert and professor emeritus at the University of Toronto. "They've already decided that the Ford government, Conservative governments generally, are not sympathetic to them. The Liberals and the teachers' unions are, in effect, courting each other."
Wiseman said he expects the unions to also be a part of NDP policy development given that their leadership tends to be more in line with the New Democrats.
Professor Jonathan Rose, who heads the political studies department at Queen's University, said "it's a competition on the left to get union support and both parties - the Liberals and the NDP - are trying to do that."
Littlewood said 2012 "was a bad year for (the Liberals) - it was a bad move. But I have to give (recent interim leader) John Fraser credit, because I can't tell you the number of times I've heard him apologize in the legislature to us for that."
Fraser has even reminded the Ford government of the Liberals' mistake when the Tories imposed wage-capping legislation on public sector workers - which was also later ruled unconstitutional by the courts, Littlewood noted.
(The Liberals' Bill 115 ultimately cost taxpayers almost $200 million in payments to teachers, and so far the Ford government has paid out $6.7 billion in retroactive pay raises to the broader public sector.)
Since the Liberal party has just nine MPPs, and lacks party status in the legislature, "they're going to say the right things, they want our support," Littlewood added. "But we approach everything with caution, to make sure that they are consulting with us and making decisions that will be beneficial to our members."
Some members do have lingering resentment over Bill 115 - and sometimes raise the unpopular "Rae Days" imposed by the NDP government in the early 1990s, she added.
"I hear it at meetings, but I also hear people say that we've got to do something. We're trying to make sure that when we develop a platform, we're sharing it with the parties, and that the parties are reflecting that in their platforms too."
At some point, Littlewood added, "you have to move on. You can't hate everybody forever."
René Jansen in de Wal, president of the Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association, said "I've always been willing to talk to whoever can help us move to the most important things we all agree on - so I've always been open to talking to all parties."
After Saturday's education panel, he said it's significant that all four teacher union presidents were asked, and accepted, the Liberals' invitation.
"We're on the same stage together," he said. "That's pretty unusual - I don't know if it's ever happened before, but it's symbolic."
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 270 of 500

Teacher unions, Ontario Liberals mend fences at party's weekend meeting: 'We have to have alliances'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/teacher-unions-ontario-liberals-mend-fences-at/docview/3107938561/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teacher+unions%2C+Ontario+Liberals+mend+fences+at+party%27s+weekend+meeting%3A+%27We+have+to+have+alliances%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LONDON, ONT. - After a decade-long rift, the Ontario Liberals and the province's teacher unions are starting to play nice in the sandbox.
The party's annual meeting here on the weekend featured a 90-minute education policy session led by the presidents of all four major teacher unions - something that has not happened in recent memory - who received an enthusiastic standing ovation at the end.
"It will be a co-operative collaboration ... There's no payoff when there's friction, only the kids suffer," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie told the Star in an interview, acknowledging the party learned the hard way when a decade of strong ties abruptly ended in 2012 after the then-McGuinty government imposed contracts on teachers as part of an austerity push, sparking 10-plus years of strained relations.
"We've all acknowledged that we need to invest in our educators," Crombie said. "We (are) serious about fixing education and training our kids for success."
On Saturday, Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario president Karen Brown told the Star she believes the PC government has failed educators and students "and we're looking at parties that have platforms that support public education. They're seeing us as key stakeholders, and they want to dialogue with us on that, and we're here to give our expertise."
The Liberals "want to listen, and we're hoping to see some of that expertise embedded in their policy," she said after the panel discussion.
But, she added, it won't be easy, as "there are some things that our members are going to have a difficult time still with reconciling," including their long-standing resentment of the McGuinty government's Bill 115, legislation that was ultimately ruled unconstitutional by the courts.
"But we need to look forward and move ahead," Brown said. "So this is part of building on our commonalities and repairing those relationships."
In 2018, ETFO - the country's largest such union - took the unusual step of endorsing the NDP in the provincial election.
Karen Littlewood, president of the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation, said "we don't have the kind of relations we'd want to have with the Conservatives, and we've been on-again, off-again with the Liberals. They are at least saying the right things right now" and consulting with the unions.
"The trust level still remains to be rebuilt with our members because I participate in an event like this, and I will hear from people who will be very angry, but we can't disagree with everyone," said Littlewood. "At some point in time, we have to have alliances ... what we need are parties that are going to listen to what's happening in education and are actually going to do something about it."
Experts say both sides benefit from improved relations.
The unions "recognize that if there is going to be a change in government, it's going to be Liberal," said Nelson Wiseman, a longtime political expert and professor emeritus at the University of Toronto. "They've already decided that the Ford government, Conservative governments generally, are not sympathetic to them. The Liberals and the teachers' unions are, in effect, courting each other."
Wiseman said he expects the unions to also be a part of NDP policy development given that their leadership tends to be more in line with the New Democrats.
Professor Jonathan Rose, who heads the political studies department at Queen's University, said "it's a competition on the left to get union support and both parties - the Liberals and the NDP - are trying to do that."
Littlewood said 2012 "was a bad year for (the Liberals) - it was a bad move. But I have to give (recent interim leader) John Fraser credit, because I can't tell you the number of times I've heard him apologize in the legislature to us for that."
Fraser has even reminded the Ford government of the Liberals' mistake when the Tories imposed wage-capping legislation on public sector workers - which was also later ruled unconstitutional by the courts, Littlewood noted.
(The Liberals' Bill 115 ultimately cost taxpayers almost $200 million in payments to teachers, and so far the Ford government has paid out $6.7 billion in retroactive pay raises to the broader public sector.)
Since the Liberal party has just nine MPPs, and lacks party status in the legislature, "they're going to say the right things, they want our support," Littlewood added. "But we approach everything with caution, to make sure that they are consulting with us and making decisions that will be beneficial to our members."
Some members do have lingering resentment over Bill 115 - and sometimes raise the unpopular "Rae Days" imposed by the NDP government in the early 1990s, she added.
"I hear it at meetings, but I also hear people say that we've got to do something. We're trying to make sure that when we develop a platform, we're sharing it with the parties, and that the parties are reflecting that in their platforms too."
At some point, Littlewood added, "you have to move on. You can't hate everybody forever."
René Jansen in de Wal, president of the Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association, said "I've always been willing to talk to whoever can help us move to the most important things we all agree on - so I've always been open to talking to all parties."
After Saturday's education panel, he said it's significant that all four teacher union presidents were asked, and accepted, the Liberals' invitation.
"We're on the same stage together," he said. "That's pretty unusual - I don't know if it's ever happened before, but it's symbolic."
CAPTION: Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario president Karen Brown, seen in 2023, told the Star that her members give the governing PCs a failing grade and 'we're looking at parties that have platforms that support public education.'Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombieat the Liberals' convention in London, Ont. on Saturday.Introduced as "Energizer Bonnie" to the crowd of party faithful, Crombie said - to cheers - the Liberals "are the only opposition party able to take on Doug Ford, and that's exactly what we are going to do."Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, seen speaking at her party's policy convention in London, Ont. on Saturday, told the Star in an exclusive interview that a new ad will begin airing when the legislature resumes in late October so Ontarians can get to know her and the party's direction.

Subject: Public schools; Leadership; Presidents; Education policy; Legislatures; Public sector; Teachers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Public sector

Title: Teacher unions, Ontario Liberals mend fences at party's weekend meeting: 'We have to have alliances'

Document 271 of 500

Why the stakes are the highest they've ever been for Jagmeet Singh: 'The moment that makes or breaks his political career'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-stakes-are-highest-theyve-ever-been-jagmeet/docview/3107696203/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+the+stakes+are+the+highest+they%27ve+ever+been+for+Jagmeet+Singh%3A+%27The+moment+that+makes+or+breaks+his+political+career%27&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Inside the world of Jagmeet Singh, things are looking pretty swell.
The NDP leader prematurely withdrew from his increasingly perilous alliance with Justin Trudeau's minority Liberals - after extracting almost everything he wanted from him.
He posted a byelection result he insists proves the New Democrats are more competitive than any other party.
He confronted a heckler on Parliament Hill, and achieved the impossible by earning the internet's respect.
That was just in the last two weeks.
But the worst thing Singh could do now is be complacent.
The stakes are the highest they've ever been for the NDP leader, who is approaching his eighth year - and third election - at the party's helm.
For several years, the New Democrats have been stagnant in the polls, and over the past two federal contests, the party's seat count has only grown by one.
"It's a big inflection point," said Christo Aivalis, a historian of Canadian politics and labour issues.
"This is going to be the moment that makes or breaks his political career."
Many in New Democratic circles are acutely aware of this moment: support for the Liberals is the lowest it has ever been under Singh's reign, even if it seems like Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are the immediate beneficiaries of that decline.
"This is our opportunity. We have a unique window, and windows like this don't often open up for the New Democrats, given the old school politics of Canada," veteran NDP MP Charlie Angus told the Star.
"There's a whole huge swath of middle voters who are going to be testing us. They're going to be checking us out. So this is our opportunity to reach out to them."
Singh must show Canadians that he can make the transition from an opposition leader to someone with the potential to be a national one, the outgoing MP said, just as former NDP leader Jack Layton did in 2011.
"That really, to me, was a transformative moment. And I think this is the moment where Jagmeet will need to step up and show that he is prime ministerial material," said Angus, of Singh's need to harness his likability, but also demonstrate strength in areas like economic policy.
"That's going to come down to putting an offer on the table that Canadians come to believe in, particularly given the very polarized conditions we're in," Angus said.
For others, Singh's most urgent challenge is defending his early exit from his supply-and-confidence agreement with the Liberals and inching out from beneath Justin Trudeau's shadow.
"The prime minister is deeply unpopular and I think it's: how do you position yourself as the alternative for people who don't want to vote for the Conservatives and don't support Pierre Poilievre?" said Matthew Dubé, the vice-president of Proof Strategies and a former NDP MP who served under the party's last three permanent leaders.
"What remains a challenge is that despite the free fall of Liberal support, it hasn't yet translated into support for the NDP."
At doorsteps, Singh can safely point to the policy goals around health care and workers' rights that he pushed for and secured under the deal.
But the Liberals can also take credit for those achievements. And in the first days after he left the governing pact, Singh struggled to explain to the public why, despite his belief that Canadians want Trudeau out and his insistence that the NDP is ready to mount an election campaign, he wouldn't commit to triggering a national contest at the earliest opportunity.
(Singh has since said he's looking for extra commitments on issues like food affordability, and that he wouldn't support Poilievre's immediate efforts to take down the Trudeau government.)
Angus said he was initially "worried" about the political repercussions of leaving the deal, but said Singh needs to cut through the drama of election speculation and instead "speak directly to Canadians."
The difficulty lies now in taking on a Conservative party that has thrived on using charged rhetoric to undermine Canada's left, said former New Democratic Party president Dhananjai Kohli.
"I think it is going to be a challenge to break through that and be able to communicate with folks," Kohli said.
"I think the biggest challenge for Jagmeet is going to be how he's going to be able to cut through that noise and be able to cut through the persona that Poilievre has falsely created of him, and be able to connect with people in his true self."
That "true self" is one of the most important things in Singh's arsenal, said Elias Assefa, the riding association president for Edmonton-Centre.
It has surfaced in the past when Singh has responded to protesters with "love and courage," and again this week when he channelled that intensity in a blunt confrontation with a heckler in Ottawa.
"I love how he connects to people. I love how he always brings it back to material realities. It's not just about esoteric ideas of freedom. It's about how much groceries can you afford? How are you going to move into a house or afford rent?" Assefa told the Star.
Even so, Assefa acknowledged that Poilievre's message appears to be resonating: something that is cause for concern in a riding like Edmonton-Centre, which is poised for a showdown between the NDP and Conservatives to unseat the Liberals.
"People are frustrated and they're reaching out to anybody that's willing to tell them what they want to hear," he said.
One of the best things Singh can do now is be seen where it counts, said Davenport riding association president Corrigan Hammond.
Hammond's Toronto riding is a red-orange split: the New Democrats lost to the Liberals there in 2021 by a margin of fewer than 100 votes.
"We're pretty formidable in Davenport and I think we could win it. I just want to see more of him," Hammond said.
The NDP's local ground game is one of the biggest areas where the party could stand to improve, said Miles Krauter, a longtime organizer for the Ontario and federal NDP.
While the NDP can credibly say it did well in a pair of byelections held last week - the party held on to their seat in Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona, and significantly grew their share of the vote in Montreal's LaSalle-Émard-Verdun - there are lessons to be learned.
The Conservatives led a compelling challenge in Winnipeg, closing the gap between both parties. And in Montreal's Liberal stronghold, the NDP landed in third place after a tight, three-way race that saw the party roll out one of its strongest ever byelection campaigns.
"I think we failed to win that election for the same reason a lot of NDP campaigns have failed. And I hesitate to say that. I know folks are still raw from the loss and are not going to want to hear it," said Krauter, who briefly volunteered on the Montreal campaign and said the party needs to improve its voter outreach strategy and provide more guidance to local campaigns.
The grassroots organizer said that not all of that work is up to Singh, but that he thought the NDP leader - who has "pleasantly surprised" Krauter - might usher in a new era of ground-level strategizing.
"I was happily surprised by the hustle that (Singh's) leadership campaign showed. And I was really hopeful that we would see more of that translate into the actual operations of the party," Krauter said, of Singh's 2017 first-ballot win.
"He really demonstrated that he was willing to do the work and do that relationship building and that outreach on a massive scale. It was very impressive. But where is that now?"
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh walks on Parliament Hill, in Ottawa Sept. 16, 2024, as members of Parliament return from summer break.

Subject: Canadians; Presidents; Political leadership; Political parties

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Why the stakes are the highest they've ever been for Jagmeet Singh: 'The moment that makes or breaks his political career'

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 22, 2024

Section: politics/federal

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

Document 272 of 500

Ontario's controversial nursing home transfer law is being challenged in court. Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-controversial-nursing-home-transfer-law/docview/3107696148/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+controversial+nursing+home+transfer+law+is+being+challenged+in+court.+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A controversial Ontario law that can force hospital patients to choose between moving into a long-term-care home they didn't pick or face fines will be challenged in court this week.
Beginning on Monday, lawyers for the Ontario Health Coalition and Advocacy Centre for the Elderly will argue against the Ford government's More Beds, Better Care Act, introduced in 2022 as a way to deal with a shortage of acute care beds in hospitals.
The two organizations argue that the legislation violates the Charter rights of hospital patients by targeting a specific group of "older, ill and very vulnerable patients" and removing their right to informed consent about where they will live and the care they receive.
Under the Act, patients designated "alternate level of care" (ALC) - believed to no longer need hospital care - who are waiting for space in long-term care can be required to move to a different long-term care facility not of their choosing or pay $400 each day they remain in hospital.
However, there are no clear standards for how to determine who is ALC, leading to inconsistencies, including some patients being labelled ALC who still need hospital treatment, say the organizations challenging the law.
As a result, the law violates the patients' right to life, liberty and security of the person, is contrary to fundamental justice and denies patients' right to equality, the groups say in a summary of their submission.
"When you hear from the patients who've gone through this and their families ... it's torture," said Natalie Mehra, executive director of the health coalition.
The government denies that the law violates the Charter, maintaining that it is necessary in light of the shortage of public hospital beds and that there is no evidence that it targets patients of a particular age or ability.
"The patient waiting in the emergency room has the same constitutional right to 'medical autonomy' as the ALC patient waiting for an LTC home in a hospital bed that they no longer need," reads the province's submission.
The law says hospitals must still make "reasonable efforts" to get the consent of the patient or their decision-maker. Before the law, hospitals could charge much higher amounts to ALC patients.
The problem with the law, the critics said, is that it strips patients of their choices and can mean patients are moved into facilities which don't offer culture- or religion-specific services. Patients can also be moved far from family and friends - up to 150 kilometres away in northern Ontario, or 70 kilometres away in southern Ontario.
"I think people are being forced into situations that are not good for the patients and not good for the families," said Gail Herrington, 67, who said a hospital told her to change her care home preferences for her late husband to prioritize ones with shorter wait-lists.
Chris Glover, NDP MPP Spadina-Fort York, called the law "the thin edge of the wedge for privatizing our health-care system in Ontario."
Though patients and their families may be told to add different long-term care homes to their choices for temporary stay until a bed in another home becomes available, the Advocacy Centre for the Elderly wrote in an online document about the law, once a hospital patient is admitted to a home, they are no longer considered a priority for another home.
Under the law, an ALC patient can decline any medical treatment - including a physical exam to assess eligibility for long-term care - and decline moving into LTC. The Act does not allow ALC patients to be physically transferred to LTC without their consent, the submission adds.
"There is no Charter right to publicly funded health care," the province argued. "It would therefore be anomalous if a patient had a ... right to choose to remain in a public hospital free of charge even after they no longer require the intensity of resources or services provided in the hospital care setting."
The Canadian Press reported that 293 ALC patients were moved from hospital to long-term care homes they didn't choose from September 2022 to January 2024. As of April 2024, seven people had been fined under the Act.
CAPTION: TheFord government's More Beds, Better Care Act was introduced in 2022 as a way to deal with a shortage of acute care beds in hospitals.

Subject: Patients; Long term health care; Hospitalization; Consent

Title: Ontario's controversial nursing home transfer law is being challenged in court. Here's what you need to know

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 22, 2024

Section: news/ontario

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

The stakes are highest they've ever been for Singh: Experts weigh in on what NDP leader needs to do next

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stakes-are-highest-theyve-ever-been-singh/docview/3107645886/se-2?accountid=15090

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+stakes+are+highest+they%27ve+ever+been+for+Singh%3A+Experts+weigh+in+on+what+NDP+leader+needs+to+do+next&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 274 of 500

Law forcing patients out of hospitals faces challenge over Charter rights: Provincial plan designed to free up acute-care beds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/law-forcing-patients-out-hospitals-faces/docview/3107645864/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Law+forcing+patients+out+of+hospitals+faces+challenge+over+Charter+rights%3A+Provincial+plan+designed+to+free+up+acute-care+beds&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Though patients and their families may be told to add different long-term care homes to their choices for temporary stay until a bed in another home becomes available, the Advocacy Centre for the Elderly wrote in an online document about the law, once a hospital patient is admitted to a home, they are no longer considered a priority for another home.
Under the law, an ALC patient can decline any medical treatment - including a physical exam to assess eligibility for long-term care - and decline moving into LTC. The Act does not allow ALC patients to be physically transferred to LTC without their consent, the submission adds.
"There is no Charter right to publicly funded health care," the province argued. "It would therefore be anomalous if a patient had a ... right to choose to remain in a public hospital free of charge even after they no longer require the intensity of resources or services provided in the hospital care setting."
The Canadian Press reported that 293 ALC patients were moved from hospital to long-term care homes they didn't choose from September 2022 to January 2024. As of April 2024, seven people had been fined under the Act.
CAPTION: The More Beds, Better Care Act forces patients who are waiting for space in long-term care to move to any available facility or pay $400 each day they remain in hospital.The Canadian Pressfile photo
CREDIT: Maria Iqbal Toronto Star

Subject: Long term health care; Hospitalization; Charter of Rights-Canada; Health facilities; Informed consent; Legislatures; Health care policy

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Title: Law forcing patients out of hospitals faces challenge over Charter rights:   Provincial plan designed to free up acute-care beds

Document 275 of 500

Poilievre back with more flop than flex

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-back-with-more-flop-than-flex/docview/3107645775/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+back+with+more+flop+than+flex&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Parliament is back with a bang and things are not going Pierre Poilievre's way.
It started with a stinging byelection loss for the Conservatives in Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona, where they spent considerable resources trying to win a seat from the NDP. Conservative signs urged voters to reject NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh for crafting a deal with the Grits - "Sellout Singh" as he was branded on posters standing next to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - and for supporting the Liberals' carbon levy.
It's too early to tell how many voters were swayed by the message - Singh certainly tried to distance himself from the Grits by pulling out of the supply and confidence agreement and opened the possibility he'll walk back support for a consumer price on carbon.
But what is clear is that progressive voters, faced with a binary choice, flocked to the NDP. Despite a 16 per cent increase in Conservative support, the NDP vote held steady, likely because Liberal voters shifted to its side.
The result? Conservatives were unable to project themselves as a party with momentum, riding a wave of support after their byelection success in Toronto-St. Paul's in June, and demonstrating to voters that Canadians were rejecting the Liberal/NDP agenda.
It wasn't the only Conservative setback. Welcoming parliamentarians back to Ottawa was a small crowd of "F--k Trudeau" hecklers, who accused the prime minister of being "Satan," of sexually grooming children, and of treason.
If Canadians needed a reminder of whose support Poilievre has been courting, it was right there for all to see. It didn't help that Conservative MP Michael Cooper was photographed with the protesters and thanked on social media for meeting with them. In a statement, Cooper said he was "in no way associated with these individuals" and had just been in a restaurant where he was recognized by them. He accused the Liberals of spreading lies about him.
Wednesday should have been a great day for the Tories. The Liberals had offered them an opposition day in the House next week, and Poilievre announced he would use it to put forward a simple motion of non-confidence in the government.
It was a win-win: he would either get to show voters that Singh and Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet are hypocrites who claim not to support Trudeau but prop up his government, or he would get a federal election.
But Blanchet stole Poilievre's thunder when he said the Bloc would vote against the motion. Blanchet didn't have confidence in the Trudeau government but, he said, he was "reasonably confident" he could get increased pension payments and protection for supply management - and, if the Liberals didn't meet his demands, he was "reasonably confident" he'd get a chance to defeat them.
What was more telling, however, was his take on Poilievre. "I know the Conservatives' values and they are not Quebecers' values," he said. There was nothing in the non-confidence motion, Blanchet said, but Poilievre's "personal and disproportionate ambition" and his "impatience" to rule - which he didn't give "a damn" about.
The next day, it was the NDP's turn. Despite Singh having said the Liberals "can expect no help and no support from us," the New Democrats wisely tried to unpaint themselves out of that corner.
With cover from the Bloc, the NDP tried to safeguard any influence it could have in obtaining policy gains and setting the timing of the next election. Singh set his sights on Poilievre as well, accusing him of wanting to cut pharmacare, health care, pensions and dental care. "Our plan is to stop him."
Though the Bloc and the NDP have their beefs with the Liberals, their leaders acknowledged this past week that the greatest threat to the policies they champion are the Conservatives.
Even when Quebec Premier François Legault called on the Bloc Thursday to side with the Conservatives and defeat the Trudeau government, it was Poilievre who suffered from the endorsement. Quebec's newspapers called Legault to task for suggesting the province would be better off with a prime minister who wants to build a controversial natural gas project, opposes construction of a tramway in Quebec City, and has offered no commitments on health care or dental care.
Meanwhile in the House of Commons, Poilievre was asking the Bloc why it was abandoning Quebec's sovereigntists. Yes, that's right, the leader of the Conservative party - a federalist party - accused the Bloc of not defending the interests of separatists.
Liberal House Leader Karina Gould was all smiles that day. Assured the government would survive, she scheduled another Conservative opposition day for next Thursday, minimizing the Grits' chances of defeat as swiftly as possible.
The Liberals appeared revived in Parliament.
The Conservatives sounded like trained seals. Over and over, they stood up to repeat the same line - "after nine years of the NDP-Liberal government, taxes are up, prices are up, crime is up and time is up" - and to demand a "carbon-tax election" that no other party wants.
And that leaves Poilievre with other problems on his hands. He tightly controls his ship, but it has unhappy sailors.
Conservatives MPs are mostly banned from official travel with other parliamentarians. Whether that is, as some suggest, to prevent them from befriending other MPs, or so that Poilievre can accuse the other parties of spending taxpayers' money unnecessarily, the result is discouraged members who want to be more than partisan parrots.
Another problem for the Conservatives is that encouraging economic data is starting to challenge Poilievre's talking points. Inflation is now down to two per cent, the lowest rate since February 2021. Gas prices are down.
Interest rates are coming down. Even Canada's greenhouse gas emissions are down (although still far from the 2030 target).
If Canadians start feeling like life isn't all that bad, what will Poilievre offer then?
CAPTION: From a stinging byelection loss in Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona to a Bloc-led setback to his planned non-confidence motion against the Liberals this week, things are not going Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's way.Adrian WyldThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Subject: Conservatism; Voters; Tax elections; Political analysis; Election results; Special elections; Economic indicators

Business indexing term: Subject: Tax elections Economic indicators

Document 276 of 500

For all his bluster, Ford hasn't been much of a housing champion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/all-his-bluster-ford-hasnt-been-much-housing/docview/3107645774/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=For+all+his+bluster%2C+Ford+hasn%27t+been+much+of+a+housing+champion&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Earlier this month, Scott Aitchison, Conservative MP for Parry Sound-Muskoka, posted a video he filmed at 91 Barton Ave. in Toronto's Seaton Village neighbourhood. The address is notable because a proposal to build a three-storey, eight-unit multiplex apartment building plus two laneway units here was rejected twice by Toronto's Committee of Adjustment.
The committee decides if applications that don't conform to existing regulations are allowed or not. The Barton project required some minor variances but was denied in the spring. The developer revised plans but it was rejected again in the summer.
Never mind that three blocks away on Barton, far larger apartment buildings exist, happily part of the neighbourhood for decades, or that Seaton Village, like many parts of Toronto, has gone down in population over the last 70 years, or that there are two subway stations nearby, an ideal location for more density.
All of this instigated a rightful fury in housing and civic circles. Over at Spacing, columnist John Lorinc argued we should burn down the Committee of Adjustment and create something that isn't a tool used by NIMBYs to block reasonable and needed projects during a housing crisis.
For his part, Aitchison strutted down the alleyway next to 91 and railed against Trudeau, Toronto City Hall gatekeepers and its NIMBY residents too. Perhaps he would do the same if residents in his own riding - in towns like Huntsville, Bracebridge, Gravenhurst or Muskoka - objected to a similar development.
One hopes, but he strutted in Toronto and it's hard to argue with some of his aspersions. The Barton situation is another grievously embarrassing Toronto episode, instigated by both the committee and residents. Mayor Chow could and should be leading some of the civic castigation as it undermines her own housing goals, but some of her progressive allies on council can be quite NIMBY themselves when it comes to adding density.
It's dismal: few have the courage to stand up to powerful and selfish homeowners.
Still, Toronto aside, it's extremely conspicuous that Aitchison chose to go after just Trudeau and Toronto, leapfrogging over the great big legislative and political elephant in the middle: Ontario. The province has incredible power over cities, not the least of which is the Ontario Planning Act. As Torontonians have experienced first-hand, Premier Ford has also not hesitated to meddle in city affairs, particularly when cutting the number of councillors in half during the 2018 election. Ford is willing to intervene when he fancies.
If Ford wanted to, he could abolish Toronto's Committee of Adjustment. He could clear out every gate and its keeper at City Hall. He could allow 10-unit apartment buildings to be built anywhere in the city (not a bad idea, actually, but Ford even backtracked on expanding permissions for smaller fourplexes last winter). He could allow skyscrapers on Barton if he and his party wanted to.
He could do whatever he wants to facilitate housing but he isn't, and Aitchison and his boss Pierre Poilievre aren't talking about it. They also aren't talking about Premier Ford's failure to deliver on housing and deliberate undermining of his own pro-housing bluster. Just look at the considerable evidence.
As Noor Javed reported in the Star last week, the Ford government fast-tracked the development of a block of land in Vaughan using a Ministerial Zoning Order (MZO), overriding the usual process, yet nothing has been built. Then the Star's David Rider reported on an affordable housing project in Conservative MPP Stan Cho's ward where the government has refused to issue an MZO for this desperately needed modular housing project that has some NIMBY constituents upset. So the housing rots away in a warehouse.
Ford has largely ignored the strong prescriptions set out by his own housing task force, and last week Global News reported that the "massive density" Ford proposed around transit stations is gone because Ontario PCs gutted their own housing bill. Ford, a resident of Toronto himself, doesn't sound like much of a housing champion - in fact, he sounds exactly like the kind of gatekeepers and local NIMBYs Aitchison was lambasting.
Poilievre has gotten a lot of traction with the gatekeeper rants, but Ford has proven he and his party are Ontario's most powerful gatekeepers. This is a problem for the federal conservatives because everyone who has watched Ford has heard the bluster about housing and they have seen how Ford can run roughshod over a city when he wants to, but they've also seen his government actively gut their own housing bill, backtracked on pro-housing positions and even prevented and failed to get actual housing built.
Toronto doesn't get off the hook, but the silence from Poilievre and company on Ford is conspicuous. How do we know they're not the same kind of conservatives who talk tough but don't actually follow through?
CAPTION: Toronto's Committee of Adjustment denied an application for a small multiplex apartment building here, at 91 Barton Ave. The premier has the power to lift barriers to housing projects like this, but his record suggests he doesn't want to, Shawn Micallef writes.Shawn Micallef
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Committees; Affordable housing

Document 277 of 500

Liberals vow 'more for you' as Crombie kick-starts party: Leader promises to restore a sense of opportunity

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/liberals-vow-more-you-as-crombie-kick-starts/docview/3107645742/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+vow+%27more+for+you%27+as+Crombie+kick-starts+party%3A+Leader+promises+to+restore+a+sense+of+opportunity&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "More for you" is the new message as Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie looks to kick-start her party's fortunes and prepare for a provincial election that could be called sooner than expected.
In an exclusive, sit-down interview with the Star, Crombie also said a new ad - one that she's just finished filming - will begin airing when the legislature resumes in late October so Ontarians can get to know her and what direction the party is going in.
But experts say Crombie faces a tough road ahead, trying to rebuild a party after back-to-back dismal showings in the last two elections and as polls continue to show it a distant second to the Ontario PCs, while also feeling the weight of the federal Liberals' woes.
"We truly believe the only people getting ahead in Ontario today are the wealthy friends, rich insiders. We've seen that time and time again," Crombie told the Star, referring to several controversies, including the Ford government's recent decision to speed up beer and cooler sales in corner stores at a cost it has pegged at $225 million, but she's estimated to be as high as $1 billion.
"It's the lobbyists, it's the well-connected, rich insiders, friends of the premier," said Crombie, adding the Liberals will focus on the theme "it's going to be less for them, more for you."
In a spirited speech Saturday to the more-than 1,000 gathered for the Liberals' annual meeting and policy convention, Crombie took aim at the Ford government - mentioning the premier more than 20 times in her 20-minute speech. She repeatedly spoke of health care, saying the top priority of a Liberal government would be fixing an ailing system and ensure everyone has a family doctor.
"Our hospital hallways are crammed with patients who can't get care - 2,000 people, every single day, here in Ontario lay on stretchers in break rooms or crammed in corridors while they wait for hospital care," she said.
"Instead of fixing health care, Doug spends his time on a billion-dollar scheme to put beer in the 7-Eleven a bit sooner. It's time for a government in Ontario that's focused on you."
Crombie, who spoke of her humble roots as the child of single mom who grew up in her family's rooming house, said "too many people are struggling to pay the bills in the province ... that's not who we are. That's not how things should be. People deserve more - and we're going to get it for them."
She recalled how her grandfather worked as a janitor at the old Globe and Mail building in downtown Toronto for 40 years, and how she was "hit hard" with emotion going there years later as a federal member of Parliament to do an interview.
"Hard work and education used to be the recipe for success in Ontario," she said. "We need to get back to that Ontario. We need to restore that sense of opportunity for this generation - that's what we need to fight for."
Some insiders are frustrated about why the Liberal leader, who was elected nine months ago, has not been more visible, urging Crombie to boost her public profile - especially if an election is called earlier than 2026.
But Jonathan Rose, head of the political studies department at Queen's University, said "if she's keeping a low profile because she is working with party leaders and party presidents across the province, and candidates, and if she's keeping a low profile because she's ensuring that she is election-ready in terms of fundraising ... that's a great thing at this stage."
But the danger, he added, is that "she runs the risk of being defined by the government ... and if there's no alternative, competing messaging, that messaging will stick."
Since becoming leader, Crombie has spent time with her caucus, been at Queen's Park and travelled "to the key ridings that were important for us, continuing to get to know stakeholders and their issues," she said, adding she's also spoken to people across the province, attending dozens of events over the summer.
Crombie told the Star she's also spent time in rural and farming areas - where the Liberals have no seats - listening to voters there "to see how we can better speak to those communities, because they haven't heard from us in quite a long time."
She also said the PC's efforts, using attack ads to paint her as someone who is out of touch and who would raise taxes, haven't worked.
The Liberals, which now have confirmed or soon-to-be confirmed candidates in 19 ridings, also unveiled a new logo at the weekend convention - held in a city that was a one-time Liberal stronghold where all three city ridings have gone to the NDP in the past decade, with the PCs holding Elgin-Middlesex-London.
Crombie said she feels the party had momentum coming into the weekend after Thursday's byelection in the Bay of Quinte riding, where Liberal candidate Sean Kelly came in second but boosted the party's performance well beyond its results in the riding in the 2022 general election.
Introduced as "Energizer Bonnie" to the crowd of party faithful, Crombie said - to cheers - the Liberals "are the only opposition party able to take on Doug Ford, and that's exactly what we are going to do."
CAPTION: In her speech Saturday to more than 1,000 party faithful at the Liberals' convention in London, Ont., Bonnie Crombie said her government's top priority would be to fix the health-care system.Sophie Bouquillon Special to the Star
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Document 278 of 500

City on the right track, while Ford blames bikes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-on-right-track-while-ford-blames-bikes/docview/3107645728/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+on+the+right+track%2C+while+Ford+blames+bikes&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: For politicians, there is an art to avoiding questions in a way that makes it appear you're answering them. It's fair to say Ontario provincial Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria has not mastered it. You never saw a person repeatedly respond to questions in a way that so obviously, transparently ignored their entire topic.
Friday morning, at a press conference that was supposed to be about the Finch West LRT (short takeaway: still no opening date), three reporters in a row asked Sarkaria about reports that he and Premier Doug Ford would soon ban bike lanes on streets where they replace car-traffic lanes. Each time, he blathered on about transit initiatives and generically looking for "ways to make life easier." A CBC reporter called him out directly for an answer that had nothing to do with the question. He persisted in ignoring the subject of the question.
The entire substance-free performance was enough to make one dream that perhaps this idiotic ban on bike lanes wasn't going to make it past the trial balloon stage.
Dream on. According to sources talking to the Star's Queen's Park bureau, Ford will introduce the ban next month.
No surprise there, really. Ford, like his brother Rob before him, has long made bashing bike lanes a defining political principle.
And the move may pay off politically in the areas of Toronto and the GTA where Ford stands any hope of competing. Spend any time in Etobicoke or Mississauga and you'll hear plenty of whining about how bike lanes on Bloor Street West and Eglinton Avenue are snarling everything up. Seems to me that it is construction - especially work on Ford's beloved underground LRT line - backing things up. But you cannot convince people that bike lanes, or anything that removes car lanes, isn't the source of the problem.
It is one of those common-sense things - like that the Earth is flat by the clear evidence of our own eyes - that some people are resistant to hearing any evidence about. And make no mistake, there is plenty of evidence that redesigning roads to give bikes and transit their own lanes is not a significant contributor to traffic congestion. Often they actually makes car traffic move faster and safer. They saw this in New York City, where they tracked car travel times through the GPS units in taxis. We've seen it in plenty of places in Toronto where separated bike lanes have been installed.
And at a press conference this week on Spadina Avenue, where it was buses that have recently gotten the priority dedicated-lane treatment, deputy mayor Ausma Malik noted that after one traffic lane was reserved for buses that sped them up dramatically, travel times for cars on the same route "decreased or stayed the same across all time periods."
This is hard for many people to believe for at least two reasons.
First, even if traffic flow is the same or better than before, it still sucks. Because traffic everywhere in Toronto always sucks. It's just that when there's a lane that cars could drive in right there dedicated to something else, drivers have an apparent culprit they can blame.
Second, it seems to defy logic that more car lanes wouldn't mean faster travel. It isn't obvious to most people that much of traffic congestion is caused by cars frequently changing lanes, so streamlined roads with fewer lanes often flow more smoothly.
According to a CBC reporter speaking at Sarkaria's press conference Friday, even the province's internal documents proposing the ban suggest it may not help relieve congestion at all. Which aligns with expert reaction to the proposal.
Meanwhile, the city released its own congestion-fighting plan Friday. Among the moves it contemplates is asking the province to allow automated cameras to enforce new, higher "blocking-the-box" fines, and allocating more money to traffic wardens to prevent people from committing that offence.
These are both moves that would actually help fight congestion. Where traffic wardens are present, blocking the box infractions virtually disappear. When that happens, the grid doesn't lock. Traffic moves better.
Aside from their negligible impact on car travel times, bike lanes get more people onto bikes by providing them a faster, safer option. That makes the lives of those cyclists better, and also means there are fewer cars on the road. But we already know Premier Ford doesn't give a flying fig about making cycle commuting better or more attractive, so put that all aside.
If Ford is actually concerned with moving cars better, he could accomplish that goal much more effectively by leaving bike lanes alone and joining Toronto's fight to prevent blocking intersections through automatic camera enforcement power, and sharing more dollars for traffic wardens.
Hard to fit on a bumper sticker, I know. But it has the potential advantage of actually working, which you'd hope would still count for something.
CAPTION: Sources told the Star that, starting next month, Premier Doug Ford's government plans to ban new bike lanes that require removal of a lane of vehicle traffic.Steve RussellToronto Star
CREDIT: edward Keenan Opinion

Subject: Automobiles; Bans; Traffic flow; Bicycles; Traffic congestion; Traffic control; Roads & highways; Criticism

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug; Sarkaria, Prabmeet

Title: City on the right track, while Ford blames bikes

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

First page: A.8

Section: News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 279 of 500

'More for you' to be Ontario Liberals' new message as Bonnie Crombie tries to kick-start party

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-you-be-ontario-liberals-new-message-as/docview/3107645765/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27More+for+you%27+to+be+Ontario+Liberals%27+new+message+as+Bonnie+Crombie+tries+to+kick-start+party&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: LONDON, ONT. - "More for you" is the new message as Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie looks to kick-start her party's fortunes and prepare for a provincial election that could be called sooner than expected.
In an exclusive, sit-down interview with the Star, Crombie also said a new ad - one that she's just finished filming - will begin airing when the legislature resumes in late October so Ontarians can get to know her and what direction the party is going in.
But experts say Crombie faces a tough road ahead, trying to rebuild a party after back-to-back dismal showings in the last two elections and as polls continue to show it a distant second to the Ontario PCs, while also feeling the weight of the federal Liberals' woes.
"We truly believe the only people getting ahead in Ontario today are the wealthy friends, rich insiders. We've seen that time and time again," Crombie told the Star, referring to a number of controversies, including the Ford government's recent decision to speed up beer and cooler sales in corner stores at a cost it has pegged at $225 million, but she's estimated to be as high as $1 billion.
"It's the lobbyists, it's the well-connected, rich insiders, friends of the premier," said Crombie, adding the Liberals will focus on the theme "it's going to be less for them, more for you."
In a spirited speech Saturday to the more-than 1,000 gathered for the Liberals' annual meeting and policy convention, Crombie took aim at the Ford government - mentioning the premier more than 20 times in her 20-minute speech. She repeatedly spoke of health care, saying the top priority of a Liberal government would be fixing an ailing system and ensure everyone has a family doctor.
"Our hospital hallways are crammed with patients who can't get care - 2,000 people, every single day, here in Ontario lay on stretchers in break rooms or crammed in corridors while they wait for hospital care," she said.
"Instead of fixing health care, Doug spends his time on a billion-dollar scheme to put beer in the 7-Eleven a bit sooner. It's time for a government in Ontario that's focused on you."
Crombie, who spoke of her humble roots as the child of single mom who grew up in her family's rooming house, said "too many people are struggling to pay the bills in the province ... that's not who we are. That's not how things should be. People deserve more - and we're going to get it for them."
She recalled how her grandfather worked as a janitor at the old Globe and Mail building in downtown Toronto for 40 years, and how she was "hit hard" with emotion going there years later as a federal member of Parliament to do an interview.
"Hard work and education used to be the recipe for success in Ontario," she said. "We need to get back to that Ontario. We need to restore that sense of opportunity for this generation - that's what we need to fight for."
Some insiders are frustrated about why the Liberal leader, who was elected nine months ago, has not been more visible, urging Crombie to boost her public profile - especially if an election is called earlier than 2026.
But Jonathan Rose, head of the political studies department at Queen's University, said "if she's keeping a low profile because she is working with party leaders and party presidents across the province, and candidates, and if she's keeping a low profile because she's ensuring that she is election-ready in terms of fundraising ... that's a great thing at this stage."
But the danger, he added, is that "she runs the risk of being defined by the government ... and if there's no alternative, competing messaging, that messaging will stick."
Since becoming leader, Crombie has spent time with her caucus, been at Queen's Park and travelled "to the key ridings that were important for us, continuing to get to know stakeholders and their issues," she said, adding she's also spoken to people across the province, attending dozens of events over the summer.
Crombie told the Star she's also spent time in rural and farming areas - where the Liberals have no seats - listening to voters there "to see how we can better speak to those communities, because they haven't heard from us in quite a long time."
She also said the PC's efforts, using attack ads to paint her as someone who is out of touch and who would raise taxes, haven't worked.
The Liberals, which now have confirmed or soon-to-be confirmed candidates in 19 ridings, also unveiled a new logo at the weekend convention - held in a city that was a one-time Liberal stronghold where all three city ridings have gone to the NDP in the last decade, with the PCs holding Elgin-Middlesex-London.
Crombie said she feels the party had momentum coming into the weekend after Thursday's byelection in the Bay of Quinte riding, where Liberal candidate Sean Kelly came in second but boosted the party's performance well beyond its results in the riding in the 2022 general election.
"I'm delighted," she said. "I mean, obviously you always want to win, but I think it demonstrates now, a second or third time, that we really are the party that people are looking for if they're not going to vote for Doug Ford, and they want to see a change of government."
Introduced as "Energizer Bonnie" to the crowd of party faithful, Crombie said - to cheers - the Liberals "are the only opposition party able to take on Doug Ford, and that's exactly what we are going to do.".
She said the provincial government under Ford "spends more than ever, but less and less on you."
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie, at an event in Peterborough this past May, says a new ad that she's just wrapped up filming will begin airing when the legislature resumes in late October so Ontarians can get to know her and the party's direction.

Subject: Provincial elections; Election results; Opposition parties; Negative campaigning; Legislatures; Political advertising
