Document 331 of 500

In Ontario, we make records for the wrong reasons

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-we-make-records-wrong-reasons/docview/3105447415/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=In+Ontario%2C+we+make+records+for+the+wrong+reasons&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Making records for the wrong reasons
Ontario broke a record this past year, but it's not something we should be proud of: Keenan, Sept. 11
Reporter Edward Keenan's excellent column contains insightful commentary on the complexity of human nature and "political will." He wrote that the positive aspect of this tragedy is that food banks, and the people who donate and volunteer, or work there, are, "a testament to our collective capacity to help when we see suffering." However, they are not the sustainable solution to hunger for one million people in Ontario. Carolyn Stewart, the CEO of Feed Ontario, affirms that, "We're there for an emergency provision of food when people are most in need, but we're not a solution to food insecurity." Food insecurity is no longer an emergency. It has become a regular part of life.
There are solutions to food insecurity in Ontario. During the pandemic the federal government quickly initiated a universally accessible financial support program (CERB) to assist people. Policymakers also know there are many reports advocating guaranteed livable incomes that would help to provide dignity, hope and health for everyone. A truly livable income would eliminate the necessity for food banks, and the excessive use of volunteer time and energy that Keenan also documented. If such a universal program is a bridge too far in the struggle against food insecurity, a livable minimum wage is another sustainable option, coupled with realistic income support for people who cannot be employed for a variety of reasons. These are just two possibilities.
Keenan concluded his column with a challenge to all of us, including the politically powerful. He wrote: "We are good at putting in the effort to mange suffering when we see it. We need to get a whole lot better at our efforts to solve the problems that cause it." In other words, a significant part of the problem is lack of political will.
Allan Baker, Scarborough
Debates aren't indicative of who will win presidency
Kamala Harris won the debate. So why I am so scared? Phillips, Sept. 13
According to the most recent polls, 60 per cent of Americans did not watch the June debate between U.S. President Joe Biden and Donald Trump. It's not that they don't care, they don't want to see the idiocies that go on during a debate. It's a waste of one's valuable time. The Democrats are celebrating a victory and so are the Republicans. History has shown that winning a debate has very little impact on who will win the presidency.
Rita Maio, Woodbridge, Ont.
It boggles my mind watching and reading about the U.S. election. The Republicans continually focus on the inflation rate that the country has encountered in the last four years as being the fault of the Democrats. The Democrats do little to defend the situation. Are the American people so self-centered that they fail to see the whole world suffered with this situation?
Bill Kelly, Keswick, Ont.
Where is Bonnie Crombie?
Every day we hear Premier Doug Ford say and do many really dumb things, including liberalizing the sale of alcohol in convenience stores and gas stations, closing the Science Centre, trying to mess with the Greenbelt and building the unnecessary Highway 413. My question is simple. Where is Liberal leader Bonnie Crombie? We never hear or see her. If she wants my vote, I need to learn what the Liberals think and would do. She seems to have gone into hiding.
Jack Hughes, Welland
Never OK to disparage someone
Pierre Poilievre's nickname for Jagmeet Singh is 'racist,' Liberal minister charges, Sept. 12
Why does Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre thinks it's OK to disparage someone's name? Of course he knows that by bullying Jagmeet Singh, the NDP leader, he is pandering to some in his base who are not ready for a prime minister wearing a turban. Singh knows what he's up against in regards to that type discrimination. I consider him an honest and brave politician. Would Poilievre ever ridicule a member of the Bloc Québécois in that way? Or would the political costs be too high?
Dorothy Low, Richmond Hill, Ont.
I don't believe the nickname for Jagmeet Singh is racist - calling someone a sellout has nothing to do with their race - it does confirm that Trump-wannabe Pierre Poilievre is happy to take another page out of the orange fraudster's playbook, resorting to childish nicknames to ridicule his opponents.
Andrew Elrick, Whitby, Ont.
Build a gondola!
To get visitors to Toronto Islands, the most efficient and cost effective method would be a gondola over the shipping channel. It would be much less expensive from both a capital and operating cost standpoint, be much more attractive as it becomes a tourist destination by itself and much more efficient in taking visitors both to and from the Islands. It is fascinating that this option has been ignored for decades.
Eric Dempster, Markham, Ont.
Federal Liberals have expired
Being tired of Justin Trudeau is not a good reason to vote him out, Sept. 8
You asked your readers if we would choose a prime minister like we choose bagels. In the last 100 years, Canada had 62 years of Liberal leadership. How much more time do Liberals need to deliver results? Canadians have witnessed a painfully slow decline. The analogy between a 10-minute experience and a major decision like voting is wrong. Voting can affect life expectancy. That's how critical voting is, compared to eating a bagel. This is what we mean by being tired - we are lacking opportunities, we have less purchasing power, people are unable to seek treatment and die waiting, people are cutting costs on medications, our kids will not be able to buy a home and our diet is changing because we can't afford decent meals. Do you want us to keep eating this expired bagel?
Melisa Barrilli, Toronto
Who is harassing who?
Conservatives say they have solution for online harassment after Liberals 'failed,' Sept. 12
The federal Conservatives have a solution to prevent online harassment? That's hilarious. To use a little schoolyard terminology, I say those who smelt it probably dealt it.
Richard Kadziewicz, Scarborough
CAPTION: Volunteers work at Daily Bread Food Bank's food sorting operation.

Subject: Food security; Leadership; Cyberbullying; Food programs; Prime ministers

Document 332 of 500

Ontario's fiscal watchdog probes cost of early move to put beer, wine in convenience stores

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontarios-fiscal-watchdog-probes-cost-early-move/docview/3105447411/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+fiscal+watchdog+probes+cost+of+early+move+to+put+beer%2C+wine+in+convenience+stores&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's fiscal watchdog is investigating the cost of Premier Doug Ford's deal to put beer and wine in convenience stores more than a year early to see if it will give taxpayers a hangover.
The probe by the Financial Accountability Office (FAO) comes at the request of Ontario's Liberals, which have dubbed the alcohol policy change announced last spring "a billion-dollar boozedoggle."
Sales of wine, beer, cider and ready-to-drink cocktails began in about 4,000 convenience stores Sept. 5.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie charged Monday that the Progressive Conservative government is "spending potentially more than $1 billion on booze, rather than hiring the nurses, doctors and health-care workers that we need" at a time when 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a family physician.
Ford has maintained the cost of an early end of the "master framework agreement" with the Beer Store - which was to run until the end of next year - is $225 million.
But critics say the tab could be three or four times higher because of an additional $375 million in rebates to the Beer Store, owned by Labatt, Molson and Sleeman, in addition to the cost of a 10 per cent discount for retailers that will buy the beer, wine and other alcoholic beverages from the LCBO. That could cost the provincial liquor monopoly $150 million a year.
Broader alcohol sales will begin at big box stores by the end of October.
Ford shrugged off the FAO probe Monday at a campaign-style stop in Windsor, where he pledged over $9 million to expand a training centre for electricians and autoworkers as Ontario builds more electric vehicle battery plants.
"I think it's a great deal," he told reporters, citing greater convenience to consumers who can also buy wine and beer in many supermarkets in addition to Beer Stores and LCBO outlets.
Ford also stumbled over the timing of the next election, referring to it taking place this year when the next scheduled provincewide vote is not until June 2026.
"I'm sorry, 2024 was a slip," said the premier, who has ruled out an early election this fall but has repeatedly left the door open to calling one next spring.
In a letter to Liberal MPP Stephanie Bowman (Don Valley West), her party's finance critic, the FAO said it will "estimate the financial costs and benefits of accelerated expansion ... and compare these fiscal impacts to a scenario where the province expands alcohol sales" as per the previous plan starting in 2026.
A report from the watchdog agency is expected early next year, providing its investigators receive "timely information from the province." There is a risk that information considered "commercially sensitive" could impact the results, the agency said.
That means the report could be available if Ford - who enjoys a solid lead in the latest Abacus public opinion poll - were to call an early election. If Ford were to send voters to the polls early next year it would happen ahead of an expected October 2025 federal vote, that polls suggest could put a cost-cutting Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre in the prime minister's office. There are also concerns the RCMP investigation of the Ford government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal could be released right in the regular election cycle.
Ford has hailed the expansion of alcohol sales to convenience stores as a long-awaited fulfilment of a 2018 election promise.
CAPTION: Alcohol sales began in about 4,000 convenience stores Sept. 5.

Subject: Provincial elections; Election results; Alcohol; Beer; Liquor; Convenience stores

Title: Ontario's fiscal watchdog probes cost of early move to put beer, wine in convenience stores

Document 333 of 500

More child-care supports coming for women working in the trades, Ford government says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-child-care-supports-coming-women-working/docview/3105447405/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+child-care+supports+coming+for+women+working+in+the+trades%2C+Ford+government+says&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province plans to introduce legislation this fall that will provide child-care supports for women working in the trades, Labour Minister David Piccini said.
The move comes as the government ups its efforts to boost the number of women working in construction - and keep them there - amid a 30-per-cent jump in the number of female workers registering as apprentices.
"There's no one silver bullet, but focused round tables and surveys of women have shown a couple of things. One, personal protective equipment (PPE) matters, and for too long we've had women wearing PPE that's designed for men or larger body types, so we've mandated that in construction in a previous working-for-workers bill," Piccini told the Star in a telephone interview before attending a LevelUp! careers fair last week in Cobourg, Ont., one of many hands-on events to be held across the province to promote the trades to teens and their parents.
The province also has legislation mandating large construction sites to keep washrooms clean and provide menstrual products, something tradeswomen have been asking for.
"Retention is a key piece, and one of the things we've heard in the last while is around opportunities for mentorship - programming that the premier and I are looking at going forward," added Piccini.
"In addition to that, child care is a big piece for women in the skilled trades. Ontario does support women going through their apprenticeships with child-care support, and we're looking at further building on that in the upcoming working-for-workers legislation" expected to be introduced after the legislature resumes, Piccini added.
While the number of women in the trades is on the rise, comprising four per cent of the workforce, retention remains a challenge, with about one-third leaving after a year. A 2022 survey by the Ontario Building and Construction Tradeswomen found child care was among the main challenges women in the sector face.
They said more maternity and child-care help is needed, and three-quarters said the hours of construction have posed problems for them. More than half reported turning down work because they were unable to find any care for their children. Most agreed that there's little flexibility for them when raising a family.
"I encourage more women get into the skilled trades," Premier Doug Ford said at last Wednesday's announcement of $26 million in funding for a construction training centre in Vaughan.
"Our skilled-trade workers are the backbone of this province."
While the province does offer some child-care support for women when they are apprentices, "we're looking to build on those child-care supports in the year ahead, Premier Ford and I," Piccini said.
"These are just some of the many things I think that contribute to retention."
The government has introduced a number of initiatives to increase interest in the trades, including mandating one high school tech course and creating an easier path to apprenticeship for teens while they continue to earn a high school diploma.
The province recorded an increase of about 30 per cent in female apprentice registrations in construction from 2022-23 to 2023-24.
When compared to rates in 2017, there's been a 227 per cent rise.
CAPTION: Labour Minister David Piccini is shown in the Ontario legislature on Nov. 28, 2023.

Subject: Construction; Legislation; Personal protective equipment; Child care; Apprenticeship

Title: More child-care supports coming for women working in the trades, Ford government says

Document 334 of 500

Today's byelections could be a preview of things to come + a modular housing project left to rot

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-byelections-could-be-preview-things-come/docview/3105447116/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+byelections+could+be+a+preview+of+things+to+come+%2B+a+modular+housing+project+left+to+rot&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Monday, Sept. 16 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the predictive importance of today's byelections, a languishing modular housing project and another attempt on Trump's life.
DON'T MISSToday's byelections could be a sign of things to come
Two byelections, one in Montreal and another in Winnipeg, could be a glimpse of our electoral future. To the east, the Liberals are fending off challengers from the NDP and Bloc, hoping to prevent another surprise loss like the one they suffered in Toronto-St. Paul's not long ago. To the west, the New Democrats hope to stave off a strong Tory candidate. Raisa Patel sets you up for tonight's nail-biters.
A game of chicken: The Liberals have said they won't stand in the way of a parliamentary vote that could trigger an election, a move that likely has Pierre Poilievre ready to pounce.Past is prologue: Want a sneak peak into the campaign trail? The Star tracked where all three big party leaders went this summer. Here's what it says about their priorities going into what could be the last full summer to court voters.Why is this modular home project rotting in a warehouse?
It's a tale of two supportive housing projects. Though announced at the same time, the one in East York is housing 59 people with rent geared to their income, while its counterpart in Willowdale has been stonewalled. David Rider explains how a quick solution to the housing crisis turned into a slog.
Collecting dust: The shelved modular project has inflated the cost of the Willowdale project to $36-million, from an earlier estimate of $14.6 million, for the 59-unit project.Cho's woes: Part of the delay is attributable to local PC MPP Stan Cho, who has called for more and more community consultations for the past two years.Donald Trump is safe after another assassination attempt
The former U.S. president and Republican presidential candidate was the target of "what appears to be an attempted assassination" at his Florida golf club on Sunday, the FBI said. Agents fired at a gunman whose AK-style rifle was sticking out through the bushes, nearly 400 yards away from where Trump was playing. The man fled but was arrested. Here's how Trump has responded.
Not the first time: Trump was the target of an assassination attempt in July, when a bullet grazed his ear at a rally.Just a coincidence, probably: The former president had just sent out a social media post earlier in the day that read, "I HATE TAYLOR SWIFT!" The pop star endorsed Kamala Harris last week.WHAT ELSE

So why have no houses been built yet?More child-care supports coming for women working in the trades, Ford government says.The return of ISIS: How a Toronto case fits into the global resurgence of a terror group we thought had been defeated.Why a U.S. military academy welcomed this Canadian general's irreplaceable documents about the Rwanda genocide.The story of a stolen Winston Churchill portrait has captivated industry experts. Accept it, Toronto: traffic will never go back to the way it was. Here's why that's a good thing.

Colours came to life in the mind of the cartographer from small town Saskatchewan as he walked through Toronto in May - purple for Mandarin, green for Italian, a dark red for Tagalog. Here's how McPhee's map shows the colourful coexistence of diverse language and cultures in the city.

Province vows supports for women in trades

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-vows-supports-women-trades/docview/3104952087/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+vows+supports+for+women+in+trades&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The province plans to introduce legislation this fall that will provide child-care supports for women working in the trades, says Labour Minister David Piccini.
The move comes as the government ups its efforts to boost the number of women working in construction - and keep them there - amid a 30 per cent jump in the number of females registering as apprentices.
"There's no one silver bullet, but focused roundtables and surveys of women have shown a couple of things. One, personal protective equipment matters, and for too long we've had women wearing PPE that's designed for men or larger body types, so we've mandated that in construction in a previous working-for-workers bill," Piccini told the Star in a telephone interview before attending a LevelUp! careers fair in Cobourg, one of a number of hands-on events to be held across the province to promote the trades to teens and their parents.
The province also has legislation mandating large construction sites keep washrooms clean and provide menstrual products, something tradeswomen have been asking for.
"Retention is a key piece, and one of the things we've heard in the last while is around opportunities for mentorship - programming that the premier and I are looking at going forward," added Piccini.
"In addition to that, child care is a big piece for women in the skilled trades. Ontario does support women going through their apprenticeships with child-care support, and we're looking at further building on that in the upcoming working-for-workers legislation" expected to be introduced after the legislature resumes, Piccini added.
While the number of women in the trades is on the rise, comprising four per cent of the workforce, retention remains a challenge, with about one-third leaving after a year. A 2022 survey by the Ontario Building and Construction Tradeswomen found child care among the main challenges women in the sector face.
They say more maternity and child-care help is needed, and three-quarters said the hours of construction have posed problems for them. More than half reported turning down work because they were unable to find any care for their children. Most say there's little flexibility for them when raising a family.
"I encourage more women get into the skilled trades," Premier Doug Ford said at last Wednesday's announcement of $26 million in funding for a construction training centre in Vaughan.
While the province does offer some child-care support for women when they are apprentices, "we're looking to build on those child-care supports in the year ahead," Piccini said.
The government has introduced a number of initiatives to increase interest in the trades, including mandating one high school tech course and creating an easier path to apprenticeship for teens while earning a high school diploma.
CAPTION: Ontario Labour Minister David Piccini said the province is working on improving child-care supports.

CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Toronto Star Queen's Park Bureau

Document 336 of 500

Traffic will never go back to the way it was

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/traffic-will-never-go-back-way-was/docview/3104951765/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Traffic+will+never+go+back+to+the+way+it+was&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: My doctor has an annoying habit. When I visit with an ailment or pain, looking for a cure, he will sometimes tap a little medicine bottle in his office with the word "acceptance" handwritten on it. Whatever my problem is - aching knees from jogging, disappearing hair - he will say that it's just part of life, aging and I have to accept it, all while tapping that bottle.
It's annoying because I would rather have drugs. Powerful drugs. Drugs to make the problem go away and to instantly feel good and young again. Acceptance, however, is really just facing reality and is a treatment Toronto could use right now to help with its traffic woes.
Everyone is talking about Toronto traffic. It's what we make incessant small talk about with acquaintances and strangers. It's even what visiting celebrities and tourists talk about. It's what politicians and other civil society groups are trying to solve.
The latter efforts have been futile so far because we've never fully faced reality. That is, accepting Toronto's traffic problems are not ever going to be cured. Not in the way we've been going about it, thinking getting around in a car will one day be easy again, a "Make Driving Great Again" fever dream as seen in car commercials.
I've long thought of Toronto as a teenage city, still becoming an adult but experiencing all the growing pains and awkwardness teenagers do. Adulthood is very soon but memories of childhood are still quite present. There was a time not long ago, in living memory for many Torontonians, when it was easy to drive to most places in the city and park nearby.
When viewing photos of the city from the 1980s and even the 1990s, I'm often struck by how comparatively empty the streets seem, at least in the neighbourhoods. Understanding this is a window into today's approach to traffic: the good old days weren't so long ago and hard to let go of. Were they that good, though? In other parts of the city, traffic has been a problem for decades.
When I was a kid in Windsor during the 1980s, I would plot out, in the days before Highway 407, elaborate 401 detours around Toronto on paper maps during our annual drive to Nova Scotia to visit family. Too many times we were caught in midday traffic jams through Toronto, the likes of which we never saw at home. Steaming hot pavement, semi trucks, noise, keeping the air conditioning off so the old car wouldn't overheat: it was a dystopia to avoid.
Since then, "drugs" in the form of more lanes and more highways, including the 407, haven't made the 401 or other highways any better. Proposed Highway 413, offered like another drug to cure traffic problems, will just generate more traffic, creating more car-dependent sprawl and encouraging more people to drive, feeding more cars onto existing roads.
The drugs don't work.
This is the proven phenomenon of induced demand: new roads create more traffic. Even back in the 1930s, as described in the epic book "The Power Broker," when Robert Moses built his beloved parkways in and around New York City, they became jammed within months. That was during the Depression even, when fewer people could purchase cars.
There are interventions that can help traffic of course, like my colleague Matt Elliott's column on how employing more traffic cameras could benefit flow if only Premier Doug Ford let us. And when new transit lines like the Eglinton Crosstown, Finch West and the Ontario Line subway eventually begin to operate, it will be a game changer, but a big dose of acceptance now would free Toronto to do other things that need to be done.
Ed Keenan has written in the Star on how the excellent idea that was the King Street Transit Priority Corridor has been allowed to degrade in design and enforcement. An adult Toronto would actually fix King Street permanently, but then take steps to make other transit lines a priority, too.
Toronto is a city of three million people where a streetcar packed with up to 130 people can get stuck for multiple light cycles behind one person in a car turning left. Packed buses on routes like Dufferin Street have little or no priority and crawl along. Transit needs to be the best option to get around in order to coax people out of their cars. There will always be people who insist on driving, but no tweak or new highway will make that choice much better.
We have reached rock bottom (hopefully) and the usual traffic drugs don't work. Let's face reality and go big on transit priority across the city. Take drastic and bold steps, get transit moving fast and reliably, and see what happens. What have we got to lose?
CREDIT: Shawn Micallef OPINION

Subject: Automobiles; Roads & highways; Traffic congestion; Traffic control

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

Document 337 of 500

Singh is an inspiration, but he must go

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-is-inspiration-he-must-go/docview/3104951122/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+is+an+inspiration%2C+but+he+must+go&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I forget exactly how it happened, but a few months ago, on a patio in Toronto's west end, the name Rupi Kaur arose. Kaur is a Brampton poet who, though globally popular, is often derided by literary snobs. Never having been a fan of her work, I said something dismissive about her to the group of mostly white, arty and progressive friends who had gathered for a birthday.
I was immediately struck by a pang of regret. Why was I badmouthing one of my own, especially to this crowd?
I may not share Kaur's literary sensibilities, but like her, I am a Punjabi, Sikh, Indo-Canadian. Normally I would have no compunction about critiquing an artist whose work I dislike, but when it came to a fellow Punjabi, I was suddenly conflicted.
Maybe it shouldn't be this way. Kaur's success has been part of a broader phenomenon in which Sikhs in Canada have become far more prominent. Whether entertainers like Lily Singh or Jasmeet Raina, business leaders like Manjit Minhas or Navdeep Bains, or an array of politicians such as Tim Uppal or Harjit Sajjan, Sikh Canadians have been pushing their way into the spotlight for years.
Arguably the most visible success story is that of Jagmeet Singh. After first arriving in Queen's Park as an MPP and then ascending to the leader of the federal NDP, Singh not only became a minor celebrity, he also emerged as a sort of symbol of Canada's multicultural bona fides.
Singh's coronation as NDP leader warmed even my cold, cynical heart. It wasn't just the usual question of representation (though that mattered too). It was also the way he interrupted the stereotypes of Canadian Sikhs as jolly but harmless bit players. Singh had style and social media savvy. Unlike the vast majority of Canadian politicians, he occasionally seems cool.
How then to approach the idea that, as a left-leaning Indo-Canadian, Singh is an inspiration as a Sikh but a crushing disappointment as a politician? On policy, leadership and strategy, Singh has been shockingly ineffectual, ceding popular sentiment to the Conservatives while also ceding potential votes to the Liberals. Unable to provide a compelling alternative to the centrism of Trudeau's party, Singh has instead left a vacuum on Canada's left - and, for me anyway, has left despondency in its place.
The sentiment that Singh simply isn't up to the task was cemented recently when he tore up the Supply and Confidence Agreement he had with the Liberals. While he claimed it was because the Liberals were beholden to corporate interests, in truth it appears it was because the NDP gained almost nothing from the deal, continuing to hover nearly 25 points behind the Conservatives. It instead looked like Singh had caved to Pierre Poilievre's demands that he break the agreement, and in the process, he and the party looked weak and clumsy. Things only got worse a short while later when Singh signalled his plan to oppose the Liberal's carbon tax, enraging leftists and Liberals alike.
Just as criticizing Rupi Kaur left me uneasy, I now wonder: Should I be writing this piece at all? When I first mentioned the idea for this essay to my partner, who is also Sikh and Punjabi, she had concerns. After waiting for so long for one of us to make it, she suggested, what sense was there in criticizing a Sikh who ascended to such heights?
This is the bind I find myself in as a minority. When a desire for more representation comes into conflict with my other politics, it seems impossible to resolve. You might say, you should only care about what a politician does or achieves, regardless of who they are. But who Jagmeet Singh is, and the effect his position has on combating prejudice, is precisely part of the equation here. Ignoring identity is a luxury reserved for the majority.
But criticizing a Sikh Canadian at this particular juncture is even less straightforward than it might be otherwise.
For one, we are in the midst of a right wing backlash against diversity, perhaps most clearly evinced by American conservatives criticizing Kamala Harris as a "DEI hire." Worse still: beneath the surface of ordinary Canadian politesse, a surge of anti-Indian, anti-Sikh hate is emerging, particularly on social media sites including Reddit and X. The federal Liberal's disastrous approach to the temporary foreign worker program, coupled with poor planning around the influx of international students from India, has produced textbook xenophobic backlash, with new Indo-Canadians being wrongly blamed for everything from the housing crisis to soiling beaches.
In that context, speaking out against our most prominent public representative becomes fraught. At root, it's a question familiar to leftists of colour everywhere: is one's allegiance to one's own, however defined, or is it to some sense of class solidarity that crosses lines of identity?
Calling Singh to account seems vital. Canada faces serious, perhaps unprecedented challenges, from the stunning mismanagement of housing to the toxic drug crisis to the half-hearted way the country is trying to wean itself off fossil fuels and transition to green energy.
But in so many key ways, the NDP under Singh has simply been ineffective. On housing for example, the NDP has been an utter failure. Singh's solutions have largely been market-based: helping people pay mortgages, or cutting taxes on development. We now find ourselves in the ridiculous situation in which Canada's only left-leaning party is proposing that we fix housing woes by aiding the privileged few who can actually afford mortgages. True, he has proposed building half a million affordable homes - the right step to addressing a key part of the crisis - but he simultaneously allowed the conversation around housing to be co-opted by the Conservatives who, in tapping into popular anxiety about an obvious problem of inequality, occupied exactly the position the NDP should.
Meanwhile, if one were looking for big picture-thinking like Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez's Green New Deal, or radical proposals like, say, a negative income tax or universal basic income, the NDP is almost entirely absent. Even the NDP's more obviously progressive policies - like a proposed wealth tax - function better as talking points rather than a pragmatic plan to boost government revenue; around the world, wealth taxes have been notoriously ineffective at actually doing anything.
True, the NDP has had some wins. It is unlikely, for example, that the Liberals would have moved on the national dental program as quickly as they did without pressure from the NDP. But to achieve so little while also gaining so little electorally has led to a sense that it's time for Singh to go. In the Walrus earlier this summer, writer David Moscrop made the case for Singh's departure as leader, arguing that the NDP exists in a political no man's land in which the party "simply floats along, heading nowhere in particular, and certainly not up." That result can be laid almost entirely at the feet of Singh, and it's why the NDP specifically and the Canadian left more broadly desperately need a new figurehead.
Singh's rise was a genuine inspiration. But Singh's continued tenure as NDP leader is now an impediment to the goal of a fairer, more just version of Canada. The longer he stays on, the longer the NDP will continue to stagnate. And perhaps there is someone better waiting in the wings who, like Singh, might reflect Canada's broadening makeup - but who, unlike him, can do more than simply hover in the shadows of the Liberals, and instead push for a comprehensive, aggressive, modern leftist politics.
Yet, for all that, I would lament the loss of Jagmeet Singh as a public figure. Recent years have reminded me that despite its many benefits, Canada can still be unforgiving, and outright hostile to its minorities. I may not wear a turban myself, or even be a believer. But to have seen a smart, empathetic, compelling Sikh up on the public stage has mattered enormously to me. The difference it has made to young Sikh Canadians is surely even greater. And as anti-Sikh bigotry threatens to spill out into the Canadian mainstream, Singh' status and success is a symbol of what can happen when Canada lives up to its purported ideals.
For that, Jagmeet Singh should feel an enormous amount of pride. It would be a shame if he didn't see the writing on the wall, and refused to step aside - so that young people all across the country could be inspired not just by who a leader is, but how they made the country a better, fairer, more progressive place.
Navneet Alang is an editor with the Star's opinion section.
CAPTION: Jagmeet Singh's rise to leader of the federal NDP was a genuine inspiration to Sikhs, writes Navneet Alang. But Singh's continued tenure is now an impediment to the goal of a fairer, more just version of Canada.Ryan Remiorz The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Navneet Alang Toronto Star Navneet Alang is an editor with the Star's opinion section.;

Document 338 of 500

Stakes high as parties jostle in byelections: Tories threaten NDP's Winnipeg stronghold, Trudeau not included on Montreal candidate's signs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stakes-high-as-parties-jostle-byelections/docview/3104950753/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stakes+high+as+parties+jostle+in+byelections%3A+Tories+threaten+NDP%27s+Winnipeg+stronghold%2C+Trudeau+not+included+on+Montreal+candidate%27s+signs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A pair of byelections on Monday are poised to serve as a referendum on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - and his chief opposition leaders.
One will see the Liberals fighting to spare a Montreal fortress from the fate that befell Toronto-St. Paul's: a surprise byelection loss in a longtime stronghold.
In LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, Trudeau's embattled party is fending off strong campaigns from the New Democrats and the Bloc Québécois in a race that will test whether voters will turn to the NDP's Jagmeet Singh following his achievements under his now-lapsed alliance with the minority Liberals.
But in Winnipeg, Singh is contending with a compelling challenge of his own amid a Conservative offensive in Elmwood-Transcona, a riding the New Democrats have largely held since it appeared on the electoral map 36 years ago.
That battle has morphed into a fight for the working class: an issue central to both Singh and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's pitches to Canadians is that each is the clearest alternative to the Liberals.
Trudeau called the byelections back in July, after former justice minister David Lametti, who handily held the Montreal riding since its creation in 2015, left federal politics after he was dropped from cabinet last year.
Former NDP MP Daniel Blaikie triggered the race in Elmwood-Transcona after he left the federal NDP this year to advise Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew.
Advanced voting for both races ended Sept. 9, with remaining voters heading to the polls on Monday.
The more consequential of the two contests for the government is LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, with some in Liberal circles signalling that another loss in a Liberal bastion would renew questions about Trudeau's continued leadership.
Lametti has always raked in sizable leads in the riding, something Montreal city councillor Laura Palestini will be banking on in her effort to retain the seat. But the Bloc and NDP have previously held sections of the riding before its current formation, parts of which were also once home to Liberal prime minister Paul Martin.
The dynamics are different now.
Unlike Singh, Poilievre and the Bloc's Yves-François Blanchet, Trudeau is not pictured on any of Palestini's signage. Dozens of Liberal staffers said last month they would not assist with the Montreal campaign over the party's handling of the war in Gaza - something the NDP's Craig Sauvé, a well-known city councillor, has worked hard to seize on.
The Liberals have also faced accusations they parachuted Palestini into the race after other hopefuls had put their names forward, a charge the party denies.
The Bloc's Louis-Philippe Sauvé hopes to come up the middle when voters are faced with a choice between the ex-political staffer, the slumping Liberals and a New Democratic Party that propped Trudeau up for more than two years.
Both the Liberals and the NDP, on the other hand, charge that the Bloc, with its separatist agenda, doesn't have widespread appeal in urban Montreal.
"We can't take any election for granted," Liberal campaign co-chair Soraya Martinez Ferrada told the Star.
"But we feel pretty confident. We have the right team and the right volunteers, a great candidate, lots of outreach from volunteers, from other MPs ... (Palestini) has been on the ground since day one, every single day, so that makes a huge difference in terms of outreach."
The people of LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, however, are split on where to park their votes.
Ho Thi Anh, a resident of the Liberal-leaning Montreal borough of LaSalle, told the Star she has already voted for Palestini, because she's happy with Trudeau's record, particularly on implementing dental care for seniors.
But Sierra Codeluppi and Colin Arrowsmith, a young couple who recently moved to Montreal from Toronto, said they're thrilled to live in a riding where they no longer have to vote strategically for the Liberals to keep the Conservatives out. That means the NDP's Sauvé has earned their votes.
"The Liberal party has become more centre-left, and it feels like a vote for the Liberal party is just a vote for the same old," Codeluppi said at her doorstep in Verdun.
"We haven't really seen any really progressive changes, and all of the changes, I feel like that were more progressive that were made, were kind of pushed by the NDP."
Speaking to reporters in Quebec on Friday, Trudeau accused the NDP of "caving" to Conservative pressure to undermine the federal carbon levy, an issue he suggested Montreal voters should consider.
Poilievre, on a local Montreal radio program the same day, said the city is "falling apart," with "crime and chaos" rampant in the streets thanks to Trudeau's policies. The Conservatives are running business owner Louis Ialenti in the riding, which has never generated strong support for the Tories.
Poilievre brought that same tough-on-crime message to Winnipeg in a rally with Colin Reynolds, the Conservative candidate in Elmwood-Transcona, in late July.
Reynolds, a construction electrician and self-described "proud" union member, is holding the banner for Poilievre in the largely working-class riding.
His main rival is NDP candidate Leila Dance, the head of a local business development organization, who is vying to keep the New Democrats' hold on Elmwood-Transcona.
The riding has always gone to the NDP, except once in 2011 when the Conservatives scored an upset. It was long held by veteran New Democrat Bill Blaike, whose son, Daniel, won back the riding in 2015.
The end of the Blaikie dynasty means the NDP can't be complacent in Monday's race, where a Poilievre win would indicate that the Conservative leader has the ability to take down both red and orange bastions.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh with LaSalle-Émard-Verdun candidate Craig Sauvé. The riding is seen as a test of voter support for Singh after ending his deal with the Liberals.Prime Minister Justin Trudeau appears with LaSalle-Émard- Verdun candidate Laura Palestini last month.Ryan Remiorz THE CANADIAN PRESSPeter McCabeTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

Letters: Ontario broke a record this past year, but it's not something we should be proud of, Keenan, Sept. 11 Kamala Harris won the debate. So why I am so scared? Phillips, Pierre Poilievre's nickname for Jagmeet Singh is 'racist,' Liberal minister charges, Being tired of Justin Trudeau is not a good reason to vote him out, Sept. 8 Conservatives say they have solution for online harassment after Liberals 'failed,' Sept. 12

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3104950469/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ontario+broke+a+record+this+past+year%2C+but+it%27s+not+something+we+should+be+proud+of%2C+Keenan%2C+Sept.+11+Kamala+Harris+won+the+debate.+So+why+I+am+so+scared%3F+Phillips%2C+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+nickname+for+Jagmeet+Singh+is+%27racist%2C%27+Liberal+minister+charges%2C+Being+tired+of+Justin+Trudeau+is+not+a+good+reason+to+vote+him+out%2C+Sept.+8+Conservatives+say+they+have+solution+for+online+harassment+after+Liberals+%27failed%2C%27+Sept.+12&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario broke record for the wrong reasons
Ontario broke a record this past year, but it's not something we should be proud of, Keenan, Sept. 11
Edward Keenan's excellent column contains insightful commentary on the complexity of human nature and "political will."
He wrote that the positive aspect of this tragedy is that food banks, and the people who donate and volunteer, or work there, are, "a testament to our collective capacity to help when we see suffering." However, they are not the sustainable solution to hunger for one million people in Ontario.
Carolyn Stewart, the CEO of Feed Ontario, affirms that, "We're there for an emergency provision of food when people are most in need, but we're not a solution to food insecurity." Food insecurity is no longer an emergency. It has become a regular part of life.
There are solutions to food insecurity in Ontario. During the pandemic the federal government quickly initiated a universally accessible financial support program (CERB) to assist people. Policymakers also know there are many reports advocating guaranteed livable incomes that would help to provide dignity, hope and health for everyone.
A truly livable income would eliminate the necessity for food banks, and the excessive use of volunteer time and energy that Keenan also documented. If such a universal program is a bridge too far in the struggle against food insecurity, a livable minimum wage is another sustainable option, coupled with realistic income support for people who cannot be employed for a variety of reasons. These are just two possibilities.
Keenan concluded with a challenge to all of us. He wrote: "We are good at putting in the effort to manage suffering when we see it. We need to get a whole lot better at our efforts to solve the problems that cause it." In other words, a significant part of the problem is lack of political will.
Allan Baker, Scarborough
U.S. presidency isn't decided by who wins debate
Kamala Harris won the debate. So why I am so scared?
Phillips, Sept. 13
According to the most recent polls, 60 per cent of Americans did not watch the June debate between U.S. President Joe Biden and Donald Trump. It's not that they don't care, they don't want to see the idiocies that go on during a debate. It's a waste of one's valuable time. The Democrats are celebrating a victory and so are the Republicans. History has shown that winning a debate has very little impact on who will win the presidency.
Rita Maio, Woodbridge
It boggles my mind watching and reading about the U.S. election. The Republicans continually focus on the inflation rate that the country has encountered in the past four years as being the fault of the Democrats. The Democrats do little to defend the situation. Are the American people so self-centred that they fail to see the whole world suffered with this situation?
Bill Kelly, Keswick
Where has Crombie been?
Every day we hear Premier Doug Ford say and do many really dumb things, including liberalizing the sale of alcohol in convenience stores and gas stations, closing the Science Centre, trying to mess with the Greenbelt and building the unnecessary Highway 413.
My question is simple. Where is Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie? We never hear or see her. If she wants my vote, I need to learn what the Liberals think and would do. She seems to have gone into hiding.
Jack Hughes, Welland, Ont.
It is never OK
to disparage someone
Pierre Poilievre's nickname for Jagmeet Singh is 'racist,' Liberal minister charges, Sept. 12
Why does Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre thinks it's OK to disparage someone's name? Of course he knows that by bullying Jagmeet Singh, the NDP leader, he is pandering to some in his base who are not ready for a prime minister wearing a turban.
Singh knows what he's up against in regards to that type discrimination. I consider him an honest and brave politician.
Would Poilievre ever ridicule a member of the Bloc Québécois in that way? Or would the political costs be too high?
Dorothy Low, Richmond Hill
I don't believe the nickname for Jagmeet Singh is racist - calling someone a sellout has nothing to do with their race - but it does confirm that Trump-wannabe Pierre Poilievre is happy to take another page out of the orange fraudster's playbook, resorting to childish nicknames to ridicule his opponents.
Andrew Elrick, Whitby
Build a gondola to the islands
To get visitors to the Toronto Islands, the most efficient and cost-effective method would be a gondola over the shipping channel. It would be much less expensive from both a capital and operating cost standpoint, be much more attractive as it becomes a tourist destination by itself and much more efficient in taking visitors both to and from the Islands. It is fascinating that this option has been ignored for decades.
Eric Dempster, Markham
Federal Liberals have expired
Being tired of Justin Trudeau is not a good reason to vote him out, Sept. 8
You asked your readers if we would choose a prime minister like we choose bagels. In the past 100 years, Canada had 62 years of Liberal leadership. How much more time do Liberals need to deliver results? Canadians have witnessed a painfully slow decline.
The analogy between a 10-minute experience and a major decision like voting is wrong. Voting can affect life expectancy. That's how critical voting is, compared to eating a bagel.
This is what we mean by being tired - we are lacking opportunities, we have less purchasing power, people are unable to seek treatment and die waiting, people are cutting costs on medications, our kids will not be able to buy a home and our diet is changing because we can't afford decent meals.
Do you want us to keep eating this expired bagel?
Melisa Barrilli, Toronto
Tories proposing solution to online bullying is a joke
Conservatives say they have solution for online harassment after Liberals 'failed,' Sept. 12
The federal Conservatives have a solution to prevent online harassment? To use a little schoolyard terminology, I say those who smelt it probably dealt it.
Richard Kadziewicz,
Scarborough

Document 340 of 500

Accept it, Toronto: traffic will never go back to the way it was. Here's why that's a good thing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/accept-toronto-traffic-will-never-go-back-way-was/docview/3104813611/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Accept+it%2C+Toronto%3A+traffic+will+never+go+back+to+the+way+it+was.+Here%27s+why+that%27s+a+good+thing&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: My doctor has an annoying habit. When I visit with an ailment or pain, looking for a cure, he will sometimes tap a little medicine bottle in his office with the word "acceptance" handwritten on it. Whatever my problem is - aching knees from jogging, disappearing hair - he will say that it's just part of life, aging and I have to accept it, all while tapping that bottle.
It's annoying because I would rather have drugs. Powerful drugs. Drugs to make the problem go away and to instantly feel good and young again. Acceptance, however, is really just facing reality and is a treatment Toronto could use right now to help with its traffic woes.
Everyone is talking about Toronto traffic. It's what we make incessant small talk about with acquaintances and strangers. It's even what visiting celebrities and tourists talk about. It's what politicians and other civil society groups are trying to solve.
The latter efforts have been futile so far because we've never fully faced reality. That is, accepting Toronto's traffic problems are not ever going to be cured. Not in the way we've been going about it, thinking getting around in a car will one day be easy again, a "Make Driving Great Again" fever dream as seen in car commercials.
I've long thought of Toronto as a teenage city, still becoming an adult but experiencing all the growing pains and awkwardness teenagers do. Adulthood is very soon but memories of childhood are still quite present. There was a time not long ago, in living memory for many Torontonians, when it was easy to drive to most places in the city and park nearby.
When viewing photos of the city from the 1980s and even the 1990s I'm often struck by how comparatively empty the streets seem, at least in the neighbourhoods. Understanding this is a window into today's approach to traffic: the good old days weren't so long ago and hard to let go of. Were they that good though? In other parts of the city traffic has been a problem for decades.
When I was a kid in Windsor during the 1980s, I would plot out, in the days before Highway 407, elaborate 401 detours around Toronto on paper maps during our annual drive to Nova Scotia to visit family. Too many times we were caught in midday traffic jams through Toronto, the likes of which we never saw at home. Steaming hot pavement, semi trucks, noise, keeping the air conditioning off so the old car wouldn't overheat: it was a dystopia to avoid.
Since then "drugs" in the form of more lanes and more highways, including the 407, haven't made the 401 or other highways any better. Proposed Highway 413, offered like another drug to cure traffic problems, will just generate more traffic, creating more car-dependent sprawl and encouraging more people to drive, feeding more cars onto existing roads.
The drugs don't work.
This is the proven phenomenon of induced demand: new roads create more traffic. Even back in the 1930s, as described in the epic book "The Power Broker," when Robert Moses built his beloved parkways in and around New York City, they became jammed within months. That was during the Depression even, when fewer people could purchase cars.
There are interventions that can help traffic of course, like my colleague Matt Elliott's recent column on how employing more traffic cameras could benefit flow if only Doug Ford let us. And when new transit lines like the Eglinton Crosstown, Finch West and the Ontario Line subway eventually begin to operate it will be a game changer, but a big dose of acceptance now would free Toronto to do other things that need to be done.
Ed Keenan has written in the Star on how the excellent idea that was the King Street Transit Priority Corridor has been allowed to degrade in design and enforcement. An adult Toronto would actually fix King Street permanently, but then take steps to make other transit lines a priority too.
Toronto is a city of three million people where a streetcar packed with up to 130 people can get stuck for multiple light cycles behind one person in a car turning left. Packed buses on routes like Dufferin Street have little or no priority and crawl along. Transit needs to be the best option to get around in order to coax people out of their cars. There will always be people who insist on driving, but no tweak or new highway will make that choice much better.
We have reached rock bottom (hopefully) and the usual traffic drugs don't work. Let's face reality and go big on transit priority across the city. Take drastic and bold steps, get transit moving fast and reliably, and see what happens. What have we got to lose?
CAPTION: There will always be people who insist on driving, but no tweak or new highway will make that choice much better, writes Shawn Micallef.

Subject: Drugs; Automobiles; Roads & highways

Title: Accept it, Toronto: traffic will never go back to the way it was. Here's why that's a good thing

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Document 341 of 500

Doug Ford's government helped speed development on this parcel of GTA land. So why have no houses been built yet?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-helped-speed-development-on/docview/3104813590/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+helped+speed+development+on+this+parcel+of+GTA+land.+So+why+have+no+houses+been+built+yet%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: From the outside, the parcel of land in north Vaughan looks like any other large green space destined for development.
But Block 41 - 428 hectares bounded by Kirby and Teston Roads to the north and south, and Weston Road and Pine Valley Drive to the east and west - has captured the attention of local and provincial governments like few other properties in the GTA.
It could be because it's one of the Vaughan's last greenfield developments - an entirely new subdivision built where there are few houses or the necessary servicing - that also contains a host of environmentally sensitive areas, including the Greenbelt, provincially significant wetlands and endangered species habitat. It's also home to a critical compressor station and pipeline infrastructure that pumps natural gas out to eastern Ontario and Quebec.
Over the past five years, this plot of land has been the subject of significant intervention from local and provincial governments intended to both speed the development process, and open up more land for housing. Among them: a minister's zoning order, a rare downgrading of Greenbelt designations on the land, and even Greenbelt land removed - and then returned - following the scandal in 2023.
Yet despite such substantive moves, no new housing has been built on the site. The subdivision plan for Block 41 is expected to be discussed at Vaughan council next week.
Critics say that Block 41 illustrates how Doug Ford government's ad hoc housing policy - which they say has disrupted the planning system and dismantled environmental protections - has done little to actually fulfil the promise of "building more homes faster." Those who have followed the progress of Block 41 say it also shows how complex greenfield development is in the GTA, even with government intervention.
"The rationale for opening up these sensitive lands for development has been that we have a housing shortage, and we need to build housing at all costs. But ... building single-family homes in remote locations will not help housing affordability at all," said Dawn Cassandra Parker, a professor at the School of Planning at the University of Waterloo. "The Ford government planning and policy changes have caused widespread environmental harm without achieving their stated goals of increasing housing supply and affordability."
Housing fast-track slows
There are several reasons why the province's efforts to fast-track housing hasn't succeeded, experts say, most of them being economic.
High interest rates have diminished the demand for single-family homes, said Frank Clayton, a senior research fellow at the Centre for Urban Research and Land Development at Toronto Metropolitan University. Market conditions aren't favourable enough for private developers to build - regardless of how co-operative the political regulatory conditions may be.
"Ninety per cent of our housing is provided by the private sector, and developers build housing to make money," said Clayton. "If they can't see themselves making money, they won't do it."
However, he believes expediting the planning process will allow "shovel-ready" development to be available "when that demand does return much quicker, which is in the public interest."
But Lauren Capilongo, a senior planner with Malone Given Parsons, the lead consultant working for the Block 41 developers, said the Vaughan parcel is indicative of the "complex" planning system in Ontario.
The plot of land is owned by more than a dozen landowners, including some of the province's most influential developers, such as Silvio DeGasperis, founder, president and CEO of the TACC Group of Companies, and Jack Eisenberger, president of Fieldgate Developments, who came together to form the Block 41 landowners groups. A handful of landowners are not participating in the development application.
Capilango said getting approvals for the 5,000-home subdivision has "taken much longer" than many of her regular projects. She added that she doesn't believe the development has received any special attention as "evident by the 15-plus year planning process that is ongoing."
The parcel was officially incorporated into Vaughan's urban boundary in 2010 with the idea that the project would be finished by 2031, she said. Now she's unsure if that will happen.
"The lengthy planning and approval process has left little time to build out the Block 41 community before the 2031 planning horizon," said Capilongo.
A parcel with problems
From the outset the parcel had several complicating factors. Among them were 137 hectares of Greenbelt lands, tributaries and valley lands associated with East Purpleville Creek, provincial wetlands, fish and significant wildlife habitat, a 40-hectare TransCanada Pipeline and compressor station to pump natural gas across the province, and no water and wastewater infrastructure in the ground.
Capilongo said that the developers who own Block 41 have spent millions on studies of the site, studies normally required by the province, region and city to approve development plans.
The scope and scale of the approvals required "has contributed to the length of time it has taken to get shovels in the ground to build homes," she added.
A minister intervenes
In the summer of 2020, the Block 41 landowners asked the city of Vaughan to request a minister's zoning order (MZO) from the province. The tool allows the Minister of Municipal Affairs and Housing to fast-track zoning changes on the land.
The province approved the MZO in November, allowing for lowrise and midrise development on a large portion of the site, including housing and retail uses.
At the time, Vaughan Mayor Maurizio Bevilacqua said the MZO was needed because of concerns the pandemic would delay an ongoing Ontario Land Tribunal appeal by TransCanada Pipeline Limited (TCPL). The company had long-standing concerns about the impact of noise, buffering and vibrations from its industrial site on future housing.
At the time, TC Energy, TCPL's parent company, wrote a letter to the city opposing the MZO, for "circumventing the public process and comprehensive studies that are contemplated and required by the Planning Act."
In the end, the MZO didn't cancel the OLT appeal, according to Capilongo, as it still took 18 months for all parties to reach a settlement.
She said some of the disputed issues included the location of roads and utilities, the buffers between the facility and future housing, and assurance that the development would not interfere with current or future operations. She said the work continues to address these concerns.
TC Energy referred all questions to the city of Vaughan or the landowners.
Changing land protection
Meanwhile, in 2021, the Block 41 landowners also asked York Region to downgrade the designation of the Greenbelt lands on the site from the protected agricultural classification to a more general rural one.
This would permit the developers to use the bulk of developable land of 175 hectares to be used for homes and schools, while parks, fields and other infrastructure uses could be put onto the Greenbelt area, of which there are 137 hectares.
Environmental groups and local municipal planning staff objected to the use of the Greenbelt area for active uses and storm water infrastructure, fearing that it would lead to degradation of the natural heritage space.
Despite this, York Region approved the request for the designation change in its official plans. The province approved the region's official plan in 2022, finalizing these Greenbelt changes.
However, it will be up to Vaughan put this policy into practice. A report to council next week on Block 41 will offer details on what this change will look like.
"This was a very unique area, and to see what has happened to it, gives me a lot of heartbreak," said Deb Schulte, a former Vaughan regional councillor and MP, who was part of the original Greenbelt task force.
She said this area was added into the Greenbelt because it is a key habitat for the Redside Dace, an endangered fish native to Ontario.
"If you have development incursions into this area, it will destroy this habitat. That's why this land was put into the Greenbelt in the first place."
Capilango said the Block 41 landowners "are committed to respecting, protecting and enhancing the features that make up the Natural Heritage Network."
She said the landowners have conducted extensive studies including "multi-year inventories of flora and fauna" given the heightened environmental sensitivity of the area.
The downgrading of the classification wasn't the only time the Greenbelt played a role in this parcel's progress.
According to the auditor general and integrity commissioner reports from 2023, six hectares of Block 41 were removed from the Greenbelt after a request was made by the member of the DeGasperis family to Ryan Amato, then chief of staff for former housing minister Steve Clark.
The lands were returned to the Greenbelt after the reports by provincial watchdogs found the Ford government "favoured" certain developers with close ties to the government. The RCMP is still investigating.
Still waiting on housing
Despite the government interventions, Capilango said Block 41 is still at least two years away from construction, with housing sales and construction expected to start in 2026.
The province said it will continue to monitor Block 41, adding that it sees "progress toward completion through applications for plans of subdivision and site plan approval," said Bianca Meta, press secretary for Minister of Housing Paul Calandra.
Phil Pothen, counsel for the environmental advocacy group Environmental Defence, said Vaughan site offers a lesson in how long it can take for greenfield development to be built.
He also said it raises questions about why the province is pushing for more land to be opened for housing, when development approved decades ago are still years away from construction.
"Despite all of this extra effort put in by the provincial government, including the minister's zoning order and compromising the Greenbelt ... all of these measures still haven't been effective on getting housing built on the site," said Pothen.
"You have to ask if building homes this way is really the answer to the housing crisis. Because the evidence shows, it clearly isn't."
CAPTION: Block41 is also home to a critical compressor station for a natural gas pipeline.A portion of Block41 was removed from Greenbelt protection - and then returned after reports by provincial watchdogs on the Greenbelt land swap.Block 41 is still at least two years away from construction, according to developers.Block 41 in Vaughan has captured the attention of local and provincial governments like few other properties in the GTA.

Subject: Landowners; Infrastructure; Zoning ordinances; Single family; Endangered & extinct species; Planning; Housing developments

Document 342 of 500

These byelections could be a referendum on Justin Trudeau and his rivals. Here's what you need to know

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/these-byelections-could-be-referendum-on-justin/docview/3104813572/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=These+byelections+could+be+a+referendum+on+Justin+Trudeau+and+his+rivals.+Here%27s+what+you+need+to+know&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A pair of byelections on Monday are poised to serve as a referendum on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - and his chief opposition leaders.
One will see the Liberals fighting to spare a Montreal fortress from the fate that befell Toronto-St. Paul's: a surprise byelection loss in a longtime stronghold.
In LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, Trudeau's embattled party is fending off strong campaigns from the New Democrats and the Bloc Québécois in a race that will test whether voters will turn to the NDP's Jagmeet Singh following his achievements under his now-lapsed alliance with the minority Liberals.
But in Winnipeg, Singh is contending with a compelling challenge of his own amid a Conservative offensive in Elmwood-Transcona, a riding the New Democrats have largely held since it appeared on the electoral map 36 years ago.
That battle has morphed into a fight for the working class: an issue central to both Singh and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's pitches to Canadians is that each is the clearest alternative to the Liberals.
Trudeau called the byelections back in July, after former justice minister David Lametti, who handily held the Montreal riding since its creation in 2015, left federal politics after he was dropped from cabinet last year. Former NDP MP Daniel Blaikie triggered the race in Elmwood-Transcona after he left the federal NDP earlier this year to advise Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew.
Advanced voting for both races ended Sept. 9, with remaining voters heading to the polls on Monday.
The more consequential of the two contests for the government is LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, with some in Liberal circles signalling that another loss in a Liberal bastion would renew questions about Trudeau's continued leadership.
Lametti has always raked in sizable leads in the riding, something Montreal city councillor Laura Palestini will be banking on in her effort to retain the seat. But the Bloc and NDP have previously held sections of the riding before its current formation, parts of which were also once home to Liberal prime minister Paul Martin.
The dynamics are different now.
Unlike Singh, Poilievre and the Bloc's Yves-François Blanchet, Trudeau is not pictured on any of Palestini's signage. Dozens of Liberal staffers said last month they would not assist with the Montreal campaign over the party's handling of the war in Gaza - something the NDP's Craig Sauvé, a well-known city councillor, has worked hard to seize on. The Liberals have also faced accusations they parachuted Palestini into the race after other hopefuls had put their names forward, a charge the party denies.
What's more, a protest movement bringing attention to electoral reform - a subject Trudeau pledged to address in 2015 and later abandoned - has produced a ballot with more than 90 names, which could delay results.
The Bloc's Louis-Philippe Sauvé is hoping to come up the middle when voters are faced with a choice between the former political staffer, the slumping Liberals and a New Democratic Party that propped Trudeau up for more than two years.
Both the Liberals and the NDP, on the other hand, charge that the Bloc, with its separatist agenda, doesn't have widespread appeal in urban Montreal.
"We can't take any election for granted," Liberal campaign co-chair Soraya Martinez Ferrada told the Star Thursday.
"But we feel pretty confident. We have the right team and the right volunteers, a great candidate, lots of outreach from volunteers, from other MPs ...(Palestini) has been on the ground since day one, every single day, so that makes a huge difference in terms of outreach."
The people of LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, however, are split on where to park their votes.
Ho Thi Anh, a resident of the Liberal-leaning Montreal borough of LaSalle, told the Star she has already voted for Palestini, because she's happy with Trudeau's record, particularly on implementing dental care for seniors.
But Sierra Codeluppi and Colin Arrowsmith, a young couple who recently moved to Montreal from Toronto, said they're thrilled to live in a riding where they no longer have to vote strategically for the Liberals to keep the Conservatives out. That means the NDP's Sauvé has earned their votes.
"The Liberal party has become more centre-left, and it feels like a vote for the Liberal party is just a vote for the same old," Codeluppi told the Star at her doorstep in Verdun.
"We haven't really seen any really progressive changes, and all of the changes, I feel like that were more progressive that were made, were kind of pushed by the NDP."
Speaking to reporters in Quebec on Friday, Trudeau accused the NDP of "caving" to Conservative pressure to undermine the federal carbon levy, an issue he suggested Montreal voters should consider.
Poilievre, on a local Montreal radio program the same day, said the city is "falling apart," with "crime and chaos" rampant in the streets thanks to Trudeau's policies. The Conservatives are running business owner Louis Ialenti in the riding, which has never generated strong support for the Tories.
What's the race like in Winnipeg?
Poilievre brought that same tough-on-crime message to Winnipeg in a rally with Colin Reynolds, the Conservative candidate in Elmwood-Transcona, in late July.
Reynolds, a construction electrician and self-described "proud" union member, is holding the banner for Poilievre in the largely working-class riding.
His main rival is NDP candidate Leila Dance, the head of a local business development organization, who is vying to keep the New Democrats' hold on Elmwood-Transcona.
The riding has always gone to the NDP, except once in 2011 when the Conservatives scored an upset. It was long held by veteran New Democrat Bill Blaike, whose son, Daniel, won back the riding in 2015.
The end of the Blaikie dynasty means the NDP can't be complacent in Monday's race, where a Poilievre win would indicate that the Conservative leader has the ability to take down both red and orange bastions.
"It is often a close race, and often between us and the Conservatives," a senior NDP source told the Star on the condition they not be named.
"We expected a fight from them and we have a fight from them. I think we've done everything we need to do to keep that seat."
Singh, who the Conservatives allege has "sold out" union workers because of his deal with Trudeau, visited Winnipeg six times over the summer, while Poilievre appeared to only visit once.
Fred DeLorey, a partner at NorthStar Public Affairs and former national campaign manager for the Conservatives, said the NDP's ties to the Liberals through their former deal won't play well in a riding where the Tories are competitive.
"It'll be great if the Conservatives go in and have a big upset and win, but at the same time, just coming close, just increasing their vote is success for the Conservatives," he said.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and Lasalle-Emard-Verdun candidate Craig Sauve speak to the media while campaigning ahead of a federal byelection in Montreal on Sept. 6, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Referendums; Voters; Prime ministers; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Trudeau, Justin

Title: These byelections could be a referendum on Justin Trudeau and his rivals. Here's what you need to know

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 15, 2024
