Document 353 of 500

Zameer case raises tough questions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/zameer-case-raises-tough-questions/docview/3104427900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Zameer+case+raises+tough+questions&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford crossed every line when he condemned the courts for granting bail to an accused cop-killer in a controversial 2021 case.
Letting Umar Zameer out of jail "was beyond comprehension" and "completely unacceptable," according to Ford.
That said, the premier wasn't the only one to say it: John Tory, then Toronto's mayor, uttered similar improprieties and hyperboles.
Let's be honest. In truth, these two top leaders were merely voicing publicly what many Torontonians were likely thinking privately at the time.
Yet we look to our leaders to lead - to do (and say) the right thing in tough times, not just tell people what they want to hear. Today, it's time for a reckoning about them, but also about us - our society's strengths and weaknesses.
In reality, the righteous indignation of the premier and mayor of the day was dead wrong - not merely a misreading of their leadership roles, but a misunderstanding of the case.
In fact, there was good reason to release the suspect on bail, because the case against him was so weak and the flight risk so low (with house arrest and an ankle bracelet). As my colleague Betsy Powell revealed in the Star a week ago, the case was full of holes and ultimately collapsed in court, culminating in a jury acquittal.
What's so compelling about Powell's in-depth analysis is not just the contradictions of the case but the complexities. When the full story came out in court, public opinion shifted dramatically - instead of questioning the suspect's bail conditions, people were doubting police testimony and the entire prosecution.
I won't relitigate the case here, but what's so interesting about Powell's explanatory piece is how it addresses and answers questions of policing and prosecuting. Flowing from that are some societal questions that still remain unanswered.
First, prosecutorial discretion isn't as simple as it sounds.
It's worth noting that even Zameer's lawyer agreed, after a preliminary hearing testing the testimony and evidence, that there was enough to proceed to trial. The defence acknowledged that questions of credibility are up to jurors as triers of fact.
Still, as more details emerged at the full trial, first-degree murder charges looked less and less plausible. Zameer had been getting into his car after a Canada Day celebration with his pregnant wife and child when they were suddenly accosted by a couple of tough-looking plain clothes police officers.
They feared being robbed in an underground garage and escaped in a panic - running over one of the cops. The jury believed them, and seemingly disbelieved the police testimony that Zameer had wilfully killed their colleague.
It was a vindication of sorts for the accused, a verdict that resonated with the public. But it took a trial to clear the air.
Zameer paid a heavy price for that vindication - incarceration for three months before he got bail, years of house arrest, and onerous lawyers' bills. Initially, he faced public vilification and suspicion, given his roots as an immigrant from Malaysia, originally from Pakistan.
There can be no doubt that the trial imposed an unfair burden on the accused, but justice isn't about social justice. It's about adjudicating disputes and ascertaining truth.
And yet Zameer had a hideous introduction to Canada so soon after arriving as an immigrant. Was he discriminated against as a brown Muslim with a foreign name and no citizenship?
Initially, almost certainly. Here's another question, at a time when white privilege is so often offered as the sole explanation for everything: Did Zameer ultimately benefit from stereotypes, or more precisely, positive archetypes - not his immigration status but his social status, including education and religious faith?
It could not have escaped the notice of any jury, which is only human, that he was an accountant driving a BMW, accompanied by his pregnant wife in a hijab and his innocent child - images of assiduity, piety and even maternity.
That's not to deny the reality of white privilege, just a reminder that other class and social indicators are also influential (at least in multicultural Toronto).
Zameer had the misfortune to stumble into a perfect storm - a plain clothes operation gone awry, a police death, and a controversial prosecution. In truth, that fate could befall any of us.
The test of law and order is not that it achieves perfection, but that it strives for a just resolution. Our premier and mayor behaved badly (Tory later apologized), the police chief spoke badly, and the front-line cops may have testified wrongly.
But the courts handled the cases dispassionately and the jury acted wisely, with full credit to Zameer's defence team. Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw later asked the OPP to conduct an independent review into the officers' testimony, their procedures and training - notably in plain clothes operations.
Ultimately, Zameer was given the benefit of the doubt - not just in the law courts but the court of public opinion - and a fundraising campaign has raised hundreds of thousands of dollars to defray the cost of his defence. As for the prosecutors, they got a bum rap in the end - a trial seems to have been the correct course - but that's part of the job description in our judicial system.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Document 354 of 500

Trudeau unleashed is new Grit weapon against Poilievre

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-unleashed-is-new-grit-weapon-against/docview/3104427848/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+unleashed+is+new+Grit+weapon+against+Poilievre&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "I believe we're going to win everything!" Prime Minister Justin Trudeau responded when asked this week whether he thought the Liberals will win Monday's byelection in LaSalle-Émard-Verdun.
"We've got everything going, man!" Trudeau declared, as he dashed out of his party's final caucus meeting Wednesday in Nanaimo, a big smile on face.
"Elmwood-Transcona, too?" asked CBC reporter Raffy Boudjikanian incredulously.
"Ah! We're coming for Elmwood-Transcona!" Trudeau enthusiastically declared, later giving a wholehearted laugh when asked if he'd had a Nanaimo bar.
Was Trudeau well? Was he on a sugar rush? Was he joking? Who was this Trudeau?
The rather odd off-the-cuff remarks - there is no chance the Liberals will win in Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona, and Montreal's LaSalle-Émard-Verdun is a tough three-way race with the NDP and the Bloc Québécois - were actually purposeful.
Trudeau "unleashed" is the Prime Minister's Office's new communication plan.
This week, the PMO told Liberal MPs that Trudeau will be "unshackled." They'll see him speak more authentically, more passionately.
And they have.
On Friday, in an interview with Montreal talk radio station CJAD, Trudeau didn't mince words when describing his Conservative opponent. "Pierre Poilievre is a liar," he told host Aaron Rand. "I'm not going anywhere," he said, when asked if he planned to stick around if the Liberals lose Montreal's byelection. He said he was "happy to pick a fight with (Quebec Premier François) Legault." And he threw shade at NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh for ripping up their "progressive agreement," complaining Singh didn't have the decency to call him "personally" to let him know. "That, that's telling," he told Rand.
Then he slammed Singh for "jettisoning" the environment - a reference to the NDP appearing to walk away from its support for consumer carbon pricing. "He doesn't have the foggiest idea of how to actually protect people, grow the economy and fight climate change at the same time. He never has," Trudeau said.
Much of the Liberals' summer caucus retreat in Nanaimo was centred on a plan - or perhaps more accurately, the concepts of a plan - to turn things around.
The prime minister acknowledged to his MPs there that he knows people don't like him and are tired of him.
His office and party officials tried to allay concerns through a series of presentations before an open mic session with the prime minister. They aired clips from Sylvester Stallone in "Rocky Balboa," and Al Pacino's famous speech in the football film "Any Given Sunday": "Either we heal as a team or we're going to crumble, inch by inch, play by play, 'till we're finished."
"We can stay here and get the s--t kicked out of us or we can fight our way back into the light," Pacino says. (It's probably a safe bet Liberal officials didn't show the rest of the speech, in which Pacino's character tells the team, "Now, I can't do it for you. I'm too old.")
The boost to team morale may have temporarily worked - no MP called for the prime minister to resign during the open forum.
Inside the room, MPs were told they should get used to repeating ad nauseam government talking points about what the Liberals had accomplished - on child care, dental care, pharmacare.
They were told they needed to do a better job sharing party graphics on social media. Canadians needed to be made aware of what the government had done - only then, they argued, could the Liberals successfully prosecute the case that Poilievre's Conservatives would take those things away. "Laying down the tracks," was how one adviser described it.
The Liberals plan to use more government advertising to tell Canadians about programs and benefits they've enacted.
The party also showed MPs several prospective Poilievre attack ads it plans to test and hopes to push out this fall.
"The biggest problem that the government is facing right now is that people feel like Trudeau and the government have really not done much to help them in the midst of this inflation crisis," Dan Arnold, the party's former pollster, told the Star's "It's Political" podcast this week.
Being able to convey the things the Liberals have done would not only support the Grits' message that they know what voters are going through, said Arnold, but "drawing that sharper contrast (with Poilievre) would be certainly part of any kind of path back."
However, perhaps the most telling change, according to several MPs, was when a PMO official told the caucus that former U.S. first lady Michelle Obama was wrong when she declared, "When they go low, we go high." Instead, Liberal MPs were told they should "prepare to fight back."
"The message was, 'It's OK to be a little bit more aggressive in the attacks in question period or on social media,'" said a third MP. The Liberals appear to believe they must fight fire with fire. The Conservatives' stunts in question period and often inflammatory questions during committee meetings are not only driving social media engagement, the Grits complain, but also traditional media coverage.
"So, it's like a race to the bottom," that MP acknowledged.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's office told Liberal MPs they were about to see an "unshackled" leader speaking authentically and passionately, and that's what's happening, Althia Raj writes.ETHAN CAIRNS THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Document 355 of 500

Greenbelt issue impeding other projects: Poll finds public distrust a hurdle in getting Ontarians onside with ways to speed up infrastructure

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greenbelt-issue-impeding-other-projects/docview/3104427847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greenbelt+issue+impeding+other+projects%3A+Poll+finds+public+distrust+a+hurdle+in+getting+Ontarians+onside+with+ways+to+speed+up+infrastructure&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of the largest factors standing in the way of Ontario being able to speed up critical infrastructure projects is a sense of public distrust fostered by the government's own history with the Greenbelt, polling commissioned by the government suggests.
Leger conducted an online survey about infrastructure in mid-to-late December and held focus groups in January. The Greenbelt was frequently cited, more than a year after media reports first raised questions about government ties to developers and several months after the release of two legislative officers' scathing reports.
Both the auditor general and the integrity commissioner found the government's process to remove 15 parcels of land from the Greenbelt to build 50,000 homes favoured certain developers.
The integrity commissioner found then-Municipal Affairs and Housing minister Steve Clark, who resigned after the report was released, violated ethics rules, but said he had no evidence developers were tipped off about the government's plans for Greenbelt removals. The auditor general found the property owners stood to see their land value rise by $8.3 billion.
Premier Doug Ford ultimately reversed his decision and returned the parcels of land to the Greenbelt, but the RCMP has launched a criminal investigation.
Leger pollsters said the Greenbelt issue was a "clear example" in participants' minds of government overreach and is a "significant" hurdle to getting Ontarians onside with ways to speed up infrastructure.
"The substantial barrier to implementing any mechanism for fast tracking infrastructure projects lies in the current sense of distrust with the government," Leger wrote in the report on their findings, which the government recently tabled with the legislature.
"Overcoming this, plus some skepticism surrounding motives for expediting projects is crucial for fostering public confidence and facilitating the successful execution (of) initiatives aimed at streamlining processes."
Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said it's unfortunate the government has put itself in this position when Ontario is facing a housing crisis. "People don't trust this government to make investments, especially in infrastructure, that put people first instead of the interests of wealthy, well-connected insiders," he said. "It's clear that the Greenbelt scandal and the $8.3 billion that would have gone to a handful of Ford-connected insiders have really undermined the public's trust and confidence in this government to make investments, especially in housing, that put people first."
The vast majority of survey respondents agreed that infrastructure is important, in particular hospitals and public transportation. Highways and the redevelopment of Ontario Place, some of Ford's signature projects, were seen as less of a priority, Leger said.
Ministerial Zoning Orders, which allow the province to override municipal decisions, have been a key way to speed up some projects and also a point of contention among Ford's critics. While about half of the survey respondents supported the use of MZOs, with 36 per cent opposed, almost none of the focus group members did.
Grace Lee, a spokesperson for Ford, said in a statement the government is getting critical infrastructure built.
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Subject: Polls & surveys; Infrastructure; Criminal investigations; Projects; Public Key Infrastructure; Public opinion

Document 356 of 500

A matter of wheel power: At 69, I tried the e-bike and roamed around Toronto. For folks like me, is it the future?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/matter-wheel-power/docview/3104427739/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+matter+of+wheel+power%3A+At+69%2C+I+tried+the+e-bike+and+roamed+around+Toronto.+For+folks+like+me%2C+is+it+the+future%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I didn't get an e-bike so that I could sail past a 40-year-old man slogging up Poplar Plains Road on his non-e-bike (analog, acoustic, legacy, what to call the old bike?), smiling and waving as I did. Although as thrills go at 69, that's right up at the top.
My e-bike wasn't about teaching other Toronto e-bike riders road etiquette, either. I had the right of way on a roundabout as a fellow e-biker failed to signal whether he was turning left or right. "Remember your hand signals," I said in my brightest NDP-teacher-lady voice. "Remember to f-k off, ma'am," he replied as he skirted around me.
I rented an e-bike for the month of September - it's adorably French, teal blue, and compact enough to be mistaken for a regular two-wheeler - so that I could get to Lake Ontario and ride for miles along our Great Lake Waterfront Trail, which can make you feel like you're in Paris, there's so much beauty all around. Or maybe I'd head for the new white-and-red Port Lands Bridge, rising lightly like a metal bird, on my way to Cherry Beach. I wanted to hear the waves crash. I wanted an open view. I wanted to connect with a part of the city I'd turned my back on for too long, living in the annex as an aging non-driver.
And I wanted to get there on my own pedal power, with a little more kick to go the distance than my 40-year old Nishiki 12-speed was going to give me.
Clearly, I wasn't the only one with this idea.

The e-bike explosion began with the coronavirus pandemic, when fleets of mostly young men raced through the streets to make sure our Szechuan noodles were delivered piping hot, or else get docked in pay. Then, they wore the halo of essential workers. Now, as Toronto and other cities around the world scramble to build infrastructure to accommodate them, not so much.
The Star has been out in front of the story all year, reporting how the explosion of electric vehicles is overtaking the bike lanes; how the millions spent on those bike lanes isn't protecting cyclists from getting killed; and asking who's to blame for the e-bikes swarming the city sidewalks (all of us). A new column in the New York Times, titled "Street Wars," described that city's bike lanes as the most crowded offices in NYC for more than 70,000 delivery workers. The privileged Upper West Side, tellingly, is said by another source to be "one of the neighbourhoods where people love getting food deliveries. By some counts it's around 14,000 orders a day."
Copenhagen and Amsterdam, with their long-established biking populations, are out in front making room for bikes of all kinds: The Danish capital devotes entire train cars to the mostly e-bike workers commuting into that city. But even car-mad Mexico City, the largest city in North America, has a booming bike-sharing program, and Los Angeles, astonishingly, is planning a car-free Olympic Games.
"I've spent most of my life looking at what other cities are doing and then trying to bring that to Toronto to help make it a better place to get around," said Kevin McLaughlin, owner of ZyggE-bikes, the shop on Sorauren Avenue where I rented mine. Twenty-five years ago, he introduced car sharing to Toronto via AutoShare; now it's bike sharing. The place he's watching currently, as a passionate e-bike supporter, is Paris. "It's the new Amsterdam."
Mayor Anne Hidalgo made a statement last March that Paris has made a "major and radical rupture: the end of car-dependence." Cycling is now said to be more popular than driving in the centre of Paris. Fifty thousand parking spaces have been removed and more than 100 streets have been permanently closed to cars. At the same time, the city added 20,000 bike parking spaces and 700 charging stations for e-bikes.Paris will spend 250 million euros to complete 1,300 kilometres of bike lanes by 2026. Toronto will invest $105 million in 500 kilometres of bike routes by 2030.
There are all kinds of reasons why we can't be like Paris, I know that. Our city was built for cars, theirs was not. We're underserved by public transit, while the vast spider web of the Paris Metro is there to catch all the non-drivers who don't want to bike. But there are plenty of reasons for us to try.

I chose Cherry Beach as my first long ride. On the trip there, however, from my home in midtown Toronto - and none of this will be news to anyone navigating the city - I first needed to: withstand the other the bikes, e-bikes, scooters, skateboards and mopeds ringing for me to give way on the single-lane Bloor Street bike path; veer onto the sidewalk from an unprotected bike lane on College Street to avoid being clipped by a massive black pickup truck (not a working truck, but a style statement driven by a teenage girl); zig into racing traffic on Adelaide Street to bypass the cars and trucks parked in the precious bike lanes; make steady eye contact with drivers who often merely slowed down at stop signs; and dodge jaywalking pedestrians.
A 30-ish woman on Sherbourne Street was clearly doing the mental calculation - "old woman, bike, I can beat her" - before she stepped directly in front of me on the protected bike lane. Moving at 25 km/h, my dramatic stop nearly sent me over my handlebars.
I got as far as that pretty Port Lands Bridge on my first shot at the lake, then discovered the Cherry Street bridge was closed. Too daunted to go farther, I rode back through the same obstacle course, and found the pedal-assisted ride home was easy on my legs but hard on my heart, which was raging at all the bikers and drivers and pedestrians who'd ruined my ride. I imagined gliding free and easy along the Seine on my French e-bike and became sad about my failed outing.
But McLaughlin, who is 57 and hasn't owned a car in 30 years, "for environmental and for hassle reasons," isn't complaining. "I'm over the moon with where we are in Toronto. I can bike up Bayview in a protected bike lane. I never dreamed that would happen in my lifetime." He rides his own e-bike 12 months a year, says he never arrives at work "sweaty or tired," and hopes to covert more Torontonians to the e-bike commute.
His primary Zygg rental customers are couriers, parents who don't or won't own cars and want to e-cargo their kids to school, and people over 50 who are coming back to bikes and have more time to ride them. Some have parked their cars, at least in the city, for good, often for environmental reasons; and others just want more assistance as they get older to do the big hills and head out on longer rides.
"It sounds corny," McLaughlin said, "but what I say is, you're 15 or 20 years younger on an e-bike."

Marjorie Nichol has been riding a bike for almost 70 years, and her current bike for 40, same as me. "No one steals it because it's pink," she told me on the phone. After she retired as a producer at CBC, she and her husband began to go on long rides, "but at 72 some of the hills were getting bigger." She tried an e-bike but felt she didn't get enough exercise, so instead added a motor to her pink model. "I just love it."
As a working mother Nichol used to drive her three kids around the city in a minivan, often back and forth to the hospital to treat her son - for asthma. "I'd ask myself, 'what's wrong with this picture?' " The worst thing you can do for our health and safety is drive a car, said Nichol. "Imagine if all the people making our deliveries were in cars? Every time you see someone on an e-bike they are making the city safer, not more dangerous. I say God bless them every time."
This is an unconventional view of the safety of biking in Toronto: most older people drive instead of bike because it seems safer, because in a collision with a car it is a cyclist who dies. Of the 30 people who have been killed on our city roads so far in 2024, the CBC reported last week, 12 were pedestrians, eight motorists, four motorcyclists - and six cyclists.
E-biking is not without risk, but Nichol is right: the solution is fewer and better regulated cars, not fewer bikes. Her switch from mostly driving to mostly biking and then going electric was motivated by environmental considerations, but by happy coincidence "it turned out to be so much more fun than driving." She described herself as a "super coward" like me, who would never cycle on wide-open thoroughfares like Avenue Road, "even though I live on Avenue Road."
In 2019, Nichol and two other women got together to co-found Cycle55+ (you can find them on Facebook) - "just the three of us talking about how we ride." Holly Reid, another founder, was involved in seniors' advocacy for better bike lanes. "I'd look around me at the meetings and think, there's a lot of grey hair under these helmets," said Reid, herself 67. "We all wanted to feel safe and comfortable on Toronto streets." Now Cycle55+ organizes about five rides a year to help men and women explore the city by bike.
"Can I come?" I asked, excited as a kid, but the next ride is after my rental is over. I told Marjorie that I wanted to go far but wasn't sure of the way. Her answer: "All you need is an attachment for your phone on the front of your bike, and then turn on Google Maps. It'll change everything."

"Not a cloud in the sky," I said to myself at 7 a.m. Sunday on the Labour Day weekend. I entered the "Great Lake Waterfront Trail" route on my phone, and then attached it to the new holder on my e-bike handlebars. I folded some Kleenex for the road, to go with a small snack, water, sunglasses, sunscreen and credit cards. As I sat down to tie my shoelaces, I remembered the years when I would hop on my bike with nothing but my hands and feet to go wherever, for however long. Time changes most things.
As I rode under the blue-domed sky I got to know my e-bike. For all its compactness, it was much heavier - by almost 20 kilograms - than my nimble Nishiki.I averaged about 25 km/h, which was more speed than my physical effort merited. I had the sensation of moving fast and not moving at the same time. I suppose it's a lot like driving a car. The less work I did, the more I became a passenger.
It was a smooth trip on bike paths all the way to the Great Waterfront Trail and then - so fast, so fun - I was exactly where I wanted to be. On one side was the lake, as far as I could see, and on the other the beautifully treed waterfront park. I rode beside the lapping waves, grinning my head off, until I got to the wire fence of the massive construction zone that was once Ontario Place. Eating up acres of lakeshore. I managed not let that boondoggle ruin my ride and made my way farther west to Sunnyside Beach.
I sat there for a while, looking across the water to see if I could locate my hometown, Grimsby, on the other side. You sometimes can on a clear day, people say. Encouraged by the thrilling speed of the e-bike and the beauty of the day, I decided to go farther, up the Humber trail in a loop back home. This would eventually add about 20 km to my ride (I got lost for a while), not something I would have considered sans motor.
More people were out as I pedalled north. The paths were full of families with dogs and strollers and runners and bikers like me. Most of the e-bikes were bigger and more powerful than my own, but they throttled down on the shared paths. Groups of men my age on regular bikes chatted happily as they peddled past. A girl riding a bike for the first time veered from one side of the path to the other as her father ran behind her with his hand resting on her back. "Looking good," I said, smiling at her. She stared straight ahead and remained serious. It's still Toronto.
One man talking about some legal details on his phone took up the whole path, as did a woman also on her phone a little later, as if the rest of us were intruding on the personal space they'd created on their devices. They were the only two people on my two-hour ride who didn't make way, as they missed out on the best part of living in our city.
What's a city but a community of people who've chosen to live in proximity with each other, looking out not just for themselves, but fellow citizens. I'm going to try to rage less about who's clogging the roads and hogging my space and breaking the rules, and instead try to channel my road rage onto the roads themselves, and how we make them more accommodating for everyone, however we get around. I hear Paris has some good ideas.
Cathrin Bradburyis a Toronto-based journalist and freelance contributor for the Star. She is the author of "The Bright Side," published by Penguin Random House. Reach her via email: mcbradbury @gmail.com.

Document 357 of 500

Trudeau's carbon pricing is losing allies: Progressives such as B.C.'s premier, federal NDP are backing away after Conservative criticisms

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeaus-carbon-pricing-is-losing-allies/docview/3104427717/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+carbon+pricing+is+losing+allies%3A+Progressives+such+as+B.C.%27s+premier%2C+federal+NDP+are+backing+away+after+Conservative+criticisms&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When it comes to its national carbon pricing regime, the Liberal government is getting lonelier and lonelier.
First it was the opposition New Democrats who raised questions about their support for a policy they had unambiguously championed in the past two federal election campaigns. Earlier this spring, and again this week, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh suggested his party is looking at other carbon pricing options as it designs a new climate plan for the next campaign. Whatever they come up with, Singh claimed, would not impose a burden on everyday Canadians.
Then, hours later, one of the last pro-consumer carbon price premiers in Canada - British Columbia's David Eby, also a New Democrat - told reporters he's not so keen on the policy anymore. The province has had a consumer price on carbon since 2008, and until recently it wasn't a big political issue.
Until, that is, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre roared forward to dominate national public opinion polls with a relentless assault on carbon pricing. In B.C., the provincial Conservatives have now shaken off decades of irrelevance to challenge the NDP government in Victoria with an echo of Poilievre's drive to "axe the tax."
Eby told reporters in Vancouver he would ditch B.C.'s existing carbon tax if the federal government removes the requirement - introduced under the federal Liberals in 2018 - for all provinces to maintain a minimum carbon pricing system to drive down greenhouse gas emissions that fuel the climate crisis.
"Two things will happen. One is we'll remove the carbon tax for everyday British Columbians, for the farmers, for the truckers, for the average British Columbian," Eby said.
"The second thing is we believe that climate change is a real and present threat, unlike (B.C. Conservative Leader) John Rustad, who thinks it's a hoax. And so we will continue to ensure ... that the big polluters are paying their fair share."
The rhetoric is similar to that of Singh, who has said climate policy should target heavy industry rather than consumers. And it comes after sustained carbon price opposition from governments from Alberta to Ontario and the Atlantic - including Manitoba's NDP regime under Wab Kinew and Liberal Premier Andrew Furey's government in Newfoundland.
The Trudeau Liberals have long argued their policy is an essential piece of their arsenal in the fight against climate change, as Canada strives to meet its international pledge to slash greenhouse gas pollution to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030.
On Friday, Trudeau told reporters at a news conference near Quebec City that the NDP is "totally caving" to pressure from Poilievre's Conservatives to back away from consumer carbon pricing. He accused the party of walking away from a "world-leading" carbon pricing scheme that returns revenues to households in rebates that get doled out four times per year.
A family of four in Ontario, for example, is set to receive $1,120 in rebatesthis year.
"I do believe that Jagmeet Singh and the NDP actually do care about the environment," Trudeau said. "It's just increasingly obvious that they have no idea what to do to fight against climate change."
Asked to comment on Trudeau's criticism, a spokesperson for the NDP pointed to a news release on Friday that denounced the Liberal government's carbon pricing system as too soft on "big polluters," like the oil and gas company Suncor. The NDP also accused Trudeau of protecting "big oil profits" instead of "trying to make life easier for everyday families."
The federal pricing regime has two components: the consumer carbon price and a separate system for industrial polluters that the NDP has long criticized as too lenient.
But it's not only opposition parties and provinces casting doubt on the carbon price. Some federal Liberals have questioned it, and concerns from Atlantic Canadian MPs prompted the government to exempt home heating oil from the federal carbon price last year, while boosting funds for a program to replace that fuel with electric heat pumps.
On "The Herle Burly" podcast this summer, Liberal MP Marco Mendicino said it may be time for the government to revisit the consumer carbon price.
"I think that is an area that we need to look at. There's no doubt that when we did the carve out for Atlantic Canadians that people really started to scratch their heads and ask, you know, what is the point?" Mendicino said.
"And I know this is a contentious issue, particularly with people who care a lot about fighting climate change and protecting the environment. And it became a lightning rod. And it has become a very difficult issue for the government to navigate."
Mendicino declined Friday to discuss his comments further.
Environmentalists like Caroline Brouillette are concerned that politicians who are dropping their support for carbon pricing are wrongly blaming climate policies for inflation that has fuelled affordability woes. Brouillette pointed to a July report from the International Institute for Sustainable Development that found rising fossil fuel prices accounted for 33 per cent of Canada's overall inflation between February 2021 and June 2022.
"I am worried when I see politicians try to compete about who's most excited to axe the tax," Brouillette said.
In a statement, Mark Zacharias from the climate policy think tank Clean Energy Canada also blamed B.C.'s retreat from consumer carbon pricing on "politicians who misled Canadians" about the policy for political gain.
To Andrew Leach, an economist and law professor at the University of Alberta, politicians backing away from carbon pricing are reacting to widespread challenges with the rising cost of living. But he predicted that - as an economist who sees carbon pricing as the least disruptive way to push society away from greenhouse gas pollution - most observers will regret it if Canada loses its national pricing regime.
He also said it's wrong for politicians to suggest there is a "magical policy" that can combat climate change without impacting the economy.
With files from
Tonda MacCharles
and The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Premier David Eby told reporters in Vancouver he would ditch British Columbia's existing carbon pricing scheme if the federal government removes the requirement.ETHAN CAIRNSTHE CANADIAN PRESS

Document 358 of 500

Alcohol sales: Ford's beer boondoggle is nothing to brag about: Cohn, Sept. 10

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/alcohol-sales/docview/3104427645/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alcohol+sales%3A+Ford%27s+beer+boondoggle+is+nothing+to+brag+about%3A+Cohn%2C+Sept.+10&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Questioning the wisdom of booze
in corner stores
Ford's beer boondoggle is nothing to brag about: Cohn, Sept. 10
I had my first experience with the sale of beer in corner stores last weekend. It was about 11 p.m., and I stopped at a gas station on Brock Road, just south of Highway 407. A young fellow came into the store trying to buy beer. The clerk politely refused and an argument ensued. Possibly due to the fact there were other people in the store, the young fellow backed down and left cursing. Confrontation was minimal, but think of how it could have gone down if there were no witnesses. Would it not make more sense to have all the alcoholic drinks in a lockable cooler rather than displayed in the open? I'm sure this scenario will occur again and again, forcing the unfortunate night clerk to deal with a potentially violent situation.
Clark Bain, Mississauga

Why is it that cigarettes are kept behind the counter and out of sight in convenience stores and yet now our second-most-addictive drug available for sale is in plain view? Some are in kid-friendly containers with no health warnings. This is a big win for the alcohol industry, as we simultaneously are paying the Beer Store at least $225 million of taxpayers' money.
James Wigmore, Toronto

In my experience in the U.S., convenience stores and other outlets near tourist or recreation areas like parks benefit from the sale of alcohol the most. People don't want to drive farther away from their tourist destination to find an alcoholic beverage to enjoy while relaxing. The inner city of Toronto and residential areas will probably not see a lot of sales.
Dean Wiley, Mississauga

Premier Doug Ford has decided to restrict safe drug consumption sites, by severely restricting where they may be located in spite of scientific evidence that they save lives and mitigate harm. At the same time, he has decided to liberalize alcohol consumption, making it available at convenience stores and also gas stations, which are usually frequented by motor vehicle drivers. I've not heard a peep from police, road safety advocates or injury prevention professionals about the "wisdom" of this policy change. I trust that the next time a driver causes a collision or fatality after picking up a six pack at a gas station convenience store, Ford will be charged as an accessory to this crime. Can we hope for mandatory jail time for the premier if the cause of the crime occurred near a school or daycare centre?
Brigitte Nowak, Toronto

Most Ontarians are not "blissfully oblivious" to the sale of beer in corner stores. We have lost a family member to a drunk driver, a driver who chose to drink and drive and destroy our lives, as well as many others.
Beverly Northeast, Goodwood

Document 360 of 500

PM won't resign if Liberals lose byelection: Montreal riding previously held by Trudeau's former justice minister

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-wont-resign-if-liberals-lose-byelection/docview/3104427467/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+won%27t+resign+if+Liberals+lose+byelection%3A+Montreal+riding+previously+held+by+Trudeau%27s+former+justice+minister&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A combative Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Friday he's not going to resign as Liberal leader if his party loses a high-stakes Montreal byelection on Monday, the same day his minority government is slated to return to Parliament without the NDP promising to prop it up.
In an interview on the Montreal radio station CJAD 800, Trudeau said he will stay on regardless of the result in LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, a riding held by the Liberals' former justice minister David Lametti from 2015 until he resigned from federal politics earlier this year.
Asked what it means for him if his party loses the contest, Trudeau said it would show the Liberals simply have more work to do to convince voters to support them.
He also suggested the federal Conservatives under Pierre Poilievre would take actions that harm Canadians if they take power in the next election.
The byelection in Montreal is one of two set for Monday, with the other contest taking place in Elmwood-Transcona, a Winnipeg riding long held by the New Democratic Party.
It also comes more than two months after Trudeau faced a wave of pressure over his leadership when the Liberal candidate lost to the Conservative in a byelection in Toronto-St. Paul's, which had been a Liberal stronghold. The defeat unleashed calls for Trudeau to resign from Liberals like former environment minister Catherine McKenna and former B.C. Premier Christy Clark, who told the Star she's not ruling out a future campaign for the federal leadership.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long remains the only sitting Liberal MP to publicly call for Trudeau's resignation, although Toronto MP Nathaniel Erskine-Smith has said the party's members should vote on the prime minister's continued leadership.
Ahead of the Montreal byelection, some Liberals have suggested another loss in long-held Grit territory could put even more pressure on Trudeau.
"If we lose the Montreal one, people are going to be up in arms," one MP told the Star in July.
In the interview Friday, Trudeau expressed excitement about returning to Parliament after the summer break, even without the formal support of the NDP.
Last week, the New Democrats dropped out of an alliance with the Liberals, in which they propped up Trudeau's minority government in exchange for policies like dental care for lower-income Canadians without coverage, public coverage of birth control and diabetes medication costs, and a new law banning replacement workers during strikes or lockouts.
The prime minister also took a direct shot at Poilievre's integrity after the radio station played a clip of the Conservative leader denouncing the federal government's price on carbon by claiming it has "deleted" $25 billion of annual gross domestic product (GDP) and is therefore costing "every family in Canada" $1,400 per year.
Without elaborating, Trudeau said what Poilievre claimed is false and derided him as dishonest.
"There's a convention around parliamentarians that we're not supposed to call each other liars," he said, adding, "Pierre Poilievre is a liar."
Trudeau noted that the federal carbon pricing system includes rebates that return proceeds from the consumer levy to households four times a year. A family of four in Ontario, for instance, will get more than $1,100 in rebates under that system this year.
A spokesperson for Poilievre did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
CAPTION: "I'm not going anywhere," Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said during an interview on Montreal radio station CJAD 800. "I've gota fight to lead against people who want to hurt this country, who want to hurt our communities."
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall Toronto Star

Document 361 of 500

'I'm not going anywhere': Justin Trudeau says he won't resign if Liberals lose Montreal byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/im-not-going-anywhere-justin-trudeau-says-he-wont/docview/3104599586/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27m+not+going+anywhere%27%3A+Justin+Trudeau+says+he+won%27t+resign+if+Liberals+lose+Montreal+byelection&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - A combative Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said Friday he's not going to resign as Liberal leader if his party loses a high-stakes Montreal byelection on Monday, the same day his minority government is slated to return to Parliament without the NDP promising to prop it up.
In an interview on the Montreal radio station CJAD 800, Trudeau said he will stay on regardless of the result in LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, a riding held by the Liberals' former justice minister David Lametti from 2015 until he resigned from federal politics earlier this year.
Asked what it means for him if his party loses the contest, Trudeau said it would show the Liberals simply have more work to do to convince voters to support them.
He also suggested the federal Conservatives under Pierre Poilievre would take actions that harm Canadians if they take power in the next election.
"I'm not going anywhere," Trudeau said. "I've got a fight to lead against people who want to hurt this country, who want to hurt our communities, and who want to take the country in directions that - quite frankly - are exactly the opposite of where the world needs to go."
The byelection in Montreal is one of two set for Monday, with the other contest taking place in Elmwood-Transcona, a Winnipeg riding long held by the New Democratic Party.
It also comes more than two months after Trudeau faced a wave of pressure over his leadership when the Liberal candidate lost to the Conservative in a byelection in Toronto-St. Paul's, which had been a Liberal stronghold. The defeat unleashed calls for Trudeau to resign from Liberals like former environment minister Catherine McKenna and former B.C. Premier Christy Clark, who told the Star she's not ruling out a future campaign for the federal leadership.
New Brunswick MP Wayne Long remains the only sitting Liberal MP to publicly call for Trudeau's resignation, although Toronto MP Nathaniel Erskine-Smith has said the party's members should vote on the prime minister's continued leadership.
Ahead of the Montreal byelection, some Liberals have suggested another loss in long-held Grit territory could put even more pressure on Trudeau.
"If we lose the Montreal one, people are going to be up in arms," one MP told the Star in July.
In the interview Friday, Trudeau expressed excitement about returning to Parliament after the summer break, even without the formal support of the NDP.
Last week, the New Democrats dropped out of an alliance with the Liberals, in which they propped up Trudeau's minority government in exchange for policies like dental care for lower-income Canadians without coverage, public coverage of birth control and diabetes medication costs, and a new law banning replacement workers during strikes or lockouts.
The prime minister also took a direct shot at Poilievre's integrity after the radio station played a clip of the Conservative leader denouncing the federal government's price on carbon by claiming it has "deleted" $25 billion of annual gross domestic product (GDP) and is therefore costing "every family in Canada" $1,400 per year.
Without elaborating, Trudeau said what Poilievre claimed is false and derided him as dishonest.
"There's a convention around parliamentarians that we're not supposed to call each other liars," he said, adding, "Pierre Poilievre is a liar."
Trudeau noted that the federal carbon pricing system includes rebates that return proceeds from the consumer levy to households four times a year. A family of four in Ontario, for instance, will get more than $1,100 in rebates under that system this year.
A spokesperson for Poilievre did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau makes an aerospace announcementin Ste.Anne-de-Bellevue, Quebec onSept. 13, 2024.

Document 362 of 500

Justin Trudeau's 'carbon tax' is losing more allies as Pierre Poilievre's relentless assault continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeaus-carbon-tax-is-losing-more-allies/docview/3104599539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau%27s+%27carbon+tax%27+is+losing+more+allies+as+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+relentless+assault+continues&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - When it comes to its national carbon pricing regime, the Liberal government is getting lonelier and lonelier.
First it was the opposition New Democrats who raised questions about their support for a policy they had unambiguously championed in the past two federal election campaigns. Earlier this spring, and again this week, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh suggested his party is looking at other carbon pricing options as it designs a new climate plan for the next campaign. Whatever they come up with, Singh claimed, would not impose a burden on everyday Canadians.
Then, hours later, one of the last pro-consumer carbon price premiers in Canada - British Columbia's David Eby, also a New Democrat - told reporters he's not so keen on the policy anymore. The province has had a consumer price on carbon since 2008, and until recently it wasn't a big political issue.
Until, that is, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre roared forward to dominate national public opinion polls with a relentless assault on carbon pricing. In B.C., the provincial Conservatives have now shaken off decades of irrelevance to challenge the NDP government in Victoria with an echo of Poilievre's drive to "axe the tax."
Eby told reporters in Vancouver he would ditch B.C.'s existing carbon tax if the federal government removes the requirement - introduced under the federal Liberals in 2018 - for all provinces to maintain a minimum carbon pricing system to drive down greenhouse gas emissions that fuel the climate crisis.
"Two things will happen. One is we'll remove the carbon tax for everyday British Columbians, for the farmers, for the truckers, for the average British Columbian," Eby said.
"The second thing is we believe that climate change is a real and present threat, unlike (B.C. Conservative Leader) John Rustad, who thinks it's a hoax. And so we will continue to ensure ... that the big polluters are paying their fair share."
The rhetoric is similar to that of Singh, who has said climate policy should target heavy industry rather than consumers. And it comes after sustained carbon price opposition from governments from Alberta to Ontario and the Atlantic - including Manitoba's NDP regime under Wab Kinew, and Liberal Premier Andrew Furey's government in Newfoundland.
The Trudeau Liberals have long argued their policy is an essential piece of their arsenal in the fight against climate change, as Canada strives to meet its international pledge to slash greenhouse gas pollution to at least 40 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030.
On Friday, Trudeau told reporters at a news conference near Quebec City that the NDP is "totally caving" to pressure from Poilievre's Conservatives to back away from consumer carbon pricing. He accused the party of walking away from a "world-leading" carbon pricing scheme that returns revenues to households in rebates that get doled out four times per year.
A family of four in Ontario, for example, is set to receive $1,120 in rebates this year.
"I do believe that Jagmeet Singh and the NDP actually do care about the environment," Trudeau said. "It's just increasingly obvious that they have no idea what to do to fight against climate change."
Asked to comment on Trudeau's criticism, a spokesperson for the NDP pointed to a news release on Friday that denounced the Liberal government's carbon pricing system as too soft on "big polluters," like the oil and gas company Suncor. The NDP also accused Trudeau of protecting "big oil profits" instead of "trying to make life easier for everyday families."
The federal pricing regime has two components: the consumer carbon price, and a separate system for industrial polluters that the NDP has long criticized as too lenient.
But it's not only opposition parties and provinces casting doubt on the carbon price. Some federal Liberals have questioned it, and concerns from Atlantic Canadian MPs prompted the government to exempt home heating oil from the federal carbon price last year, while boosting funds for a program to replace that fuel with electric heat pumps.
On The Herle Burly podcast this summer, Liberal MP Marco Mendicino said it may be time for the government to revisit the consumer carbon price.
"I think that is an area that we need to look at. There's no doubt that when we did the carve out for Atlantic Canadians that people really started to scratch their heads and ask, you know, what is the point?" Mendicino said.
"And I know this is a contentious issue, particularly with people who care a lot about fighting climate change and protecting the environment. And it became a lightning rod. And it has become a very difficult issue for the government to navigate."
Mendicino declined Friday to discuss his comments further.
Environmentalists like Caroline Brouillette are concerned that politicians who are dropping their support for carbon pricing are wrongly blaming climate policies for inflation that has fuelled affordability woes. Brouillette pointed to a July report from the International Institute for Sustainable Development that found rising fossil fuel prices accounted for 33 per cent of Canada's overall inflation between February 2021 and June 2022.
"I am worried when I see politicians try to compete about who's most excited to axe the tax," Brouillette said.
In a statement, Mark Zacharias from the climate policy think tank Clean Energy Canada also blamed B.C.'s retreat from consumer carbon pricing on "politicians who misled Canadians" about the policy for political gain.
To Andrew Leach, an economist and law professor at the University of Alberta, politicians backing away from carbon pricing are reacting to widespread challenges with the rising cost of living. But he predicted that - as an economist who sees carbon pricing as the least disruptive way to push society away from greenhouse gas pollution - most observers will regret it if Canada loses its national pricing regime.
He also said it's wrong for politicians to suggest there is a "magical policy" that can combat climate change without impacting the economy.
With files from Tonda MacCharles and The Canadian Press
CAPTION: B.C. Premier David Eby speaks during a campaign event in Vancouver on Sept. 12, 2024.

Document 363 of 500

A jury found Umar Zameer not guilty of murder. His case raises tougher questions about all of us

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jury-found-umar-zameer-not-guilty-murder-his-case/docview/3104599529/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+jury+found+Umar+Zameer+not+guilty+of+murder.+His+case+raises+tougher+questions+about+all+of+us&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford crossed every line when he condemned the courts for granting bail to an accused cop-killer in a controversial 2021 case.
Letting Umar Zameer out of jail "was beyond comprehension" and "completely unacceptable," according to Ford.
That said, the premier wasn't the only one to say it: John Tory, then Toronto's mayor, uttered similar improprieties and hyperboles.
Let's be honest. In truth, these two top leaders were merely voicing publicly what many Torontonians were likely thinking privately at the time.
Yet we look to our leaders to lead - to do (and say) the right thing in tough times, not just tell people what they want to hear. Today, it's time for a reckoning about them, but also about us - our society's strengths and weaknesses.
In reality, the righteous indignation of the premier and mayor of the day was dead wrong - not merely a misreading of their leadership roles, but a misunderstanding of the case.
In fact, there was good reason to release the suspect on bail, because the case against him was so weak and the flight risk so low (with house arrest and an ankle bracelet). As my colleague Betsy Powell revealed in the Star a week ago, the case was full of holes and ultimately collapsed in court, culminating in a jury acquittal.
What's so compelling about Powell's in-depth analysis is not just the contradictions of the case but the complexities. When the full story came out in court, public opinion shifted dramatically - instead of questioning the suspect's bail conditions, people were doubting police testimony and the entire prosecution.
I won't relitigate the case here, but what's so interesting about Powell's explanatory piece is how it addresses and answers questions of policing and prosecuting. Flowing from that are some societal questions that still remain unanswered.
First, prosecutorial discretion isn't as simple as it sounds.
It's worth noting that even Zameer's lawyer agreed, after a preliminary hearing testing the testimony and evidence, that there was enough to proceed to trial. The defence acknowledged that questions of credibility are up to jurors as triers of fact.
Still, as more details emerged at the full trial, first-degree murder charges looked less and less plausible. Zameer had been getting into his car after a Canada Day celebration with his pregnant wife and child when they were suddenly accosted by a couple of tough-looking plain clothes police officers.
They feared being robbed in an underground garage and escaped in a panic - running over one of the cops. The jury believed them, and seemingly disbelieved the police testimony that Zameer had wilfully killed their colleague.
It was a vindication of sorts for the accused, a verdict that resonated with the public. But it took a trial to clear the air.
Zameer paid a heavy price for that vindication - incarceration for three months before he got bail, years of house arrest, and onerous lawyers' bills. Initially, he faced public vilification and suspicion given his roots as an immigrant from Malaysia, originally from Pakistan.
There can be no doubt that the trial imposed an unfair burden on the accused, but justice isn't about social justice. It's about adjudicating disputes and ascertaining truth.
And yet Zameer had a hideous introduction to Canada so soon after arriving as an immigrant. Was he discriminated against as a brown Muslim with a foreign name and no citizenship?
Initially, almost certainly. Here's another question, at a time when white privilege is so often offered as the sole explanation for everything: Did Zameer ultimately benefit from stereotypes, or more precisely, positive archetypes - not his immigration status but his social status, including education and religious faith?
It could not have escaped the notice of any jury, which is only human, that he was an accountant driving a BMW, accompanied by his pregnant wife in a hijab and his innocent child - images of assiduity, piety and even maternity.
That's not to deny the reality of white privilege, just a reminder that other class and social indicators are also influential (at least in multicultural Toronto).
Zameer had the misfortune to stumble into a perfect storm - a plain clothes operation gone awry, a police death, and a controversial prosecution. In truth, that fate could befall any of us.
The test of law and order is not that it achieves perfection, but that it strives for a just resolution. Our premier and mayor behaved badly (Tory later apologized), the police chief spoke badly, and the front-line cops may have testified wrongly.
But the courts handled the cases dispassionately and the jury acted wisely, with full credit to Zameer's defence team. Toronto police Chief Myron Demkiw later asked the OPP to conduct an independent review into the officers' testimony, their procedures and training - notably in plain clothes operations.
Ultimately, Zameer was given the benefit of the doubt - not just in the law courts but the court of public opinion - and a fundraising campaign has raised hundreds of thousands of dollars to defray the cost of his defence. As for the prosecutors, they got a bum rap in the end - a trial seems to have been the correct course - but that's part of the job description in our judicial system.
CAPTION: Umar Zameer and his lawyers walk away from Toronto courthouse following his not-guilty verdict on April 21, 2024.

Subject: Bail; Public opinion; Leadership; Testimony; Acquittals & mistrials; House arrest; Murders & murder attempts; Trials; Social privilege

A Toronto ferry crashed that went unreported + accusations of racism for Pierre Poilievre's nicknaming

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/toronto-ferry-crashed-that-went-unreported/docview/3103995254/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+Toronto+ferry+crashed+that+went+unreported+%2B+accusations+of+racism+for+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+nicknaming&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, September 13 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on how a ferry crashed into an airport, Pierre Poilievre's potentially problematic nicknaming and a historic home construction crackdown.
DON'T MISSThe city didn't report a ferry's crash into an airport runway
The federal authority that owns and operates Billy Bishop Airport didn't know that a city ferry had crashed into one of its runways in April. The city blamed heavy fog for the crash of the Thomas Rennie, a ferry carrying 16 people, while an expert said it "never should have happened." Here's why it did.
Mixed signals: The city reported the accident to Canada's Transportation Safety Board but neither Billy Bishop nor the public were informed.Ferry tale: Toronto's ferries have hit some turbulent waters in recent years. In 2022, a ferry crashed into the Jack Layton docks, injuring 20 passengers and prompting a $2-million lawsuit. A report released last month blamed the crash on safety failures.Is Pierre Poilievre's nickname for Jagmeet Singh racist?
A Liberal minister is going after Pierre Poilievre for his use of the "Sellout Singh" nickname he coined for the NDP leader. "Singh" is a name given to all practicing Sikh men as a symbol of equality in their faith. Minister Harjit Singh Sajjan said Poilievre's use of the nickname was "absolutely offensive" and "racist" - an allegation the Tories reject. How did this war of accusations even start? Mark Ramzy explains.
The defence: "If Sellout Jagmeet Singh wants to shake the name, he can commit today to voting down the costly NDP-Liberal coalition to trigger a carbon tax election," a spokesperson for Poilievre said.Singh says: Not much about the nickname, but he did go after Poilievre for his habit of resorting to personal attacks instead of critiquing policy.Bigger fish: Singh is more focused on cementing his party's position on the war in Gaza, which will soon be put to the test in a Montreal byelection.Ontario's regulator slapped Albion with a long list of charges
The Home Construction Regulatory Authority has laid 124 charges against Albion Building Consultant Inc. for illegally building and selling homes - and it gets worse. In its largest investigation ever, the regulator is seeking a restraining order to prevent the builder from "further violating the law." Still, it's no surprise that the charges were incoming - Albion's has a sordid history.
Hammered home: The charges laid by the regulator include illegal building and selling, failure to enrol homes, failure to comply with conditions and being a party to an offence.A warning: "We advise all consumers to be on the lookout and extremely cautious of Albion falsely presenting itself as a licensed builder, which it is not," the regulator added. "They are not qualified or authorized to build or sell homes."WHAT ELSE

Document 365 of 500

After tragic death, it's time for justice and accountability

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/after-tragic-death-time-justice-accountability/docview/3103746141/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+tragic+death%2C+it%27s+time+for+justice+and+accountability&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "I believe the system failed her," Doug Ford said on Wednesday, my Queen's Park colleague Rob Ferguson reported. The premier was talking about Neveah, the four-year-old girl whose remains were found in a dumpster in 2022, about a year after her death.
Yes, the system failed her. That's putting it mildly. When this small child died and wound up in the garbage in Rosedale, no one even knew she was missing. No one was looking for her. It took police more than a year of investigation involving dozens of officers to even figure out who she was.
And when they did find out, as my colleagues Wendy Gillis and Jennifer Pagliaro reported in a staggering report late last month, the story was almost as shocking as her death. It turned out that she had been in care of child protection agencies for much of her short life. And that they had essentially closed the book on her, returning her to her mother in part because of a jurisdictional technicality, and then never noticing that she disappeared. Her mother claims (though police are skeptical) that she simply gave Neveah away one day to complete strangers she met at a doughnut shop, and then never followed up with those strangers or authorities to see how she was doing.
This is a mother who the Toronto Children's Aid society had supported in an application for full unsupervised custody of Neveah just three months earlier - support that the Star's reporting says indicates that "child protection concerns have been resolved and, crucially, that the agency will no longer officially monitor the child's well being." And this just months after York Region's CAS had sought to review the mother's custody, but lost the chance because she had moved to Toronto.
From beginning to end, it's a story of failure. Shocking failure. Saddening failure. Enraging failure.
And as of right now, there have been no arrests in the case. If any of the workers at the child protection authorities who should have overseen Neveah's care, or followed up to notice she'd gone missing, or at least seen the police reports about the dead child and checked to see if they knew who she was - if any of them have been fired or demoted or disciplined in any way, we don't know about it, because the agencies involved refused to comment to the Star on the case. There still doesn't seem to be an identified cause of death.
It was a failure, all right. Though that word seems inadequate to describe the horrific totality of the situation.
Something should had been done to protect Neveah. And nothing was. And those who should have done the protecting didn't even care enough to notice.
So something should be done now. To find out what happened, to demand a measure of accountability, to ensure this cannot happen again to some other child.
Premier Ford says he's open to a coroner's inquest into the case, though it is up to Ontario's chief coroner to call one. The coroner should get the hell on with it.
An inquest, examining Neveah's case and all the ways it went wrong, and publicly pursuing an explanation for how and why this could happen - how, as the Star's editorial board put it, "a system that understood from her earliest days that this was a child in trouble could not keep her alive." And making clear exactly what needs to be changed to prevent it from happening again seems like the very least thing we could collectively do now.
As Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's Child Advocate from 2008 to 2019, told the Star, the authorities involved here displayed either "incompetence" or "obstruction," and we need to find out which, and either hold people directly accountable or change the procedures that allowed this to happen. Or both.
It will not be enough, of course. Confronted with a four-year-old found dead in a dumpster, it is already too late to do anything that would be considered "enough."
But an inquest would be a start. It would provide some answers, however unsatisfying and maddening they might ultimately be, to the very many horrifying questions raised by this case. More importantly, it could and should point out what could be done to spare the next child who finds themselves in Neveah's shoes from suffering a similar fate.
We did not take any action in time to help Neveah, to our collective shame. We owe it to her memory to take action now to ensure nothing like it ever happens again.
CREDIT: Edward Keenan Opinion

Document 366 of 500

Ford promises jobs for staff: Cleaning service employees warned of layoffs Oct. 31

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-promises-jobs-staff/docview/3103745926/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+promises+jobs+for+staff%3A+Cleaning+service+employees+warned+of+layoffs+Oct.+31&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is promising no one working at the shuttered Ontario Science Centre will be left unemployed as the museum is moved to a new location.
"We'll find jobs for those people within the system, so we'll help them out," Ford told reporters Thursday in Toronto.
"I don't like anyone ever losing their jobs," he said.
Ford's comments came after the Canadian Press reported Dexterra Group, which provided the museum's cleaning services, informed the Ontario Public Service Employees Union that 28 employees will lose their jobs by Oct. 31.
"They're devastated," said Martin Fischer, president of OPSEU Local 549, which represents 500 science centre workers.
"With the plans for science centre to continue at a new location, they really wanted to be part of that," said Fischer.
In June, the Don Mills facility was abruptly closed after an engineering report warned the aerated concrete used in the roof could collapse.
Opened in 1969, the science centre was due to be moved to Ontario Place in 2028 as part of the controversial redevelopment of that waterfront park, which includes a privately owned Therme water spa.
Ford's political opponents claimed he wanted the 55-year-old building to become unusable so he could more easily relocate the science centre. Critics have said the heritage building, designed by Canadian architect Raymond Moriyama, could and should be salvaged.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said last week the "science centre closure was a blatantly political move orchestrated by the Ford government alone."
"Other buildings - like the ROM or the CN Tower - are properly repaired and restored because we recognize their historical, cultural and educational value. Why not this building in this community?" said Schreiner.
A temporary home for the science centre is not expected to open until January 2026.
Ford said Thursday that no interim site has yet been chosen amid reports a Mississauga convention centre could serve as its home for two years.
"What I want to do is listen to other options, because I know Toronto has options. Mississauga does and everyone else," he said.
"I just want to get a new one built as quickly as possible."
The Don Mills Road property is owned by the city and was leased to the province for a century in 1964.
But city hall - already struggling to maintain the recreational facilities it now operates, such as parks that are increasingly sites of homeless tent encampments - has said it could not afford to operate a museum there.
With files from
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: The Ontario Science Centre was closed in June after a report showed some concrete roofing panels were deteriorating and needed to be replaced.R.J. JohnstonToronto Star
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief With files from The Canadian Press
