Document 367 of 500

'Removed from our shelves': Concerns over mould in margarine sold through Loblaws, No Frills, Shoppers Drug Mart, Real Canadian Superstore and Zehrs plus vegan chocolate and more recalls trigger Health Canada warnings to shoppers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/removed-our-shelves-concerns-over-mould-margarine/docview/3104161671/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Removed+from+our+shelves%27%3A+Concerns+over+mould+in+margarine+sold+through+Loblaws%2C+No+Frills%2C+Shoppers+Drug+Mart%2C+Real+Canadian+Superstore+and+Zehrs+plus+vegan+chocolate+and+more+recalls+trigger+Health+Canada+warnings+to+shoppers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Several items have been recalled by Health Canada over the past week due to a number of health and safety concerns.
PC Blue Menu brand original margarine and margarine with olive oil
Health Canada says these products have been recalled over concerns of mould. Consumers are advised "not to use, sell, serve or distribute the affected products."
Loblaw said the products are no longer being sold at its locations nationwide.
"Food safety and quality assurance are fundamental to our business," a spokesperson from Loblaw said.
"The PC Blue Menu brand Original Margarine and Margarine with Olive Oil recall impacted Loblaw grocery (i.e. Real Canadian Superstore, No Frills, Zehrs) and Shoppers Drug Mart stores nationally, and these products have since been removed from our shelves."
Ceiling hoists, field spray and vegan chocolate sold through Amazon
A number of products sold through Amazon have been recalled over the past week.
"Our Product Safety Team proactively investigates and addresses reported safety complaints and incidents to ensure customer protection from potential product-related safety risks," Amazon says on its website.
"We closely monitor public recall alert websites and receive notifications from vendors and sellers. Upon discovering a product recall, we immediately halt the affected product offerings and promptly inform both the customers and sellers involved about the recall."
Delta Cycle ceiling hoists with straps have been recalled over a potential injury hazard.
"The plastic clip used to hold larger items may fail, causing the larger items to fall and presenting an injury hazard," said Health Canada in its recall notice. "Use of this product without the use of straps (ie. just a bicycle) does not present a hazard."
According to Health Canada, the company has received no reports of incidents or injuries in Canada while there have been four reported incidents, one of which involved a minor injury in the United States as of Aug. 7.
The recall was also posted on The Home Depot's website.
"These straps are used in storing a kayak, canoe, ladder or other larger items," Delta Cycle said in its recall.
"If you are not using the straps and only using the hooks, no further action is required."
For more information, including how to get replacement straps from Delta Cycle, consumers can contact the company by phone at 1-508-698-0606, Monday to Friday, 9 a.m.-5 p.m. EST, by email at hoiststraprecall@deltacycle.com or visit the company website.
Dead Down Wind odour eliminating field spray has also been recalled due to improper labelling.
The recalled products do not have the proper consumer chemical labelling as required by the Consumer Chemicals and Containers Regulations, 2001, under the Canada Consumer Product Safety Act, Health Canada said in its recall.
"The lack of appropriate labelling information could result in unintentional exposure to the products and lead to serious illness or injury."
Consumers are advised to immediately stop using the product and dispose of it according to municipal hazardous waste guidelines.
Arcus Hunting, LLC can be reached by telephone at 1-678-342-9000, Monday to Friday, 8 a.m. - 5 p.m. EST for more information about the recall.
Nutrabolics brand Feed Me vegan real food protein & oats bars (chocolate coconut flavour) have also been recalled because of undeclared milk in the product, prompting a warning to not consume the product if you are allergic or sensitive to milk.
Health Canada advises those who may be affected by consuming the product to throw it out or return it to the location where it was purchased.
Unauthorized sexual enhancement products
A wide range of sexual enhancement products have been recalled due to concerns they contain ingredients that may pose "serious health risks."
The full list of products can be found here.
Health Canada says some of the products have been found to include ingredients such as sildenafil and/or tadalafil, prescription drugs used to treat erectile disfunction but may cause "potentially life-threatening low blood pressure."
The agency also says other recalled products were found to include levodopa - a prescription drug is combined with other drug ingredients in anti-Parkinson's medications - and/or prasterone - recognized as a controlled substance in Canada and illegal to sell without proper authorization from Health Canada.
"Health Canada is advising about unauthorized sexual enhancement products that may pose serious health risks," the agency said in its recall.
"The products are labelled to contain or have been tested and found to contain dangerous ingredients."
The products were sold out of a number of convenience stores and adult shops in Mississauga, Markham, Whitby, Woodbridge, Niagara Falls, Toronto, Etobicoke, North York, Kitchener, Vaughan and St. Catharines.

The Kirkland Signature brand Greek Yogurt (24 x 100 gram pack) has been recalled due to mould. The recall impacts a product with a best before date of Aug. 10, 2024.
The Greek yogurt has a UPC code of 0 96619 22215 5.
"Do not use, sell, serve or distribute the affected product," a recall notice says.
The product was sold in Ontario, Alberta, British Columbia, Manitoba, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador, Nova Scotia, Quebec and Saskatchewan.
Also this week, Health Canada issued a recall on margarine sold at Loblaw-branded stores, also over concerns of mould.
The recall impacts PC Blue Menu original margarine (907 g) with best before dates of Nov. 17, 2024 and Nov. 18, 2024, PC Blue Menu original margarine (1.81 kg) with a best before date of Nov. 18, 2024, and PC Blue Menu margarine with olive oil (907 g) with a best before date of Nov. 17, 2024.

Document 370 of 500

Neveah's awful story and tragic death mean we must seek justice and accountability - for the next child's sake

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/neveahs-awful-story-tragic-death-mean-we-must/docview/3103746213/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Neveah%27s+awful+story+and+tragic+death+mean+we+must+seek+justice+and+accountability+-+for+the+next+child%27s+sake&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "I believe the system failed her," Doug Ford said on Wednesday, my Queen's Park colleague Rob Ferguson reported. The premier was talking about Neveah, the four-year-old girl whose remains were found in a dumpster in 2022, about a year after her death.
Yes, the system failed her. That's putting it mildly. When this small child died and wound up in the garbage in Rosedale, no one even knew she was missing. No one was looking for her. It took police more than a year of investigation involving dozens of officers to even figure out who she was.
And when they did find out, as my colleagues Wendy Gillis and Jennifer Pagliaro reported in a staggering report late last month, the story was almost as shocking as her death. It turned out that she had been in care of child protection agencies for much of her short life. And that they had essentially closed the book on her, returning her to her mother in part because of a jurisdictional technicality, and then never noticing that she disappeared. Her mother claims (though police are skeptical) that she simply gave Neveah away one day to complete strangers she met at a doughnut shop, and then never followed up with those strangers or authorities to see how she was doing.
This is a mother who the Toronto Children's Aid society had supported in an application for full unsupervised custody of Neveah just three months earlier - support that the Star's reporting says indicates that "child protection concerns have been resolved and, crucially, that the agency will no longer officially monitor the child's well being." And this just months after York Region's CAS had sought to review the mother's custody but lost the chance because she had moved to Toronto.
From beginning to end, it's a story of failure. Shocking failure. Saddening failure. Enraging failure.
And as of right now, there have been no arrests in the case. If any of the workers at the child protection authorities who should have overseen Neveah's care, or followed up to notice she'd gone missing, or at least seen the police reports about the dead child and checked to see if they knew who she was - if any of them have been fired or demoted or disciplined in any way, we don't know about it, because the agencies involved refused to comment to the Star on the case. There still doesn't seem to be an identified cause of death.
It was a failure, all right. Though that word seems inadequate to describe the horrific totality of the situation.
Something should had been done to protect Neveah. And nothing was. And those who should have done the protecting didn't even care enough to notice.
So something should be done now. To find out what happened, to demand a measure of accountability, to ensure this cannot happen again to some other child.
Premier Ford says he's open to a coroner's inquest into the case, though it is up to Ontario's chief coroner to call one. The coroner should get the hell on with it.
An inquest, examining Neveah's case and all the ways it went wrong, and publicly pursuing an explanation for how and why this could happen - how, as the Star's editorial board put it, "a system that understood from her earliest days that this was a child in trouble could not keep her alive." And making clear exactly what needs to be changed to prevent it from happening again seems like the very least thing we could collectively do now.
As Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's Child Advocate from 2008 to 2019, told the Star, the authorities involved here displayed either "incompetence" or "obstruction," and we need to find out which, and either hold people directly accountable or change the procedures that allowed this to happen. Or both.
It will not be enough, of course. Confronted with a four-year-old found dead in a dumpster, it is already too late to do anything that would be considered "enough."
But an inquest would be a start. It would provide some answers, however unsatisfying and maddening they might ultimately be, to the very many horrifying questions raised by this case. More importantly, it could and should point out what could be done to spare the next child who finds themselves in Neveah's shoes from suffering a similar fate.
We did not take any action in time to help Neveah, to our collective shame. We owe it to her memory to take action now to ensure nothing like it ever happens again.
CAPTION: A composite sketch of Neveah, whose body was found in a Rosedale dumpster on May 2, 2022.

Subject: Children & youth; Inquests; Accountability

Title: Neveah's awful story and tragic death mean we must seek justice and accountability - for the next child's sake

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 12, 2024

Section: opinion/star-columnists

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Document 371 of 500

Canadian credit card holders' financial health worsening, report finds

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadian-credit-card-holders-financial-health/docview/3103433351/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canadian+credit+card+holders%27+financial+health+worsening%2C+report+finds&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadian credit card holders' financial health is falling, with credit card interest rates and revolving debt on the rise, according to a new report.
Some 57 per cent of credit card customers are now "financially unhealthy," up from 52 per cent in 2023, with 36 per cent of customers now carrying revolving credit card debt, up from 34 per cent a year ago, according to a study by J.D. Power.
"Essentially, we have seen a decline in financial health and an increase in financial pressures in consumers over the last year, which is a continuation of some trends that we saw in 2023," said John Cabell, managing director of payments intelligence at J.D. Power. "And that has played out in terms of creating some lower satisfaction in the different areas of consumer experience with their credit card."
The 2024 Canada Credit Card Satisfaction Study also notes that consumers are cashing in rewards points for groceries and essentials instead of travel and entertainment.
"Many consumers might be spending less on discretionary items like travel," Cabell said, "and really concentrating more on trying to manage their monthly budgets."
While Canadians struggling to pay bills are carrying more credit card debt, the average monthly credit card spend is falling. The average credit card holder in Canada is spending $1,342 a month, down from $1,618 in 2023.
"Canada's slow-growth economic environment is starting to take its toll on credit card customers," Cabell said, "and card usage has started to fundamentally change as a result."
When it comes to credit card satisfaction, Tangerine Bank ranks first, followed by American Express and PC Financial, the report notes.
Popular credit cards include the Canadian Tire Triangle World Elite Mastercard, Tangerine Money-Back Credit Card, and American Express Cobalt Card.
Financially healthy consumers tend to be more satisfied with their credit cards than the financially unhealthy ones, Cabell said.
He defines "financially unhealthy" consumers as those who are unable to make monthly payments, or missing them altogether.
"Cardholders who are financially unhealthy tend to struggle more with higher interest rates," Cabell said. "They are incurring service charges, maybe late payments and obviously paying interest. And they may also be struggling with credit limit issues as well."
On the other hand, Cabell said, financially healthy customers tend to pay their statements in full and optimize rewards programs.
The trend varies between provinces.
Quebecers, for example, tend to be less affluent and spend less, said Cabell, but are more financially healthy on average, with higher credit scores and less credit card debt.
"They believe that the (credit) card is healthy thing," he added. "That's an interesting - and very positive - usage pattern for that part of the Canadian population."
CAPTION: Some57 per cent of credit card customers are now "financially unhealthy," up from 52 per cent in 2023, according to a study by J.D. Power.

Document 373 of 500

Doug Ford's slap at Jagmeet Singh reveals his indifference to his own MPPs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-slap-at-jagmeet-singh-reveals-his/docview/3103431227/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+slap+at+Jagmeet+Singh+reveals+his+indifference+to+his+own+MPPs&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Greed is the deadliest of the seven deadly sins - so distasteful it distorts our thinking.
So when Doug Ford casually lashed out at Jagmeet Singh the other day, accusing him of greedily lusting after a parliamentary pension, it was a low blow.
Not because it wounded the federal NDP leader. Perched in Ottawa, Singh is probably impervious to the premier's pugnacity.
No, Ford's words sounded a death knell for the secret hopes of his long suffering fellow Tories at Queen's Park who quietly worry about money. Unlike the premier, who was born into wealth and inherited a job for life at the family firm before running for politics, backbenchers from all parties are mindful of their financial insecurities.
Little wonder Ford likes to boast that he's not in it for the money. He's been swimming in it from birth.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension," the premier proclaimed with righteous wrong-headedness this week.
"Let's face it, he wants his pension," Ford said dismissively of Singh, when asked if the NDP might force an early federal election. "There's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money" by holding out until they qualify for generous pensions.
Allow me to push back against Ford's penchant for pressing our buttons about money-grubbing, greedy politicos. Let me remind the premier that not everyone inherits a motherlode from his father.
I've watched politicians up close since I was a cub reporter decades ago. Based on my own unscientific survey, I can tell you that people don't go into public office to get rich.
There are far more lucrative and long-lasting career paths to choose from. Most are motivated by public service or personal ego - or both.
The base salary of an MPP is $116,550 a year, roughly comparable to what many school principals can earn. That salary has been frozen since 2008, after the last Liberal government opted for symbolic sacrifice (Ford's Tories have perpetuated the cold war on salaries ever since).
Know anyone else who's been stuck at zero for 16 years? Adjusted for inflation, that $116,550 is worth the equivalent of $83,400 in 2008 dollars, according to the Bank of Canada - in effect a massive pay cut.
It's not just pay but pensions that are much misunderstood. PC premier Mike Harris abolished provincial pensions after taking power in 1995, leaving MPPs with little more than a glorified defined contribution savings plan ever since.
Ford has resisted appeals from his own caucus colleagues to bring back a decent pension plan. Which means experienced politicians can spend their prime earning years fighting for improved defined pension benefits for their constituents, only to find themselves without a serious pension of their own when they retire.
Or are retired by voters.
That's a major argument for better salaries. Politicians live perilous lives, prone to defeat at any time through no fault of their own if they get caught in a province-wide downturn due to their leader's sagging performance.
They can emerge without a job waiting, without a pension to speak of, without a family firm to return to. Ultimately, that would leave the legislature with only rich folks like Ford who don't need the money - but are too close to the moneyed classes, and too far removed from people in need.
At one level, Ford gets it. Responding earlier this month to the sudden resignation of his popular energy minister, Todd Smith, the premier publicly sympathized with the need to secure his financial future.
"If there's other opportunities to make three, four, five times as much money, take care of his family, God bless him," Ford opined.
That's it? If you want to be well paid, leave public life?
In fairness, cabinet ministers make considerably more than backbenchers - nearly $166,000 a year. But Smith spent most of his 13 years at Queen's Park out of power and out of pocket, a backbencher with a frozen salary.
All that said, Ford's Tories quietly take care of their own (like Liberals before them) by boosting the salaries of almost all government MPPs, either through appointments as committee chairs or parliamentary assistants. That can be worth as much as $16,700 more a year.
By contrast, the pay on Parliament Hill is far more generous: MPs now earn $203,100 annually. Far from frozen, their salaries are automatically indexed to inflation, which takes the perennial politics out of the equation.
Previously, MPP salaries were supposed to be pegged at 75 per cent of what their federal counterparts earned; now, it's barely 57 per cent. That growing gap may partly explain why two New Democrat MPPs - Monique Taylor and Joel Harden - have announced they want to leave Queen's Park for sunnier climes and wealthier times on Parliament Hill.
Those are the numbers, yet Ford keeps feeding the public perception that MPPs are overpaid. Bad enough that we are a society of envy; we need not stereotype our leaders as greedy.
Public service requires an oath of office. It should not demand a vow of poverty on pensions.
CAPTION: Todd Smith, then Ontario's energy minister, attends an announcement with Premier Doug Ford in Belleville, Ont. on March 1, 2024.

Subject: Pensions; Family owned businesses; Politics; Politicians; Wages & salaries

Business indexing term: Subject: Pensions Family owned businesses Wages & salaries

People: Singh, Jagmeet

Title: Doug Ford's slap at Jagmeet Singh reveals his indifference to his own MPPs

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 12, 2024

Section: politics/political-opinion

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada

Source type: Blog, Podcast, or Website

Language of publication: English

Document type: News

ProQuest document ID: 3103431227

Document URL: https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-slap-at-jagmeet-singh-reveals-his/docview/3103431227/se-2?accountid=15090

Ford shows indifference to his own MPPs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-shows-indifference-his-own-mpps/docview/3103127784/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+shows+indifference+to+his+own+MPPs&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Greed is the deadliest of the seven deadly sins - so distasteful it distorts our thinking.

Document 375 of 500

'the system failed her': Ford open to broader examination of what went wrong in case of child found dead in dumpster

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/system-failed-her/docview/3103127741/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27the+system+failed+her%27%3A+Ford+open+to+broader+examination+of+what+went+wrong+in+case+of+child+found+dead+in+dumpster&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's child protection system "failed" a four-year-old girl named Neveah whose remains were found in a Rosedale dumpster about a year after her mysterious death, Premier Doug Ford acknowledges.
While it's up to Ontario's chief coroner to call an inquest into the case - as child welfare advocates have been urging - Ford said he is open to a broader examination of what went wrong after Neveah was returned to her mother by a court order following time in the care of Children's Aid.
"We've got to make sure it never happens again, and no matter if it takes a wider scope to look at it, we need to do everything we can," the premier told reporters Wednesday in Vaughan, where he announced $26 million for new facilities at LiUNA 183's skilled trades training campus.
"I believe the system failed her," Ford added. "It's an absolute tragedy."
Although Neveah's remains were found in 2022, a recent Star investigation that uncovered new details about her death has raised troubling questions about how she died in 2021 and how a child who had been under protection could end up dead in a dumpster - with no one looking for her in the intervening months or held responsible for her death.
"It's heartbreaking," said Ford.
As the Star's Wendy Gillis and Jennifer Pagliaro reported last month, there was no missing person's report for Neveah, whose name was variation of "heaven" spelled backwards. Her last name is subject to a court-ordered publication ban to prevent identifying members of her family.
Once her decomposed remains were found wrapped in blankets by a contractor in a posh neighbourhood, Toronto police began efforts to discover her identity, eventually relying on advanced DNA testing and a lucky tip.
In a rare public court record from a Child, Youth and Family Services Act hearing held in June to consider the future of Neveah's younger siblings, it was revealed her mother, who has an extensive history with child protection agencies and substance abuse challenges, had likely hidden the pregnancy and Neveah had limited prenatal care.
Toxicology reports at her birth revealed marijuana, prompting the York Region Children's Aid Society to obtain a protection order that placed the newborn into the care of a foster family at just five days old. The court records show the mother's six children, at various points in their lives, have been taken into care by child protective services in York and Peel regions and Toronto.
While with her foster parents, Neveah was diagnosed with developmental delays and deemed non-verbal at the age of two, and was diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder before her third birthday.
After Neveah's mother gave birth to a boy around her second birthday, he was placed in the care of the same foster family at the age of five months over concerns about the mother's alcohol and drug use.
Then in March 2020 - as the world was locking down for the COVID-19 pandemic - came a re-evaluation of their protection status that resulted in Neveah and her younger brother placed back in the care of their mother, but under supervision of child welfare authorities.
Four months after that, the York Region Children's Aid Society flagged concerns the mother had broken the supervision agreement by moving to a new residence in Toronto. The case was transferred to the Toronto Children's Aid Society. Six months later, the mother and Toronto CAS requested the courts end the supervision order for Neveah and her younger brother, a sign concerns about the children's care had eased. They had been registered to begin attending a downtown daycare centre.
The supervision order was ended on March 31, 2021, but the two children never showed up for daycare as scheduled the next day - or at all. Neveah vanished in June.
Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child advocate from 2008 to 2019, when the position was eliminated by the Ford government, told the Star last month there are many unanswered questions as to what went wrong and how Neveah fell through the cracks of the child protection system.
"We must learn about where those errors in judgment lay and where the mistakes happened."
New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor (Hamilton Mountain) said the death of any child who has been in care should automatically trigger a coroner's inquest, and Neveah's case screams for one.
"It's unacceptable that nobody knew this child was missing," said Taylor, her party's longtime children's services "There's no 'I'm sorrys,' there's no 'we made a mistake.' It's completely unacceptable."
Ontario's chief coroner, Dirk Huyer, recently told the Star it's too soon to call an inquest into Neveah's death because a police investigation into the case is continuing.
A coroner's inquest is a quasi-judicial hearing that probes the circumstances of a death, determines how it happened and presents recommendations to prevent similar tragedies but does not assign blame.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 376 of 500

Credit card holders feeling pinch: Report finds more Canadians cashing in reward points for essentials over travel, entertainment

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/credit-card-holders-feeling-pinch/docview/3103127498/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Credit+card+holders+feeling+pinch%3A+Report+finds+more+Canadians+cashing+in+reward+points+for+essentials+over+travel%2C+entertainment&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadian credit card holders' financial health is falling, with credit card interest rates and revolving debt on the rise, according to a new report.
Some 57 per cent of credit card customers are now "financially unhealthy," up from 52 per cent in 2023, with 36 per cent of customers now carrying revolving credit card debt, up from 34 per cent a year ago, according to a study by J.D. Power.
"Essentially, we have seen a decline in financial health and an increase in financial pressures in consumers over the last year, which is a continuation of some trends that we saw in 2023," said John Cabell, managing director of payments intelligence at J.D. Power. "And that has played out in terms of creating some lower satisfaction in the different areas of consumer experience with their credit card."
The 2024 Canada Credit Card Satisfaction Study also notes that consumers are cashing in rewards points for groceries and essentials instead of travel and entertainment.
"Many consumers might be spending less on discretionary items like travel," Cabell said, "and really concentrating more on trying to manage their monthly budgets."
While Canadians struggling to pay bills are carrying more credit card debt, the average monthly credit card spend is falling. The average credit card holder in Canada is spending $1,342 a month, down from $1,618 in 2023.
"Canada's slow-growth economic environment is starting to take its toll on credit card customers," Cabell said, "and card usage has started to fundamentally change as a result."
When it comes to credit card satisfaction, Tangerine Bank ranks first, followed by American Express and PC Financial, the report notes.
Financially healthy consumers tend to be more satisfied with their credit cards than the financially unhealthy ones, Cabell said.
He defines "financially unhealthy" consumers as those who are unable to make monthly payments, or missing them altogether.
"Cardholders who are financially unhealthy tend to struggle more with higher interest rates," Cabell said. "They are incurring service charges, maybe late payments and obviously paying interest. And they may also be struggling with credit limit issues as well."
On the other hand, Cabell said, financially healthy customers tend to pay their statements in full and optimize rewards programs.
The trend varies between provinces.
Quebecers, for example, tend to be less affluent and spend less, said Cabell, but are more financially healthy on average, with higher credit scores and less credit card debt.
"They believe that the (credit) card is healthy thing," he added. "That's an interesting - and very positive - usage pattern for that part of the Canadian population."
CREDIT: Marisa Coulton Toronto Star

Subject: Consumers; Interest rates; Revolving credit; Credit cards

Document 377 of 500

TIFF won't pull controversial 'Russians At War' documentary, despite protests

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/tiff-wont-pull-controversial-russians-at-war/docview/3103602976/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TIFF+won%27t+pull+controversial+%27Russians+At+War%27+documentary%2C+despite+protests&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Toronto International Film Festival said Wednesday that it won't pull "Russians At War," a documentary about Russian soldiers fighting in Ukraine, from its schedule, despite protests from Ukrainian officials and the Ukrainian-Canadian community.
"This documentary is an official Canada-France co-production with funding from several Canadian agencies, at both the federal and provincial level," TIFF said in a statement.
"Our understanding is that it was made without the knowledge or participation of any Russian government agencies. In our view, in no way should this film be considered Russian propaganda."
The statement also expressed sympathy for the Ukrainian people, and the suffering resulting from Russia's invasion.
"As we engage with the art made at this politically charged time, we are guided by the democratic values of freedom of conscience, opinion, expression and peaceful assembly as protected in the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for all Canadians including Canadian artists," the statement read.
In response to TIFF's statement, Yulia Kovaliv, Ukraine's ambassador to Canada, expressed her disappointment in a post on X.
"Russian propaganda is not the same as freedom of speech, Russian war crimes have been committed and well documented by international investigators, the whole world seen them," she wrote. "By showing this film you are whitewashing killer and rapers."
The Ukrainian Canadian Congress (UCC), a non-profit umbrella organization that represents Canada's Ukrainian community, also weighed in on X. It called TIFF's statement "insulting and appalling."
"Whitewashing Russian propaganda at a time when Russia is committing war crimes against Ukrainians," the UCC wrote.
The TIFF statement arrived a day after TVO withdrew its support for TIFF, following protests from the Ukrainian community.
"TVO's Board of Directors has decided to respect the feedback we have received, and TVO will no longer be supporting or airing 'Russians at War,'" a press release posted to the public broadcaster's website on Tuesday stated.
"TVO will be reviewing the process by which this project was funded and our brand leveraged."
Meanwhile, the Documentary Organization of Canada (DOC), which represents the collective voice of Canada's independent documentary creators, expressed alarm at TVO's "unilateral decision" to withdraw support for "Russians at War."
"This project, a Canadian-French co-production, was developed through a multi-year collaborative effort involving the filmmakers and TVO's Documentary team," read a statement released on Wednesday. "TVO's team has traditionally operated with editorial independence, fostering essential collaboration with independent filmmakers through established journalistic processes of review and discussion." The statement went on: "TVO's decision risks setting a dangerous precedent and must be immediately reversed."
The documentary, which follows Russian soldiers on the front lines of the war in Ukraine, also sparked a protest on Tuesday at the Scotiabank Theatre in Toronto.
Speaking from the Liberals' caucus retreat, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland told reporters Tuesday that both diplomats and the Ukrainian-Canadian community have expressed "grave concerns" about the film "Russians at War" - concerns which she shares.
"We as a country have to be very, very clear that there can be no moral equivalency in our understanding of this conflict," she said. "It's not right for Canadian public money to be supporting the screening and production of a film like this."
A synopsis of the film on TIFF's website described it as a first-person documentary chronicling the lives of Russian soldiers as they grow increasingly disillusioned by the war. "They all come to realize that everything they heard about the war in Russian media is false," it read. "They begin to doubt their purpose - and fight only to survive."
The documentary, which premiered at the Venice Film Festival last week and is set to make its North American debut at TIFF on Friday, was produced in association with Ontario's TVO and British Columbia's Knowledge Network. It also received $340,000 in funding from the not-for-profit Canada Media Fund.
Ukraine's consul general in Toronto, Oleh Nikolenko, previously urged TIFF to pull the documentary from its schedule, accusing the film of absolving Russian soldiers of responsibility in the ongoing invasion.
"It is irresponsible to allow the Toronto International Film Festival, one of the most reputable world film stages, to be used to whitewash the responsibility of Russian soldiers committing war crimes in Ukraine during the ongoing Russian invasion," Nikolenko wrote in a letter to TIFF CEO Cameron Bailey dated Sep. 5.
He added that representatives from the Consulate General of Ukraine in Toronto, the Embassy of Ukraine in Ottawa and the Ukrainian Canadian Congress raised concerns with TIFF management, but did not find their response satisfactory.
TIFF did not immediately respond to the Star's requests for comment.
In response to the backlash, the film's Russian-Canadian director, Anastasia Trofimova, asserted in a written statement that the film is not propaganda, and that it was filmed without the permission of the Russian government.
"I want to be clear that this Canada-France co-production is an antiwar film made at great risk to all involved, myself especially," she said.
"I unequivocally believe that Russia's invasion of Ukraine is unjustified, illegal and acknowledge the validity of the International Criminal Court investigation of war crimes in Ukraine."
In producing the film, Trofimova followed a Russian unit through parts of Russian-occupied eastern Ukraine. Nikolenko said he believed the documentary wasn't filmed legally, as Trofimova did not receive permission to cross the border into Russian-occupied Ukraine.
By screening the film, TIFF is making itself complicit, he continued.
TVO initially stood by the film, responding to the controversy last week with a statement that the documentary "is at its core an anti-war film."
In Venice, Trofimova told reporters that she witnessed no war crimes, characterizing the soldiers she accompanied as: "Absolutely ordinary guys with families, with a sense of humour, with their own understanding of what's happening in this war."
Outside the Scotiabank Theatre on Tuesday afternoon, dozens of protesters gathered to protest the TIFF's decision to air the documentary.
For her part, Freeland noted that "This is a war where Russia is breaking international law and committing war crimes.
"There is very clearly good and evil in this war. Ukrainians are fighting for their sovereignty and for democracy around the world."
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland is denouncing the use of public funds for a documentary about Russian soldiers. On the same day, a crowd of people protested the film's screening at the Toronto International Film Festival. Freeland says she shares the "grave concerns" raised about "Russians at War." (Sept. 10, 2024 / The Canadian Press)People protest outside of Scotiabank Theatre about the documentary "Russians at War," the press screening of which was playing at the Toronto International Film Festival on Tuesday. Public screenings start Friday.

Document 378 of 500

Doug Ford says child protection system 'failed' girl whose body was found in Toronto dumpster

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-child-protection-system-failed/docview/3103602938/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+child+protection+system+%27failed%27+girl+whose+body+was+found+in+Toronto+dumpster&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's child protection system "failed" a four-year-old girl named Neveah whose remains were found in a Rosedale dumpster about a year after her mysterious death, Premier Doug Ford acknowledges.
While it's up to Ontario's chief coroner to call an inquest into the case - as child welfare advocates have been urging - Ford said he is open to a broader examination of what went wrong after Neveah was returned to her mother by a court order following time in the care of Children's Aid.
"We've got to make sure it never happens again, and no matter if it takes a wider scope to look at it, we need to do everything we can," the premier told reporters Wednesday in Vaughan, where he announced $26 million for new facilities at LiUNA 183's skilled trades training campus.
"I believe the system failed her," Ford added. "It's an absolute tragedy."
Although Neveah's remains were found in 2022, a recent Star investigation that uncovered new details about her death has raised troubling questions about how she died in 2021 and how a child who had been under protection could end up dead in a dumpster - with no one looking for her in the intervening months or held responsible for her death.
"It's heartbreaking," said Ford.
As the Star's Wendy Gillis and Jennifer Pagliaro reported last month, there was no missing person's report for Neveah, whose name was variation of "heaven" spelled backwards. Her last name is subject to a court-ordered publication ban to prevent identifying members of her family.
Once her decomposed remains were found wrapped in blankets by a contractor in a posh neighbourhood, Toronto police began efforts to discover her identity, eventually relying on advanced DNA testing and a lucky tip.
In a rare public court record from a Child, Youth and Family Services Act hearing held in June to consider the future of Neveah's younger siblings, it was revealed her mother, who has an extensive history with child protection agencies and substance abuse challenges, had likely hidden the pregnancy and Neveah had limited prenatal care.
Toxicology reports at her birth revealed marijuana, prompting the York Region Children's Aid Society to obtain a protection order that placed the newborn into the care of a foster family at just five days old. The court records show the mother's six children, at various points in their lives, have been taken into care by child protective services in York and Peel regions and Toronto.
While with her foster parents, Neveah was diagnosed with developmental delays and deemed non-verbal at the age of two and was diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder before her third birthday.
After Neveah's mother gave birth to boy around her second birthday, he was placed in the care of the same foster family at the age of five months over concerns about the mother's alcohol and drug use.
Then in March 2020 - as the world was locking down for the COVID-19 pandemic - came a re-evaluation of their protection status that resulted in Neveah and her younger brother placed back in the care of their mother but under supervision of child welfare authorities.
Four months after that, the York Region Children's Aid Society flagged concerns the mother had broken the supervision agreement by moving to a new residence in Toronto. The case was transferred to the Toronto Children's Aid Society. Six months later, the mother and Toronto CAS requested the courts end the supervision order for Neveah and her younger brother, a sign concerns about the children's care had eased. They had been registered to begin attending a downtown daycare centre.
The supervision order was ended on March 31, 2021 but the two children never showed up for daycare as scheduled the next day - or at all. Neveah vanished in June.
Irwin Elman, who served as Ontario's child advocate from 2008 to 2019, when the position was eliminated by the Ford government, told the Star last month there are many unanswered questions as to what went wrong and how Neveah fell through the cracks of the child protection system.
"We must learn about where those errors in judgment lay and where the mistakes happened."
New Democrat MPP Monique Taylor (Hamilton Mountain) said the death of any child who has been in care should automatically trigger a coroner's inquest, and Neveah's case screams for one.
"It's unacceptable that nobody knew this child was missing," said Taylor, her party's longtime children's services critic. "There's no 'I'm sorrys,' there's no 'we made a mistake.' It's completely unacceptable."
Ontario's chief coroner, Dirk Huyer, recently told the Star it's too soon to call an inquest into Neveah's death because a police investigation into the case is continuing.
A coroner's inquest is a quasi-judicial hearing that probes the circumstances of a death, determines how it happened and presents recommendations to prevent similar tragedies but does not assign blame.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends the US-Canada Summit in Toronto on June 11, 2024.

Document 379 of 500

There are reasons voters feel tired of Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/there-are-reasons-voters-feel-tired-trudeau/docview/3102859036/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There+are+reasons+voters+feel+tired+of+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mixed feelings on Trudeau
Tired of Trudeau is not a good reason to vote him out, Sept. 8
Contributor Mark Bulgutch raises some interesting points, but his conclusions and analogy are in error. Trying something different for the sake of change is exactly what Canadians did when they first elected Justin Trudeau as leader of the federal Liberals. It was a gamble as Trudeau had minimal experience as a leader in the political arena and his previous career experience was weak (the exact same criticism levelled at Stephen Harper and Pierre Poilievre). Now many Canadians are considering taking a chance on Poilievre. How is it then that taking a gamble on Poilievre is any different than when we took a gamble on Trudeau?
The tired analogy misses the point about why Canadians are tired of Trudeau. Consider the number of times Trudeau has not been honest or transparent to Canadians, the numerous scandals, and abuses of the public purse. Thus, it is quite reasonable for people to grow tired of him. Even Trudeau believes this. On Oct. 17, 2013, he tweeted: "It's hard not to feel disappointed in your government when every day there is a new scandal." True words spoken by Trudeau and words he should heed. Trudeau has been given every opportunity to be the leader Canada needs and deserves.
Chris Turner, Aurora
Out of touch Justin Trudeau mistakenly thinks Canadians are experiencing mere discomfort when in reality they are really suffering. Whatever sunny utopia Trudeau thinks exists in Canada was burned to the ground long ago by Liberal policies and debt that overburdened the immigration system, destroyed the economy and has left many Canadians jobless, penniless and homeless. Trudeau epitomizes everything wrong with Canada today. The sooner the kid with a silver spoon in his mouth is gone the better!
Chris Robertson, Stony Plain, Alta.
Contributor Mark Bulgutch tells us that a majority of people with negative views of Justin Trudeau are tired of him. Perhaps they are just as sick of the grade school level attacks on everything Trudeau posted on social media daily by so called influencers and their sheeplike followers. Tell a lie or trash someone enough times on these platforms and eventually some people will start believing and fall in line, usually reposting the ignorance. As the polls indicate, it seems to be working. The problem is the knee jerk reaction of voters to vote for the people and party that create this trash without even asking what their plan is for our country. I certainly wouldn't vote for a party or movement that is willing to employ these types of ignorant attacks on their fellow citizens. I'll vote for the party that is trying to bring us into the electric future.
Gord Deane, Mississauga
One reason to vote for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the Liberals is: "better the devil you know than the devil you don't know."
Raymond Peringer, Toronto
As a freethinking Canadian, I wonder how I am being influenced by the chatter of the right who have painted a picture of disastrous proportions, which began with the "Trudeau must go" chant from so-called "Freedom Convoy" participants. As I said to a CBC commentator the other day, "say something long enough, loud enough and often enough, it will become a truism." Trudeau's demise has little to do with his capacity and more to do with everyone jumping onto the "Trudeau must go" or "F**k Trudeau" bandwagons. How many share the message but aren't educated on the reasons for him to go? How quickly we forget about federal Liberal party successes in the last nine years, including the progress in gender equity, positive steps forward in our Indigenous relationships, affordable child care, Canadian dental care plan, a school food program, Pharmacare and leading us through the tumultuous years of COVID-19.
Matthew Marosszeky, Aurora, Ont.
Toronto Sceptres?
PWHL Toronto finally reveals its name. And it's not what you might have expected, Sept. 9
I don't think much of this PWHL name. I cannot feel any emotional connection to a dead, wooden and metal symbol of power and authority. Where is the human warmth and bonding attraction in that? A good chance to say something spirited, meaningful and appealing about Toronto or Ontario has been botched. May the name still be changed to a better one? I hope so!
Norm Ferguson, Richmond Hill, Ont.
It is important for Toronto citizens to get behind their new PWHL team, the Sceptres, but the logo seems a little unfortunate. To me it falls short of suggesting hockey, and instead is suggestive of something more medical. Go Dermatologists!
Joe Wright, Toronto
Consider impacts of logging
Emissions from last year's Canadian wildfires more than most countries: study, Aug. 29
Greenhouse gas emissions from wildfires are indeed massive and dangerous, and fires will only get worse without action to rein in human-caused emissions. Currently, however, the federal government's accounting means we're missing another major forest-based source of climate-altering emissions: logging. Specifically, Canada doesn't count emissions from large wildfires because it deems them "natural," but it does take credit for a massive amount of carbon dioxide - 129 million tonnes (mt) in 2022 - absorbed by regrowing trees after wildfires, even though this regrowth is just as natural as the fires that preceded it. This one-sided accounting hides the true carbon footprint of industrial logging, which, as a recent report shows, is the third highest emitting sector in Canada after oil and gas, and transportation. To fully address its role in driving climate change-caused wildfires, the government should start transparently and accurately reporting the climate impact of logging, and put in place policies to reduce it.
Michael Polanyi, Policy and Campaign Manager, Nature Canada, Ottawa
Motorcyclists are affected by gridlock
I know one group of drivers who don't mind gridlock at all ... motorcyclists! Whenever I'm in gridlock, I grudgingly watch motorcyclists merrily weaving between cars and barely affected at all by city traffic. Is it dangerous? Of course! Is it ever addressed? Never! I'm thinking of buying a motorcycle to ensure that I get to work on time.
John Cowles, Oakville

Document 380 of 500

I toured Doug Ford's $225 million beer utopia

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-toured-doug-fords-225-million-beer-utopia/docview/3102858983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+toured+Doug+Ford%27s+%24225+million+beer+utopia&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a Saturday night at Stanley's Variety, an unassuming bodega wedged between Victorian row houses on Gerrard Street East, near Jarvis. It's 9:30 pm, but the store is devoid of customers, save for a taxi driver buying lottery tickets. Signs in the window advertise Bitcoin, Lotto Max, fresh coffee.
Near the store's entrance is the only thing marking this night as different from any other, a small fridge stocked with beer; cans, bottles, mostly from Molson Coors. The clerk tells me he hasn't sold any yet. Clerks at two other variety stores on Jarvis tell me they're still waiting on full shipments - presumably from the LCBO - before selling to the public. "When do you think it might get here?" I ask one of them. "I don't know," he says, waving his hand lazily from behind the counter. "Probably later."
Last week, new provincial regulations kicked in allowing more than 4,000 convenience and grocery stores across Ontario to sell beer, wine, cider and pre-mixed alcoholic drinks. It hasn't been a politically smooth transition. Doug Ford's government is spending hundreds of millions of dollars to make it not just happen, but happen this year, before an existing contract with the Beer Store runs out at the end of 2025.
I wanted to understand the urgency, to get sense for all the pent-up demand driving this sudden change. So last weekend, I spent two nights touring convenience stores across Toronto to check out the stock and quiz their clerks. What did I find? Not much.
After wandering around corner stores downtown, I took the streetcar to Humewood, a mostly residential neighbourhood centered around Cedarvale Park. There, it was a similar story. One variety store was waiting on a shipment, although the clerk directed me to a handful of six-packs on a shelf at the back of the store beside the toilet paper. A variety store across the street had assembled a DIY beer cabinet in one of the aisles with plywood framing and two padlocks. But that clerk too, told me sales have been minimal.
The only place I encountered that actually seemed to be moving product was a 7-11 at College and Spadina, on the border of the University of Toronto campus. Within 10 minutes of my arrival, around 11:00 pm, I saw three groups of student-age customers shlepping six-packs of Coors Lite that a clerk had plucked from inside a locked fridge between the cat food and chips aisles. The clerk told me that, so far, booze sales had been unremarkable during the daytime but were ramping up somewhat after the LCBOs close. During the time I was there, it still seemed like most customers were making conventional late-night purchases: Slurpees, Twizzlers, two-litre bottles of Coke.
It is, of course, early days for the rollout. Maybe in six months things will be different: Ford would have us believe that a sizeable chunk of Ontario's population has been clamouring for the option to purchase alcohol at their local corner store at all hours of the day, perhaps while picking up cigarettes and some peanut butter. But browsing the dusty aisles of the city's variety stores, pacing past three cases of Molson wedged in a back fridge between cartons of milk, it was hard for me to imagine this taxpayer-funded, $225 million (at least) rollout was worth it for the small business "growth opportunities" it purports to support.
Supposedly, one of the stipulations of this more permissive licensing is that at least 20 per cent of the beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink beverages on display in grocery and convenience stores must be produced by craft producers. But in my - admittedly limited - tour of various variety stores, I didn't see any craft beer. The stock I saw was dominated by Molson Coors products. Maybe, as my pal on Jarvis Street claims, the craft beer is coming "later."
Nothing I found on my after-dark variety store tour helped me understand why this had to happen now, why the government couldn't have just pushed this transition down the road a year and saved the public a heap of cash.Ford says it's about supporting local consumers and small businesses. But much like the Coors Lite next to the toilet paper, I'm not buying it.
For now, it looks like the government spent all that cash just to make sure some university students can get a six-pack after dark.
CAPTION: A file photo of a mostly empty grocery store beer shelf in Toronto from July 2024.More than 4,200 convenience stores in Ontario were allowed to sell beer and wine starting last Thursday morning.Lana Hall is a Toronto-based journalist who writes about urban affairs.
CREDIT: Lana Hall Contributor

Port Lands gas plant emissions seven times too high for new development at mouth of Don River

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/port-lands-gas-plant-emissions-seven-times-too/docview/3102858974/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Port+Lands+gas+plant+emissions+seven+times+too+high+for+new+development+at+mouth+of+Don+River&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Levels of toxic emissions from the Port Lands gas plant are so high they could imperil the construction of tall residential buildings along the eastern waterfront - land that has been remediated and flood-protected at public expense to prepare for new housing.
According to an air quality assessment report prepared for the developer of towers on Villiers Island, emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx) from the plant's smokestacks are seven times higher than the legal limit at the 160-metre height of the tallest proposed building.
This means that either the gas plant would have to drastically reduce its operations or the towers would have to be constructed without balconies or windows that open above 118 m, or about 35 stories, the report concluded. (Below that height, the report found NOx levels would be within the provincial guidelines.)
The power plant is Toronto's biggest source of both greenhouse gas emissions and NOx. The assessment, prepared for a proposed development at 309 Cherry St., some 1.7 km away, reveals how air pollution could impact new housing throughout the East Harbour and Port Lands areas, which have been earmarked for tens of thousands of units in the coming years.
The former industrial area along the city's eastern waterfront is one of the largest swaths of undeveloped land in the city and has undergone $1.4 billion in publicly financed flood protection work.
"This report shows that the vision for the future of Toronto's waterfront is fundamentally at odds with the long-term operation of the Port Lands gas plant," said Bryan Purcell, vice-president of policy and programs at The Atmospheric Fund, a climate agency that advocates for low carbon solutions in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton area.
"There's simply no way to reduce emissions at the plant to allow the construction of tall residential towers in the vicinity," he added.
Atura, a subsidiary of publicly-owned Ontario Power Generation, owns and operates the plant. In response to questions from the Star, spokesperson Darius Sokal wrote that the plant operates within all public health and environmental emissions limits.
"We regularly work with our neighbours - current and future - related to compatibility with other nearby land uses," he added.
A Star investigation last year found the Port Lands gas plant operates more than 21 hours a day during the summer, sending carbon emissions into the atmosphere and pollutants harmful to human health into the local community.
Despite City Council twice voting against its expansion, the plant is embarking on upgrades that will increase its output by nine per cent. At the same time, the province has extended its operating contract from 2029 to 2034.
The proposed development on Cherry St. includes towers of 49, 39, 27 and 10 stories and the report analyzed the air quality at 160 meters - the height of the tallest building. It also looked at air quality at another proposed development in the East Harbour Lands, which is closer to the 75-meter-tall smokestacks at the Portlands Energy Centre (PEC). In both cases, it found NOx far exceeding the legal limit at heights above 35 stories.
"If the development at East Harbour Lands is constructed for residential use at the heights approved in the zoning, PEC would not remain in compliance with the air quality standards for NOx," stated the report. "PEC would need to reduce its NOx emission rate ... (by) approximately a factor of seven reduction to maintain compliance."
Castlepoint Numa, the developer that commissioned the report, and the report's authors, did not respond to a request for comment. Nor did Cadillac Fairview, the developer of East Harbour.
This summer, the city unveiled a re-naturalized mouth of the Don River, which was rerouted as part of a massive public infrastructure project to prevent flooding in and around Villiers Island, which is slated to host at least 15 towers and 9,000 housing units but could contain far more, activists say.
If tower heights are capped at 35 stories to allow the Port Lands gas plant to continue to operate, it would limit the amount of housing that could be built in the surrounding area and could imperil the economics of the developments, which rely on tall towers to be profitable.
The original air quality assessment completed for the Port Lands gas plant in 2003 said the pollution levels from the plant would not restrict future development in the area. But the report did not consider buildings over 20 stories tall.
"It is very clear that the operation of the Portlands Energy Centre could mean less housing will be built in the area near it or that the plant operation will have to be heavily restrained," said Peter Tabuns, the MPP who represents the area. "Those heights and the NOx are a really bad mix."
Exposure to NOx has been shown to cause lung and heart disease, asthma, Alzheimer's, cancer and birth complications; Health Canada estimates that there are more than 15,000 premature deaths per year due to air pollution.
"On one side of the issue is housing and clean air, on the other side is an irresponsible plan to expand gas burning for power production. The province's approach is wrong," Tabuns said.
Dr. Mili Roy, an assistant professor at U of T's faculty of medicine and the co-chair of the Canadian Association of Physicians for the Environment, stressed there is no "safe" level of NOx exposure.
Immediately after the election of Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government in 2018, Ontario halted construction of renewable energy, like wind and solar, and instead doubled down on natural gas plants to meet a growing demand for electricity.
This reversed the decline in carbon emissions from our electrical grid, which were reduced by more than 80 per cent by the previous Liberal government after it shut down all the coal plants in the province. Now, electricity emissions are on the rise as new gas plants are built and existing ones are used more often.
Climate experts have questioned the increasing use of gas to generate electricity, especially as renewable energy has plummeted in cost and is now considered the cheapest form of energy in history.
The air quality assessment introduces new questions.
"This report sharpens the conversation around the Port Lands gas plant, which has until now been about climate change and emissions. Now it's about billions of dollars in development and tens of thousands of units of housing," Purcell said.
As the health and climate impacts of relying on gas have become clear, local residents have started organizing against the Port Lands gas plant.
"Toronto can get all the energy it needs from renewables. So it's hard to understand why we're pursuing gas, especially with all the health implications," said David Smith, a longtime east-end resident and member of Toronto East Residents for Renewable Energy.
Smith is excited about the renewal of the Port Lands and has poured over the renderings of dozens of new condo towers planned for the area. He notes that none of them depict the smokestacks of the power plant next door.
"All these people want a nice place to live. But is it a nice place to live if you can't breathe the air?"
CAPTION: The Portlands Energy Centre, near the site of a series of high-profile developments near the mouth of the Don River.CI-CLIMATE-GASPLANTEMISSIONS9 TORONTO, ON - September 10 Wisps of greenish gasses are seen during the noon hour coming out of the large smoke stacks of the Portland's gas generating station in the Leslie and Unwin avenue area. The pollution levels from the portlands gas plant are 7x higher than the legal limit at heights above 35 stories, putting into risk future residential development across the eastern waterfront. September 10 2024 Richard Lautens/Toronto Star

Subject: Electricity; Height; Floods; Emissions; Carbon; Renewable resources; Air pollution; Energy; Outdoor air quality; Emission standards; Coal-fired power plants; Climate change; Industrial plant emissions

Document 382 of 500

No one asked for alcohol in corner stories, but they have been asking for our health care to be fixed

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/no-one-asked-alcohol-corner-stories-they-have/docview/3102858935/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+one+asked+for+alcohol+in+corner+stories%2C+but+they+have+been+asking+for+our+health+care+to+be+fixed&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Last Thursday night, I went to the Mississauga Hospital Emergency Department with my 11-year-old son. The place was packed. My son wasn't in a lot of pain but there were many other people who clearly were.
There was at least an hour wait before being triaged and even longer to see a doctor. Total anticipated wait time, according to the screen in the waiting room, was between 3 hours 27 minutes and 6 hours 37 minutes.
I wasn't surprised. I wasn't angry. I was just resigned to waiting. I've had similar experiences in the past. I've heard similar stories, if not far worse, of ER wait times. Same old story.
Needless to say, I had a lot of time to scroll social media. Did you know that you can now buy alcohol in convenience stores in Ontario? You probably knew this because this announcement has been made many times in the media. Last week it hit a fever pitch. Doug Ford's own X (Twitter) and LinkedIn pages have not one post about this but 4 in a row. My favourite:
"Attention Ontario, the wait is over! Starting today you can buy beer, cider, wine and ready to drink alcoholic beverages in convenience stores."
Minister of Health Sylvia Jones reposted a similar announcement on X (Twitter).
As each minute ticked by in the ER, I read through a seemingly endless number of congratulatory statements from our elected members of our provincial government. About how this increases choice for consumers. About how alcohol now being available in convenience stores is a big win for small business owners.
(Not included in these posts: convenience stores were going to be able to sell anyways in a few months; Mr. Ford just accelerated this in exchange for having to pay at least $225 million to breach a contract with the Beer Store, but I digress.)
Maybe it is a win for small business. I'm not sure. But I went from resignation, to mild annoyance, to being pissed off the more I read and the longer I waited.
Post after post after post about alcohol and choice and convenience - you'd think this was the most pressing political issue of our time, that people were holding protests demanding the right to buy booze at their local corner store at 7 a.m., bleary-eyed in their pyjamas.
Maybe they were. I haven't met any of those people. I have met people who don't have a family doctor. I have met people who have a similar story to mine of waiting for hours in a crowded emergency room. I met someone last Thursday who got so fed up with waiting she pulled the IV out of her arm herself and just walked out in disgust. My son had thoughts of doing the same when he saw her.
Ford has given Ontarians a handy map of all the new places where we can buy booze. Who needed this? Who asked for this? How about a map of family doctors in Ontario who are taking on new patients? Guess the wait isn't over for that one.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announces beer and wine will be available at corner stores during a media event at Pioneer On The Run Convenience Store in Toronto, on May 24. Beer and wine became available at corner stores after Labour Day and Ontario's long-awaited liberalization of booze sales in supermarkets and big-box outlets should be completed by Halloween -- thanks to a $225-million payout to the privately run Beer Store.
CREDIT: Angelique Moss Contributor
