Document 383 of 500

Who benefits most from the end of the Liberal-NDP alliance?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-benefits-most-end-liberal-ndp-alliance/docview/3102858829/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+benefits+most+from+the+end+of+the+Liberal-NDP+alliance%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The collapse of the Confidence and Supply Agreement (CASA) between the Liberals and NDP has undoubtedly changed the calculus for every major federal party. But it is the two major parties - the ones in government and the ones likely to be the next government - whose strategies must change the least.
Consider this new state of affairs from the perspective of the federal Liberals. It is probably not controversial to say that the Liberals are not having a great week. Not only have they lost the CASA with the NDP, but as first reported by the Toronto Star last week, they also lost the man intended to run their next re-election campaign.
Jeremy Broadhurst, the Star reported, chose to quit after concluding the party had no realistic chance to win. That is not good news. Nor will the Liberals be encouraged by what the Globe and Mail reported on Monday morning: a series of ministerial chiefs of staff are also signalling their intention to leave soon. None of this is encouraging if you are Justin Trudeau.
But none of it really alters his political reality. For the prime minister, the first order of business remains survival. The CASA somewhat simplified that for him, at least in theory. So long as the NDP was prepared to play ball, he had an easier path to the necessary votes in the House to keep his government in power. Without that guarantee, he's just going to have to bargain. Indeed, he's now able to bargain more freely. If he doesn't like what the NDP is demanding, he can always give the Bloc Québécois leader a call and see what deal can be made there.
But more on that in a minute.
Before we move to the smaller parties, let's look at how things have changed from the perspective of the Conservatives. The reality is, they haven't really changed at all. The top priorities for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservative Party of Canada remain exactly what they were last week: continue to hound the government, looking for and exploiting any weaknesses; continue to raise funds and keep the party at a high state of electoral readiness; continue to try and drive a wedge between the Liberals and the other opposition parties, since the Conservatives will need both of the other major opposition parties to bring the government down.
The end of the CASA has probably given heart to the Conservatives. It makes it at least possible that the NDP will vote Trudeau down. But the Conservatives must still make what is possible into something probable. What issues can pry the two progressive opposition parties away from the government, even in a scenario where that would almost guarantee a Conservative majority? Finding the answer to that is Poilievre's new top priority.
For the NDP, the Conservative machinations are going to be a real cause for concern. It has been obvious for a long time that the Conservatives are looking to gobble up as many of the NDP's working-class seats as possible. The Conservatives have a huge amount of money they can now spend on targeted ads in the NDP's working-class ridings, painting the party and its leader as the stooges of Trudeau.
Meanwhile, assuming the Liberals are still capable of doing anything other than reacting to bad news, there's a hell of an opportunity for them here, too. Poilievre will blast every NDP riding he wants to flip with messages about how "Sellout Singh" is keeping the widely disliked Trudeau in power. In the NDP's urban seats, the Liberals will be telling every progressive voter that Singh has made Pierre Poilievre the likely PM by pulling out of the CASA and only the Liberals can stop him.
That won't be fun. The New Democrats were struggling under the CASA, but they're going to struggle differently without it. They are going to get squeezed on both ends. We'll see if the party has both the money and, frankly, the leadership to fend off that kind of simultaneous attack.
This leaves us with the Bloc Québécois. This is about as good an opportunity as the Bloc has had in a long time: they can be Trudeau's parliamentary partner of last resort for as long as they so choose. If Trudeau or some successor finds themselves staring down a united Conservative and NDP opposition ahead of a confidence vote, why not aim for the stars when making demands?
If the Bloc can wring any concessions out of the government, that's great for them. If they can't, and polls show that the Bloc would continue to return a large group of MPs from Quebec - perhaps even enough to form the official opposition to a Conservative majority - hey, that's not the worst thing, either.
But it's not all upside for the Bloc. The threat is going to be Conservative pressure. The Conservatives have yet to show signs of a major breakthrough in Quebec, but some polls have offered hints that this could be possible. Besides, the Bloc must be worried that in the face of a large Conservative majority, some of Quebec's typically ruthlessly pragmatic voters might decide to pivot to the Conservatives to make sure their interests are represented inside a strong majority, and not outside of it in opposition.
For the Conservatives, targeting Quebec seats and voters is probably more important, therefore, than targeting NDP voters. It might be a harder hill for them to climb, but getting the Bloc onside with bringing down the Liberals before the Conservatives can really make inroads with Bloc voters is an option Yves-François Blanchet will not be able to ignore.
So yeah, a lot has changed. But the biggest decisions and problems probably now rest with the smaller opposition parties. And when you put all this together, part of what you're left with is simply the danger of chaos. Getting the parties election-ready - as in imminently ready - just became a much higher priority for every leader. Only time will tell if all this election preparation and political manoeuvring leaves any bandwidth left for policy-making and actual governing. And it might not be that much time at all.
CAPTION: Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau, left, and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, prepare for the start of the federal election English-language Leaders debate in Gatineau, Que., on Thursday, Sept. 9, 2021. The NDP has pulled out of a confidence and supply agreement with the Liberals.
CREDIT: Matt Gurney Contributor

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Opposition parties; Working class; Voters

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership Working class

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Who benefits most from the end of the Liberal-NDP alliance?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 11, 2024

Section: opinion/contributors

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: Toronto

Country of publication: Canada

Document 384 of 500

Carney tries to position himself as 'an outsider'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carney-tries-position-himself-as-outsider/docview/3102666060/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carney+tries+to+position+himself+as+%27an+outsider%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mark Carney wants you to know he's answering a call from his country, not overtly positioning himself for the Liberal leadership, or painting himself to be the Grits' saviour.
"I'm an outsider," the former Bank of Canada governor told journalists in Nanaimo, B.C., where Grit MPs are gathered for three days of caucus meetings. On Monday, Carney was announced by the Liberal party as the chair of its leader's task force on economic growth.
He is to travel across the country, meeting with stakeholders and writing up a plan to address near- and longer-term economic growth and productivity issues, with an eye to shaping the party's electoral platform. On Tuesday, he met with Liberal MPs for about 40 minutes, with most of the time spent answering their questions about the economy and what he planned to do.
For those wanting to know why one of the country's most impressive economic minds - he steered Canada through the global economic crisis, earned widespread praise as a prudent financial manager and was tapped to lead the Bank of England - was hitching his wagon to the struggling Liberals, Carney suggested he wasn't joining a partisan fight, but just answering the call of duty.
"I have a couple of simple rules. One, if the prime minister of Canada asked me to do something, I will do it," he told reporters. "Secondly, you know, I'm interested. I have some experience in these issues and I'm interested in helping our country to grow."
Asked if he had been asked to run for the Liberals in either of the Sept. 16 byelections, or if he planned to run for a seat in the next general election, Carney sidestepped.
"I understood the question, but my answer stands," he said. "I'm interested in doing something, not being something" - a line he repeated twice.
Carney also sidestepped when asked if there was daylight between his position on carbon pricing and that of the Grits. In the spring, "Carbon Tax Carney" as Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are trying to define him, told a Senate committee that carbon pricing has "served a purpose up until now. I think one can always look for better solutions."
Last year, however, Carney was one of Trudeau's fiercest critics after the prime minister announced a three-year pause on the carbon levy for Canadians using heating oil.
At the time, Carney said, Canadians were not struggling because of the carbon tax, but because of broad increases in energy prices, in food prices, the impact on wages and the lingering efforts of COVID, and he suggested he would have addressed affordability concerns differently.
"Being low carbon is going to be a determinant of whether we can create jobs, whether we can sell our products in the United States, in Europe, around the world," he said on Tuesday. "We have to help our companies become more competitive. There is a huge range of things that the federal government can do."
It wasn't immediately clear Tuesday what Carney had told Liberal MPs about carbon pricing. Procurement Minister Jean-Yves Duclos told the Star that Carney had been asked about it and "he still believes that if we put a price on pollution, which puts more money in the pockets of eight families out of 10, that's a great thing to do."
Outside of the Vancouver Island Conference Centre, however, a handful of protesters reminded the Liberals that, so far, they appear to have lost the communication battle when it comes to carbon pricing and cost of living increases.
They'll face another challenge on that front next spring when the carbon price is set to increase yet again.
Carney's carbon pricing stance isn't the only line of attack the Conservatives plan to hit the Liberals with.
While Carney sought to frame himself as "a catalyst" to help the government define a new course for economic growth, rather than an appointee designated to bolster the popularity of the Liberal party, so too did the Conservatives.
In a letter to the prime minister, Ontario Tory MP Michael Barrett told Trudeau he was deeply concerned that Carney had been appointed a Liberal party adviser, rather than an adviser to the government, in order to bypass conflict of interest rules.
"Ministerial advisers are reporting public office holders under the (law) and are legally required to disclose potential conflicts of interest, ethics screens, recusals and all controlled assets, liabilities and outside activities," Barrett wrote.
He noted Carney had done very well for himself and sits on several corporate boards, including of asset management companies overseeing trillions of dollars.
Interestingly, Barrett did not demand Trudeau cancel Carney's appointment, but instead that Carney's position be placed within the government structure - essentially, allowing the Conservatives to prod Carney's financial dealings and read his emails.
"This is a laughable stunt from the Conservative party," Liberal spokesperson Parker Lund told the Star.
"The Conservatives are just upset that Mark Carney supports the Liberal economic plan and not Pierre Poilievre's disastrous cuts that would damage the economy."
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Document 385 of 500

Should Ottawa brace for chaos?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/should-ottawa-brace-chaos/docview/3102666048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Should+Ottawa+brace+for+chaos%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The collapse of the Confidence and Supply Agreement (CASA) between the Liberals and NDP has undoubtedly changed the calculus for every major federal party. But it is the two major parties - the ones in government and the ones likely to be the next government - whose strategies must change the least.
Consider this new state of affairs from the perspective of the federal Liberals. It is probably not controversial to say that the Liberals are not having a great week. Not only have they lost the CASA with the NDP, but as first reported by the Star last week, they also lost the man intended to run their next re-election campaign.
Jeremy Broadhurst, the Star reported, chose to quit after concluding the party had no realistic chance to win. That is not good news. Nor will the Liberals be encouraged by what the Globe and Mail reported on Monday morning: a series of ministerial chiefs of staff are also signalling their intention to leave soon. None of this is encouraging if you are Justin Trudeau.
But none of it really alters his political reality. For the prime minister, the first order of business remains survival. The CASA somewhat simplified that for him, at least in theory. So long as the NDP was prepared to play ball, he had an easier path to the necessary votes in the House to keep his government in power.
Without that guarantee, he's just going to have to bargain. Indeed, he's now able to bargain more freely. If he doesn't like what the NDP is demanding, he can always give the Bloc Québécois leader a call and see what deal can be made there.
But more on that in a minute.
Before we move to the smaller parties, let's look at how things have changed from the perspective of the Conservatives. The reality is, they haven't really changed at all. The top priorities for Pierre Poilievre and the Conservative party of Canada remain exactly what they were last week: continue to hound the government, looking for and exploiting any weaknesses; continue to raise funds and keep the party at a high state of electoral readiness; continue to try and drive a wedge between the Liberals and the other opposition parties, since the Conservatives will need both of the other major opposition parties to bring the government down.
The end of the CASA has probably given heart to the Conservatives. It makes it at least possible that the NDP will vote Trudeau down. But the Conservatives must still make what is possible into something probable.
What issues can pry the two progressive opposition parties away from the government, even in a scenario where that would almost guarantee a Conservative majority? Finding the answer to that is Poilievre's new top priority.
For the NDP, the Conservative machinations are going to be a real cause for concern. It has been obvious for a long time that the Conservatives are looking to gobble up as many of the NDP's working-class seats as possible. The Conservatives have a huge amount of money they can now spend on targeted ads in the NDP's working-class ridings, painting the party and its leader as the stooges of Trudeau.
Meanwhile, assuming the Liberals are still capable of doing anything other than reacting to bad news, there's a hell of an opportunity for them here, too. Poilievre will blast every NDP riding he wants to flip with messages about how "Sellout Singh" is keeping the widely disliked Trudeau in power. In the NDP's urban seats, the Liberals will be telling every progressive voter that Singh has made Pierre Poilievre the likely PM by pulling out of the CASA and only the Liberals can stop him.
That won't be fun. The New Democrats were struggling under the CASA, but they're going to struggle differently without it. They are going to get squeezed on both ends. We'll see if the party has both the money and, frankly, the leadership to fend off that kind of simultaneous attack.
This leaves us with the Bloc Québécois. This is about as good an opportunity as the Bloc has had in a long time: they can be Trudeau's parliamentary partner of last resort for as long as they so choose. If Trudeau or some successor finds themselves staring down a united Conservative and NDP opposition ahead of a confidence vote, why not aim for the stars when making demands?
If the Bloc can wring any concessions out of the government, that's great for them. If they can't, and polls show that the Bloc would continue to return a large group of MPs from Quebec - perhaps even enough to form the official opposition to a Conservative majority - hey, that's not the worst thing, either.
But it's not all upside for the Bloc. The threat is going to be Conservative pressure. The Conservatives have yet to show signs of a major breakthrough in Quebec, but some polls have offered hints that this could be possible. Besides, the Bloc must be worried that in the face of a large Conservative majority, some of Quebec's typically ruthlessly pragmatic voters might decide to pivot to the Conservatives to make sure their interests are represented inside a strong majority, and not outside of it in opposition.
For the Conservatives, targeting Quebec seats and voters is probably more important, therefore, than targeting NDP voters. It might be a harder hill for them to climb, but getting the Bloc onside with bringing down the Liberals before the Conservatives can really make inroads with Bloc voters is an option Yves-François Blanchet will not be able to ignore.
So yeah, a lot has changed. But the biggest decisions and problems probably now rest with the smaller opposition parties. And when you put all this together, part of what you're left with is simply the danger of chaos. Getting the parties election-ready - as in imminently ready - just became a much higher priority for every leader.
Only time will tell if all this election preparation and political manoeuvring leaves any bandwidth left for policy-making and actual governing. And it might not be that much time at all.
Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. Reach him via email: matt@readtheline.ca

The end of the Confidence and Supply Agreement between Trudeau's Liberals and Singh's NDP has probably given heart to the Conservatives, Matt Gurney writes.DARRYL DYCKTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

Document 386 of 500

Singh fires back at Ford saying his priorities are out of order

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-fires-back-at-ford-saying-his-priorities/docview/3102666017/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+fires+back+at+Ford+saying+his+priorities+are+out+of+order&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's the booze talking.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh fired back at Doug Ford on Tuesday, accusing the premier of being "obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores" instead of more pressing challenges.
One day after Ford derided Singh for abruptly ending the two-year supply and confidence agreement to prop up Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority Liberals, the New Democrat responded.
"Doug Ford needs to worry about delivering health care. People in Ontario can't get the health care they need, they're struggling with the cost of living and he's obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores," the federal NDP leader said in Montreal.
"I don't know any person that I've spoken to when I tour Ontario, when I'm in the communities, that says that's their No. 1 pressing issue," said Singh, referring to the premier's booze liberalization scheme that took effect last week and will cost taxpayers between $225 million and $1 billion.
"Doug Ford should focus on those things instead of being stuck on Twitter," he said.
On Monday, the premier raised eyebrows after being asked whether the threat of an imminent federal election would force him to reconsider any plans for a potential early provincial vote next year.
"As far as I'm concerned, MP Singh is just blowing smoke," Ford said in Prince Edward County.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension and he was elected (in) ... 2019 and I'll be floored and I'll stand up here and apologize to all the greedy politicians if he calls the election early," the premier said, referring to the six-year threshold for federal MPs to qualify for a pension.
"But let's face it, he wants his pension. He's not going anywhere until ... Oct. 20, 2025 because there's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money. So my bet is, he's not doing anything. He's just putting on a big show like he has for the last number of years there."
Last Thursday, Singh - who has said an election could come at any time, though his party would not automatically vote against the Liberals - dismissed the pension charge as "a ludicrous suggestion."
"I'm obviously going to get re-elected in my riding (of Burnaby South), I'm not worried about that at all. This is a silly argument being brought about by Conservatives," he told Vassy Kapelos on CTV's "Power Play."
A snap federal vote could throw a wrinkle in Ford's plan to have an Ontario election one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
His Progressive Conservatives, who lead in every public-opinion poll, would prefer to face voters sooner rather than later.
That's because they're worried a Pierre Poilievre-led Conservative government might make cuts to transfer payments and to federal subsidies for electric-vehicle manufacturers in Ontario.
There's also concern at the potential for charges being laid in the ongoing RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Political tradition is another reason why Ford could go early - historically, Ontario voters prefer to have different parties in power in Ottawa and Queen's Park at the same time.
When Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they usually choose Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the New Democrats - tend to win provincially.
The phenomenon, known as the "Underhill balance theory," has held true for about 70 of the past 81 years.
While Ford has sparred with Trudeau in the past, he has worked closely with the prime minister since the COVID-19 pandemic began in 2020.
In the wake of that health emergency, the two leaders have focused on shared economic priorities like tens of billions of dollars in planned subsidies for EV manufacturers, such as Volkswagen, Stellantis and Honda.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh accused Premier Doug Ford Tuesday of being "obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores" instead of more pressing challenges like health care.Ryan RemiorzThe Canadian Press
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 387 of 500

Not much to see at Ford's $225-million beer utopia

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/not-much-see-at-fords-225-million-beer-utopia/docview/3102665922/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Not+much+to+see+at+Ford%27s+%24225-million+beer+utopia&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's a Saturday night at Stanley's Variety, an unassuming bodega wedged between Victorian row houses on Gerrard Street East, near Jarvis. It's 9:30 p.m., but the store is devoid of customers, save for a taxi driver buying lottery tickets. Signs in the window advertise Bitcoin, Lotto Max, fresh coffee.
Near the store's entrance is the only thing marking this night as different from any other, a small fridge stocked with beer; cans, bottles, mostly from Molson Coors. The clerk tells me he hasn't sold any yet. Clerks at two other variety stores on Jarvis tell me they're still waiting on full shipments - presumably from the LCBO - before selling to the public.
"When do you think it might get here?" I ask one of them.
"I don't know," he says, waving his hand lazily from behind the counter. "Probably later."
Last week, new provincial regulations kicked in allowing more than 4,000 convenience and grocery stores across Ontario to sell beer, wine, cider and pre-mixed alcoholic drinks. It hasn't been a politically smooth transition. Doug Ford's government is spending hundreds of millions of dollars to make it not just happen, but happen this year, before an existing contract with the Beer Store runs out at the end of 2025.
I wanted to understand the urgency, to get sense for all the pent-up demand driving this sudden change. So last weekend, I spent two nights touring convenience stores across Toronto to check out the stock and quiz their clerks.
What did I find? Not much.
After wandering around corner stores downtown, I took the streetcar to Humewood, a mostly residential neighbourhood centred around Cedarvale Park. There, it was a similar story. One variety store was waiting on a shipment, although the clerk directed me to a handful of six-packs on a shelf at the back of the store beside the toilet paper. A variety store across the street had assembled a DIY beer cabinet in one of the aisles with plywood framing and two padlocks. But that clerk too, told me sales have been minimal.
The only place I encountered that actually seemed to be moving product was a 7-Eleven at College and Spadina, on the border of the University of Toronto campus. Within 10 minutes of my arrival, around 11 p.m., I saw three groups of student-age customers schlepping six-packs of Coors Lite that a clerk had plucked from inside a locked fridge between the cat food and chips aisles. The clerk told me that, so far, booze sales had been unremarkable during the daytime, but were ramping up somewhat after the LCBOs close. During the time I was there, it still seemed like most customers were making conventional late-night purchases: Slurpees, Twizzlers, two-litre bottles of Coke.
It is, of course, early days for the rollout. Maybe in six months things will be different: Ford would have us believe that a sizable chunk of Ontario's population has been clamouring for the option to purchase alcohol at their local corner store at all hours of the day, perhaps while picking up cigarettes and some peanut butter. But browsing the dusty aisles of the city's variety stores, pacing past three cases of Molson wedged in a back fridge between cartons of milk, it was hard for me to imagine this taxpayer-funded, $225-million (at least) rollout was worth it for the small business "growth opportunities" it purports to support.
Supposedly, one of the stipulations of this more permissive licensing is that at least 20 per cent of the beer, cider, wine and ready-to-drink beverages on display in grocery and convenience stores must be produced by craft producers. But in my - admittedly limited - tour of various variety stores, I didn't see any craft beer. The stock I saw was dominated by Molson Coors products. Maybe, as my pal on Jarvis Street claims, the craft beer is coming "later."
Nothing I found on my after-dark variety store tour helped me understand why this had to happen now, why the government couldn't have just pushed this transition down the road a year and saved the public a heap of cash. Ford says it's about supporting local consumers and small businesses. But much like the Coors Lite next to the toilet paper, I'm not buying it.
For now, it looks like the government spent all that cash just to make sure some university students can get a six-pack after dark.
Lana Hall is a Toronto-based journalist who writes about urban affairs.

Full text: Mixed feelings on Trudeau
Tired of Trudeau is not a good reason to vote him out, Sept. 8
Contributor Mark Bulgutch raises some interesting points, but his conclusions and analogy are in error. Trying something different for the sake of change is exactly what Canadians did when they first elected Justin Trudeau as leader of the federal Liberals. It was a gamble as Trudeau had minimal experience as a leader in the political arena and his previous career experience was weak (the exact same criticism levelled at Stephen Harper and Pierre Poilievre). Now many Canadians are considering taking a chance on Poilievre. How is it then that taking a gamble on Poilievre is any different than when we took a gamble on Trudeau?
The tired analogy misses the point about why Canadians are tired of Trudeau. Consider the number of times Trudeau has not been honest or transparent to Canadians, the numerous scandals, and abuses of the public purse. Thus, it is quite reasonable for people to grow tired of him. Even Trudeau believes this. On Oct. 17, 2013, he tweeted: "It's hard not to feel disappointed in your government when every day there is a new scandal." True words spoken by Trudeau and words he should heed. Trudeau has been given every opportunity to be the leader Canada needs and deserves.
Chris Turner, Aurora
Out of touch Justin Trudeau mistakenly thinks Canadians are experiencing mere discomfort when in reality they are really suffering. Whatever sunny utopia Trudeau thinks exists in Canada was burned to the ground long ago by Liberal policies and debt that overburdened the immigration system, destroyed the economy and has left many Canadians jobless, penniless and homeless. Trudeau epitomizes everything wrong with Canada today. The sooner the kid with a silver spoon is gone the better!
Chris Robertson,
Stony Plain, Alta.
Contributor Mark Bulgutch tells us that a majority of people with negative views of Justin Trudeau are tired of him. Perhaps they are just as sick of the grade school-level attacks on everything Trudeau posted on social media daily by so-called influencers and their sheeplike followers. Tell a lie or trash someone enough times on these platforms and eventually some people will start believing and fall in line, usually reposting the ignorance. As the polls indicate, it seems to be working. The problem is the knee-jerk reaction of voters to vote for the people and party that create this trash without even asking what their plan is for our country. I certainly wouldn't vote for a party or movement that is willing to employ these types of ignorant attacks on their fellow citizens. I'll vote for the party that is trying to bring us into the electric future.
Gord Deane, Mississauga
One reason to vote for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the Liberals is: "better the devil you know than the devil you don't know."
Raymond Peringer, Toronto
As a free-thinking Canadian, I wonder how I am being influenced by the chatter of the right who have painted a picture of disastrous proportions, which began with the "Trudeau must go" chant from so-called "Freedom Convoy" participants. As I said to a CBC commentator the other day, "say something long enough, loud enough and often enough, it will become a truism." Trudeau's demise has little to do with his capacity and more to do with everyone jumping onto the "Trudeau must go" or "F--k Trudeau" bandwagons. How many share the message but aren't educated on the reasons for him to go? How quickly we forget about federal Liberal party successes in the past nine years, including the progress in gender equity, positive steps forward in our Indigenous relationships, affordable child care, Canadian dental care plan, a school food program, pharmacare and leading us through the tumultuous years of COVID-19.
Matthew Marosszeky,
Aurora
Toronto Sceptres?
PWHL Toronto finally reveals its name. And it's not what you might have expected, Sept. 9
I don't think much of this PWHL name. I cannot feel any emotional connection to a dead, wooden and metal symbol of power and authority. Where is the human warmth and bonding attraction in that? A good chance to say something spirited, meaningful and appealing about Toronto or Ontario has been botched. May the name still be changed to a better one? I hope so!
Norm Ferguson,
Richmond Hill

It is important for Toronto citizens to get behind their new PWHL team, the Sceptres, but the logo seems a little unfortunate. To me it falls short of suggesting hockey and instead is suggestive of something more medical. Go Dermatologists!
Joe Wright, Toronto
Consider impacts of logging
Emissions from last year's Canadian wildfires more than most countries: study, Aug. 29
Greenhouse gas emissions from wildfires are indeed massive and dangerous, and fires will only get worse without action to rein in human-caused emissions. Currently, however, the federal government's accounting means we're missing another major forest-based source of climate-altering emissions: logging. Specifically, Canada doesn't count emissions from large wildfires because it deems them "natural," but it does take credit for a massive amount of carbon dioxide - 129 million tonnes (mt) in 2022 - absorbed by regrowing trees after wildfires, even though this regrowth is just as natural as the fires that preceded it. This one-sided accounting hides the true carbon footprint of industrial logging, which, as a recent report shows, is the third-highest-emitting sector in Canada after oil and gas, and transportation. To fully address its role in driving climate change-caused wildfires, the government should start transparently and accurately reporting the climate impact of logging and put in place policies to reduce it.
Michael Polanyi,
Policy and Campaign Manager, Nature Canada, Ottawa
Motorcyclists
not affected
by gridlock
I know one group of drivers who don't mind gridlock at all ... motorcyclists! Whenever I'm in gridlock, I grudgingly watch motorcyclists merrily weaving between cars and barely affected at all by city traffic. Is it dangerous? Of course! Is it ever addressed? Never! I'm thinking of buying a motorcycle to ensure that I get to work on time.
John Cowles, Oakville
CREDIT: ;

Subject: Canadians; Emissions; Climate change

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 389 of 500

Province taps ex-city budget chief as 'new deal' advisor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-taps-ex-city-budget-chief-as-new-deal/docview/3102665741/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+taps+ex-city+budget+chief+as+%27new+deal%27+advisor&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's government has enlisted a familiar figure to advise it on talks with Toronto about shoring up the city's finances: John Tory's former budget chief.
The province confirmed Tuesday that it had appointed Gary Crawford to serve as a special adviser on the next stage of the fiscal "new deal" between Ontario and Toronto.
As chair of the city's budget committee between 2014 and 2023, Crawford was in charge of nine budgets under Tory, the former mayor. A councillor for 13 years, Crawford resigned his seat in Scarborough Southwest last July and ran unsuccessfully for the Ontario PCs in the Scarborough-Guildwood byelection.
The adviser role is the second provincial appointment he has landed in recent weeks. The Star reported in August Crawford had been selected to chair the Ontario Cannabis Retail Corp., which operates the Ontario Cannabis Store.
"We welcome Gary Crawford as Ontario's special adviser on the new deal with Toronto," said Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy in a statement.
"Serving as the Toronto budget chief for nearly a decade, Gary brings extensive municipal experience and will play a critical role in advancing Ontario's priorities laid out in the Toronto new deal."
Crawford's job will include ensuring full implementation of the deal, and responsible use of tax dollars, according to the province.
Premier Ford and Mayor Olivia Chow announced the landmark agreement between the two governments in November. It's expected to be worth up to $9 billion to Toronto over the next decade, and included Queen's Park agreeing to take ownership of the costly Gardiner Expressway and Don Valley Parkway.
But with Canada's largest municipality still struggling to pay for priorities like housing, transit and emergency services, talks between the city and province about longer-term financial relief have continued.
Speaking to reporters at an unrelated press conference last week, Chow noted that some of the funding Ontario committed in last year's agreement will run out in 2026. She said the city needs a "new deal part two."
In an interview, Crawford said his experience makes him uniquely qualified to assist in the talks.
"I know the city quite well and how it works and how it functions," he said.
"It's a good opportunity for me, it's a good opportunity for the city and frankly, for the province to really work together on something that is incredibly important," in putting Toronto on sound financial footing.
According to the province, Crawford's initial term runs until the end of Ontario's fiscal year on March 31, 2025, with an expectation it will be renewed. He will be paid $627 per workday in the part-time role, for a maximum of just over $61,000 between now and the end of his first term. If the appointmentis renewed, he will earn a maximum of $79,000 in the next fiscal year.
CREDIT: Ben Spurr Toronto Star Chief

Document 390 of 500

Gas plant emissions could affect new housing projects: Poor air quality threatens scale of development

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gas-plant-emissions-could-affect-new-housing/docview/3102665486/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gas+plant+emissions+could+affect+new+housing+projects%3A+Poor+air+quality+threatens+scale+of+development&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Levels of toxic emissions from the Port Lands gas plant are so high they could imperil the construction of tall residential buildings along the eastern waterfront - land that has been remediated and flood-protected at public expense to prepare for new housing.
According to an air quality assessment report prepared for the developer of towers on Villiers Island, emissions of nitrogen oxides (NOx) from the plant's smokestacks are seven times higher than the legal limit at the 160-metre height of the tallest proposed building.
This means that either the gas plant would have to drastically reduce its operations or the towers would have to be constructed without balconies or windows that open above 118 metres, or about 35 storeys, the report concluded. (Below that height, the report found NOx levels would be within the provincial guidelines.)
The power plant is Toronto's biggest source of both greenhouse gas emissions and NOx. The assessment, prepared for a proposed development at 309 Cherry St., some 1.7 kilometres away, reveals how air pollution could impact new housing throughout the East Harbour and Port Lands areas, which have been earmarked for tens of thousands of units in the coming years.
The former industrial area along the city's eastern waterfront is one of the largest swaths of undeveloped land in the city and has undergone $1.4 billion in publicly financed flood protection work.
"This report shows that the vision for the future of Toronto's waterfront is fundamentally at odds with the long-term operation of the Port Lands gas plant," said Bryan Purcell, vice-president of policy and programs at The Atmospheric Fund.
"There's simply no way to reduce emissions at the plant to allow the construction of tall residential towers in the vicinity," added Purcell, whose agency advocates for low-carbon solutions in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton area.
Atura, a subsidiary of publicly owned Ontario Power Generation, owns and operates the plant. In response to questions from the Star, spokesperson Darius Sokal wrote that the plant operates within all public health and environmental emissions limits.
"We regularly work with our neighbours - current and future - related to compatibility with other nearby land uses," he added.
A Star investigation last year found the Port Lands gas plant operates more than 21 hours a day during the summer, sending carbon emissions into the atmosphere and pollutants harmful to human health into the local community.
Despite city council twice voting against its expansion, the plant is embarking on upgrades that will increase its output by nine per cent. At the same time, the province has extended its operating contract from 2029 to 2034.
The proposed development on Cherry Street includes towers of 49, 39, 27 and 10 storeys and the report analyzed the air quality at 160 metres - the height of the tallest building. It also looked at air quality at another proposed development in the East Harbour Lands, which is closer to the 75-metre-tall smokestacks at the Portlands Energy Centre (PEC). In both cases, it found NOx far exceeding the legal limit at heights above 35 storeys.
"If the development at East Harbour Lands is constructed for residential use at the heights approved in the zoning, PEC would not remain in compliance with the air quality standards for NOx," stated the report. "PEC would need to reduce its NOx emission rate ... (by) approximately a factor of seven reduction to maintain compliance."
Castlepoint Numa, the developer that commissioned the report, and the report's authors, did not respond to a request for comment. Nor did Cadillac Fairview, the developer of East Harbour.
This summer, the city unveiled a re-naturalized mouth of the Don River, which was rerouted as part of a massive public infrastructure project to prevent flooding in and around Villiers Island, which is slated to host at least 15 towers and 9,000 housing units but could contain far more, activists say.
If tower heights are capped at 35 storeys to allow the Port Lands gas plant to continue to operate, it would limit the amount of housing that could be built in the surrounding area and could imperil the economics of the developments, which rely on tall towers to be profitable.
The original air quality assessment completed for the Port Lands gas plant in 2003 said the pollution levels from the plant would not restrict future development in the area. But the report did not consider buildings over 20 storeys tall.
"It is very clear that the operation of the Portlands Energy Centre could mean less housing will be built in the area near it or that the plant operation will have to be heavily restrained," said Peter Tabuns, the MPP who represents the area. "Those heights and the NOx are a really bad mix."
Exposure to NOx has been shown to cause lung and heart disease, asthma, Alzheimer's, cancer and birth complications; Health Canada estimates that there are more than 15,000 premature deaths per year due to air pollution.
"On one side of the issue is housing and clean air, on the other side is an irresponsible plan to expand gas burning for power production. The province's approach is wrong," Tabuns said.
Dr. Mili Roy, an assistant professor at U of T's faculty of medicine and the co-chair of the Canadian Association of Physicians for the Environment, stressed there is no "safe" level of NOx exposure.
Immediately after the election of Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government in 2018, Ontario halted construction of renewable energy, like wind and solar, and instead doubled down on natural gas plants to meet a growing demand for electricity.
This reversed the decline in carbon emissions from our electrical grid, which were reduced by more than 80 per cent by the previous Liberal government after it shut down all the coal plants in the province. Now, electricity emissions are on the rise as new gas plants are built and existing ones are used more often.
Climate experts have questioned the increasing use of gas to generate electricity, especially as renewable energy has plummeted in cost and is now considered the cheapest form of energy in history.
The air quality assessment introduces new questions.
"This report sharpens the conversation around the Port Lands gas plant, which has until now been about climate change and emissions. Now it's about billions of dollars in development and tens of thousands of units of housing," Purcell said.
As the health and climate impacts of relying on gas have become clear, local residents have started organizing against the Port Lands gas plant.
"Toronto can get all the energy it needs from renewables. So it's hard to understand why we're pursuing gas, especially with all the health implications," said David Smith, a longtime east-end resident and member of Toronto East Residents for Renewable Energy.
Smith is excited about the renewal of the Port Lands and has poured over the renderings of dozens of new condo towers planned for the area.
He notes that none of them depict the smokestacks of the power plant next door.
"All these people want a nice place to live. But is it a nice place to live if you can't breathe the air?"
CREDIT: Marco Chown Oved Climate Change Reporter

Subject: Air pollution; Outdoor air quality; Emission standards; Coal-fired power plants; Emissions; Industrial plant emissions; Renewable resources; Environmental impact; Nitrogen

Document 391 of 500

TVO backs away from film about Russian soldiers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tvo-backs-away-film-about-russian-soldiers/docview/3102665396/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=TVO+backs+away+from+film+about+Russian+soldiers&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TVO walked away Tuesday from a documentary following Russian soldiers in Ukraine as the backlash grew over its inclusion at the Toronto International Film Festival.
The documentary "Russians at War," which premiered at the Venice Film Festival last week and is set to make its North American debut at TIFF on Friday, was produced in association with Ontario's TVO and British Columbia's Knowledge Network. TVO board chair Chris Day, however, said in a statement Tuesday that the channel would no longer be "supporting or airing" the film, citing "thoughtful and heartfelt input" from Ukrainian-Canadians.
Speaking from the Liberals' caucus retreat, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland told reporters Tuesday that both diplomats and the Ukrainian-Canadian community have expressed "grave concerns" about the film "Russians at War" - concerns she shares.
"We as a country have to be very, very clear that there can be no moral equivalency in our understanding of this conflict," she said.
The film also received $340,000 in funding from the not-for-profit Canada Media Fund.
A synopsis of the film on TIFF's website said it chronicles the lives of Russian soldiers as they grow increasingly disillusioned. "They all come to realize that everything they heard about the war in Russian media is false," it read. "They begin to doubt their purpose - and fight only to survive."
Ukraine's consul general in Toronto, Oleh Nikolenko, previously urged TIFF to pull the documentary, accusing the film of absolving Russian soldiers of responsibility.
TIFF did not immediately respond to the Star's requests for comment.
Outside the Scotiabank Theatre on Tuesday, dozens of protesters gathered to protest the decision to air the documentary.
The film's Russian-Canadian director, Anastasia Trofimova, asserted in a written statement that the film is not propaganda and was filmed without the permission of the Russian government.
"I want to be clear that this Canada-France co-production is an antiwar film made at great risk to all involved, myself especially," she said. "I unequivocally believe that Russia's invasion of Ukraine is unjustified, illegal and acknowledge the validity of the International Criminal Court investigation of war crimes in Ukraine."
TVO initially stood by the film, responding to the controversy last week with a statement that the documentary "is at its core an anti-war film." On Tuesday, Day said it "will be reviewing the process by which" the film won the channel's backing.
With files from
The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Kevin Jiang Toronto Star With files from The Canadian Press

Document 392 of 500

Chrystia Freeland, Ukrainian officials slam TIFF screening a documentary following Russian soldiers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/chrystia-freeland-ukrainian-officials-slam-tiff/docview/3102972100/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Chrystia+Freeland%2C+Ukrainian+officials+slam+TIFF+screening+a+documentary+following+Russian+soldiers&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A documentary following Russian soldiers on the front lines of the war in Ukraine is facing backlash over its inclusion at the Toronto International Film Festival.
Speaking from the Liberals' caucus retreat, Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland told reporters Tuesday that both diplomats and the Ukrainian-Canadian community have expressed "grave concerns" about the film "Russians at War" - concerns which she shares.
"We as a country have to be very, very clear that there can be no moral equivalency in our understanding of this conflict," she said. "It's not right for Canadian public money to be supporting the screening and production of a film like this."
A synopsis of the film on TIFF's website described it as a first-person documentary chronicling the lives of Russian soldiers as they grow increasingly disillusioned by the war. "They all come to realize that everything they heard about the war in Russian media is false," it read. "They begin to doubt their purpose - and fight only to survive."
The documentary, which premiered at the Venice Film Festival last week and is set to make its North American debut at TIFF on Friday, was produced in association with Ontario's TVO and British Columbia's Knowledge Network. It also received $340,000 in funding from the not-for-profit Canada Media Fund.
Ukraine's consul general in Toronto, Oleh Nikolenko, previously urged TIFF to pull the documentary from its schedule, accusing the film of absolving Russian soldiers of responsibility in the ongoing invasion.
"It is irresponsible to allow the Toronto International Film Festival, one of the most reputable world film stages, to be used to whitewash the responsibility of Russian soldiers committing war crimes in Ukraine during the ongoing Russian invasion," Nikolenko wrote in a letter to TIFF CEO Cameron Bailey dated Sep. 5.
He added that representatives from the Consulate General of Ukraine in Toronto, the Embassy of Ukraine in Ottawa and the Ukrainian Canadian Congress raised concerns with TIFF management, but did not find their response satisfactory.
TIFF did not immediately respond to the Star's requests for comment.
In response to the backlash, the film's Russian-Canadian director, Anastasia Trofimova, asserted in a written statement that the film is not propaganda, and that it was filmed without the permission of the Russian government.
"I want to be clear that this Canada-France co-production is an antiwar film made at great risk to all involved, myself especially," she said.
"I unequivocally believe that Russia's invasion of Ukraine is unjustified, illegal and acknowledge the validity of the International Criminal Court investigation of war crimes in Ukraine."
In producing the film, Trofimova followed a Russian unit through parts of Russian-occupied eastern Ukraine. Nikolenko said he believed the documentary wasn't filmed legally, as Trofimova did not receive permission to cross the border into Russian-occupied Ukraine.
By screening the film, TIFF is making itself complicit, he continued.
TVO responded to the controversy in a statement, saying the documentary "is at its core an anti-war film. It is unauthorized by Russian officials and was made at great personal risk to the filmmaker, who was under constant threat of arrest and incarceration for trying to tell an unofficial story."
In Venice, Trofimova told reporters that she witnessed no war crimes, characterizing the soldiers she accompanied as: "Absolutely ordinary guys with families, with a sense of humour, with their own understanding of what's happening in this war."
For her part, Freeland noted that "This is a war where Russia is breaking international law and committing war crimes.
"There is very clearly good and evil in this war. Ukrainians are fighting for their sovereignty and for democracy around the world."
With files from The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland is denouncing the use of public funds for a documentary about Russian soldiers. On the same day, a crowd of people protested the film's screening at the Toronto International Film Festival. Freeland says she shares the "grave concerns" raised about "Russians at War." (Sept. 10, 2024 / The Canadian Press)People protest outside of Scotiabank Theatre about the documentary "Russians at War," the press screening of which was playing at the Toronto International Film Festival on Tuesday. Public screenings start Friday.

"As former budget chief, Gary Crawford understands the structural financial challenges the city faces and knows the city needs a strong new deal," Chow's press secretary said in a statement.
"The mayor looks forward to working with him to find solutions to make the city more affordable and more safe for all Torontonians."

Document 394 of 500

Doug Ford is 'obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores' instead of health care, Jagmeet Singh charges

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-obsessed-with-alcohol-convenience/docview/3102970797/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+%27obsessed+with+alcohol+in+convenience+stores%27+instead+of+health+care%2C+Jagmeet+Singh+charges&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's the booze talking.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh fired back at Doug Ford on Tuesday, accusing the premier of being "obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores" instead of more pressing challenges.
One day after Ford derided Singh for abruptly ending the two-year supply and confidence agreement to prop up Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority Liberals, the New Democrat responded.
"Doug Ford needs to worry about delivering health care. People in Ontario can't get the health care they need, they're struggling with the cost of living and he's obsessed with alcohol in convenience stores," the federal NDP leader said in Montreal.
"I don't know any person that I've spoken to when I tour Ontario, when I'm in the communities, that says that's their number one pressing issue," said Singh, referring to the premier's booze liberalization scheme that took effect last week and will cost taxpayers between $225 million and $1 billion.
"Doug Ford should focus on those things instead of being stuck on Twitter," he said.
On Monday, the premier raised eyebrows after being asked whether the threat of an imminent federal election would force him to reconsider any plans for a potential early provincial vote next year.
"As far as I'm concerned, MP Singh is just blowing smoke," Ford said in Prince Edward County.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension and he was elected (in) ... 2019 and I'll be floored and I'll stand up here and apologize to all the greedy politicians if he calls the election early," the premier said, referring to the six-year threshold for federal MPs to qualify for a pension.
"But let's face it, he wants his pension. He's not going anywhere until ... Oct. 20, 2025 because there's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money. So my bet is, he's not doing anything. He's just putting on a big show like he has for the last number of years there."
Last Thursday, Singh - who has said an election could come at any time, though his party would not automatically vote against the Liberals - dismissed the pension charge as "a ludicrous suggestion."
"I'm obviously going to get re-elected in my riding (of Burnaby South), I'm not worried about that at all. This is a silly argument being brought about by Conservatives," he told Vassy Kapelos on CTV's Power Play.
A snap federal vote could throw a wrinkle in Ford's plan to have an Ontario election one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
His Progressive Conservatives, who lead in every public-opinion poll, would prefer to face voters sooner rather than later.
That's because they're worried a Pierre Poilievre-led Conservative government might make cuts to transfer payments and to federal subsidies for electric-vehicle manufacturers in Ontario.
There's also concern at the potential for charges being laid in the ongoing RCMP investigation into the Greenbelt land swap scandal.
Political tradition is another reason why Ford could go early - historically, Ontario voters prefer to have different parties in power in Ottawa and Queen's Park at the same time.
When Ontarians elect Liberals federally, they usually choose Tories provincially.
Conversely, when they elect federal Tory governments, the Liberals - and, in one instance, the New Democrats - tend to win provincially.
The phenomenon, known as the "Underhill balance theory," has held true for about 70 of the past 81 years.
While Ford has sparred with Trudeau in the past, he has worked closely with the prime minister since the COVID-19 pandemic began in 2020.
In the wake of that health emergency, the two leaders have focused on shared economic priorities like tens of billions of dollars in planned subsidies for EV manufacturers, such as Volkswagen, Stellantis and Honda.
CAPTION: NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh speaks to the media at the party's caucus retreat in Montreal on Sept. 10, 2024.

Help the Liberals? Oh, come on - Mark Carney is here for Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/help-liberals-oh-come-on-mark-carney-is-here/docview/3102666098/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Help+the+Liberals%3F+Oh%2C+come+on+-+Mark+Carney+is+here+for+Canada&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mark Carney wants you to know he's answering a call from his country, not overtly positioning himself for the Liberal leadership, or painting himself to be the Grits' saviour.
"I'm an outsider," the former Bank of Canada governor told journalists in Nanaimo, B.C., where Grit MPs are gathered for three days of caucus meetings. On Monday, Carney was announced by the Liberal party as the chair of its leader's task force on economic growth. He is to travel across the country, meeting with stakeholders and writing up a plan to address near and longer-term economic growth and productivity issues, with an eye to shaping the party's electoral platform. On Tuesday, he met with Liberal MPs for about 40 minutes, with most of the time spent answering their questions about the economy and what he planned to do.
For those wanting to know why one of the country's most impressive economic minds - he steered Canada through the global economic crisis, earned widespread praise as a prudent financial manager and was tapped to lead the Bank of England - was hitching his wagon to the struggling Liberals, Carney suggested he wasn't joining a partisan fight, but just answering the call of duty.
"I have a couple of simple rules. One, if the prime minister of Canada asked me to do something, I will do it," he told reporters. "Secondly, you know, I'm interested. I have some experience in these issues and I'm interested in helping our country to grow."
Asked if he had been asked to run for the Liberals in either of the Sept. 16 byelections, or if he planned to run for a seat in the next general election, Carney sidestepped. "I understood the question, but my answer stands," he said. "I'm interested in doing something, not being something" - a line he repeated twice.
Carney also sidestepped when asked if there was daylight between his position on carbon pricing and that of the Grits. In the spring, "Carbon Tax Carney" as Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are trying to define him, told a Senate committee that carbon pricing has "served a purpose up until now. I think one can always look for better solutions."
Last year, however, Carney was one of Trudeau's fiercest critics after the prime minister announced a three-year pause on the carbon levy for Canadians using heating oil. At the time, Carney said, Canadians were not struggling because of the carbon tax, but because of broad increases in energy prices, in food prices, the impact on wages and the lingering efforts of COVID, and he suggested he would have addressed affordability concerns differently.
"Being low carbon is going to be a determinant of whether we can create jobs, whether we can sell our products in the United States, in Europe, around the world," he said on Tuesday. "We have to help our companies become more competitive. There is a huge range of things that the federal government can do."
It wasn't immediately clear Tuesday what Carney had told Liberal MPs about carbon pricing. Procurement Minister Jean-Yves Duclos told the Star that Carney had been asked about it and "he still believes that if we put a price on pollution, which puts more money in the pockets of eight families out of 10, that's a great thing to do!"
Outside of the Vancouver Island Conference Centre, however, a handful of protesters reminded the Liberals that, so far, they appear to have lost the communication battle when it comes to carbon pricing and cost of living increases. They'll face another challenge on that front next spring when the carbon price is set to increase yet again.
Carney's carbon pricing stance isn't the only line of attack the Conservatives plan to hit the Liberals with.
While Carney sought to frame himself as "a catalyst" to help the government define a new course for economic growth, rather than an appointee designated to bolster the popularity of the Liberal party, so too did the Conservatives.
In a letter to the prime minister, Ontario Tory MP Michael Barrett told Trudeau he was deeply concerned that Carney had been appointed a Liberal party adviser, rather than an adviser to the government, in order to bypass conflict of interest rules.
"Ministerial advisers are reporting public office holders under the (law) and are legally required to disclose potential conflicts of interest, ethics screens, recusals and all controlled assets, liabilities and outside activities," Barrett wrote. He noted Carney had done very well for himself and sits on several corporate boards, including of asset management companies overseeing trillions of dollars. Interestingly, Barrett did not demand Trudeau cancel Carney's appointment, but instead that Carney's position be placed within the government structure - essentially, allowing the Conservatives to prod Carney's financial dealings and read his emails.
"This is a laughable stunt from the Conservative party," Liberal spokesman Parker Lund told the Star. "The Conservatives are just upset that Mark Carney supports the Liberal economic plan and not Pierre Poilievre's disastrous cuts that would damage the economy."
CAPTION: Former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney speaks to reporters at the Liberal caucus retreat in Nanaimo, B.C., onSept. 10, 2024.

A union helped open this medical clinic. Now its members have been dropped as patients because there aren't enough doctors

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/union-helped-open-this-medical-clinic-now-members/docview/3102454050/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+union+helped+open+this+medical+clinic.+Now+its+members+have+been+dropped+as+patients+because+there+aren%27t+enough+doctors&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Premier Doug Ford's government scrambles to improve access to health care, fallout from the front lines of Ontario's growing doctor shortage is moving from the examination room to the courtroom.
A Sault Ste. Marie medical clinic that made headlines across Canada for its dramatic decision to cut loose 10,000 patients in May because of physician retirements and departures is the subject of a lawsuit from United Steelworkers Local 2251.
The lead plaintiff is local president Mike Da Prat, one of the patients who was dropped from the clinic roster and who has yet to find another doctor. His lawyer has filed a statement of claim in Ontario Superior Court of Justice against the Group Health Centre.
Among other things, it alleges that age discrimination played a role in the decision, and seeks an injunction forcing the clinic to take back Da Prat and about 200 "original subscribers" of the clinic (in addition to their immediate family members), which was founded and built with $800,000 in contributions from the union local's members in 1963, before medicare and the Ontario Health Insurance Plan.
"We were to be members of Group Health for the rest of our lives. Instead, we were de-rostered," said Da Prat.
The allegations in the lawsuit have not been proven in court, which will hold a hearing on the injunction request Oct. 30. To put it in perspective, the original $800,000 at stake is the equivalent of $8 million today, according to the Bank of Canada inflation calculator.
Group Health called the lawsuit "unfortunate," given the challenges in recruiting new doctors at a time when physician shortages are widespread across the country.
"We are disappointed to reallocate limited health-care funds and resources to legal proceedings. Our focus remains on providing quality health-care services to our community, and we believe that collaboration and constructive dialogue are the most effective ways to address issues," it said in a written statement.
"We continue our efforts to recruit additional primary care physicians and nurse practitioners."
The lawsuit points to the high stakes in the remote northern Ontario city of 80,000, says New Democrat MPP France Gelinas (Nickel Belt), a physiotherapist by training and her party's longtime health critic.
"It has divided the community," Gelinas said of the de-rostering. "You just can't imagine the hurt people feel. It's just awful what's happening."
Unlike in more populous Toronto or southern Ontario, medical alternatives are few.
"There's nowhere else to go," Gelinas said, noting the nearest major population centre is Sudbury, a four-hour drive east in perfect weather.
Concerns about medical care are certain to become a delicate issue in a provincial election campaign that Ford is expected to call as early as next spring, likely putting local Progressive Conservative MPP Ross Romano on the hot seat.
Romano could not be reached for comment despite several requests made to his office by the Star.
The de-rostering of patients at the end of May turned out to be not as severe as the 10,000 predicted by Group Health in its January bombshell.
But the situation is fluid in a province the Ontario Medical Association says is short about 2,500 doctors, with 40 per cent of working physicians considering retirement in the next five years. The OMA estimates about 2.5 million Ontarians do not have a family doctor.
Two doctors who left Group Health to start their own health team took 3,000 patients with them. However, another Group Health doctor subsequently left, prompting the de-rostering of 1,200 patients.
And while new nurse practitioners will begin working at Group Health this fall, allowing patients to be returned to the roster, it said, "known physician retirements in 2025 ... will result in additional de-rostering of approximately 6,000 patients.
Group Health's plan is to use its "access care clinic" - which de-rostered patients can call first thing in the morning in hopes of getting a same-day appointment - "to provide interim care to these patients with the intent to re-roster all patients," the health centre's statement said.
That, of course, will depend on attracting new physicians to Group Health which now has 30 primary care doctors and 10 nurse practitioners in addition to 26 specialists including a cardiologist, an ophthalmologist, dermatologists, oncologists and surgeons doing procedures to take pressure off the local hospital.
The access care clinic operates with $2.8 million in provincial funding announced in late May by Health Minister Sylvia Jones, who said at the time "we recognize there is more work to be done."
Jones said she is in touch with Romano on expanding access to primary care in the city.
A Toronto lawyer handling the lawsuit maintains the Group Health Centre made a bad choice in the de-rostering.
"We understand that there were issues with having practitioners leave, but the answer was not to just kick out the Steelworkers," said Louis Sokolov of Sotos LLP.
"In exchange for their contribution to the centre's seed funding and payment of a monthly premium, the original subscribers were guaranteed medical care from the centre," he wrote in the 24-page lawsuit that argues the introduction of medicare in Ontario in 1969 does not detract from their right.
"The de-rostered patients were chosen simply because they happened to be on the rosters of the departing physicians, without regard to their relative medical needs. This methodology discriminates on the bases of age (given that patients on the rosters of retiring physicians are older, on average), contrary to section 1 (1) of the Human Rights Code."
As to those allegations, the Group Health Centre said it "will not be commenting on the particulars of matters before the court."
In another sign of local tensions over the de-rostering, a video went viral last week picturing a man wearing a Local 2251 T-shirt who teed off on Prime Minister Justin Trudeau during his visit to Sault Ste. Marie.
"What about the 40 per cent taxes I am paying and I don't have a family doctor?" the unidentified man said as a TV camera recorded the exchange with Trudeau, who was rebuffed after touting his new tariffs on Chinese steel as a way to protect local jobs at Algoma Steel.
CAPTION: NDP health critic France Gelinas speaks at Queen's Park on Sept. 14, 2022.

Subject: Health care access; Primary care; Injunctions; Nurse practitioners; Health care policy; Litigation; Medicare; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Medicare Physicians

Location: France
