Document 397 of 500

Doug Ford's beer boondoggle is nothing to brag about

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-beer-boondoggle-is-nothing-brag-about/docview/3102454046/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+beer+boondoggle+is+nothing+to+brag+about&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amazingly, Doug Ford is bringing people together over expanded alcohol sales.
The premier has miraculously united the two opposing sides in a symbiotic debate about his latest move to bring beer and wine into corner stores.
In one corner are Ford's Tories, cheerfully raising a glass to their supposedly life-changing achievement of boosting booze distribution.
In the other corner are the premier's opponents, desperately raising the alarm about the allegedly life-ending disaster of increased alcohol availability.
Neither group was attracting any attention - until recently, most Ontarians were blissfully oblivious. Now, together, they are now making common cause in creating a cause célèbre.
Today, beer in corner stores is a stale idea first trotted out 40 years ago by opposition leaders looking for attention. When David Peterson and later Mike Harris became premier, they found bigger issues facing the province.
All these years later, Ford can think of nothing more important. It's his signature issue politically (even if not personally - he's a teetotaller).
Ahead of a possible spring election, the premier has been touring Ontario touting his obsession. But Ford's Tories weren't gaining traction - until researchers and anti-alcohol activists raised the volume by warning of more drunkenness, illness and fatalities from expanded sales.
Now, on both sides of the divide, the premier and his opponents - aided and abetted by the media - are trying to convert an anticlimax into a controversy, turning a nonstory into a big deal.
Consider the premise that beer and wine will suddenly become both ubiquitous and iniquitous. In reality, the previous Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne broke up the unholy duopoly of the LCBO and The Beer Store, which had a stranglehold on sales, by giving a large piece of the action to hundreds of large supermarkets nearly a decade ago.
Those big supermarkets achieved far more economies of scale than small convenience stores can today. But it's worth recalling that the same scaremongering voices arguing against sales in convenience stores today were warning against letting it into supermarkets a decade ago.
Moreover, when COVID hit, Ford's government allowed thousands of already-licensed bars and restaurants to sell takeout alcohol. Yet there was no evidence of social upheaval then, just as there isn't any reliable predictor of it now.
In fact, governments of all political persuasions have long understood that the easiest way to raise revenue is to open more LCBO stores across the province. More outlets equals more inputs, and yet you never hear anyone complaining about new government-run stores.
Over time, Ontario has learned to restrain the temptation for prohibition or restriction. At the same time, we've seen that wider availability works just fine in other jurisdictions, not just in Quebec but across the continent and around the world.
All that said, convenience stores will not revolutionize the market, despite the pretensions and promises of this Progressive Conservative government. They have not started a stampede to stock beer, wine or pre-mixed cocktails, because they know the market for growth is limited.
Convenience stores are about convenience, not competitive pricing. You pay a premium for having a location at your corner, open late at night, offering small quantities in outlying locations with increased distribution costs and markups.
This isn't the 1980s, with corner stores on every corner. Today, supermarkets have more locations with longer hours than they did four decades ago, and the proliferation of cannabis stores has long since overtaken alcohol as a talking point.
That hasn't stopped the naysayers from saying no to the iterative convenience of corner stores. The more fuss they make, the more fun for Ford's Tories, because it makes their small beer look like a big deal.
In the premier's mind, this is a proof point for his 2022 campaign promise to "Get it Done" - on beer and wine as on homebuilding and road-building. But if booze is the best he can do, it's not saying much - and it's costing way too much.
To deliver on his ill-conceived and long-delayed pledge from the 2018 campaign, Ford had to pay off The Beer Store's ownership in order to get around the previous "master framework agreement" that phased in beer sales through 2025. The PC government paid $225 million outright, and is on the hook for hundreds of millions of dollars in potential ancillary costs, just so they buy themselves a 15-month head start - ahead of the early election Ford's campaign team wants for next spring.
If the teetotalling, bellowing Ford were in opposition, he'd be lampooning and lambasting an NDP or Liberal government for this beer boondoggle. Remarkably, he seems to have gotten away with it in power - even if he hasn't gotten much else done.
Get it done? The Greenbelt has come undone, highway construction is off track, and Metrolinx mass transit is derailed or delayed.
Beer is here belatedly, but the bottle is empty.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford and Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy announced plans last May to allow beer sales in corner stores this summer.

Subject: Alcohol; Highway construction; Beer; Convenience stores; Distribution costs; Supermarkets

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Distribution costs Supermarkets

Title: Doug Ford's beer boondoggle is nothing to brag about

Doug Ford's prediction on a federal election + Air Canada's looming cancellations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-prediction-on-federal-election-air/docview/3102454009/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+prediction+on+a+federal+election+%2B+Air+Canada%27s+looming+cancellations&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Tuesday, September 10 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Doug Ford's take on the dissolved NDP-Liberal deal, impending Air Canada cancellations and tonight's U.S. presidential debate.
DON'T MISSDoug Ford calls Jagmeet Singh a 'greedy politician'
The premier didn't mince his words when speaking with reporters in Prince Edward County on Monday. Ford, whose Progressive Conservative government has worked closely with the federal Liberals, said Jagmeet Singh was just "blowing smoke" when he tore up the NDP-Liberal supply and confidence agreement last week. "There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension," he said, taking aim at the federal NDP leader. Here's what else Ford had to say.
Flashback: Seven years ago, Ford and Singh appeared on TVO's "Political Blind Date" series, where the pair rode bicycles and shared breakfast.Meanwhile... Liberal MPs are relaying a message they're increasingly more and more: a lot of people are tired of Trudeau.Air Canada cancellations could begin as early as Friday
Are you flying somewhere this week? Be sure to check your flight - Air Canada cancellations could begin as early as Friday as the airline continues to be at loggerheads with the Air Line Pilots Association. If a 72-hour strike or lockout notice is issued by either party on Sunday, the airline said it will progressively cancel flights, completely suspending operations by Sept. 18. Here's what you need to know.
Booking problems? Air Canada is allowing free rebooking of flights between Sept. 15 and Sept. 23.What the pilots want: Better pay rates that are competitive with U.S. wages, quality-of-life provisions and retirement benefits.What Kamala Harris needs to do in tonight's debate with Donald Trump
A debate has already ended one presidential candidacy - now its Kamala Harris' turn to take on Donald Trump in a war of words. Tonight's debate will be Harris' first big chance to put more distance between her and Trump in the polls as her current lead narrows. Allan Woods has some tips for Harris' big night.
Fight card: 90-minutes, on ABC News, beginning at 9 p.m. EST. Star subscribers can catch a livestream on the Star's website. Here's what else you need to know.WHAT ELSE

TORONTO: James Earl Jones, whose voice is forever ingrained in the childhood memories of millions, has died. The 93-year-old was a pioneer with a long, illustrious career, having voiced Mufasa in "The Lion King," Darth Vader in "Star Wars" and being one of the first Black actors in a continuing role on a daytime drama. Here's how the world will remember him.

Doug Ford won't let Toronto use cameras to get traffic moving. The city should do it anyway

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-wont-let-toronto-use-cameras-get/docview/3102453998/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+won%27t+let+Toronto+use+cameras+to+get+traffic+moving.+The+city+should+do+it+anyway&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There have been a lot of bad stories about Toronto drivers lately. They've killed cyclists. They've run down pedestrians at pedestrian crossovers. They've ignored transit lanes and blocked intersections and somehow gone the wrong way down highway off-ramps. But there is some good news amidst all this bad behaviour: it turns out there's a ridiculously simple and obvious thing that would help stop it.
It's cameras. The answer is cameras.
Lots and lots of automated enforcement cameras.
When it comes to traffic enforcement, the data suggests automated cameras are much more efficient and much more effective at catching bad behaviour than police officers.
That's likely to be especially true in our city, where some police officers seem to regard traffic enforcement as an unwanted chore. In an article last week by the Star's Mahdis Habibinia about drivers who get away with "blocking the box" - stopping in the middle of an intersection during a red light - former Toronto police officer Matthew Wood said there's a culture amongst officers that prefers "action-oriented, high-adrenalin, high-octane" calls and does not consider issuing traffic tickets to be "real police work."
Acting Supt. Matt Moyer, the head of the Toronto police Traffic Services unit, also pumped the brakes on the idea of a police crackdown of drivers who block the box. He noted that the police generally need to block a lane of traffic for an extended period while pulling over a driver to issue a ticket, so a crackdown may not actually improve traffic conditions.
I'm not thrilled with either of those explanations. In my mind, any would-be candidate to be a Toronto police officer who says they want the job for the "high adrenaline" thrill of it should immediately get screened out of consideration. And I've never really understood why, in my experience, cops spend an eternity sitting in their cars during traffic stops. It feels like the process could be sped up significantly with a bit of urgency.
But again, the good news is that automated cameras don't have an adrenal system and are capable of issuing tickets in a literal snap.
Cameras work. I recently stumbled across a segment from a local news station in Washington, D.C. reporting that cameras mounted on buses have resulted in a whopping 147,251 citations to drivers for improperly using bus lanes since last November.
And D.C. is not unique. New York City has long had bus-mounted cameras to catch bus lane violators through their Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) program. In June, the ABLE program was expanded to also issue tickets to drivers who are double parked. Chicago's city council approved an ambitious "Smart Streets" pilot last year that will see drivers mailed tickets when they're caught on camera parking in bike lanes, across crosswalks and in other no-parking areas.
Compared to these cities, Toronto is way behind on automated enforcement. Sure, city hall complemented its long-standing red-light camera program with photo radar cameras for speeding in 2019, and sure, they've been effective at issuing tickets and slowing drivers down, but city hall has been slow to roll out new ones. The program started with 50 cameras in 2020. These days, there are 75. Another 75 are on order. It's progress - but it's slow progress.
And more importantly, there's not been enough movement toward getting cameras that can enforce other traffic violations, like driving in transit lanes or blocking the box. That's despite TTC streetcars running with active external cameras since 2019, and the city's speed camera supplier - Verra Mobility (formerly Redflex Systems) - offering tech that can also catch box blockers and transit lane violators.
At city hall, the explanation for slow progress is usually to blame Queen's Park. But while it's certainly frustrating that Premier Doug Ford government hasn't yet passed legislation allowing vehicle owners to be charged for offences like blocking the box even if they weren't behind the wheel, I'm tiring of that excuse.
If the legislation isn't yet in place to allow automated ticketing of more traffic violations, I see no reason why the city couldn't activate more cameras now to at least capture better data - and to ensure we've got the tech in place to immediately start issuing tickets whenever Ford gets around to changing the rules.
This would mirror the approach taken by former mayor John Tory when he was advocating for photo radar. When Ford was dragging his feet on enabling the regulations to allow the use of cameras, Tory pushed for council to move forward with the cameras anyway - almost daring Ford to stand in the way. After cameras captured some drivers exceeding 100 km/h in school zones, it got a lot harder to say no.
With city council set to debate a congestion management strategy later this fall, Mayor Olivia Chow should take a page from the same playbook: push forward aggressively and dare the provincial government to block it. Don't accept a slow small-scale pilot project. Other cities have already tested this for us. And with the police abdicating responsibility, it's so clear what Toronto needs to do to hold drivers to account: get the cameras rolling.
CAPTION: The automated speed cameras in Toronto have been effective at issuing tickets and slowing drivers down, but city hall has been slow to roll out new ones.

Subject: Speed limits; Cameras; Police; Automation; Councils; Enforcement; Traffic violations

Business indexing term: Subject: Automation

Take it from me, I lived under communism. Kamala Harris is no communist

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/take-me-i-lived-under-communism-kamala-harris-is/docview/3102453854/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Take+it+from+me%2C+I+lived+under+communism.+Kamala+Harris+is+no+communist&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Reading recovery required
Ford government risking the reading skills of thousands of kindergarten students, Human Rights Report says, Sept. 5
As a former primary teacher with the Toronto District School Board who retired just before the release of the recent report on reading, I always taught phonics in kindergarten. I knew how important it was to all learners, especially immigrant children, children with dyslexia and those with special needs. Unfortunately, I often found that the teaching of phonics would not be carried on by many teachers in other primary, junior, and intermediate grades. Scientific research demonstrates that the teaching of phonics is critical because English is a complex language to master. Letter sounds and letter combinations have to be taught. For example, we don't expect students to learn the concept of the number "12" by osmosis. We teach it to them. The critical piece in reading and writing success is that phonics needs to be taught systematically across the grades from kindergarten to Grade 8 and in age-appropriate ways. This means that the principal needs to take the lead in making sure that this happens. I never once had a principal who provided leadership in phonics education and this needs to change if all Ontario students are to have the opportunity to become successful readers and writers of English.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
This is an unfortunate situation. It is also unfortunate that the "Reading Recovery Program" in the York board was discontinued a little while ago. This program took young students who were having trouble with reading and provided extra support with a dedicated reading recovery teacher, prior to Grade 2. Maybe the program will be reintroduced under a new name.
Joel Hertz, Toronto
As an educator in kindergarten for more than 10 years, I can say we have to remember the different stages of growth for kindergarten along with variations in age. Junior students are four years old while the seniors are five. However, I remember celebrating three birthdays over a Christmas break with one student turning four after Christmas, another turning five just before New Years and a third student turning six on Jan. 1. The developmental learning of these three ages is critical when implementing the kindergarten curriculum. Four-year-olds are beginning to recognize symbols (letters and numbers), five-year-olds are learning letter sounds and "stretching" words together, while six-year-olds are capable of reading simple sentences phonetically. These students all learn at different times throughout their kindergarten career. Reading/letter recognition should be fun. Reading/letter recognition should be ongoing. These young students will grasp the ability of reading/letter recognition when their developing brains are capable and academically ready.
Melanie Kohek, Whitby, Ont.
We still need electoral reform
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh's latest repositioning manoeuvre is an attempt to get the best for his party in the context of our flawed winner-take-all electoral system. His move will cause many voters to recalculate their own strategic voting patterns to fit the new situation. But with all that strategic voting, our current electoral system is never a true representation of the true will of the people. We don't have a truly representative democracy. To try to correct that, I support the Charter Challenge for Fair Voting project, which is arguing that we all have a Charter right to effective representation and equal treatment. We all have a right to vote for our first choice and then actually (effectively) get the proportion of representation that we voted for with proportional representation.
Boyd Reimer, Toronto
The world will still turn with Poilievre as PM
I'm amazed at just how many of your readers are absolutely convinced that the world will end if the Conservatives get into power. Do they actually think all is OK in Canada under the Liberals? One out of every 20 families are now using a food bank. That's five per cent of Canadian families. Pretty much every city and large town has a tent city because people are homeless. This happened under the Liberals' watch. Are you all OK with this? I'm not. The carbon program has put the price of everything up by a lot more than normal inflation. That's OK too? This is just a short list of all the things that have happened purely because of the Liberals. So let me ask you one question. How can things possibly get worse under the Conservatives? Especially when they want to fix these things. I'd really like to know.
Jeremy Thornton, Ballentrae, Ont.
I demand an election as soon as possible (as soon as Pierre-ible!)
Terry Toll, Campbell's Bay, Que.
What are the root causes?
Toronto's solution to not hitting its goals for disposing of dead animals? Move the goalposts, Sept. 5
If animal services staff are unable to handle an increasing volume of calls for help with roadkill, perhaps the solution lies not with adjusting expected response times but with addressing the reasons why there has been an increase in deaths of our wild friends. Are people driving too fast? Are we removing habitats for wildlife and forcing them onto our streets in search of food and shelter? Are off-leash dogs chasing them into streets? Let's address these root causes so we're not just picking up dead animals faster but saving more of their lives in the first place.
Barbi Lazarus, Toronto
Who is the communist?
Recently, Donald Trump described Kamala Harris as a communist and a Marxist. Take it from someone who spent 22 years of his life under communist dictatorship - Trump knows nothing about communism. But if you consider that Project 2025 says "current federal workers to be replaced with people loyal to the president," it is the Republicans who are keen on how a communist dictatorship works - embrace and reward those who are willing to support the regressive regime and oppress, even punish, those who do not.
Francis J. Zita, Scarborough
City needs to deal with how construction interferes with traffic patterns
There are other traffic offences, Sept. 9
Mary Sullivan who visited Toronto from Lindsay is exactly right about traffic disorder and delays under the Gardiner. For residents, especially those east of the Don River, this has been going on for years since the Gardiner ramps were dismantled, and it will be many more before they are rebuilt. The city has tried to improve the situation with bollards, but it will only be fixed when traffic lights are programmed to deal with construction related traffic patterns, or even better, real time traffic circumstances. I see no evidence of the city willing to do so - anywhere in the city. Unless construction is going to stop, this has to change. Our quality of life, and our reputation, is at stake.
Rob Hatton, Toronto
CAPTION: Early childhood educator Zelinda Reinoso works with kindergarten students.

Document 401 of 500

Beer boondoggle is nothing to brag about

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/beer-boondoggle-is-nothing-brag-about/docview/3102303863/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Beer+boondoggle+is+nothing+to+brag+about&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Amazingly, Doug Ford is bringing people together over expanded alcohol sales.
The premier has miraculously united the two opposing sides in a symbiotic debate about his latest move to bring beer and wine into corner stores.
In one corner are Ford's Tories, cheerfully raising a glass to their supposedly life-changing achievement of boosting booze distribution.
In the other corner are the premier's opponents, desperately raising the alarm about the allegedly life-ending disaster of increased alcohol availability.
Neither group was attracting any attention - until recently, most Ontarians were blissfully oblivious. Now, together, they are now making common cause in creating a cause célèbre.
Today, beer in corner stores is a stale idea first trotted out 40 years ago by opposition leaders looking for attention. When David Peterson and later Mike Harris became premier, they found bigger issues facing the province.
All these years later, Ford can think of nothing more important. It's his signature issue politically (even if not personally - he's a teetotaller).
Ahead of a possible spring election, the premier has been touring Ontario touting his obsession. But Ford's Tories weren't gaining traction - until researchers and anti-alcohol activists raised the volume by warning of more drunkenness, illness and fatalities from expanded sales.
Now, on both sides of the divide, the premier and his opponents - aided and abetted by the media - are trying to convert an anticlimax into a controversy, turning a nonstory into a big deal.
Consider the premise that beer and wine will suddenly become both ubiquitous and iniquitous. In reality, the previous Liberal government of Kathleen Wynne broke up the unholy duopoly of the LCBO and the Beer Store, which had a stranglehold on sales, by giving a large piece of the action to hundreds of large supermarkets nearly a decade ago.
Those big supermarkets achieved far more economies of scale than small convenience stores can today. But it's worth recalling that the same scaremongering voices arguing against sales in convenience stores today were warning against letting it into supermarkets a decade ago.
Moreover, when COVID hit, Ford's government allowed thousands of already-licensed bars and restaurants to sell takeout alcohol. Yet there was no evidence of social upheaval then, just as there isn't any reliable predictor of it now.
In fact, governments of all political persuasions have long understood that the easiest way to raise revenue is to open more LCBO stores across the province. More outlets equals more inputs, and yet you never hear anyone complaining about new government-run stores.
Over time, Ontario has learned to restrain the temptation for prohibition or restriction. At the same time, we've seen that wider availability works just fine in other jurisdictions, not just in Quebec but across the continent and around the world.
All that said, convenience stores will not revolutionize the market, despite the pretensions and promises of this Progressive Conservative government. They have not started a stampede to stock beer, wine or pre-mixed cocktails, because they know the market for growth is limited.
Convenience stores are about convenience, not competitive pricing. You pay a premium for having a location at your corner, open late at night, offering small quantities in outlying locations with increased distribution costs and markups.
This isn't the 1980s, with corner stores on every corner. Today, supermarkets have more locations with longer hours than they did four decades ago, and the proliferation of cannabis stores has long since overtaken alcohol as a talking point.
That hasn't stopped the naysayers from saying no to the iterative convenience of corner stores. The more fuss they make, the more fun for Ford's Tories, because it makes their small beer look like a big deal.
In the premier's mind, this is a proof point for his 2022 campaign promise to "Get it Done" - on beer and wine as on homebuilding and road-building. But if booze is the best he can do, it's not saying much - and it's costing way too much.
To deliver on his ill-conceived and long-delayed pledge from the 2018 campaign, Ford had to pay off the Beer Store's ownership in order to get around the previous "master framework agreement" that phased in beer sales through 2025. The PC government paid $225 million outright, and is on the hook for hundreds of millions of dollars in potential ancillary costs, just so they buy themselves a 15-month head start - ahead of the early election Ford's campaign team wants for next spring.
If the teetotalling, bellowing Ford were in opposition, he'd be lampooning and lambasting an NDP or Liberal government for this beer boondoggle. Remarkably, he seems to have gotten away with it in power - even if he hasn't gotten much else done.
Get it done? The Greenbelt has come undone, highway construction is off track, and Metrolinx mass transit is derailed or delayed.
Beer is here belatedly, but the bottle is empty.
CREDIT: Martin Regg cohn OPINION

Subject: Alcohol; Highway construction; Beer; Convenience stores; Distribution costs; Supermarkets

Business indexing term: Subject: Convenience stores Distribution costs Supermarkets

Document 402 of 500

Singh 'blowing smoke,' premier says: Ford says he doesn't believe federal NDP leader will trigger election by voting against Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-blowing-smoke-premier-says/docview/3102303849/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+%27blowing+smoke%2C%27+premier+says%3A+Ford+says+he+doesn%27t+believe+federal+NDP+leader+will+trigger+election+by+voting+against+Liberals&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says the threat of an imminent federal election does not alter any plans for a potential provincial vote next year.
Blasting NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who last week dramatically ended his supply and confidence agreement with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority Liberal government, Ford predicted there would be no snap election in Ottawa.
"As far as I'm concerned, MP Singh is just blowing smoke," the premier told reporters during a campaign-style swing through Prince Edward County, where there's a Sept. 19 provincial byelection.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension and he was elected (in) ... 2019 and I'll be floored and I'll stand up here and apologize to all the greedy politicians if he calls the election early," said Ford.
"But let's face it, he wants his pension. He's not going anywhere until ... Oct. 20, 2025 because there's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money," he said, referring to the six-year threshold for federal MPs to qualify for a pension.
"So my bet is, he's not doing anything. He's just putting on a big show like he has for the last number of years there."
Singh dismissed the pension claim as "a ludicrous suggestion" in an interview Thursday with Vassy Kapelos on CTV's "Power Play."
"I'm obviously going to get re-elected in my riding (of Burnaby South), I'm not worried about that at all. This is a silly argument being brought about by Conservatives," the NDP leader said.
MPPs at Queen's Park have not had a pension plan since 1996 and, unlike federal MPs, who receive annual salary raises, provincial members' pay last increased in 2008.
Ford's salvo at Singh comes as the premier is ruminating about holding an Ontario election next spring, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
His Tories have worked closely with Trudeau's Liberals since the COVID-19 pandemic began in 2020 - especially on shared economic priorities like subsidies for electric vehicle manufacturers.
He emphasized "we haven't made that decision yet" about a 2025 Ontario election.
A federal campaign within the next six months, however, could complicate things for his Progressive Conservatives, who have been in power since 2018.
"I'll tell you, we aren't calling an election this year - and we're going to make sure that we continue with prosperity and creating jobs and opportunities for the people of Ontario," said Ford.
"We're going to continue giving opportunities to the regions just like this one," he said.
His comments came as he announced the province was giving the region up to $18.3 million toward waterworks infrastructure that will help build 3,895 new homes in Prince Edward County.
The cash is part of a $970-million "housing-enabling water systems fund" that Ford has been touting all summer across Ontario.
Voters in the riding of Bay of Quinte will be headed to the polls next week to elect a successor to Todd Smith, who resigned suddenly as education minister on Aug. 16 to take an executive job with nuclear power company CANDU.
To try to retain a seat the Tories have held since 2011, the governing party is fielding small businessman Tyler Allsopp, a Belleville city councillor.
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals have tapped fellow city councillor Sean Kelly, who is also a prominent radio morning show host.
Kelly has warned Belleville, which declared a state of emergency last February after 13 drug overdose calls to 911 in a one-hour period, needs more help from Queen's Park on coping with the opioid crisis.
Marit Stiles's New Democrats are running school board trustee Amanda Robertson, who said Monday that Ford's Tories are failing students in the riding.
"Students are missing school because there aren't enough (bus) drivers to get them there," said Robertson. "Our students, teachers, and communities deserve much better than this. We need to fix this broken system, and we need urgent investment in our schools. At the doors, people are telling me they want better."
Respiratory therapist Lori Borthwick is the Green candidate.
Last Wednesday, Singh jolted the national political landscape when he ripped up the accord to prop up the Liberals that had been in place since 2022.
In exchange for supporting the Grits in confidence votes, the NDP extracted government commitments on dental care, pharmacare and anti-scab legislation.
Ford's outburst against Singh comes despite their storied shared history.
Seven years ago, the two appeared together on the most popular episode of the TVO series "Political Blind Date," riding bicycles and sharing breakfast in a diner in Ford's riding.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Students; Politicians; Provincial elections; Federal elections-Canada; Politics; Special elections

Location: Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Ford, Doug

Title: Singh 'blowing smoke,' premier says:   Ford says he doesn't believe federal NDP leader will trigger election by voting against Liberals

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Source type: Newspaper

Language of publication: English

Document 403 of 500

More MPs admit anti-Trudeau tide rising

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/more-mps-admit-anti-trudeau-tide-rising/docview/3102303692/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+MPs+admit+anti-Trudeau+tide+rising&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's become too obvious to hide. Liberal MPs publicly acknowledged Monday that some of their constituents want Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to go. Using words like "too preachy" and "tired," a handful of MPs told the Star there is a clear appetite for change, and some worry the prime minister's unpopularity will affect their own bids for re-election.
There's "a sentiment of exasperation, I would say, in many people," offered Quebec MP Alexandra Mendès. "A lot of the comments bordered on, 'We are sick and tired of being preached to.'"
But while Mendès said Trudeau's presence is "not helpful," she and other Liberal MPs who are gathering for a caucus retreat this week in Nanaimo stopped short of calling for the prime minister to step down as their leader. "I personally don't think he should go, but my constituents definitely do, and that is the message they've been asking me to convey," she said.
"Some out there in the community certainly believe it's time for him to move on," said Nova Scotia MP Kody Blois. "There's others that actually think he needs to stay on the job."
"I still believe that we have the best plan to present to Canadians," said Ontario MP Francis Drouin, "but I think people have lost an emotional connection to the prime minister."
The calls for Trudeau to go are unlikely to lead to a full blowout during the Liberals' three days of meetings, and the prime minister has made it clear he plans to stick around for another term.
But there are now more MPs publicly willing to say what everyone has been hearing for the past year, and it may lead to more fireworks and tougher conversations behind closed doors this week.
Behind the scenes, there are also questions being asked about a surprise guest of honour - former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney, who, as the Star reported Friday, has been tapped to advise Trudeau and the Liberal party on economic matters. He is expected to brief MPs on Tuesday morning.
Carney's presence ruffled a few cabinet ministers last week, who were perplexed as to why Trudeau had asked a likely leadership rival to show up.
Had the prime minister called in the big gun because he had little faith in his own front bench? Was he designating Carney his dauphin? Was this the beginning of the jockeying over leadership? Why was Carney going to Nanaimo? These were questions asked over and over again.
Attuned to their anxiety, Carney called a handful of ministers over the weekend, hoping to smooth some feathers.
Trudeau's team hopes Carney's presence demonstrates to Canadians that the prime minister can still attract big names to the Liberal fold - that while the news might be about staff departures, impressive Canadians like Carney are willing to get engaged with the Grits and lend the party their brand's cache.
For his part, the Carney calculations appear to be focused on dispelling any notion he backed away from the party after being offered the role of finance minister - and yes, although no one will publicly say it, it's also about leadership positioning.
A Liberal party statement announcing his new role as chair of the Liberal "leader's task force on economic growth" billed Carney as a "globally acclaimed Canadian economist," "a public policy leader," a "sustainability advocate, and author."
It said Carney would help develop and shape ideas for the next phase of Canada's strategy for near and longer-term economic growth and productivity and help the party create a "pragmatic, focused and high-impact vision for Canada's economic success."
The Liberals plan to send Carney on a cross-country tour to hear from Canadians in different sectors, and then have him report back to its election platform committee.
It's easy to see why those around the cabinet table who want Trudeau's job might be miffed by this challenger's exposure.
Reaction from MPs to Carney's presence in Nanaimo was mixed.
"Whatever. I mean, we'll see what he does. I don't think he's going to be the messiah," said Drouin.
Several fear Carney is too much of a technocrat - that he resembles former leader Michael Ignatieff - and that they would flounder under his leadership. Others looked forward to Tuesday's presentation and his ability to respond to media questions afterward.
"There's been a lot of talk about Mark Carney in one way or another, so it's great that he's making time to come out and share his thoughts with us," said Ontario MP Ruby Sahota.
The only parliamentarian who seemed agitated by Carney's presence was Conservative House leader Andrew Scheer, who flew all the way out to Nanaimo to tell reporters "it doesn't matter what happens at this retreat."
"It doesn't matter who is leading the Liberal party ... It doesn't matter what kind of paint they slap on the Liberal brand," Scheer insisted. Neither Carney nor any other Liberal cabinet minister would change the direction the country is headed in now, he claimed.
If Scheer hoped to convince reporters his party isn't worried about a Liberal leadership change, he accomplished the opposite. It's no secret that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh would rather run against Trudeau than against another Liberal who could represent change from a tired brand.
Change is what Liberal MPs have on their minds, whether that's changing the prime minister or changing communication strategy - something they've long griped about. (It's also far easier to champion that than calling for the leader to go, and plunging the party into disunity.)
"Right now," said Blois, change "would be the prospect of a Pierre Poilievre prime ministership, and there's a lot of my constituents that are concerned about the direction he would take the country."
Blois, Drouin and others want the party to focus Canadians' attention on Poilievre's record. There is a lot of material to mine, from his disparaging comments about Indigenous Peoples to his work on the so-called Fair Elections Act.
And on that front, the team around Trudeau is listening.
Their major challenge, however, is financing - how much money to spend to get bang for their buck when their opponents have so much more than they do. So far this year, the Conservatives have raised $20.53 million, nearly three times more than the Liberals, with $6.87 million, from nearly twice as many donors.
Two years ago, the Grits shied away from spending money. Now, with an election on the horizon, their calculations may be shifting.
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Document 404 of 500

City should use cameras to move traffic

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-should-use-cameras-move-traffic/docview/3102303574/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+should+use+cameras+to+move+traffic&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There have been a lot of bad stories about Toronto drivers lately. They've killed cyclists. They've run down pedestrians at pedestrian crossovers. They've ignored transit lanes and blocked intersections and somehow gone the wrong way down highway off-ramps.
But there is some good news amidst all this bad behaviour: it turns out there's a ridiculously simple and obvious thing that would help stop it.
It's cameras. The answer is cameras.
Lots and lots of automated enforcement cameras.
When it comes to traffic enforcement, the data suggests automated cameras are much more efficient and much more effective at catching bad behaviour than police officers.
That's likely to be especially true in our city, where some police officers seem to regard traffic enforcement as an unwanted chore. In an article last week by the Star's Mahdis Habibinia about drivers who get away with "blocking the box" - stopping in the middle of an intersection during a red light - former Toronto police officer Matthew Wood said there's a culture amongst officers that prefers "action-oriented, high-adrenalin, high-octane" calls and does not consider issuing traffic tickets to be "real police work."
Acting Supt. Matt Moyer, the head of the Toronto police Traffic Services unit, also pumped the brakes on the idea of a police crackdown of drivers who block the box. He noted that the police generally need to block a lane of traffic for an extended period while pulling over a driver to issue a ticket, so a crackdown may not actually improve traffic conditions.
I'm not thrilled with either of those explanations. In my mind, any would-be candidate to be a Toronto police officer who says they want the job for the "high adrenalin" thrill of it should immediately get screened out of consideration. And I've never really understood why, in my experience, cops spend an eternity sitting in their cars during traffic stops. It feels like the process could be sped up significantly with a bit of urgency.
But again, the good news is that automated cameras don't have an adrenal system and are capable of issuing tickets in a literal snap.
Cameras work. I recently stumbled across a segment from a local news station in Washington, D.C., reporting that cameras mounted on buses have resulted in a whopping 147,251 citations to drivers for improperly using bus lanes since last November.
And D.C. is not unique. New York City has long had bus-mounted cameras to catch bus lane violators through their Automated Bus Lane Enforcement (ABLE) program. In June, the ABLE program was expanded to also issue tickets to drivers who are double parked. Chicago's city council approved an ambitious "Smart Streets" pilot last year that will see drivers mailed tickets when they're caught on camera parking in bike lanes, across crosswalks and in other no-parking areas.
Compared to these cities, Toronto is way behind on automated enforcement. Sure, city hall complemented its long-standing red-light camera program with photo radar cameras for speeding in 2019, and sure, they've been effective at issuing tickets and slowing drivers down, but city hall has been slow to roll out new ones. The program started with 50 cameras in 2020. These days, there are 75. Another 75 are on order. It's progress - but it's slow progress.
And more importantly, there's not been enough movement toward getting cameras that can enforce other traffic violations, like driving in transit lanes or blocking the box. That's despite TTC streetcars running with active external cameras since 2019, and the city's speed camera supplier - Verra Mobility (formerly Redflex Systems) - offering tech that can also catch box blockers and transit lane violators.
At city hall, the explanation for slow progress is usually to blame Queen's Park. But while it's certainly frustrating that Premier Doug Ford government hasn't yet passed legislation allowing vehicle owners to be charged for offences like blocking the box even if they weren't behind the wheel, I'm tiring of that excuse.
If the legislation isn't yet in place to allow automated ticketing of more traffic violations, I see no reason why the city couldn't activate more cameras now to at least capture better data - and to ensure we've got the tech in place to immediately start issuing tickets whenever Ford gets around to changing the rules.
This would mirror the approach taken by former mayor John Tory when he was advocating for photo radar. When Ford was dragging his feet on enabling the regulations to allow the use of cameras, Tory pushed for council to move forward with the cameras anyway - almost daring Ford to stand in the way. After cameras captured some drivers exceeding 100 km/h in school zones, it got a lot harder to say no.
With city council set to debate a congestion management strategy later this fall, Mayor Olivia Chow should take a page from the same playbook: push forward aggressively and dare the provincial government to block it. Don't accept a slow small-scale pilot project. Other cities have already tested this for us. And with the police abdicating responsibility, it's so clear what Toronto needs to do to hold drivers to account: get the cameras rolling.
CAPTION: The automated speed cameras in Toronto have been effective at issuing tickets and slowing drivers down, but city hall has been slow to roll out new ones, writes Matt Elliott.Richard LautensToronto Star File Photo
CREDIT: Matt Elliott OPINION

Subject: Speed limits; Automation; Enforcement; Traffic violations; Traffic police; Digital cameras; Traffic accidents & safety

Letters: Ford government risking the reading skills of thousands of kindergarten students, Human Rights Report says, Sept. 5 Toronto's solution to not hitting its goals for disposing of dead animals? Move the goalposts, Sept. 5 There are other traffic offences, Sept. 9

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters/docview/3102303466/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Ford+government+risking+the+reading+skills+of+thousands+of+kindergarten+students%2C+Human+Rights+Report+says%2C+Sept.+5+Toronto%27s+solution+to+not+hitting+its+goals+for+disposing+of+dead+animals%3F+Move+the+goalposts%2C+Sept.+5+There+are+other+traffic+offences%2C+Sept.+9&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: English is a tough language to learn
Ford government risking the reading skills of thousands of kindergarten students, Human Rights Report says, Sept. 5
As a former primary teacher with the Toronto District School Board who retired just before the release of the recent report on reading, I always taught phonics in kindergarten. I knew how important it was to all learners, especially immigrant children, children with dyslexia and those with special needs. Unfortunately, I often found that the teaching of phonics would not be carried on by many teachers in other primary, junior and intermediate grades. Scientific research demonstrates that the teaching of phonics is critical because English is a complex language to master. Letter sounds and letter combinations have to be taught. The critical piece in reading and writing success is that phonics needs to be taught systematically across the grades from kindergarten to Grade 8 and in age-appropriate ways. This means that the principal needs to take the lead in making sure that this happens. I never once had a principal who provided leadership in phonics education and this needs to change if all Ontario students are to have the opportunity to become successful readers and writers of English.
Joanne Clarke, Toronto
This is an unfortunate situation. It is also unfortunate that the "Reading Recovery Program" in the York board was discontinued a little while ago. This program took young students who were having trouble with reading and provided extra support with a dedicated reading recovery teacher, prior to Grade 2. Maybe the program will be reintroduced under a new name.
Joel Hertz, Toronto
As an educator in kindergarten for more than 10 years, I can say we have to remember the different stages of growth for kindergarten along with variations in age. Junior students are four years old while the seniors are five. However, I remember celebrating three birthdays over a Christmas break with one student turning four after Christmas, another turning five just before New Years and a third student turning six on Jan. 1. The developmental learning of these three ages is critical when implementing the kindergarten curriculum. Four-year-olds are beginning to recognize symbols (letters and numbers), five-year-olds are learning letter sounds and "stretching" words together, while six-year-olds are capable of reading simple sentences phonetically. These students all learn at different times throughout their kindergarten career. Reading/letter recognition should be fun. Reading/letter recognition should be ongoing. These young students will grasp the ability of reading/letter recognition when their developing brains are capable and academically ready.
Melanie Kohek, Whitby
We still need electoral reform
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh's latest repositioning manoeuvre is an attempt to get the best for his party in the context of our flawed winner-take-all electoral system. His move will cause many voters to recalculate their own strategic voting patterns to fit the new situation. But with all that strategic voting, our current electoral system is never a true representation of the true will of the people. We don't have a truly representative democracy. To try to correct that, I support the Charter Challenge for Fair Voting project, which is arguing that we all have a charter right to effective representation and equal treatment. We all have a right to vote for our first choice and then actually (effectively) get the proportion of representation that we voted for with proportional representation.
Boyd Reimer, Toronto
The world will still turn if Poilievre is PM
I'm amazed at just how many of your readers are absolutely convinced that the world will end if the Conservatives get into power. Do they actually think all is OK in Canada under the Liberals? One out of every 20 families are now using a food bank. That's five per cent of Canadian families. Pretty much every city and large town has a tent city because people are homeless. This happened under the Liberals' watch. Are you all OK with this? I'm not. The carbon program has put the price of everything up by a lot more than normal inflation. That's OK too? How can things possibly get worse under the Conservatives? Especially when they want to fix these things. I'd really like to know.
Jeremy Thornton,
Ballentrae, Ont.
What are
the root causes?
Toronto's solution to not
hitting its goals for disposing of dead animals? Move the goalposts, Sept. 5
If animal services staff are unable to handle an increasing volume of calls for help with roadkill, perhaps the solution lies not with adjusting expected response times but with addressing the reasons why there has been an increase in deaths of our wild friends. Are people driving too fast? Are we removing habitats for wildlife and forcing them onto our streets in search of food and shelter? Let's address these root causes so we're not just picking up dead animals faster but saving more of their lives in the first place.
Barbi Lazarus, Toronto
Who is the communist?
Recently, Donald Trump described Kamala Harris as a communist and a Marxist. Take it from someone who spent 22 years of his life under communist dictatorship - Trump knows nothing about communism. But if you consider that Project 2025 says "current federal workers to be replaced with people loyal to the president," it is the Republicans who are keen on how a communist dictatorship works - embrace and reward those who are willing to support the regressive regime and oppress, even punish, those who do not.
Francis J. Zita, Scarborough
City needs
to deal with construction
There are other traffic
offences, Sept. 9
Mary Sullivan who visited Toronto from Lindsay, Ont., is right about traffic disorder and delays under the Gardiner. For residents, especially those east of the Don River, this has been going on for years since the Gardiner ramps were dismantled, and it will be many more before they are rebuilt. The city has tried to improve the situation with bollards, but it will only be fixed when traffic lights are programmed to deal with construction-related traffic patterns, or even better, real time traffic circumstances. I see no evidence of the city willing to do so - anywhere in the city. Unless construction is going to stop, this has to change.
Rob Hatton, Toronto

Document 406 of 500

NDP MPP making jump to federal level

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-mpp-making-jump-federal-level/docview/3102303356/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+MPP+making+jump+to+federal+level&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A second Ontario New Democrat MPP is looking to make the leap to federal politics even though provincial parties are scrambling to nominate candidates amid expectations Premier Doug Ford will call an early election.
Monique Taylor, who has represented Hamilton Mountain in the legislature since 2011, said Monday she will seek the NDP nomination for the riding in the next federal vote.
"This decision has not come easily ... but under (Prime Minister) Justin Trudeau, things have gotten worse," Taylor, a lifelong Hamiltonian who has served as children and social services critic under Ontario New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, told supporters.
"Seniors who have built our communities are not living the retirement dream they had expected. The cost of living is eating up more and more of their pension cheques ... and they live in terror of needing long-term care."
Hamilton Mountain is held by Liberal MP Lisa Hepfner, a former television reporter for CHCH, who was elected in 2021.
"It's good to see there are still women willing to run in this climate," Hepfner said from Nanaimo, B.C., where the Liberal caucus is meeting this week.
"I see Monique at community events all the time. I don't think it will be acrimonious."
Taylor's move comes just days after federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh ended an agreement to support Trudeau's Liberals in the minority Parliament, which could result in a federal election earlier than one slated for October 2025.
Two months ago, Ottawa Centre New Democrat MPP Joel Harden signalled he will seek the federal party's nomination in his riding, now represented by Liberal MP Yasir Naqvi, a former Ontario cabinet minster who last year ran for the provincial Liberal leadership won by Bonnie Crombie.
"The stakes couldn't be higher," Harden said as he announced his bid and set up a website for it. "The climate crisis, the cost of living trap, and Canadians' trust in government all need a community approach."
Should they win the nominations, neither Taylor nor Harden would have to resign as MPPs until a federal election is called.
MPs earn a base salary of $203,100 versus $116,500 for Ontario MPPs, who do not have a pension plan - unlike their federal counterparts.
CAPTION: NDP Hamilton Mountain MPP Monique Taylor said Monday she will seek the NDP nomination for the riding in the next federal vote.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Document 407 of 500

Doug Ford says Jagmeet Singh is a 'greedy politician' who won't topple Trudeau government

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-says-jagmeet-singh-is-greedy-politician/docview/3102544128/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+says+Jagmeet+Singh+is+a+%27greedy+politician%27+who+won%27t+topple+Trudeau+government&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford says the threat of an imminent federal election does not alter any plans for a potential provincial vote next year.
Blasting NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who last week dramatically ended his supply and confidence agreement with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority Liberal government, Ford predicted there would be no snap election in Ottawa.
"As far as I'm concerned, MP Singh is just blowing smoke," the premier told reporters during a campaign-style swing through Prince Edward County, where there's a Sept. 19 provincial byelection.
"There's nothing more important to these greedy politicians than collecting a pension and he was elected (in) ... 2019 and I'll be floored and I'll stand up here and apologize to all the greedy politicians if he calls the election early," said Ford.
"But let's face it, he wants his pension. He's not going anywhere until ... Oct. 20, 2025 because there's him and a whole bunch of other politicians that want to grab the taxpayers' money," he said, referring to the six-year threshold for federal MPs to qualify for a pension.
"So my bet is, he's not doing anything. He's just putting on a big show like he has for the last number of years there."
Singh dismissed the pension claim as "a ludicrous suggestion" in an interview Thursday with Vassy Kapelos on CTV's Power Play.
"I'm obviously going to get re-elected in my riding (of Burnaby South), I'm not worried about that at all. This is a silly argument being brought about by Conservatives," the NDP leader said.
MPPs at Queen's Park have not had a pension plan since 1996 and, unlike federal MPs, who receive annual salary raises, provincial members' pay last increased in 2008.
Ford's salvo at Singh comes as the premier is ruminating about holding an Ontario election next spring, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote.
His Tories have worked closely with Trudeau's Liberals since the COVID-19 pandemic began in 2020 - especially on shared economic priorities like subsidies for electric vehicle manufacturers.
He emphasized "we haven't made that decision yet" about a 2025 Ontario election.
A federal campaign within the next six months, however, could complicate things for his Progressive Conservatives, who have been in power since 2018.
"I'll tell you, we aren't calling an election this year - and we're going to make sure that we continue with prosperity and creating jobs and opportunities for the people of Ontario," said Ford.
"We're going to continue giving opportunities to the regions just like this one," he said.
His comments came as he announced the province was giving the region up to $18.3 million toward waterworks infrastructure that will help build 3,895 new homes in Prince Edward County.
The cash is part of a $970-million "housing-enabling water systems fund" that Ford has been touting all summer across Ontario.
Voters in the riding of Bay of Quinte will be headed to the polls next week to elect a successor to Todd Smith, who resigned suddenly as education minister on Aug. 16 to take an executive job with nuclear power company CANDU.
To try to retain a seat the Tories have held since 2011, the governing party is fielding small businessman Tyler Allsopp, a Belleville city councillor.
Bonnie Crombie's Liberals have tapped fellow city councillor Sean Kelly, who is also a prominent radio morning show host.
Kelly has warned Belleville, which declared a state of emergency last February after 13 drug overdose calls to 911 in a one-hour period, needs more help from Queen's Park on coping with the opioid crisis.
Marit Stiles's New Democrats are running school board trustee Amanda Robertson, who said Monday that Ford's Tories are failing students in the riding.
"Students are missing school because there aren't enough (bus) drivers to get them there," said Robertson.
"Our students, teachers, and communities deserve much better than this. We need to fix this broken system, and we need urgent investment in our schools. At the doors, people are telling me they want better."
Respiratory therapist Lori Borthwick is the Green candidate.
Last Wednesday, Singh jolted the national political landscape when he ripped up the accord to prop up the Liberals that had been in place since 2022.
In exchange for supporting the Grits in confidence votes, the NDP extracted government commitments on dental care, pharmacare and anti-scab legislation.
Ford's outburst against Singh comes despite their storied shared history.
Seven years ago, the two appeared together on the most popular episode of the TVO series Political Blind Date, riding bicycles and sharing breakfast in a diner in Ford's riding.
CAPTION: Jagmeet Singh and Doug Ford appeared together in an episode of the TVO series Political Blind Date in 2017.

Another New Democrat MPP will try to run in the next federal election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/another-new-democrat-mpp-will-try-run-next/docview/3102544061/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+New+Democrat+MPP+will+try+to+run+in+the+next+federal+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A second Ontario New Democrat MPP is looking to make the leap to federal politics even though provincial parties are scrambling to nominate candidates amid expectations Premier Doug Ford will call an early election.
Monique Taylor, who has represented Hamilton Mountain in the legislature since 2011, said Monday she will seek the NDP nomination for the riding in the next federal vote.
"This decision has not come easily ... but under (Prime Minister) Justin Trudeau, things have gotten worse," Taylor, a lifelong Hamiltonian who has served as children and social services critic under Ontario New Democrat Leader Marit Stiles, told supporters.
"Seniors who have built our communities are not living the retirement dream they had expected. The cost of living is eating up more and more of their pension cheques ... and they live in terror of needing long-term care."
Hamilton Mountain is held by Liberal MP Lisa Hepfner, a former television reporter for CHCH, who was elected in 2021.
"It's good to see there are still women willing to run in this climate," Hepfner said from Nanaimo, B.C., where the Liberal caucus is meeting this week.
"I see Monique at community events all the time. I don't think it will be acrimonious."
Taylor's move comes just days after federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh ended an agreement to support Trudeau's Liberals in the minority Parliament, which could result in a federal election earlier than one slated for October 2025.
Two months ago, Ottawa Centre New Democrat MPP Joel Harden signalled he will seek the federal party's nomination in his riding, now represented by Liberal MP Yasir Naqvi, a former Ontario cabinet minster who last year ran for the provincial Liberal leadership won by Bonnie Crombie.
"The stakes couldn't be higher," Harden said as he announced his bid and set up a website for it. "The climate crisis, the cost of living trap, and Canadians' trust in government all need a community approach."
Should they win the nominations, neither Taylor nor Harden would have to resign as MPPs until a federal election is called.
There is a major difference in salaries for serving in the federal and Ontario parliaments, with MPP's wages frozen since 2008.
MPs earn a base salary of $203,100 versus $116,500 for MPPs, who do not have a pension plan - unlike their federal counterparts.
CAPTION: NDP MPP Monique Taylor is shown in the Ontario legislature on Feb. 20, 2019.

Subject: Nominations; Leadership; Cost of living

Document 409 of 500

'Too preachy': Liberal MPs admit some of their constituents want Justin Trudeau gone

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/too-preachy-liberal-mps-admit-some-their/docview/3102303387/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Too+preachy%27%3A+Liberal+MPs+admit+some+of+their+constituents+want+Justin+Trudeau+gone&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: NANAIMO, B.C. - It's become too obvious to hide. Liberal MPs publicly acknowledged Monday that some of their constituents want Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to go. Using words like "too preachy" and "tired," a handful of MPs told the Star there is a clear appetite for change, and some worry the prime minister's unpopularity will affect their own bids for re-election.
There's "a sentiment of exasperation, I would say, in many people," offered Quebec MP Alexandra Mendès. "A lot of the comments bordered on, 'We are sick and tired of being preached to.'"
But while Mendès said Trudeau's presence is "not helpful," she and other Liberal MPs who are gathering for a caucus retreat this week in Nanaimo stopped short of calling for the prime minister to step down as their leader. "I personally don't think he should go, but my constituents definitely do, and that is the message they've been asking me to convey," she said.
"Some out there in the community certainly believe it's time for him to move on," said Nova Scotia MP Kody Blois. "There's others that actually think he needs to stay on the job."
"I still believe that we have the best plan to present to Canadians," offered Ontario MP Francis Drouin, "but I think people have lost an emotional connection to the prime minister."
The calls for Trudeau to go are unlikely to lead to a full blowout during the Liberals' three days of meetings, and the prime minister has made it clear he plans to stick around for another term. But there are now more MPs publicly willing to say what everyone has been hearing for the past year, and it may lead to more fireworks and tougher conversations behind closed doors this week.
Behind the scenes, there are also questions being asked about a surprise guest of honour - former Bank of Canada governor Mark Carney, who, as the Star reported Friday, has been tapped to advise Trudeau and the Liberal party on economic matters. He is expected to brief MPs Tuesday morning.
Carney's presence ruffled a few cabinet ministers last week, who were perplexed as to why Trudeau had asked a likely leadership rival to show up. Had the prime minister called in the big gun because he had little faith in his own front bench? Was he designating Carney his dauphin? Was this the beginning of the jockeying over leadership? Why was Carney going to Nanaimo? These were questions asked over and over again.
Attuned to their anxiety, Carney called a handful of ministers over the weekend, hoping to smooth some feathers.
Trudeau's team hopes Carney's presence demonstrates to Canadians that the prime minister can still attract big names to the Liberal fold - that while the news might be about staff departures, impressive Canadians like Carney are willing to get engaged with the Grits and lend the party their brand's cache.
For his part, the Carney calculations appear to be focused on dispelling any notion that he backed away from the party after being offered the role of finance minister - and yes, although no one will publicly say it, it's also about leadership positioning.
A Liberal party statement announcing his new role as chair of the Liberal "leader's task force on economic growth" billed Carney as a "globally acclaimed Canadian economist," "a public policy leader," a "sustainability advocate, and author." It said Carney would help develop and shape ideas for the next phase of Canada's strategy for near- and longer-term economic growth and productivity and help the party create a "pragmatic, focused, and high-impact vision for Canada's economic success."
The Liberals plan to send Carney on a cross-country tour to hear from Canadians in different sectors, and then have him report back to its election platform committee.
It's easy to see why those around the cabinet table who want Trudeau's job might be miffed by this challenger's exposure.
Reaction from MPs to Carney's presence in Nanaimo was mixed.
"Whatever. I mean, we'll see what he does. I don't think he's going to be the messiah," said Drouin.
Several fear Carney is too much of a technocrat - that he resembles former leader Michael Ignatieff - and that they would flounder under his leadership. Others looked forward to Tuesday's presentation and his ability to respond to media questions afterward.
"There's been a lot of talk about Mark Carney in one way or another, so it's great that he's making time to come out and share his thoughts with us," said Ontario MP Ruby Sahota.
The only parliamentarian who seemed agitated by Carney's presence was Conservative House leader Andrew Scheer, who flew all the way out to Nanaimo to tell reporters that "it doesn't matter what happens at this retreat."
"It doesn't matter who is leading the Liberal party ... It doesn't matter what kind of paint they slap on the Liberal brand," Scheer insisted. Neither Carney nor any other Liberal cabinet minister would change the direction the country is headed in now, he claimed.
If Scheer hoped to convince reporters that his party isn't worried about a Liberal leadership change, he accomplished the opposite. It's no secret that Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh would rather run against Trudeau than against another Liberal who could represent change from a tired brand .
Change is what Liberal MPs have on their minds, whether that's changing the prime minister or changing communication strategy - something they've long griped about. (It's also far easier to champion that than calling for the leader to go, and plunging the party into disunity.)
"Right now," said Blois, change "would be the prospect of a Pierre Poilievre prime ministership, and there's a lot of my constituents that are concerned about the direction he would take the country."
Blois, Drouin and others want the party to focus Canadians' attention on Poilievre's record. There is a lot of material to mine from his disparaging comments about Indigenous Peoples to his work on the so-called Fair Elections Act.
And on that front, the team around Trudeau is listening.
Their major challenge, however, is financing - how much money to spend to get bang for their buck when their opponents have so much more than they do. So far this year, the Conservatives have raised $20.53 million, nearly three times more than the Liberals, with $6.87 million, from nearly twice as many donors.
Two years ago, the Grits shied away from spending money. Now, with an election on the horizon, their calculations may be shifting.
CAPTION: Liberals convened in Nanaimo to strategize for the upcoming election year. Still stinging from a devastating byelection loss earlier this summer, the caucus is now also reeling from news that its national campaign director has resigned and the party can no longer count on the NDP to stave off an early election. (Sep. 9, 2024 / The Canadian Press)Prime Minister Justin Trudeau answers reporters' questions following an announcement in Rocky Harbour, Nfld., on Sept. 4, 2024.
