Document 410 of 500

Pickering officials concerned over 'dangerous rhetoric' of city councillor Lisa Robinson

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pickering-officials-concerned-over-dangerous/docview/3102058382/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pickering+officials+concerned+over+%27dangerous+rhetoric%27+of+city+councillor+Lisa+Robinson&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Elected officials in Pickering are asking the province for stronger powers to address the "dangerous rhetoric" of one of their colleagues, after she defended her presence at an event where some participants wore swastikas.
In a letter to Premier Doug Ford and Municipal Affairs Minister Paul Calandra dated Sept. 6, Mayor Kevin Ashe and five of Pickering's six councillors expressed "serious concerns" about Coun. Lisa Robinson's alleged "connections to Nazi sympathizers."
Robinson has denied the allegations, and described them as "slanderous."
"The City is deeply troubled by the potential impact any association between publicly elected officials and neo-Nazi supporters may have on the safety of our residents and neighbours, particularly within the Jewish community," read the letter, which said Robinson had inflamed the situation by "clumsily" attempting to downplay the swastika as "a tribal symbol with no connection to Nazism."
The group told the province Robinson's behaviour, which comes amid an increase in reports of antisemitic incidents, "reinforces the urgent need for a stronger legislative response." They asked the premier and minister to consider legislative changes that would allow municipalities to impose stricter penalties on councillors "whose actions foster hate and division," including removal from office.
The letter marks the latest episode in a protracted conflict between Robinson, who was elected city councillor for Ward 1 in 2022, and other officials in the municipality of 100,000 just east of Toronto.
She has faced criticism for opposing Pride events and Black History Month, and her colleagues accuse her and her supporters, some of whom are linked with alt-right groups involved in the Freedom Convoy, of hijacking meetings and even making them feel unsafe.
She and her backers claim they're fighting corruption and government overreach, and that the mayor and council are trying to silence dissent.
Ashe and the other five councillors previously wrote to the province asking for tougher penalties for members, after Robinson's July appearance on a webcast hosted by a man who in 2021 pleaded guilty to a hate crime in connection with anti-Muslim posts.
Last year, Robinson's pay was suspended for a total of 90 days after two integrity commissioner investigations determined she promoted homophobic and transphobic attitudes, and posted bullying messages to Facebook. Robinson alleged the commissioner was biased against her.
The latest incident relates to her appearance at an event in Ottawa last weekend, where according to posts on social media, she was seen talking with a group that included people wearing T-shirts emblazoned with swastikas.
In a Sept. 7 letter of her own to Ford and Calandra, Robinson accused the mayor and the rest of council of spreading "blatant falsehoods" about the event for political gain.
According to a copy of the letter Robinson shared with the Star, she said she didn't formally attend the Ottawa event, but "stumbled upon" it while returning to her hotel. She described it as a "Cowboys and Indians Peace Talk" intended to improve relations between "Indigenous Peoples and settlers."
"I was briefly introduced to individuals present, but I left shortly after," she wrote.
According to Robinson, although she later learned that one attendee may have had a swastika tattoo, "at no point did I observe any tattoos or symbols on these individuals" while at the event. Asked about photos that show some people in the group had swastikas on their clothing, Robinson told the Star she didn't look at the "intricate details" of their shirts.
While denying ties to the event, Robinson wrote in the letter that the symbol the mayor and councillors objected to was "not a swastika, but an ancient one commonly used in Native American, Hindu, and Buddhist cultures" that had been "misappropriated." She said it would be "deeply irresponsible" to claim that "every appearance of a similar shape is linked to Nazism."
"The slanderous claim that I have any association with Nazi sympathizers is categorically false," she continued, alleging that an "innocent encounter" was being misrepresented as part of an "ongoing smear campaign."
In an interview, Ashe rejected Robinson's explanation of the swastikas at the event as non-offensive.
"Whenever you think she can't go lower, she goes lower," he said.
"The reality is, most people associate the swastika with the atrocities of the Holocaust," he said. "And when you have a publicly elected official suggesting that the use of this hateful symbol is fine - it's not fine."
The mayor said he wants Queen's Park to amend the Municipal Act to provide a mechanism that would allow a councillor to be removed by either the city integrity commissioner or municipal affairs minister, or through a judicial process. He said he wouldn't support changes that would enable council members to eject one of their own.
Ashe said Calandra has signalled he's looking into Pickering officials' previous request, but he had yet to hear back about Friday's letter.
The minister and premier didn't immediately return a request for comment Sunday.
CAPTION: Mayor Kevin Ashe and five of the city's six councillors expressed "serious concerns" about Coun.Lisa Robinson's alleged "connections to Nazi sympathizers."

Document 411 of 500

'He's not getting the message': Justin Trudeau shrugs off the naysayers as he heads into a Liberal caucus retreat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/hes-not-getting-message-justin-trudeau-shrugs-off/docview/3102058165/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27He%27s+not+getting+the+message%27%3A+Justin+Trudeau+shrugs+off+the+naysayers+as+he+heads+into+a+Liberal+caucus+retreat&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The summer ride is over. So too is the Justin Trudeau listening tour.
The prime minister spent much of two months meeting in small groups or individually with nervous Liberal MPs, many angry after the June loss of a supposedly safe Toronto seat to the Conservatives.
Trudeau also spent two months running, paddling, boxing and bounding from one summer festival to another, taking thousands of selfies with Canadians who appeared not to have read polls about how disliked he is.
So what did the prime minister take away from it all?
His practiced answers at a cabinet retreat and again this week after his co-operation deal with the NDP fell apart show clearly he does not believe he is the problem.
Rather, Trudeau believes he is the solution.
In public and in private, Trudeau has expressed his determination to stay at the helm of his party through to the next election.
And he believes he now has most of his caucus behind him.
The Star spoke to more than a dozen Liberals inside and outside the Prime Minister's Office, cabinet and caucus, and agreed to requests they not be identified in order to gauge where they believe the prime minister's head is at.
All said Trudeau plans to stay the course.
Not all are thrilled with that.
But most said Trudeau is newly energized for the fight ahead. And the death of the NDP co-operation deal doesn't bother the prime minister or many of those around him.
If anything, after his summer listening tour, Trudeau is, surprisingly, "more emboldened," said one Liberal.
Another said, "I think there is a path to victory that he's confident about. Obviously it's not what it was in 2015 by any stretch of the imagination, but it's there, and I don't think anyone doubts his capacity to win the last two weeks of the campaign."
Trudeau, several said, is deeply concerned about the rise of a right-wing populism that threatens Canadian social programs and cohesion. He sees Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as an agent of that.
Some said Trudeau believes he must stay on to counter Poilievre, even in the face of polls showing he is leading the Liberal party to likely disaster.
"He's not getting the message. He thinks he's the only one who can fight for the progressive agenda in Canada against the big, evil Conservatives," said one.
Some Liberals genuinely believe Trudeau is the best leader the party's got for that fight, and all they need to do in the months ahead is a better job of prosecuting the case against Poilievre.
They and the prime minister are relying on a conviction that the more Canadians see of Poilievre, the more they will dislike him.
Trudeau also thinks that most of the negative spin about his leadership comes from "groupthink" in the Ottawa press gallery.
That there is an unending stream of negative stories is certainly true, because there is a seemingly endless stream of polls documenting the mood of Canadians.
So what has Trudeau's own outreach taught him?
The prime minister has often talked in the past about how he discounts the excesses of both his "haters" and admirers.
Yet after a bruising early summer, where MPs spoke openly about their concerns to reporters (although only one, New Brunswick's Wayne Long, agreed to be identified), Trudeau seems to have refuelled his tank on the encouragement he encountered in crowds.
From the Vancouver Pride parade to the St. John's Regatta, Trudeau has told those around him that people were eager and happy to meet "the prime minister" not least because he brings "the celebrity factor."
The son of a former prime minister who grew up in the public spotlight, he creates a buzz most places he shows up.
Yet one Liberal cautioned it's not an indication Canadians want him to stay. "He confuses the selfie with support and it's not," they said. "Some of it may be support, but the selfie is largely celebrity-driven and not politically driven."
A clear sign that Trudeau's public relations and security staff worried he would encounter protesters were Trudeau's carefully sanitized public itineraries. Early in the summer, his office stopped issuing timely public notices of where the prime minister would be, sometimes concealing Trudeau's whereabouts until after an event was over.
And there was one telling, unscripted moment in Trudeau's summer travels.
In Sault Ste-Marie, a steelworker declined to shake Trudeau's hand, complained about taxes, the lack of a family doctor, the cost of dental care and about the "lazy neighbour" who doesn't work but lives the same life he, the ordinary worker, does.
Trudeau engaged the man and quickly tried to talk his way out of the awkward encounter, but the worker wanted no part of it and looked forward to Trudeau not being around "in another year."
It's a strategy: talking your way through difficult meetings, and talking over your critics.
It's one some say Trudeau has employed in caucus and in other tense meetings.
Whether it's the strategy the Liberal leader intends to employ at this week's national caucus retreat in Nanaimo - the first face-to-face gathering of all Liberal MPs since the loss of Toronto-St. Paul's - could determine whether his MPs think Trudeau has truly listened.
It may also determine what face they put on as they emerge before the cameras.
One senior government official said the Nanaimo caucus meeting is important because people will compare notes, and express their views very directly to the prime minister, the PMO, and to each other. Yet that same Liberal - along with others - is convinced the vast majority of the caucus is united behind Trudeau leading them into the next campaign, and will be focused on how to win that election, not on leadership changes.
So will Trudeau offer a major course correction - say, on the Middle East, the economic direction of the country, or the carbon pricing regime that has caused him endless political headaches? Don't expect that, said several.
Trudeau plans to stay on the same track, even if the prospect of a federal election before October 2025 suddenly looms large. Certainly, Trudeau's favourite catchphrase is, "We will continue to ..."
At the cabinet retreat, Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne summed up the strategy: "Deliver, deliver, deliver."
In an interview Friday, Champagne said, "We just need to double down and focus more on delivering and let others play their political game."
Canadians still feel "a lot of anxiety" as they look for economic relief with lower inflation and interest rates in sight, he said. "People have little appetite for the kind of spectacle you see in Ottawa, of gamesmanship and political play and, you know, House tactics and all that. They say, 'Listen, get on with it. You've been elected. Just do it.'"
Even Trudeau's ardent supporters say the path forward is set.
"The biggest course correction would be him - him leaving," said one.
And that is not something anyone is predicting, for now.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau visits with city transit workers during a scheduled event at a transit facility in Sault Ste.-Marie on Aug.29, 2024.

Subject: Conservatism; Leadership; Canadians; Celebrities; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: 'He's not getting the message': Justin Trudeau shrugs off the naysayers as he heads into a Liberal caucus retreat

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Document 412 of 500

'I've never seen anything like it': The behind-the-scenes attempts to unite against the right in a Toronto byelection

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ive-never-seen-anything-like-behind-scenes/docview/3102058137/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27ve+never+seen+anything+like+it%27%3A+The+behind-the-scenes+attempts+to+unite+against+the+right+in+a+Toronto+byelection&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An ex-Ontario premier and a former Toronto mayor could play pivotal roles in an upcoming city council byelection, amid concerns from some left-leaning insiders that a trio of candidates with Liberal connections are on track to split the vote and give a controversial conservative a path to victory.
Although voters won't go to the polls to elect a new councillor in Don Valley West until Nov. 4, the race is already being shaped by behind-the-scenes manoeuvring featuring Kathleen Wynne and John Tory, the Star has learned.
Wynne, the former Liberal premier who represented Don Valley West provincially for nearly two decades, confirmed she has spoken with three candidates with links to the Grits about the importance of rallying around a single challenger to block right-wing pundit Anthony Furey from winning the seat.
Meanwhile, the Star has confirmed that representatives of both Furey and Rachel Chernos Lin, the Toronto District School Board chair who is also running in Don Valley West, have reached out to Tory seeking his endorsement.
Observers say the swirling discussions involving local political heavyweights are unusual in municipal byelections, which are normally low-key affairs.
"There's so much jockeying ... I've never seen anything like it," said one insider who isn't directly involved in the race, and who like other sources spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss private conversations.
The seat in Ward 15, Don Valley West was left empty in May after the death of longtime representative Jaye Robinson. As of Friday, 14 candidates had signed up to replace her. While political parties play no official role in municipal elections, at least three contenders have strong ties to the federal or provincial Liberals.
Dhruv Jain, who is on leave from the office of the TTC chair, is executive vice president of the Don Valley West Provincial Liberal Association, and worked as executive assistant to Stephanie Bowman, the Liberal MPP for the riding.
Evan Sambasivam recently left his job as a federal Liberal government staffer, and his campaign is being managed by Alex Keys, Ontario field manager for the Liberal party of Canada. Sambasivam is also the nephew of former Ontario Liberal deputy premier Deb Matthews.
Chernos Lin has had help from Mandy Moore, former executive director of the Ontario Liberal party, while Jane Rounthwaite, a principal at the Osborne Group consultant firm and Wynne's partner, is chairing her fundraising efforts.
In an interview with the Star, Wynne said she had spoken to the three candidates about her concern that Furey could take advantage of the crowded field. Bowman, who has announced her support for Chernos Lin, has held similar talks with the candidates, according to sources.
Furey, a former Toronto Sun columnist and editorial director of a right-wing charity, has faced accusations of Islamophobia over his columns about the threat of what he called "political Islam," as well as criticism for his ties to anti-trans activists. He placed fourth in last year's mayoral byelection.
Wynne said she was concerned Furey's "right-wing ideology would not be helpful" to residents in Ward 15, and claimed he wasn't interested in representing the community but hopes to use the council seat as a platform for another mayoral run.
Furey said he wasn't paying attention to conversations among his opponents, but voters want a representative who can address local issues like rising car thefts, and would be turned off by insiders "brokering backroom deals."
He rejected the accusations of Islamophobia and transphobia against him as "out-of-context smears." Asked whether he sees the Ward 15 seat merely as a stepping stone to the mayor's office, Furey said he couldn't rule out contesting the 2026 mayoral election but for now was focused on Don Valley West.
According to Jain's campaign, Wynne asked their candidate not to sign up for the byelection. Wynne gave a different account, telling the Star she respects anyone who decides to run for office, and it would be an "overstatement" to say she had asked any of the Liberal-linked candidates to step aside. The deadline to sign up for or withdraw from the byelection is 2 p.m. on Sept. 19.
But Wynne said she hoped "there will be a clear, progressive, candidate who will emerge" soon. She said she has yet to make a public statement about who that candidate should be, but described Chernos Lin as having a higher profile than Jain or Sambasivam, and suggested she could make an endorsement in the "next couple of weeks."
Chernos Lin told the Star she's spoken to Wynne and Bowman "about me being the strongest candidate," and is expecting the former premier's endorsement. She said she hadn't enlisted Wynne and Bowman to try to push Jain or Sambasivam out.
"I think people are endorsing me because they believe in me, because they've seen I have a proven track record of getting things done," said Chernos Lin, who has been chair of the TDSB for two years and a trustee for six. Her platform includes making the ward safer and more walkable.
Jain and Sambasivam told the Star they are out campaigning every day and don't intend to step aside.
"It would be very cynical to ask the most energetic, hardworking campaign to drop out ... And I don't think that Kathleen and Stephanie are cynical people," said Sambasivam, who is promising to tackle gridlock and housing issues in the ward.
"I have a lot of respect for Kathleen Wynne," said Jain. "But I'm the one who's been at the doors every day, and both my neighbours and I know that I'm the best candidate to represent Don Valley West." He has vowed to promote family-sized housing options and improve public safety.
Multiple sources told the Star that both Chernos Lin's and Furey's camps have put out feelers to Tory seeking his support.
The former mayor, who resigned in February 2023 after admitting to an inappropriate relationship with a former staffer, won 72 per cent of the vote in Don Valley West in the 2022 mayoral election. His endorsement could go a long way to either cementing Chernos Lin as the most viable left-leaning candidate - particularly if paired with Wynne's support - or vaulting Furey into a prime position.
In a text message, Tory said he hadn't "made any plans or decisions on involvement in the byelection at this point."
Other candidates on the ballot include architect and environmental advocate Sheena Sharp, food and lifestyle educator Lesley Stoyan and international development consultant Habiba Desai.
With files from David Rider
CAPTION: Candidate Dhruv Jain files his nomination papers for the Ward 15 byelection on July 22.Evan Sambasivamresisters his candidacy for the Ward 15 byelection on July 22 at city hall.Rachel Chernos Lin, seen here in an October 2022 file photo, is running in the Ward 15 byelection in November.Former mayor John Tory, seen here in a 2023 file photo, told the Starhe hadn't "made any plans or decisions on involvement in the byelection at this point."Former premier Kathleen Wynne, right, who represented Don Valley West provincially for nearly two decades, confirmed she has spoken with three candidates about the importance of rallying around a single challenger to block right-wing pundit Anthony Furey, left.

Subject: Local elections; Mayors; Political activism; Candidates; Islamophobia; Voters; Xenophobia

People: Wynne, Kathleen

Title: 'I've never seen anything like it': The behind-the-scenes attempts to unite against the right in a Toronto byelection

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 9, 2024

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trio+urged+to+unite+against+the+right%3A+Ex-premier+among+those+touting+plan+to+thwart+Furey&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 414 of 500

Councillors ask province for stronger powers: Officials express concern over colleague's alleged 'connection to Nazi sympathizers'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/councillors-ask-province-stronger-powers/docview/3101919050/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Councillors+ask+province+for+stronger+powers%3A+Officials+express+concern+over+colleague%27s+alleged+%27connection+to+Nazi+sympathizers%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Elected officials in Pickering areasking the province for stronger powers to address the "dangerous rhetoric" of one of their colleagues, after she defended her presence at an event where some participants wore swastikas.
In a letter to Premier Doug Ford and Municipal Affairs Minister Paul Calandra dated Sept. 6, Mayor Kevin Ashe and five of Pickering's six councillors expressed "serious concerns" about Coun. Lisa Robinson's alleged "connections to Nazi sympathizers."
Robinson has denied the allegations, and described them as "slanderous."
"The City is deeply troubled by the potential impact any association between publicly elected officials and neo-Nazi supporters may have on the safety of our residents and neighbours, particularly within the Jewish community," read the letter, which said Robinson had inflamed the situation by "clumsily" attempting to downplay the swastika as "a tribal symbol with no connection to Nazism."
The group told the province Robinson's behaviour, which comes amid an increase in reports of antisemitic incidents, "reinforces the urgent need for a stronger legislative response." They asked the premier and minister to consider legislative changes that would allow municipalities to impose stricter penalties on councillors "whose actions foster hate and division," including removal from office.
The letter marks the latest episode in a protracted conflict between Robinson, who was elected city councillor for Ward 1 in 2022, and other officials in the municipality of 100,000 just east of Toronto.
She has faced criticism for opposing Pride events and Black History Month, and her colleagues accuse her and her supporters, some of whom are linked with alt-right groups involved in the so-called "Freedom Convoy," of hijacking meetings and even making them feel unsafe.
She and her backers claim they're fighting corruption and government overreach, and that the mayor and council are trying to silence dissent.
Ashe and the other five councillors previously wrote to the province asking for tougher penalties for members, after Robinson's July appearance on a webcast hosted by a man who in 2021 pleaded guilty to a hate crime in connection with anti-Muslim posts.
Last year, Robinson's pay was suspended for a total of 90 days after two integrity commissioner investigations determined she promoted homophobic and transphobic attitudes, and posted bullying messages to Facebook. Robinson alleged the commissioner was biased against her.
The latest incident relatesto her appearance at an event in Ottawa last weekend, where according to posts on social media, she was seen talking with a group that included people wearing T-shirts emblazoned with swastikas.
In a Sept. 7 letter of her own to Ford and Calandra, Robinson accused the mayor and the rest of council of spreading "blatant falsehoods" about the event for political gain.
According to a copy of the letter Robinson shared with the Star, she said she didn't formally attend the Ottawa event, but "stumbled upon" it while returning to her hotel. She described it as a "Cowboys and Indians Peace Talk" intended to improve relations between "Indigenous Peoples and settlers."
"I was briefly introduced to individuals present, but I left shortly after," she wrote.
According to Robinson, although she later learned that one attendee may have had a swastika tattoo, "at no point did I observe any tattoos or symbols on these individuals" while at the event. Asked about photos that show some people in the group had swastikas on their clothing, Robinson told the Star she didn't look at the "intricate details" of their shirts.
While denying ties to the event, Robinson wrote in the letter that the symbol the mayor and councillors objected to was "not a swastika, but an ancient one commonly used in Native American, Hindu, and Buddhist cultures" that had been "misappropriated." She said it would be "deeply irresponsible" to claim that "every appearance of a similar shape is linked to Nazism."
"The slanderous claim that I have any association with Nazi sympathizers is categorically false," she continued, alleging that an "innocent encounter" was being misrepresented as part of an "ongoing smear campaign."
In an interview, Ashe rejected Robinson's explanation of the swastikas at the event as non-offensive.
"The reality is, most people associate the swastika with the atrocities of the Holocaust," he said. "And when you have a publicly elected official suggesting that the use of this hateful symbol is fine - it's not fine."
The mayor said he wants Queen's Park to amend the Municipal Act to provide a mechanism that would allow a councillor to be removed by either the city integrity commissioner or municipal affairs minister, or through a judicial process. He said he wouldn't support changes that would enable council members to eject one of their own.
Ashe said Calandra has signalled he's looking into the Pickering officials' previous request, but he had yet to hear back about Friday's letter.
The minister and premier didn't immediately return a request for comment Sunday.
CREDIT: Ben SpuRR Toronto Star Chief

Document 415 of 500

'Climate literacy' ads coming: Ottawa plans $7-million campaign to promote carbon pricing, rebates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/climate-literacy-ads-coming/docview/3101918073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Climate+literacy%27+ads+coming%3A+Ottawa+plans+%247-million+campaign+to+promote+carbon+pricing%2C+rebates&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau government will launch a multimillion-dollar advertising campaign in the year ahead of the next election to promote its carbon pricing plan and the rebates it gives to Canadians, the Star has learned.
Environment and Climate Change Canada is planning a $7-million "climate literacy and action" advertising campaign that would focus on how "carbon pricing reduces emissions and benefits Canadians through the Canada Carbon Rebate," in addition to other actions to combat climate change, according to a copy of the department's advertising plan obtained by the Star through an Access to Information request.
It would mark the latest shift in the way the Trudeau Liberals communicate their flagship climate policy - which makes fossil-fuel products more expensive but encourages consumers to use less of them off with quarterly rebates - in the face of mounting Conservative attacks that label it as a driver of inflation.
The Liberals have long asserted that the rebates send more money to Canadians than the plan costs them. However, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has vowed to "axe the tax" and has called for a "carbon tax election." Combined with the more significant industrial price on pollution, the Liberals' carbon pricing plan is expected to account for a third of Canada's greenhouse gas reductions by 2030, according to government estimates.
In an acknowledgment that the Conservatives have dominated the narrative, the Trudeau government this year rebranded the plan's rebates, forced banks to more clearly label payments and made a push to counter Conservative claims.
The Liberal government had previously avoided calls urging it to use large paid advertising campaigns to make its case to Canadians.
Running advertisements would be one more way to raise awareness and boost support for the plan, said Kaitlin Power, a spokesperson for Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault, pointing to public polling that suggested Canadians were more likely to know they received a rebate last March than they would have in November 2023. That same polling, however, suggested a majority of Canadians support reducing or eliminating the consumer price on carbon.
The ads will include "plain language messaging designed to cut through misinformation and disinformation that educates Canadians on the Government of Canada's climate plan," she said.
Senior government officials had previously told the Star that advertising restrictions brought in by the Liberals after the previous Conservative government was criticized for overly partisan advertising had made it difficult to advertise the carbon pricing plan. The Trudeau government has generally spent less on advertising than the Harper government did.
In 2019, the watchdog Ad Standards flagged a proposed Canada Revenue Agency ad campaign as "self-congratulatory" for stating that carbon pricing was "making a cleaner economy more affordable for everyone," a key point of the Liberal argument. The CRA had spent just over $2 million on marketing what was then known as the Climate Action Incentive, which was a refundable tax credit rather than a quarterly rebate.
Any coming ad campaign must respect such restrictions, though "it really dulls and blunts advertising so that it's not very effective," said Alex Marland, a political science professor at Acadia University.
It could also be seen as "too little, too late," he said.
"Usually these types of advertising campaigns need to be done at the time of the launch, not like reactive messaging," he said.
Last year, the Liberals ran a similar climate literacy ad campaign, which cost $6.8 million and included a variety of media buys, including during the Super Bowl, though it did not touch on the contentious price on carbon.
The Conservative Party of Canada routinely runs ads opposing the plan, and fossil-fuel lobby groups and Conservative-led provinces like Alberta and Ontario have also advertised against carbon pricing.
CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Carbon; Climate change; Tax elections; Advertising campaigns; Federal elections-Canada; Climate action; Political finance; Literacy

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Tax elections Advertising campaigns

Location: Canada

People: Trudeau, Justin

Document 416 of 500

Time running out for PM to choose a path

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-running-out-pm-choose-path/docview/3101917928/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Time+running+out+for+PM+to+choose+a+path&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: These should be consequential meetings. Justin Trudeau's Liberals face the greatest challenge they have ever faced - the possibility of a fourth-place finish after the next election. Aside from the leadership question hanging over all Grit MPs' heads - should the prime minister stay or should he go - there are other challenges to address.
What is the Liberals' offer? Beyond asking the electorate to vote for Trudeau because Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives are scary - a playbook used in 2019 and 2021 - what are the Liberals running on, beyond more of the same? What is their answer to the palpable appetite for change? How do they plan to turn the ship around? What will form the basis of the next election platform? Where is the campaign team and what's the state of the machine, so obviously present in 2015, that can avoid close calls like last June's devastating byelection loss in Toronto-St. Paul's?
In short, what is the plan?
Perhaps, it's a bad sign that Liberal MPs expect a third of their colleagues won't show up at their summer caucus, three days of meetings, beginning Monday in Nanaimo, B.C. If Grit MPs are checked out, how can you expect the electorate to get excited?
For some, the expectation is the fireworks will come next week. Two high-stakes byelections, on Sept. 16, will test both the Liberals' and the NDP's strength, and may shed light on which party and leader will run off with the marker of being the federal progressive option for voters at the next campaign.
The race in Winnipeg's Elmwood-Transcona riding is the NDP's to lose. They are running on the coattails of popular Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew but face headwinds from the Conservatives, who see an opportunity with NDP incumbent Daniel Blaikie (son of the former popular MP Bill Blaikie) stepping aside.
If the NDP is to credibly portray itself to Canadians as the only choice against the Conservatives in the next election, as Leader Jagmeet Singh declared last week when he announced the end of the supply-and-confidence agreement with the Liberals, the party must show it can successfully push back against Poilievre's encroachment here.
Similarly, a byelection in the Montreal riding of LaSalle-Émard-Verdun, offers the NDP a chance to showcase it can defeat the Liberals, on their turf. Since the riding's formation in 2015, it's been held by former Liberal MP and cabinet minister David Lametti. Former prime minister Paul Martin held part of this riding from 1988 to 2008, winning six elections. While part of the riding was previously represented by the Bloc Québécois, in 2011, when the Liberal party was displaced by Jack Layton's NDP as the anti-Conservative option, New Democrats won three of the four ridings that now make up this electoral district (only Marc Garneau, the popular astronaut, who was in a very safe seat, held on).
If Singh and his candidate, city councillor Craig Sauvé, displace the Liberals' candidate, another city councillor named Laura Palestini, it will reinforce this message and send shock waves across the country.
The NDP's local campaign is already raising eyebrows in Liberal circles. In a flyer distributed in the riding, Sauvé is pictured in front of a Palestinian flag, asking voters to "send a strong message to Ottawa to stop the genocide in Gaza." The Liberals' stance on the Israel/Hamas conflict, which is seen in some corners as not defensive enough of Israel and in other quarters as too lenient of Israel and not defensive enough of Palestinian lives, has mobilized swaths of voters against the Grits and the concern among some GTHA MPs is that it will be a significant marker of distinction between the centre-left parties come election time.
If the Liberals lose LaSalle-Émard-Verdun to Bloc Québécois candidate Louis-Philippe Sauvé - which last week's polling suggested was a real possibility - one can expect renewed calls for a leadership change next week.
If the byelections result in status quo, the fear amid Liberals is that it will continue to lead to status quo - an excuse to delay change and to continue making decisions at a lethargic pace.
The NDP threw down the gauntlet last week. They've attempted to secure Trudeau as the Liberal candidate they're up against. Now the question is which progressive party will capitalize on the first mover advantage?
Will the NDP frame up Poilievre and the Conservatives before the Grits, show Canadians they can win, and mobilize enthusiasm for their leader? Or will Trudeau and his team finally deliver their Poilievre ads - two years after they were first floated to MPs - and attempt to push the NDP out of the frame, refocus their policy offer, energize donors and prepare for a campaign that could possibly come this December?
Both Singh and Trudeau talk about fighting the fight. But, so far, the only one in the ring is Poilievre.
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Document 417 of 500

'He's not getting the message': Trudeau shrugs off the naysayers as he heads into a Liberal caucus retreat

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hes-not-getting-message/docview/3101917835/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27He%27s+not+getting+the+message%27%3A+Trudeau+shrugs+off+the+naysayers+as+he+heads+into+a+Liberal+caucus+retreat&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The summer ride is over. So too is the Justin Trudeau listening tour.
The prime minister spent much of two months meeting in small groups or individually with nervous Liberal MPs, many angry after the June loss of a supposedly safe Toronto seat to the Conservatives.
Trudeau also spent two months running, paddling, boxing and bounding from one summer festival to another, taking thousands of selfies with Canadians who appeared not to have read polls about how disliked he is.
So what did the prime minister take away from it all?
His practised answers at a cabinet retreat and again this past week after his co-operation deal with the NDP fell apart show clearly he does not believe he is the problem.
Rather, Trudeau believes he is the solution.
In public and in private, Trudeau has expressed his determination to stay at the helm of his party through to the next election.
And he believes he now has most of his caucus behind him.
The Star spoke to more than a dozen Liberals inside and outside the Prime Minister's Office, cabinet and caucus, and agreed to requests they not be identified in order to gauge where they believe the prime minister's head is at.
All said Trudeau plans to stay the course.
Not all are thrilled with that.
But most said Trudeau is newly energized for the fight ahead. And the death of the NDP co-operation deal doesn't bother the prime minister or many of those around him.
If anything, after his summer listening tour, Trudeau is, surprisingly, "more emboldened," said one Liberal.
Another said, "I think there is a path to victory that he's confident about. Obviously it's not what it was in 2015 by any stretch of the imagination, but it's there, and I don't think anyone doubts his capacity to win the last two weeks of the campaign."
Trudeau, several said, is deeply concerned about the rise of a right-wing populism that threatens Canadian social programs and cohesion. He sees Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre as an agent of that.
Some said Trudeau believes he must stay on to counter Poilievre, even in the face of polls showing he is leading the Liberal party to likely disaster.
"He's not getting the message. He thinks he's the only one who can fight for the progressive agenda in Canada against the big, evil Conservatives," said one.
Some Liberals genuinely believe Trudeau is the best leader the party's got for that fight, and all they need to do in the months ahead is a better job of prosecuting the case against Poilievre.
They and the prime minister are relying on a conviction that the more Canadians see of Poilievre, the more they will dislike him.
Trudeau also thinks that most of the negative spin about his leadership comes from "groupthink" in the Ottawa press gallery.
That there is an unending stream of negative stories is certainly true, because there is a seemingly endless stream of polls documenting the mood of Canadians.
So what has Trudeau's own outreach taught him?
The prime minister has often talked in the past about how he discounts the excesses of both his "haters" and admirers.
Yet after a bruising early summer, where MPs spoke openly about their concerns to reporters (although only one, New Brunswick's Wayne Long, agreed to be identified), Trudeau seems to have refuelled his tank on the encouragement he encountered in crowds.
From the Vancouver Pride parade to the St. John's Regatta, Trudeau has told those around him that people were eager and happy to meet "the prime minister" not least because he brings "the celebrity factor."
Yet one Liberal cautioned it's not an indication Canadians want him to stay. "He confuses the selfie with support and it's not," they said. "Some of it may be support, but the selfie is largely celebrity-driven and not politically driven."
A clear sign that Trudeau's public relations and security staff worried he would encounter protesters were Trudeau's carefully sanitized public itineraries. Early in the summer, his office stopped issuing timely public notices of where the prime minister would be, sometimes concealing Trudeau's whereabouts until after an event was over.
And there was one telling, unscripted moment in Trudeau's summer travels.
In Sault Ste-Marie, a steelworker declined to shake Trudeau's hand, complained about taxes, the lack of a family doctor, the cost of dental care and about the "lazy neighbour" who doesn't work but lives the same life he, the ordinary worker, does.
Trudeau engaged the man and quickly tried to talk his way out of the awkward encounter, but the worker wanted no part of it and looked forward to Trudeau not being around "in another year."
It's a strategy: talking your way through difficult meetings, and talking over your critics.
It's one some say Trudeau has employed in caucus and in other tense meetings.
Whether it's the strategy the Liberal leader intends to employ at this week's national caucus retreat in Nanaimo - the first face-to-face gathering of all Liberal MPs since the loss of Toronto-St. Paul's - could determine whether his MPs think Trudeau has truly listened.
It may also determine what face they put on as they emerge before the cameras.
One senior government official said the Nanaimo caucus meeting is important because people will compare notes, and express their views very directly to the prime minister, the PMO, and to each other. Yet that same Liberal - along with others - is convinced the vast majority of the caucus is united behind Trudeau leading them into the next campaign, and will be focused on how to win that election, not on leadership changes.
So will Trudeau offer a major course correction - say, on the Middle East, the economic direction of the country, or the carbon pricing regime that has caused him endless political headaches? Don't expect that, said several.
Trudeau plans to stay on the same track, even if the prospect of a federal election before October 2025 suddenly looms large. Certainly, Trudeau's favourite catchphrase is, "We will continue to ..."
At the cabinet retreat, Industry Minister Francois-Philippe Champagne summed up the strategy: "Deliver, deliver, deliver."
In an interview Friday, Champagne said, "We just need to double down and focus more on delivering and let others play their political game."
Canadians still feel "a lot of anxiety" as they look for economic relief with lower inflation and interest rates in sight, he said. "People have little appetite for the kind of spectacle you see in Ottawa, of gamesmanship and political play and, you know, House tactics and all that. They say, 'Listen, get on with it. You've been elected. Just do it.' "
Even Trudeau's ardent supporters say the path forward is set.
"The biggest course correction would be him - him leaving," said one.
And that is not something anyone is predicting, for now.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau visits city transit workers in Sault Ste Marie, Ont., last month. In the face of polls suggesting a crushing defeat, Trudeau continues to express his determination to stay at the helm of the Liberal party through to the next election, writes Tonda MacCharles.Kenneth Armstrong THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles Toronto Star

People: Trudeau, Justin

Liberals are facing a palpable appetite for change, but their path forward is unclear

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/liberals-are-facing-palpable-appetite-change/docview/3102148988/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Liberals+are+facing+a+palpable+appetite+for+change%2C+but+their+path+forward+is+unclear&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 419 of 500

Who was the biggest loser in the Liberal-NDP breakup?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/who-was-biggest-loser-liberal-ndp-breakup/docview/3101793095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+was+the+biggest+loser+in+the+Liberal-NDP+breakup%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.
Matt Gurney: Well, that was a week. I'd hoped for a bit more of a gradual re-entry to reality as the summer wrapped up, but Jagmeet Singh and Jeremy Broadhurst, the man who will no longer be leading the next Liberal election campaign, had other plans. There is a LOT to take in here, but I'll start with a basic question: have the developments of this week cemented Pierre Poilievre's already pretty good position, or is this shakeup an opportunity for either (or both!) of the Liberals and NDP to reinvigorate themselves ahead of the next campaign, which feels closer today than it did a week ago?
Susan Delacourt: Whenever Justin Trudeau does write his memoirs, I wonder if the title will be, "Didn't See That Coming." I think we need to break this all down into component parts. In answer to your basic question, yes, this is obviously good news for the Conservatives. They get to spend the next weeks and months trying to tip Parliament into an election they would dearly love to have sooner than later. But I think they are going to have to watch their tone. Calling the NDP leader "Sellout Singh" reminds me of another politician who loves to nickname his rivals. I think as Canadians watch that spectacle in the U.S. this fall, they may develop a heightened aversion to nasty schoolyard politics.
MG: I remain skeptical of that. I think any attempt to force those comparisons just makes Poilievre look more normal by comparison. I know the Liberal party brain trust disagrees with me on that. We'll see. As for the new dynamic politically, I am weirdly the least interested in how the Liberals respond. I think it's fairly obvious what they have to do: survive. I won't be shocked if they prorogue to get through the coming byelections and then the U.S. election. I'm much more interested in the pressure campaign the Conservatives are now going to hit the Bloc and the NDP with. And what the government may be forced into conceding to prevent the Conservative campaign from working.
SD: That's an interesting idea - prorogation. That does present an opportunity for renewal of some kind, as does the need for a new campaign director. At the risk of sounding like a wild-eyed optimist, let me suggest one possible upside in the collapse of the Liberal-NDP deal.
I have heard - as have you, I'm sure - from lots of Liberals who were a bit queasy with the tilt to the left in the Trudeau government. These are the people who winced when Conservatives kept hammering away at the "Liberal-NDP coalition." I know that was coming up a lot at the doors in the St. Paul's byelection. So a little distance with the NDP may not be the worst thing in the world for the so-called blue Liberals.
MG: Maybe? I dunno. I am wary of ever underestimating Justin Trudeau. He's a very good politician. Better than Singh, as I think Singh may soon discover anew. But, honestly, I don't think Trudeau or the Liberals can meaningfully pivot, tilt, renew, reinvent or whatever. They are too baked in. They're the known quantity. I think any kind of major overhaul or shift would be really, really hard, even if they were very good at responding with deft, swift moves to developing events. And as it so happens, I think they're actually pretty objectively bad at doing those things. So yeah. I honestly just don't see that.
SD: I'd like to pause here to lament the collapse of the deal. Not because of any tactical considerations or the fact that it puts this Parliament closer to collapse - doesn't everyone love elections? - but because I thought it was one of the most interesting developments of the last couple of years. The public likes when politicians work together - witness the success of Trudeau-Doug Ford collaboration on so many fronts. The fact that Trudeau and Singh forged this agreement during the height of the convoy protest impressed me; it was a pact of stability in unstable times. I remember asking the NDP what could possibly be a breaking point. "Implementation" was the answer. Singh's in a tough spot here, it seems. He has to boast about all the things the deal did implement - pharmacare, a dental program, tougher labour laws. But now he has to say that Trudeau can't do anything?
MG: I didn't like the agreement. I think I could have liked an agreement. A coalition, a pure case-by-case bargaining before every confidence vote, or even something like a confidence-and-supply agreement - those are all legitimate. And I'd have respected the right of Messrs. Trudeau and Singh to pursue any of those arrangements. But that's in theory. In practice, I think Singh has humiliated himself. He wanted the benefits of signing an agreement to support the government for a set period of time and also the benefits of talking and acting as if he was an opposition leader. It's pure having-cake-and-eating-it-too delusion. Singh never navigated this well. Maybe that was impossible, but I think he came away looking ridiculous, and that he could have gotten all that he got without sacrificing so much pride. So it's not the idea of a confidence-and-supply agreement I object to, it was how Singh navigated it. It was genuinely mortifying to watch, and I wish we hadn't had to.
SD: The news conference last week was difficult to watch, too. The NDP leader was trying just a bit too hard to climb aboard the I-hate-Trudeau-too train. I never did hear exactly what was the straw that broke the back of this deal. It was like he came to the news conference with three lines and a keen desire to say as little as possible. What I guess we need to see next is whether this gambit pays off, either in the polls or those two byelections on Sept 16 in Quebec and Manitoba. The NDP is a contender in both. A victory in either could persuade the NDP that breaking up with the Liberals was the right thing to do. So much again for that idea of a gradual re-entry to fall.
CAPTION: Justin Trudeau and Jagmeet Singh, prepare for the start of the federal election English-language leaders' debate in Gatineau, Que., onSept. 9, 2021.

Subject: Agreements; Press conferences; Politics

Location: United States--US

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Gurney, Matt; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Who was the biggest loser in the Liberal-NDP breakup?

Publication title: The Toronto Star (Online); Toronto

Document 420 of 500

'Too little, too late'? Trudeau government plans ad campaign to promote carbon pricing - and rebates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/too-little-late-trudeau-government-plans-ad/docview/3101793037/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Too+little%2C+too+late%27%3F+Trudeau+government+plans+ad+campaign+to+promote+carbon+pricing+-+and+rebates&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA - The Trudeau government will launch a multimillion-dollar advertising campaign in the year ahead of the next election to promote its carbon pricing plan and the rebates it gives to Canadians, the Star has learned.
Environment and Climate Change Canada is planning a $7-million "climate literacy and action" advertising campaign that would focus on how "carbon pricing reduces emissions and benefits Canadians through the Canada Carbon Rebate," in addition to other actions to combat climate change, according to a copy of the department's advertising plan obtained by the Star through an Access to Information request.
It would mark the latest shift in the way the Trudeau Liberals communicate their flagship climate policy - which makes fossil-fuel products more expensive but encourages consumers to use less of them off with quarterly rebates - in the face of mounting Conservative attacks that label it as a driver of inflation.
The Liberals have long asserted that the rebates send more money to Canadians than the plan costs them. However, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre has vowed to "axe the tax" and has called for a "carbon tax election." Combined with the more significant industrial price on pollution, the Liberals' carbon pricing plan is expected to account for a third of Canada's greenhouse gas reductions by 2030, according to government estimates.
In an acknowledgment that the Conservatives have dominated the narrative, the Trudeau government this year rebranded the plan's rebates, forced banks to more clearly label payments and made a push to counter Conservative claims.
But the Liberal government had previously avoided calls urging it to use large paid advertising campaigns to make its case to Canadians.
Running advertisements would be one more way to raise awareness and boost support for the plan, a spokesperson for Environment Minister Steven Guilbeault said, pointing to public polling that suggested Canadians were more likely to know they received a rebate last March than they would have in November 2023. That same polling, however, suggested a majority of Canadians support reducing or eliminating the consumer price on carbon.
The ads will include "plain language messaging designed to cut through misinformation and disinformation that educates Canadians on the Government of Canada's climate plan," said spokesperson Kaitlin Power.
"Our plan is working, the economy is strong and we're building resilient communities."
Senior government officials had previously told the Star that advertising restrictions brought in by the Liberals after the previous Conservative government was criticized for overly partisan advertising had made it difficult to advertise the carbon pricing plan. The Trudeau government has generally spent less on advertising than the Harper government did.
In 2019, for example, a proposed Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) ad campaign was flagged by the watchdog Ad Standards as "self-congratulatory" for stating that carbon pricing was "making a cleaner economy more affordable for everyone," a key point of the Liberal argument. The CRA had spent just over $2 million on marketing what was then known as the Climate Action Incentive, which was a refundable tax credit rather than a quarterly rebate.
Any coming ad campaign will have to respect such restrictions, even though "it really dulls and blunts advertising so that it's not very effective," said Alex Marland, a political science professor at Acadia University.
It could also be seen as "too little, too late," he said.
"Usually these types of advertising campaigns need to be done at the time of the launch, not like reactive messaging," he said. "It's actually really hard to change people's minds."
Last year, the Liberals ran a similar climate literacy ad campaign, which cost $6.8 million and included a variety of media buys, including during the Super Bowl, though it did not touch on the contentious price on carbon.
The Conservative Party of Canada routinely runs ads opposing the plan, and fossil-fuel lobby groups and Conservative-led provinces like Alberta and Ontario have also advertised against carbon pricing.
CAPTION: The federal government'sflagship climate-change policy makes fossil-fuel products more expensive but encourages Canadians to use less of them off with quarterly rebates.

Subject: Rebates; Canadians; Decades; Carbon; Climate change; Literacy; Advertising campaigns; Tax elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Rebates Advertising campaigns Tax elections

Location: Canada

Title: 'Too little, too late'? Trudeau government plans ad campaign to promote carbon pricing - and rebates

Document 421 of 500

We should focus on how to stop Gaza carnage, not on CUPE leader Fred Hahn

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/we-should-focus-on-how-stop-gaza-carnage-not-cupe/docview/3101793018/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=We+should+focus+on+how+to+stop+Gaza+carnage%2C+not+on+CUPE+leader+Fred+Hahn&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford rarely has kind words for his political enemies. But he doesn't usually go so far as to call them "disgusting human beings."
These, however, were the words he used to attack CUPE Ontario president Fred Hahn last month, as the premier joined in the media pile-on of those attempting to drive the democratically-elected labour leader from his key union position over a video he posted, which he described as criticism of Israel but that many labelled antisemitic.
The campaign against Hahn could purge one of Canada's most effective labour leaders.
It could also further silence critics of Israel's war on Gaza, signalling that anyone condemning Israel's actions better be prepared to be fired, blacklisted, even possibly denounced by the premier as "disgusting."
Despite calls for his resignation, Hahn says he will continue in his job, as long as he has the support of CUPE Ontario members.
He has served as the elected president of CUPE Ontario, representing 290,000 workers, since 2010. He was re-elected to the post last May and was endorsed by the CUPE provincial executive last week, amid the current controversy.
More than 195,000 emails have been sent supporting Hahn, in a campaign organized by the group Labour 4 Palestine.
But Hahn has long gotten under the skin of the premier, particularly for leading public sector employees in opposition to the province's restrictive wage legislation.
He's also made enemies as an outspoken pro-Palestinian activist in the labour movement.
Last October, the day after Hamas' murderous raid into Israel, Hahn was widely criticized for posting a tweet celebrating political resistance movements.
In an open letter of apology, he wrote: "Hamas committed a horrific terrorist attack on civilians in Israel. On the day immediately following that, I tweeted about resistance. The timing was wrong. That was an error, and I apologize."
Then, last month Hahn sparked controversy again when he reposted on his Facebook page a doctored video depicting an Israeli Olympic athlete coming down from a dive in the form of a bomb exploding on Gaza.
There were calls for Hahn's resignation by pro-Israel groups, media commentators and the CUPE national executive. They said that, since the athlete had a Star of David tattooed on his arm, the video suggested that Jews were bombing Gaza.
Hahn says he saw the video as blaming Israel, in the context of the Olympics where athletes compete on the basis of nationality, not religion. But, when others disagreed, he removed the video and apologized, acknowledging on Facebook that it is "a terrible mistake, and antisemitic, to conflate abhorrent actions by the state of Israel with Jewish humanity or identity."
Larry Haiven, spokesperson for Independent Jewish Voices, and the son of a Holocaust survivor, insists that the video clearly blames the state of Israel - not Jewish people in general - for the bombing of Gaza, and that Canadians understand the difference.
Pro-Israel advocates often blur that distinction, suggesting that criticism of Israel amounts to antisemitism - a vicious accusation which conjures up the vileness of Nazi Germany and therefore intimidates people from criticizing Israel.
This has had the effect of stifling public criticism of Israel, allowing the Trudeau government to largely ignore calls to impose sanctions on Israel as a way to add to international pressure on Israel to stop its brutal campaign in Gaza.
The Trudeau government rightfully condemned Hamas' Oct. 7 raid that killed 1,200 people and kidnapped 250. But it has refused to condemn Israel's killing of 40,000 Gazans since then, even after the International Criminal Court sought a warrant last May (along with warrants for Hamas leaders) to arrest Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu for war crimes.
And so, here we are, with the media focused on punishing Fred Hahn, rather than focusing on how to stop Israel's relentless bombing that has left an estimated 14,000 Gazan children dead, with many more crippled, blinded or trapped under the rubble.
CAPTION: Canadian Union of Public Employees Ontario president Fred Hahn speaks at the pro-Palestine encampment at the University of Toronto in May 2024.

Subject: Bombings; Antisemitism; Resignations; War crimes; Apologies

Business indexing term: Subject: Resignations

Location: Gaza Strip; Israel

Company / organization: Name: Hamas; NAICS: 813940

Title: We should focus on how to stop Gaza carnage, not on CUPE leader Fred Hahn

Publication date: Sep 8, 2024

Section: opinion/star-columnists

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Focus on stopping carnage, not Hahn

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/focus-on-stopping-carnage-not-hahn/docview/3101777754/se-2?accountid=15090

Linda McQuaig is an author, journalist and a freelancecontributing columnist for The Star.

Document 423 of 500

Who was the biggest loser in the Liberal-NDP breakup?: In a recurring feature, Susan Delacourt, a small-l liberal, and Matt Gurney, a small-c conservative, bring their different perspectives - and shared commitment to civil disagreement - to the political debates of the moment.

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/who-was-biggest-loser-liberal-ndp-breakup/docview/3101777745/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Who+was+the+biggest+loser+in+the+Liberal-NDP+breakup%3F%3A+In+a+recurring+feature%2C+Susan+Delacourt%2C+a+small-l+liberal%2C+and+Matt+Gurney%2C+a+small-c+conservative%2C+bring+their+different+perspectives+-+and+shared+commitment+to+civil+disagreement+-+to+the+political+debates+of+the+moment.&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Matt Gurney Well, that was a week. I'd hoped for a bit more of a gradual re-entry to reality as the summer wrapped up, but Jagmeet Singh and Jeremy Broadhurst, the man who will no longer be leading the next Liberal election campaign, had other plans. There is a LOT to take in here, but I'll start with a basic question: have the developments of this week cemented Pierre Poilievre's already pretty good position, or is this shakeup an opportunity for either (or both!) of the Liberals and NDP to reinvigorate themselves ahead of the next campaign, which feels closer today than it did a week ago?
Susan DelacourtWhenever Justin Trudeau does write his memoirs, I wonder if the title will be, "Didn't See That Coming." I think we need to break this all down into component parts. In answer to your basic question, yes, this is obviously good news for the Conservatives. They get to spend the next weeks and months trying to tip Parliament into an election they would dearly love to have sooner than later. But I think they are going to have to watch their tone. Calling the NDP leader "Sellout Singh" reminds me of another politician who loves to nickname his rivals. I think as Canadians watch that spectacle in the U.S. this fall, they may develop a heightened aversion to nasty schoolyard politics.
MG I remain skeptical of that. I think any attempt to force those comparisons just makes Poilievre look more normal by comparison. I know the Liberal party brain trust disagrees with me on that. We'll see. As for the new dynamic politically, I am weirdly the least interested in how the Liberals respond. I think it's fairly obvious what they have to do: survive. I won't be shocked if they prorogue to get through the coming byelections and then the U.S. election. I'm much more interested in the pressure campaign the Conservatives are now going to hit the Bloc and the NDP with. And what the government may be forced into conceding to prevent the Conservative campaign from working.
SD That's an interesting idea - prorogation. That does present an opportunity for renewal of some kind, as does the need for a new campaign director. At the risk of sounding like a wild-eyed optimist, let me suggest one possible upside in the collapse of the Liberal-NDP deal.
I have heard - as have you, I'm sure - from lots of Liberals who were a bit queasy with the tilt to the left in the Trudeau government. These are the people who winced when Conservatives kept hammering away at the "Liberal-NDP coalition." I know that was coming up a lot at the doors in the St. Paul's byelection. So a little distance with the NDP may not be the worst thing in the world for the so-called blue Liberals.
MG Maybe? I dunno. I am wary of ever underestimating Justin Trudeau. He's a very good politician. Better than Singh, as I think Singh may soon discover anew. But, honestly, I don't think Trudeau or the Liberals can meaningfully pivot, tilt, renew, reinvent or whatever. They are too baked in. They're the known quantity. I think any kind of major overhaul or shift would be really, really hard, even if they were very good at responding with deft, swift moves to developing events. And as it so happens, I think they're actually pretty objectively bad at doing those things. So yeah. I honestly just don't see that.
SD I'd like to pause here to lament the collapse of the deal. Not because of any tactical considerations or the fact that it puts this Parliament closer to collapse - doesn't everyone love elections? - but because I thought it was one of the most interesting developments of the last couple of years. The public likes when politicians work together - witness the success of Trudeau-Doug Ford collaboration on so many fronts. The fact that Trudeau and Singh forged this agreement during the height of the convoy protest impressed me; it was a pact of stability in unstable times. I remember asking the NDP what could possibly be a breaking point. "Implementation" was the answer. Singh's in a tough spot here, it seems. He has to boast about all the things the deal did implement - pharmacare, a dental program, tougher labour laws. But now he has to say that Trudeau can't do anything?
MG I didn't like the agreement. I think I could have liked an agreement. A coalition, a pure case-by-case bargaining before every confidence vote, or even something like a confidence-and-supply agreement - those are all legitimate. And I'd have respected the right of Messrs. Trudeau and Singh to pursue any of those arrangements. But that's in theory. In practice, I think Singh has humiliated himself. He wanted the benefits of signing an agreement to support the government for a set period of time and also the benefits of talking and acting as if he was an opposition leader. It's pure having-cake-and-eating-it-too delusion. Singh never navigated this well. Maybe that was impossible, but I think he came away looking ridiculous, and that he could have gotten all that he got without sacrificing so much pride. So it's not the idea of a confidence-and-supply agreement I object to, it was how Singh navigated it. It was genuinely mortifying to watch, and I wish we hadn't had to.
SD The news conference last week was difficult to watch, too. The NDP leader was trying just a bit too hard to climb aboard the I-hate-Trudeau-too train. I never did hear exactly what was the straw that broke the back of this deal. It was like he came to the news conference with three lines and a keen desire to say as little as possible. What I guess we need to see next is whether this gambit pays off, either in the polls or those two byelections on Sept, 16 in Quebec and Manitoba. The NDP is a contender in both. A victory in either could persuade the NDP that breaking up with the Liberals was the right thing to do. So much again for that idea of a gradual re-entry to fall.
Susan Delacourt is an Ottawa-based columnist for the Star, where she has been covering politics on Parliament Hill since the late 1980s. A political science graduate of Western University, she's written four political books; her latest, "Shopping for Votes," was a finalist for the 2014 Hilary Weston Writers' Trust prize in non-fiction. Susan is also a regular commentator on CBC and CTV, and has taught journalism and political communication at Carleton University. Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is co-editor of "The Line," an online magazine, which publishes at readtheLine.ca. He can be reached at matt@readtheline.ca
