Document 444 of 500

NDP leader should take cue from Harris

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-leader-should-take-cue-harris/docview/3101087222/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+leader+should+take+cue+from+Harris&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Now that NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has pulled the pin on the NDP's governing agreement with the Liberals, he has an opportunity to distance himself from both Justin Trudeau's unpopular party and fire-breathing Pierre Poilievre. To do so, though, he and his people need to rethink how they think and talk and dream.
Fortunately, they have a rather prominent example south of the border in Kamala Harris and the Democratic Party. In three short weeks, Harris took her party from being on the ropes to being jubilant, cheeky, appealing and very much in contention.
The dynamics in Canadian politics are entirely different, but there are lessons to be learned. Memes, pop star endorsements and an upbeat image will help Singh, as they're helping Harris. The transformation Democrats are undertaking goes much deeper, though.
The Democrats clearly have found a playbook, and they're using it to call every play they make. This book has become an American bestseller and tool kit essential for Democrats. In "The Persuaders: At the Front Lines of the Fight for Hearts, Minds, and Democracy," by Anand Giridharadas, the former columnist for The New York Times profiles left-wing activists, including Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, a Democratic Party maverick a few years ago and most recently a star at the Democratic National Convention (DNC), who take brilliant new approaches that work.
Giridharadas preaches about the need for progressives to take back key frames the Right has laid claim to for years. Freedom. Family. Patriotism. Masculinity. Normalcy. It's no coincidence that after Giridharadas advice, the DNC repeatedly beamed all these values into the heads and hearts of everyone who watched. Democrats will now take these values out on the stump.
What politicians have done in the past and what they promise for the future are important. They are secondary, though, to how they make people feel about themselves and their vision for the future. Singh and the NDP need, at this point, to decouple themselves entirely from the Liberals and work on connecting with voters on their values.
In "The Persuaders," communications whiz Anat Shankar Osorio - a disciple of the godfather of political framing, George Lakoff - advises "Sell the brownie, not the recipe." That Kamala Harris hasn't detailed her platform is not as important as the sense you get from her and her running mate Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz about the world they envision for voters and their families.
More advice from "The Persuaders": Democrats need to offer "blue meat." (For the NDP, this might be called "orange meat.") This is a takeoff from the advice of revolutionary Ronald Reagan pollster and strategist Frank Luntz, who preached that rather than worrying about the mushy middle, Republicans should offer "red meat," meaning ideas that excite their base, upset the opposition and drive the narrative and all discussion. Democrats are now doing this. The NDP needs to do the same, to make mouth-watering offers to voters the Liberals can't match, and the Tories revile.
Advertising the other side is never a good thing, as Lakoff and "The Persuaders" tell us, yet the NDP never stops talking about the other guys. They waste precious time denouncing the big-money ties of their opponents.
Singh and company need to reframe the narrative. They need Canadians to understand they will be freer if they don't have to worry about costs of dental care. That their families will be more secure if we help homeless people get off the street. That it's patriotic for Canada to take the lead on climate change and to make polluters pay (and unpatriotic not to). That real men support others, not just themselves. That it's weird to want to kill medicare, which is what Poilievre wants to do by allowing more private health-care companies.
What the NDP has accomplished through its deal with the Liberals is not to be sneezed at, but it's not enough. Closing the deal for the NDP may mean what it appears to mean for American Democrats, namely reclaiming those key concepts and frames.
Ish Theilheimer is a writer, theatre producer and community activist in Golden Lake, Ont.
He is a former Ontario NDP
vice-president and adviser.
CREDIT: Ish Theilheimer Contributor;

Subject: Voters; Political advertising; Political parties; Political campaigns; Strategic planning; Decision making

Business indexing term: Subject: Strategic planning

People: Harris, Kamala; Singh, Jagmeet

Company / organization: Name: New Democratic Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940

Document 445 of 500

Ford's booze dreams might turn out to be small beer

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fords-booze-dreams-might-turn-out-be-small-beer/docview/3101087037/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford%27s+booze+dreams+might+turn+out+to+be+small+beer&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: At just before 2 p.m. on Thursday, I was the fifth-ever customer to buy beer from the Little Bee Mart at the corner of Runnymede and Annette, when I picked up a few $4 tall boy cans of Mill Street Organic from among the seven varieties of suds on offer there. I missed out on being first by about an hour, Charlie, the owner told me, and in that time a few regular customers had picked up a six pack or a can mostly for the novelty.
"Honestly, it's going better than I thought," he said with a shrug. No problems yet, none really expected, but no sales bonanza either. (He also offers two kinds of wine - though he was still waiting to sell his first bottle.)
As for me, buying alcohol in a corner store in Toronto for the first time, it was ... well, no big deal, really. Although that may be what you want in a beer purchase - just in and out and done. A lot of ritual or fanfare might seem appropriate when you buy a house or a car or an engagement ring. But a six pack? Let's just get it over with.
As of Wednesday, there were four retail spots within roughly a 15-minute walk of my house where you could buy alcohol - two LCBO locations and two Wine Racks. That seemed set to change Thursday, as Ontario's 4,200 convenience stores got the right, at long last, to sell beer and wine.
There are at least 17 places you might call convenience stores within that same 15-minute walking radius of my home - places that sell lottery tickets, cigarettes, pop and snacks. In the first few hours that they were legally able to sell booze, I set out to visit each and see how it was going.
For most of them it wasn't going at all. As in, they were choosing so far not to offer it for sale.
"We're not doing that, too much hassle," the owner of High Park Variety told me as she monitored a lunch-hour lineup of middle schoolers crowding the candy counter.
"Too much work," the owner of Bruce's Gift & Variety said.
The owner of T.J.'s Variety on Bloor West said that with an LCBO right next door, he wanted to wait and see what others in the area were doing, to judge whether it would be worth the time, effort and shelf space.
In all, of the 17 places that seemed to qualify, only three had beer and wine on sale.
And in those places, no great crowds of drunks were rushing in to take sudden advantage of the convenience.
At Jug Mart convenience on Jane Street, where they have a separately enclosed area for vape products as well as lottery tickets, cigarettes and junk food - a one-stop vice shop alongside the usual variety-store groceries and knick-knacks - they had a few major brews in the fridge and a handwritten sign advertising the introduction of the new product line.
Through a language barrier that initially had her trying to explain to me that her store didn't sell newspapers (journalism: a vice too far), the woman who runs the place with her husband said they'd sold their first six pack after opening in the morning, and had seen nothing further on the sales front by noon. "We think it's good," she said.
In anticipation of this day, some people expected Toronto to turn into a wild untamed party, while others expected some business sales revolution. Many, one way or the other, portrayed it as a big deal. But in the first hours, at least in one area of the city, it seemed to be neither bonanza nor blight. Instead it seemed a bit blah.
Which, as I say, might be what you want from a routine beer purchase. If you can walk a few minutes to get it rather than driving a few minutes, so much the better. But either way it's probably fine, right? (Maybe it's worth mentioning that in my walk past the 17 corner stores and four more traditional booze outlets, I passed dozens and dozens of licensed bars and restaurants. So it's not as if alcohol was ever scarce around here.)
This could be a repeat of past issues like drinking in parks, or expanded patios, or so many others that generated much discussion in advance and seemed like much ado about nothing once they were implemented.
Which makes it all the more puzzling that Premier Doug Ford seems to think this is a policy change he base a re-election campaign on, as my Queen's Park colleagues were reporting Thursday. He paid - depending on whose numbers you go on - between $225 million and $1 billion in taxpayer money to end an agreement with the Beer Store a year early to rush these bottles onto corner-store shelves.
After that expensive regulatory rush, it seems many retailers are in no rush to take advantage.
Likely it will help the bottom lines of many of those small retailers who participate. It will certainly seem like a welcome convenience to those who can get what they're looking for a few steps closer to home.
It's a good deal. But what it's not, it appears so far, is a big deal.
CREDIT: Edward Keenan Opinion

Subject: Alcohol; Sales; Beer; Liquor; Convenience stores

Business indexing term: Subject: Sales Convenience stores

Title: Ford's booze dreams might turn out to be small beer

Letters: NDP kills deal that propped up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority government at risk, Sept. 4 Singh won't say whether he'll help topple 'selfish' Justin Trudeau's government, Sept. 5 In message of hope, Singh says challenge ahead is convincing people NDP can win, Sept. 5

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+NDP+kills+deal+that+propped+up+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals%2C+putting+his+minority+government+at+risk%2C+Sept.+4+Singh+won%27t+say+whether+he%27ll+help+topple+%27selfish%27+Justin+Trudeau%27s+government%2C+Sept.+5+In+message+of+hope%2C+Singh+says+challenge+ahead+is+convincing+people+NDP+can+win%2C+Sept.+5&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Singh made
a big mistake
NDP kills deal that propped
up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority
government at risk, Sept. 4
The last time the NDP abandoned a Liberal government, we lost the landmark Kelowna Accord agreement with Indigenous Peoples and a national child-care program, ushering in 10 years of unpleasant Conservative rule. A majority of Canadians hold centre/left values, so the left needs to get together either by merging or by supporting a single left-oriented candidate in each riding. Take a lesson from the recent French election when the leftist groups worked together to prevail.
David B. Clemens, Toronto
I have to believe that NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has a shred of reason left and still intends to support the Liberal government until the next scheduled election. To do otherwise would hand Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre an open licence to run roughshod over the social safety nets that have made Canada a beacon in the western world and which form the very basis of the NDP's raison d'être. Can you imagine Poilievre championing universal drug benefits, dental care, climate change initiatives? It defies belief!
Let's be realistic here. The NDP has zero chance of forming a government, particularly in the right-wing political climate that seems to be overtaking the world. The only chance Singh has of pushing the NDP agenda forward is to rely on Liberal support (not the other way around).
If anything, a merger of the two parties would ensure a Canada with a socially responsible platform that could actually do some good, but, again, that's not going to happen. I implore Singh to do what's actually in the best interests of Canadians and to resist the impossible dream of 24 Sussex Drive for now.
Michael Bines, Toronto
There are more questions for NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh that were not asked on Wednesday. Singh kept referring to the choices in any upcoming election being between him and Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre. Why was he not reminded that, like it or not, the Liberals would still be one of the choices?
Why was the important problem of the lack of affordable housing in Canada never addressed should we lose this Liberal government? Not to minimize the accomplishments of dental care and pharmacare, but why was there no mention of how he would manage Canada's economy? So many other questions could have been asked.
Beverley Murray, Burlington
These are just words. Nothing will change. NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh will still support Justin Trudeau's Liberals to prevent a fall of government. The federal NDP don't have money to properly fund an election campaign. Bloc Leader Yves-François Blanchet must be salivating over ways he can pressure Trudeau, now in dire need of support, to give more money and enhanced programs to Quebec.
Jim McEwen, Bowmanville, Ont.
Jagmeet's move
is no surprise
Singh won't say whether he'll help topple 'selfish' Justin Trudeau's government, Sept. 5
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh's choice to end the arrangement with the Liberals was bound to happen. The Liberals likely hoped it would happen much later, but it seems the NDP no longer saw any benefit to continue a relationship.
They accomplished their goals, now it was time to distinguish themselves from the unpopular Liberals and offer the voter an alternative other than the Conservatives. Sadly, despite what they say, they look like they gave into Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's demand for them to grow a spine and bring the government down.
In their attempt to separate themselves from the Liberals and by calling Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "weak, selfish and too beholden to corporate interests," they actually supported the Conservative position that Trudeau is unfit for the job. This name calling was unnecessary. All Singh had to do was to say, "I am ending the supply and confidence agreement because Trudeau does not support labour."
James Drake, Kingston
In cancelling his party's supply and confidence agreement that has helped sustain Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberal minority government, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh again rightfully denounced the Liberals' continual catering to big money and corporate interests. An unprecedentedly large portion of Canadians are financially struggling, especially with continuing food and housing price inflation. Thus, fiscally progressive governmental policy/practice is also needed. It's no longer morally or politically sufficient for the Liberals to solely support the core social issues - those of race, sexuality, gender, and unrestricted abortion access - while so many people strain to pay for some of life's basic necessities and this issue needs addressing.
Frank Sterle Jr., White Rock, B.C.
NDP leader must look to Layton
In message of hope, Singh says challenge ahead is convincing people NDP can win, Sept. 5
Former NDP leader Jack Layton was able to be non-partisan and cross lines with other leaders. But Jagmeet Singh listening to Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre squawk and complain, and then acting as Poilievre would want, is something very different. Former NDP leader Thomas Mulcair started the decline when it was clear he thought the party could stand on its own, and then the party lost its way. I am a lifetime NDP voter, but l guess I will now vote for the party that gives rather than takes. The Liberals gave me a great pension and legal weed so Singh can beat it.
Russ Young, Fort Erie, Ont.
Just as Jack Layton's NDP bid for power brought us the Conservatives in 2011, so will this move by NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh. It is as though he is playing right into Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's hands. A Conservative government will be and do the exact opposite of NDP policies. Poilievre himself has implied social programs will be under threat. Remember, "those who don't remember history are condemned to repeat it."
Barbara Sternberg, Toronto
CAPTION: Just as former party leader Jack Layton's NDP bid for power brought us the Conservatives in 2011, so will this move by Jagmeet Singh, a reader writes.The Canadian Press file photo

Subject: Conservatism; Dental care; Prime ministers

Location: Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 447 of 500

Some Liberals think change may help: Many believe departure of campaign director can act as reset button, give party a better chance

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/some-liberals-think-change-may-help/docview/3101086909/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Some+Liberals+think+change+may+help%3A+Many+believe+departure+of+campaign+director+can+act+as+reset+button%2C+give+party+a+better+chance&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trailing in the polls and suddenly facing the prospect of an early federal election, many Liberals say there is a welcome chance for renewal now that the governing party's national campaign director is leaving his post.
Jeremy Broadhurst, a veteran strategist who was set to quarterback the Liberals' election campaign after serving as a top adviser in Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's government, announced Thursday he is stepping down from his party role on Sept. 30.
The decision, first reported in the Star, came one day after the New Democratic Party declared it was pulling out of the parliamentary alliance that was supposed to keep the Liberal minority government in power until at least June 2025. However, the Broadhurst departure was not directly related to the possibility an election could come sooner than later - a prospect multiple Liberals also downplayed.
Rather, after more than a year of national polls giving the opposition Conservatives a significant lead in voting intentions, many Liberals greeted the news of Broadhurst's departure as an opportunity to change course and improve their chances in the next federal campaign. Many doubted an election would come quickly, arguing the NDP doesn't have the money or public support to go to the polls at this time.
Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, the Liberal MP for the Toronto riding of Beaches-East York who has called for a partywide vote on Trudeau's leadership, said Broadhurst's decision to quit is a much-needed change in the Liberals' top ranks.
"The way we communicate to Canadians, the national director sets that agenda as much as anyone ... and we obviously need to change that approach in a serious way," Erskine-Smith said.
"I've got nothing against Jeremy," he added, "but we need significant change, and guess what? This is a significant change."
Speaking to reporters at the party's Quebec caucus meeting Thursday, Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly also said the move gives the Liberals a chance to look at "a new approach" and "new ideas," according to a report from Radio-Canada.
A cabinet minister who spoke to the Star on condition they not be identified said, although Broadhurst had left the Prime Minister's Office to become campaign director more than a year ago, "the view inside was that he had not accomplished what he had intended."
That source said the party is financially ready for an election campaign, and fundraising has been surprisingly strong in spite of what polls suggest, but there is a lot of work to do on policy and the party's candidate roster, with questions remaining about how many of its incumbent MPs are leaving or running again.
Asked about such complaints, a senior government official who spoke on a background-only basis acknowledged some caucus and cabinet members had aired their concerns to both the prime minister and to Broadhurst. However, the insider also defended Broadhurst and said he was "definitely not pushed out."
Liberal party spokesperson Parker Lund said the party has 105 nominated candidates to date, and had its best overnight email fundraising effort of the year in the wake of the NDP withdrawal from the deal, but declined to specify how much money was raised.
Ottawa Liberal MP Yasir Naqvi downplayed the significance of Broadhurst's resignation, saying "people come and go" in politics, but added Broadhurst's departure could be an opportunity for fresh ideas to counter the Liberals' slump in the polls. "We need a campaign team that is focused and disciplined and adaptive to new technology," he said. "But it's never about just one person ... politics is a team sport."
Another Liberal MP, who agreed to speak on condition of anonymity, told the Star it's good that Broadhurst is leaving because the Liberals need a "street fighter" leading their campaign against Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, who are positioning themselves as scrappy populists.
Under the Liberals' deal with the NDP, the New Democrats pledged to prop up the government in exchange for progress on progressive policies like dental care for low-income Canadians and broader public pharmacare coverage.
But several Liberals who spoke to the Star on Thursday said the pact never made for smooth sailing with the NDP. One said it will be a challenge to get vote-by-vote support to get laws passed in Parliament, but characterized dealing with the NDP as always "a slog. It will continue to be a slog."
Meanwhile, several Liberal MPs acknowledged to the Star an earlier election is more likely now that the deal has been scrapped.
"I don't know if it's a well-thought-through strategy," said Liberal MP John McKay. He predicted the NDP will face "relentless pressure" from the Conservatives to declare non-confidence in the government, which will force the NDP to side with the Liberals if it wants to prevent an election over the coming weeks and months.
McKay also said the death of the NDP deal cements Trudeau's position as Liberal leader. The prime minister has insisted he will stay on for the next campaign, but after the party lost a Toronto byelection in June, several Liberals called for him to step down.
McKay said the return to a more precarious minority Parliament should put that issue to rest.
"The prime minister is pretty well locked in now for the next election," he said.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau "is pretty well locked in" as party leader for the next election, Liberal MP John McKay said Thursday.Sean Kilpatrick The Canadian Press
CREDIT: Alex Ballingall, Tonda MacCharles and Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Subject: Fund raising; Political leadership; Prime ministers; Elections

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: Some Liberals think change may help:   Many believe departure of campaign director can act as reset button, give party a better chance

Document 448 of 500

Federal vote could derail Ford's plans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/federal-vote-could-derail-fords-plans/docview/3101086639/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+vote+could+derail+Ford%27s+plans&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Doug Ford counts down to an early spring election, watch out for the fallout from a federal election he hadn't counted on - even earlier next spring.
It's now looking as if his best-laid plans to get out ahead of events could be overtaken by events in Ottawa. This week, the federal NDP announced a breakup by "ripping up" its two-year nonelection accord with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
That's politics. When you play politics, there's always a faster player in town.
The premier had fervently hoped to beat the clock on a police probe into the Greenbelt imbroglio. But there was always a second motive for flouting the province's fixed election date law, which mandates a vote nearly two years from now in June 2026.
That's an eternity in politics. Ford's provincial Tories have long worried that their fellow Tory, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, could already be in the prime minister's office by then.
Which would make for an awkward federal-provincial byplay, pitting these two fellow travellers - who are far from pals - against one another on the public stage. Far better to steer clear of a collision course that could be personal, political, fiscal and ideological.
For Ford and Poilievre are utterly unlike one another:
The premier can be personable when he is feeling folksy; Poilievre is prone to being impersonal and impetuous.
Ford is a salesman at heart, cutting deals in the business world; his federal Conservative counterpart is a career politician who is quick to cut people down.
The premier is a people pleaser who'd rather spread money around; Poilievre is all about cutting programs to eliminate deficits.
Ford sees himself as a populist, not an ideologue; Poilievre is a party man who backs the party line.
Against that backdrop, the last thing Ford wants is to run for re-election with future prime minister Poilievre cutting Ontario down to size and pushing the premier around. Far better to campaign with Trudeau still in power, tapping into anti-Liberal animus across the province, while tarring the New Democrats with the same cloth.
For years, Ford's Tories have taunted the Ontario NDP by falsely claiming they joined a formal coalition with the previous minority governments of former premiers Kathleen Wynne and Dalton McGuinty. In fact, there was never any arrangement, formal or informal, between the two parties to keep the minority legislature alive from 2011 to '14.
Instead of targeting the Liberals and New Democrats for the supposed sins of their federal counterparts, Ford could find himself taunted to explain or excuse the excesses of Poilievre in power should a federal election take place first. All that said, gaming it out only goes so far.
The long life of this federal minority Parliament may offer lessons for a future minority in Ontario. Just as it drew inspiration from this province's past experiment with a durable NDP-Liberal minority accord from 1985 to '87.
Political posturing aside, the latest federal pact was a credit to federal NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, even though he got little credit for it from pundits and public opinion. He wielded the balance of power to make the most of this minority, winning successes on pharmacare, dental care and low-income protection.
By granting stability to the Liberals - promising to support the minority government on confidence and money matters - Singh not only made progress on progressive issues, he also avoided the constant scrutiny and squirming over whether or not he'd support the government on every budget measure. But the NDP must now pre-position, pre-election, to promote its own brand as distinct from the Liberals.
Consider it a collaborative divorce, or perhaps a separation with room for reconciliation in future. Singh certainly played his cards better than the Ontario NDP in 2014, when then leader Andrea Horwath pulled the plug on Wynne's progressive budget only to propel the Liberals to a majority government in the election that followed (as an MPP at the time, he doubtless learned from Horwath's blunder).
The 40th anniversary is fast approaching for the 1985 minority accord between Liberals and New Democrats that achieved great things. Will there be another minority in 2025, if Ford's early election goes awry?
In a campaign, you never know how votes will split and campaigns will come together at the finish line. But, just as there's no telling how the next Ontario election will turn out, no one can say when that early campaign call will come - if ever.
I began by noting the premier's plan for a premature election has been overtaken by events, but perhaps we are all being taken in by his team. Possibly this is merely a call to arms, a way for the Progressive Conservatives to whip their team into following the party line.
The only certainty is that, by launching so much pre-election speculation, the Tories have got the media talking about suspense instead of substance. When you have a fading attraction, distraction can be a fail-safe.
CAPTION: With this week's events making an early federal vote more likely, the last thing Premier Doug Ford wants is to run for re-election with future prime minister Pierre Poilievre cutting Ontario down to size and pushing the premier around, Martin Regg Cohn writes.Christopher Katsarov The Canadian Press file photo
CREDIT: Martin Regg Cohn OPINION

Subject: Prime ministers; Political parties

People: Poilievre, Pierre

Title: Federal vote could derail Ford's plans

Publication title: Toronto Star; Toronto, Ont.

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Sep 6, 2024

Last updated: 2024-09-06

Document 449 of 500

'This is a significant change': How Liberals are reacting to the resignation of their party's campaign director

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/this-is-significant-change-how-liberals-are/docview/3101086697/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27This+is+a+significant+change%27%3A+How+Liberals+are+reacting+to+the+resignation+of+their+party%27s+campaign+director&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-Trailing in the polls and suddenly facing the prospect of an early federal election, many Liberals say there is a welcome chance for renewal now that the governing party's national campaign director is leaving his post.

Another Liberal MP, who agreed to speak on condition of anonymity, told the Star that it's good that Broadhurst is leaving because the Liberals need a "street fighter" leading their campaign against Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives, who are positioning themselves as scrappy populists.
"Going into a campaign like this, renewal is good," the MP said.
Under the Liberals' deal with the NDP, the New Democrats pledged to prop up the government in exchange for progress on progressive policies like dental care for low-income Canadians and broader public pharmacare coverage.
But several Liberals who spoke to the Star on Thursday said the pact never made for smooth sailing with the NDP. One said it will be a challenge to get vote-by-vote support to get laws passed in Parliament, but characterized dealing with the NDP as always "a slog. It will continue to be a slog."
Meanwhile, several Liberal MPs acknowledged to the Star that an earlier election is more likely now that the deal has been scrapped. Some expressed disappointment over the sudden shift in the political landscape, while others downplayed its significance since they don't believe the NDP is willing to immediately join forces with the Bloc Québécois and the Conservatives to topple the government.
"I don't know if it's a well-thought-through strategy," said Liberal MP John McKay. He predicted the NDP will face "relentless pressure" from the Conservatives to declare non-confidence in the government, which will force the NDP to side with the Liberals if it wants to prevent an election over the coming weeks and months.
McKay also said the death of the NDP deal cements Trudeau's position as Liberal leader. The prime minister has insisted he will stay on for the next campaign, but after the party lost a Toronto byelection in June, several Liberals called for him to step down.
McKay said the return to a more precarious minority Parliament should put that issue to rest.
"The prime minister is pretty well locked in now for the next election," he said.
Marcus Powlowski, a Liberal MP from northern Ontario, said he still believes there would be a way to quickly install a new leader if Trudeau changes his mind and decides to resign, but that he's "not contemplating that" since it is outside of his control.
"The prime minister's the prime minister," and he's "earned the right" to decide his own fate, Powlowski said.
At the same time, Powlowski argued that it is in the government's interest to stave off an election. He said he believes voters will start to sour on Poilievre if they have more time to watch him in action, and predicted the Liberals' standing in the polls would improve if economic growth picks up, inflation keeps cooling, and interest rates that have jacked up mortgage payments continue to come down.
Others, like Kingston and the Islands MP Mark Gerretsen, said the Liberals could compete in an earlier election.
"I'm as ready now as I will be a year from now," he said.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau answers media questions following an announcement in Rocky Harbour, Nfld., on Sept. 4, 2024.

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, September 5 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on the forensic timeline of the Sherman murders, a rap video's possible link to a teen's fatal shooting and a look back on the Liberal-NDP deal.
DON'T MISSPhotos reveal the timeline of Barry and Honey Sherman's murders
The forensic timeline for the unsolved murders of a billionaire couple is slowly being revealed. Police photos show the Honey Sherman was not carjacked, as initially speculated, but was in her home and had settled her bags. It's all part of new information that the Star has battled in court for, revealing new details around the Shermans' deaths seven years ago. Kevin Donovan walks you through the evidence - piece by piece.
Who were the Shermans? Barry, founder of generic drug giant Apotex, and his wife Honey were found dead on the deck of their basement swimming pool on Friday, Dec. 15, 2017. No arrests have been made in the years since.To recap: Toxic emails exchanged by the couple may have led to police to chase a murder-suicide theory behind the deaths, though that theory was abandoned after a second set of autopsies pointed to a double murder.A social media video is being investigated after a teen's fatal shooting
Police are looking into a rap video that was filmed and posted shortly before 15-year-old Mario Giddings was shot and killed on Labour Day. The short video was posted to YouTube 48 hours before the shooting. Police did not confirm whether Giddings was one of the people featured in the video, but one source warned rap battles online can lead to explosive confrontations with deadly consequences.
Remembering Mario: The 15-year-old had told his aunt he was anxious about returning to high school, fearing for his life after an altercation with other students before summer break.What the Liberal-NDP deal did - and didn't - accomplish
The deal is dead. After two-and-a-half years, NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh has torn up his party's agreement with Justin Trudeau's Liberals. It doesn't mean an election - at least not yet - but it brings us to the brink. The original deal had 24 pledges spread across major policy areas, some of which got done while others stalled. Raisa Patel and Alex Ballingall look back on the pact.
The nitty gritty: NDP support for a non-confidence vote would trigger an election and the news of the deal's dissolution comes just as Parliament is set to resume on Sept. 16.Althia Raj's take: Singh had a good reason to tear up his deal with Trudeau - and it's not that he wants an election.Meanwhile: Queer advocates want Trudeau to step aside from the Liberal leadership, warning that a Poilievre-led Tory government would be dangerous for LGBTQ+ Canadians.WHAT ELSE
The Bank of Canada cuts interest rate to 4.25 per cent, leaving the door open for larger cuts amid a struggling economy.A 14-year-old student fatally shot four people in a rampage at a Georgia high school, officials say.Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu is demanding open-ended control of Gaza's border with Egypt.Toronto condo sales plummeted almost 15 per cent and prices took a hit, August real estate board data shows.A man has been charged after trying to drive over police cruisers in a Tim Horton's parking lot, Peel police say.Will grocery chains and consumers reward growers for treating migrant farm workers right?The Giller Prize dropped Scotiabank from its name amid ongoing controversy.A lawsuit over the management of The Stronach Group's wealth has been settled out of court, according to the company.Canada has issued new travel advice as the Oropouche virus spread by bug bites sparks outbreaks in Cuba and South America.TTC mismanagement keeps riders in the slow lane. What we really need is fast tracked funding to get us up to speed again.Bowden Francis is learning from adversity and emerging as a key part of the Blue Jays' plans.This Toronto pair put their unorthodox sex lives on film in "Paying for It," premiering at TIFF.POV

Document 451 of 500

Delivering on a six-year-old campaign promise gives Doug Ford something to run on in a snap election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/delivering-on-six-year-old-campaign-promise-gives/docview/3100901983/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Delivering+on+a+six-year-old+campaign+promise+gives+Doug+Ford+something+to+run+on+in+a+snap+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Six years after first campaigning to liberalize alcohol sales in Ontario, Premier Doug Ford has kept a promise that sets the table for the next election.
With beer, wine, cider and premixed cocktails available in 4,187 licensed convenience stores across Ontario as of Thursday morning, Ford finally realized a cornerstone 2018 election pledge.
"Today's expansion delivers on our government's promise to give people in Ontario the same choice and convenience enjoyed by people throughout the rest of Canada," the premier said in a statement.
"With today's announcement, we're also creating new opportunities to produce and sell world-class Ontario-made beer, wine and ready-to-drink beverages in nearly 4,200 convenience stores across the province," he said.
As first announced in May, the Progressive Conservatives are paying the privately run Beer Store $225 million to break the 10-year "master framework agreement" former Liberal premier Kathleen Wynne signed in 2015, which allowed six-packs and wine in 450 supermarkets.
While that deal would have ended on Dec. 31, 2025 with minimal cost to taxpayers, Ford's Tories scrambled to deliver the promise earlier.
That's because they wanted to give themselves the flexibility to call a snap election next spring, a year earlier than the scheduled June 2026 vote.
The Tories retreated from an initial 2019 bid to unilaterally cancel the Beer Store accord after the United States Chamber of Commerce, the world's largest business organization, accused them of "sending a negative signal to U.S. and other international investors about the business and investment climate in Ontario."
That consigned the liberalization scheme onto the back burner for years - though the COVID-19 pandemic forced the government to allow 19,000 restaurateurs and publicans to sell booze to go.
Deriding Ford's gambit as "a $1-billion booze boondoggle," Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie predicted the tab will skyrocket.
"There is no reason for the cost of convenience to be $1 billion," said Crombie, noting the money could have been better used to hire teachers and nurses, build housing and improve transit, among other priorities.
"Doug Ford's buddies have made a killing off his latest giveaway at the expense of Ontario families," she said.
But Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said the plan, which will be fully implemented by the end of October when big box outlets such as Walmart and Costco and other grocery stores can be licensed, is about treating Ontarians as adults.
"People finally have more options to responsibly and conveniently support local Ontario breweries and wineries - or whatever their drink of choice may be - even closer to home, and (this) is another milestone in the government's commitment to deliver more choice and convenience to consumers," he said.
Bethlenfalvy noted that, as part of the reforms, owners of multiple bars and restaurants will be allowed to move wine and spirits between locations to help them manage inventory with less red tape.
As well, supermarkets will be allowed to display alcohol throughout their stores instead of just on designated aisles.
The provincially owned Liquor Control Board of Ontario will retain its monopoly on the sale of spirits such as whisky, gin, vodka, rum and tequila and handle distribution of products to the private retailers.
Against the backdrop of the changes, Ford was asked Tuesday if he was planning to trigger an early election.
The premier would only confirm that "we aren't having an election this year," but was less definitive about ruling out a $150-million vote next spring - despite having a massive majority in the legislature, where the opposition parties cannot thwart his governing agenda.
When reporters asked him why PC cabinet ministers and MPPs are being instructed to decide by Christmas if they are running again, Ford emphasized "you always have to be prepared."
"That's what it comes down to - no matter which way you look at it, we aren't too far from any election, no matter if it's next year (or) the following year," he said.
Ford is ruminating about the early vote because he is leading in public-opinion polls and is concerned about the next federal election that could see Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre topple Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals.
Ontario voters traditionally prefer different parties to be in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time - the Tories dominated federally during the provincial Liberal dynasty from 2003 to 2018.
So Ford's team worries a Poilievre victory could be bad for their fortunes if they wait until 2026, especially if a new federal Tory government makes deep cuts in transfer payments to the provinces.
There is also the looming spectre of the RCMP investigation in the Greenbelt land swap scandal that could jolt the political landscape provincially.
CAPTION: Employees at a 7-Eleven store in Toronto on Wednesday prepare the new beer section for sales to begin at 7 a.m. on Thursday.

Document 452 of 500

Doug Ford's government is humiliating children with this degrading practice. It needs to stop

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-fords-government-is-humiliating-children/docview/3100901672/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford%27s+government+is+humiliating+children+with+this+degrading+practice.+It+needs+to+stop&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is allowing private contractors to strip children as young as 12, to examine their naked bodies, to make them squat and cough, to keep them fully naked while they search and take notes on everything they see.
The government can't, or won't, tell you how often this happens. No government minister will say anything about it all - nothing about the sanctioned, degrading practice of stripping children nude, of humiliating them in the name of safety, of leaving some of the most vulnerable and damaged kids in this province with even more lasting psychological scars.
Those are the headline takeaways from the Star's reporting this week and last on strip searches in Ontario youth jails. To call the stories, by crime reporter Jennifer Pagliaro, outrageous would be an understatement. They are heartbreaking, both in minute detail and broad scope.
They make it very clear how much this government cares about the children - and again, these are children - who are being routinely strip searched in private and public facilities in Ontario both before and after they've been put on trial. Put short, they don't even bother to keep accurate stats on how often this is going on.
Almost everything Pagliaro was able to dig out about the prevalence and details of this practice came not from the ministry responsible, not from the facilities or private companies holding the kids, and certainly not from Michael Parsa, the Progressive Conservative minister ultimately in charge of all of this, but from lawsuits, court records and testimony in criminal trials.
The Ontario Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services has so far stonewalled Pagliaro's attempts to obtain any kind of comprehensive data on youth strip searches. In the process, it managed to reveal that it doesn't even keep particularly detailed stats on how often kids in Ontario are being subjected to a practice the Supreme Court calls "inherently humiliating and degrading."
Pagliaro asked the ministry how many strip searches took place in the province's 27 youth detention facilities (five public, 22 private) between 2022 and 2023. The ministry told her it couldn't answer because it doesn't know. It doesn't collect strip search data from its own facilities in a way that would allow it to be aggregated.
That's bad enough. What's worse is that the government doesn't even bother to collect strip search data at all from the private contractors that run the bulk of all youth facilities in the province. "We do mandate that the records be produced and kept," a spokesman told Pagliaro in a statement, "but we do not collect them."
Running a jail, any jail, isn't easy. Contraband - especially drugs and weapons - is a real issue in any facility holding people of any age charged or convicted of a crime. Guards, like any other worker, have a right to be safe on the job. They also have a duty to keep other prisoners safe from attack. But strip searching is not the answer.
As Pagliaro's reporting, and years of litigation and research, show, forcing prisoners to strip, expose their genitals and have their bodily orifices inspected, not just when they are suspected of smuggling contraband, but routinely, anytime they want to enter or leave a facility, is more than just humiliating. It's traumatic, for prisoners of any age, let alone children. People in prisons have been convicted of crimes, but their punishment is the removal of their liberty, not their dignity.
It's also unnecessary. Full body scanners, like the kind used in all major Canadian airports, could achieve the same aims, without the degradation and trauma. The government, in other words, could have solved this problem years ago.
The ministry told Pagliaro a review of strip search practices in youth jails is now underway. Whatever data that review collects, the correct policy answer is already clear. Doug Ford's government needs to stop strip searching children, now.
CAPTION: The Roy McMurtry Youth Centre in Brampton.

Subject: Children & youth; Prisons; Prisoner treatment; Strip searches

Title: Doug Ford's government is humiliating children with this degrading practice. It needs to stop

My sister was tireless. If ALS hadn't claimed her life, I'm sure she'd be knocking on Doug Ford's door

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/my-sister-was-tireless-if-als-hadnt-claimed-her/docview/3100901606/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=My+sister+was+tireless.+If+ALS+hadn%27t+claimed+her+life%2C+I%27m+sure+she%27d+be+knocking+on+Doug+Ford%27s+door&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: My sister Sheila was a keener.Five years younger, with a disposition far sunnier than mine, she was: a volunteer, an activist, a photographer, a belter-outer of songs, a skilled cook, a committed NDP supporter, a music lover. She believed in people, cherished her family and friends with quiet ferocity, and routinely dispersed whatever clouds she spotted on someone else's horizon. She laughed easily and smiled often.She never lost that contagious smile either, even when she'd lost everything else.Ten years ago, she wore it while participating in the Ice Bucket Challenge, the phenomenon that raised $220 million globally for ALS clinical care and research. Canada's $17.2 million was more per capita than any other country.Sheila could still talk then, though her speech had a drunken slur to it, which she hated. But sitting there in her wheelchair, friends around her, local Vancouver TV cameras rolling, she endured the icy water poured over her head and laughed.That was Sheila and her ALS - indomitable spirit captured in a smile. By the time she died a year later in hospice, age 63, she was trapped inside an unresponsive body, no longer able to move, eat, breathe without help, talk or communicate in any way other than by using her eyes to spell out a sentence, letter by agonizing letter. Amazingly, though, she could still smile.ALS (Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis) is a neurodegenerative disease that attacks the body's voluntary muscles progressively and relentlessly. Ten per cent of cases are genetic, while the rest, like my sister's, are random. Despite ongoing research and some discoveries, there is still no cure, and most people diagnosed with it will die within five years. ALS is unspeakably cruel.Roughly 4,000 Canadians are living with ALS at any one time (in the grim calculus, 1,000 annual deaths cancel out 1,000 new diagnoses), so, unlike cancer, it is not one of healthcare's headliners. ALS is much easier for governments to ignore. Which is tragic."It's such a horrendously expensive disease," says Tammy Moore, CEO of ALS Canada. The need for complex equipment and assistive devices evolves for patients with dismaying speed, but sufferers and their caregivers are often left to the kindness of strangers. Government help across Canada for such needs is uneven and, in Ontario, nonexistent.ALS Canada provides nearly 40 different kinds of necessary equipment to patients, involving purchase, maintenance, shipping, setting up, education - and gets zero dollars from Ontario."We're actually augmenting the system," says Moore. "We're funding it through philanthropy."And don't get her started on the disparities in clinical care. You're in good hands if you live near the five multidisciplinary ALS clinics in Toronto, London, Hamilton, Kingston and Ottawa. But good luck if you live in Kapuskasing or Kirkland Lake.ALS Canada and the Ontario clinics are currently lobbying the government for $6.6 million in funding, says Moore. "After all, one stay in ICU for an ALS patient at Sunnybrook costs $30,000. We're just asking for $5,000 per patient annually." Sounds reasonable.If Sheila were still here, I suspect she'd be at Doug Ford's door, knocking with gusto. She was tireless that way, a committed activist to the end. Six months before she died in 2015, she received the "Exceptional Public Awareness" award from ALS BC, whose executive director spoke at her memorial service.From the beginning, Sheila knew her nearly four-year battle with ALS would end in defeat. That didn't stop her from preparing gracefully for her death while animating each painful day with her own guiding spirit. With assistance, she voted early in the October federal election. On Thanksgiving, a week before she died, she insisted on posting a message. It exhausted her, but she knew she was leaving soon, and saying a final thank you to those she'd loved was urgent.No science will ever prove this, but I suspect that the ALS monster chooses its victims from the ranks of exceptional people, people with great hearts and stunning courage.The Ice Bucket Challenge is history now, with ALS associations back to less frenzied days of lobbying and fundraising. But the hope - for donations, for government support, for a cure - that hope remains.It's stubborn that way. Like a smile that will not fade.
CAPTION: Thi group shot, June 2015, four months before Sheila Tynan died, shows her family with her at the annual ALS Walk in Vancouver. Sheila, front centre, was joined by, seen left to right brother-in-law Dan Kennedy, sister Janice Kennedy, niece Jessica Kennedy, niece Sarah Kennedy, and nephew-in-law Brien Whalen.In this photo from August, 2014, Amir Johnson and Tim Leiweke douse Nazem Kadri in the ALS Ice Bucket Challenge outside the Air Canada Centre.
CREDIT: Janice Kennedy, Contributor

Subject: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis; Lobbying

Location: Canada

Title: My sister was tireless. If ALS hadn't claimed her life, I'm sure she'd be knocking on Doug Ford's door

School washrooms should be the least of our worries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/school-washrooms-should-be-least-our-worries/docview/3100901417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+washrooms+should+be+the+least+of+our+worries&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Least of our worries
Vaping, assaults, culture wars: How the school washroom has become a battleground where many students fear to tread, Sept. 1
As the school year begins, the need for students to access washrooms and feel safe should be the least of their worries but unfortunately for some it is a leading source of fear and apprehension. I was disappointed to read the comments from one parent who said teachers should not require students to sign a sheet before they visit a washroom, claiming that students should have the right to visit a washroom at any time. While the parent raises a valid point, when nature calls, part of a teacher's job is to control who leaves the classroom and where students are. A teacher's task is made more challenging in larger classrooms. While the article offers readers some interesting perspectives on student fears of entering washrooms, the number of distractions that occur in classrooms - from the pandemic to cellphones to student apathy - are more disruptive for students who want to learn but struggle to focus and concentrate on their lessons.
Robert Ariano, Scarborough
We need a wake up call
Canada's student housing shortage: Only 1 in 10 secure dorms as demand surges, Sept. 3
One wonders why our universities have been able to ignore this challenge for so long. Interestingly enough, University of Toronto has one of the leading housing scholars in the country, David Hulchanski. I have read articles in student newspapers for years that report of the plight of students and the lack of housing options. Are there too many academic silos or are university boards out of touch? I hope this crisis is a wake-up call for the universities to join with others in responding to this challenge.
David Walsh, Toronto
Trudeau must love a fight
NDP kills deal that propped up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority government at risk, Sept. 4
The reasons Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should resign have been restated over and over again. His vision is stale, voters are fed up with him, and Liberals need to avoid a devastating defeat to Pierre Poilievre and provide Canadians the political choice they deserve. Some of Trudeau's predecessors have resigned for similar reasons. So, will he say goodbye? No. He loves a good fight. He may seem down and on the ropes, but like Mohammed Ali won many fights from the ropes, Trudeau expects to do the same. Despite what many Canadians say about him, Trudeau is trusting his record. He believes what his party has accomplished speaks for itself. He believes his opponent Poilievre has a good punch, but lacks substance when it comes time to deliver. His tactic appears to be staying the course. As he recently explained to the media during the Liberal retreat in Halifax, he is going to continue using the strong economy to support Canadians in responsible ways, rather than focusing on culture wars and complaining that everything is broken.
James Drake, Kingston
What is Singh thinking?
NDP kills deal that propped up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority government at risk, Sept. 4
Isn't NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh simply helping the Conservatives gain power a little sooner than expected? How does this help the NDP? They don't have a chance against the Conservatives. The fence-sitting NDP are simply putty in the hands of any political party that is willing enough to hop into the political bed with them. They are not a political party. They are a political weathervane.
Douglas Cornish, Ottawa
Poilievre's strategy
Pierre Poilievre's attack on me is a symptom of a larger problem, Aug. 31
Doris Grinspun skillfully exposes the dangerous and Trump-like attacks the Poilievre Conservatives are using against every one and every thing that does not fit their narrow-minded, scare-mongering strategy. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's support of Premier Doug Ford's dismantling of safe injection sites is just one example of their following the Republican-like playbook. Assisted by the Conservative government in Ontario, and right-wing leaders in Alberta and other provinces, the Poilievre Conservatives paint a false picture of Canada. Their flirtation with the so-called "Freedom Convoy" participants in Ottawa and elsewhere is reminiscent of Trump's encouragement of those attacking the U.S. Capitol. Sadly, under a vulnerable and disliked Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, the Liberals have not effectively countered these troubling tactics that Poilievre and the Conservatives have embraced.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls, Ont.
Understanding Ford's likability
Ford has faced controversy after controversy - yet remains as popular as ever, Aug. 29
For some, it comes down to image and presence. Premier Doug Ford has a very wide face, nice hair and the nice beaming smile of a guy who doesn't delve too deeply into issues. While Donald Trump hocks Bibles and gold-coloured running shoes, Ford hawks fast roads, cheap gas, alcohol and gambling. While he makes no one who watches him feel dumb, many viewers may not mind just how canny he is and fail to identify his self-interest and the interest of people in business.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Let's not forget that Premier Doug Ford is a salesman, smooth, jovial, full of promises, but no ability really to make things better. Most of his deeds are very expensive mistakes, for which responsibility slides off him. His many ministers follow his orders, take on responsibilities and the blame when another boondoggle becomes known to the public.
Helga Weick, Toronto
Gratitude for a good deed
Art Gallery of Ontario accepts one of its largest ever private art donations, Sept. 3
Toronto owes businessman Philip Lind's family and estate a debt of gratitude for donating 37 works of art from 24 noted artists including Philip Guston, a leading abstract painter who returned to figurative work; Ai Weiwei, a Chinese dissident artist whose work is on display worldwide; British sculptor Anthony Gormley; and a selection of work from the so-called "Vancouver School" of photographers including Jeff Wall, Stan Douglas and Rodney Graham. My colleague Lind was instrumental in the building of Rogers Communications. I hope to be the first in line to see this collection.
Mark Borkowski, Toronto
CAPTION: Toronto District School Board Director of Education Colleen Russell-Rawlins makes an Instagram video welcoming students back to school.

Document 455 of 500

Singh breaks with Liberals: A loveless marriage, a strategic split

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-breaks-with-liberals/docview/3100782890/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+breaks+with+Liberals%3A+A+loveless+marriage%2C+a+strategic+split&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a marriage of convenience that ended because it was no longer convenient. No need for progressives to shed a tear, or for Liberals to fear the fall of the government, or for Conservatives to celebrate.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh did what he needed to do, what caucus members wanted him to do, what many New Democrat members believed should have been done last year.
On Wednesday, days before New Democrat MPs were set to meet in Montreal, and less than two weeks before two important byelections, Singh pulled the plug on his agreement with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - 10 months before it was set to expire.
The supply and confidence agreement - signed in March of 2022 in the shadows of a capital blockaded by a convoy of truckers and protesters angry over COVID vaccination requirements, and amid the invasion of Ukraine by Russia - is no more.
The deal was framed by Trudeau and Singh as one that offered stability and delivered on the mandate voters gave parliamentarians in the fall of 2021, when they returned MPs to Ottawa in nearly the same formation as in the previous Parliament.
Canadians wanted MPs to "work together to put people and families first. Deliver results and build a better future," Trudeau said in announcing the deal. "This is what Canadians expect from their politicians."
But what Canadians have also learned to expect from politicians is that they make decisions that best serve their partisan interests.
Singh signed on because the agreement provided a good answer to the question of why the NDP was propping up the Grits. With a minority of seats in the House of Commons, the Liberals needed a dance partner to avoid an early election call. The NDP thought it could be seen as extracting concessions from the Liberals - action on climate change, reconciliation, affordable housing, and big dream items such as a new national dental care program, progress toward universal pharmacare, and a ban on the use of "scabs" during lockouts or strikes - in return for its continued support. The agreement allowed the NDP to vote with the Liberals without appearing spineless.
But as the deal wore on, it became less of a blessing and more of a liability.
In June, Singh's team concluded there was little left to accomplish. They had extracted all they thought they could get from it.
They also faced a bigger problem: it was the Liberals who took most of the credit from programs such as dental care. There was no bump in support for the New Democrats who sit at 18 per cent, according to the latest polling by Abacus Data.
Meanwhile, Singh and the NDP spent months being defined by the Conservatives as one and the same as the Liberals, an "NDP-Liberal coalition" responsible for all the ills plaguing the country.
With the next election gearing up to be a change election - 86 per cent of those polled say it's time for one - the Conservatives' framing wrote off Singh as a change option.
It was a brilliant strategic move by the Conservatives, and a big hurdle for the NDP.
It's one the party hopes to overcome in announcing that the deal is ripped up, with an election-style ad showcasing Singh as ready to defeat both Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives and Trudeau's Liberals in order to fight for the middle class.
The next few months are guaranteed to be rockier but, unless the parties miscalculate, this announcement won't bring much immediate change.
Neither the NDP nor the Liberals want an election campaign with the Conservatives leading massively in public support.
Poilievre is itching to go to the polls. On Wednesday, he said he'd test the NDP leader's resolve to distance himself from the Grits with a non-confidence motion at the first chance he gets.
But that opportunity might not come until November, if the Liberals delay offering opposition days to the Conservatives. It could come even later if the Liberals decide to prorogue Parliament.
New Democrats are getting ready for any possibility. If the House of Commons returns as planned on Sept. 16, the NDP's national director is already laying the groundwork for continued support of the Liberal government.
Speaking to CBC last week, Anne McGrath said that while the "Liberals have kind of run out of gas," there are things the NDP wants to accomplish, such as getting the government to "improve the EI system."
The party may still have to play that game - the we-demanded-the-Liberals-act-and-they-acted game - to save face and ensure the short-term continuity of this Parliament. Few New Democrats want a repeat of 2005, when the NDP pulled their support from Paul Martin's Liberal minority government and ushered in a decade of Conservative rule under Stephen Harper.
Other than the NDP, only the Bloc Québécois can offer the Liberals a lifeline. But it too has been targeted by the Conservatives, who greatly exaggerate the BQ's closeness to the Liberals in order to score points in Quebec. With the Bloc still strong in public support, however, it has less to fear about triggering a trip to the polls.
So until Singh and the NDP are confident their new rebranding has them leading as the progressive option for change, the result of Wednesday's announcement may be more sizzle than substance.
CREDIT: Althia raj OPINION

Subject: Agreements; Politics; Political parties

Location: Toronto Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Singh, Jagmeet

Title: Singh breaks with Liberals:   A loveless marriage, a strategic split

Document 456 of 500

Singh breaks with Liberals: NDP's exit from 'supply and confidence' agreement could lead to early election call

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/singh-breaks-with-liberals/docview/3100782882/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Singh+breaks+with+Liberals%3A+NDP%27s+exit+from+%27supply+and+confidence%27+agreement+could+lead+to+early+election+call&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The prospect of a snap federal election is suddenly on the table after NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh prematurely killed the 2022 deal he struck to support Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's minority government.
The early collapse of the agreement - which gave Trudeau the necessary votes to pass three federal budgets and operate as if he had a majority - does not automatically trigger an election.
That's because the 24 NDP members of Parliament could continue to support the 154-member minority Liberal government on a vote-by-vote basis - which is exactly what the Liberals are counting on - rather than automatically moving to declare lost confidence in Trudeau.
It does mean, however, that Singh could introduce a non-confidence vote or vote for any such motion the Conservatives or Bloc Québécois could present, potentially plunging the country into a federal election campaign - which the Conservatives immediately demanded, and Trudeau cautioned against. Parliament is set to resume sitting on Sept. 16.
But ahead of that, Singh released a campaign-style video statement on social media Wednesday to announce he was pulling out of the "supply and confidence agreement" 10 months before it was set to expire because the Liberals cannot be trusted to fight or win what he called a battle "for the future of the middle class."
He did not cite specific policy differences except to say the governing Liberals "will always cave to corporate greed," "have let people down" and "don't deserve another chance."
Over images of the NDP leader glad-handing with Canadians and party supporters, and walking with a child, Singh talked tough about getting ready for the next federal vote.
He said the Conservatives would form a government that would make deep cuts that will hurt workers, retirees, young people, patients and families "in order to give more to big corporations and wealthy CEOs."
"In the next federal election, Canadians will choose between Pierre Poilievre's callous cuts or hope - hope that when we stand united, we win, that Canada's middle class will once again thrive together.
"I am running for prime minister because, together, we can and will stop Conservative cuts," he said.
Singh was not available to answer media questions, but a senior NDP source central to the decision, speaking on the condition they not be named, told the Star there wasn't a singular issue that tipped the scales in recent weeks. The insider acknowledged that the Liberals' handling of last month's railway shutdown, and the government's order that forced the labour dispute into binding arbitration, did little to inspire renewed confidence in the deal.
Wednesday's move was a "long time coming," and discussions with caucus began in earnest when last spring's session of Parliament started winding down, said the source.
"I think there's a number of frustrations, as always," the source said, characterizing Liberal progress on issues like housing and grocery prices as "exceedingly slow" and "halfway measures."
"We've delivered as much as we're going to, as much as they're willing to, and so it's just time to move on now."
The NDP says it is in solid financial shape, with the senior source saying the party paid off its $22-million loan from the 2021 campaign this year and can withstand an early election call - a possibility they said could happen "sooner rather than later."
The party has nominated more than 50 candidates, with "many more ready to go," and the source said Wednesday's decision to exit the deal is expected to push more people to come forward.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, who wrote to Singh last week demanding he pull out of the deal, dared the NDP leader to follow through on his latest "media stunt."
Speaking in Nanaimo, B.C. - where Trudeau will meet his restive Liberal caucus next week - Poilievre said Singh's decision to can the deal finally offers the Conservatives a chance to topple the government.
"But it will depend on whether sellout Singh is serious or a stuntman. Will he vote for a carbon tax election at the earliest opportunity or will he vote again to keep the costly coalition with Trudeau in power?" Poilievre said.
Poilievre called on Singh to vote non-confidence in Trudeau "at the earliest opportunity" and, speaking in French, he called on Bloc Québécois Leader Yves-François Blanchet to do the same.
However, it is not clear how soon in the parliamentary calendar such a vote could be called. This fall, the Liberal government is expected to allot seven days for opposition party motions, with five of those to the official Opposition Conservatives, and one each to the NDP and Bloc. If it wants to buy time, the Trudeau government could delay those days until the late fall, meaning a confidence vote could be pushed to the end of November or early December.
Although Liberals last week at a cabinet retreat publicly predicted the deal with the NDP would endure until next spring as planned, privately Liberal sources have said they expected Singh might pull out before the end of the year because he was not gaining traction in the polls.
Still, on Wednesday, Singh's move took many of them by surprise.
Trudeau, speaking less than an hour after Singh's video went public, said he would remain focused on ensuring Canada's economic recovery continues, hailing the Bank of Canada's decision Wednesday to cut the interest rate to 4.25 per cent as proof his government's approach is working, and underscoring progress on $10-a-day daycare, dental care and free insulin and contraceptives.
In Rocky Harbour, N.L., to highlight federal funding for a provincial school lunch program, Trudeau said he looked forward to talking to Singh about how they could continue to work together to "invest in the future, because that's what we're doing." But he framed Singh's decision as playing politics.
"The NDP is going to have to make decisions about whether or not they want to stand with Pierre Poilievre, who's going to cut the school food programs, cut dental care, not deliver on pharmacare, or whether they're going to focus on what it means to Canadians to be there to give them support through this difficult time," he said.
"Every politician gets to choose whether they want to play politics or whether they want to serve Canadians, and I certainly hope the NDP will stay true to its fundamental values, which is making sure that Canadians get the support they need, and keeping away the austerity, the cuts and the damage that will be done by Conservatives if they get the chance," Trudeau said.
Trudeau and Singh negotiated and signed their co-operation agreement in the aftermath of chaos caused by the truckers' convoy blockade of Ottawa and border crossings in the spring of 2022, and as war in Ukraine broke out. Although the Conservatives, then in the midst of a leadership race, blasted it as a Liberal-NDP "coalition," it was never a formal coalition. It never guaranteed the NDP positions in cabinet or a veto on decision-making.
Instead, the deal secured advancements on a series of progressive policies, which the Liberals agreed to in exchange for Singh's support in votes of confidence in the Commons and in committees on a range of budgetary and key policy matters like dental care, pharmacare and federal anti-scab legislation.
Polls show the NDP is at between 15 per cent and 20 per cent in public support, and has gained nothing from supporting the Liberals, while the Liberals under Trudeau have plunged in the polls to the low to mid-20s in the face of withering criticism by the Conservatives. Meanwhile, the Conservatives, pulling in more than 40 per cent in most polls, have a wide lead that puts them in reach of a majority government if a vote were held today.
Just a few short months ago, Singh told the Star's Althia Raj that he viewed the deal as a way to leverage additional changes from the Trudeau government, underscoring the ongoing need to improve the dental care program and benefits for disabled Canadians. "We want to see the work finished," he said then.
However, a New Democrat with a senior position in Canada's labour movement, who spoke to the Star earlier this week on the condition they not be named, said the Liberals' decision to impose binding arbitration to end a work stoppage, after Canada's two largest railways and the union representing their workers failed to reach a new contract, crossed a line for the NDP.
"That decision was pretty bruising to the relationship," they told the Star.
In an interview with the Star last month, veteran New Democrat MP Charlie Angus said he felt his party had "achieved what we were going to achieve in the agreement."
"There's an election coming. So now we need to begin a transition. Where are we going next?" he said.
CREDIT: Tonda MacCharles and Raisa Patel Toronto Star
