Full text: Unnecessary source of worry
Vaping, assaults, culture wars: How the school washroom has become a battleground where many students fear to tread, Sept. 1
As the school year begins, the need for students to access washrooms and feel safe should be the least of their worries, but unfortunately for some it is a leading source of fear and apprehension. I was disappointed to read the comments from one parent who said teachers should not require students to sign a sheet before they visit a washroom, claiming that students should have the right to visit a washroom at any time. While the parent raises a valid point, when nature calls, part of a teacher's job is to control who leaves the classroom and where students are. A teacher's task is made more challenging in larger classrooms. While the article offers readers some interesting perspectives on student fears of entering washrooms, the number of distractions that occur in classrooms - from the pandemic to cellphones to student apathy - are more disruptive for students who want to learn but struggle to focus and concentrate on their lessons.
Robert Ariano, Scarborough
Universities need a wake-up call
Canada's student housing shortage: Only 1 in 10 secure dorms as demand surges, Sept. 3
One wonders why our universities have been able to ignore this challenge for so long. Interestingly enough, University of Toronto has one of the leading housing scholars in the country, David Hulchanski. I have read articles in student newspapers for years that report of the plight of students and the lack of housing options. Are there too many academic silos or are university boards out of touch? I hope this crisis is a wake-up call for the universities to join with others in responding to this challenge.
David Walsh, Toronto
Trudeau must love a fight
NDP kills deal that propped up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority government at risk, Sept. 4
The reasons Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should resign have been restated over and over again. His vision is stale, voters are fed up with him, and Liberals need to avoid a devastating defeat to Pierre Poilievre and provide Canadians the political choice they deserve. Some of Trudeau's predecessors have resigned for similar reasons. So, will he say goodbye? No. He loves a good fight. He may seem down and on the ropes, but like Mohammed Ali won many fights from the ropes, Trudeau expects to do the same. Despite what many Canadians say about him, Trudeau is trusting his record. He believes what his party has accomplished speaks for itself. He believes his opponent Poilievre has a good punch, but lacks substance when it comes time to deliver. His tactic appears to be staying the course. As he recently explained to the media during the Liberal retreat in Halifax, he is going to continue using the strong economy to support Canadians in responsible ways, rather than focusing on culture wars and complaining that everything is broken.
James Drake, Kingston, Ont.
What is Singh thinking?
NDP kills deal that propped up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority government at risk, Sept. 4
Isn't NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh simply helping the Conservatives gain power a little sooner than expected? How does this help the NDP? They don't have a chance against the Conservatives. The fence-sitting NDP are simply putty in the hands of any political party that is willing enough to hop into the political bed with them. They are not a political party. They are a political weather vane.
Douglas Cornish, Ottawa

Doris Grinspun skilfully exposes the dangerous and Trump-like attacks the Poilievre Conservatives are using against every one and every thing that does not fit their narrow-minded, scare-mongering strategy. Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's support of Premier Doug Ford's dismantling of safe injection sites is just one example of their following the Republican-like playbook. Sadly, under a vulnerable and disliked Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, the Liberals have not effectively countered these troubling tactics that Poilievre and the Conservatives have embraced.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls, Ont.
Understanding Ford's likability
Ford has faced controversy after controversy - yet remains as popular as ever, Aug. 29
For some, it comes down to image and presence. Premier Doug Ford has a very wide face, nice hair and the nice beaming smile of a guy who doesn't delve too deeply into issues. While Donald Trump hocks Bibles and gold-coloured running shoes, Ford hawks fast roads, cheap gas, alcohol and gambling. While he makes no one who watches him feel dumb, many viewers may not mind just how canny he is and fail to identify his self-interest and the interest of people in business.
Ron Charach, Toronto
Let's not forget that Premier Doug Ford is a salesman, smooth, jovial, full of promises, but no ability really to make things better. Most of his deeds are very expensive mistakes, for which responsibility slides off him. His many ministers follow his orders, take on responsibilities and the blame when another boondoggle becomes known to the public.
Helga Weick, Toronto
Gratitude for a good deed
Art Gallery of Ontario accepts one of its largest ever private art donations, Sept. 3
Toronto owes businessman Philip Lind's family and estate a debt of gratitude for donating 37 works of art from 24 noted artists including Philip Guston, a leading abstract painter who returned to figurative work; Ai Weiwei, a Chinese dissident artist whose work is on display worldwide; British sculptor Anthony Gormley; and a selection of work from the so-called "Vancouver School" of photographers including Jeff Wall, Stan Douglas and Rodney Graham. My colleague Lind was instrumental in the building of Rogers Communications. I hope to be the first in line to see this collection.
Mark Borkowski, Toronto

Document 458 of 500

Strip searches of children must stop now

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/strip-searches-children-must-stop-now/docview/3100781950/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Strip+searches+of+children+must+stop+now&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.14&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is allowing private contractors to strip children as young as 12, to examine their naked bodies, to make them squat and cough, to keep them fully naked while they search and take notes on everything they see.
The government can't, or won't, tell you how often this happens. No government minister will say anything about it all - nothing about the sanctioned, degrading practice of stripping children nude, of humiliating them in the name of safety, of leaving some of the most vulnerable and damaged kids in this province with even more lasting psychological scars.
Those are the headline takeaways from the Star's reporting this week and last on strip searches in Ontario youth jails. To call the stories, by crime reporter Jennifer Pagliaro, outrageous would be an understatement. They are heartbreaking, both in minute detail and broad scope.
They make it very clear how much this government cares about the children - and again, these are children - who are being routinely strip searched in private and public facilities in Ontario, both before and after they've been put on trial. Put short, they don't even bother to keep accurate stats on how often this is going on.
Almost everything Pagliaro was able to dig out about the prevalence and details of this practice came not from the ministry in charge, not from the facilities or private companies holding the kids, and certainly not from Michael Parsa, the Progressive Conservative minister ultimately in charge of all of this, but from lawsuits, court records and testimony in criminal trials.
The Ontario Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services has so far stonewalled Pagliaro's attempts to obtain any kind of comprehensive data on youth strip searches. In the process, it managed to reveal that it doesn't even keep particularly detailed stats on how often kids in Ontario are being subjected to a practice the Supreme Court calls "inherently humiliating and degrading."
Pagliaro asked the ministry how many strip searches took place in the province's 27 youth detention facilities (five public, 22 private) between 2022 and 2023. The ministry told her it couldn't answer because it doesn't know. It doesn't collect strip search data from its own facilities in a way that would allow it to be aggregated.
That's bad enough. What's worse is that the government doesn't even bother to collect strip search data at all from the private contractors that run the bulk of all youth facilities in the province. "We do mandate that the records be produced and kept," a spokesperson told Pagliaro in a statement, "but we do not collect them."
Running a jail, any jail, isn't easy. Contraband - especially drugs and weapons - is a real issue in any facility holding people of any age charged or convicted of a crime. Guards, like any other worker, have a right to be safe on the job. They also have a duty to keep other prisoners safe from attack. But strip searching is not the answer.
As Pagliaro's reporting, and years of litigation and research, show, forcing prisoners to strip, expose their genitals and have their bodily orifices inspected, not just when they are suspected of smuggling contraband, but routinely, anytime they want to enter or leave a facility, is more than just humiliating. It's traumatic, for prisoners of any age, let alone children. People in prisons have been convicted of crimes, but their punishment is the removal of their liberty, not their dignity.
It's also unnecessary. Full body scanners, like the kind used in all major Canadian airports, could achieve the same aims, without the degradation and trauma. The government, in other words, could have solved this problem years ago.
The ministry told Pagliaro a review of strip search practices in youth jails is now underway. Whatever data that review collects, the correct policy answer is already clear. Doug Ford's government needs to stop strip searching children, now.
CAPTION: The Ontario Ministry of Children, Community and Social Services doesn't collect strip search data from its own facilities, including the Roy McMurtry Youth Centre in Brampton, in a way that would allow it to be aggregated.Andrew Francis Wallace Toronto Star file photo
CREDIT: ;

Document 459 of 500

Jagmeet Singh had a good reason to tear up his deal with Justin Trudeau - and it's not that he wants an election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/jagmeet-singh-had-good-reason-tear-up-his-deal/docview/3100963421/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jagmeet+Singh+had+a+good+reason+to+tear+up+his+deal+with+Justin+Trudeau+-+and+it%27s+not+that+he+wants+an+election&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a marriage of convenience that ended because it was no longer convenient. No need for progressives to shed a tear, or for Liberals to fear the fall of the government, or for Conservatives to celebrate.
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh did what he needed to do, what caucus members wanted him to do, what many New Democrats members believed should have been done last year.
On Wednesday, days before NDP MPs were set to meet in Montreal, and less than two weeks before two important byelections, Singh pulled the plug on his agreement with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau - 10 months before it was set to expire.
The supply and confidence agreement - signed in March of 2022 in the shadows of a capital blockaded by a convoy of truckers and protesters angry over COVID vaccination requirements, and the invasion of Ukraine by Russia - is no more. The deal was framed by Trudeau and Singh as one that offered stability and delivered on the mandate voters gave parliamentarians in the fall of 2021, when they returned MPs to Ottawa in nearly the same formation as in the previous Parliament. Canadians wanted MPs to "work together to put people and families first. Deliver results and build a better future," Trudeau said in announcing the deal. "This is what Canadians expect from their politicians."
But what Canadians have also learned to expect from politicians is that they make decisions that best serve their partisan interests.
Singh signed on because the agreement provided a good answer to the question of why the NDP was propping up the Grits. With a minority of seats in the House of Commons, the Liberals needed a dance partner to avoid an early election call. The NDP thought it could be seen as extracting concessions from the Liberals - action on climate change, reconciliation, affordable housing, and big dream items such as a new national dental care program, progress toward a universal pharmacare, and a ban on the use of "scabs" during lock outs or strikes - in return for its continued support. The agreement allowed the NDP to vote with the Liberals without appearing spineless.
But as the deal wore on, it became less of a blessing and more of a liability.
In June, Singh's team concluded there was little left to accomplish. They had extracted all they thought they could get from it.
They also faced a bigger problem: it was the Liberals who took most of the credit from programs such as dental care. There was no bump in support for the New Democrats who sit at 18 per cent, according to the latest polling by Abacus Data .
Meanwhile, Singh and the NDP spent months being defined by the Conservatives as one and the same as the Liberals, an "NDP-Liberal coalition" responsible for all the ills plaguing the country.
With the next election gearing up to be a change election - 86 per cent of those polled say it's time for one - the Conservatives' framing wrote off Singh as a change option.
It was a brilliant strategic move by the Conservatives, and a big hurdle for the NDP.
It's one the party hopes to overcome in announcing that the deal is ripped up, with an election-style ad showcasing Singh as ready to defeat both Pierre Poilievre's Conservatives and Trudeau's Liberals in order to fight for the middle class.
The next few months are guaranteed to be rockier but, unless the parties miscalculate, this announcement won't bring much immediate change.
Neither the NDP nor the Liberals want an election campaign with the Conservatives leading massively in public support.
Poilievre is itching to go to the polls. On Wednesday, he said he'd test the NDP leader's resolve to distance himself from the Grits with a non-confidence motion at the first chance he gets.
But that opportunity might not come until November, if the Liberals delay offering opposition days to the Conservatives. It could come even later if the Liberals decide to prorogue Parliament.
New Democrats are getting ready for any possibility. If the House of Commons returns as planned on Sept. 16, the NDP's national director is already laying the groundwork for continued support of the Liberal government.
Speaking to CBC last week, Anne McGrath said that while the "Liberals have kind of run out of gas," there are things the NDP wants to accomplish such as getting the government to "improve the EI system."
The party may still have to play that game - the we-demanded-the-Liberals-act-and-they-acted game - to save face and ensure the short-term continuity of this Parliament. Few New Democrats want a repeat of 2005, when the NDP pulled their support from Paul Martin's Liberal minority government and ushered in a decade of Conservative rule under Stephen Harper.
Other than the NDP, only the Bloc Québécois can offer the Liberals a lifeline. But it too has been targeted by the Conservatives, who greatly exaggerate the BQ's closeness to the Liberals in order to score points in Quebec. With the Bloc still strong in public support, however, it has less to fear about triggering a trip to the polls.
So until Singh and the NDP are confident their new rebranding has them leading as the progressive option for change, the result of Wednesday's announcement may be more sizzle than substance.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh prepare for the start of the federal election English-language leaders' debate in Gatineau, Que., on Sept. 9, 2021.

Subject: Agreements; Dental care; Politics; Prime ministers

Title: Jagmeet Singh had a good reason to tear up his deal with Justin Trudeau - and it's not that he wants an election

Document 460 of 500

NDP kills deal that propped up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority government at risk

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ndp-kills-deal-that-propped-up-justin-trudeaus/docview/3100546773/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+kills+deal+that+propped+up+Justin+Trudeau%27s+Liberals%2C+putting+his+minority+government+at+risk&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-New Democrat Leader Jagmeet Singh is ripping up his 2022 deal with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to support the Liberal government- a move that immediately makes for a more unstable minority Parliament and could create the conditions for a snap election call.
It does not automatically trigger an election because the 24 NDP members of Parliament could continue to support the 154-member minority Liberal government on a vote-by-vote basis - which is exactly what the Liberals are counting on - rather than automatically moving to declare lost confidence in Trudeau.
The collapse of the agreement does, however, mean Singh could introduce a non-confidence vote or vote for any such motion the Conservatives or Bloc Québécois could present, potentially plunging the country into a federal campaign - which the Conservatives immediately demanded.
In a recorded video statement released Wednesday on social media, Singh said he is cancelling the "supply and confidence agreement" 10 months early because the Liberals cannot be trusted to fight or win what he says is a battle "for the future of the middle class."
Singh said the governing Liberals have proved they "will always cave to corporate greed," and "don't deserve another chance from Canadians," adding the Liberals are "careering towards irrelevancy."
Singh talked tough about getting ready for the next federal vote, suggesting the Conservatives would form a government that would make deep cuts that will hurt workers, retirees, young people, patients, and families "in order to give more to big corporations and wealthy CEOs."
"The fact is, the Liberals are too weak, too selfish and too beholden to corporate interests to fight for people. They cannot be changed, they cannot restore the hope, they cannot stop the Conservatives," he said. "But we can."
"In the next federal election, Canadians will choose between Pierre Poilievre's callous cuts or hope - hope that when we stand united, we win, that Canada's middle class will once again thrive together."
"I am running for prime minister because together, we can and will stop Conservative cuts," he said.
Trudeau, speaking less than an hour after Singh's withdrawal from the deal, turned the NDP leader's comments back on the New Democrats.
He said he would remain focused on ensuring Canada's economic recovery continues, hailing the Bank of Canada's decision Wednesday to cut the interest rate to 4.25 per cent as proof his government's approach is working. He said there is more work to do to deliver dental care, free insulin and contraceptives, and environmental policies that will improve the lives of Canadians.
In Rocky Harbour, Newfoundland to highlight federal money for its school lunch program, Trudeau said he looked forward to speaking with Singh "about how we're going to continue to demonstrate that confident countries invest in their citizens, invest in the future, because that's what we're doing."
"The NDP is going to have to make decisions about whether or not they want to stand with Pierre Poilievre who's going to cut the school food programs, cut dental care, not deliver on pharmacare, or whether they're going to focus on what it means to Canadians to be there to give them support through this difficult time.
"Every politician gets to choose whether they want to play politics or whether they want to serve Canadians, and I certainly hope the NDP will stay true to its fundamental values, which is making sure that Canadians get the support they need, and keeping away the austerity, the cuts and the damage that will be done by Conservatives if they get the chance."
Just last week, Liberal House Leader Karina Gould at a cabinet retreat predicted the deal would endure, saying they had made good progress on things like dental care and pharmacare, and there was more to do.
However, polls show the NDP is at between 15-20 per cent approval ratings, has gained nothing at all from supporting the Liberals, and the Liberals under Trudeau have plunged in the polls, in the face of withering criticism by the Conservatives that they had formed a "costly coalition."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre wrote Singh last week to pull the plug on the deal with Trudeau and called for a fall "carbon tax" election, and in a statement after Singh's announcement, Poilievre called it a "media stunt."
"Sellout Singh refuses to state whether the NDP will vote with non-confidence to cause a carbon tax election at the first chance," said Poilievre, saying the NDP leader's support for the Liberals' increased carbon tax "will drive Canadians to food banks and grind our economy to a halt- killing hundreds of thousands of jobs."
New Democrats say Singh's move comes after a summer in which senior representatives discussed next steps with party members and whether now was the time to pull out of the deal.
Just a few short months ago, Singh told the Star's Althia Raj that he viewed the deal as a way to leverage additional changes from the Trudeau government, underscoring the ongoing need to improve the dental care program and benefits for disabled Canadians. "We want to see the work finished," he said then.
However, a New Democrat with a senior position in Canada's labour movement, who spoke to the Star earlier this week on the condition they not be named, said the Liberals' decision to impose binding arbitration to end a work stoppage after Canada's two largest railways and the union representing their workers failed to reach a new contract, crossed a line for the NDP.
Singh's response to that move - calling the Trudeau Liberals "cowardly, anti-worker and proof that they will always cave to corporate greed" - the language Singh used Wednesday - was evidence the New Democrats were approaching the "natural end" of the agreement, the source said.
"That decision was pretty bruising to the relationship," they told the Star.
In an interview with the Star last month, veteran NDP MP Charlie Angus said he felt his party had "achieved what we were going to achieve in the agreement."
"There's an election coming. So now we need to begin a transition. Where are we going next?" he said.
A senior NDP source central to the decision told the Star on the condition they not be named that Wednesday's move was a "long time coming," and that discussions with caucus began in earnest when last spring's session of parliament started winding down.
"I think it was just becoming clear it's time to move ahead now and to kind of reassert our own view of the world," the source said.
There wasn't a singular issue that tipped the scales in recent weeks, the source said, with the caveat that the Liberals' handling of last month's railway shutdown did little to inspire renewed confidence in the deal.
"I think there's a number of frustrations, as always," the source said, characterizing the Liberals' progress on issues like housing and grocery prices as "exceedingly slow" and "halfway measures."
"We've delivered as much as we're going to, as much as they're willing to. And so, it's just time to move on now," the source said.
The source insisted the party is in solid financial shape to withstand an early election call - a possibility they said could happen "sooner rather than later."
The NDP paid off its $22-million 2021 campaign loan earlier this year, and plans to release more details on its election preparedness in the coming weeks.
The party has nominated more than 50 candidates with "many more ready to go," the source said, adding that the decision to exit the deal is expected to push more people to come forward.
In the aftermath of chaos caused by the truckers' convoy blockade of Ottawa and border crossings in the spring of 2022, Trudeau and Singh negotiated and signed the co-operation agreement.
Although the Conservatives, then in the midst of a leadership race, blasted it as a Liberal-NDP "coalition," it was never a formal coalition. It never guaranteed the NDP positions in cabinet or a veto on decision-making.
Instead, the deal secured advancements on a series of progressive policies, which the Liberals agreed to in exchange for Singh's support in votes of confidence in the Commons and in committees on a range of budgetary and key policy matters like dental care, pharmacare and federal anti-scab legislation.
The NDP's backing on confidence votes until June 2025, four months before the legislated fixed date for an October 2025 election, was meant to avert triggering an early vote.
The law only applies to majority governments, and is not binding on minority parliaments, which can face a confidence test at any time.
With the NDP support, the Liberals could count on its bills passing with majority support in the Commons. Without it, the Liberals could try to woo Bloc Québécois support.
Currently, the party standings in the House of Commons are Liberals at 154, Conservatives at 119, Bloc Québécois at 32, NDP at 24, Green Party at two. There are three Independent MPs, and four seats are vacant (with two byelections scheduled for Sept. 16, the day that Parliament resumes sitting for the fall session).
Singh was elected leader of the NDP in 2017 to replace Thomas Mulcair, and in his first federal campaign in 2019 he led the party to a 20-seat drop, placing it as fourth in the Commons. In the following 2021 campaign, he managed to increase the party's total by one seat.
Although it is being cancelled 10 months early, both Singh and Trudeau can check off - in some form - most of the deal's 24 objectives.
The pact saw the creation of a national dental coverage plan that has delivered oral health services to hundreds of thousands of Canadians.
It resulted in the introduction and passage of "anti-scab" legislation, which banned the use of replacement workers in situations where employees in federally regulated industries were locked out from the job or went on strike.
Another bill aimed at protecting jobs in the transition into a clean energy economy also became law under the arrangement.
Both parties also took steps to introduce election reforms, while other goals, like tabling legislation focused on improving long-term care and ushering in a homebuyers' bill of rights, have yet to fully materialize.
Perhaps the most contentious of the deal's major pillars was a central promise to pass legislation committing to a universal, national pharmacare program by the end of 2023.
Negotiations to hash out the details of that framework nearly pushed the New Democrats to prematurely abandon the deal; eventually, a bill was introduced earlier this year that outlined a plan to first cover diabetes and birth control drugs and devices, and laid out a road map for wider government-paid drug coverage. That bill has passed the House of Commons, but not the Senate.
But as much as the deal - which was struck in early 2022 in the shadow of a receding global pandemic, the historic invocation of the Emergencies Act and Russia's invasion of Ukraine - was meant to secure parliamentary stability, it also boxed Singh into an increasingly precarious political position.
As the Liberals began stumbling in the polls and Poilievre's Conservatives ascended to new heights, Singh's decision to stand by Trudeau while simultaneously claiming to keep him in check did little to boost the NDP's fortunes - or, at times, its leader's credibility.
And while the arrangement did give the fourth-party-status New Democrats a tantalizing taste of what governing might be like, they too felt pressure inside the deal.
One year in, faith in the pact - which operated on a foundational principle of "no surprises" - was shaken when the NDP were blindsided by proposed changes to the Liberals' then-firearms bill. Nearly a year after that, facing heat from party membership, Singh's inner circle planned for a potential election scenario when pharmacare talks began to stall.
But the New Democrats wanted to see the health-care centrepieces of the deal through, and frequently pointed to issues outside the deal structure, like affordability measures, the Canada Disability Benefit and accountability on foreign interference, as areas where they could push for additional action.
CAPTION: NDP kills deal that propped up Justin Trudeau's Liberals, putting his minority government at riskPrime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh.

Document 461 of 500

Will higher fines stop drivers from blocking the box? + a teenage boy shot dead over the long weekend

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/will-higher-fines-stop-drivers-blocking-box/docview/3100546690/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+higher+fines+stop+drivers+from+blocking+the+box%3F+%2B+a+teenage+boy+shot+dead+over+the+long+weekend&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Wednesday, September 4 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Toronto's newest efforts to solve gridlock, a teenage boy shot dead over the long weekend and the fate of a fund critical to Canada's green economy.
DON'T MISSToronto wants $450 for blocking the box - will anyone actually get ticketed?
Blocking the box might be downtown Toronto's most ubiquitous traffic offence. It's gotten so bad, city council recently asked Ontario for permission to permanently hike the fine for the offence from $85 to $450 a ticket, Mahdis Habibinia reports. But habitual box-blockers are rarely ticketed, with policing experts pondering: "What's the point if we're not enforcing these rules?"
Traffic jammed: In 10 minutes at one downtown intersection last month, the Star counted 26 box-blockers - drivers rushing into busy intersections right as the light turns red - over just six red light cycles. None got tickets.Wait, what? That's little surprise - according to historical data, Toronto police have issued less than one ticket a day for box-blocking over the last five years.Meanwhile: People are hesitant to brave Toronto's perpetual gridlock to see a friend or partner on the opposite end of the city - and it's tearing relationships apart.A 15-year-old boy was killed in a Labour Day shooting
Mario Giddings was anxious about returning to high school, telling his aunt he feared for his life after an altercation with other students before the summer break. He wouldn't get the chance to start Grade 11, Calvi Leon reports. The evening before school began, the 15-year-old was shot dead outside a pizza shop in what police are calling a targeted attack. Take a look at what happened.
The motive: It's unclear whether Mario's shooting was related to his fight at the end of last school year, as police continue searching for suspects and a motive.Word from the family: Mario's aunt said she'd been trying to transfer him to a new school this week. Now she's left scrambling for answers. "I want justice for him," she told the Star.A bloody weekend: Toronto police are sounding the alarm over surging gun violence in the city after three homicides - including Mario's - occurred over the long weekend. Here's how we can remedy the situation.Scandal threatens to tear down this clean-tech powerhouse
Sustainable Development Technology Canada was once the driver of Canada's nascent clean-technology industry. But after weathering drastic overhauls following a damaging mismanagement scandal, it's become a punching bag for Justin Trudeau's rivals, Mark Ramzy reports. "There are tons of jobs at stake, tons of fundamental business opportunities," experts say. With the fund hamstrung politically and its future uncertain, industry insiders say Canada risks falling behind in this once-burgeoning sector.
The promise: Established in 2001, the independent public fund provided a financial lifeline to what it identified were the best early-stage startups in clean-tech. By 2022, it had provided $13 billion in funding, creating at least 25,000 jobs and generating billions in revenue.The scandal: An auditor general probe last year found tens of millions in funding went to ineligible projects, while conflict of interest rules were violated and legal requirements weren't met.The resolution: In response, Canada's industry minister shut down the fund and transitioned it to the National Research Council of Canada - which reports directly to the minister. Experts worry the fund may become strangled by government bureaucracy.WHAT ELSE

Document 462 of 500

Doug Ford is trying to beat the clock. What are the opposition leaders doing to beat him?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-is-trying-beat-clock-what-are/docview/3100440200/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+is+trying+to+beat+the+clock.+What+are+the+opposition+leaders+doing+to+beat+him%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has been underestimated by the media and misunderstood by his opponents in two elections, paving his way to two majority government victories.
Will history repeat itself yet again?
With Labour Day out of the way, the campaign now moves into high gear. Never mind the U.S. presidential race, Ontario's election preparations are ramping up on the home front.
The premier is seeking a pretext for a premature spring vote, one year ahead of schedule. With an RCMP criminal probe looming over the Greenbelt imbroglio, his survival is more about beating the clock than defeating his opponents.
What is less well known is precisely how Ontario's two untested opposition leaders - the NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie - are preparing for the anticipated showdown. How will these two rookie leaders respond?
To defeat your adversary you must know your enemy. And define yourself to your audience.
Later this month, both provincial parties are holding key strategy and policy meetings that will set the stage for the coming campaign. But what they first need to understand is that Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives are a moving target, which means the Liberals and New Democrats need to fight the next war - not the last one.
Ford's critics frequently make the mistake of assuming that everyone thinks like them. The opposition likes to think they need only diss the premier and disseminate their critiques to win the day on election day.
That's a misreading of opinion polling about Ford and a misunderstanding of the public's low opinion of opposition leaders. The premier's strongest antagonists can't fathom the strength of his enduring appeal.
The media typically stress Ford's high negatives as evidence of his polarizing personality. But the numbers in the latest poll by Abacus Data for the Toronto Star tell a more revealing story.
In fact, 44 per cent considered the premier to be "friendly," versus only 20 per cent who picked "mean." That's a far cry from Ford's bombastic image in 2018 when he campaigned provincially for the first time after an equally cantankerous career on city council.
To be sure, there are clear signs of vulnerability for the premier: 42 per cent described him as "dishonest" and 41 per cent as "corrupt" in the wake of the Greenbelt scandal (a remarkable 82 per cent of respondents were familiar with it).
All that said, the Abacus poll showed Ford's Tories enjoying 42 per cent support, far ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent and the Stiles's NDP at 21 per cent. In other words (and numbers), people have already taken into account - discounted - Ford's conduct or misconduct.
Which means that Ford's biggest fear isn't Stiles or Crombie coming after him, it's the cops closing in. That's why his PC campaign team is in such a hurry to flout the province's fixed election date law (which dictates a June 2026 election) - it wants the election out of the way before an RCMP investigation leads to any formal charges in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
But the opposition can't count on the police to do their political work for them. Corruption catches people's attention - until it doesn't.
The worst thing New Democrats and Liberals could do is base their campaigns on the premier's thinking and timing, rather than doing their own timely thinking. And yet, time after time, the opposition falls into Ford's traps.
When word first leaked out of an early election, Stiles boasted that New Democrats were ready - and goaded the Tories to "bring it on." Why play into Ford's playbook?
Crombie, by contrast, is calling Ford to account for jumping the gun with an early election costing $150 million, noting that governments are "typically punished" for such self-interest: "We know what the lesson has been in the past. It would be very disrespectful to the taxpayer."
Crombie's Liberals are convening a policy conference on the Sept. 21 weekend to help cobble together an election platform while distancing herself from her unpopular federal Liberal counterpart, Justin Trudeau. In an unmistakable signal, the Ontario Liberals have invited two outspoken ex-politicians who have publicly called for the prime minister to leave (former cabinet minister Jane Philpott and ex-B.C. premier Christie Clark).
The New Democrats are also holding a provincial council meeting that same weekend to help lay down the party line. But both parties remain prisoners of Ford's timelines, and captive of federal polling trends that bleed into provincial results.
Rather than focusing on events beyond their control - a police probe and federal politics - far better to dwell on voter preoccupations closer to home.
Unfortunately, the opposition parties have failed to lay claim to a governing mandate of their own. Neither has outlined their visions, delineated their policies, or distinguished themselves from one another.
As for the premier, he hasn't remotely made the case for a renewed mandate so far ahead of schedule. If Ford is running out of gas early, he should turn over the keys.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford speaks to reporters at the Council of the Federation meetings in Halifax on July 15, 2024.

Subject: Meetings; Local elections; Election results; Councils; Scandals

Document 463 of 500

How scandal threatens clean-tech innovation: The Sustainable Development Technology Canada fund has been mired in mismanagement and conflicts of interest

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/how-scandal-threatens-clean-tech-innovation/docview/3100439796/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=How+scandal+threatens+clean-tech+innovation%3A+The+Sustainable+Development+Technology+Canada+fund+has+been+mired+in+mismanagement+and+conflicts+of+interest&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was once the driver of Canada's nascent clean-technology industry, supported by Liberal and Conservatives alike.
Publicly funded but arms-length from government bureaucracy since 2001, Sustainable Development Technology Canada (SDTC) was a lifeline to entrepreneurs desperate to find money for their clean-tech innovations.
That was until a damaging mismanagement scandal rocked the agency and the House of Commons last year, forcing the Trudeau government on its heels and prompting it to launch a drastic overhaul of the fund.
Now, in the backdrop of the crisis, industry insiders worry the growth of a sector critical to Canada's transition to a green economy is under threat.
"There are tons of jobs at stake, tons of fundamental business opportunities," said Peter McArthur, chair of the Ontario Clean Technology Industry Association.
"That brilliant idea that's coming out of a lab in Hamilton, in Edmonton, in Sherbrooke, is at risk of not seeing the light of day without this sort of funding. Maybe that solution is going to reduce the likelihood of those forest fires in Alberta and B.C., maybe it's going to ... help us go in a different direction regarding the flooding in Ontario and Quebec this year, or the heat that's been so punitive to so many people."
"This gives us a chance."
Here's the story of how a once-lauded federal fund became a punching bag for Justin Trudeau's political rivals, and the uncertainty it faces going forward.
What is Sustainable Development Technology Canada?
Even while acknowledging it's a cliché, industry experts who spoke to the Star say Canada has always punched above its weight when it comes to clean tech or products that help reduce the negative environmental impacts of using natural resources.
As of 2022, more than 2,000 companies contributed $37 billion to Canada's GDP, good enough for the country to claim 13 of the top 100 clean-tech companies in the world in the latest rankings from the Global Cleantech Innovation Index, only second behind the U.S.
SDTC was integral to that success, McArthur said. Launched in 2001 by the Jean Chrétien government, it was a public fund led by experts without political interference that identified the best early stage startups in an industry that required heavy capital investment.
It was "totally necessary, and it didn't exist anywhere else in the world," said Jane Kearns, the co-founder of the Canada Cleantech Alliance and a partner at Evok Innovations, a clean energy venture capital firm. "It set Canada apart."
By 2022, SDTC had provided more than $13 billion in funding to clean-tech projects, leading to billions in revenues and creating at least 25,000 Canadian jobs. Companies that received SDTC funding were seen as most credible by other organizations, said Lynn Côté, the former clean-tech lead at Export Development Canada.
Successive governments of different stripes saw its value and in 2021, the federal government gave it an injection of $1 billion to spend over five years.
How did scandal shut down SDTC's funding?
Behind the scenes, concerns about rampant financial and workplace mismanagement were brought to the government in early 2023 and quickly blew things up.
By October 2023, a third-party report spurred by whistleblower complaints raised enough red flags that Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne froze funding and said SDTC's governance model would have to be reviewed. The embattled president and the chair of the board would both resign weeks later.
The consequences for business were devastating, said McArthur. In a survey of members by the Canada Cleantech Alliance, two-thirds of companies said they had their businesses interrupted and many more revealed they were unable to find alternative funding. To this day, McArthur told the Star, the money has yet to start flowing again.
"It has resulted in some layoffs and people having to sell off portions of their business, at the same time that our American cousins have turned up their focus on this sector dramatically through the Inflation Reduction Act," he said. "It's been a very tough situation, so companies are being lured to the United States."
An auditor general probe concluded in June would later find tens of millions in funding went to ineligible projects, conflict of interest rules were violated in funding decisions and requirements in the law governing the foundation were not met. A long list of recommendations in the report were accepted and misspent funds would be recouped.
Conservative MPs dubbed it the "green slush fund," arguing it was wasteful, corrupt, potentially criminal, and pursued parliamentary hearings on what went wrong. One employee at the company told the Star that's when things "went off the rails."
Champagne took a drastic measure, announcing SDTC would be shut down and transitioned to the National Research Council of Canada, which reports directly to the minister rather than operates at arms-length. Eventually, Champagne said, the fund would move to the Canada Innovation Corporation, a crown corporation first promised by the Trudeau government in 2021. Its establishment, however, is not expected until after the next election.
Even Canada's auditor general, Karen Hogan, told MPs it was the first time she was aware of a critical report leading to such measures.
Later, a Tory motion to compel the auditor general to cough up extensive records obtained during her investigation to the RCMP succeeded, despite protests from Liberal MPs and Hogan, who argued it would have "unintended consequences" on her access to public servants and agencies.
Over the summer, the Mounties also quietly raised concerns about the motion, with commissioner Mike Duheme declaring in a letter to the House law clerk obtained by the Star that it was "highly unlikely" information received due to the motion could be used in an RCMP investigation. The RCMP had not yet found any criminal wrongdoing in the available reports but would continue its review, Duheme added.
Can clean-tech industries survive?
Now, clean-tech industry experts worry a history of consistent funding is at risk.
Champagne's response addressed the governance problems head on, but it's left too much uncertainty and its ramifications on the industry that relied on SDTC could be severe, said several sources who spoke to the Star. The fund's success was attributed to fast, bold decision-making typical only of the private sector. Now, unprecedented political spotlight could lead to slower, more risk-averse decisions that fall short of innovators' expectations, some fear.
"If the money is in a government department, I'd be worried about political interference," said Jonathan Rhone, a serial clean-tech investor who has received SDTC funding in the past. "Honestly, I worry that the process of securing funding becomes overly onerous and costly, and takes too long and (is) bureaucratic."
That's why moving the fund under direct government oversight was a "real overreach" and a "political overreaction," said Stewart Elgie, an environmental law and policy professor at the University of Ottawa. In a statement, a spokesperson for SDTC said funding should resume in the coming weeks and a "seamless transition that meets everyone's needs" was in the works.
"Yes, it needs to be publicly accountable, it needs to show that it's delivering results ... it needed to have its governance tightened up," Elgie said. "But you didn't need to strangle SDTC to solve the governance problem."
"If you get a speeding ticket, well, then you're a little bit cautious," Côté said. "But money hates uncertainty."
Those fears are compounded by questions about how a potential Conservative government whose leader has claimed his climate plan would centre on "technology, not taxes" would approach funding.
"When Pierre Poilievre was a cabinet minister in (the Stephen Harper) government, they poured hundreds of millions of dollars into what he's now calling a green slush fund," Elgie said. "And now, to call that a failure that should be killed is really, shallow opportunism of something that's been a massive Canadian economic success story."
Conservative ethics critic Michael Barrett said in a statement the fund is "nothing but a fraud" that has been "used solely to benefit Trudeau's friends and Liberal insiders."
Spokespeople for Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada and the NRC said the federal government remains committed to homegrown clean-tech funding. The NRC, they said, has a history of supporting clean-tech innovation with a dedicated team that has doled out more than $400 million in clean-tech funding since 2020, and the incoming changes will "enhance accountability, transparency, and public confidence."
To Rhone, whether Ottawa bureaucrats find a way to continue funding clean-tech startup success is a choice in the type of country we want to be.
"It's important for us to realize that Canada has a choice to make about whether we're going to be a supplier of clean-tech solutions into this, you know, trillion dollar global clean-tech market, or we're going to be a buyer," he said. "How do we create the conditions for success?"
CAPTION: A helicopter hauls water to smouldering fires outside of Jasper, Alta., in July. Peter McArthur, chair of the Ontario Clean Technology Industry Association, says clean tech could one day provide the solutions that help reduce the likelihood of forest fires, flooding or heat waves.AMBER BRACKEN THE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

CREDIT: Mark Ramzy Toronto Star

Document 464 of 500

Ford rejects comparing booze in corner stores to safe-injection sites: Premier argues vendors have dealt with tobacco, lottery tickets for decades

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-rejects-comparing-booze-corner-stores-safe/docview/3100439733/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+rejects+comparing+booze+in+corner+stores+to+safe-injection+sites%3A+Premier+argues+vendors+have+dealt+with+tobacco%2C+lottery+tickets+for+decades&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford insists there's "absolutely no comparison" between convenience stores selling booze near schools and the supervised drug consumption sites he is restricting.
With thousands of corner stores licensed to sell beer, wine and premixed cocktails as of Thursday, Ford scoffed at suggestions the retailers should face limits similar to those imposed on injection facilities or cannabis shops.
"Let's be fair, you're comparing a convenience store to a safe consumption site, injection sites," the premier told reporters Tuesday in Brampton.
"Last time I checked, the convenience stores don't have needles lying around the front of their stores," he said, noting variety stores are "well equipped" for dealing with restricted products, having sold tobacco and lottery tickets for decades.
"They're going to be very responsible. There's absolutely no comparison."
Two weeks ago, the premier decreed 10 of Ontario's 17 consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites in Toronto, Hamilton, Guelph, Kitchener, Ottawa and Thunder Bay will close by March 31 because such facilities must be 200 metres away from schools and daycares.
At the same time, he earmarked $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs that will boast 375 supportive housing units and addiction recovery and treatment beds.
The HART facilities are designed to help thousands of people transition to long-term housing annually, but they will not offer supervised drug consumption or safe supply of narcotics or needle exchange programs.
Some proponents have argued the sites are essential to harm-reduction measures for those addicted to drugs.
Under Ontario law, cannabis shops must be at least 150 metres from schools, but there are no restrictions on where LCBO outlets, Beer Stores or licensed restaurants and supermarkets can be located.
Ford, who shares his cellphone number with the public, said he has been inundated with messages about the CTS initiative amid concerns about crime near supervised injection sites.
Among the five closing in Toronto is one in Leslieville's South Riverdale Community Health Centre, near where mother-of-two Karolina Huebner-Makurat was killed by stray bullet last summer.
That brazen daylight crime against an innocent bystander shocked the city.
"I've never had more phone calls in support about closing down the safe consumption sites - I call them unsafe consumption sites - and ... making sure that we support these people," said Ford, touting the new funding to help those with addiction issues.
The premier stressed the additional dollars will "get them social assistance, to make sure that they have housing, make sure that they have addiction treatment beds and detox beds."
With an eye toward an election next spring, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote, Ford is also spending hundreds of millions of dollars to expedite the liberalization of booze sales.
While the government maintains it will cost taxpayers $225 million to hasten an end to the 10-year contract with the privately owned Beer Store that was set to expire on Dec. 31, 2025, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has warned the price tag could balloon to $1 billion.
As first disclosed by the Star in May, Ford is spending that money to give himself the flexibility to call an election one year earlier because he will have delivered a key campaign pledge dating back to 2018.
On Tuesday, the premier would only confirm that "we aren't having any election this year."
But he was coy about whether he would trigger a $150-million vote next spring - despite enjoying a huge Progressive Conservative majority in the legislature, where the opposition parties cannot slow his agenda.
Pressed on why MPPs and cabinet ministers are being told to decide by Christmas if they will seek re-election, Ford said, "You always have to be prepared."
"That's what it comes down to - no matter which way you look at it, we aren't too far from any election, no matter if it's next year (or) the following year."
The premier was in Brampton, where the Tories hold all five seats, for a campaign-style announcement promising up to $29.7 million to boost flood-protection and water systems.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said the money would enable the construction of 12,900 homes in the city's downtown, which has been prone to flooding due to aging infrastructure.
"Our downtown - like many old communities and old downtowns - has challenges that come with it," said Brown.
"Since the 1820s, we've had some significant challenges with flooding in our downtown because of the impact of the Etobicoke Creek," he said, noting that upgrading 600 metres of the creek as part of the city's Riverwalk project will improve storm water runoff.
Ford has been touring Ontario municipalities all summer, doling out money from the $970-million "housing-enabling water systems fund" in a bid to tackle the province's demand for additional homes.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford said two weeks ago that 10 Ontario consumption and treatment service sites will close because they are less than 200 metres away from schools and daycares.
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Document 465 of 500

Ford trying to beat the clock to survive: Premier's re-election depends on getting ahead of RCMP probe of Greenbelt scandal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-trying-beat-clock-survive/docview/3100438568/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+trying+to+beat+the+clock+to+survive%3A+Premier%27s+re-election+depends+on+getting+ahead+of+RCMP+probe+of+Greenbelt+scandal&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has been underestimated by the media and misunderstood by his opponents in two elections, paving his way to two majority government victories.

With Labour Day out of the way, the campaign now moves into high gear. Never mind the U.S. presidential race, Ontario's election preparations are ramping up on the home front.
The premier is seeking a pretext for a premature spring vote, one year ahead of schedule. With an RCMP criminal probe looming over the Greenbelt imbroglio, his survival is more about beating the clock than defeating his opponents.
What is less well known is precisely how Ontario's two untested opposition leaders - the NDP's Marit Stiles and the Liberals' Bonnie Crombie - are preparing for the anticipated showdown. How will these two rookie leaders respond?
To defeat your adversary you must know your enemy. And define yourself to your audience.
Later this month, both provincial parties are holding key strategy and policy meetings that will set the stage for the coming campaign. But what they first need to understand is that Ford's governing Progressive Conservatives are a moving target, which means the Liberals and New Democrats need to fight the next war - not the last one.
Ford's critics frequently make the mistake of assuming that everyone thinks like them. The opposition likes to think they need only diss the premier and disseminate their critiques to win the day on election day.
That's a misreading of opinion polling about Ford and a misunderstanding of the public's low opinion of opposition leaders. The premier's strongest antagonists can't fathom the strength of his enduring appeal.
The media typically stress Ford's high negatives as evidence of his polarizing personality. But the numbers in the latest poll by Abacus Data for the Toronto Star tell a more revealing story.
In fact, 44 per cent considered the premier to be "friendly," versus only 20 per cent who picked "mean." That's a far cry from Ford's bombastic image in 2018 when he campaigned provincially for the first time after an equally cantankerous career on city council.
To be sure, there are clear signs of vulnerability for the premier: 42 per cent described him as "dishonest" and 41 per cent as "corrupt" in the wake of the Greenbelt scandal (a remarkable 82 per cent of respondents were familiar with it).
All that said, the Abacus poll showed Ford's Tories enjoying 42 per cent support, far ahead of Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent and the Stiles's NDP at 21 per cent. In other words (and numbers), people have already taken into account - discounted - Ford's conduct or misconduct.
Which means that Ford's biggest fear isn't Stiles or Crombie coming after him, it's the cops closing in. That's why his PC campaign team is in such a hurry to flout the province's fixed election date law (which dictates a June 2026 election) - it wants the election out of the way before an RCMP investigation leads to any formal charges in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.
But the opposition can't count on the police to do their political work for them. Corruption catches people's attention - until it doesn't.
The worst thing New Democrats and Liberals could do is base their campaigns on the premier's thinking and timing, rather than doing their own timely thinking. And yet, time after time, the opposition falls into Ford's traps.
When word first leaked out of an early election, Stiles boasted that New Democrats were ready - and goaded the Tories to "bring it on." Why play into Ford's playbook?
Crombie, by contrast, is calling Ford to account for jumping the gun with an early election costing $150 million, noting that governments are "typically punished" for such self-interest: "We know what the lesson has been in the past. It would be very disrespectful to the taxpayer."
Crombie's Liberals are convening a policy conference on the Sept. 21 weekend to help cobble together an election platform while distancing herself from her unpopular federal Liberal counterpart, Justin Trudeau.
In an unmistakable signal, the Ontario Liberals have invited two outspoken ex-politicians who have publicly called for the prime minister to leave (former cabinet minister Jane Philpott and ex-B.C. premier Christie Clark).
The New Democrats are also holding a provincial council meeting that same weekend to help lay down the party line. But both parties remain prisoners of Ford's timelines, and captive of federal polling trends that bleed into provincial results.
Rather than focusing on events beyond their control - a police probe and federal politics - far better to dwell on voter preoccupations closer to home.
Unfortunately, the opposition parties have failed to lay claim to a governing mandate of their own. Neither has outlined their visions, delineated their policies, or distinguished themselves from one another.
As for the premier, he hasn't remotely made the case for a renewed mandate so far ahead of schedule. If Ford is running out of gas early, he should turn over the keys.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford's biggest fear isn't the opposition parties coming after him, it's the cops closing in, Martin Regg Cohn writes. His campaign team wants the election out of the way before an RCMP investigation leads to any formal charges in the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap.Darren Calabrese The Canadian Press file photo

Document 466 of 500

Canada's clean-tech funding created a global powerhouse. Now scandal threatens to tear it down

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/canadas-clean-tech-funding-created-global/docview/3100437820/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+clean-tech+funding+created+a+global+powerhouse.+Now+scandal+threatens+to+tear+it+down&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: OTTAWA-It was once the driver of Canada's nascent clean-technology industry, supported by Liberal and Conservatives alike.
Publicly funded but arms-length from government bureaucracy since 2001, Sustainable Development Technology Canada (SDTC) was a lifeline to entrepreneurs desperate to find money for their clean-tech innovations.
That was until a damaging mismanagement scandal rocked the agency and the House of Commons last year, forcing the Trudeau government on its heels and prompting it to launch a drastic overhaul of the fund.
Now, in the backdrop of the crisis, industry insiders worry the growth of a sector critical to Canada's transition to a green economy is under threat.
"There are tons of jobs at stake, tons of fundamental business opportunities," said Peter McArthur, chair of the Ontario Clean Technology Industry Association.
"That brilliant idea that's coming out of a lab in Hamilton, in Edmonton, in Sherbrooke, is at risk of not seeing the light of day without this sort of funding. Maybe that solution is going to reduce the likelihood of those forest fires in Alberta and B.C., maybe it's going to ... help us go in a different direction regarding the flooding in Ontario and Quebec this year, or the heat that's been so punitive to so many people."
"This gives us a chance."
Here's the story of how a once lauded federal fund became a punching bag for Justin Trudeau's political rivals, and the uncertainty it faces going forward.
What is SDTC?
Even while acknowledging it's a cliché, industry experts who spoke to the Star say Canada has always punched above its weight when it comes to clean tech or products that help reduce the negative environmental impacts of using natural resources.
As of 2022, more than 2,000 companies contributed $37 billion to Canada's GDP, good enough for the country to claim 13 of the top 100 clean-tech companies in the world in the latest rankings from the Global Cleantech Innovation Index, only second behind the U.S.
SDTC was integral to that success, McArthur said. Launched in 2001 by the Jean Chrétien government, it was a public fund led by experts without political interference that identified the best early stage startups in an industry that required heavy capital investment.
It was "totally necessary, and it didn't exist anywhere else in the world," said Jane Kearns, the co-founder of the Canada Cleantech Alliance and a partner at Evok Innovations, a clean energy venture capital firm. "It set Canada apart."
By 2022, SDTC had provided more than $13 billion in funding to clean-tech projects, leading to billions in revenues and creating at least 25,000 Canadian jobs. Companies that received SDTC funding were seen as most credible by other organizations, said Lynn Côté, the former clean-tech lead at Export Development Canada.
Successive governments of different stripes saw its value and in 2021, the federal government gave it an injection of $1 billion to spend over five years.
How did scandal shut down SDTC's funding?
Behind the scenes, concerns about rampant financial and workplace mismanagement were brought to the government in early 2023 and quickly blew things up.
By October 2023, a third-party report spurred by whistleblower complaints raised enough red flags that Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne froze funding and said SDTC's governance model would have to be reviewed. The embattled president and the chair of the board would both resign weeks later.
The consequences for business were devastating, said McArthur. In a survey of members by the Canada Cleantech Alliance, two-thirds of companies said they had their businesses interrupted and many more revealed they were unable to find alternative funding. To this day, McArthur told the Star, the money has yet to start flowing again.
"It has resulted in some layoffs and people having to sell off portions of their business, at the same time that our American cousins have turned up their focus on this sector dramatically through the Inflation Reduction Act," he said. "It's been a very tough situation, so companies are being lured to the United States."
An auditor general probe concluded in June would later find tens of millions in funding went to ineligible projects, conflict of interest rules were violated in funding decisions, and requirements in the law governing the foundation were not met. A long list of recommendations in the report were accepted, and misspent funds would be recouped.
Conservative MPs dubbed it the "green slush fund," arguing it was wasteful, corrupt, potentially criminal, and pursued parliamentary hearings on what went wrong. One employee at the company told the Star that's when things "went off the rails."
Champagne took a drastic measure, announcing SDTC would be shut down and transitioned to the National Research Council of Canada, which reports directly to the minister rather than operates at arms-length. Eventually, Champagne said, the fund would move to the Canada Innovation Corporation, a crown corporation first promised by the Trudeau government in 2021. Its establishment, however, is not expected until after the next election.
Even Canada's auditor general Karen Hogan told MPs it was the first time she was aware of a critical report leading to such measures.
Later, a Tory motion to compel the auditor general to cough up extensive records obtained during her investigation to the RCMP succeeded, despite protests from Liberal MPs and Hogan, who argued it would have "unintended consequences" on her access to public servants and agencies.
Over the summer, the Mounties also quietly raised concerns about the motion, with commissioner Mike Duheme declaring in a letter to the House law clerk obtained by the Star that it was "highly unlikely" information received due to the motion could be used in an RCMP investigation. The RCMP had not yet found any criminal wrongdoing in the available reports but would continue its review, Duheme added.
Can clean tech industries survive?
Now, clean-tech industry experts worry a history of consistent funding is at risk.
Champagne's response addressed the governance problems head on, but it's left too much uncertainty and its ramifications on the industry that relied on SDTC could be severe, said several sources who spoke to the Star. The fund's success was attributed to fast, bold decision-making typical only of the private sector. Now, unprecedented political spotlight could lead to slower, more risk-averse decisions that fall short of innovators' expectations, some fear.
"If the money is in a government department, I'd be worried about political interference," said Jonathan Rhone, a serial clean tech investor who has received SDTC funding in the past. "Honestly, I worry that the process of securing funding becomes overly onerous and costly, and takes too long and (is) bureaucratic."
That's why moving the fund under direct government oversight was a "real overreach" and a "political overreaction," said Stewart Elgie, an environmental law and policy professor at the University of Ottawa. In a statement, a spokesperson for SDTC said funding should resume in the coming weeks and a "seamless transition that meets everyone's needs" was in the works.
"Yes, it needs to be publicly accountable, it needs to show that it's delivering results ... it needed to have its governance tightened up," Elgie said. "But you didn't need to strangle SDTC to solve the governance problem."
"If you get a speeding ticket, well, then you're a little bit cautious," Côté said. "But money hates uncertainty."
Those fears are compounded by questions about how a potential Conservative government whose leader has claimed his climate plan would centre on "technology, not taxes" would approach funding.
"When Pierre Poilievre was a cabinet minister in (the Stephen Harper) government, they poured hundreds of millions of dollars into what he's now calling a green slush fund," Elgie said. "And now, to call that a failure that should be killed is really, shallow opportunism of something that's been a massive Canadian economic success story."
Conservative ethics critic Michael Barrett said in a statement the fund is "nothing but a fraud" that has been "used solely to benefit Trudeau's friends and Liberal insiders."
Spokespeople for Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada and the NRC said the federal government remains committed to homegrown clean-tech funding. The NRC, they said, has a history of supporting clean-tech innovation with a dedicated team that has doled out more than $400 million in clean-tech funding since 2020, and the incoming changes will "enhance accountability, transparency, and public confidence."
To Rhone, whether Ottawa bureaucrats find a way to continue funding clean-tech startup success is a choice in the type of country we want to be.
"It's important for us to realize that Canada has a choice to make about whether we're going to be a supplier of clean-tech solutions into this, you know, trillion dollar global clean-tech market, or we're going to be a buyer," he said. "How do we create the conditions for success?"
CAPTION: A helicopter buckets water onto smouldering fires outside of Jasper, Alta., on July 26, 2024. Peter McArthur, chair of the OntarioClean Technology Industry Association, says clean tech could one day provide the solutions that help reduce the likelihood of forest fires, flooding or heat waves.

Document 467 of 500

The reason Ontarians still like Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reason-ontarians-still-like-ford/docview/3100437806/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+reason+Ontarians+still+like+Ford&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're surprised by Doug Ford's enduring popularity as his seventh summer as premier comes to a close, don't be. The explanation could be pretty simple: you might be paying too much attention.
The average voter and citizen doesn't spend much time paying attention to the news. They might be able to name Olivia Chow as the mayor of Toronto, but there's a good chance they don't know who their local councillor is. They almost certainly know that Justin Trudeau is the prime minister, but it's not a given that they know he's a Liberal.
This is why I'm no longer surprised when I see polls like the one produced by Abacus for the Star, released last week. The poll found that Ford, who has been premier of this province for the past six years - years marked by incredible tragedies and significant scandals - remains very popular. Asked which descriptors apply to Ford, the largest plurality of voters in this province picked "friendly." And based on his perceived image, if a vote were held today, Ford would win a majority - his third.
I dared peek at X last week and the reaction was, uh, what you'd expect. For all his general popularity, Ford's critics really don't like the guy.
And some seem to struggle to understand why anyone would. I offer this as both explanation and solace: most Canadians do pay attention to the news, but it's only a tiny percentage that pay close attention. A Maru poll from last year pegged the number of Canadians who were hyper-engaged in the news at 16 per cent, and that felt about right to me.
Ford has done an excellent job over the years of maintaining a folksy image. As one Ford associate told me years ago, Ford is, above all else, a salesman. He's really good at cultivating that friendly, "I'm just here to help" persona and running with it. For voters who don't follow the news or limit their news consumption to catching a few top stories on the radio while commuting or scanning headlines while scrolling on their phones, the salesman persona is likely all they've ever seen from Ford.
The catastrophes in our long-term care homes during the pandemic may not have registered with them because they were busy managing the pandemic's effects on their own lives. The Greenbelt scandal is probably something they've heard mentioned but haven't looked into deeply. The closing of the Science Centre, if it registered at all, was probably forgotten in days. And so on.
Lest any of this sound condemnatory or critical, many of these disengaged voters are highly intelligent, well-educated and overall great people. They've just chosen, rightly or wrongly, not to be engaged in the news. To the extent they have any interest in politics, they reach quick decisions after a cursory examination of the facts. If Ford seems friendly, they may vote for him. This blows the minds of the hyper-engaged, but it's true.
Among those hyper-engaged are Ford's actual opponents - our provincial Liberals, NDP and Green MPPs, and their supporters. I've been baffled over the years by the continued insistence of so many of Ford's harshest critics on portraying him as some kind of extremist. When a huge part of the electorate has decided that Ford is a nice guy, trying to portray him as some kind of freak doesn't change how they feel about Ford. It helps change how they feel about you. They write you off as a kook, and tune you out.
One day, Ford is going to step in it in some way that finally penetrates his deflector shield of genial affability. The Abacus poll speaks to this. There are some metrics, like trustworthiness and effectiveness, where the voters are much more evenly split on Ford, and for good reason. As time goes on and flops accumulate, the voters will sour on him. As far as political predictions go, that's about the safest one I could possibly make: any politician who serves long enough finds themselves in that situation eventually (see Trudeau, Justin).
So for Ford's critics, that's the good news, such as it is. The voters won't like him forever. But they like him for now, and there's no mystery why. They think he's a nice guy, and then they think about something else.
Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is co-editor of "The Line," an online magazine, which publishes at readtheLine.ca. He can be reached at matt@readtheline.ca
CREDIT: Matt Gurney Contributing Columnist Matt Gurney is a Toronto-based journalist, broadcaster and freelance contributing columnist for the Star. He is co-editor of "The Line," an online magazine, which publishes at readtheLine.ca. He can be reached at matt;
