Document 468 of 500

Doug Ford rejects comparison of booze sales in corner stores to safe-injection sites

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-rejects-comparison-booze-sales-corner/docview/3100256265/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+rejects+comparison+of+booze+sales+in+corner+stores+to+safe-injection+sites&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford insists there's "absolutely no comparison" between convenience stores selling booze near schools and the supervised drug consumption sites he is restricting.
With thousands of corner stores licensed to sell beer, wine and premixed cocktails as of Thursday, Ford scoffed at suggestions the retailers should face limits similar to those imposed on safe injection sites or cannabis shops.
"Let's be fair, you're comparing a convenience store to a safe consumption site, injection sites," the premier told reporters Tuesday in Brampton.
"Last time I checked, the convenience stores don't have needles lying around the front of their stores," he said, noting variety stores are "well equipped" for dealing with restricted products, having sold tobacco and lottery tickets for decades.
"They're going to be very responsible. There's absolutely no comparison."
Two weeks ago, the premier decreed 10 of Ontario's 17 consumption and treatment service (CTS) sites in Toronto, Hamilton, Guelph, Kitchener, Ottawa and Thunder Bay will close by March 31 because such facilities must be 200 metres away from schools and daycares.
At the same time, he earmarked $378 million for 19 new "Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment" (HART) hubs that will boast 375 supportive housing units and addiction recovery and treatment beds.
The HART facilities are designed to help thousands of people transition to long-term housing annually, but they will not offer supervised drug consumption or safe supply of narcotics or needle exchange programs.
Some proponents have argued the sites are essential to harm-reduction measures for those addicted to drugs.
Ford, who shares his cellphone number with the public, said he has been inundated with messages about the initiative.
"I've never had more phone calls in support about closing down the safe consumption sites - I call them unsafe consumption sites - and ... making sure that we support these people," he said, touting the new funding.
The premier stressed the additional dollars will "get them social assistance, to make sure that they have housing, make sure that they have addiction treatment beds and detox beds."
With an eye toward an election next spring, one year ahead of the scheduled June 2026 vote, Ford is also spending hundreds of millions of dollars to expedite the liberalization of booze sales.
While the government maintains it will cost taxpayers $225 million to hasten an end to the 10-year contract with the privately owned Beer Store that was set to expire on Dec. 31, 2025, Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie has warned the price tag could balloon to $1 billion.
As first disclosed by the Star in May, Ford is spending that money to give himself the flexibility to call an election one year earlier because he will have delivered a key campaign pledge dating back to 2018.
On Tuesday, the premier would only pledge that "we aren't have any election this year."
But he was coy about whether he would trigger a $150-million vote next spring - despite enjoying a huge Progressive Conservative majority in the legislature, where the opposition parties cannot slow his agenda.
Asked why MPPs and cabinet ministers are being told to decide by Christmas if they will seek re-election, Ford said, "You always have to be prepared."
"That's what it comes down to - no matter which way you look at it, we aren't too far from any election, no matter if it's next year (or) the following year."
The premier was in Brampton, where the Tories hold all five seats, for a campaign-style announcement promising up to $29.7 million to boost flood-protection and water systems.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said the money would enable the construction of 12,900 homes in the city's downtown, which has been prone to flooding due to aging infrastructure.
"Our downtown - like many old communities and old downtowns - has challenges that come with it," said Brown.
"Since the 1820s, we've had some significant challenges with flooding in our downtown because of the impact of the Etobicoke Creek," he said, noting that upgrading 600 metres of the creek as part of the city's Riverwalk project will improve storm water runoff.
Ford has been touring Ontario municipalities all summer, doling out money from the $970-million "housing-enabling water systems fund" in a bid to tackle the province's demand for additional homes.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford attends the U.S.-Canada Summit in Toronto on June 11, 2024.

Document 469 of 500

The air show is part of CNE history. We should be proud of it

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/air-show-is-part-cne-history-we-should-be-proud/docview/3100256211/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+air+show+is+part+of+CNE+history.+We+should+be+proud+of+it&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Let's be proud of the air show
Down with the Canadian International Air Show, Aug. 29
Get real! Planes are often flying over downtown to and from Billy Bishop airport. The Tutor planes used for the Snowbirds are primarily used for the aerobatic team for the pleasure of the public. The other planes, that are in fact used for military purposes, are also used to entertain, and to honour Canadians and special days of remembrance with flyby formations. The skills of the pilots are to be commended! Why do we want to denigrate those who, with this skill set, are prepared to risk their lives to defend Canada in the event of war? We are very blessed not to have any wars on our land, but I am very thankful that they are there to defend us if necessary.
Lillian Shery, Toronto
Air shows are exciting, thrilling, and an adventure. They have provided great entertainment to spectators of all ages, but most especially children. As the writer suggests, there are some who object to these shows or simply don't like them. But the shows are scheduled well in advance, lasting but a few hours over a very few days one time each year. Presented out over the lake, the show can be easily avoided or ignored.
Consider the history of the CNE. Year after year, decade after decade, the CNE has had such traditions removed or faded away to be replaced by, well, by nothing. The evening grandstand show is only a fading memory. Hobbies? Flowers and gardens? Electrical and electronic wizardry? All gone. The Ontario building, showcasing much of the forest and stream life of the province? It is now a transient casino. The food building and midway are about all that's left of the old CNE. So far new traditions have been at best a wish or a dream. Let's not do it again. Keep the air show for the many who enjoy it.
David Kister, Kingston
Nothing sticks to 'Teflon Ford'
Ford has faced controversy after controversy - yet remains as popular as ever, Aug. 29
All the many major crises in Ontario have one thing in common: Premier Doug Ford. He has strong powers to make improvements, but instead the pressing issues and crises continue, in health care, elder care, education, jobs, loss of fertile land, the justice system, massive spending and homelessness. It is time for voters to wake up and vote for a change at the first opportunity.
Dorothy Langhorne, Cobourg, Ont.
With all the mistakes Premier Doug Ford's government has made with destructive end results, Ontario citizens still clearly see him working for them! What happened to the disaster in our long-term care homes during the pandemic, with so many residents losing their lives? What happened to the hospitals that have doctors and nurses bailing because of underfunding? Remember that the lack of investment in their salaries? Remember when our schools were held in high esteem throughout the world? Remember the spa with a 99-year lease being built on our Ontario Place? Ford has been good for some people in the business world, but his government has created havoc in areas like education, health care, and others. Ontario needs a thinking and collaborative premier!
Bonnie Bacvar, Toronto
To serve and protect who?
Toronto cop Stacy Clarke betrayed her profession. In most jobs, she'd have been fired: DiManno, Aug. 29
This column should alarm everyone. Although I'm not a Toronto resident, malfeasance is a problem that pervades policing in every jurisdiction across Ontario and Canada as a whole. What's worse is that even when people have died at the hands of police officers, notwithstanding the justification or "worthiness" of the death, there does not seem to be meaningful accountability. In very recent years, police officers across the province have been criminally charged with killing people on the job, sexual assault, cheating and theft and often obfuscate at every turn. There are few guilty verdicts in court. For a profession whose stated purpose is to serve and protect, it is clear who benefits from this.
Meghan McKie, Kanata, Ont.
Bail should not be an option
Project Burton: 32 arrested by Durham police and over 180 charges laid in firearms investigation, Aug. 29
The firearm investigation dubbed "Project Burton" produced 32 arrests, but 23 per cent of those arrested are out on bail. Three of them are now wanted for breaching their bail conditions. Even police are not surprised. Why bother arresting people at all? This must stop. If you are arrested in a gun-related charge, you should not be out on bail!
Gary Slippoy, Toronto
Shaking head at Poilievre
Poilievre says NDP should drop support for Liberal government and trigger an October election, Aug. 29
I am shaking my head as I marvel at Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre who so often insults NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh and now has the audacity to ask Singh to trigger an election this October so he can become prime minister because of his party's sizable lead in the polls. How can Canadians trust a person who lacks the simple wisdom to understand that you don't get things done by insulting others?
Charles Campisi, Oakville, Ont.
Progressive voters like me would rather have NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh propose a merger of his party with Justin Trudeau's Liberals, just as the centre-right is doing in BC. How would Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre like that? How would provincial Progressive Conservative Premier Doug Ford like it if NDP Leader Marit Stiles went to Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie to propose a merger of their parties? They could muster 47 per cent of the vote to Ford's 42 per cent. I'm daydreaming, of course. More than likely, Liberals and New Democrats will continue separately, early elections will split the progressive vote and the right will win again, nationally and provincially. Pity.
Salvatore (Sal) Amenta, Stouffville, Ont.
Poilievre is right to ask
It's time for an election. Once and for all Canadians should have the right to endorse or replace the current government. With dental care and pharmacare, the NDP has seen its two major demands enacted. So it's time to cancel the parliamentary agreement with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals. The people are always right in a true democracy. It is time to allow us to express ourselves at the ballot box.
Mark A. Greenberg, Toronto
CAPTION: Canadian Forces Snowbirds, in a seven plane formation, at the 74th edition of the Canadian International Air Show at the CNE in 2023.

Full text: Let's be proud of the air show
Down with the Canadian
International Air Show,
Aug. 29
Get real! Planes are often flying over downtown to and from Billy Bishop airport. The Tutor planes used for the Snowbirds are primarily used for the aerobatic team for the pleasure of the public. The other planes, that are in fact used for military purposes, are also used to entertain, and to honour Canadians and special days of remembrance with flyby formations. The skills of the pilots are to be commended! Why do we want to denigrate those who, with this skill set, are prepared to risk their lives to defend Canada in the event of war? We are very blessed not to have any wars on our land, but I am very thankful they are there to defend us if necessary.
Lillian Shery, Toronto
Air shows are exciting, thrilling, and an adventure. They have provided great entertainment to spectators of all ages, but most especially children. As the writer suggests, there are some who object to these shows or simply don't like them. But the shows are scheduled well in advance, lasting but a few hours over a very few days one time each year. Presented out over the lake, the show can be easily avoided or ignored.
Consider the history of the CNE. Year after year, decade after decade, the CNE has had such traditions removed or faded away to be replaced by, well, by nothing. The evening grandstand show is a fading memory. Hobbies? Flowers and gardens? Electrical and electronic wizardry? All gone. The Ontario building, showcasing much of the forest and stream life of the province? It is now a transient casino. The food building and midway are about all that's left of the old CNE. So far, new traditions have been at best a wish or a dream. Let's not do it again. Keep the air show for the many who enjoy it.
David Kister, Kingston

Ford has faced controversy after controversy - yet
remains as popular as ever, Aug. 29

Document 471 of 500

Democracy isn't working for us. We need electoral reform

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/democracy-isnt-working-us-we-need-electoral/docview/3099790634/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Democracy+isn%27t+working+for+us.+We+need+electoral+reform&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Electoral reform required
These Canadians don't care much about politics. Here's why a growing number feel democracy isn't working for them, Aug. 25
It is no surprise that levels of apathy and dissatisfaction are high when the government has been in for about a decade and is very unpopular, facing the added impact of social media. The idea that Canada is an "elected dictatorship" came to the fore in the 1990s. If voters are not engaged, it is partly because of this and the fact that MPs can be elected with less than 30 per cent of the vote and once in Ottawa, they vote over 99 per cent of the time with their party, according to a study done be Samara a few years ago. Proportional representation will only make things worse, as MPs will still do what their leader wants. What we need is to make local MPs more accountable to voters, and less beholden to their party leader. This will require reforms, starting with a change to ranked choice voting to make each MP elected by 50 per cent of votes. Then we need parliamentary reform to give more power to backbenchers and take power out of the Prime Minister's Office. Other reforms to make political parties more democratic, and financing reform to shift from tax credits and give every voter an equal "voucher" would completely reform our system and give voters more reason to be politically engaged.
Brian Graff, Toronto
Others have done worse and gotten less
Trailblazing Toronto cop who cheated to get Black officers promoted stripped of rank, Aug. 28
What am I missing here? Toronto police Supt. Stacy Clarke has been demoted for her role in helping Black officers with their exams despite what the judge called her "stellar record" and her " trailblazing achievements." Meanwhile, in court, a judge tossed a case after the judge said two officers fabricated notes and the gave false testimony while under oath. The judge believes those officers lied but no charges have been put forward by the Crown, while Clarke owned up to every single one of the charges against her. Tell me, who is the better officer and why was there such a difference in the handling of their cases? Just asking.
Hazel Regan, Toronto
Not rocket science
Last summer's wildfires were huge and terrible and very visible. Their resulting carbon dioxide emissions are huge and terrible but invisible. However, that carbon dioxide is still there. More carbon dioxide in the atmosphere means higher temperatures, which means forests dry out earlier in the year and dry out more thoroughly, making wildfire seasons longer and with bigger fires that are harder to control, which adds even more carbon dioxide to the atmosphere. This is just one of many loops that are the hallmark of climate change. We need to stop burning fossil fuels as fast as we can before we hit tipping points that put it out of our control. We have the technology to do this. We just need to stop the fossil fuel companies from their lobbying and lies.
Roberta Tevlin, Toronto
Unintended consequences
Next week The Beer Store in Geraldton, Ont., will close. This will be accompanied by closures in the towns of Cochrane and Nipigon. These three northern Ontario communities will not only lose their beer purchase locations, but also the facilities to recycle beer cans and liquor bottles. This loss is the direct result of the Ford government's plan to put alcohol sales into grocery and convenience stores. The local closure means the citizens of Geraldton and neighbouring communities would have to travel to Hearst, Ont., (248 kilometres away) and Thunder Bay (280 kilometres away) to recycle their cans and bottles. With no curbside recycling, it doesn't take a crystal ball to foresee that the bottles and cans will end up in the trash. This is just another example of the unintended consequences that will arise from the poor planning and decision-making by this government in order to expand accessibility to alcohol. Does anyone at Queen's Park care?
Audrey Chorkawy, Geraldton, Ont.
Congratulations
Congratulations to the Bank of Canada, the institution largely created in 1935 after the Depression to improve the Canadian economy. Now, in 2024, as a direct result of ridiculous increases in the interest rate, our vibrant real estate market has been devastated, people are losing their homes or deposits on new homes and their hopes for the future. There's a bright lining in all this. Bank profits are soaring. Again, congratulations.
David Brannan, Mississauga
Do the 'Dutch reach'
Toronto is considered one of the most dangerous cities in Canada for cyclists. According to Cycle Toronto, there were 209 reported dooring accidents in 2016. In my experience having been doored myself, drivers did not apologize and one walked away as I stood there bleeding. Dooring can often be prevented by doing the "Dutch reach" - open the door with the hand furthest from the door in order to force the head to swivel. This is a 10 second effort that could prevent injury or even save a life. Why won't most drivers perform the Dutch reach? Ignorance and/or apathy.
Moses Shuldiner, Toronto
A track is great, but what about Toronto?
Track cyclist Kate O'Brien claims Canada's first Paralympic medal in Paris, Aug. 29
Three cheers for paralympic medallists Kate O'Brien (cycling) and Aurelie Rivard (swimming), and for all the gifted athletes doing things most of us couldn't attempt. But I can't help wondering how many paralympians who are so mobile on the track would have problems using the Toronto subway. So many stations are inaccessible to wheelchairs, strollers and walkers as they do not have elevators or, if they do, they down for maintenance. Escalators, of course, need maintenance and don't always work. I wonder how many businesses are losing customers because their doors are impassable with steps, heavy doors with no buttons to open them, and so on. And every spring, motorists clamour for pot holes to be fixed but folks with walkers are trapped indoors because of sidewalks that are cracked or not passable due to construction.
Julie Beddoes, Toronto
CAPTION: In 2015, Liberal Leader Justin Trudeau promised to replace the first-past-the-post voting system.

Document 472 of 500

Booze is coming to corner stores this week. Who's in charge of policing the new rules?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/booze-is-coming-corner-stores-this-week-whos/docview/3099790633/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Booze+is+coming+to+corner+stores+this+week.+Who%27s+in+charge+of+policing+the+new+rules%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario has staffed up in advance of booze coming to corner store shelves next week and says it plans to carry out "unannounced inspections" to make sure the new rules are being abided by.
One thing it won't be looking for, however, is whether alcohol carrying stores are near schools. Unlike with cannabis stores or supervised drug consumption sites, the province is not creating bubble zones around students to protect them from alcohol.
The AGCO said that with increased provincial funding it now has more than 100 inspectors to monitor the 4,146 convenience stores approved so far to sell beer, wine and canned cocktails starting Thursday. The commission is the sole enforcer when it comes to ensuring only eligible stores get to sell eligible beverages.
These inspectors are supported by "back-office analysts and advanced, data-driven monitoring tools," said AGCO spokesperson Raymond Kahnert.
"Proactive inspections allow us to help educate licensees and address potential issues before they escalate, while responsive inspections follow up on any public complaints or issues flagged by local authorities," he added.
The city of Toronto said it had no influence on the rollout and will not be watching over it.
"There are no plans for the city of Toronto to track retail stores that sell alcohol, as the AGCO regulates the sale, service and delivery of liquor in Ontario, including licences and permits," said spokesperson Elise von Scheel in an email to the Star.
Toronto police also defer to the AGCO, but are able to conduct inspections, issue tickets and lay charges should the situation demand.
"Depending on the circumstances, police should be called in emergencies or if there is an immediate safety concern and the AGCO should be called with concerns about liquor licences or sales," said Toronto police spokesperson Shannon Eames.
Even before the new rules have taken effect, the AGCO has had to punish an offending retailer. An Etobicoke shop this week jumped the gun and sold alcohol before the official start date. It also sold spirits, which is still forbidden under the new regulations, and didn't buy the liquor through the LCBO.
The penalty was a three-week suspension of its licence to sell liquor and lottery tickets.
Depending on how severe it considers a particular violation, the AGCO has the ability to fine retailers up to $100,000. The most costly offence for a store owner is knowingly selling liquor to a minor.
While Ontario's Cannabis Licence Act prohibits cannabis stores from being located less than 150 meters from a school, its Liquor Licence and Control Act puts no geographic restrictions on alcohol outlets.
In contrast, it was announced in August that 10 of the 17 supervised consumption sites in Ontario will be closed by springtime because of forthcoming legislation from the province mandating they stay at least 200 meters away from schools and daycares.
Both decisions could be harmful to Ontarians' health, according to experts. In May, the Canadian Public Health Association wrote that allowing alcohol in corner stores is a public health risk.
"Expanding the sale of alcohol to more retail locations will increase its accessibility and consumption. Research shows that greater availability of alcohol leads to higher rates of consumption and, consequently, more alcohol-related harms."
Meanwhile, supervised consumption has been shown to save lives. A study this year in The Lancet found overdose deaths were reduced by 67 per cent in neighbourhoods within 500 meters of supervised consumption sites.
Newly shuttered drug consumption clinics will not have the chance to relocate. The province is banning the creation of new sites.
When asked why the province wasn't also imposing restrictions on alcohol sales near schools, Premier Doug Ford's office forwarded the Star's inquiry to the Ministry of the Attorney General.
A spokesperson for the ministry said it wouldn't be fair to force store owners to relocate away from schools to sell booze.
"As grocery and convenience stores are existing businesses in the marketplace, it would be inappropriate for these businesses to relocate should proximity requirements prevent their businesses from selling alcohol," said Jack Fazzari.
Fazzari dismissed the comparison with drug consumption sites.
"The legal and regulated sale of alcohol in our province has been occurring in communities for nearly 100 years," he said. "Places where alcohol is sold, and the locations of drug consumption sites are not comparable."
As for cannabis, Ontario had to take "careful steps" to regulate it, including keeping sales away from schools, as it was a "brand-new market" when it was legalized federally.
Fazzari said the province's tools for mitigating the harms alcohol causes are still in use. This includes only allowing sale from 7 a.m. to 11 p.m., enforcing the legal drinking age of 19, making sure alcohol doesn't get any cheaper than the legal minimum retail price of beer at $2.81 per litre, putting up signs warning of the dangers of drinking while pregnant and mandatory staff training for all new retailers.
"Our government will continue upholding some of the highest existing standards of social responsibility through safe and responsible implementation of our plan to bring Ontario in-line with the rest of Canada," he said.
CAPTION: Beer, wine and coolers will be for sale in corner stores across the province starting Sept. 5.

Subject: Public health; Retail stores; Liquor laws & regulations; Alcohol; Sales; Beer; Convenience stores; Schools; Marijuana

Title: Booze is coming to corner stores this week. Who's in charge of policing the new rules?

Document 473 of 500

I've used a Toronto supervised consumption site for a year. What it's really like in these facilities Doug Ford is bent on shuttering

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ive-used-toronto-supervised-consumption-site-year/docview/3099790564/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I%27ve+used+a+Toronto+supervised+consumption+site+for+a+year.+What+it%27s+really+like+in+these+facilities+Doug+Ford+is+bent+on+shuttering&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-09-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Imagine learning that someone you deeply care for has suddenly died, and their death was entirely preventable. It's a feeling most addicts have come to know well, especially in the past decade as the street drug supply has grown ever more toxic and lethal. The shock, and the grief and the rage?
That's pretty much exactly how I felt when I learned that our Premier and his Minister of Health planned to close the Queen West supervised consumption site, along with four other sites in Toronto and another five across Ontario.
I'm not nearly the voracious consumer of news I once was. Addiction and homelessness have a way of doing that to a person, but by some strange coincidence, I decided to have a peek at Google News just a few hours after the Ford government's recent announcement.
The news was horrifying, but not a complete surprise. In recent months, conservative politicians at all levels of government across Canada, have been using increasingly bellicose rhetoric to attack the harm reduction model of addressing substance use.
Supervised consumption sites (SCS) are perhaps the most visible manifestation of harm reduction. These sites provide a safe environment, sterile tools and are staffed by health care professionals and trained harm reduction workers, for people to use drugs with less risk.
Taken together, the impending closure of all ten sites is a gut punch, but the Queen West SCS has particular personal resonance. I was buzzed through its doors - the first time I had ever availed myself of an SCS - about a year ago.
That first visit marked the beginning of my long and as-yet unfinished climb out of the unbearably dark pit I occupied. For years, I had struggled alone, not just with my addiction, but with a host of other demons including, but not limited to, burnout, mental illness and self-loathing. None of that mattered inside the SCS.
I felt welcomed and valued from the moment I stepped inside. I slowly opened up to one worker, Josh. We bonded during one of his breaks over crossword puzzles. He's since moved to another role with another agency, but another harm reduction worker who remains in contact with him told me he asks about me often.
I kept coming back, and since that first visit, a transformation has taken hold. Words like "addict," "homeless," and "crazy" are no longer words that define me. Rather, they have become words that serve to help inform who I have become, in the here and now. But I am so much more than just those three words. I am a parent, friend, daredevil, raconteur and despite my best efforts to not be, I am also an activist, apparently.
Whatever doubt I may have had about my activism went out the window days ago, courtesy of Doug Ford and Sylvia Jones, and for good reason.
The Queen West site provided me with more than a place to safely use drugs. The staff provided medical attention when I needed it, food and snacks when I was hungry, water and juice when I was thirsty, a sympathetic ear and a hug when I despaired.
Through them, I was connected with a phenomenal support worker from Parkdale Queen West Community Health Centre (PQWCHC), which operates the Queen West SCS. They has been my advocate and biggest supporter. With their help, obstacles that seemed insurmountable have vanished.
After I suffered a badly broken arm and required surgery, that worker and their colleagues were able to secure a respite care bed where I could convalesce.
When it was time for me to be discharged, staff from the respite care facility and PQWCHC found me a shelter bed. Now, for the first time in what seems like an eternity, I see a path opening to stable, secure housing.
Along the way, my drug use has fallen dramatically. Apparently, that's a thing that happens when despair gets replaced with hope. I can't say I've escaped that dark pit, but I no longer feel like I'm sinking.
Instead of quicksand threatening to engulf me, I feel like I have enough of a toehold, along with the outstretched arms of people like Josh and my support worker, to finally climb out.
None of these things would ever have happened in a place our Premier all but labelled a drug den in his announcements. The house where his brother, then-mayor Rob Ford was filmed smoking crack? That was a drug den.
Doug Ford of all people should know the difference.
CAPTION: The impending closure of all ten supervised consumption sites in Ontario is a gut punch, but the Queen West SCS has particular personal resonance for Kevin Wilson.Instead of quicksand threatening to engulf me, I feel like I have enough of a toehold, along with the outstretched arms to finally climb out, Kevin Wilson writes about his local supervised consumption site.
CREDIT: Kevin Wilson, Contributor

Subject: Homeless people; Respite care; Harm reduction

Province vows to enforce rules on alcohol sales: Team of inspectors to monitor rollout to corner stores starting this week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-vows-enforce-rules-on-alcohol-sales/docview/3099482041/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+vows+to+enforce+rules+on+alcohol+sales%3A+Team+of+inspectors+to+monitor+rollout+to+corner+stores+starting+this+week&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario has staffed up in advance of booze coming to corner store shelves this week and says it plans to carry out "unannounced inspections" to make sure the new rules are being followed.
One thing it won't be looking for, however, is whether alcohol-carrying stores are near schools. Unlike with cannabis stores or supervised drug consumption sites, the province is not creating bubble zones around students to protect them from alcohol.
The AGCO said with increased provincial funding it now has more than 100 inspectors to monitor the 4,146 convenience stores approved so far to sell beer, wine and canned cocktails starting Thursday. The commission is the sole enforcer when it comes to ensuring only eligible stores get to sell eligible beverages.
These inspectors are supported by "back-office analysts and advanced, data-driven monitoring tools," said AGCO spokesperson Raymond Kahnert.
"Proactive inspections allow us to help educate licensees and address potential issues before they escalate, while responsive inspections follow up on any public complaints or issues flagged by local authorities," he added.
The City of Toronto said it had no influence on the rollout and will not be watching over it.
"There are no plans for the City of Toronto to track retail stores that sell alcohol, as the AGCO regulates the sale, service and delivery of liquor in Ontario, including licences and permits," spokesperson Elise von Scheel said in an email to the Star.
Toronto police also defer to the AGCO, but are able to conduct inspections, issue tickets and lay charges should the situation demand.
"Depending on the circumstances, police should be called in emergencies or if there is an immediate safety concern and the AGCO should be called with concerns about liquor licences or sales," said Toronto police spokesperson Shannon Eames.
Even before the new rules have taken effect, the AGCO has had to punish an offending retailer. An Etobicoke shop this week jumped the gun and sold alcohol before the official start date. It also sold spirits, which is still forbidden under the new regulations, and didn't buy the liquor through the LCBO.
The penalty was a three-week suspension of its licence to sell liquor and lottery tickets.
Depending on how severe it considers a particular violation, the AGCO has the ability to fine retailers up to $100,000.
The most costly offence for a store owner is knowingly selling liquor to a minor.
While Ontario's Cannabis Licence Act prohibits cannabis stores from being located less than 150 metres from a school, its Liquor Licence and Control Act puts no geographic restrictions on alcohol outlets.
In contrast, it was announced in August that 10 of the 17 supervised drug consumption sites in Ontario will be closed by springtime because of forthcoming legislation from the province mandating they stay at least 200 metres away from schools and daycares.
Both decisions could be harmful to Ontarians' health, according to experts. In May, the Canadian Public Health Association wrote that allowing alcohol in corner stores is a public health risk.
"Expanding the sale of alcohol to more retail locations will increase its accessibility and consumption. Research shows that greater availability of alcohol leads to higher rates of consumption and, consequently, more alcohol-related harms."
Meanwhile, supervised consumption has been shown to save lives. A study this year in the Lancet found overdose deaths were reduced by 67 per cent in neighbourhoods within 500 metres of supervised consumption sites.
Newly shuttered drug consumption clinics will not have the chance to relocate. The province is banning the creation of new sites.
When asked why the province wasn't also imposing restrictions on alcohol sales near schools, Premier Doug Ford's office forwarded the Star's inquiry to the Ministry of the Attorney General.
A spokesperson for the ministry said it wouldn't be fair to force store owners to relocate away from schools to sell booze.
"As grocery and convenience stores are existing businesses in the marketplace, it would be inappropriate for these businesses to relocate should proximity requirements prevent their businesses from selling alcohol," said Jack Fazzari.
Fazzari dismissed the comparison with drug consumption sites.
"The legal and regulated sale of alcohol in our province has been occurring in communities for nearly 100 years," he said. "Places where alcohol is sold and the locations of drug consumption sites are not comparable."
As for cannabis, Ontario had to take "careful steps" to regulate it, including keeping sales away from schools, as it was a "brand-new market" when it was legalized federally.
Fazzari said the province's tools for mitigating the harms alcohol causes are still in use. This includes only allowing sale from 7 a.m. to 11 p.m., enforcing the legal drinking age of 19, making sure alcohol doesn't get any cheaper than the legal minimum retail price of beer at $2.81 per litre, putting up signs warning of the dangers of drinking while pregnant, and mandatory staff training for all new retailers.
"Our government will continue upholding some of the highest existing standards of social responsibility through safe and responsible implementation of our plan to bring Ontario in line with the rest of Canada," he said.
CREDIT: Ben Cohen Toronto Star

Subject: Public health; Retail stores; Liquor laws & regulations; Alcohol; Sales; Convenience stores; Schools; Marijuana

School bus operators warn of delays: Association says provincial funding changes mean there isn't enough to cover fuel costs, driver pay

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/school-bus-operators-warn-delays/docview/3099482006/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+bus+operators+warn+of+delays%3A+Association+says+provincial+funding+changes+mean+there+isn%27t+enough+to+cover+fuel+costs%2C+driver+pay&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: School bus operators warn of a bumpy road ahead after a change in provincial funding they say will lead to delays and cancellations.
The Ontario government, while increasing transportation funding overall, still does not cover actual costs and is not providing enough to cover "adequate driver compensation, driver retention bonuses and new buses," said Nancy Daigneault, executive director of School Bus Ontario.
"To make matters worse, the Ministry of Education imposed a devastating reduction of between 56 to 58 cents per litre of fuel, which has left operators in certain regions worried about filling their tanks to cover the millions of kilometres a day travelled," and some operators are still owed money from the previous school year, added Daigneault.
Daigneault called the problem "unintended consequences" of the new funding model, which was announced last year and since then "we've been working to try to rectify some issues" with it.
"We also need to warn people that over the next couple of weeks and months, things could get worse before they get better. This could result in slowdowns ... there are some operators that are just going on a wing and a prayer right now, and relying on local banks to finance them."
The province provides the funding for transportation, but there is a patchwork of contracts between the operators and individual school boards.
Education Minister Jill Dunlop, who has been on the job two weeks, said in a statement that "providing stability for students, parents and our schools is critical, that why our government has increased funding to school boards, enhanced wages for bus drivers and invested in strengthening safety of school buses to ensure children can reliably get to and from school."
The province is spending $80 million more in student transportation, for a total of $1.3 billion, Dunlop added, and "ensured every board received an increase of at least three per cent this school year, with many receiving double digit increases" as well as monies to pay a driver retention bonus of up to $2,000 a year.
"That includes a focus on recruiting and retaining drivers with our driver retention program that provides each eligible driver with bonus payments of up to $2,000 per year," she said.
But Michael Bellmore, president of the Ontario Catholic School Trustees Association, said transportation has been a concern and "truth be told is that funding just doesn't meet funding needs."
The reality is "that wages have gone up ... (as has) the cost of fuel," and the association has told the ministry about its concerns, he added.
"I'm sure we're going to hear a lot next week, once the school year starts officially, of the issues and pitfalls that they've experienced," he said. "I suspect strongly that transportation is going to be one of the top items on that list that we hear feedback on" from boards.
Kathleen Woodcock, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association, said "while the most recent round (of education funding) included a welcome increase in transportation funding and addressed some key concerns identified by boards - such as the need for funding for special purpose vehicles - there are still challenges that remain."
She said rural, remote and northern boards in particular face busing challenges.
NDP Education critic Chandra Pasma, who represents Ottawa West-Nepean, said boards are struggling "and it's going to be more and more school boards every year, unless the government is actually willing to admit that they made a mistake and fix the funding formula for student transportation."
She said the amounts allocated are based on pre-pandemic pricing "and costs have massively escalated," and when it comes to wages, "the benchmark doesn't cover all the time that a bus driver is actually driving the bus," including on the way to pick up students.
"That's why you saw in Ottawa last year, an operator walked away from the contract, which is what contributed to huge chaos," she added.
CREDIT: Kristin Rushowy Queen's Park Bureau

Poilievre's attack on me a symptom of larger problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievres-attack-on-me-symptom-larger-problem/docview/3099481960/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre%27s+attack+on+me+a+symptom+of+larger+problem&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-09-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When nurses speak out, we do so based on evidence and on health and clinical knowledge. Our advocacy for healthy public policy is grounded on the lived experiences of those we care for and by our in-depth expertise on social determinants of health.
This is one element that makes nursing the most trusted profession, except apparently for Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, a man who aspires to become prime minister. His office recently mocked the expertise of myself and others with "too many letters after their names." This strange behaviour reveals the divisive and belittling leadership we can expect from Poilievre and his team if given the opportunity.
There is a history to his lack of empathy and consistent undermining of scientific evidence and expertise. We are still reeling from extremist politicians who openly sabotaged vaccination efforts during and after the COVID-19 pandemic. As recently as October 2023, Poilievre and at least one of his colleagues pushed a private member's anti-vaccine mandate bill in Parliament to tie the hands of public health officers - a bill the majority of MPs wisely agreed to drop.
From a healthy public policy perspective, the callous message coming from Poilievre's office after I voiced support for supervised consumption site (SCS) is dangerous on many fronts. He is shutting down dialogue on an escalating and preventable human tragedy that needs immediate attention.
And unfortunately, Poilievre and team are not alone in this rhetoric. For example, the Ford government announced on Aug. 20 a misguided decision to gut consumption site services in the province. The next day, Premier Doug Ford slammed the federal government's prior approval of 16 safer supply projects in Ontario, stating, "As far as I'm concerned the federal government is the biggest drug dealer in the entire country."
Ford's overall message is deceptive. His claim that we need to get rid of safe supply begs a serious question: what will happen when there are no guardrails against unsafe supply? The answer? Ontario's communities will look like some of the big cities in the U.S., with people convulsing and dying in the streets.
While nurses do welcome the Ontario government's investment in a set of Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs, these alone will do little to confront the public health emergency that will arise if life-saving consumption sites and needle exchange programs are withdrawn.
Here's what we know. An average of 10 people per day are dying due to an unregulated drug supply in Ontario. In 2023 alone, almost 4,000 lives were lost to the toxic drug crisis.
In a reckless move that amounts to a death sentence for even more vulnerable people, the Ford government has given notice that it is withdrawing life-saving health-care services offered at supervised consumption sites under the pretence of safety and security. Yet the facts show that crime has decreased in communities that have consumption sites.
Poilievre's referring to these sites as "drug dens" is callous and deceptive. These sites offer a lifeline to those struggling with substance use. The very same harm reduction programs that some leaders are targeting don't only save lives; they also open the door to treatment and recovery.
Whether it's overdose prevention, primary care or mental health support, these services help keep people alive. For many struggling with substance use, these sites are their only hope when they are ready for treatment and recovery. Shutting sites down will lead to more preventable deaths, increased HIV and Hepatitis C infections and cost taxpayers more. And our communities will become less safe with more contaminated needles lying around.
Star readers likely know people who struggle with substance use. They are sons, daughters, mothers, fathers, friends and neighbours. These people need our compassion and support, not stigma, sarcasm and withdrawal of life-saving solutions that can put them onto healthier paths.
For these reasons, Ford must reverse the thoughtless decision to gut supervised consumption site services in Ontario. It is imperative that we work on all fronts - genuinely, courageously and together - to save lives being lost to the toxic drug supply.
As for Poilievre's office's recent mocking of actual expertise, yes, we nurses - including me - have many letters after our names. We are proud of our education. It affords us the in-depth knowledge to care for people in health and illness.
Instead of mocking, Poilievre and his team could benefit from experts to understand the evidence on the importance of supervised consumption sites as an essential pillar of a multi-pronged approach to address this public health crisis and to make our communities safer for all.
Dr. Doris Grinspun, RN, O.ONT.,
is chief executive officer of Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario.
CREDIT: Doris Grinspun Contributor;

Subject: Public health; Health care; Nurses; Public policy; Drug use

Why Canada will see wage growth and better jobs into 2025 - without triggering inflation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/why-canada-will-see-wage-growth-better-jobs-into/docview/3099130178/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Canada+will+see+wage+growth+and+better+jobs+into+2025+-+without+triggering+inflation&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In this three-part series, contributing columnist Armine Yalnizyan explores the connection between wage growth and inflation, and what it means for our future costs of living.
As an eruption of high-profile strikes heighten anxiety about more price hikes, the public mood is shifting on wage growth.
What was once widely viewed as essential - catching up to inflation - is now seen as potentially hazardous to our economic health. Echoes of the Bank of Canada's warning in 2022 that wage growth could lead to another wave of inflation ring loud in the ear of businesses, shoppers and even workers who have fallen behind inflation.
But let's check the facts: Yes, average hourly wages are growing at twice the pace of inflation in Canada. And they've been growing consistently at the same pace, around five per cent from the previous year, since February 2023. But inflation has been falling all the while. Last month it hit 2.5 per cent.
As the chart shows, this isn't the first time wage growth has eclipsed inflation for a protracted period without triggering a rise in inflation.
Not only is wage growth not driving inflation - when you look at whose wages are rising it's a good news story: fewer people working in the poorest-paid quartile of jobs, even as those jobs pay more; more people working in the top quartile, as more high-skill and higher-paid jobs get added to the mix. People aren't just bargaining for more. They're getting better jobs.
Of course newsworthy union-negotiated collective agreements grab attention, but overall it's not unions driving up wage growth. Rather, it's the changing composition of the job market. How (and why) would you make that stop?
What happens next will be profoundly shaped by politics, and how our chosen leaders narrate how the world works.
Political upheaval in the United States potentially portends a fresh wave of progressive politics that support workers' and human rights, and the freedom to associate, including organizing unions.
Or, we could be on the threshold of a massive rollback of workers' rights under the guise of making manufacturing and oil production great again, in our own backyards.
These two diametrically opposed political tendencies chart starkly different trajectories for wage growth in 2025 and beyond as administrations enact or axe rules that systemically shape bargaining power.
Ultimately, though, it will be the constantly evolving economy that drives wage growth.
The Bank of Canada recently said there's a good type of wage growth, the kind that is tied to productivity growth. Looks like compositional change (fewer jobs in hotels, restaurants and bars and retail, more jobs in professional, scientific and technical businesses and health) has packed a wage-growth punch as powerful as productivity growth.
Occupational changes also affect productivity and inequality. For example, over the past five years jobs in management swelled by a third. If management means focusing on attracting and retaining needed labour, lower-paid jobs could continue to see rapid increases in pay and benefits, reducing inequality.
Or, if economies slow further, those same managers could be tasked with making those further down the totem pole pay for tougher business conditions, increasing inequality.
Indeed, the economy is slowing, and unemployment is rising as a direct result of the Bank of Canada's attempt to tame inflation. Normally less economic security increases the number of poorly paid, unstable jobs and suppresses wage growth.
Yet, despite tumultuous politics and erratic economics, I see three reasons solid wage growth and better jobs may continue in 2025:
 The federal Liberals' long-overdue decision to roll back some elements of the use of temporary foreign workers, in particular the exploitative low-paid workers parts of the program, and tightening intake of international students will likely improve wages for the poorest paid. And there will be more pressure to extend these rollbacks to industries still struggling with labour shortages, such as health care, construction and food processing, while creating more paths to permanence.
 Due to demographics, we're at the point that the labour force would shrink if it weren't for newcomers. They're not the problem; in fact, just the opposite. It's possible to both add newcomers to avoid the economic impacts of bona fide labour shortages throughout the skills and pay spectrum, as well as improve skills development and on-the job-training for Canadians, improving outcomes for all workers and businesses.
 A wave of corporate concentration and profitability has spurred a surge in the fight against corporate power, from workers organizing in individual workplaces for the first time to regulatory fights against price-fixing and insufficient competition. Working for workers is not just a slogan. It's a meaningful industrial strategy.
I'm hopeful for better because it's becoming increasingly obvious that the economic orthodoxies of the past 40 years are no longer delivering on their promises.
The "olds" are being challenged. Youth are feeling the electricity of the moment. In their workplaces. On the streets. On campuses. In campaigns.
They are seeing that when they organize, they can nudge change. They're experiencing first-hand, to paraphrase a slogan, when they fight, they win.
We're so wired to feel outrage - about temporary foreign workers, about strikers asking for too much, about how unaffordable life is particularly for the young - we are overlooking the light for the heat.
This moment offers us the chance to make every job a good job, without triggering inflation.
And who wouldn't want to be part of that kind of growth?
CAPTION: Workers with the Art Gallery of Ontario hit the picket line inMarch.Newsworthy union-negotiated collective agreements grab attention, but overall it's not unions driving up wage growth, Armine Yalnizyan writes.

Subject: Strikes; Inequality; Productivity; Art galleries & museums; Wages & salaries; Skills

Business indexing term: Subject: Strikes Productivity Art galleries & museums Wages & salaries

Location: Canada

Document 478 of 500

There's a simple reason Ontarians still like Doug Ford despite all the scandals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/theres-simple-reason-ontarians-still-like-doug/docview/3099130176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=There%27s+a+simple+reason+Ontarians+still+like+Doug+Ford+despite+all+the+scandals&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you're surprised by Doug Ford's enduring popularity as his seventh summer as premier slides into its final long weekend, don't be. The explanation could be pretty simple: you might be paying too much attention.
The average voter and citizen doesn't spend much time paying attention to the news. They might be able to name Olivia Chow as the mayor of Toronto, but there's a good chance they don't know who their local councillor is. They almost certainly know that Justin Trudeau is the prime minister, but it's not a given that they know he's a Liberal.
This is why I'm no longer surprised when I see polls like the one produced by Abacus for the Star, released just a few days ago. The poll found that Ford, who has been premier of this province for the last six years - years marked by incredible tragedies and significant scandals - remains very popular. Asked which descriptors apply to Ford, the largest plurality of voters in this province picked "friendly." And based on his perceived image, if a vote were held today, Ford would win a majority - his third.
I dared peek at X late this week and the reaction was, uh, what you'd expect. For all his general popularity, Ford's critics really don't like the guy.
And some seem to struggle to understand why anyone would. I offer this as both explanation and solace: most Canadians do pay attention to the news, but it's only a tiny percentage that pay close attention. A Maru poll from last year pegged the number of Canadians who were hyper-engaged in the news at 16 per cent, and that felt about right to me.
Ford has done an excellent job over the years of maintaining a folksy image. As one Ford associate told me years ago, Ford is, above all else, a salesman. He's really good at cultivating that friendly, "I'm just here to help" persona and running with it. For voters who don't follow the news or limit their news consumption to catching a few top stories on the radio while commuting or scanning headlines while scrolling on their phones, the salesman persona is likely all they've ever seen from Ford.
The catastrophes in our long-term care homes during the pandemic may not have registered with them because they were busy managing the pandemic's effects on their own lives. The Greenbelt scandal is probably something they've heard mentioned but haven't looked into deeply. The closing of the Science Centre, if it registered at all, was probably forgotten in days. And so on.
Lest any of this sound condemnatory or critical, many of these disengaged voters are highly intelligent, well-educated and overall great people. They've just chosen, rightly or wrongly, not to be engaged in the news. To the extent they have any interest in politics, they reach quick decisions after a cursory examination of the facts. If Ford seems friendly, they may vote for him. This blows the minds of the hyper-engaged, but it's true.
Among those hyper-engaged are Ford's actual opponents - our provincial Liberals, NDP and Green MPPs, and their supporters. I've been baffled over the years by the continued insistence of so many of Ford's harshest critics on portraying him as some kind of extremist. When a huge part of the electorate has decided that Ford is a nice guy, trying to portray him as some kind of freak doesn't change how they feel about Ford. It helps change how they feel about you. They write you off as a kook, and tune you out.
One day, Ford is going to step in it in some way that finally penetrates his deflector shield of genial affability. The Abacus poll speaks to this. There are some metrics, like trustworthiness and effectiveness, where the voters are much more evenly split on Ford, and for good reason. As time goes on and flops accumulate, the voters will sour on him. As far as political predictions go, that's about the safest one I could possibly make: any politician who serves long enough finds themselves in that situation eventually (see Trudeau, Justin).
So for Ford's critics, that's the good news, such as it is. The voters won't like him forever. But they like him for now, and there's no mystery why. They think he's a nice guy, and then they think about something else.
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford announced that beer and wine will be available at corner stores during a media event at a Pioneer On The Run Convenience Store in May.

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters; Pandemics

People: Trudeau, Justin

Title: There's a simple reason Ontarians still like Doug Ford despite all the scandals

Arguments for, and against, Fred Hahn's resignation from CUPE

Full text: CUPE vice-president defies union board's demand to resign, Aug. 22
I have known Fred Hahn, Ontario president and national vice-president of the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE), for over 25 years and worked with him fighting hydro privatization. Hahn has a lifelong record of fighting hate in all its forms. Of course, the Oct. 7 attack by Hamas was absolutely horrendous and now Hahn is under fire. Yes, Hahn made a big mistake in this video and has apologized for it. That mistake does not make him antisemitic. National President Mark Hancock should accept Hahn's apology. The intent to be antisemitic simply was never there. Hahn is right to refuse to resign in this exaggerated campaign against him.
Paul Kahnert, Markham
I can accept Fred Hahn's assertion that he is not antisemitic. That makes it even more tragic that he posted a video which is clearly antisemitic. It's a bit like a great mathematician being unable to calculate a 20 per cent tip at a restaurant. By posting the video he may have given a completely misleading impression of his true values. He showed a blind spot but that should not cause us to rush to judgment.
Bruce Couchman, Ottawa
Premiers in glass houses shouldn't throw stones
Premier Doug Ford recently called Fred Hahn "a disgusting human being" and urged discipline. Ford has no business lecturing anyone about antisemitism. It wasn't that long ago that he was peddling stereotypes - bringing up his Jewish doctor, dentist, and accountant - after his brother used an antisemitic slur. Hahn apologized for posting the video. If that isn't enough, then Ford should surely resign for his own history. Our province would be better with more Hahns and fewer Fords.
Amir Fleischmann, Toronto
Hahn must be accountable
CUPE's Fred Hahn is a bully and only a bully would refuse to step down when the pressure is on. If CUPE cannot do the right thing and demand Hahn's resignation, then they should stop discussing or negotiating contracts where taxpayer money is involved. The taxpayer should not have to support antisemitic activity.
Beverly Northeast, Goodwood, Ont.
We can criticize Israel without criticizing Jewish people
The actions of Israel, like those of any other country, are fair game for scrutiny. That it is a Jewish state as opposed to, say, a Muslim one, makes no difference. The destruction of Gaza and the illegal occupation of Palestine are human rights complaints. These complaints are against the Israeli administration, not members of the Jewish faith.
Ali Manji, Thornhill
CAPTION: CUPE leader Fred Hahn says he will defy the union executive board's demand that he resign for posting a video characterized as divisive and antisemitic, a criticism voiced by a growing number of his own members.

The road ahead should not include Justin Trudeau

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/road-ahead-should-not-include-justin-trudeau/docview/3099130103/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+road+ahead+should+not+include+Justin+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Behind closed doors, Trudeau ponders the road ahead - as one of his former advisers says the PM faces a massive challenge, Aug. 27
In her article, reporter Tonda MacCharles quotes House leader Karina Gould asking, "what is the change that Canadians are looking for?" To pose that question in the current Canadian political climate is to reveal a tone deafness, something of which the federal Liberals are often accused. Canadians, even dyed-in-the-wool Liberal Canadians, are looking for Justin Trudeau to step aside. Of course the Liberals are going to lose to the federal Conservatives next time around, but the margin of loss will be far less devastating if Trudeau steps aside in favour of the continued well being of Liberal voters and a progressive Canada. His party needs to take serious account of the lessons to be learned from the electoral fate of the Kathleen Wynne Liberals as a result of her decision to hold fast at Queen's Park. We dyed-in-the-wool Liberals are counting on it.
James McKnight, Toronto
It pains me to write this, but Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre might be right about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau being out of sync with our national character, which often involves politely deferring to the rights of others. Polls clearly show that Canadians have lost confidence in Trudeau and want a change in leadership. While they rightly fear Poilievre's mean-spirited character, they still tolerate Trudeau, hoping for a progressive alternative under new leadership. However, Trudeau refuses to give Canadians the political choice they deserve to prevent a Poilievre victory, which many see as a threat to our peace, order and good government. Trudeau has become an obstacle and has overstayed his welcome. Prime Minister, why stay if the people don't want you?
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Perhaps the federal Liberals and New Democrats should consider uniting before the next election. The United Liberal Democratic Party would energize the next election and give this coalition a chance to continue to govern. There would be several tough issues to be resolved, but surely it is better to decide how you would govern and who would lead than to sit in the opposition seats, few in number.
David Gladstone, Toronto
Not just Trudeau
These Canadians don't care much about politics. Here's why a growing number feel democracy isn't working for them, Aug. 25
Reporter Raisa Patel hit the nail on the head when she wrote that "the system isn't working" for some voters. The electoral system isn't working for the majority of us! We inherited an electoral system that was created to keep wealthy landowners in power and it is still working to keep the wealthy in power. Right now, it is the minority of voters who are heard by our politicians. Our system of exclusively acknowledging winners by seats rather than by a true (over 50 per cent) majority of voters means that the majority don't have a voice in our "democratic" institutions. Let's have a meaningful and heeded citizens' assembly where the people, not the politicians, choose what the best electoral system is for Canada.
Stephen Cohen, Toronto
'Canadians are exhausted'
Can Justin Trudeau's Liberals seize on the U.S. Democrats' success? Aug. 28
The short answer is no. Justin Trudeau is far from being Kamala Harris and Pierre Poilievre is far from being Donald Trump. Canadians are exhausted from Trudeau's failures and screw-ups. They are tired of being told how the federal Liberals are learning from their mistakes and how seriously they take them and how they're going to do better. Just like it has taken years to start fixing the temporary foreign worker program, years after they were given sound advice from experts. They've also failed over the years to fix housing, affordability, homelessness and labour unrest, not to mention health care. Canadians are exhausted.
Brian Mellor, Midland, Ont.
Our society of rage
A menacing stranger harassed an MP at a lake. Onlookers stood up for democracy, Aug. 27
While on a bus tour of Italy in 2019, I was part of a lineup at a restaurant that included many tourists when I was introduced to a fellow Canadian, a man from Alberta who was told I was from Toronto. The man turned around, looked at me, sneered, and said "Toronto eh? I bet you're voting for Trudeau." I was a little taken aback, but not surprised. I quietly and calmly told him I don't discuss politics while on vacation. He didn't say anything, sneered again and turned his back. I was embarrassed by the disrespect he showed not only to me but to our country. I know the situation could have escalated but I didn't want to engage or encourage him further. I suppose, considering the political atmosphere these days, I got off lightly. It frightens me terribly to think that this, and, probably worse, is ahead for my grandchildren.
Bonnie Beaudry, Burlington
CAPTION: Several ministers who spoke to the Star say nobody at the cabinet table is actively elbowing him out, but nonetheless Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is pondering the road ahead.

A stray bullet is being used for Doug Ford's political purposes

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stray-bullet-is-being-used-doug-fords-political/docview/3099130065/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+stray+bullet+is+being+used+for+Doug+Ford%27s+political+purposes&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with addictions, Aug. 21
Ontario began a review of safe consumption sites last summer. The review was triggered after the death of Karolina Huebner-Makurat, a mother in Toronto, who was killed by a stray bullet near one of the city's sites. She was walking near Queen Street and Carlaw Avenue on July 7, 2023, when a fight between three men broke out. This led to a study of clientele and activities at the site, and all of a sudden, Premier Doug Ford's government is banning sites within 200-metres of schools and daycares.
In June of this year, three people were shot and killed near Don Mills and York Mills roads in North York. The shooter, who also took his life, was allegedly defrauded in a mortgage scam by the two victims. The triple shooting took place near a school. That same month, a 14-year-old boy allegedly shot and killed two men in the parking lot of North Albion Collegiate Institute, a high school. A Grade 12 student was killed in an execution-style shooting inside David and Mary Thomson Collegiate, a high school, in February 2022. I guess we will have to close the schools themselves for public safety.
My point is, the safe injection sites have nothing to do with public safety and the stray bullet that tragically killed Huebner-Makurat is being used by Ford and his government to do what they always wanted to do - try to hide what they see as the ugliness of the less fortunate. A death is a death, whether it is from a shooting or an overdose. Ford clearly cares more about one than the other.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge, Ont.
CAPTION: The Ford government says safe consumption sites, like Kensington Market Overdose Prevention Site on Augusta Street, must close because they are in close proximity to schools.
