Document 482 of 500

Toronto's new favourite photo spots, perfect picnic food and more. 7 ways to make the most of summer's last long weekend

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/torontos-new-favourite-photo-spots-perfect-picnic/docview/3099130064/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Toronto%27s+new+favourite+photo+spots%2C+perfect+picnic+food+and+more.+7+ways+to+make+the+most+of+summer%27s+last+long+weekend&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Students head back to school on Tuesday and the city, softer in the golden light of August, will return to a busier pace.
Gridlock will enter a new dimension; the TTC will be slammed; there are 72 lunches to be prepared between Sept. 3 and the last day of classes before Christmas if your children are in public school.
But summer isn't over - not until Sept. 22. Here are some new ways to eke the last bit of summer across the GTA before autumn cools things down.
In a city famous for being grey, the new bridges in Toronto's Port Lands spark joy.
Swooping and white on the outside, the interior canopies have been painted in vibrant colours: Red on the two Cherry Street Bridges North; yellow for the Cherry Street Bridge South and orange for the Commissioners Street Bridge.
It's like moving through a crayon landscape.
The goal was to link the city to the new Port Lands development, but also to create a new Toronto landmark.
"People are doing what we hoped they would - sharing pictures of them on social media," said Waterfront Toronto spokesperson Mira Shenker. "They keep coming back."
The first bridge opened in October 2022, and the last two in early 2024.
Instagrammers have taken up the challenge of snapping them.
"It just might be Toronto's new favourite photo spot," according to @thelandofdustin on Instagram.
The bridges were designed by Entuitive, schlaich bergermann partner and Grimshaw Architects.
- Francine Kopun
At the corner of Steeles Avenue East and Middlefield Road, on the northeastern edge of Scarborough, is a delightful one-two punch of a food-filled plaza called Milliken Crossing and the 79-acre Milliken Park, perfect for picnics of any size.
First, the food. Inside the Field Fresh Supermarket is a food court with a bustling stall called Happy Congee, which specializes in Chinese dough fritters made from scratch. (Most restaurants in the GTA fry them from frozen.) Other options include the halal-friendly Montreal smoked meat joint SumiLicious, banh mi at Nguyen Huong and Hong Kong café Pine House Café - the breakfast is an incredible deal.
Across the street is one of my favourite parks in the GTA, with all the amenities, right off a major intersection: big parking lot, washrooms; a big children's playground that was renovated in 2022; splash pad; covered picnic areas; a big pond for bird-watching; paved, flat walking paths that circle the park's perimeter; and fields straight out of a Windows 95 desktop wallpaper. Come here after lunch, or grab the wrapped banh mi and some cold drinks from the supermarket for a picnic.
- Karon Liu
Toronto has hundreds of great playgrounds, but the one at the northwest corner of Grange Park, behind the Art Gallery of Ontario and within view of the murals and multicoloured steel beams that adorn the entrance to OCAD University, is among the best - worth a visit even without kids in tow. More than a place to pass time before dinner, it feels like a cultural destination.
Designed by PFS Studio in 2017 to blend into the surrounding area, its play structures are inspired by art supplies. Kids can climb through a paint-can tunnel, take shade under a painter's palette canopy and ascend a climbing structure that resembles balls of crumpled paper.
An impressive tree canopy shades the two-hectare park. Beyond the trees, condo and office towers sprouting from the entertainment and financial districts remind you that you're still in the centre of downtown.
- Amy Dempsey Raven
Nestled just beyond Mississauga's bustling city centre is Riverwood Park, a tranquil 150-acre escape of woodlands, meadows and ravines.
A short walk from the Erindale GO Station, the park features a striking green and blue aluminum sculpture by New York based architect and designer Marc Fornes at the entrance. The winding roadway has signs that warn motorists of local wildlife such as white-tailed deer and red fox that call this conservation area home.
On a hot summer day, the big ancient trees offer relief for hikers and dog walkers.
The manicured gardens, managed by Riverwood Conservancy volunteers, offer benches where visitors can rest and enjoy the chirps and whirrs of birds and insects. Photographers can be seen carefully positioning their cameras and tripods to capture the images of the more than 475 species of plants and animals in the habitat alongside the Credit River. There are education, cultural and conservation programs year-round.
"We're people's backyard. It is a community in harmony with nature," said Sara Wilbur-Collins, executive director of the Riverwood Conservancy. "I don't know if you've got a chickadee land on your hand. It's totally amazing."
- Nicholas Keung
After making the unpopular decision to permanently close the skating rink overlooking Lake Ontario, Harbourfront Centre unveiled a new attraction in the space this spring.
Small nozzles embedded in the ground in what is now a public square spray mist into the air, creating a foggy effect that beautifully frames the city in one direction, or boats on the water in another. The potential for photographs in the space is endless.
Catching the misters in operation can be tricky - they are supposed to operate from 10 a.m. to 10 p.m., seven days a week in summer and on a reduced schedule in the fall. However, inclement weather can affect scheduling, as can event programming. But the location of the new public square - steps north of a boardwalk stretching from York Street to the Narnia-like Toronto Music Garden - makes it easy to return again and again. Rain or shine, the boardwalk offers unparalleled views of the water and the Toronto Islands, with plenty of restaurants, marinas and sculptures, including the kid-friendly wavedecks at Simcoe, Rees and Spadina slips to make the visit worthwhile, even if the mist proves elusive.
- Francine Kopun
Checking out Nathan Phillips Square and city hall might sound like something an out-of-touch guidebook would recommend - unless you look at it from a different angle.
Instead of entering through Queen Street West, where the fleet of tour buses are parked, head to Elizabeth Street at the back of city hall. A series of stairs will get you up to a quiet platform where you can wander through the grassy plots and benches, offering a less-seen view of the Toronto skyline and public square.
From there, walk up the stairs towards the UFO-like heart of city hall, which is wonderfully peaceful on weekends when the buildings are closed (though you might spot the occasional bridal party en route to the wedding chambers). Architecture aficionados will enjoy the close-up of this modernist building, while others will appreciate the shaded spots and picnic tables in the heart of the city. Head toward Nathan Phillips Square and you'll be rewarded with a bird's-eye view of all the action below.
- Karon Liu
Toronto's Necropolis is a portal to another time.
Approaching the gates from Riverdale Park, the clamour of life recedes as the Gothic Revival entry pavilion and chapel come into view.
The seven verdant hectares inside the walls of the cemetery are the final resting place for some of Toronto's most famous citizens, including Anderson Ruffin Abbott, the country's first Black surgeon; Roy (Brownie) Brown, the First World War pilot who shot down Germany's infamous Red Baron; champion rower Ned Hanlan; and Toronto's first mayor, William Lyon Mackenzie.
The remains of 984 early settlers from the Town of York were removed from Potter's Field, formerly at the corner of Yonge and Bloor Streets, and re-interred here in a section called The Resting Place of the Pioneers 1851-1881.
More recently, former NDP leader Jack Layton's remains were committed to the cemetery. His wife, Olivia Chow, now the mayor, later erected a sculpture she made in his memory.
The site offers welcome shade on a hot day and while sunsets in Riverdale Park bathe the evening horizon in riotous hues of orange, the light in the Necropolis is filtered softly through the leaves of mature and stately trees. The narrow pathways encourage exploration and contemplation of Torontonians who lived in a city entirely different from the one that has since grown up around their final resting place. What would they think?
- Francine Kopun
CAPTION: Swooping and white on the outside, the interior canopies of the Port Lands bridges are painted in vibrant colors, like the striking red seen on the two Cherry Street Bridges North shown here.Chinese dough fritters being freshly made at Happy Congee, located at the Field Fresh Supermarket food court in Scarborough.The playground in Grange Park's northwest corner is located near the murals and colourful steel beams at the entrance to OCAD University.Riverwood Park in Mississauga offers a 150-acre urban retreat of woodlands, meadows and ravines.Children and families cool off in the misting pad that replaced the pond at Harbourfront Centre.Take the stairs from Elizabeth Street behind city hall to a quiet platform with views of the Toronto skyline and public square.The Necropolis cemetery in Riverdale, the final resting place of some of Toronto's most notable figures, also offers cool shade and a peaceful retreat on hot days.Grange Park's art-inspired playground, shaded by a lush tree canopy, sits behind the Art Gallery of Ontario and near OCAD University.
CREDIT: Star Staff

Document 483 of 500

Pierre Poilievre's attack on me is a symptom of a larger problem

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-attack-on-me-is-symptom-larger/docview/3099130057/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+attack+on+me+is+a+symptom+of+a+larger+problem&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When nurses speak out, we do so based on evidence and on health and clinical knowledge. Our advocacy for healthy public policy is grounded on the lived experiences of those we care for and by our in-depth expertise on social determinants of health.
This is one element that makes nursing the most trusted profession, except apparently for Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, a man who aspires to become prime minister. His office recently mocked the expertise of myself and others with "too many letters after their names." This strange behaviour reveals the divisive and belittling leadership we can expect from Poilievre and his team if given the opportunity.
There is a history to his lack of empathy and consistent undermining of scientific evidence and expertise. We are still reeling from extremist politicians who openly sabotaged vaccination efforts during and after the COVID-19 pandemic. As recently as October 2023, Poilievre and at least one of his colleagues pushed a private member's anti-vaccine mandate bill in Parliament to tie the hands of public health officers - a bill the majority of MPs wisely agreed to drop.
From a healthy public policy perspective, the callous message coming from Poilievre's office after I voiced support for supervised consumption site (SCS) is dangerous on many fronts. He is shutting down dialogue on an escalating and preventable human tragedy that needs immediate attention.
And unfortunately, Poilievre and team are not alone in this rhetoric. For example, the Ford government announced on Aug. 20 a misguided decision to gut consumption site services in the province. The next day, Premier Doug Ford slammed the federal government's prior approval of 16 safer supply projects in Ontario, stating, "As far as I'm concerned the federal government is the biggest drug dealer in the entire country."
Ford's overall message is deceptive. His claim that we need to get rid of safe supply begs a serious question: what will happen when there are no guardrails against unsafe supply? The answer? Ontario's communities will look like some of the big cities in the U.S., with people convulsing and dying in the streets.
While nurses do welcome the Ontario government's investment in a set of Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment (HART) hubs, these alone will do little to confront the public health emergency that will arise if life-saving consumption sites and needle exchange programs are withdrawn.
Here's what we know. An average of 10 people per day are dying due to an unregulated drug supply in Ontario. In 2023 alone, almost 4,000 lives were lost to the toxic drug crisis.
In a reckless move that amounts to a death sentence for even more vulnerable people, the Ford government has given notice that it is withdrawing life-saving health-care services offered at supervised consumption sites under the pretence of safety and security. Yet the facts show that crime has decreased in communities that have consumption sites.
Poilievre's referring to these sites as "drug dens" is callous and deceptive. These sites offer a lifeline to those struggling with substance use. The very same harm reduction programs that some leaders are targeting don't only save lives; they also open the door to treatment and recovery.
Whether it's overdose prevention, primary care or mental health support, these services help keep people alive. For many struggling with substance use, these sites are their only hope when they are ready for treatment and recovery. Shutting sites down will lead to more preventable deaths, increased HIV and Hepatitis C infections and cost taxpayers more. And our communities will become less safe with more contaminated needles lying around.
Star readers likely know people who struggle with substance use. They are sons, daughters, mothers, fathers, friends and neighbours. These people need our compassion and support, not stigma, sarcasm and withdrawal of life-saving solutions that can put them onto healthier paths.
For these reasons, Ford must reverse the thoughtless decision to gut supervised consumption site services in Ontario. It is imperative that we work on all fronts - genuinely, courageously and together - to save lives being lost to the toxic drug supply.
As for Poilievre's office's recent mocking of actual expertise, yes, we nurses - including me - have many letters after our names. We are proud of our education. It affords us the in-depth knowledge to care for people in health and illness.
Instead of mocking, Poilievre and his team could benefit from experts to understand the evidence on the importance of supervised consumption sites as an essential pillar of a multi-pronged approach to address this public health crisis and to make our communities safer for all.
CAPTION: After Doris Grinspun, CEO of the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario (RNAO), appeared on CBC News to make the case for keeping safe injection sites open, a spokesperson from Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's office mocked her professional designations and called her a "WACKO expert."
CREDIT: Doris Grinspun, Contributor

Subject: Public health; Leadership; Health care; Nurses; Public policy; Drug use

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

People: Poilievre, Pierre

I have known Fred Hahn, Ontario president and national vice-president of the Canadian Union of Public Employees (CUPE), for over 25 years and worked with him to fight hydro privatization. Hahn has a lifelong record of fighting hate in all its forms. Of course, the Oct. 7 attack by Hamas was absolutely horrendous and now Hahn is under fire. Yes, Hahn made a big mistake in this video and has apologized for it. That mistake does not make him antisemitic. National president Mark Hancock should accept Hahn's apology.
Paul Kahnert, Markham
I can accept Fred Hahn's assertion that he is not antisemitic. That makes it even more tragic that he posted a video which is clearly antisemitic. It's a bit like a great mathematician being unable to calculate a 20 per cent tip at a restaurant. By posting the video he may have given a completely misleading impression of his true values. He showed a blind spot, but that should not cause us to rush to judgment.

Premier Doug Ford recently called Fred Hahn "a disgusting human being" and urged discipline. Ford has no business lecturing anyone about antisemitism. It wasn't that long ago that he was peddling stereotypes - bringing up his Jewish doctor, dentist and accountant - after his brother used an antisemitic slur. Hahn apologized. If that isn't enough, then Ford should surely resign for his own history.
Amir Fleischmann, Toronto
Union must hold Hahn accountable
CUPE's Fred Hahn is a bully and only a bully would refuse to step down when the pressure is on. If CUPE cannot do the right thing and demand Hahn's resignation, then they should stop negotiating contracts where taxpayer money is involved. The taxpayer should not have to support antisemitic activity.
Beverly Northeast, Goodwood, Ont.
We can criticize Israel without criticizing Jews
The actions of Israel, like those of any other country, are fair game for scrutiny. That it is a Jewish state as opposed to, say, a Muslim one, makes no difference. These complaints are against the Israeli administration, not members of the Jewish faith.
Ali Manji, Thornhill

Stray bullet used for Ford's political purposes: Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with addictions, Aug. 21

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stray-bullet-used-fords-political-purposes/docview/3099047873/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stray+bullet+used+for+Ford%27s+political+purposes%3A+Doug+Ford+says+safe+injection+sites+are+a+%27failed+policy%27+that+harms+communities+and+people+with+addictions%2C+Aug.+21&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=IN.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with addictions, Aug. 21
Ontario began a review of safe consumption sites last summer. The review was triggered after the death of Karolina Huebner-Makurat, a mother in Toronto, who was killed by a stray bullet near one of the city's sites. She was walking near Queen Street and Carlaw Avenue on July 7, 2023, when a fight between three men broke out. This led to a study of clientele and activities at the site, and all of a sudden, Premier Doug Ford's government is banning sites within 200-metres of schools and daycares.
In June of this year, three people were shot and killed near Don Mills and York Mills roads in North York. The shooter, who also took his life, was allegedly defrauded in a mortgage scam by the two victims. The triple shooting took place near a school.
That same month, a 14-year-old boy allegedly shot and killed two men in the parking lot of North Albion Collegiate Institute, a high school. A Grade 12 student was killed in an execution-style shooting inside David and Mary Thomson Collegiate, a high school, in February 2022. I guess we will have to close the schools themselves for public safety.
My point is, the safe injection sites have nothing to do with public safety and the stray bullet that tragically killed Huebner-Makurat is being used by Ford and his government to do what they always wanted to do - try to hide what they see as the ugliness of the less fortunate.
A death is a death, whether it is from a shooting or an overdose. Ford clearly cares more about one than the other.
Clyde McDonald, Bracebridge, Ont.
CAPTION: The Ford government says safe consumption sites, like the Kensington Market Overdose Prevention Site on Augusta Street, must close because they are in close proximity to schools.Steve Russell Toronto Star

Full text: Behind closed doors, Trudeau ponders the road ahead - as one of his former advisers says the PM faces a massive challenge, Aug. 27
In her article, reporter Tonda MacCharles quotes House leader Karina Gould asking, "what is the change that Canadians are looking for?" To pose that question in the current Canadian political climate is to reveal a tone deafness, something of which the federal Liberals are often accused.
Canadians, even dyed-in-the-wool Liberal Canadians, are looking for Justin Trudeau to step aside. Of course the Liberals are going to lose to the federal Conservatives next time around, but the margin of loss will be far less devastating if Trudeau steps aside in favour of the continued well being of Liberal voters and a progressive Canada. His party needs to take serious account of the lessons to be learned from the electoral fate of the Kathleen Wynne Liberals as a result of her decision to hold fast at Queen's Park. We dyed-in-the-wool Liberals are counting on it.
James McKnight, Toronto
It pains me to write this, but Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre might be right about Prime Minister Justin Trudeau being out of sync with our national character, which often involves politely deferring to the rights of others. Polls clearly show that Canadians have lost confidence in Trudeau and want a change in leadership. While they rightly fear Poilievre's mean-spirited character, they still tolerate Trudeau, hoping for a progressive alternative under new leadership. However, Trudeau refuses to give Canadians the political choice they deserve to prevent a Poilievre victory, which many see as a threat to our peace, order and good government. Trudeau has become an obstacle and has overstayed his welcome.
Tony D'Andrea, Toronto
Perhaps the federal Liberals and New Democrats should consider uniting before the next election. The United Liberal Democratic Party would energize the next election and give this coalition a chance to continue to govern. There would be several tough issues to be resolved, but surely it is better to decide how you would govern and who would lead than to sit in the opposition seats, few in number.
David Gladstone, Toronto
Canadians are exhausted of Liberal mistakes
Can Justin Trudeau's Liberals seize on the U.S. Democrats' success? Aug. 28
The short answer is no. Justin Trudeau is far from being Kamala Harris and Pierre Poilievre is far from being Donald Trump. Canadians are exhausted from Trudeau's failures and screw-ups. They are tired of being told how the federal Liberals are learning from their mistakes and how seriously they take them and how they're going to do better. Just like it has taken years to start fixing the temporary foreign worker program, years after they were given sound advice from experts. They've also failed over the years to fix housing, affordability, homelessness and labour unrest, not to mention health care. Canadians are exhausted.
Brian Mellor, Midland, Ont.
Our society has become one of rage
A menacing stranger harassed an MP at a lake. Onlookers stood up for democracy, Aug. 27
While on a bus tour of Italy in 2019, I was part of a lineup at a restaurant that included many tourists when I was introduced to a fellow Canadian, a man from Alberta who was told I was from Toronto. The man turned around, looked at me, sneered and said "Toronto eh? I bet you're voting for Trudeau." I was a little taken aback, but not surprised. I quietly and calmly told him I don't discuss politics while on vacation. He didn't say anything, sneered again and turned his back. I was embarrassed by the disrespect he showed not only to me but to our country. I know the situation could have escalated, but I didn't want to engage or encourage him further. I suppose, considering the political atmosphere these days, I got off lightly. It frightens me terribly to think that this, and, probably worse, is ahead for my grandchildren.
Bonnie Beaudry, Burlington
The system itself isn't working for most Canadians
These Canadians don't care much about politics. Here's why a growing number feel democracy isn't working for them, Aug. 25
Reporter Raisa Patel hit the nail on the head when she wrote that "the system isn't working" for some voters. The electoral system isn't working for the majority of us! We inherited an electoral system that was created to keep wealthy landowners in power and it is still working to keep the wealthy in power. Right now, it is the minority of voters who are heard by our politicians. Our system of exclusively acknowledging winners by seats rather than by a true (over 50 per cent) majority of voters means that the majority don't have a voice in our "democratic" institutions. Let's have a meaningful and heeded citizens' assembly where the people, not the politicians, choose what the best electoral system is for Canada.
Stephen Cohen, Toronto
CAPTION: Of course the Liberals are going to lose to the federal Conservatives next time around, but the margin of loss will be far less devastating if Prime Minister Justin Trudeau steps aside, writes James McKnight of Toronto.Christinne MuschiTHE CANADIAN PRESS file photo

Document 487 of 500

Is fear of Ford driving Poilievre's push for election?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-fear-ford-driving-poilievres-push-election/docview/3099047478/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+fear+of+Ford+driving+Poilievre%27s+push+for+election%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford don't have a lot in common, except maybe their desire to hold elections early.
Yes, they are conservatives and chances are that the many Ontarians who have voted for Ford are likely leaning toward voting for the federal Conservatives when the next election comes.
Both leaders in their own ways would call themselves populists, too.
But that's where Ford and Poilievre have very different approaches. Ford is what many would call a happy populist - a guy who just wants everyone to vote for him, whether that requires giving out his phone number or shovelling someone's vehicle out of a snowbank.
Poilievre, on the other hand, seems happiest when shaking his fist at his enemies, rattling off all the ways in which the country has been broken by government. His press conference this week was a perfect example.
"Happy end of summer," the Conservative leader greeted reporters gathered outside Parliament's West Block. "But really, what's there to be happy about after Justin Trudeau's recent cabinet retreat?"
The stated reason for the press conference was to call for an election. Poilievre wrote a letter to NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, imploring him to end his working deal with the Trudeau government, so Canada can have an "axe the tax election" as soon as possible.
In the letter, Poilievre tells Singh to "pull out of the costly coalition and vote non-confidence in the government this September to trigger a carbon tax election in October of this year. Or you will forever be known as 'Sell-Out Singh.'"
Well, since he is asking so nicely, how could the NDP refuse?
It is, of course, extremely unlikely Singh will go along with Poilievre's request. Just this week at the retreat in Halifax, Trudeau and his House Leader Karina Gould said they expected the supply and confidence arrangement (not a coalition) to keep sparing Canadians an election until the fall of 2025.
But, as my colleagues at Queen's Park have been reporting for months now, there's a very good chance Ontarians will be voting in a provincial election before then.
"Sources say Ford is worried that if, as polls suggest, Pierre Poilievre wins an election expected in October 2025, there would be reduced transfer payments to the provinces, a scrapping of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's electric-vehicle strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy and other slashed spending that would hurt the Progressive Conservatives," Queen's Park Bureau Chief Robert Benzie wrote in May.
Poilievre hasn't said publicly how he feels about an Ontario vote possibly upstaging the Conservatives' much-anticipated romp to victory. The fact that Ford sees a Poilievre victory as not great for Ontario, however, is fascinating and further underlines that there are serious tensions between the Ford and Poilievre brands of conservatism.
What remains to be seen is whether an early Ontario election could harm Poilievre's chances.
For instance, what if Ontarians use a provincial election as their chance to vent at Trudeau and get some of the anti-Liberal sentiment we keep hearing about? What if Ontarians decide that, as long as Ford remains premier, they might as well have a prime minister with whom he has a good working relationship?
Maybe that's one of the reasons Poilievre was out there this week, agitating for Canada to go to the polls this fall, not next fall.
Now, anyone who is looking at a 20-point lead in the polls, as Poilievre is, would naturally be eager to get that election show on the road. Being prime minister is definitely more fun than being leader of the opposition.
But, if this summer in the U.S. has taught us anything, it's that a lot can change in a matter of weeks in politics these days. One wonders if Conservatives remain worried they're peaking too soon and the next year is going to be a slog of more intense scrutiny, more attention to mistakes and a struggle to maintain discipline over complacency.
Again, in the United States, there's a sense that politics may be turning against all the negativism of Donald Trump and his angry brand of populism, and tuning in more to a positive, progressive message coming from Kamala Harris and Tim Walz.
Poilievre doesn't like being compared to Trump, but the nickname tossing in Singh's direction this week sure seemed like an echo of the former president's schoolyard style.
Right now, as for most of the past year, Poilievre is having a moment. His sudden keenness for an October election may be rooted in concern that two other elections - one in Ontario, one in the U.S. - may make his moment fleeting.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre called this week for an "axe the tax election" this fall, and wrote a letter imploring NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh to scrap his coalition with the Liberal government. "Or you will forever be known as 'Sell-Out Singh,'" he warned.PATRICK DOYLE THE CANADIAN PRESS
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION

Document 488 of 500

Is Pierre Poilievre's election push driven by fear of Doug Ford?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-pierre-poilievres-election-push-driven-fear/docview/3099171688/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+election+push+driven+by+fear+of+Doug+Ford%3F&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre and Doug Ford don't have a lot in common, except maybe their desire to hold elections early.
Yes, they are conservatives and chances are that the many Ontarians who have voted for Ford are likely leaning toward voting for the federal Conservatives when the next election comes.
Both leaders in their own ways would call themselves populists, too.
But that's where Ford and Poilievre have very different approaches. Ford is what many would call a happy populist - a guy who just wants everyone to vote for him, whether that requires giving out his phone number or shovelling someone's vehicle out of a snowbank.
Poilievre, on the other hand, seems happiest when shaking his fist at his enemies, rattling off all the ways in which the country has been broken by government. His press conference this week was a perfect example.
"Happy end of summer," the Conservative leader greeted reporters gathered outside Parliament's West Block. "But really, what's there to be happy about after Justin Trudeau's recent cabinet retreat?"
The stated reason for the press conference was to call for an election. Poilievre wrote a letter to NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, imploring him to end his working deal with the Trudeau government, so Canada can have an "axe the tax election" as soon as possible.
In the letter, Poilievre tells Singh to "pull out of the costly coalition and vote non-confidence in the government this September to trigger a carbon tax election in October of this year. Or you will forever be known as 'Sell-Out Singh.'"
Well, since he is asking so nicely, how could the NDP refuse?
It is, of course, extremely unlikely Singh will go along with Poilievre's request. Just this week at the retreat in Halifax, Trudeau and his House Leader Karina Gould said they expected the supply and confidence arrangement (not a coalition) to keep sparing Canadians an election until the fall of 2025.
But as my colleagues at Queen's Park have been reporting for months now, there's a very good chance Ontarians will be voting in a provincial election before then.
"Sources say Ford is worried that if, as polls suggest, Pierre Poilievre wins an election expected in October 2025, there would be reduced transfer payments to the provinces, a scrapping of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's electric-vehicle strategy that is a cornerstone of Ontario economic policy and other slashed spending that would hurt the Progressive Conservatives," Queen's Park Bureau Chief Robert Benzie wrote in May.
Poilievre hasn't said publicly how he feels about an Ontario vote possibly upstaging the Conservatives' much-anticipated romp to victory. The fact that Ford sees a Poilievre victory as not great for Ontario, however, is fascinating and further underlines that there are serious tensions between the Ford and Poilievre brands of conservatism.
What remains to be seen is whether an early Ontario election could harm Poilievre's chances. For instance, what if Ontarians use a provincial election as their chance to vent at Trudeau and get some of the anti-Liberal sentiment we keep hearing about? What if Ontarians decide that as long as Ford remains premier, they might as well have a prime minister with whom he has a good working relationship?
Maybe that's one of the reasons Poilievre was out there this week, agitating for Canada to go to the polls this fall, not next fall.
Now, anyone who is looking at a 20-point lead in the polls, as Poilievre is, would naturally be eager to get that election show on the road. Being prime minister is definitely more fun than being leader of the opposition.
But if this summer in the U.S. has taught us anything, it's that a lot can change in a matter of weeks in politics these days. One wonders if Conservatives remain worried they're peaking too soon and the next year is going to be a slog of more intense scrutiny, more attention to mistakes and a struggle to maintain discipline over complacency.
Again, in the United States, there's a sense that politics may be turning against all the negativism of Donald Trump and his angry brand of populism, and tuning in more to a positive, progressive message coming from Kamala Harris and Tim Walz.
Poilievre doesn't like being compared to Trump, but the nickname tossing in Singh's direction this week sure seemed like an echo of the former president's schoolyard style.
Right now, as for most of the past year, Poilievre is having a moment. His sudden keenness for an October election may be rooted in concern that two other elections - one in Ontario, one in the U.S. - may make his moment fleeting.
CAPTION: Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks at a press conference on Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Aug. 29, 2024.

Document 489 of 500

School bus operators warn of delays and cancellations saying Ford government's new funding falls short

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/school-bus-operators-warn-delays-cancellations/docview/3099047494/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+bus+operators+warn+of+delays+and+cancellations+saying+Ford+government%27s+new+funding+falls+short&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: School bus operators warn of a bumpy road ahead after a change in provincial funding they say will lead to delays and cancellations.
The Ontario government, while increasing transportation funding overall, still does not cover actual costs and is not providing enough to cover "adequate driver compensation, driver retention bonuses and new buses," said Nancy Daigneault, executive director of School Bus Ontario.
"To make matters worse, the Ministry of Education imposed a devastating reduction of between 56 to 58 cents per litre of fuel, which has left operators in certain regions worried about filling their tanks to cover the millions of kilometres a day travelled," and some operators are still owed money from the previous school year, added Daigneault.
Daigneault called the problem "unintended consequences" of the new funding model, which was announced last year and since then "we've been working to try to rectify some issues" with it.
"We also need to warn people that over the next couple of weeks and months, things could get worse before they get better. This could result in slowdowns ... there are some operators that are just going on a wing and a prayer right now, and relying on local banks to finance them."
The province provides the funding for transportation, but there is a patchwork of contracts between the operators and individual school boards.
Education Minister Jill Dunlop, who has been on the job two weeks, said in a statement that "providing stability for students, parents and our schools is critical, that why our government has increased funding to school boards, enhanced wages for bus drivers and invested in strengthening safety of school buses to ensure children can reliably get to and from school."
The province is spending $80 million more in student transportation, for a total of $1.3 billion, Dunlop added, and "ensured every board received an increase of at least three per cent this school year, with many receiving double digit increases" as well as monies to pay a driver retention bonus of up to $2,000 a year.
"That includes a focus on recruiting and retaining drivers with our driver retention program that provides each eligible driver with bonus payments of up to $2,000 per year," she said.
But Michael Bellmore, president of the Ontario Catholic School Trustees Association, said transportation has been a concern and "truth be told is that funding just doesn't meet funding needs."
The reality is "that wages have gone up ... (as has) the cost of fuel," and the association has told the ministry about its concerns, he added.
"I'm sure we're going to hear a lot next week, once the school year starts officially, of the issues and pitfalls that they've experienced," he said. "I suspect strongly that transportation is going to be one of the top items on that list that we hear feedback on" from boards.
Kathleen Woodcock, president of the Ontario Public School Boards' Association, said "while the most recent round (of education funding) included a welcome increase in transportation funding and addressed some key concerns identified by boards - such as the need for funding for special purpose vehicles - there are still challenges that remain."
She said rural, remote and northern boards in particular face busing challenges.
NDP Education critic Chandra Pasma, who represents Ottawa West-Nepean, said boards are struggling "and it's going to be more and more school boards every year, unless the government is actually willing to admit that they made a mistake and fix the funding formula for student transportation."
She said the amounts allocated are based on pre-pandemic pricing "and costs have massively escalated," and when it comes to wages, "the benchmark doesn't cover all the time that a bus driver is actually driving the bus," including on the way to pick up students.
"That's why you saw in Ottawa last year, an operator walked away from the contract, which is what contributed to huge chaos," she added.
CAPTION: The Ontario government provides the funding for school transportation, but there is a patchwork of contracts between the operators and individual school boards.

Document 490 of 500

Justin Trudeau bends over backwards for America

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/justin-trudeau-bends-over-backwards-america/docview/3098788894/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Justin+Trudeau+bends+over+backwards+for+America&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's not much doubt what was on the minds of Justin Trudeau's cabinet when members met in Halifax this week - at least when it comes to policy and not their party's dismal standing in the polls.
You could tell by who they brought in to speak to them. There was Joe Biden's national security adviser, Jake Sullivan and one of Donald Trump's leading advisers on trade. There was Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to Washington, along with two former Canadian ambassadors and others deeply involved in cross-border issues.
Who wins the U.S. presidential election in November will obviously have a big impact on Canada. What's a bit less obvious is how the political dynamic south of the border is already shaking things up. There's a strong tide of protectionism and American nationalism across the political spectrum.
If Donald Trump hadn't already patented the slogan "Make America Great Again" you have to wonder if Kamala Harris wouldn't steal it, given the orgy of flag waving and ritual chants of "U.S.A! U.S.A!" the Democrats staged as they nominated her in Chicago.
The Trudeau government has clearly taken this on board, given its focus on U.S.-Canada issues in Halifax. It would have been nice to have heard what lessons the PM drew from what he heard.
But at the end of their retreat Trudeau was nowhere to be seen. Instead it was left to Ambassador Hillman to tell journalists that, despite all the Sturm und Drang south of the border, no need to worry. "I don't think we have any reason to be concerned under, to be frank, either administration," she averred.
It's hard to take that at face value, given the threats from Trump to impose costly across-the-board tariffs. But Hillman, after all, is a diplomat and it's her job to smooth the waters. And you have to suspect Liberal strategists simply didn't want to put Trudeau out front and centre, fearing he would just be peppered by more questions on the Liberals' sinking fortunes and his own political future.
But the American factor weighs heavily. It played a key role in one of the major announcements out of the retreat, the government's decision to impose 100-per-cent tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles and lower tariffs on steel and aluminum from China.
That matches tariffs imposed by Washington and it was inconceivable that Ottawa would allow a Canadian back door for Chinese imports, given the integration of the North American auto industry. Only a few lonely voices were raised to point out that banning cheap Chinese EVs undercuts the Liberals' own stated policy of promoting zero-carbon vehicles.
Hillman and the rest of "Team Canada" have certainly been busy. They've visited 33 states and met with 42 governors to drive home the importance of Canada to the U.S. economy. That's significant, but perhaps more important are changes going on in the background long before the November vote.
In that regard I'm struck by a development that hasn't had much attention. It turns out the U.S. Defense Department, the Pentagon in fact, is investing American tax dollars in Canadian mining ventures. So far it's put just over $26 million (U.S.) into three projects, the latest one announced last week. It involves developing a cobalt sulphate refining facility in Northern Ontario.
It's not huge money but it is hugely significant for how Washington sees the world these days and how Canada can fit into that vision. The U.S. wants to wean itself and the West generally off dependence on China for so-called critical minerals that are vital to all sorts of strategic industries - and to modern military hardware as well.
The last time the U.S. military invested abroad was back during the Second World War, when it helped to develop the Quebec aluminum industry to secure supplies of that metal for warplanes. Now, decades later, the Pentagon is putting money into ensuring supplies of cobalt, lithium, graphite and other critical minerals from Canada.
In a previous era at least someone in Canada would have raised an eyebrow about the Pentagon investing in our resources, with who knows what strings attached. Now it goes almost unnoticed. It's a sign of what Washington now expects from Canada - and how far this country is prepared to go to accommodate it.
CAPTION: Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell (R-KY) Canadian Ambassador to the United States Kirsten Hillman, Prime Minister of Canada Justin Trudeau, and Senate Majority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-NY) stand for a photo opportunity in Schumer's office at the U.S. Capitol on July 9 Washington, D.C. Trudeau and leaders from all NATO member states are in Washington to attend the 75th NATO Summit.

Subject: Strategic materials; Critical minerals; Decades; Diplomatic & consular services; Tariffs; Aluminum; Presidential elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US; Canada; China

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Title: Justin Trudeau bends over backwards for America

Is Doug Ford immune to controversy? + the community toll of Ontario Line construction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/is-doug-ford-immune-controversy-community-toll/docview/3098788862/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Doug+Ford+immune+to+controversy%3F+%2B+the+community+toll+of+Ontario+Line+construction&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Friday, August 30 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on Doug Ford's immunity to controversy, the community toll of a new Ontario Line station and the cost of Canada's aging population.
DON'T MISSDoug Ford's a magnet for controversy - but remains as popular as ever
Start calling Ford the Teflon Tory, because little seems to stick - at least for now. New polling data from Abacus show his Progressive Conservatives maintaining a healthy lead over all other parties, despite facing one controversy after another, Robert Benzie reports. But as concerns around his honesty and handling of taxpayer money grow, will the results of an RCMP investigation be the scandal to finally sink the untouchable premier?
The last straw? The RCMP are now probing Ford's ill-fated $8.28 billion Greenbelt land swap. "If something bad comes out of that investigation, it could be the thing that breaks through that Teflon," said Abacus' president.Back to the polls? Tory insiders tell the Star they're mulling an early election next year, well ahead of the scheduled vote in 2026.By the numbers: According to the poll, 42 per cent of committed voters intend on voting for Ford's party - that's well ahead of the Liberals at 26 per cent, the NDP at 21 per cent and the Greens at eight per cent.An Ontario Line station is coming to their neighbourhood - they're heartbroken
Residents of Pape village are visiting local stores to say goodbye and get one last meal, haircut or dress alteration before Ontario Line construction upends their neighbourhood, Mahdis Habibinia reports. Dozens of businesses along a 200-metre stretch on Pape Avenue are being forced to close or relocate to make way for the new Cosburn Station. On top of mourning beloved shops, residents are facing down several years of construction.
Word from residents: "It's ridiculous, it's dumb and it's unnecessary. So many communities are being destroyed," said one local, who felt the "heartbreak" dawn on her after watching shops she visits weekly being boarded up.End of an era: The two-dozen long-standing shops on Pape between Gamble and Gowan Avenues must go by Sept. 1. But while Metrolinx pays to expropriate property, some business owners say it's not enough to relocate or make up for uprooting their lives.Under construction: The new 15-station Ontario Line is slated to finish construction by 2031. The Star trekked its 15.6-kilometre length to document the toll construction has had on communities. Here's what we found.Canada could hit a $2 trillion shortfall as residents age
As Canada's baby boomers continue to age, provincial governments may soon be in for severe financial strain, a new report from the C.D. Howe Institute suggests. To contend with rising health care costs and income support, hard-hit provinces may soon need to raise taxes, Marisa Coulton reports. Meanwhile, economic growth is expected to slow as boomers exit the workforce. It paints a grim picture of Canada's future.
Word from an expert: "If you're a young person now, as time goes by, you're going to see a much bigger increase in taxes and a much smaller increase in services than your parents did," one author of the report predicted.Rising costs: By 2067, health care costs as a percentage of GDP are expected to almost double. In Ontario, costs are predicted to rise from 7.7 per cent of GDP to 12.6 per cent.Down the road: The issue will take some time to materialize, as boomers are living longer, working longer and retiring later, experts say.WHAT ELSE

Here's what's going on.A new strike season is unfolding. Document 492 of 500

Teflon Ford: 42% Committed voters who say they would vote for Ford's Tories 34% Where Ford polled a year ago after the AG's Greenbelt report last August

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/teflon-ford/docview/3098639697/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Teflon+Ford%3A+42%25+Committed+voters+who+say+they+would+vote+for+Ford%27s+Tories+34%25+Where+Ford+polled+a+year+ago+after+the+AG%27s+Greenbelt+report+last+August&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford appears to be the Teflon Tory.
In his lucky seventh summer as Ontario premier, Ford is emerging as a politician to whom little seems to stick.
At least for now.
In new polling for the Star, Abacus Data found voters are willing to forgive - if not quite forget - transgressions that would have derailed the electoral careers of others.
But significant concerns around Ford's honesty and his stewardship of taxpayers' money loom large because of the $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal now being probed by the RCMP.
"If something bad comes out of that investigation, it could be the thing that breaks through that Teflon," Abacus president David Coletto said this week, referring to the non-stick coating that seems to protect the premier.
Despite that risk, Progressive Conservative insiders confided to the Star they are ruminating about an early election next year, well before the scheduled June 2026 vote.
That's because the Tories are confident about the two-term premier's skills on the stump and his ability to connect with Ontarians in their everyday lives.
"It's an enigma - he's withstanding the same pressures that a lot of other political leaders haven't been able to and so the question is why? He's polarizing in the sense that if you don't like him, you don't like him. And there's a lot of Ontarians who don't like Doug Ford," said Coletto.
"But he has enough who do and they think he's just a friendly, nice guy who isn't perfect, but admits mistakes when he makes them and tries to fix them," the pollster said.
"He's forgiven because he asks for forgiveness."
Revealingly, when asked how to describe Ford, 44 per cent of respondents felt he was "friendly," while 20 per cent said he was "mean."
Similarly, 39 per cent said he "gets things done" while 38 per cent insisted he "fails to deliver"; 38 per cent said he is "normal" while 28 per cent said he's "weird"; and 37 per cent said he "admits mistakes and corrects them" while 38 per cent said he "refuses to admit mistakes."
"That's a winner - during the last campaign that became his new brand: the guy who gets things done," said Coletto, hearkening to the Tories' successful 2022 re-election slogan, Get It Done.
However, there are potentially ominous findings for the premier in the poll that may concern the governing party looking ahead to a spring campaign.
Fully 42 per cent felt he was "dishonest," while only 25 per cent said he was "honest"; 42 per cent said he was "reckless with taxpayers' money" compared to 25 per cent who said he was "careful" with it; 41 per cent said he's "corrupt" with 23 per cent saying he's "ethical"; and 39 per cent said he's "out of touch," while 35 per cent said he's "down to earth."
"The Greenbelt scandal did signal, perhaps, a recklessness," said Coletto, expressing surprise that so many respondents believed Ford wasted money given his frequent boasts of fiscal probity.
"That's why that scandal was so damaging. He is running large deficits. He's not raising people's taxes, but he's still spending a lot of money," he said, adding the $225 million to an estimated $1 billion Ford has earmarked to expedite liberalization of alcohol sales in the coming days may also be hurting the premier.
Abacus surveyed 1,028 Ontarians Aug. 14-17 using online panels based on the Lucid exchange platform. While opt-in polls cannot be assigned a margin of error, for comparison purposes, a random sample of this size would have one of plus or minus 3.057 percentage points, 19 times out of 20.
Overall, Abacus found Ford's Tories at 42 per cent, well ahead of Bonnie Crombie's Liberals at 26 per cent, Marit Stiles' New Democrats at 21 per cent and Mike Schreiner's Greens at eight per cent. That healthy PC lead is despite the fact there's strong awareness of the government's problems.
Fully 50 per cent of respondents were "familiar" with the Greenbelt debacle, in which Tory-connected developers were "favoured" in the unexpected November 2022 rezoning of 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive protected land to build housing.
Another 32 per cent were "aware of it, but not familiar" with the land swap an embattled Ford cancelled last Sept. 21, and 18 per cent didn't know.
Of the 82 per cent aware of the Greenbelt saga, 58 per cent were "less likely to support" him because of it, 12 per cent were "more likely to support" him while 28 per cent said it would have no impact.
About one-third - 35 per cent - were familiar with Ford's invoking of the Constitution's "notwithstanding" clause in 2022 to try to impose a contract settlement on 55,000 education workers. Under pressure from labour unions and the opposition, he backed off.
Forty per cent said they were aware of the constitutional controversy, but not familiar with it and 25 per cent didn't know anything about it.
Of the 75 per cent aware of the imbroglio, 43 per cent were less likely to support Ford over it, 26 per cent were more likely to back him while 28 per cent said it would have no impact.
Almost one-third - 31 per cent - were familiar with Ford's unilateral move to give "strong mayor" powers to the heads of city councils, while 31 per cent had heard about it but weren't familiar with the issue and 37 per cent didn't know about it. That enabled mayors to override council majorities on issues deemed "provincial priorities," such as fast-tracking construction of new housing.
Of the nearly two-thirds aware of the mayoral maelstrom, 43 per cent were less likely to back the premier over it, 23 per cent were more likely to support him while 32 per cent said it would have no impact.
Finally, Abacus asked respondents about last summer's contentious King's Counsel patronage award to 91 lawyers, many of whom had strong connections to the Tories, including MPPs, cabinet ministers and donors.
Just 16 per cent were familiar with the botched revival of an honorific former Liberal premier David Peterson had scrapped in 1985 because it had been "corrupted" by previous Tory governments rewarding cronies. (Peterson, now chair of the Star's parent company, Torstar, has criticized Ford for reinstituting the award.)
Another 28 per cent had heard of it but were not familiar with the KC kerfuffle, while 56 per cent didn't know about it.
Of the 44 per cent aware of it, 49 per cent were less likely to support Ford due to the flap, 16 per cent were more likely to support him while 33 per cent said it would have no impact.
Those lingering difficulties aside, Coletto said the poll may encourage Ford to forge ahead with a 2025 campaign because "all of these positive and favourable conditions, I believe, still likely outweigh the risk of that early election."
In June, Abacus found 44 per cent of Ontarians would be "unhappy" or "very angry" if the premier triggered a snap election, which would cost taxpayers at least $150 million.
"But if people already kind of think you're reckless with their money, spending another $150 million more may not be the thing that causes his own base to leave him," he said.
One senior Conservative, speaking confidentially in order to discuss the internal debate over whether to head to the polls early, said the hefty cost of what could be deemed "an unnecessary election" may be a reason to worry.
"When (Ford) has been asked by you guys why he needs (an earlier vote), he hasn't had a great answer yet," admitted the insider, pointing to the premier's responses to journalists' questions on such a gambit.
"The opposition is useless, so it's not like he can't get his agenda through," the source said, referring to the massive majority the Tories have in the legislature.
"As we saw with Peterson (whose Liberals were at 50 per cent in the polls when he called a snap election in 1990 only to lose to Bob Rae's New Democrats), things can change fast in a campaign."
Another top Tory said while "there may be stuff in (this latest Abacus) poll that gives the premier pause," the PC team trusts his judgment and his knack for reading public opinion.
"He's not ideological. It's like golf. He aims for the middle of the fairway because that's how you win," said the second insider.
Indeed, as longtime supporter Irene Borecky told the Star's Peter Edwards at the annual Ford Fest picnic in Milton two weeks ago, the premier's missteps - and his accessibility to the public - only humanize him.
"It's service above self. He's very easy to talk to," said Borecky, a retired program analyst.
To Coletto, "that is at the core of Doug Ford's brand," the perception of a folksy populist that has developed since he came to office in 2018.
"He is the guy who will call you back. You run into him in the airport or on the street, he's going to shake your hand, he wants to meet you - he's that ultimate retail politician," he said.
"That said, ethics and being careful with taxpayers' money and honesty remain areas of vulnerability."
CREDIT: Robert Benzie Queen's Park Bureau Chief

Subject: Scandals; Public opinion; Premier-Canada; Political campaigns; Polls & surveys; Elections; Voter behavior

Location: Ontario Canada; Canada

People: Ford, Doug

Company / organization: Name: Progressive Conservative Party-Canada; NAICS: 813940; Name: Abacus Data Systems Inc; NAICS: 513210

Title: Teflon Ford:   42% Committed voters who say they would vote for Ford's Tories 34% Where Ford polled a year ago after the AG's Greenbelt report last August

Document 493 of 500

Trudeau bends over backward for America

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-bends-over-backward-america/docview/3098638581/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+bends+over+backward+for+America&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's not much doubt what was on the minds of Justin Trudeau's cabinet when members met in Halifax this week - at least when it comes to policy and not their party's dismal standing in the polls.
You could tell by who they brought in to speak to them. There was Joe Biden's national security adviser, Jake Sullivan, and one of Donald Trump's leading advisers on trade. There was Kirsten Hillman, Canada's ambassador to Washington, along with two former Canadian ambassadors and others deeply involved in cross-border issues.
Who wins the U.S. presidential election in November will obviously have a big impact on Canada. What's a bit less obvious is how the political dynamic south of the border is already shaking things up. There's a strong tide of protectionism and American nationalism across the political spectrum.
If Donald Trump hadn't already patented the slogan "Make America Great Again" you have to wonder if Kamala Harris wouldn't steal it, given the orgy of flag waving and ritual chants of "U.S.A! U.S.A!" the Democrats staged as they nominated her in Chicago.
The Trudeau government has clearly taken this on board, given its focus on U.S.-Canada issues in Halifax. It would have been nice to have heard what lessons the PM drew from what he heard.
But at the end of their retreat Trudeau was nowhere to be seen. Instead it was left to Ambassador Hillman to tell journalists that, despite all the Sturm und Drang south of the border, no need to worry. "I don't think we have any reason to be concerned under, to be frank, either administration," she averred.
It's hard to take that at face value, given the threats from Trump to impose costly across-the-board tariffs. But Hillman, after all, is a diplomat and it's her job to smooth the waters. And you have to suspect Liberal strategists simply didn't want to put Trudeau out front and centre, fearing he would just be peppered by more questions on the Liberals' sinking fortunes and his own political future.
But the American factor weighs heavily. It played a key role in one of the major announcements out of the retreat, the government's decision to impose 100 per cent tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles and lower tariffs on steel and aluminum from China.
That matches tariffs imposed by Washington and it was inconceivable that Ottawa would allow a Canadian back door for Chinese imports, given the integration of the North American auto industry. Only a few lonely voices were raised to point out that banning cheap Chinese EVs undercuts the Liberals' own stated policy of promoting zero-carbon vehicles.
Hillman and the rest of "Team Canada" have certainly been busy. They've visited 33 states and met with 42 governors to drive home the importance of Canada to the U.S. economy. That's significant, but perhaps more important are changes going on in the background long before the November vote.
In that regard, I'm struck by a development that hasn't had much attention. It turns out the U.S. Defence Department, the Pentagon in fact, is investing American tax dollars in Canadian mining ventures. So far it's put just over $26 million (U.S.) into three projects, the latest one announced last week. It involves developing a cobalt sulphate refining facility in northern Ontario.
It's not huge money but it is hugely significant for how Washington sees the world these days and how Canada can fit into that vision. The U.S. wants to wean itself and the West generally off dependence on China for so-called critical minerals that are vital to all sorts of strategic industries - and to modern military hardware as well.
The last time the U.S. military invested abroad was back during the Second World War, when it helped to develop the Quebec aluminum industry to secure supplies of that metal for warplanes. Now, decades later, the Pentagon is putting money into ensuring supplies of cobalt, lithium, graphite and other critical minerals from Canada.
In a previous era at least someone in Canada would have raised an eyebrow about the Pentagon investing in our resources, with who knows what strings attached. Now it goes almost unnoticed. It's a sign of what Washington now expects from Canada - and how far this country is prepared to go to accommodate it.
CAPTION: Recent comments by Canadian Ambassador to the U.S. Kirsten Hillman, second from left, that Canada needn't be worried no matter who wins the U.S. presidential election are hard to take considering the strong tide of protectionism and nationalism across the American political spectrum, Andrew Phillips writes.Bonnie Cash Getty Images file photo
CREDIT: Andrew Phillips OPINION;

Subject: Presidential elections; Critical minerals; Politics; Diplomatic & consular services; Protectionism; Tariffs; Canada-US relations; International trade; Federal government

Business indexing term: Subject: Protectionism Tariffs International trade

Location: United States--US; Canada

People: Trump, Donald J; Trudeau, Justin

Company / organization: Name: Department of Defense; NAICS: 928110

Doug Ford has faced controversy after controversy - yet remains as popular as ever. Here's why Ontarians say they still like him

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/doug-ford-has-faced-controversy-after-yet-remains/docview/3098891753/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Doug+Ford+has+faced+controversy+after+controversy+-+yet+remains+as+popular+as+ever.+Here%27s+why+Ontarians+say+they+still+like+him&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 495 of 500

Underage girl subjected to six 'routine' strip searches + Toronto cop gets demoted after a cheating scandal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/underage-girl-subjected-six-routine-strip/docview/3098264978/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Underage+girl+subjected+to+six+%27routine%27+strip+searches+%2B+Toronto+cop+gets+demoted+after+a+cheating+scandal&amp;title=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Toronto+Star+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Good morning. This is the Thursday, August 29 edition of First Up, the Star's daily morning digest. Sign up to get it earlier each day, in your inbox.
Here's the latest on an underaged girl subjected to six strip searches, why a trailblazing Toronto cop was demoted and the end of a controversial COVID-era immigration policy.
DON'T MISSA girl who participated in a deadly swarming faced 'routine' strip searches
One of the teenage girls who pleaded guilty in the swarming death of Kenneth Lee was routinely strip-searched while in custody - including being forced to be completely naked on six separate occasions, a court has heard. "It was humiliating," said the 15-year-old girl, who was 13 at the time of her arrest and the first four strip searches. Jennifer Pagliaro dives into the disturbing revelation.
The details: The unnamed girl's first four searches were at the Sundance youth facility in Kingston, where it was policy to have girls strip completely naked during searches. The other two were done at Woodview in London, which had no such policy.Background: Months earlier, the girl pleaded guilty to second-degree murder in the death of 59-year-old Lee, who police allege was overpowered and stabbed by a group of eight teen girls in 2022.Now what? The girl's lawyer planned to argue on Wednesday that her client's charter rights had been violated. She plans to seek a reduced sentence.The cop at the centre of a cheating scandal has been demoted
A trailblazing cop who became Toronto police's first Black female superintendent will be stripped of her rank after leaking confidential exam questions to six Black officers in a "desperate effort to level the playing field," Wendy Gillis reports. Supt. Stacy Clarke's actions amounted to "extremely serious" misconduct, the police tribunal adjudicator said: "There is no room in policing for noble cause corruption." Here's what happens now.
The consequences: Clarke will be knocked down to the rank of inspector for two years. She must reapply to regain her hard-fought higher rank.Knock-on effects: All six of the officers she helped faced career consequences when the cheating came to light; five received unit-level discipline and were docked thousands in pay, while a sixth was demoted.Divided: Clarke's actions won her hero status among many, in part for shining a light on the systemic barriers faced by racialized officers, others say her decisions backfired, ultimately damaging public trust.Visitors can no longer apply for work permits from within Canada
Ottawa has ended a COVID-era measure blamed for contributing to Canada's surging numbers of temporary residents, Nicholas Keung reports. Starting immediately, visitors can no longer apply for work permits from within Canada - part of the Immigration Department's plans to "recalibrate the number of temporary residents in Canada and preserve the integrity of the immigration system." Here's what this could mean for immigration in Canada.
Background: The measure was instituted partly to mitigate the financial hardship faced by visitors stuck in Canada amid COVID-era border closures and to fill labour shortages after the pandemic.Word from advocates: It will now be harder - if not impossible - for temporary foreign workers to maintain employment from within Canada once their work authorization expires.Meanwhile: The number of "open work permits" has surged in the last five years - a development experts say points to rampant abuse within the temporary foreign worker program.WHAT ELSE

Here's why he's expected to last only a few weeks.Mississauga parents fear for their children's lives if another flood hits during the school year.Whooping cough infections are surging across Canada and Toronto. Document 496 of 500

Ontario to boost power generation: Province to choose projects based on 'affordability'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-boost-power-generation/docview/3098162405/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+to+boost+power+generation%3A+Province+to+choose+projects+based+on+%27affordability%27&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario is touting protections for farmland and the possibility of more carbon-free power as it seeks proposals for 5,000 megawatts of electricity generation - enough to power five million homes.
"The message we're sending today is renewables are very much part of this," Energy and Electrification Minister Stephen Lecce said Wednesday in a switch from Premier Doug Ford's decision to cancel 758 green energy projects after taking office in 2018.
Lecce added the approach to the electricity procurement is "technology-agnostic" with officials ready to weigh proposals using "affordability" as the main determinant to avoid shocks on consumers' hydro bills.
"Ontario must produce more affordable energy to keep up" with a fast-growing population and electrification needs fuelled by electric vehicles and artificial intelligence and data services, he said at a farm in King City, north of Toronto.
Keith Brooks of Environmental Defence cautioned against over-optimism about more green energy from the Conservatives.
He noted Lecce's "agnostic" reference makes the procurement "open to natural gas and nukes" and other forms of electricity generation in addition to renewables like solar and wind power.
"We look forward to the opportunity to compete," Leonard Kula of the Canadian Renewable Energy Association told reporters at the announcement with Lecce.
While applauding the government's "U-turn" on renewables, Green Leader Mike Schreiner said the province has been increasingly relying on natural gas, which produces carbon emissions that contribute to climate change.
"Renewables are cheaper, cleaner and make far more economic sense than fossil gas," he added in a statement.
As recently as 2017, Ontario's grid was 96 per cent free of carbon emissions but that has fallen to 87 per cent with nuclear reactors offline for refurbishments. It remains one of the cleanest systems in the world and the emission-free level will rise once nuclear plants return to service.
Promised protections for agricultural lands come in the wake of the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal, now under investigation by the RCMP as Ford considers an early election next year.
The protections include a prohibition on energy projects in "specialty crop areas" such as the Holland Marsh, ensure no ground-mounted solar installations in prime agricultural areas, and require "agricultural impact assessments" for projects proposed for prime farmland.
This approach "strikes the right balance between protecting how we grow our food and how we generate our energy," said Agriculture, Food and Agribusiness Minister Rob Flack.
The procurement will be handled by Ontario's Independent Electricity System Operator. Energy developers proposing projects will be required to get resolutions of support from local municipal councils to ensure new facilities are welcome.
"We are ready to move quickly to secure supply from all available technologies," Lesley Gallinger, chief executive of the IESO, said in a statement.
Shortly after taking over the energy portfolio in June, Lecce said the province can't afford an "ideological" push to wean its electricity system off fossil fuels despite criticism the province has been increasing its reliance on natural gas-fuelled plants to meet peak demand.
The procurement plan also offers incentives for projects in northern Ontario, with a plan to make Crown lands available for renewable energy.
Incentives will be provided for projects by Indigenous communities on their traditional territories.
CREDIT: Rob Ferguson Queen's Park Bureau

Subject: Alternative energy sources; Carbon; Electricity generation; Renewable resources; Farms; Natural gas
