Document 497 of 500

MPP calls for inquest into child's death

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-calls-inquest-into-childs-death/docview/3097745995/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+calls+for+inquest+into+child%27s+death&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The government critic for child welfare says an inquest must be called into the death of Neveah, whose remains were found in a Toronto dumpster, and that the death of every child who has been involved in care should automatically trigger that public scrutiny.
"It's unacceptable that nobody knew this child was missing," said Hamilton Mountain NDP MPP Monique Taylor, who has been the critic for children, community and social services since 2011.
"There's no 'I'm sorry's. There's no 'We made a mistake.' It's completely unacceptable and can never happen again."
As reported by the Star this weekend, four-year-old Neveah spent the majority of her life in Children's Aid Society care, first in York Region, then in Toronto. She was taken into protective custody at birth after toxicology reports found marijuana in her system. In March 2021, a CAS request and subsequent court order placed her back with her birth mother, who had a lengthy history with the child protection system.
By the end of that year, police said Neveah was already dead, her tiny, decomposing body left the following spring wrapped in blankets inside a construction dumpster outside a home in Rosedale.
Investigators, who spent 14 months trying to identify the girl who was never reported missing, resorted to a resource-intensive technique involving genetic genealogy that combines advanced DNA technology with ancestry research on public genetic genealogy sites, similar to Ancestry.com. By June 2023, they had determined Neveah's name (The Star is not using Neveah's full name because a court publication ban prevents identifying members of her family).
"This is a prime example of how a child completely fell through the cracks and nobody could put together the pieces of who that child was because systems weren't talking to each other," Taylor told the Star in an interview Tuesday.
Premier Doug Ford's office did not respond to the Star's questions and instead forwarded the paper's requests to the Ministry for Children, Community and Social Services.
In a statement, Minister Michael Parsa's press secretary, Sean Forsyth, called Neveah's death "an absolute tragedy to family, friends, and everyone in our communities."
"While calling an inquest is an independent decision made by the Chief Coroner, we will fully support and co-operate with his office to help prevent future tragedies like this."
Dr. Dirk Huyer, Ontario's Chief Coroner, recently told the Star it was too early to call an inquest into Neveah's death due to ongoing police involvement.
"My understanding is the investigation is ongoing, and so it would be premature to make a decision about that," he said in an interview.
A coroner's inquest is a quasi-judicial hearing that doesn't assign blame but probes the circumstances of a death, determining how a death happened and producing recommendations aimed at preventing future fatalities.
The Toronto police investigation into Neveah's suspicious death remains open but no charges have been laid to date.
Taylor likened Neveah's death to that of seven-year-old Katelynn Sampson, who died in 2008 and whose legal guardians were convicted of second-degree murder after various agencies lost track of her.
An inquest led to 173 recommendations, which advocates hoped would lead to an overhaul of the system that better protects the province's most vulnerable children.
"I really hoped that after that, that from her inquest recommendations, that we would have been able to make strides to make a better system," Taylor said. "And now that we see what happened with Neveah it's very clear that ... whatever they've done has not been enough to ensure children's' safety."
The MPP also called into question the provincial government's review of the child welfare system that the ministry says has been ongoing since 2020, saying the only apparent changes are a lack of funding which would also make implementing inquest recommendations more difficult.
"The government needs to come clean about what their review actually looks like because nobody knows," Taylor said. She also criticized the lack of institutional secrecy when a child dies.
"How would we have known about Neveah if it wasn't for these stories coming out? I had no idea. I've been the critic for all this time, I had no idea. That's not OK."
After she was born, Neveah was placed in a foster home for nearly three years. She was eventually joined by a younger brother. She was non-verbal and was diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder, but her complex needs were being met by her foster parents, who took her to specialist appointments and speech therapy and provided educational toys for children with autism.
In 2020, when Neveah was nearly three, she and her brother - who was also diagnosed with autism spectrum disorder - were removed from the foster family and placed under a supervision order that would return her to the custody of her mother while being monitored by York Region CAS.
All five of Neveah's siblings were also involved with the child protection system at various points in their lives due to ongoing concerns about neglect, unsanitary conditions, and the mother's substance use.
In March 2021, at the request of CAS Toronto, a judge terminated the supervision order altogether - meaning Neveah and her brother would be returned to the sole custody of their mother, with no supervision by any children's aid society.
Neveah's remains were found in a dumpster on Dale Avenue on May 2, 2022. Though it's not known how or exactly when she died, forensic testing on her remains determined she died that same year, after she was returned to her mother's care.
Critics say the decision to end the supervision order and close the CAS Toronto file must be reviewed now, knowing the tragic outcome.
"I can't fathom how a judge would release a child like Neveah, with the complex special needs that she had, back to a single mom who has her own battles around addictions ... let alone poverty and other children," Irwin Elman, who served as the province's Child Advocate from 2008 to 2019, recently told the Star.
CREDIT: Jennifer Pagliaro and Wendy Gillis Toronto Star

'Star Wars Outlaws' heavy with Canadian talent: Game's featured voice artist honed her craft studying theatre while growing up in Alberta

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/star-wars-outlaws-heavy-with-canadian-talent/docview/3097745976/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Star+Wars+Outlaws%27+heavy+with+Canadian+talent%3A+Game%27s+featured+voice+artist+honed+her+craft+studying+theatre+while+growing+up+in+Alberta&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=B.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Star Wars Outlaws" would have had a distinctly Canadian touch if the game's narrative director had his way.
Early in the development process for the upcoming video game, Navid Khavari suggested an idea that ultimately never made it out of the writers' room.
"I wanted to write a scene that was just two smugglers apologizing to each other the entire time," said Khavari, who is based in Toronto along with lead writer Nikki Foy and lead voice actor Humberly Gonzalez.
Developed by Ubisoft, "Outlaws" is the first open-world "Star Wars" game and is set between the events depicted in the movies "The Empire Strikes Back" and "Return of the Jedi."
While Sweden-based Massive Entertainment led the development, support provided by other Ubisoft studios included motion capture work that Khavari says was done in Toronto and Montreal.
As its name suggests, "Outlaws" explores the criminal underbelly of the galaxy, a side of "Star Wars" that has rarely been seen.
Players will have the opportunity to explore several planets, including the classic Tatooine, but also lesser-known and even new locales.
Foy says it was a fun challenge to create a brand new story in a well-established universe.
"Getting to work on this project, in this galaxy, at this time period, it was like a cheat code that got all of us on the same page right away," Foy said.
"You didn't have to worry about what's the world going to be like, what's the tone, what's the vibe - that stuff we already know."
Khavari, who was also the narrative director for "Far Cry 6" and has worked on a number of other Ubisoft titles, says Massive contacted him early on about joining the "Star Wars" project.
"We have a huge abundance of writing talent in Toronto," he said. "The idea really became, let's build a story team in Toronto. This was years and years ago, and Massive was up for it."
In a departure from recent "Star Wars" video games, the main character in "Outlaws" is not a lightsaber-wielding, Force-pushing Jedi but a blaster-packing scoundrel.
Gonzalez voices protagonist Kay Vess, a charming and witty rogue trying to find her way in the galaxy.
Gonzalez was born in Venezuela and learned English in the nearby Caribbean island nation of Aruba before moving with her family to Fort McMurray, Alta., where she spent nearly 10 years and studied theatre.
"Being in a small city, I think it really allowed me to home in on my art," said Gonzalez, who later lived in Montreal and is now based in Toronto.
"Moving to Canada changed everything for me," she added.
"Much like Kay - she left everything behind, she had big dreams, she went to an unknown place where there were a lot of opportunities, and you just have to find them and seize them."
"Star Wars Outlaws" will be released Friday for Xbox Series S and X, PS5 and PC.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Humberly Gonzalez, the main voice actor who plays the character Kay Vess in the upcoming video game "Star Wars Outlaws," at Ubisoft's Toronto offices.Chris YoungTHE CANADIAN PRESS File Photo
CREDIT: Curtis Ng The Canadian Press

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters%3A+Pierre+Poilievre%27s+overheated+insults.+Canadians+of+all+stripes+being+nasty+to+each+other+online.+It%27s+past+time+for+all+of+us+to+heed+Jack+Layton%2C+Aug.+26+Trudeau+government+matches+U.S.+tariffs+on+Chinese+EVs+and+Beijing+warns+of+%27retaliation%2C%27+Aug.+26+After+the+Republicans+leaned+way+into+weird%2C+the+Democrats+made+a+big+bet+on+normal%2C+Aug.+24+I%27ve+been+Blue+Jays+fan+for+decades.+Now+I+need+to+take+a+break%2C+Aug.+24+Danny+Jansen+makes+MLB+history%2C+playing+for+the+Blue+Jays+and+Red+Sox+in+the+same+game%2C+Aug.+26+Italian+prosecutors+open+shipwreck+and+manslaughter+investigation+into+superyacht+sinking%2C+Aug.+24&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Politicians shouldn't attack doctors, nurses
Pierre Poilievre's overheated insults. Canadians of all stripes being nasty to each other online. It's past time for all of us to heed Jack Layton, Aug. 26
I have just finished reading the insults hurled towards Doris Grinspun, the head of the Registered Nurses Association of Ontario, after she dared to stand up and say the idea of eliminating safe injection sites is the wrong approach. Sadly, her words won't have impact on our premier, the premiers of other provinces or federal leaders who made this call or support it, without an education in medicine. Do I think that safe injection sites are a great idea? Not really. However, at this moment in time I do not see much in the way of viable solutions to replace them. If the people standing in the way of solutions are so naïve and blind to the present situation then we are doomed to continue to see no end in sight. To hurl insults and negative comments at people who are trying to find a solution is counterproductive. I shall remember such political leaders when election time comes around. They will not be getting my vote.
Catherine Hetu, Merlin, Ont.
Much like the Greenbelt flip-flop, perhaps Premier Doug Ford would be wise to listen to the "folks" and logically and humanely pivot to maintain harm reduction resources. This should not be a case of "If I want your opinion, I'll give it to you." We must prioritize saving lives.
James Gervais, Toronto
Tariffs won't help lower-income Canadians
Trudeau government matches U.S. tariffs on Chinese EVs and Beijing warns of 'retaliation,' Aug. 26
The Trudeau government's decision to impose a 100 per cent duty on Chinese-made electric vehicles (EVs) raises significant concerns, particularly for its impact on lower-income Canadians. Because this will significantly increase the cost of these vehicles, the policy disproportionately affects those who already struggle with the cost of living. Access to affordable EVs would alleviate some financial burdens by offering a low-cost transportation option with reduced fuel expenses. The policy also appears inconsistent and potentially discriminatory, given that similar duties are not being applied to EVs from other countries. This discrepancy undermines the principle of fair trade. The decision limits the availability of cost-effective and environmentally friendly transportation options for Canadian families who need them most.
Ronald Strauss, Waterloo
Americans seem ready to move on from the chaos
After the Republicans leaned way into weird, the Democrats made a big bet on normal, Aug. 24
The Democratic Party planned, organized and fine-tuned their mammoth convention in record time, weeks after Kamala Harris and Tim Walz took over the reins of the Democrat ticket. Remarkably, most everything went off as planned. Presenting Harris and Walz as "candidates of difference and joy" was highly successful and they were acclaimed by the overjoyed audience as leaders who will deliver on promises made. Remarkably, there was full agreement/cohesion among the different factions of the party as never before! When Harris thundered "we're not going back," it was clear Americans are ready to move on. The entire world is longing for a new era of calm, amid success, in the U.S.
Maureen Fernandes, Mississauga
Jays woes start with Ed Rogers, management
I've been Blue Jays fan for decades. Now I need to take a break, Aug. 24
This letter to the editor clearly indicated, in a very pointed manner, why so many long time fans are distancing themselves from the Blue Jays. It's time for some major changes. They should start at the top with general manager Ross Atkins and president Mark Shapiro, who have long overstayed their welcome and have to move on. But sadly, chair Edward Rogers is no George Steinbrenner, the former owner of the New York Yankees. These are bottom-line people and, just like they run their other businesses, they are prioritizing turning a profit, not maintaining the ballpark experience we are used to.
Marty Fruchtman, Toronto
Who are they even playing for?
Danny Jansen makes MLB history, playing for the Blue Jays and Red Sox in the same game, Aug. 26
While catcher Danny Jansen may be the only Blue Jay to also play for the other team during the same game, it seems like many of his former teammates in Toronto have been doing that on a regular basis.
Gregory Hoggarth, Stratford, Ont.
Some sunken boats matter more than others
Italian prosecutors open shipwreck and manslaughter investigation into superyacht sinking, Aug. 24
Once again, the media proves there is a hierarchy of human beings. A luxury yacht sank off the coast of Sicily in which seven drowned, including a British billionaire, and media across the world covered it extensively. Over the past many years, thousands of migrants in boats have drowned in the Mediterranean Sea and Atlantic Ocean trying to reach Europe, and little is reported. Why the difference? The former tragedy concerned white Europeans who were also prominent and wealthy; the latter, poor migrants, who were often non-white Africans. Some people are more equal than others.
Jacob Mendlovic, Toronto
Lack of support from absent condo board
Two years ago, we moved to a new condominium, a first for us. It quickly became apparent that, along with our neighbours, there were concerns regarding the board of directors. The concerns range from a 33 per cent increase in maintenance fees in less than two years, a lack of transparency, overspending their own budget by hundreds of thousands of dollars, to not holding annual general meetings on time. Only one person, who was properly elected, lives in the building and sees the daily issues. The Condominium Authority of Ontario sets the rules that govern boards, but they have not been responsive and tend to point people to legislation and will not meet even for an hour to help guide us. We pay for their service but their lack of support is abysmal.
David Marskell, Kitchener

Subject: Decades; Tariffs

Business indexing term: Subject: Tariffs

Location: United States--US

Title: Letters:   Pierre Poilievre's overheated insults. Canadians of all stripes being nasty to each other online. Can Liberals follow Democrats' success?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/can-liberals-follow-democrats-success/docview/3097744982/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Can+Liberals+follow+Democrats%27+success%3F&amp;title=Toronto+Star&amp;issn=03190781&amp;date=2024-08-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=Toronto+Star&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was a bit of bad luck for Justin Trudeau's Liberals, having to hold their annual cabinet retreat on the heels of the wildly successful Democratic convention in the U.S.
The comparisons and the questions were going to be inevitable - why can't the Trudeau Liberals do that kind of progressive political revival here in Canada?
As minister after minister explained, no one is talking about showing Trudeau the exit door, as the Democrats did with Joe Biden.
But leaving that nuclear option aside, one presumes that ministers would be drawing some lessons from how the Democrats rebranded themselves and their new leader, Kamala Harris, at last week's convention.
Some of the strategies on display in Chicago last week could be transferable techniques - at least to some measure.
The first one, the "joy" factor, is the most obvious but also the toughest. Government House Leader Karina Gould alluded to this when she was talking to reporters about possible parallels between Liberals here and the rebranded Democrats.
"Just because the Democrats do something in the United States does not then mean that we should be doing that here in Canada as Liberals," Gould said. "What I do think is that progressive voters are looking for continued hope."
Hope, positivity and joy can be hard to pull off, though, if you're a nine-year-old government, languishing as much as 20 points behind the Conservatives in the opinion polls.
The cabinet retreat featured a small flurry of announcements, but it would be a reach to call them sunny measures - tariffs on Chinese vehicles, limits to temporary foreign workers, a task force on trade barriers. They were meant to show a government hard at work, which is serious, as opposed to joyous business.
There have been signs the Liberals would dearly love to reframe their battle against Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives as the Democrats have shifted their framing of Donald Trump - from scary to "weird," from object of fear to object of ridicule.
We can probably expect to see Liberals making more forays in this direction, similar to Immigration Minister Marc Miller, talking in Halifax about Poilievre's "crap slogans." But can they diminish a 20-point lead with punchlines or mockery? That won't be easy.
There was another new Democratic strategy on display in Chicago - or maybe it's more properly noted at what was not on display, conspicuous by its absence.
Harris was being very careful how she came at the idea of being the first female president or the first Black and South Asian female president. Instead, she spoke more of herself as someone from humble beginnings, evidence that ambitious dreams are available to all in America.
This was no doubt a legacy of what happened to Hillary Clinton and her bid for the presidency, which constantly reminded voters of the identity stakes in the 2016 election.
But it's also a product of many voters' fatigue with identity politics - even voters on the progressive left. The debates, often pitting one demographic against another, have been exhausting.
One hears this in Canada, too. Trudeau and his Liberals are often criticized for spending too much time on these issues - the accusation that they're too "woke." If Liberals are taking some cues from Harris and the Democrats in the wake of Chicago, they might want to take a closer look at the caution with which their American progressives are handling identity politics in 2024.
Thanks to their change in leadership, Democrats do have a luxury that is not available to the Trudeau Liberals - an opportunity to rebrand the party on a more fundamental level. CNN commentator Van Jones, a veteran Democrat, spoke often of this last week while watching the convention.
Whether it was in reclaiming the word "freedom" from Republicans or recasting what it means to be a patriot, Jones said, Harris is staking out a new definition of progressive politics.
"They are asserting that the value of freedom is not just the freedom to not pay your taxes, or the freedom to, say, get off my lawn. It's the freedom to live a full life," Jones said.
Though Liberals can hardly set about redefining progressive politics on their own right now - especially in a party that Trudeau has already rebranded in his own image a decade ago - they should be paying close attention to where the Democrats are moving the markers on the left-right political spectrum. If nothing else, political trends that start in the U.S. often end up here.
In front of the cameras, Trudeau and his team took every opportunity to point out that it would be a mistake to do too much comparing between Liberals in Canada and Democrats in the U.S. Behind the scenes though, it wouldn't be a surprise if Trudeau's ministers were seeing if their playbook could take a few pages from Chicago.
CREDIT: susan delacourt OPINION
